d0GKyzG9OWA.text: and we have responsibilities. You have kids, you have lots of things going on. I also like spending time with my friends.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ənd wi hæv riˌspɑnsəˈbɪlətiz. ju hæv kɪdz, ju hæv lɑts əv θɪŋz goʊɪŋ ɔn. aɪ ˈɔlsoʊ laɪk ˈspɛndɪŋ taɪm wɪθ maɪ frɛndz.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: I have lots of friends. As I said, I've had a lot of social plans lately.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: aɪ hæv lɑts əv frɛndz. ɛz aɪ sɛd, aɪv hæd ə lɔt əv ˈsoʊʃəl plænz ˈleɪtli.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: Pretty much every day I've been socialising.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ˈprɪti məʧ ˈɛvəri deɪ aɪv bɪn socialising*.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: Wow, so you like socialising but do you like things like parties or
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: waʊ, soʊ ju laɪk socialising* bət du ju laɪk θɪŋz laɪk ˈpɑrtiz ər

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: do you prefer smaller social events? I do really like big parties because I like meeting new people.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: do* ju prɪˈfər sˈmɔlər ˈsoʊʃəl ɪˈvɛnts? aɪ du ˈrɪli laɪk bɪg ˈpɑrtiz bɪˈkəz aɪ laɪk ˈmitɪŋ nu people.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: I like dancing as well. So, at big parties, usually there's some music.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: aɪ laɪk ˈdænsɪŋ ɛz wɛl. soʊ, æt bɪg ˈpɑrtiz, ˈjuʒəwəli ðɛrz səm mˈjuzɪk.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: But I do have a shy side of my personality,
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: bət aɪ du hæv ə ʃaɪ saɪd əv maɪ ˌpərsəˈnælɪti,

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: so usually a smaller event is better for me.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: soʊ ˈjuʒəwəli ə sˈmɔlər ɪˈvɛnt ɪz ˈbɛtər fər mi.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: So, I can have kind of deeper conversations with fewer people. How about you?
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: so*, aɪ kən hæv kaɪnd əv ˈdipər ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz wɪθ fjuər ˈpipəl. haʊ əˈbaʊt you?

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: Yeah. I don't really like that situation where you're at a party and don't know lots of people.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: jæ. aɪ doʊnt ˈrɪli laɪk ðət ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən wɛr jʊr æt ə ˈpɑrti ənd doʊnt noʊ lɑts əv ˈpipəl.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: So, a small group of people that I mainly know, and there's one or two people who I don't know
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: soʊ, ə smɔl grup əv ˈpipəl ðət aɪ ˈmeɪnli noʊ, ənd ðɛrz wən ər tu ˈpipəl hu aɪ doʊnt noʊ

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: so well, that's fine. But a bigger group with lots of people I don't know – I really don't like that.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: soʊ wɛl, ðæts faɪn. bət ə ˈbɪgər grup wɪθ lɑts əv ˈpipəl aɪ doʊnt noʊ –* aɪ ˈrɪli doʊnt laɪk ðət.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: And why is that? Is that because you don't like small talk? Yeah. I don't really like small talk.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: and* waɪ ɪz ðət? ɪz ðət bɪˈkəz ju doʊnt laɪk smɔl tɔk? jæ. aɪ doʊnt ˈrɪli laɪk smɔl talk.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: It feels a bit empty. 'Small talk' is conversations about things that aren't important,
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ɪt filz ə bɪt ˈɛmti. 'smɔl tɔk' ɪz ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz əˈbaʊt θɪŋz ðət ˈɑrənt ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt,

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: like the weather, usually with people that you don't know very well. Yeah. And you don't like that?
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: like* ðə ˈwɛðər, ˈjuʒəwəli wɪθ ˈpipəl ðət ju doʊnt noʊ ˈvɛri wɛl. jæ. ənd ju doʊnt laɪk that?

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: No. I find it awkward. So, Georgie, you like meeting new people?
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: no*. aɪ faɪnd ɪt ˈɔkwərd. soʊ, ˈʤɔrʤi, ju laɪk ˈmitɪŋ nu people?

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: Do you feel embarrassed at all, or are you completely fine with that? I think it depends.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: do* ju fil ɪmˈbɛrəst æt ɔl, ər ər ju kəmˈplitli faɪn wɪθ ðət? aɪ θɪŋk ɪt depends.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: I think if you find a topic quite quickly that you have in common,
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk ɪf ju faɪnd ə ˈtɑpɪk kwaɪt kˈwɪkli ðət ju hæv ɪn ˈkɑmən,

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: that avoids embarrassment. But it's when you... you meet someone new, you don't know them very well, obviously,
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ðət əˈvɔɪdz ɪmˈbɛrəsmənt. bət ɪts wɪn ju... ju mit ˈsəmˌwən nu, ju doʊnt noʊ ðɛm ˈvɛri wɛl, ˈɑbviəsli,

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: because they're new, and you don't know what they like or don't like, or what they think is rude or not rude.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: bɪˈkəz ðɛr nu, ənd ju doʊnt noʊ wət ðeɪ laɪk ər doʊnt laɪk, ər wət ðeɪ θɪŋk ɪz rud ər nɑt rud.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: But if you can figure that out quite quickly, it's... yeah, it's usually OK.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: bət ɪf ju kən ˈfɪgjər ðət aʊt kwaɪt kˈwɪkli, ɪts... jæ, ɪts ˈjuʒəwəli ˈoʊˈkeɪ.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: Do you usually feel quite embarrassed then? Sometimes. Like you said, it depends.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: do* ju ˈjuʒəwəli fil kwaɪt ɪmˈbɛrəst ðɛn? ˈsəmˌtaɪmz. laɪk ju sɛd, ɪt depends.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: If you find something that you have in common and you can talk about that,
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ɪf ju faɪnd ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ju hæv ɪn ˈkɑmən ənd ju kən tɔk əˈbaʊt ðət,

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: but sometimes you don't find something in common, and then it's really, I don't know, embarrassing, I suppose.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: bət ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ju doʊnt faɪnd ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪn ˈkɑmən, ənd ðɛn ɪts ˈrɪli, aɪ doʊnt noʊ, ɪmˈbɛrəsɪŋ, aɪ səˈpoʊz.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: I feel strange just thinking about it. You obviously... you like parties, you like socialising.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: aɪ fil streɪnʤ ʤɪst ˈθɪŋkɪŋ əˈbaʊt ɪt. ju ˈɑbviəsli... ju laɪk ˈpɑrtiz, ju laɪk socialising*.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: But do you like spending time alone? I get more energy from spending time alone and relaxing
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: but* du ju laɪk ˈspɛndɪŋ taɪm əˈloʊn? aɪ gɪt mɔr ˈɛnərʤi frəm ˈspɛndɪŋ taɪm əˈloʊn ənd relaxing

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: and not talking to anyone.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ənd nɑt ˈtɔkɪŋ tɪ ˈɛniˌwən.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: I often get what I call social burnout, which means you just get exhausted
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: aɪ ˈɔfən gɪt wət aɪ kɔl ˈsoʊʃəl ˈbərˌnaʊt, wɪʧ minz ju ʤɪst gɪt ɪgˈzɔstɪd

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: from spending too much time with others. How about you? Well, yeah.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: from* ˈspɛndɪŋ tu məʧ taɪm wɪθ ˈəðərz. haʊ əˈbaʊt ju? wɛl, yeah.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: Spending time alone is also important.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ˈspɛndɪŋ taɪm əˈloʊn ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: And I like to do that, but not so much.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ənd aɪ laɪk tɪ du ðət, bət nɑt soʊ məʧ.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: Not as much as maybe spending time with people, because, like I said before, if I'm alone for too long,
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: nɑt ɛz məʧ ɛz ˈmeɪbi ˈspɛndɪŋ taɪm wɪθ ˈpipəl, bɪˈkəz, laɪk aɪ sɛd ˌbiˈfɔr, ɪf əm əˈloʊn fər tu lɔŋ,

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: I get a bit lonely and feel a bit, I don't know, just lacking energy.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: aɪ gɪt ə bɪt ˈloʊnli ənd fil ə bɪt, aɪ doʊnt noʊ, ʤɪst ˈlækɪŋ ˈɛnərʤi.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: Let's recap the vocabulary we heard in this podcast. Starting with 'socialise', which means to spend time with friends
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wi hərd ɪn ðɪs ˈpɔdˌkæst. ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ 'socialise*', wɪʧ minz tɪ spɛnd taɪm wɪθ frɛndz

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: or other people to enjoy yourself. We had 'small talk', which is conversation about things which aren't important,
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ər ˈəðər ˈpipəl tɪ ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈjɔrsɛlf. wi hæd 'smɔl tɔk', wɪʧ ɪz ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən əˈbaʊt θɪŋz wɪʧ ˈɑrənt ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt,

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: such as the weather or the traffic. We use small talk a lot in the UK.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: səʧ ɛz ðə ˈwɛðər ər ðə ˈtræfɪk. wi juz smɔl tɔk ə lɔt ɪn ðə uk*.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: We heard some adjectives to describe how you might feel about meeting new people.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: wi hərd səm ˈæʤɪktɪvz tɪ dɪˈskraɪb haʊ ju maɪt fil əˈbaʊt ˈmitɪŋ nu ˈpipəl.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: So, we had 'embarrassed', which means feeling ashamed or shy.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: soʊ, wi hæd 'ɪmˈbɛrəst', wɪʧ minz ˈfilɪŋ əˈʃeɪmd ər ʃaɪ.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: And we also heard 'awkward', which means feeling uncomfortable when you're meeting new people. We also heard 'in common',
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ənd wi ˈɔlsoʊ hərd 'ˈɔkwərd', wɪʧ minz ˈfilɪŋ ənˈkəmfərtəbəl wɪn jʊr ˈmitɪŋ nu ˈpipəl. wi ˈɔlsoʊ hərd 'ɪn ˈkɑmən',

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: so if you have something in common with someone, it means you share the same likes, dislikes, or interests with someone.
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: soʊ ɪf ju hæv ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪn ˈkɑmən wɪθ ˈsəmˌwən, ɪt minz ju ʃɛr ðə seɪm laɪks, dɪsˈlaɪks, ər ˈɪntərɪsts wɪθ ˈsəmˌwən.

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: That's it for this episode of Real Easy English. Test what you've learned by downloading the free worksheet on our website,
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: ðæts ɪt fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd əv ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ. tɛst wət juv ˈlərnɪd baɪ ˈdaʊnˌloʊdɪŋ ðə fri ˈwərkˌʃit ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt,

d0GKyzG9OWA.text: bbclearningenglish.com. See you then. Bye! Bye!
d0GKyzG9OWA.ipa: bbclearningenglish.com*. si ju ðɛn. baɪ! baɪ!

QBddNulwhKo.text: Hello and welcome to Real Easy English, the podcast where we have real conversations in easy English to help you learn.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: hɛˈloʊ ənd ˈwɛlkəm tɪ ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ, ðə ˈpɔdˌkæst wɛr wi hæv ril ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz ɪn ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ tɪ hɛlp ju lərn.

QBddNulwhKo.text: I'm Georgie. And I'm Neil, and don't forget that this podcast is now available as a video on our website,
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: əm ˈʤɔrʤi. ənd əm nil, ənd doʊnt fərˈgɛt ðət ðɪs ˈpɔdˌkæst ɪz naʊ əˈveɪləbəl ɛz ə ˈvɪdioʊ ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt,

QBddNulwhKo.text: so you can watch us at bbclearningenglish.com.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: soʊ ju kən wɔʧ ˈjuˈɛs æt bbclearningenglish.com*.

QBddNulwhKo.text: Hi Neil, how are you today? Hi, Georgie. I'm very well. Actually,
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: hi* nil, haʊ ər ju təˈdeɪ? haɪ, ˈʤɔrʤi. əm ˈvɛri wɛl. actually,

QBddNulwhKo.text: I'm really happy today because the Sun is shining, and it's warm,
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: əm ˈrɪli ˈhæpi təˈdeɪ bɪˈkəz ðə sən ɪz ˈʃaɪnɪŋ, ənd ɪts wɔrm,

QBddNulwhKo.text: and it seems like it's been a long time since we had such nice sunny weather.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: ənd ɪt simz laɪk ɪts bɪn ə lɔŋ taɪm sɪns wi hæd səʧ nis ˈsəni ˈwɛðər.

QBddNulwhKo.text: I know, the changing of the seasons is so wonderful, and actually, that's what we're talking about today.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: aɪ noʊ, ðə ˈʧeɪnʤɪŋ əv ðə ˈsizənz ɪz soʊ ˈwəndərfəl, ənd ˈæˌkʧuəli, ðæts wət wɪr ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt təˈdeɪ.

QBddNulwhKo.text: Today we're going to talk about the seasons and the different types of weather we get in the UK.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: təˈdeɪ wɪr goʊɪŋ tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt ðə ˈsizənz ənd ðə ˈdɪfərənt taɪps əv ˈwɛðər wi gɪt ɪn ðə uk*.

QBddNulwhKo.text: Yes. So in the UK, we – our seasons are roughly like this.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: jɛs. soʊ ɪn ðə uk*, wi –* ɑr ˈsizənz ər ˈrəfli laɪk ðɪs.

QBddNulwhKo.text: So March, April, May – those are the spring seasons.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: soʊ mɑrʧ, ˈeɪprəl, meɪ –* ðoʊz ər ðə spərɪŋ ˈsizənz.

QBddNulwhKo.text: Then we move into summer, which is June, July and August. Autumn, that's September, October, November.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: ðɛn wi muv ˈɪntu ˈsəmər, wɪʧ ɪz ʤun, ˌʤuˈlaɪ ənd ˈɔgəst. ˈɔtəm, ðæts sɛpˈtɛmbər, ɑkˈtoʊbər, noʊˈvɛmbər.

QBddNulwhKo.text: And then we're into winter for December, January, February. Yeah. More or less. Approximately.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: ənd ðɛn wɪr ˈɪntu ˈwɪntər fər dɪˈsɛmbər, ˈʤænjuˌɛri, ˈfɛbruˌɛri. jæ. mɔr ər lɛs. əˈprɑksəmətli.

QBddNulwhKo.text: Yeah. So, Neil, what is your favourite season? Well, you can probably guess because I'm so happy
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: yeah*. soʊ, nil, wət ɪz jʊr ˈfeɪvərɪt ˈsizən? wɛl, ju kən ˈprɑbəˌbli gɛs bɪˈkəz əm soʊ ˈhæpi

QBddNulwhKo.text: that it's spring and summer combined. So anything with long late nights and evenings I love.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: ðət ɪts spərɪŋ ənd ˈsəmər kəmˈbaɪnd. soʊ ˈɛniˌθɪŋ wɪθ lɔŋ leɪt naɪts ənd ˈivnɪŋz aɪ ləv.

QBddNulwhKo.text: What else is good about summer? In the summer, you can be outside.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: what* ɛls ɪz gʊd əˈbaʊt ˈsəmər? ɪn ðə ˈsəmər, ju kən bi outside.

QBddNulwhKo.text: You can go swimming in the sea.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: ju kən goʊ sˈwɪmɪŋ ɪn ðə si.

QBddNulwhKo.text: You can stay up late and you have wonderful light in the UK.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: ju kən steɪ əp leɪt ənd ju hæv ˈwəndərfəl laɪt ɪn ðə uk*.

QBddNulwhKo.text: What's your favourite season then, Georgie? Well, I'm a big fan of the seasons, the transition seasons.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: what's* jʊr ˈfeɪvərɪt ˈsizən ðɛn, ˈʤɔrʤi? wɛl, əm ə bɪg fæn əv ðə ˈsizənz, ðə trænˈzɪʃən seasons.

QBddNulwhKo.text: So I really like spring and autumn, especially spring. Right now we're in spring in the UK,
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: soʊ aɪ ˈrɪli laɪk spərɪŋ ənd ˈɔtəm, əˈspɛʃəli spərɪŋ. raɪt naʊ wɪr ɪn spərɪŋ ɪn ðə uk*,

QBddNulwhKo.text: and you can see everyone looking happier because we've had
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: ənd ju kən si ˈɛvriˌwən ˈlʊkɪŋ ˈhæpiər bɪˈkəz wiv hæd

QBddNulwhKo.text: a really long, hard, dark, cold winter and everyone is smiling now
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: ə ˈrɪli lɔŋ, hɑrd, dɑrk, koʊld ˈwɪntər ənd ˈɛvriˌwən ɪz sˈmaɪlɪŋ naʊ

QBddNulwhKo.text: because they... there are blossoms on the trees which make me so happy. I love seeing pink blossoms on the trees.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: bɪˈkəz ðeɪ... ðɛr ər ˈblɑsəmz ɔn ðə triz wɪʧ meɪk mi soʊ ˈhæpi. aɪ ləv siɪŋ pɪŋk ˈblɑsəmz ɔn ðə triz.

QBddNulwhKo.text: The days are warmer and, yeah, there's this sense of a transition.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: ðə deɪz ər ˈwɔrmər ənd, jæ, ðɛrz ðɪs sɛns əv ə trænˈzɪʃən.

QBddNulwhKo.text: Same with autumn. Although you're leaving the summer months,
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: seɪm wɪθ ˈɔtəm. ˌɔlˈðoʊ jʊr ˈlivɪŋ ðə ˈsəmər mənθs,

QBddNulwhKo.text: I really love the colours on the trees as well.
QBddNulwhKo.ipa: aɪ ˈrɪli ləv ðə colours* ɔn ðə triz ɛz wɛl.

QBddNulwhKo.text: Especially those cooler, crisp days when the sky is blue

S7mfygW40Cs.text: And I, it seems like maybe, like it's not long enough
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: ənd aɪ, ɪt simz laɪk ˈmeɪbi, laɪk ɪts nɑt lɔŋ ɪˈnəf

S7mfygW40Cs.text: to just to go somewhere like Paris for a day, but you can do it.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: tɪ ʤɪst tɪ goʊ ˈsəmˌwɛr laɪk ˈpɛrɪs fər ə deɪ, bət ju kən du ɪt.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: And I kind of like that idea of just going from London to Paris for the day and back on the train.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: ənd aɪ kaɪnd əv laɪk ðət aɪˈdiə əv ʤɪst goʊɪŋ frəm ˈləndən tɪ ˈpɛrɪs fər ðə deɪ ənd bæk ɔn ðə treɪn.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: Sounds a bit crazy. You'd need a really early train. Really early train, really late train back and lots of energy.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: saʊnz ə bɪt ˈkreɪzi. jʊd nid ə ˈrɪli ˈərli treɪn. ˈrɪli ˈərli treɪn, ˈrɪli leɪt treɪn bæk ənd lɑts əv ˈɛnərʤi.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: Yeah. Yeah. You wouldn't need to pay for your accommodation. Exactly. Go up the Eiffel Tower, come back down, go home.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: jæ. jæ. ju ˈwʊdənt nid tɪ peɪ fər jʊr əˌkɑməˈdeɪʃən. ɪgˈzæktli. goʊ əp ðə ˈaɪfəl taʊər, kəm bæk daʊn, goʊ hoʊm.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: Perfect.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: ˈpərˌfɪkt.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: Let's recap the vocabulary we heard during the conversation.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wi hərd ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: We had 'day trip', which means visiting a place for just the day.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: wi hæd 'deɪ trɪp', wɪʧ minz ˈvɪzɪtɪŋ ə pleɪs fər ʤɪst ðə deɪ.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: We heard 'thrill seeker', which describes someone who takes part in risky and exciting activities.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: wi hərd 'θrɪl ˈsikər', wɪʧ dɪˈskraɪbz ˈsəmˌwən hu teɪks pɑrt ɪn ˈrɪski ənd ɪkˈsaɪtɪŋ ækˈtɪvɪtiz.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: 'Adventurous' means 'unusual and exciting'. A person can be adventurous, or an experience or a day trip
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: 'ædˈvɛnʧərəs' minz 'ənˈjuˌʒuəl ənd ɪkˈsaɪtɪŋ'. ə ˈpərsən kən bi ædˈvɛnʧərəs, ər ən ɪkˈspɪriəns ər ə deɪ trɪp

S7mfygW40Cs.text: can be adventurous, like Neil's trip to Paris.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: kən bi ædˈvɛnʧərəs, laɪk nilz trɪp tɪ ˈpɛrɪs.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: We used the word 'sightseeing', which describes the activity
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: wi juzd ðə wərd 'ˈsaɪtˈsiɪŋ', wɪʧ dɪˈskraɪbz ðə ækˈtɪvɪti

S7mfygW40Cs.text: where you walk around looking at famous buildings and monuments.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: wɛr ju wɔk əraʊnd ˈlʊkɪŋ æt ˈfeɪməs ˈbɪldɪŋz ənd ˈmɑnjəmənts.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: And that's it for this episode of Real Easy English.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: ənd ðæts ɪt fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd əv ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: You can test what you've learnt with the worksheet on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: ju kən tɛst wət juv lərnt wɪθ ðə ˈwərkˌʃit ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: And we'll be back with more easy conversations soon.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: ənd wɪl bi bæk wɪθ mɔr ˈizi ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz sun.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: And we want to hear from you to tell us what you want to hear us talking about.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: ənd wi wɔnt tɪ hir frəm ju tɪ tɛl ˈjuˈɛs wət ju wɔnt tɪ hir ˈjuˈɛs ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: Send an email to learningenglish@bbc.co.uk We'd love to hear from you.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: sɛnd ən iˈmeɪl tɪ learningenglish@bbc.co.uk* wid ləv tɪ hir frəm ju.

S7mfygW40Cs.text: Thanks for joining us and see you soon. Goodbye.
S7mfygW40Cs.ipa: θæŋks fər ˈʤɔɪnɪŋ ˈjuˈɛs ənd si ju sun. ˌgʊdˈbaɪ.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Hello and welcome to Real Easy English. In this podcast,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: hɛˈloʊ ənd ˈwɛlkəm tɪ ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ. ɪn ðɪs ˈpɔdˌkæst,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: we have real conversations in easy English to help you learn. I'm Neil. And I'm Beth.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: wi hæv ril ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz ɪn ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ tɪ hɛlp ju lərn. əm nil. ənd əm bɛθ.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: You can find a video version of this podcast and a free worksheet to help you learn on our website,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ju kən faɪnd ə ˈvɪdioʊ ˈvərʒən əv ðɪs ˈpɔdˌkæst ənd ə fri ˈwərkˌʃit tɪ hɛlp ju lərn ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: bbclearningenglish.com
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: bbclearningenglish.com*

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Hi Beth, how are you? I'm good, Neil. How are you? Very well, thank you. What are we talking about today?
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: hi* bɛθ, haʊ ər ju? əm gʊd, nil. haʊ ər ju? ˈvɛri wɛl, θæŋk ju. wət ər wi ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt today?

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Today we are talking about housemates.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: təˈdeɪ wi ər ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt housemates*.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Housemates – the people that you share a house with who aren't your family.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: housemates* –* ðə ˈpipəl ðət ju ʃɛr ə haʊs wɪθ hu ˈɑrənt jʊr ˈfæməli.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Maybe they're your friends, or even strangers who just need somewhere to live.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ˈmeɪbi ðɛr jʊr frɛndz, ər ˈivɪn ˈstreɪnʤərz hu ʤɪst nid ˈsəmˌwɛr tɪ lɪv.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Exactly. And it's quite common in the UK to live with housemates when you're younger.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ɪgˈzæktli. ənd ɪts kwaɪt ˈkɑmən ɪn ðə uk* tɪ lɪv wɪθ housemates* wɪn jʊr ˈjəŋgər.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Have you ever lived with housemates?
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: hæv ju ˈɛvər lɪvd wɪθ housemates?

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Yes, of course. I had house or flatmates when I was a student
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: jɛs, əv kɔrs. aɪ hæd haʊs ər flatmates* wɪn aɪ wɑz ə ˈstudənt

lHZCWPdQr80.text: and then after I was a student in my first jobs and before I got married.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ənd ðɛn ˈæftər aɪ wɑz ə ˈstudənt ɪn maɪ fərst ʤɑbz ənd ˌbiˈfɔr aɪ gɑt ˈmɛrid.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: That's exactly the same as me. So, when I went to university, I moved in with five people,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ðæts ɪgˈzæktli ðə seɪm ɛz mi. soʊ, wɪn aɪ wɛnt tɪ ˌjunəˈvərsəti, aɪ muvd ɪn wɪθ faɪv ˈpipəl,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: and we all had a separate room and a separate bathroom.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ənd wi ɔl hæd ə ˈsɛpərˌeɪt rum ənd ə ˈsɛpərˌeɪt ˈbæθˌrum.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: But yeah, they were my flatmates for my first year of university.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: bət jæ, ðeɪ wər maɪ flatmates* fər maɪ fərst jɪr əv ˌjunəˈvərsəti.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: But in my second year, then I moved into a house and we all shared a bathroom,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: bət ɪn maɪ ˈsɛkənd jɪr, ðɛn aɪ muvd ˈɪntu ə haʊs ənd wi ɔl ʃɛrd ə ˈbæθˌrum,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: and they were my housemates because we were in a house.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ənd ðeɪ wər maɪ housemates* bɪˈkəz wi wər ɪn ə haʊs.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Did you enjoy having housemates?
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: did* ju ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈhævɪŋ housemates?

lHZCWPdQr80.text: I think it really depends on the people that you live with. I have had some bad housemates.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk ɪt ˈrɪli dɪˈpɛndz ɔn ðə ˈpipəl ðət ju lɪv wɪθ. aɪ hæv hæd səm bæd housemates*.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Bad because they weren't tidy, didn't clean.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: bæd bɪˈkəz ðeɪ wərənt ˈtaɪdi, ˈdɪdənt klin.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: There were dishes in the kitchen that were dirty and not cleaned for a long time.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ðɛr wər ˈdɪʃɪz ɪn ðə ˈkɪʧən ðət wər ˈdərti ənd nɑt klind fər ə lɔŋ taɪm.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: But I've also had some really nice housemates as well.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: bət aɪv ˈɔlsoʊ hæd səm ˈrɪli nis housemates* ɛz wɛl.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Do you enjoy living with housemates or did you enjoy living with housemates?
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: do* ju ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈlɪvɪŋ wɪθ housemates* ər dɪd ju ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈlɪvɪŋ wɪθ housemates?

lHZCWPdQr80.text: I think as you said, it really depends on the person.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk ɛz ju sɛd, ɪt ˈrɪli dɪˈpɛndz ɔn ðə ˈpərsən.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: So, you mentioned people being messy and that kind of thing.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: soʊ, ju ˈmɛnʃənd ˈpipəl biɪŋ ˈmɛsi ənd ðət kaɪnd əv θɪŋ.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Also, noisy people are difficult.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ˈɔlsoʊ, ˈnɔɪzi ˈpipəl ər ˈdɪfəkəlt.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: But some of my best friends now have been my housemates in the past.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: bət səm əv maɪ bɛst frɛndz naʊ hæv bɪn maɪ housemates* ɪn ðə pæst.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: So, I think you can really get to know people very well.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: soʊ, aɪ θɪŋk ju kən ˈrɪli gɪt tɪ noʊ ˈpipəl ˈvɛri wɛl.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: And you can also meet some people who you never want to see again in your life! That's true!
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ənd ju kən ˈɔlsoʊ mit səm ˈpipəl hu ju ˈnɛvər wɔnt tɪ si əˈgɛn ɪn jʊr laɪf! ðæts tru!

lHZCWPdQr80.text: So, Neil, what do you think is the best kind of personality,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: soʊ, nil, wət du ju θɪŋk ɪz ðə bɛst kaɪnd əv ˌpərsəˈnælɪti,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: the best kind of person to live with? They have to be easygoing. What does that mean?
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: the* bɛst kaɪnd əv ˈpərsən tɪ lɪv wɪθ? ðeɪ hæv tɪ bi ˈiziˈgoʊɪŋ. wət dɪz ðət mean?

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Somebody who is easygoing is relaxed,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ˈsəmˌbɑdi hu ɪz ˈiziˈgoʊɪŋ ɪz rɪˈlækst,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: and they don't get too upset or angry over little things.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ənd ðeɪ doʊnt gɪt tu ˈəpˌsɛt ər ˈæŋgri ˈoʊvər ˈlɪtəl θɪŋz.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: And they're quite easy to live with. And there's the clue – easygoing.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ənd ðɛr kwaɪt ˈizi tɪ lɪv wɪθ. ənd ðɛrz ðə klu –* ˈiziˈgoʊɪŋ.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: I think something else important about living with other people or renting somewhere,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk ˈsəmθɪŋ ɛls ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt əˈbaʊt ˈlɪvɪŋ wɪθ ˈəðər ˈpipəl ər ˈrɛntɪŋ ˈsəmˌwɛr,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: so paying monthly, is having a good landlord. It's very important. So, who is a landlord?
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: so* peɪɪŋ ˈmənθli, ɪz ˈhævɪŋ ə gʊd ˈlænˌdlɔrd. ɪts ˈvɛri ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt. soʊ, hu ɪz ə ˈlænˌdlɔrd?

lHZCWPdQr80.text: What is a landlord?
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: what* ɪz ə landlord?

lHZCWPdQr80.text: So, the landlord is, or landlady is, the person that owns the house or flat
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: soʊ, ðə ˈlænˌdlɔrd ɪz, ər ˈlænˌdleɪdi ɪz, ðə ˈpərsən ðət oʊnz ðə haʊs ər flæt

lHZCWPdQr80.text: that you're in and you pay them rent. So, you pay them a monthly amount.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ðət jʊr ɪn ənd ju peɪ ðɛm rɛnt. soʊ, ju peɪ ðɛm ə ˈmənθli əˈmaʊnt.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: But you might live there for a year or two years or three years, but you don't own the place.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: bət ju maɪt lɪv ðɛr fər ə jɪr ər tu jɪrz ər θri jɪrz, bət ju doʊnt oʊn ðə pleɪs.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: It's the landlord that owns the place. Yeah, and a good landlord can make a big difference.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ɪts ðə ˈlænˌdlɔrd ðət oʊnz ðə pleɪs. jæ, ənd ə gʊd ˈlænˌdlɔrd kən meɪk ə bɪg ˈdɪfərəns.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Neil, are you easy to live with? Should we be flatmates?
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: neil*, ər ju ˈizi tɪ lɪv wɪθ? ʃʊd wi bi flatmates?

lHZCWPdQr80.text: I don't know, but I think I have made some good friends
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: aɪ doʊnt noʊ, bət aɪ θɪŋk aɪ hæv meɪd səm gʊd frɛndz

lHZCWPdQr80.text: when I've been a housemate. So, they think that I was a good housemate. OK. How about you?
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: when* aɪv bɪn ə housemate*. soʊ, ðeɪ θɪŋk ðət aɪ wɑz ə gʊd housemate*. ˈoʊˈkeɪ. haʊ əˈbaʊt you?

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Yeah, I think I'm easy to live with,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: jæ, aɪ θɪŋk əm ˈizi tɪ lɪv wɪθ,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: but I like to sing a lot, so maybe people would tell me to be quiet.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: bət aɪ laɪk tɪ sɪŋ ə lɔt, soʊ ˈmeɪbi ˈpipəl wʊd tɛl mi tɪ bi kwaɪət.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Although no one's ever told me to be quiet... but maybe they wanted me to and just didn't say!
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ˌɔlˈðoʊ noʊ wənz ˈɛvər toʊld mi tɪ bi kwaɪət... bət ˈmeɪbi ðeɪ ˈwɔntɪd mi tɪ ənd ʤɪst ˈdɪdənt seɪ!

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Let's recap the vocabulary we've heard in this podcast,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv hərd ɪn ðɪs ˈpɔdˌkæst,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: starting with 'housemate'. A housemate is someone you live with
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ 'housemate*'. ə housemate* ɪz ˈsəmˌwən ju lɪv wɪθ

lHZCWPdQr80.text: who probably isn't part of your family. We also hear 'flatmate'.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: hu ˈprɑbəˌbli ˈɪzənt pɑrt əv jʊr ˈfæməli. wi ˈɔlsoʊ hir 'flatmate*'.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: And we had 'easygoing', which means relaxed and not easily upset.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ənd wi hæd 'ˈiziˈgoʊɪŋ', wɪʧ minz rɪˈlækst ənd nɑt ˈizəli ˈəpˌsɛt.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: Flatmates or housemates often 'rent' a property and that means they don't own it –
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: flatmates* ər housemates* ˈɔfən 'rɛnt' ə ˈprɑpərti ənd ðət minz ðeɪ doʊnt oʊn ɪt –*

lHZCWPdQr80.text: they pay money to the person who does own it.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ðeɪ peɪ ˈməni tɪ ðə ˈpərsən hu dɪz oʊn ɪt.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: And the person who owns the house or flat is the 'landlord'.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ənd ðə ˈpərsən hu oʊnz ðə haʊs ər flæt ɪz ðə 'ˈlænˌdlɔrd'.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: That's who you pay every month and they own the property.
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ðæts hu ju peɪ ˈɛvəri mənθ ənd ðeɪ oʊn ðə ˈprɑpərti.

lHZCWPdQr80.text: That's it for this episode of Real Easy English. To test what you've learnt,
lHZCWPdQr80.ipa: ðæts ɪt fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd əv ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ. tɪ tɛst wət juv lərnt,

lHZCWPdQr80.text: there's a worksheet on our website, bbclearningenglish.com

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: Well, 'cheat' is to, kind of, lie or say something that isn't true.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: wɛl, 'ʧit' ɪz tɪ, kaɪnd əv, laɪ ər seɪ ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ˈɪzənt tru.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: For example, you might pretend that you have something and actually you don't. Cheating is seen as negative. Yes.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ju maɪt priˈtɛnd ðət ju hæv ˈsəmθɪŋ ənd ˈæˌkʧuəli ju doʊnt. ˈʧitɪŋ ɪz sin ɛz ˈnɛgətɪv. jɛs.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: It's like you're not following the rules as well. That's it, yeah. Do you like cheating?
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: it's* laɪk jʊr nɑt ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ðə rulz ɛz wɛl. ðæts ɪt, jæ. du ju laɪk cheating?

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: When I was a child, I liked to cheat. But no, because now it just feels dishonest.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: wɪn aɪ wɑz ə ʧaɪld, aɪ laɪkt tɪ ʧit. bət noʊ, bɪˈkəz naʊ ɪt ʤɪst filz dɪˈsɑnəst.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: If I... if I win a game, I want to win because I actually was good enough
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: ɪf aɪ... ɪf aɪ wɪn ə geɪm, aɪ wɔnt tɪ wɪn bɪˈkəz aɪ ˈæˌkʧuəli wɑz gʊd ɪˈnəf

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: or did well enough to win. Yes. Are you a cheat? I'm not a cheat.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: or* dɪd wɛl ɪˈnəf tɪ wɪn. jɛs. ər ju ə ʧit? əm nɑt ə cheat.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: Because I think I'm... I'm too much of a... what we call a 'goody-goody', or someone who likes to follow the rules.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: bɪˈkəz aɪ θɪŋk əm... əm tu məʧ əv ə... wət wi kɔl ə 'goody-goody*', ər ˈsəmˌwən hu laɪks tɪ ˈfɑloʊ ðə rulz.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: So, Georgie, a quiz is a type of game, I guess. Do you like quizzes?
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: so*, ˈʤɔrʤi, ə kwɪz ɪz ə taɪp əv geɪm, aɪ gɛs. du ju laɪk quizzes?

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: Yes, I also like quizzes. I used to go quite a lot to something
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: jɛs, aɪ ˈɔlsoʊ laɪk kˈwɪzɪz. aɪ juzd tɪ goʊ kwaɪt ə lɔt tɪ ˈsəmθɪŋ

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: we have a lot in the UK called a pub quiz, where you go to a pub,
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: wi hæv ə lɔt ɪn ðə uk* kɔld ə pəb kwɪz, wɛr ju goʊ tɪ ə pəb,

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: there are lots of teams and you answer questions with your team. And I used to really enjoy that because
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: ðɛr ər lɑts əv timz ənd ju ˈænsər kˈwɛsʧənz wɪθ jʊr tim. ənd aɪ juzd tɪ ˈrɪli ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ðət bɪˈkəz

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: a) you're socialising with your friends in the pub, but I find it really satisfying when you get the questions right.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: ə) jʊr socialising* wɪθ jʊr frɛndz ɪn ðə pəb, bət aɪ faɪnd ɪt ˈrɪli ˈsætɪsˌfaɪɪŋ wɪn ju gɪt ðə kˈwɛsʧənz raɪt.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: I've actually won a couple of pub quizzes. Have you? Yeah. Well done.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: i've* ˈæˌkʧuəli wən ə ˈkəpəl əv pəb kˈwɪzɪz. hæv ju? jæ. wɛl done.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: It's really hard sometimes when all of the questions are general knowledge that you don't know anything about.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: ɪts ˈrɪli hɑrd ˈsəmˌtaɪmz wɪn ɔl əv ðə kˈwɛsʧənz ər ˈʤɛnərəl ˈnɑlɪʤ ðət ju doʊnt noʊ ˈɛniˌθɪŋ əˈbaʊt.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: That's really boring. But when you know the questions, it's fun.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: ðæts ˈrɪli ˈbɔrɪŋ. bət wɪn ju noʊ ðə kˈwɛsʧənz, ɪts fən.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: Let's recap the vocabulary we had during the conversation. We had 'board games'.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wi hæd ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən. wi hæd 'bɔrd geɪmz'.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: These are games where players usually move pieces around a flat board like chess.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: ðiz ər geɪmz wɛr pleɪərz ˈjuʒəwəli muv ˈpisɪz əraʊnd ə flæt bɔrd laɪk ʧɛs.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: We had 'quiz', which is a game where you answer questions.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: wi hæd 'kwɪz', wɪʧ ɪz ə geɪm wɛr ju ˈænsər kˈwɛsʧənz.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: 'Cheat' means to be dishonest when playing a game,
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: 'ʧit' minz tɪ bi dɪˈsɑnəst wɪn pleɪɪŋ ə geɪm,

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: and you can also call a person a 'cheat' if they are dishonest in a game.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: ənd ju kən ˈɔlsoʊ kɔl ə ˈpərsən ə 'ʧit' ɪf ðeɪ ər dɪˈsɑnəst ɪn ə geɪm.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: We had 'goody-goody', which is someone who likes following rules, like me.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: wi hæd 'goody-goody*', wɪʧ ɪz ˈsəmˌwən hu laɪks ˈfɑloʊɪŋ rulz, laɪk mi.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: And you can do a 'quiz' on our website.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: ənd ju kən du ə 'kwɪz' ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: Go to bbclearningenglish.com and you'll also find a worksheet there so you can test what you've learnt. See you then.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: goʊ tɪ bbclearningenglish.com* ənd jul ˈɔlsoʊ faɪnd ə ˈwərkˌʃit ðɛr soʊ ju kən tɛst wət juv lərnt. si ju ðɛn.

oGEa-b4sn2Q.text: Bye.
oGEa-b4sn2Q.ipa: baɪ.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Hello and welcome to Real Easy English, the podcast where we have real conversations in easy English to help you learn.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: hɛˈloʊ ənd ˈwɛlkəm tɪ ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ, ðə ˈpɔdˌkæst wɛr wi hæv ril ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz ɪn ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ tɪ hɛlp ju lərn.

eK5Tsornvng.text: I'm Neil. And I'm Beth. You can find a video version of this podcast
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: əm nil. ənd əm bɛθ. ju kən faɪnd ə ˈvɪdioʊ ˈvərʒən əv ðɪs ˈpɔdˌkæst

eK5Tsornvng.text: and a worksheet to help you learn on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd ə ˈwərkˌʃit tɪ hɛlp ju lərn ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*.

eK5Tsornvng.text: How are you today, Beth? I'm OK, Neil. I'm a little bit tired. How are you? I'm very well.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: how* ər ju təˈdeɪ, bɛθ? əm ˈoʊˈkeɪ, nil. əm ə ˈlɪtəl bɪt taɪərd. haʊ ər ju? əm ˈvɛri wɛl.

eK5Tsornvng.text: I'm fine. Great. Good, good. Well, today we're going to talk about 'mistakes'.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: əm faɪn. greɪt. gʊd, gʊd. wɛl, təˈdeɪ wɪr goʊɪŋ tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt 'mɪˈsteɪks'.

eK5Tsornvng.text: So hopefully I won't make many mistakes, being tired. No. 'Mistakes' –
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: soʊ ˈhoʊpfəli aɪ woʊnt meɪk ˈmɛni mɪˈsteɪks, biɪŋ taɪərd. noʊ. 'mɪˈsteɪks' –*

eK5Tsornvng.text: 'mistakes' can be things that are wrong like a mistake in a maths problem.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: 'mɪˈsteɪks' kən bi θɪŋz ðət ər rɔŋ laɪk ə mɪˈsteɪk ɪn ə maths* ˈprɑbləm.

eK5Tsornvng.text: But we can also use 'mistake' to talk about really big things in life.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: bət wi kən ˈɔlsoʊ juz 'mɪˈsteɪk' tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt ˈrɪli bɪg θɪŋz ɪn laɪf.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Decisions that you've made, actions that you've taken. Yes, that's true. So I think let's start the conversation.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: dɪˈsɪʒənz ðət juv meɪd, ˈækʃənz ðət juv ˈteɪkən. jɛs, ðæts tru. soʊ aɪ θɪŋk lɛts stɑrt ðə ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Let's do it. So, Beth, have you made any mistakes recently?
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: let's* du ɪt. soʊ, bɛθ, hæv ju meɪd ˈɛni mɪˈsteɪks recently?

eK5Tsornvng.text: Yes. So, I recently left my suitcase on a train by mistake.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: jɛs. soʊ, aɪ ˈrisəntli lɛft maɪ ˈsutˌkeɪs ɔn ə treɪn baɪ mɪˈsteɪk.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Oh no! So, I was travelling from home to Leeds on the train.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: oʊ noʊ! soʊ, aɪ wɑz ˈtrævəlɪŋ frəm hoʊm tɪ lidz ɔn ðə treɪn.

eK5Tsornvng.text: And I got to the station at Leeds, and there were loads of people, and they were...
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd aɪ gɑt tɪ ðə ˈsteɪʃən æt lidz, ənd ðɛr wər loʊdz əv ˈpipəl, ənd ðeɪ wər...

eK5Tsornvng.text: they were football fans and they were singing really loudly. And I just wanted to get off the train.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ðeɪ wər ˈfʊtˌbɔl fænz ənd ðeɪ wər ˈsɪŋɪŋ ˈrɪli ˈlaʊdli. ənd aɪ ʤɪst ˈwɔntɪd tɪ gɪt ɔf ðə treɪn.

eK5Tsornvng.text: And that's what I did. And then I realised about half an hour later that I didn't have my suitcase.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd ðæts wət aɪ dɪd. ənd ðɛn aɪ ˈriəˌlaɪzd əˈbaʊt hæf ən aʊər ˈleɪtər ðət aɪ ˈdɪdənt hæv maɪ ˈsutˌkeɪs.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Oh no! So... That's a bad mistake. Did you get it back? I did, I was so lucky.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: oʊ noʊ! soʊ... ðæts ə bæd mɪˈsteɪk. dɪd ju gɪt ɪt bæk? aɪ dɪd, aɪ wɑz soʊ lucky.

eK5Tsornvng.text: The train stopped at Leeds.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ðə treɪn stɑpt æt lidz.

eK5Tsornvng.text: So, when I realised I ran and said to the people on the platform, "Arrgh, my suitcase is on the train!"
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: soʊ, wɪn aɪ ˈriəˌlaɪzd aɪ ræn ənd sɛd tɪ ðə ˈpipəl ɔn ðə ˈplætˌfɔrm, "arrgh*, maɪ ˈsutˌkeɪs ɪz ɔn ðə treɪn!"

eK5Tsornvng.text: And they held the train for me and I ran up the platform.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd ðeɪ hɛld ðə treɪn fər mi ənd aɪ ræn əp ðə ˈplætˌfɔrm.

eK5Tsornvng.text: I grabbed my suitcase and then they blew the whistle and the train went. I was so lucky.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: aɪ græbd maɪ ˈsutˌkeɪs ənd ðɛn ðeɪ blu ðə ˈwɪsəl ənd ðə treɪn wɛnt. aɪ wɑz soʊ ˈləki.

eK5Tsornvng.text: But yeah, that was a big mistake. And I will not make that mistake again. Everybody says that.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: bət jæ, ðət wɑz ə bɪg mɪˈsteɪk. ənd aɪ wɪl nɑt meɪk ðət mɪˈsteɪk əˈgɛn. ˈɛvriˌbɑdi sɪz ðət.

eK5Tsornvng.text: So, earlier we said that mistakes can be small things, like my suitcase.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: soʊ, ˈərliər wi sɛd ðət mɪˈsteɪks kən bi smɔl θɪŋz, laɪk maɪ ˈsutˌkeɪs.

eK5Tsornvng.text: But there are big mistakes that we can make. So, have you ever made a big mistake, do you think?
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: but* ðɛr ər bɪg mɪˈsteɪks ðət wi kən meɪk. soʊ, hæv ju ˈɛvər meɪd ə bɪg mɪˈsteɪk, du ju think?

eK5Tsornvng.text: Yeah. Well, once I had a job that I really, really enjoyed.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: jæ. wɛl, wəns aɪ hæd ə ʤɑb ðət aɪ ˈrɪli, ˈrɪli ˌɛnˈʤɔɪd.

eK5Tsornvng.text: I was teaching at a school in Prague, in the Czech Republic, teaching English,
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: aɪ wɑz ˈtiʧɪŋ æt ə skul ɪn prɑg, ɪn ðə ʧɛk riˈpəblɪk, ˈtiʧɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ,

eK5Tsornvng.text: and I loved the job and I had loads of really good friends.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd aɪ ləvd ðə ʤɑb ənd aɪ hæd loʊdz əv ˈrɪli gʊd frɛndz.

eK5Tsornvng.text: But I had this feeling that I needed to go – move somewhere else, do something else with my life.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: bət aɪ hæd ðɪs ˈfilɪŋ ðət aɪ ˈnidɪd tɪ goʊ –* muv ˈsəmˌwɛr ɛls, du ˈsəmθɪŋ ɛls wɪθ maɪ laɪf.

eK5Tsornvng.text: And so I resigned. But then about a week or so later, I realised
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd soʊ aɪ rɪˈzaɪnd. bət ðɛn əˈbaʊt ə wik ər soʊ ˈleɪtər, aɪ ˈriəˌlaɪzd

eK5Tsornvng.text: that I really, really didn't want to leave that school.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ðət aɪ ˈrɪli, ˈrɪli ˈdɪdənt wɔnt tɪ liv ðət skul.

eK5Tsornvng.text: So, I went back to the boss and I said, "I've changed my mind. I want to stay."
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: soʊ, aɪ wɛnt bæk tɪ ðə bɔs ənd aɪ sɛd, "aɪv ʧeɪnʤd maɪ maɪnd. aɪ wɔnt tɪ steɪ."

eK5Tsornvng.text: And he said, "Well, it's too late because I've given your job to somebody else." Oh no! That's so sad.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd hi sɛd, "wɛl, ɪts tu leɪt bɪˈkəz aɪv ˈgɪvɪn jʊr ʤɑb tɪ ˈsəmˌbɑdi ɛls." oʊ noʊ! ðæts soʊ sæd.

eK5Tsornvng.text: So, you left your job, left Prague? No, actually, a bit like your suitcase, Beth,
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: so*, ju lɛft jʊr ʤɑb, lɛft prɑg? noʊ, ˈæˌkʧuəli, ə bɪt laɪk jʊr ˈsutˌkeɪs, beth,

eK5Tsornvng.text: it ended up being OK because the person who took my job then decided that he didn't want the job either.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ɪt ˈɛndɪd əp biɪŋ ˈoʊˈkeɪ bɪˈkəz ðə ˈpərsən hu tʊk maɪ ʤɑb ðɛn ˌdɪˈsaɪdɪd ðət hi ˈdɪdənt wɔnt ðə ʤɑb ˈiðər.

eK5Tsornvng.text: So, in the end, I did stay.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: soʊ, ɪn ðə ɛnd, aɪ dɪd steɪ.

eK5Tsornvng.text: So, when we make mistakes, Neil, sometimes we then have a regret
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: soʊ, wɪn wi meɪk mɪˈsteɪks, nil, ˈsəmˌtaɪmz wi ðɛn hæv ə rɪˈgrɛt

eK5Tsornvng.text: or we regret the thing that we did. That means that we feel bad about it.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ər wi rɪˈgrɛt ðə θɪŋ ðət wi dɪd. ðət minz ðət wi fil bæd əˈbaʊt ɪt.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Or we wish that we'd done something differently. Do you have any regrets?
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: or* wi wɪʃ ðət wid dən ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈdɪfərˈɛntli. du ju hæv ˈɛni regrets?

eK5Tsornvng.text: Yeah. When I was a kid, I guess I was probably 12 or 13.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: jæ. wɪn aɪ wɑz ə kɪd, aɪ gɛs aɪ wɑz ˈprɑbəˌbli 12 ər 13

eK5Tsornvng.text: I had some piano lessons, and I quit as soon as I could.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: aɪ hæd səm piˈænə ˈlɛsənz, ənd aɪ kwɪt ɛz sun ɛz aɪ kʊd.

eK5Tsornvng.text: My parents gave me that choice. And I regret that because I love music and I play guitar,
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: maɪ ˈpɛrənts geɪv mi ðət ʧɔɪs. ənd aɪ rɪˈgrɛt ðət bɪˈkəz aɪ ləv mˈjuzɪk ənd aɪ pleɪ gɪˈtɑr,

eK5Tsornvng.text: and I think I would have enjoyed playing piano.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd aɪ θɪŋk aɪ wʊd hæv ˌɛnˈʤɔɪd pleɪɪŋ piˈænə.

eK5Tsornvng.text: I always think, to be honest, you shouldn't have any regrets, because whatever you've done, you've learnt something from it.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: aɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz θɪŋk, tɪ bi ˈɑnəst, ju ˈʃʊdənt hæv ˈɛni rɪˈgrɛts, bɪˈkəz ˌwəˈtɛvər juv dən, juv lərnt ˈsəmθɪŋ frəm ɪt.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Even if something bad happened, then you know for next time.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ˈivɪn ɪf ˈsəmθɪŋ bæd ˈhæpənd, ðɛn ju noʊ fər nɛkst taɪm.

eK5Tsornvng.text: And it's a kind of self-improvement thing. That is a very positive attitude to mistakes, Beth.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd ɪts ə kaɪnd əv ˈsɛlfɪmˈpruvmənt θɪŋ. ðət ɪz ə ˈvɛri ˈpɑzətɪv ˈætəˌtud tɪ mɪˈsteɪks, bɛθ.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Yeah, I think it's important to learn from your mistakes.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: jæ, aɪ θɪŋk ɪts ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt tɪ lərn frəm jʊr mɪˈsteɪks.

eK5Tsornvng.text: And that's a common expression in English – to learn from your mistakes.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd ðæts ə ˈkɑmən ɪkˈsprɛʃən ɪn ˈɪŋlɪʃ –* tɪ lərn frəm jʊr mɪˈsteɪks.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Let's recap the vocabulary we've learnt in this podcast.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv lərnt ɪn ðɪs ˈpɔdˌkæst.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Starting with 'mistake', and a mistake is an action that is wrong. Or that you do when you don't mean to.

eK5Tsornvng.text: And we can 'make a mistake'. That means we do something wrong.

eK5Tsornvng.text: Or we can do something 'by mistake', which means we do something accidentally, without meaning to.
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ər wi kən du ˈsəmθɪŋ 'baɪ mɪˈsteɪk', wɪʧ minz wi du ˈsəmθɪŋ ˌæksəˈdɛnəli, wɪˈθaʊt ˈminɪŋ tɪ.

eK5Tsornvng.text: For example, I left my suitcase on the train by mistake.

eK5Tsornvng.text: We also heard 'regret', which means feel bad or sorry about something you've done or a mistake that you've made.

eK5Tsornvng.text: And we heard 'learn from your mistakes', which means remember the mistake
eK5Tsornvng.ipa: ənd wi hərd 'lərn frəm jʊr mɪˈsteɪks', wɪʧ minz rɪˈmɛmbər ðə mɪˈsteɪk

2x3rJMBVB28.text: You might get to that finished product the day that you write it,

2x3rJMBVB28.text: and then there's other songs that might take you months or even years. And I don’t think either of those is wrong.

2x3rJMBVB28.text: It just literally depends on how it comes to you and where your inspiration arrives from.
2x3rJMBVB28.ipa: ɪt ʤɪst ˈlɪtərəli dɪˈpɛndz ɔn haʊ ɪt kəmz tɪ ju ənd wɛr jʊr ˌɪnspərˈeɪʃən əraɪvz frəm.

2x3rJMBVB28.text: For me, it’s just recognizing that it’s not always going to go exactly the way I want.

2x3rJMBVB28.text: And that even those “dud songs,” are the ones that don’t get finished,

2x3rJMBVB28.text: And I think it’s those great songs that you end up getting,

2x3rJMBVB28.text: You will eventually get to the songs that you really want if you just continue to work on it.

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: How can you shape the future? At TED, we believe it's by shaping the future of ideas.
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: haʊ kən ju ʃeɪp ðə fˈjuʧər? æt tɛd, wi bɪˈliv ɪts baɪ ˈʃeɪpɪŋ ðə fˈjuʧər əv aɪˈdiəz.

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: And that future is in the hands of young people, sharing their vision to make an impact.
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: ənd ðət fˈjuʧər ɪz ɪn ðə hænz əv jəŋ ˈpipəl, ˈʃɛrɪŋ ðɛr ˈvɪʒən tɪ meɪk ən ˌɪmˈpækt.

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: That's why we created TED-Ed Student Talks,
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: ðæts waɪ wi kriˈeɪtɪd ted-ed* ˈstudənt tɔks,

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: empowering over a million students to develop and share their ideas with the world.
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: ɪmˈpaʊərɪŋ ˈoʊvər ə ˈmɪljən ˈstudənts tɪ dɪˈvɛləp ənd ʃɛr ðɛr aɪˈdiəz wɪθ ðə wərld.

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: And now we're taking it further: proudly announcing TED Summer School,
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: ənd naʊ wɪr ˈteɪkɪŋ ɪt ˈfərðər: ˈpraʊdli əˈnaʊnsɪŋ tɛd ˈsəmər skul,

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: a transformative journey guiding curious young minds to become the thought leaders of tomorrow.
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: ə ˌtrænsˈfɔrmɑˌtɪv ˈʤərni ˈgaɪdɪŋ ˈkjʊriəs jəŋ maɪndz tɪ bɪˈkəm ðə θɔt ˈlidərz əv təˈmɑˌroʊ.

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: Ambitious students from around the world will work with TED-trained tutors
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: æmˈbɪʃəs ˈstudənts frəm əraʊnd ðə wərld wɪl wərk wɪθ ted-trained* ˈtutərz

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: to develop original ideas and gain expert storytelling skills, culminating in a standout moment
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: tɪ dɪˈvɛləp ərˈɪʤənəl aɪˈdiəz ənd geɪn ˈɛkspərt ˈstɔriˌtɛlɪŋ skɪlz, ˈkəlməˌneɪtɪŋ ɪn ə ˈstænˌdaʊt ˈmoʊmənt

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: showcasing the power and potential of your voice.
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: ˈʃoʊkeɪsɪŋ ðə paʊər ənd pəˈtɛnʃəl əv jʊr vɔɪs.

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: We're partnering with Immerse Education, a trusted leader in academic enrichment,
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: wɪr ˈpɑrtnərɪŋ wɪθ ˌɪˈmərs ˌɛʤəˈkeɪʃən, ə ˈtrəstɪd ˈlidər ɪn ˌækəˈdɛmɪk ɪnˈrɪʧmənt,

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: to offer a safe, supportive experience, in person around the world,
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: tɪ ˈɔfər ə seɪf, səˈpɔrtɪv ɪkˈspɪriəns, ɪn ˈpərsən əraʊnd ðə wərld,

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: and virtually with the same live, small group mentorship— 100% remote.

mnLVpkkQVRU.text: We're empowering young thinkers to share their ideas with clarity, purpose, and impact.
mnLVpkkQVRU.ipa: wɪr ɪmˈpaʊərɪŋ jəŋ ˈθɪŋkərz tɪ ʃɛr ðɛr aɪˈdiəz wɪθ ˈklɛrɪti, ˈpərpəs, ənd ˌɪmˈpækt.

ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ənd ðə ˈhɛvi spərɪŋ reɪnz ər ˈsæʧərˌeɪtɪŋ ðə ərθ.

ngarNTPMclE.text: In a home near the center of town, a basement wall collapses under the pressure,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ɪn ə hoʊm nɪr ðə ˈsɛnər əv taʊn, ə ˈbeɪsmənt wɔl kəˈlæpsɪz ˈəndər ðə ˈprɛʃər,

ngarNTPMclE.text: releasing a flood of decomposing corpses— remains from the neighboring Cemetery of the Innocents.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: riˈlisɪŋ ə fləd əv ˌdikəmˈpoʊzɪŋ corpses—*— rɪˈmeɪnz frəm ðə ˈneɪbərɪŋ ˈsɛmɪˌtɛri əv ðə ˈɪnəsənts.

ngarNTPMclE.text: Rumors spread that everyone in the house got sick due to bad air emanating from the decaying flesh.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈrumərz sprɛd ðət ˈɛvriˌwən ɪn ðə haʊs gɑt sɪk du tɪ bæd ɛr ˈɛməˌneɪtɪŋ frəm ðə dɪˈkeɪɪŋ flɛʃ.

ngarNTPMclE.text: With the overcrowded cemetery housing generations of dead Parisians,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: wɪθ ðə ˈoʊvərˌkraʊdɪd ˈsɛmɪˌtɛri ˈhaʊzɪŋ ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃənz əv dɛd pərˈɪsiənz,

ngarNTPMclE.text: there was a growing worry that the entire city was in grave danger of falling ill.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðɛr wɑz ə groʊɪŋ ˈwəri ðət ðə ɪnˈtaɪər ˈsɪti wɑz ɪn greɪv ˈdeɪnʤər əv ˈfɑlɪŋ ɪl.

ngarNTPMclE.text: For centuries, Parisians had buried their dead in the Innocents, the city’s largest cemetery.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: fər ˈsɛnʧəriz, pərˈɪsiənz hæd ˈbɛrid ðɛr dɛd ɪn ðə ˈɪnəsənts, ðə city’s* ˈlɑrʤəst ˈsɛmɪˌtɛri.

ngarNTPMclE.text: While cemeteries across Europe were originally placed outside of urban areas, in the 9th century,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: waɪl ˈsɛməˌtɛriz əˈkrɔs ˈjʊrəp wər ərˈɪʤənəli pleɪst ˈaʊtˈsaɪd əv ˈərbən ˈɛriəz, ɪn ðə 9th* ˈsɛnʧəri,

ngarNTPMclE.text: the Church began allowing burials directly on its grounds.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðə ʧərʧ bɪˈgæn əˈlaʊɪŋ ˈbɛriəlz dɪˈrɛkli ɔn ɪts graʊnz.

ngarNTPMclE.text: As these urban parish cemeteries filled, some churches started creating bone chapels,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ɛz ðiz ˈərbən ˈpɛrɪʃ ˈsɛməˌtɛriz fɪld, səm ˈʧərʧɪz ˈstɑrtɪd kriˈeɪtɪŋ boʊn chapels*,

ngarNTPMclE.text: like the Sedlec Ossuary in the Czech Republic, to make room for new burials.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: laɪk ðə sedlec* ossuary* ɪn ðə ʧɛk riˈpəblɪk, tɪ meɪk rum fər nu ˈbɛriəlz.

ngarNTPMclE.text: By the 18th century, public opinion on urban cemeteries shifted,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: baɪ ðə 18th* ˈsɛnʧəri, ˈpəblɪk əˈpɪnjən ɔn ˈərbən ˈsɛməˌtɛriz ˈʃɪftɪd,

ngarNTPMclE.text: as Enlightenment thinkers and physicians promoted new scientific ideas that linked hygiene to health.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ɛz ˌɛnˈlaɪtənmənt ˈθɪŋkərz ənd fɪˈzɪʃənz prəˈmoʊtəd nu ˌsaɪənˈtɪfɪk aɪˈdiəz ðət lɪŋkt ˈhaɪˌʤin tɪ hɛlθ.

ngarNTPMclE.text: They didn't yet understand the concept of germs,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðeɪ ˈdɪdənt jɛt ˌəndərˈstænd ðə ˈkɑnsɛpt əv ʤərmz,

ngarNTPMclE.text: believing instead that disease spread through “miasma,” or bad air.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: bɪˈlivɪŋ ˌɪnˈstɛd ðət dɪˈziz sprɛd θru ““miasma,”*,” ər bæd ɛr.

ngarNTPMclE.text: Consequently, overcrowded cemeteries spewing cadaverous odors
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈkɑnsəkˌwɛntli, ˈoʊvərˌkraʊdɪd ˈsɛməˌtɛriz spjuɪŋ cadaverous* ˈoʊdərz

ngarNTPMclE.text: were cast as public health threats.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: wər kæst ɛz ˈpəblɪk hɛlθ θrɛts.

ngarNTPMclE.text: Even after the Innocents and other cemeteries closed in the early 1780s,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈivɪn ˈæftər ðə ˈɪnəsənts ənd ˈəðər ˈsɛməˌtɛriz kloʊzd ɪn ðə ˈərli 1780s*,

ngarNTPMclE.text: residents continued to worry about the foul smells and petitioned the government for a long-term solution.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈrɛzɪdənts kənˈtɪnjud tɪ ˈwəri əˈbaʊt ðə faʊl smɛlz ənd pəˈtɪʃənd ðə ˈgəvərnmənt fər ə ˈlɔŋˈtərm səˈluʃən.

ngarNTPMclE.text: But where could they move the remains of the millions of Parisians?
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: bət wɛr kʊd ðeɪ muv ðə rɪˈmeɪnz əv ðə ˈmɪljənz əv pərˈɪsiənz?

ngarNTPMclE.text: The vast abandoned network of quarries beneath the city offered a logical solution.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðə væst əˈbændənd ˈnɛtˌwərk əv kˈwɔriz bɪˈniθ ðə ˈsɪti ˈɔfərd ə ˈlɑʤɪkəl səˈluʃən.

ngarNTPMclE.text: Dating back to Roman times, the mines had provided the limestone and plaster to build Paris.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈdeɪtɪŋ bæk tɪ ˈroʊmən taɪmz, ðə maɪnz hæd prəˈvaɪdɪd ðə ˈlaɪmˌstoʊn ənd ˈplæstər tɪ bɪld ˈpɛrɪs.

ngarNTPMclE.text: But by the 18th century, they had begun collapsing under the city’s weight, creating deadly sinkholes.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: bət baɪ ðə 18th* ˈsɛnʧəri, ðeɪ hæd ˈbeɪgən kəˈlæpsɪŋ ˈəndər ðə city’s* weɪt, kriˈeɪtɪŋ ˈdɛdli sinkholes*.

ngarNTPMclE.text: So in 1777, King Louis XVI established a team of engineers
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: soʊ ɪn 1777 kɪŋ luɪs xvi* ɪˈstæblɪʃt ə tim əv ˈɛnʤəˈnɪrz

ngarNTPMclE.text: to survey and reinforce the area.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: tɪ ˈsərˌveɪ ənd ˌriɪnˈfɔrs ðə ˈɛriə.

ngarNTPMclE.text: And in 1785, Charles-Axel Guillaumot, the Inspector General of Quarries,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ənd ɪn 1785 charles-axel* guillaumot*, ðə ˌɪnˈspɛktər ˈʤɛnərəl əv kˈwɔriz,

ngarNTPMclE.text: was tasked with turning sections of the unused tunnels into a massive crypt.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: wɑz tæskt wɪθ ˈtərnɪŋ ˈsɛkʃənz əv ðə ənˈjuzd ˈtənəlz ˈɪntu ə ˈmæsɪv krɪpt.

ngarNTPMclE.text: The Catholic Church initially resisted, as it stood to lose income from burials and cemetery maintenance fees,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðə ˈkæθlɪk ʧərʧ ˌɪˈnɪʃəli rɪˈzɪstɪd, ɛz ɪt stʊd tɪ luz ˈɪnˌkəm frəm ˈbɛriəlz ənd ˈsɛmɪˌtɛri ˈmeɪntənəns fiz,

ngarNTPMclE.text: but public health concerns prevailed.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: bət ˈpəblɪk hɛlθ kənˈsərnz prɪˈveɪld.

ngarNTPMclE.text: The transfer of human remains was done mostly at night,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðə ˈtrænsfər əv ˈjumən rɪˈmeɪnz wɑz dən ˈmoʊstli æt naɪt,

ngarNTPMclE.text: to avoid upsetting passersby and to limit public exposure to the miasma.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: tɪ əˈvɔɪd əpˈsɛtɪŋ ˈpæsərzbi ənd tɪ ˈlɪmət ˈpəblɪk ɪkˈspoʊʒər tɪ ðə miˈæzmə.

ngarNTPMclE.text: Millions of anonymous bones were dug up by hand,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈmɪljənz əv əˈnɑnəməs boʊnz wər dəg əp baɪ hænd,

ngarNTPMclE.text: carted across Paris, and dumped unceremoniously in the Catacombs.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈkɑrtɪd əˈkrɔs ˈpɛrɪs, ənd dəmpt ˌənˌsɛrəˈmoʊniəsli ɪn ðə ˈkætəˌkoʊmz.

ngarNTPMclE.text: It was a distinctly modern project, focused on efficiency rather than memorialization.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ɪt wɑz ə dɪˈstɪŋktli ˈmɑdərn ˈprɑʤɛkt, ˈfoʊkɪst ɔn ɪˈfɪʃənsi ˈrəðər ðən memorialization*.

ngarNTPMclE.text: The Cemetery of the Innocents was the largest source, though bones were soon brought from cemeteries throughout the city.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðə ˈsɛmɪˌtɛri əv ðə ˈɪnəsənts wɑz ðə ˈlɑrʤəst sɔrs, ðoʊ boʊnz wər sun brɔt frəm ˈsɛməˌtɛriz θruaʊt ðə ˈsɪti.

ngarNTPMclE.text: The French Revolution temporarily stalled relocation efforts.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðə frɛnʧ ˌrɛvəˈluʃən ˌtɛmpərˈɛrəli stɔld ˌriˈloʊˈkeɪʃən ˈɛfərts.

ngarNTPMclE.text: Though the Catacombs did offer a space where inconvenient bodies could disappear. Some of the over 1,000 prisoners
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðoʊ ðə ˈkætəˌkoʊmz dɪd ˈɔfər ə speɪs wɛr ˌɪnkənˈvinjənt ˈbɑdiz kʊd ˌdɪsəˈpɪr. səm əv ðə ˈoʊvər 1,000* ˈprɪzənərz

ngarNTPMclE.text: executed in the Revolution’s September Massacres of 1792 were hastily cast there.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈɛksəˌkjutɪd ɪn ðə revolution’s* sɛpˈtɛmbər ˈmæsɪkərz əv 1792 wər ˈheɪstəli kæst ðɛr.

ngarNTPMclE.text: However, the Catacombs weren’t generally used for new burials.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ðə ˈkætəˌkoʊmz weren’t* ˈʤɛnərəli juzd fər nu ˈbɛriəlz.

ngarNTPMclE.text: Instead, the newly dead were interred in garden cemeteries
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˌɪnˈstɛd, ðə ˈnuli dɛd wər ˌɪnˈtərd ɪn ˈgɑrdən ˈsɛməˌtɛriz

ngarNTPMclE.text: established, once again, on the city’s outskirts.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ɪˈstæblɪʃt, wəns əˈgɛn, ɔn ðə city’s* ˈaʊtˌskərts.

ngarNTPMclE.text: By the end of the Revolution, the relocation project had sprung back to life.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: baɪ ðə ɛnd əv ðə ˌrɛvəˈluʃən, ðə ˌriˈloʊˈkeɪʃən ˈprɑʤɛkt hæd sprəŋ bæk tɪ laɪf.

ngarNTPMclE.text: And in 1809, Napoleon Bonaparte, aware of the unifying power of monuments,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ənd ɪn 1809 nəˈpoʊljən ˈboʊnəˌpɑrt, əˈwɛr əv ðə ˈjunəˌfaɪɪŋ paʊər əv ˈmɑnjəmənts,

ngarNTPMclE.text: ordered that a small section of the chaotic space be curated and open to the public.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈɔrdərd ðət ə smɔl ˈsɛkʃən əv ðə keɪˈɑtɪk speɪs bi curated* ənd ˈoʊpən tɪ ðə ˈpəblɪk.

ngarNTPMclE.text: Piles of bones were pushed to the sides, facades of skulls and femurs were artfully arranged,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: paɪlz əv boʊnz wər pʊʃt tɪ ðə saɪdz, fəˈsɑdz əv skəlz ənd femurs* wər ˈɑrtfəli əreɪnʤd,

ngarNTPMclE.text: and quotes about the fleeting nature of life were hung on the walls.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ənd kwoʊts əˈbaʊt ðə ˈflitɪŋ ˈneɪʧər əv laɪf wər həŋ ɔn ðə wɔlz.

ngarNTPMclE.text: The rest of the Catacombs— housing the remains of an estimated 6 million Parisians—
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðə rɛst əv ðə catacombs—*— ˈhaʊzɪŋ ðə rɪˈmeɪnz əv ən ˈɛstəˌmeɪtɪd 6 ˈmɪljən parisians—*—

ngarNTPMclE.text: was, and largely remains, untouched.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: wɑz, ənd ˈlɑrʤli rɪˈmeɪnz, ənˈtəʧt.

ngarNTPMclE.text: After its beautification, the Catacombs became a hugely popular tourist destination.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈæftər ɪts beautification*, ðə ˈkætəˌkoʊmz bɪˈkeɪm ə ˈhjuʤli ˈpɑpjələr ˈtʊrɪst ˌdɛstɪˈneɪʃən.

ngarNTPMclE.text: While signs indicated the cemetery where the bones were moved from,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: waɪl saɪnz ˈɪndəˌkeɪtɪd ðə ˈsɛmɪˌtɛri wɛr ðə boʊnz wər muvd frəm,

ngarNTPMclE.text: the remains themselves were anonymous—
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðə rɪˈmeɪnz ðɛmˈsɛlvz wər anonymous—*—

ngarNTPMclE.text: aristocrats lay next to laborers, raiders next to defenders,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ərˈɪstəˌkræts leɪ nɛkst tɪ ˈleɪbərərz, ˈreɪdərz nɛkst tɪ dɪˈfɛndərz,

ngarNTPMclE.text: the young next to their elders.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ðə jəŋ nɛkst tɪ ðɛr ˈɛldərz.

ngarNTPMclE.text: And so, the site became a symbol
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ənd soʊ, ðə saɪt bɪˈkeɪm ə ˈsɪmbəl

ngarNTPMclE.text: of what one 19th-century photographer called the “confused equality of death.”
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: əv wət wən 19th-century* fəˈtɑgrəfər kɔld ðə ““confused* ɪkˈwɑləti əv death.”*.”

ngarNTPMclE.text: Sources vary, but some say the last remains were moved as recently as the 1930s.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈsɔrsəz ˈvɛri, bət səm seɪ ðə læst rɪˈmeɪnz wər muvd ɛz ˈrisəntli ɛz ðə 1930s*.

ngarNTPMclE.text: Today, people from around the world continue to visit the Catacombs,
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: təˈdeɪ, ˈpipəl frəm əraʊnd ðə wərld kənˈtɪnju tɪ ˈvɪzɪt ðə ˈkætəˌkoʊmz,

ngarNTPMclE.text: finding amidst its caverns a haunting reminder
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: ˈfaɪndɪŋ əˈmɪdst ɪts ˈkævərnz ə ˈhɔntɪŋ riˈmaɪndər

ngarNTPMclE.text: of our shared, inevitable future.
ngarNTPMclE.ipa: əv ɑr ʃɛrd, ˌɪˈnɛvətəbəl fˈjuʧər.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Of the many, many things we’re not supposed to snack on,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: əv ðə ˈmɛni, ˈmɛni θɪŋz we’re* nɑt səˈpoʊzd tɪ snæk ɔn,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: perhaps the most common forbidden food is silica gel.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: pərˈhæps ðə moʊst ˈkɑmən ˈfɔrbɪdən fud ɪz ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: This tiny packet pops up in packaging for everything—
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðɪs ˈtaɪni ˈpækɪt pɑps əp ɪn ˈpækɪʤɪŋ fər everything—*—

QWCHnR53oIw.text: from TVs and guitar cases to sunscreen and snack foods. So why is silica gel everywhere,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: frəm ˈtiˈviz ənd gɪˈtɑr ˈkeɪsɪz tɪ ˈsənskrin ənd snæk fudz. soʊ waɪ ɪz ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl ˈɛvriˌwɛr,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: and what would actually happen if you ate it?
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ənd wət wʊd ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈhæpən ɪf ju eɪt ɪt?

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Found in the Earth’s crust, silica, also called silicon dioxide,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: faʊnd ɪn ðə earth’s* krəst, ˈsɪlɪkə, ˈɔlsoʊ kɔld ˈsɪləkən daɪˈɑkˌsaɪd,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: is typically mined as raw silica sand, which is then washed, dried, sifted, and chemically treated.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ɪz ˈtɪpɪkəli maɪnd ɛz rɑ ˈsɪlɪkə sænd, wɪʧ ɪz ðɛn wɑʃt, draɪd, ˈsɪftɪd, ənd ˈkɛmɪkli ˈtritɪd.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Once manufacturers ensure each grain is uniform, the processed sand gets mixed with sodium carbonate,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: wəns ˌmænjəˈfækʧərərz ɪnˈʃʊr iʧ greɪn ɪz ˈjunəˌfɔrm, ðə ˈprɑsɛst sænd gɪts mɪkst wɪθ ˈsoʊdiəm ˈkɑrbəˌneɪt,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: then heated and combined with an acid that links the silica particles into a jelly-like matrix.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðɛn ˈhitɪd ənd kəmˈbaɪnd wɪθ ən ˈæsəd ðət lɪŋks ðə ˈsɪlɪkə ˈpɑrtɪkəlz ˈɪntu ə jelly-like* ˈmeɪtrɪks.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: This matrix gives the material its porous structure,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðɪs ˈmeɪtrɪks gɪvz ðə məˈtɪriəl ɪts ˈpɔrəs ˈstrəkʧər,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: which stays stable after being washed and dried into hard, glassy beads.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: wɪʧ steɪz ˈsteɪbəl ˈæftər biɪŋ wɑʃt ənd draɪd ˈɪntu hɑrd, ˈglæsi bidz.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: The final product is chemically identical to the sand you might find on a beach—
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðə ˈfaɪnəl ˈprɑdəkt ɪz ˈkɛmɪkli aɪˈdɛntɪkəl tɪ ðə sænd ju maɪt faɪnd ɔn ə beach—*—

QWCHnR53oIw.text: the only difference is that porous structure.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðə ˈoʊnli ˈdɪfərəns ɪz ðət ˈpɔrəs ˈstrəkʧər.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Inside each silica gel bead is a massive microscopic network of tunnels.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ˌɪnˈsaɪd iʧ ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl bid ɪz ə ˈmæsɪv ˌmaɪkrəˈskɑpɪk ˈnɛtˌwərk əv ˈtənəlz.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: This dramatically increases the surface area of each bead, with a single gram of silica
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðɪs drəˈmætɪkəli ˌɪnˈkrisɪz ðə ˈsərfəs ˈɛriə əv iʧ bid, wɪθ ə ˈsɪŋgəl græm əv ˈsɪlɪkə

QWCHnR53oIw.text: offering over 700 square meters of surface area.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ˈɔfərɪŋ ˈoʊvər 700 skwɛr ˈmitərz əv ˈsərfəs ˈɛriə.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: But this space doesn’t stay empty for long.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: bət ðɪs speɪs doesn’t* steɪ ˈɛmti fər lɔŋ.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: The silicon dioxide molecules that make up the gel are held together by polar covalent bonds,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðə ˈsɪləkən daɪˈɑkˌsaɪd ˈmɑləˌkjulz ðət meɪk əp ðə ʤɛl ər hɛld təˈgɛðər baɪ ˈpoʊlər covalent* bɑndz,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: which attract other polar substances, including water.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: wɪʧ əˈtrækt ˈəðər ˈpoʊlər ˈsəbstənsɪz, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ˈwɔtər.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: So when silica gel is placed in a humid environment, the water in the air is drawn to the beads’ surface.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: soʊ wɪn ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl ɪz pleɪst ɪn ə ˈjumɪd ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt, ðə ˈwɔtər ɪn ðə ɛr ɪz drɔn tɪ ðə beads’*’ ˈsərfəs.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: From these surface pores, the water vapor is then pulled inside the grain
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: frəm ðiz ˈsərfəs pɔrz, ðə ˈwɔtər ˈveɪpər ɪz ðɛn pʊld ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə greɪn

QWCHnR53oIw.text: through a process called capillary condensation.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: θru ə ˈprɔˌsɛs kɔld ˈkæpəˌlɛri ˌkɑndənˈseɪʃən.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Each grain can hold up to 40% of its own weight in water,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: iʧ greɪn kən hoʊld əp tɪ 40 əv ɪts oʊn weɪt ɪn ˈwɔtər,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: allowing a standard 5-gram packet to hold roughly 2 grams of liquid.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: əˈlaʊɪŋ ə ˈstændərd 5-gram* ˈpækɪt tɪ hoʊld ˈrəfli 2 græmz əv ˈlɪkwɪd.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: And since this liquid is held inside the bead’s internal network,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ənd sɪns ðɪs ˈlɪkwɪd ɪz hɛld ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə bead’s* ˌɪnˈtərnəl ˈnɛtˌwərk,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: even fully saturated silica doesn't feel wet to the touch.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ˈivɪn ˈfʊli ˈsæʧərˌeɪtɪd ˈsɪlɪkə ˈdəzənt fil wɛt tɪ ðə təʧ.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: This ability makes silica gel a desiccant, a type of substance used to keep things dry.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðɪs əˈbɪləˌti meɪks ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl ə desiccant*, ə taɪp əv ˈsəbstəns juzd tɪ kip θɪŋz draɪ.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: But these beads can suck up more than just water.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: bət ðiz bidz kən sək əp mɔr ðən ʤɪst ˈwɔtər.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Silica attracts ammonia, sulfur dioxide, and a handful of other gases,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ˈsɪlɪkə əˈtrækts əˈmoʊnjə, ˈsəlfər daɪˈɑkˌsaɪd, ənd ə ˈhændˌfʊl əv ˈəðər ˈgæsɪz,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: making it useful in gas masks and other filtration systems.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ˈmeɪkɪŋ ɪt ˈjusfəl ɪn gæs mæsks ənd ˈəðər fɪlˈtreɪʃən ˈsɪstəmz.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: The pace of this process depends on the environment.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðə peɪs əv ðɪs ˈprɔˌsɛs dɪˈpɛndz ɔn ðə ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: In a very humid enclosed space, a packet could reach its saturation point within a day or two,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ɪn ə ˈvɛri ˈjumɪd ɪnˈkloʊzd speɪs, ə ˈpækɪt kʊd riʧ ɪts ˌsæʧərˈeɪʃən pɔɪnt wɪˈθɪn ə deɪ ər tu,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: but this could take up to a week in a moderately humid environment.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: bət ðɪs kʊd teɪk əp tɪ ə wik ɪn ə ˈmɑdərətli ˈjumɪd ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Either way, silica gel beads functionally last forever since you can dry them out in a hot oven.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ˈiðər weɪ, ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl bidz ˈfəŋkʃənəli læst fərˈɛvər sɪns ju kən draɪ ðɛm aʊt ɪn ə hɑt ˈəvən.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: These abilities and silica’s prevalence in Earth’s crust have made it the planet’s most popular commercial desiccant
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðiz əˈbɪləˌtiz ənd silica’s* ˈprɛvələns ɪn earth’s* krəst hæv meɪd ɪt ðə planet’s* moʊst ˈpɑpjələr kəˈmərʃəl desiccant*

QWCHnR53oIw.text: since we started mass producing it in 1919. At home, it keeps snacks crispy and extends their shelf life,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: sɪns wi ˈstɑrtɪd mæs prəˈdusɪŋ ɪt ɪn 1919 æt hoʊm, ɪt kips snæks ˈkrɪspi ənd ɪkˈstɛndz ðɛr ʃɛlf laɪf,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: ensures pills and vitamins stay dry,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ɪnˈʃʊrz pɪlz ənd ˈvaɪtəmənz steɪ draɪ,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: and captures any stinky scents from your cat’s litter box.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ənd ˈkæpʧərz ˈɛni ˈstɪŋki sɛnts frəm jʊr cat’s* ˈlɪtər bɑks.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Museums use silica gel in exhibit cases to prevent metal artifacts from rusting
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: mˈjuziəmz juz ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl ɪn ɪgˈzɪbɪt ˈkeɪsɪz tɪ prɪˈvɛnt ˈmɛtəl ˈɑrtəˌfækts frəm ˈrəstɪŋ

QWCHnR53oIw.text: and preserved animals from growing mold.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ənd prɪˈzərvd ˈænəməlz frəm groʊɪŋ moʊld.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: And in outer space, a form of silica gel called aerogel
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ənd ɪn ˈaʊtər speɪs, ə fɔrm əv ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl kɔld aerogel*

QWCHnR53oIw.text: is used to insulate rovers from extreme temperatures
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ɪz juzd tɪ ˈɪnsəˌleɪt ˈroʊvərz frəm ɪkˈstrim ˈtɛmpərəʧərz

QWCHnR53oIw.text: and capture high-velocity comet dust for study back home.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ənd ˈkæpʧər high-velocity* ˈkɑmət dəst fər ˈstədi bæk hoʊm.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: But what about what silica gel would do to you if you successfully swallowed a packet?
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: bət wət əˈbaʊt wət ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl wʊd du tɪ ju ɪf ju səkˈsɛsfəli sˈwɑloʊd ə ˈpækɪt?

QWCHnR53oIw.text: The answer is... not much. Those “Do Not Eat” warnings have more to do with packets being choking hazards
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðə ˈænsər ɪz... nɑt məʧ. ðoʊz ““do* nɑt eat”*” ˈwɔrnɪŋz hæv mɔr tɪ du wɪθ ˈpækɪts biɪŋ ˈʧoʊkɪŋ ˈhæzərdz

QWCHnR53oIw.text: than any silica-specific concerns.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðən ˈɛni silica-specific* kənˈsərnz.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Besides, your body has so much liquid in it— even if the packet did suck up the standard 2 grams,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ˌbiˈsaɪdz, jʊr ˈbɑdi həz soʊ məʧ ˈlɪkwɪd ɪn it—*— ˈivɪn ɪf ðə ˈpækɪt dɪd sək əp ðə ˈstændərd 2 græmz,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: you could replenish that much and more with a single glass of water. And since silica is chemically inert,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ju kʊd riˈplɛnɪʃ ðət məʧ ənd mɔr wɪθ ə ˈsɪŋgəl glæs əv ˈwɔtər. ənd sɪns ˈsɪlɪkə ɪz ˈkɛmɪkli ˌɪˈnərt,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: your body won’t break the gel down into something toxic.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: jʊr ˈbɑdi won’t* breɪk ðə ʤɛl daʊn ˈɪntu ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈtɑksɪk.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: That said, downing larger quantities of silica gel would be unpleasant.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ðət sɛd, ˈdaʊnɪŋ ˈlɑrʤər kˈwɑntətiz əv ˈsɪlɪkə ʤɛl wʊd bi ənˈplɛzənt.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Its moisture-wicking properties could dry and irritate mucous membranes along the digestive tract,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ɪts moisture-wicking* ˈprɑpərtiz kʊd draɪ ənd ˈɪrɪˌteɪt mˈjukəs ˈmɛmˌbreɪnz əˈlɔŋ ðə daɪˈʤɛstɪv trækt,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: resulting in diarrhea and an upset stomach.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: rɪˈzəltɪŋ ɪn ˌdaɪərˈiə ənd ən ˈəpˌsɛt ˈstəmək.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: Worse still, some silica is coated with cobalt chloride to indicate moisture levels,
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: wərs stɪl, səm ˈsɪlɪkə ɪz ˈkoʊtɪd wɪθ ˈkoʊˌbɔlt ˈklɔraɪd tɪ ˈɪndəˌkeɪt ˈmɔɪsʧər ˈlɛvəlz,

QWCHnR53oIw.text: and that compound actually is toxic.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: ənd ðət ˈkɑmpaʊnd ˈæˌkʧuəli ɪz ˈtɑksɪk.

QWCHnR53oIw.text: But assuming your silica snack is of the standard variety, you should survive the ordeal.
QWCHnR53oIw.ipa: bət əˈsumɪŋ jʊr ˈsɪlɪkə snæk ɪz əv ðə ˈstændərd vərˈaɪəti, ju ʃʊd sərˈvaɪv ðə ɔrˈdil.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Somewhere in medieval Switzerland, a weary traveler is relieved to see a monastery up ahead.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ˈsəmˌwɛr ɪn mɪˈdjivəl sˈwɪtsərlənd, ə ˈwɪri ˈtrævələr ɪz rɪˈlivd tɪ si ə ˈmɑnəˌstɛri əp əˈhɛd.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: He’s welcomed, guided to a crowded guesthouse,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: he’s* ˈwɛlkəmd, ˈgaɪdɪd tɪ ə ˈkraʊdɪd ˈgɛˌsthaʊs,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: and promptly served the abbey’s specialty: a tall glass of beer.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ənd ˈprɑmptli sərvd ðə abbey’s* ˈspɛʃəlti: ə tɔl glæs əv bɪr.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: For centuries, monasteries across modern-day Europe were the primary large-scale brewers of beer.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: fər ˈsɛnʧəriz, ˈmɑnəˌstɛriz əˈkrɔs modern-day* ˈjʊrəp wər ðə ˈpraɪˌmɛri ˈlɑrʤˌskeɪl bruərz əv bɪr.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: But this is hardly where this drink’s journey began.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: bət ðɪs ɪz ˈhɑrdli wɛr ðɪs drink’s* ˈʤərni bɪˈgæn.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Beer refers to any alcoholic beverage made from fermented cereal grains—
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: bɪr rɪˈfərz tɪ ˈɛni ˌælkəˈhɑlɪk ˈbɛvərɪʤ meɪd frəm fərˈmɛntəd ˈsɪriəl grains—*—

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: the edible seeds of a particular group of grasses.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðə ˈɛdəbəl sidz əv ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr grup əv ˈgræsɪz.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: The oldest evidence of beer brewing dates back 13,000 years to what’s now modern-day Israel,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðə ˈoʊldəst ˈɛvədəns əv bɪr bruɪŋ deɪts bæk 13,000* jɪrz tɪ what’s* naʊ modern-day* ˈɪzriəl,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: but experts believe the drink developed independently across the world.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: bət ˈɛkspərts bɪˈliv ðə drɪŋk dɪˈvɛləpt ˌɪndɪˈpɛndəntli əˈkrɔs ðə wərld.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: In each of these regions, its composition was shaped by native grains.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ɪn iʧ əv ðiz ˈriʤənz, ɪts ˌkɑmpəˈzɪʃən wɑz ʃeɪpt baɪ ˈneɪtɪv greɪnz.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: For example, 9,000 years ago in eastern China,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, 9,000* jɪrz əˈgoʊ ɪn ˈistərn ˈʧaɪnə,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: people drank a sweet, cloudy beer made from rice, millet, and tubers.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ˈpipəl dræŋk ə swit, ˈklaʊdi bɪr meɪd frəm raɪs, ˈmɪlət, ənd tubers*.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: In the Andean region of South America, 5,000-year-old pottery suggests production of chicha,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ɪn ðə ˈændiən ˈriʤən əv saʊθ əˈmɛrɪkə, 5,000-year-old* ˈpɑtəri səˈʤɛsts pərˈdəkʃən əv chicha*,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: a brew of fermented maize.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ə bru əv fərˈmɛntəd meɪz.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Whatever the ingredients, ancient beer brewing required two main steps.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ˌwəˈtɛvər ðə ˌɪnˈgridiənts, ˈeɪnʧənt bɪr bruɪŋ rikˈwaɪərd tu meɪn stɛps.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: First, the starches in the cereal grains had to be converted into fermentable sugars.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: fərst, ðə ˈstɑrʧɪz ɪn ðə ˈsɪriəl greɪnz hæd tɪ bi kənˈvərtɪd ˈɪntu fermentable* ˈʃʊgərz.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Some groups achieved this by chewing them, while others soaked them in water to trigger germination,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: səm grups əˈʧivd ðɪs baɪ ʧuɪŋ ðɛm, waɪl ˈəðərz soʊkt ðɛm ɪn ˈwɔtər tɪ ˈtrɪgər ˌʤərməˈneɪʃən,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: a process that naturally breaks down starches.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ə ˈprɔˌsɛs ðət ˈnæʧərəli breɪks daʊn ˈstɑrʧɪz.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: The second step was fermentation.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðə ˈsɛkənd stɛp wɑz ˌfərmənˈteɪʃən.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Unbeknownst to brewers at the time, wild yeast found its way into the mixture—
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ənbiˈnoʊnst tɪ bruərz æt ðə taɪm, waɪld jist faʊnd ɪts weɪ ˈɪntu ðə mixture—*—

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: carried through the air, on fruit, or via reused tools
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ˈkɛrid θru ðə ɛr, ɔn frut, ər ˈviə riuzd tulz

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: still harboring yeasts from prior brews.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: stɪl ˈhɑrbərɪŋ jists frəm praɪər bruz.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: With time and storage, yeasts converted some of these sugars into alcohol while releasing carbon dioxide.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: wɪθ taɪm ənd ˈstɔrɪʤ, jists kənˈvərtɪd səm əv ðiz ˈʃʊgərz ˈɪntu ˈælkəˌhɑl waɪl riˈlisɪŋ ˈkɑrbən daɪˈɑkˌsaɪd.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: This ancient beer was often imperfectly filtered,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðɪs ˈeɪnʧənt bɪr wɑz ˈɔfən ˌɪmˈpərfɪktli ˈfɪltərd,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: so it contained much more fiber, B vitamins, and protein than modern beer.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: soʊ ɪt kənˈteɪnd məʧ mɔr ˈfaɪbər, bi ˈvaɪtəmənz, ənd ˈproʊˌtin ðən ˈmɑdərn bɪr.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: And some cultures boiled the initial sugar mixture, which killed many unwanted microorganisms,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ənd səm ˈkəlʧərz bɔɪld ðə ˌɪˈnɪʃəl ˈʃʊgər ˈmɪksʧər, wɪʧ kɪld ˈmɛni ənˈwɔntɪd ˌmaɪkroʊˈɔrgəˌnɪzəmz,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: making it safer to drink than other available water sources.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ˈmeɪkɪŋ ɪt ˈseɪfər tɪ drɪŋk ðən ˈəðər əˈveɪləbəl ˈwɔtər ˈsɔrsəz.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: The oldest surviving recipe describing this process
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðə ˈoʊldəst sərˈvaɪvɪŋ ˈrɛsəpi dɪˈskraɪbɪŋ ðɪs ˈprɔˌsɛs

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: appears in a Sumerian poem dedicated to their goddess of beer, Ninkasi.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: əˈpɪrz ɪn ə sumerian* poʊəm ˈdɛdəkeɪtəd tɪ ðɛr ˈgɑdəs əv bɪr, ninkasi*.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: The Babylonians built on this adoration—
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðə ˌbæbəˈloʊniənz bɪlt ɔn ðɪs adoration—*—

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Hammurabi’s code mandated daily beer rations for every citizen.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: hammurabi’s* koʊd ˈmænˌdeɪtɪd ˈdeɪli bɪr ˈræʃənz fər ˈɛvəri ˈsɪtɪzən.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: And any brewer caught overcharging for a pint was condemned to death by drowning.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ənd ˈɛni bruər kɔt ˌoʊvərˈʧɑrʤɪŋ fər ə paɪnt wɑz kənˈdɛmd tɪ dɛθ baɪ ˈdraʊnɪŋ.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: In Ancient Egypt, beer was primarily made in the home by women, and was a staple of the daily diet.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ɪn ˈeɪnʧənt ˈiʤɪpt, bɪr wɑz praɪˈmɛrəli meɪd ɪn ðə hoʊm baɪ ˈwɪmən, ənd wɑz ə ˈsteɪpəl əv ðə ˈdeɪli daɪət.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: It also played a central role in religious festivals,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ɪt ˈɔlsoʊ pleɪd ə ˈsɛntrəl roʊl ɪn rɪˈlɪʤəs ˈfɛstɪvəlz,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: and served as an offering at temples and the tombs of pharaohs.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ənd sərvd ɛz ən ˈɔfərɪŋ æt ˈtɛmpəlz ənd ðə tumz əv ˈfɛroʊz.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: By the 20th century BCE, beer had spilled over from Egypt into Greece,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: baɪ ðə 20th* ˈsɛnʧəri bce*, bɪr hæd spɪld ˈoʊvər frəm ˈiʤɪpt ˈɪntu gris,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: though it wasn't as popular as wine.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðoʊ ɪt ˈwəzənt ɛz ˈpɑpjələr ɛz waɪn.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Nevertheless, over the following centuries, a thirst for beer spread through Europe and beyond.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ˌnɛvərðəˈlɛs, ˈoʊvər ðə ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ˈsɛnʧəriz, ə θərst fər bɪr sprɛd θru ˈjʊrəp ənd bɪɔnd.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: In the early Middle Ages, European monasteries pioneered large-scale beer production,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ɪn ðə ˈərli ˈmɪdəl ˈeɪʤɪz, ˌjʊrəˈpiən ˈmɑnəˌstɛriz ˌpaɪəˈnɪrd ˈlɑrʤˌskeɪl bɪr pərˈdəkʃən,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: both to provide to traveling pilgrims and to sell to support monastery upkeep.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: boʊθ tɪ prəˈvaɪd tɪ ˈtrævəlɪŋ ˈpɪlgrəmz ənd tɪ sɛl tɪ səˈpɔrt ˈmɑnəˌstɛri ˈəpˌkip.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Around this same time,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: əraʊnd ðɪs seɪm taɪm,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: brewers from Central and Eastern Europe made a key change to their recipe: they began adding hops.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: bruərz frəm ˈsɛntrəl ənd ˈistərn ˈjʊrəp meɪd ə ki ʧeɪnʤ tɪ ðɛr ˈrɛsəpi: ðeɪ bɪˈgæn ˈædɪŋ hɑps.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: These green flowers are rich in acidic compounds,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðiz grin flaʊərz ər rɪʧ ɪn əˈsɪdɪk ˈkɑmpaʊndz,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: imbuing beer with a new bitter flavor and acting as a preservative.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: imbuing* bɪr wɪθ ə nu ˈbɪtər ˈfleɪvər ənd ˈæktɪŋ ɛz ə priˈzərvətɪv.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Hops became so closely associated with beer that in 1516,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: hɑps bɪˈkeɪm soʊ ˈkloʊsli əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪtəd wɪθ bɪr ðət ɪn 1516

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: a Bavarian duke issued a beer purity law, known as the Reinheitsgebot,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ə bəˈvɛriən duk ˈɪʃud ə bɪr ˈpjʊrɪti lɔ, noʊn ɛz ðə reinheitsgebot*,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: that defined beer’s composition as strictly barley, hops, and water.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðət dɪˈfaɪnd beer’s* ˌkɑmpəˈzɪʃən ɛz ˈstrɪktli ˈbɑrli, hɑps, ənd ˈwɔtər.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: The Industrial Revolution ushered in other essential beer innovations, including advances in refrigeration.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðə ˌɪnˈdəstriəl ˌrɛvəˈluʃən ˈəʃərd ɪn ˈəðər ɛˈsɛnʃəl bɪr ˌɪnəˈveɪʃənz, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ədˈvænsɪz ɪn rɪˌfrɪʤərˈeɪʃən.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: In 1876, French chemist Louis Pasteur identified yeast’s role in beer fermentation.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ɪn 1876 frɛnʧ ˈkɛmɪst luɪs pəˈstur aɪˈdɛntəˌfaɪd yeast’s* roʊl ɪn bɪr ˌfərmənˈteɪʃən.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: With this, a key beer ingredient was finally made visible.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: wɪθ ðɪs, ə ki bɪr ˌɪnˈgridiənt wɑz ˈfaɪnəli meɪd ˈvɪzəbəl.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Brewers began selecting for different yeasts to refine recipes and experiment with flavor.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: bruərz bɪˈgæn səˈlɛktɪŋ fər ˈdɪfərənt jists tɪ rɪˈfaɪn ˈrɛsəpiz ənd ɪkˈspɛrəmənt wɪθ ˈfleɪvər.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Today, there are well over 100 distinct beer varieties,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: təˈdeɪ, ðɛr ər wɛl ˈoʊvər 100 dɪˈstɪŋkt bɪr vərˈaɪətiz,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: with lagers and ales being the two main types.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: wɪθ lagers* ənd eɪlz biɪŋ ðə tu meɪn taɪps.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Lagers, which include pilsners and bocks, are fermented with yeasts that thrive at colder temperatures
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: lagers*, wɪʧ ˌɪnˈklud pilsners* ənd bocks*, ər fərˈmɛntəd wɪθ jists ðət θraɪv æt ˈkoʊldər ˈtɛmpərəʧərz

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: and live at the bottom of fermentation vessels.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ənd lɪv æt ðə ˈbɑtəm əv ˌfərmənˈteɪʃən ˈvɛsəlz.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Ales, like porters, stouts, and pale ales, ferment quicker, with yeasts that live at higher temperatures
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: eɪlz, laɪk ˈpɔrtərz, stouts*, ənd peɪl eɪlz, ˈfərmɛnt kˈwɪkər, wɪθ jists ðət lɪv æt haɪər ˈtɛmpərəʧərz

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: and float at the top of the mixture.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ənd floʊt æt ðə tɔp əv ðə ˈmɪksʧər.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: But some beers break this mold.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: bət səm bɪrz breɪk ðɪs moʊld.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Kölsches, for example, start with ale yeast, but are finished at colder temperatures like lagers.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: kölsches*, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, stɑrt wɪθ eɪl jist, bət ər ˈfɪnɪʃt æt ˈkoʊldər ˈtɛmpərəʧərz laɪk lagers*.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Others draw on ancient methods, like Belgian Lambics,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ˈəðərz drɔ ɔn ˈeɪnʧənt ˈmɛθədz, laɪk ˈbɛlʤən lambics*,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: which rely on spontaneous fermentation with wild yeast.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: wɪʧ rɪˈlaɪ ɔn spɑnˈteɪniəs ˌfərmənˈteɪʃən wɪθ waɪld jist.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: Meanwhile, there are still new challenges to navigate.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ˈminˌwaɪl, ðɛr ər stɪl nu ˈʧælənʤɪz tɪ ˈnævəˌgeɪt.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: The growing demand for non-alcoholic beers has pushed producers
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ðə groʊɪŋ dɪˈmænd fər ˌnɑˌnælkəˈhɑlɪk bɪrz həz pʊʃt prəˈdusərz

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: to find ways to limit or remove alcohol while still preserving flavor.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: tɪ faɪnd weɪz tɪ ˈlɪmət ər riˈmuv ˈælkəˌhɑl waɪl stɪl prɪˈzərvɪŋ ˈfleɪvər.

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: And if history is any indication,
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: ənd ɪf ˈhɪstəri ɪz ˈɛni ˌɪndəˈkeɪʃən,

D98UJl6Ea6o.text: beer's story will continue brewing for many years to come.
D98UJl6Ea6o.ipa: birz ˈstɔri wɪl kənˈtɪnju bruɪŋ fər ˈmɛni jɪrz tɪ kəm.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: In the late 1950s, Hong Kong citizens flocked to newsstands
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ɪn ðə leɪt 1950s*, hɔŋ kɔŋg ˈsɪtɪzənz flɑkt tɪ ˈnuzˌstændz

NEFmWclWiOw.text: to pick up the latest edition of the Commercial Daily.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: tɪ pɪk əp ðə ˈleɪtəst ɪˈdɪʃən əv ðə kəˈmərʃəl ˈdeɪli.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: But not all readers were looking for timely news. Many were following a story set hundreds of years in the past—
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: bət nɑt ɔl ˈridərz wər ˈlʊkɪŋ fər ˈtaɪmli nuz. ˈmɛni wər ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ə ˈstɔri sɛt ˈhənərdz əv jɪrz ɪn ðə past—*—

NEFmWclWiOw.text: a tale of adventure and war, romance and intrigue, brotherhood and betrayal.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ə teɪl əv ədˈvɛnʧər ənd wɔr, ˈroʊmæns ənd ˌɪnˈtrig, ˈbrəðərˌhʊd ənd bɪˈtreɪəl.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Serialized from 1957 to 1959, Jin Yong’s “Legend of the Condor Heroes”
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ˈsɪˌriəˌlaɪzd frəm 1957 tɪ 1959 ʤɪn yong’s* ““legend* əv ðə ˈkɑndər heroes”*”

NEFmWclWiOw.text: is the first book in Jin’s beloved Condor Trilogy.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ɪz ðə fərst bʊk ɪn jin’s* bɪˈləvəd ˈkɑndər ˈtrɪləʤi.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: This multigenerational epic is considered one of the most important works of Chinese fiction—
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðɪs multigenerational* ˈɛpɪk ɪz kənˈsɪdərd wən əv ðə moʊst ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt wərks əv ʧaɪˈniz fiction—*—

NEFmWclWiOw.text: and one of the finest wuxia stories ever written.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ənd wən əv ðə ˈfaɪnəst wuxia* ˈstɔriz ˈɛvər ˈrɪtən.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Wuxia, roughly translated as “martial hero,”
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: wuxia*, ˈrəfli trænzˈleɪtəd ɛz ““martial* hero,”*,”

NEFmWclWiOw.text: is a popular genre of Chinese fiction with roots in folktales over 1,500 years old.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ɪz ə ˈpɑpjələr ˈʒɑnrə əv ʧaɪˈniz ˈfɪkʃən wɪθ ruts ɪn folktales* ˈoʊvər 1,500* jɪrz oʊld.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Wuxia stories generally focus on nomadic martial artists bound to an honorable code of chivalry.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: wuxia* ˈstɔriz ˈʤɛnərəli ˈfoʊkɪs ɔn noʊˈmædɪk ˈmɑrʃəl ˈɑrtɪsts baʊnd tɪ ən ˈɑnərəbəl koʊd əv ˈʃɪvəlri.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: These heroes travel through a mythologized version of ancient China called jianghu,
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðiz ˈhɪroʊz ˈtrævəl θru ə mythologized* ˈvərʒən əv ˈeɪnʧənt ˈʧaɪnə kɔld jianghu*,

NEFmWclWiOw.text: full of powerful leaders, roaming bandits, and mystical artifacts.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: fʊl əv ˈpaʊərfəl ˈlidərz, ˈroʊmɪŋ ˈbændəts, ənd ˈmɪstɪkəl ˈɑrtəˌfækts.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: To face these challenges, wuxia heroes must learn superhuman feats of martial arts
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: tɪ feɪs ðiz ˈʧælənʤɪz, wuxia* ˈhɪroʊz məst lərn ˌsupərˈhjumən fits əv ˈmɑrʃəl ɑrts

NEFmWclWiOw.text: from ancient scrolls and reclusive immortal masters.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: frəm ˈeɪnʧənt skroʊlz ənd rɪˈklusɪv ˌɪˈmɔrtəl ˈmæstərz.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: “Legend of the Condor Heroes” blends this martial arts action with pivotal Chinese history.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ““legend* əv ðə ˈkɑndər heroes”*” blɛndz ðɪs ˈmɑrʃəl ɑrts ˈækʃən wɪθ ˈpɪvətəl ʧaɪˈniz ˈhɪstəri.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: The book is set in the 13th century, amidst a century-long war between the Jurchen Jin dynasty
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðə bʊk ɪz sɛt ɪn ðə 13th* ˈsɛnʧəri, əˈmɪdst ə century-long* wɔr bɪtˈwin ðə jurchen* ʤɪn ˈdaɪnəsti

NEFmWclWiOw.text: and the Han Chinese Song Dynasty.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ənd ðə hɑn ʧaɪˈniz sɔŋ ˈdaɪnəsti.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Roughly 70 years into the conflict, a new enemy has joined the fray: Genghis Khan.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ˈrəfli 70 jɪrz ˈɪntu ðə ˈkɑnflɪkt, ə nu ˈɛnəmi həz ʤɔɪnd ðə freɪ: ˈʤɛŋhɪs kɑn.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: The Mongols threaten to topple both empires, and raids by all parties wreak havoc across the continent.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðə ˈmɑŋgəlz θˈrɛtən tɪ ˈtɑpəl boʊθ ˈɛmpaɪərz, ənd reɪdz baɪ ɔl ˈpɑrtiz rik ˈhævək əˈkrɔs ðə ˈkɑntənənt.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: The novel begins in a Han village, where two expecting fathers are binding their unborn children as blood brothers.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðə ˈnɑvəl bɪˈgɪnz ɪn ə hɑn ˈvɪlɪʤ, wɛr tu ɪkˈspɛktɪŋ ˈfɑðərz ər ˈbaɪndɪŋ ðɛr ˈənˈbɔrn ˈʧɪldrən ɛz bləd ˈbrəðərz.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: The children’s names— Guo Jing and Yang Kang— were engraved on a pair of daggers.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðə children’s* names—*— guo* ʤɪŋ ənd jæŋ kang—*— wər ɪnˈgreɪvd ɔn ə pɛr əv ˈdægərz.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: But a sudden Jurchen raid scatters the families.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: bət ə ˈsədən jurchen* reɪd scatters* ðə ˈfæməliz.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Guo Jing grows up amongst the Mongols, yet remains loyal to his Han heritage.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: guo* ʤɪŋ groʊz əp əˈməŋst ðə ˈmɑŋgəlz, jɛt rɪˈmeɪnz lɔɪəl tɪ hɪz hɑn ˈhɛrɪtɪʤ.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Meanwhile, Jurchen royalty raise Yang Kang as a scheming prince— setting the stage for the brothers’ tragic conflict.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ˈminˌwaɪl, jurchen* ˈrɔɪəlti reɪz jæŋ kɑŋ ɛz ə ˈskimɪŋ prince—*— ˈsɛtɪŋ ðə steɪʤ fər ðə brothers’*’ ˈtræʤɪk ˈkɑnflɪkt.
