NEFmWclWiOw.text: Jin’s novel follows the naive but kind-hearted Guo Jing
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: jin’s* ˈnɑvəl ˈfɑloʊz ðə naɪiv bət kind-hearted* guo* ʤɪŋ

NEFmWclWiOw.text: and his clever lover Huang Rong.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ənd hɪz ˈklɛvər ˈləvər hwɑŋ rɔŋ.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Their journey takes them to the bustling Song capital of Hangzhou,
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðɛr ˈʤərni teɪks ðɛm tɪ ðə ˈbəsəlɪŋ sɔŋ ˈkæpɪtəl əv hangzhou*,

NEFmWclWiOw.text: the harsh Mongol steppes, and the fabled market city of Samarkand.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðə hɑrʃ ˈmɑŋgəl stɛps, ənd ðə ˈfeɪbəld ˈmɑrkɪt ˈsɪti əv ˈsæmɑrˌkænd.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Our heroes traverse through jianghu, encountering colorful characters, and witnessing key historical events.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ɑr ˈhɪroʊz ˈtrævərs θru jianghu*, ɛnˈkaʊnərɪŋ ˈkələrfəl ˈkɛrɪktərz, ənd ˈwɪtnəsɪŋ ki hɪˈstɔrɪkəl ɪˈvɛnts.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: As they battle enemies, discover treasure, and help solve local crimes, their martial arts skills grow more legendary—
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ɛz ðeɪ ˈbætəl ˈɛnəmiz, dɪˈskəvər ˈtrɛʒər, ənd hɛlp sɑlv ˈloʊkəl kraɪmz, ðɛr ˈmɑrʃəl ɑrts skɪlz groʊ mɔr legendary—*—

NEFmWclWiOw.text: preparing Guo Jing for his fated confrontation.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: pərˈpɛrɪŋ guo* ʤɪŋ fər hɪz ˈfeɪtɪd ˌkɑnfrənˈteɪʃən.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: The novel showcases over 80 unique styles of martial arts,
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðə ˈnɑvəl ˈʃoʊˌkeɪsɪz ˈoʊvər 80 juˈnik staɪlz əv ˈmɑrʃəl ɑrts,

NEFmWclWiOw.text: ranging from realistic techniques to almost mystical maneuvers.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ˈreɪnʤɪŋ frəm ˌriəˈlɪstɪk tɛkˈniks tɪ ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˈmɪstɪkəl məˈnuvərz.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: But even seemingly simple moves have surprising depth.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: bət ˈivɪn ˈsimɪŋli ˈsɪmpəl muvz hæv səˈpraɪzɪŋ dɛpθ.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: To master a technique that will allow him to perform two attacks at once,
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: tɪ ˈmæstər ə tɛkˈnik ðət wɪl əˈlaʊ ɪm tɪ pərˈfɔrm tu əˈtæks æt wəns,

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Guo Jing must first learn to simultaneously draw a circle with one hand and a square with the other.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: guo* ʤɪŋ məst fərst lərn tɪ ˌsaɪməlˈteɪniəsli drɔ ə ˈsərkəl wɪθ wən hænd ənd ə skwɛr wɪθ ðə ˈəðər.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: This kind of deceptively difficult tutelage torments wuxia students and readers alike.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ðɪs kaɪnd əv dɪˈsɛptɪvli ˈdɪfəkəlt ˈtjutɪlɪʤ ˈtɔrˌmɛnts wuxia* ˈstudənts ənd ˈridərz əˈlaɪk.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Jin Yong was the pen name of Louis Cha, a journalist and founder of Ming Pao Daily News.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ʤɪn jɔŋ wɑz ðə pɛn neɪm əv luɪs ʧɑ, ə ˈʤərnəlɪst ənd ˈfaʊndər əv mɪŋ paʊ ˈdeɪli nuz.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: In the 1960s and 70s, wuxia was banned for its feudal associations.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ɪn ðə 1960s* ənd 70s*, wuxia* wɑz bænd fər ɪts fˈjudəl əˌsoʊʃiˈeɪʃənz.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: But in Hong Kong, a British colony at the time, Cha could write fiction and cover news about the Cultural Revolution freely.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: bət ɪn hɔŋ kɔŋg, ə ˈbrɪtɪʃ ˈkɑləni æt ðə taɪm, ʧɑ kʊd raɪt ˈfɪkʃən ənd ˈkəvər nuz əˈbaʊt ðə ˈkəlʧərəl ˌrɛvəˈluʃən ˈfrili.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: He even worked to add depth and complexity to a genre often considered formulaic and lowbrow.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: hi ˈivɪn wərkt tɪ æd dɛpθ ənd kəmˈplɛksɪti tɪ ə ˈʒɑnrə ˈɔfən kənˈsɪdərd ˌfɔrmjəˈleɪɪk ənd lowbrow*.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: “Legend of the Condor Heroes” celebrates Chinese identity by exploring China’s culture and history with intimate humanity.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ““legend* əv ðə ˈkɑndər heroes”*” ˈsɛləˌbreɪts ʧaɪˈniz aɪˈdɛntəˌti baɪ ɪkˈsplɔrɪŋ china’s* ˈkəlʧər ənd ˈhɪstəri wɪθ ˈɪnɪmət juˈmænɪti.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Even though Jin’s books were banned in China, his work still found Chinese readers around the globe,
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ˈivɪn ðoʊ jin’s* bʊks wər bænd ɪn ˈʧaɪnə, hɪz wərk stɪl faʊnd ʧaɪˈniz ˈridərz əraʊnd ðə gloʊb,

NEFmWclWiOw.text: and bootleg versions even made their way to the mainland.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ənd ˈbuˌtlɛg ˈvərʒənz ˈivɪn meɪd ðɛr weɪ tɪ ðə ˈmeɪnˌlænd.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: When the Chinese government relaxed its policies in the 1980s, his novels found an eager audience.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: wɪn ðə ʧaɪˈniz ˈgəvərnmənt rɪˈlækst ɪts ˈpɑləsiz ɪn ðə 1980s*, hɪz ˈnɑvəlz faʊnd ən ˈigər ˈɔdiəns.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Today, Jin Yong’s works have been adapted into film, television, and video games.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: təˈdeɪ, ʤɪn yong’s* wərks hæv bɪn əˈdæptɪd ˈɪntu fɪlm, ˈtɛləˌvɪʒən, ənd ˈvɪdioʊ geɪmz.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: His novels are so beloved it has been said, “where there are Chinese people, there must be Jin Yong’s fiction.”
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: hɪz ˈnɑvəlz ər soʊ bɪˈləvəd ɪt həz bɪn sɛd, ““where* ðɛr ər ʧaɪˈniz ˈpipəl, ðɛr məst bi ʤɪn yong’s* fiction.”*.”

NEFmWclWiOw.text: However, this literary titan remains relatively unknown in the West, due to the difficulties of translation
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ðɪs ˈlɪtərˌɛri ˈtaɪtən rɪˈmeɪnz ˈrɛlətɪvli ənˈnoʊn ɪn ðə wɛst, du tɪ ðə ˈdɪfɪˌkəltiz əv trænzˈleɪʃən

NEFmWclWiOw.text: and the high level of Chinese cultural knowledge his works demand.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ənd ðə haɪ ˈlɛvəl əv ʧaɪˈniz ˈkəlʧərəl ˈnɑlɪʤ hɪz wərks dɪˈmænd.

NEFmWclWiOw.text: Fortunately, recent English translations of the “Legend of the Condor Heroes” are helping a new generation of readers
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ˈfɔrʧənətli, ˈrisənt ˈɪŋlɪʃ trænzˈleɪʃənz əv ðə ““legend* əv ðə ˈkɑndər heroes”*” ər ˈhɛlpɪŋ ə nu ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃən əv ˈridərz

NEFmWclWiOw.text: experience the sweeping saga of the rivers and lakes.
NEFmWclWiOw.ipa: ɪkˈspɪriəns ðə sˈwipɪŋ ˈsɑgə əv ðə ˈrɪvərz ənd leɪks.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Amia and her friend Zelba have been inseparable for nearly a year.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: amia* ənd hər frɛnd zelba* hæv bɪn ˌɪnˈsɛpərəbəl fər ˈnɪrli ə jɪr.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: They share secrets, play games, and rule over imaginary kingdoms.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðeɪ ʃɛr ˈsikrɪts, pleɪ geɪmz, ənd rul ˈoʊvər ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri ˈkɪŋdəmz.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Amia loves to talk, and Zelba is a great listener.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: amia* ləvz tɪ tɔk, ənd zelba* ɪz ə greɪt ˈlɪsənər.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Amia sometimes has bad ideas, and Zelba goes along with them anyway.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: amia* ˈsəmˌtaɪmz həz bæd aɪˈdiəz, ənd zelba* goʊz əˈlɔŋ wɪθ ðɛm ˈɛniˌweɪ.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Amia is very real, and Zelba... is not.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: amia* ɪz ˈvɛri ril, ənd zelba*... ɪz nɑt.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Some parents worry about their child having playmates they can't see or hear,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: səm ˈpɛrənts ˈwəri əˈbaʊt ðɛr ʧaɪld ˈhævɪŋ ˈpleɪˌmeɪts ðeɪ kænt si ər hir,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: but imaginary companions are a normal part of psychological development for many kids.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: bət ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri kəmˈpænjənz ər ə ˈnɔrməl pɑrt əv ˌsaɪkəˈlɑʤɪkəl dɪˈvɛləpmənt fər ˈmɛni kɪdz.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: In fact, they even provide a special kind of support real friends can’t. But to understand this,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ɪn fækt, ðeɪ ˈivɪn prəˈvaɪd ə ˈspɛʃəl kaɪnd əv səˈpɔrt ril frɛndz can’t*. bət tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd ðɪs,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: we first need to explore what leads certain kids to create these fictional figures.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: wi fərst nid tɪ ɪkˈsplɔr wət lidz ˈsərtən kɪdz tɪ kriˈeɪt ðiz ˈfɪkʃənəl ˈfɪgjərz.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Most often, their inventors are simply looking for someone to play with.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: moʊst ˈɔfən, ðɛr ˌɪnˈvɛntərz ər ˈsɪmpli ˈlʊkɪŋ fər ˈsəmˌwən tɪ pleɪ wɪθ.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Imaginary friends are typically created by children roughly 3 to 4 years old who lack siblings of a similar age.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri frɛndz ər ˈtɪpɪkəli kriˈeɪtɪd baɪ ˈʧɪldrən ˈrəfli 3 tɪ 4 jɪrz oʊld hu læk ˈsɪblɪŋz əv ə ˈsɪmələr eɪʤ.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: These kids tend to be socially outgoing— they like company, so they invent more of it.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðiz kɪdz tɛnd tɪ bi ˈsoʊʃəli outgoing—*— ðeɪ laɪk ˈkəmpəˌni, soʊ ðeɪ ˌɪnˈvɛnt mɔr əv ɪt.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: And while almost all kids enjoy playing pretend,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ənd waɪl ˈɔlˌmoʊst ɔl kɪdz ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ pleɪɪŋ priˈtɛnd,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: children with invisible companions are especially fond of make-believe.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˈʧɪldrən wɪθ ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl kəmˈpænjənz ər əˈspɛʃəli fɑnd əv make-believe*.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: One study even found these kids were better storytellers, suggesting that imagining friends
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: wən ˈstədi ˈivɪn faʊnd ðiz kɪdz wər ˈbɛtər ˈstɔriˌtɛlərz, səˈʤɛstɪŋ ðət ˌɪˈmæʤənɪŋ frɛndz

qLrnn24C3zU.text: might help with linguistic and cognitive development. Outside a love of fantasy and socializing,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: maɪt hɛlp wɪθ lɪŋgˈwɪstɪk ənd ˈkɑgnɪtɪv dɪˈvɛləpmənt. ˈaʊtˈsaɪd ə ləv əv ˈfænəsi ənd ˈsoʊʃəˌlaɪzɪŋ,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: experts haven't found any meaningful differences in personality, intelligence,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˈɛkspərts ˈhævənt faʊnd ˈɛni ˈminɪŋfəl ˈdɪfərənsɪz ɪn ˌpərsəˈnælɪti, ˌɪnˈtɛləʤəns,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: or shyness between kids who do and do not have imaginary friends.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ər ˈʃaɪnəs bɪtˈwin kɪdz hu du ənd du nɑt hæv ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri frɛndz.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: However, the invisible characters themselves couldn't be more different.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ðə ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl ˈkɛrɪktərz ðɛmˈsɛlvz ˈkʊdənt bi mɔr ˈdɪfərənt.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: They can be animals, real or fictional. Humans of all ages, shapes, and sizes, with features straight out of a fairy tale.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðeɪ kən bi ˈænəməlz, ril ər ˈfɪkʃənəl. ˈjumənz əv ɔl ˈeɪʤɪz, ʃeɪps, ənd ˈsaɪzɪz, wɪθ ˈfiʧərz streɪt aʊt əv ə ˈfɛri teɪl.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Some come alone; others have a gaggle of imaginary friends and family.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: səm kəm əˈloʊn; ˈəðərz hæv ə ˈgægəl əv ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri frɛndz ənd ˈfæməli.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: They can even be what researchers call personified objects—
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðeɪ kən ˈivɪn bi wət ˈrisərʧərz kɔl pərˈsɑnəˌfaɪd objects—*—

qLrnn24C3zU.text: stuffed animals or toys brought to life by the imagination. And these manifestations vary from place to place.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: stəft ˈænəməlz ər tɔɪz brɔt tɪ laɪf baɪ ðə ˌɪˌmæʤəˈneɪʃən. ənd ðiz ˌmænəfɛˈsteɪʃənz ˈvɛri frəm pleɪs tɪ pleɪs.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: A 2004 study found roughly 67% of US kids under 8 had invisible companions,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ə 2004 ˈstədi faʊnd ˈrəfli 67 əv ˈjuˈɛs kɪdz ˈəndər 8 hæd ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl kəmˈpænjənz,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: while in Japan personified objects were much more common.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: waɪl ɪn ʤəˈpæn pərˈsɑnəˌfaɪd ˈɑbʤɛkts wər məʧ mɔr ˈkɑmən.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: These fictional figures don't just look different, they also fill a variety of roles.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðiz ˈfɪkʃənəl ˈfɪgjərz doʊnt ʤɪst lʊk ˈdɪfərənt, ðeɪ ˈɔlsoʊ fɪl ə vərˈaɪəti əv roʊlz.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Many are playmates and confidants, but others are scapegoats, errand buddies, or babies in need of care.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˈmɛni ər ˈpleɪˌmeɪts ənd ˈkɑnfəˌdænts, bət ˈəðərz ər ˈskeɪpˌgoʊts, ˈɛrənd ˈbədiz, ər ˈbeɪbiz ɪn nid əv kɛr.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: This wide range is why researchers typically call them
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðɪs waɪd reɪnʤ ɪz waɪ ˈrisərʧərz ˈtɪpɪkəli kɔl ðɛm

qLrnn24C3zU.text: invisible or imaginary “companions,” since they’re not always friends. In fact, researchers have even reported children
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl ər ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri ““companions,”*,” sɪns they’re* nɑt ˈɔlˌweɪz frɛndz. ɪn fækt, ˈrisərʧərz hæv ˈivɪn ˌriˈpɔrtəd ˈʧɪldrən

qLrnn24C3zU.text: who fight with their imaginary companion or are actively afraid of them. These situations can concern parents.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: hu faɪt wɪθ ðɛr ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri kəmˈpænjən ər ər ˈæktɪvli əˈfreɪd əv ðɛm. ðiz ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃənz kən kənˈsərn ˈpɛrənts.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: But not only are they totally normal, they highlight the special power of imaginary companions:
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: bət nɑt ˈoʊnli ər ðeɪ ˈtoʊtəli ˈnɔrməl, ðeɪ ˈhaɪˌlaɪt ðə ˈspɛʃəl paʊər əv ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri kəmˈpænjənz:

qLrnn24C3zU.text: each of these characters is designed to fulfill their creator’s needs or wants.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: iʧ əv ðiz ˈkɛrɪktərz ɪz dɪˈzaɪnd tɪ fʊlˈfɪl ðɛr creator’s* nidz ər wɔnts.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: By creating a scary invisible companion,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: baɪ kriˈeɪtɪŋ ə ˈskɛri ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl kəmˈpænjən,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: a child might be experimenting with feeling fear and how to conquer it.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ə ʧaɪld maɪt bi ɛkˈspɛrəˌmɛntɪŋ wɪθ ˈfilɪŋ fɪr ənd haʊ tɪ ˈkɑŋkər ɪt.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Negotiating imaginary disagreements lets kids practice dealing with conflict without real-world consequences,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: nɪˈgoʊʃiˌeɪtɪŋ ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri ˌdɪsəˈgrimənts lɛts kɪdz ˈpræktɪs ˈdilɪŋ wɪθ ˈkɑnflɪkt wɪˈθaʊt ˈrilˌwərld ˈkɑnsəkˌwɛnsəz,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: and resolving these arguments can help them explore empathy and caregiving. In these ways and more,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ənd riˈzɑlvɪŋ ðiz ˈɑrgjəmənts kən hɛlp ðɛm ɪkˈsplɔr ˈɛmpəθi ənd ˈkɛrˌgɪvɪŋ. ɪn ðiz weɪz ənd mɔr,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: invisible companions give developing children the agency to explore emotions
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl kəmˈpænjənz gɪv dɪˈvɛləpɪŋ ˈʧɪldrən ðə ˈeɪʤənsi tɪ ɪkˈsplɔr ˈɪˌmoʊʃənz

qLrnn24C3zU.text: and social interactions on their terms. And since research consistently shows children know these characters are imaginary,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ənd ˈsoʊʃəl ˌɪnərˈækʃənz ɔn ðɛr tərmz. ənd sɪns ˈrisərʧ kənˈsɪstəntli ʃoʊz ˈʧɪldrən noʊ ðiz ˈkɛrɪktərz ər ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: the kids are always in control— even when it doesn’t look like it. Some invisible companions stick around through their creator’s
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðə kɪdz ər ˈɔlˌweɪz ɪn control—*— ˈivɪn wɪn ɪt doesn’t* lʊk laɪk ɪt. səm ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl kəmˈpænjənz stɪk əraʊnd θru ðɛr creator’s*

qLrnn24C3zU.text: teenage and adult years.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˈtiˌneɪʤ ənd ˈædəlt jɪrz.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: But generally, as kids start making friends in school around age 5 or 6,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: bət ˈʤɛnərəli, ɛz kɪdz stɑrt ˈmeɪkɪŋ frɛndz ɪn skul əraʊnd eɪʤ 5 ər 6

qLrnn24C3zU.text: they talk about their imaginary companions less and less often.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðeɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt ðɛr ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri kəmˈpænjənz lɛs ənd lɛs ˈɔfən.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: That said, the time they spent with these fictional figures has lifelong benefits.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðət sɛd, ðə taɪm ðeɪ spɛnt wɪθ ðiz ˈfɪkʃənəl ˈfɪgjərz həz ˈlaɪˈflɔŋ ˈbɛnəfɪts.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: Research suggests pretend play strengthens a child's theory of mind.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˈrisərʧ səˈʤɛsts priˈtɛnd pleɪ ˈstrɛŋθənz ə ʧaɪldz ˈθɪri əv maɪnd.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: This is our ability to understand, imagine, and predict other people’s mental states—
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ɑr əˈbɪləˌti tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd, ˌɪˈmæʤən, ənd prɪˈdɪkt ˈəðər people’s* ˈmɛntəl states—*—

qLrnn24C3zU.text: a skill that can help kids develop empathy and build relationships.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ə skɪl ðət kən hɛlp kɪdz dɪˈvɛləp ˈɛmpəθi ənd bɪld riˈleɪʃənˌʃɪps.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: And while we might stop speaking to our invisible companions, we never stop talking to ourselves.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ənd waɪl wi maɪt stɑp ˈspikɪŋ tɪ ɑr ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl kəmˈpænjənz, wi ˈnɛvər stɑp ˈtɔkɪŋ tɪ ɑrˈsɛlvz.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: When you rehearse a difficult conversation in the shower or talk through your problems to a pet,
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: wɪn ju riˈhərs ə ˈdɪfəkəlt ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən ɪn ðə ʃaʊər ər tɔk θru jʊr ˈprɑbləmz tɪ ə pɛt,

qLrnn24C3zU.text: you’re using the same skills you learned from your imaginary companions—
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: you’re* ˈjuzɪŋ ðə seɪm skɪlz ju ˈlərnɪd frəm jʊr ˌɪˈmæʤəˌnɛri companions—*—

qLrnn24C3zU.text: talking through your inner monologue to problem solve and regulate your mood.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: ˈtɔkɪŋ θru jʊr ˈɪnər ˈmɑnəˌlɔg tɪ ˈprɑbləm sɑlv ənd ˈrɛgjəˌleɪt jʊr mud.

qLrnn24C3zU.text: So, the next time you see a kid chattering to thin air, know that you're watching the beginning of a lifelong conversation.
qLrnn24C3zU.ipa: soʊ, ðə nɛkst taɪm ju si ə kɪd ˈʧætərɪŋ tɪ θɪn ɛr, noʊ ðət jʊr ˈwɑʧɪŋ ðə bɪˈgɪnɪŋ əv ə ˈlaɪˈflɔŋ ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: In the 1930s, Danish landscape architect Carl Theodor Sørensen
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ɪn ðə 1930s*, ˈdeɪnɪʃ ˈlænˌskeɪp ˈɑrkəˌtɛkt kɑrl ˈθiədər sørensen*

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: noticed that his neighborhood’s children loved playing in old building sites.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈnoʊtɪst ðət hɪz neighborhood’s* ˈʧɪldrən ləvd pleɪɪŋ ɪn oʊld ˈbɪldɪŋ saɪts.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Watching kids swing from exposed beams, scavenge for scrap materials, and hammer together bridges
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈwɑʧɪŋ kɪdz swɪŋ frəm ɪkˈspoʊzd bimz, ˈskævənʤ fər skræp məˈtɪriəlz, ənd ˈhæmər təˈgɛðər ˈbrɪʤɪz

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: inspired him to transform an abandoned housing estate
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˌɪnˈspaɪərd ɪm tɪ ˈtrænsfɔrm ən əˈbændənd ˈhaʊzɪŋ ɛˈsteɪt

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: into a dedicated junk playground.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈɪntu ə ˈdɛdəkeɪtəd ʤəŋk ˈpleɪˌgraʊnd.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Sørensen’s site became a huge success,
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: sørensen’s* saɪt bɪˈkeɪm ə juʤ səkˈsɛs,

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: and soon countries throughout Europe were making playgrounds out of derelict building yards and even former World War I bomb sites.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ənd sun ˈkəntriz θruaʊt ˈjʊrəp wər ˈmeɪkɪŋ ˈpleɪˌgraʊnz aʊt əv ˈdɛrəˌlɪkt ˈbɪldɪŋ jɑrdz ənd ˈivɪn ˈfɔrmər wərld wɔr aɪ bɔm saɪts.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: These environments might look dangerous, especially to 21st century parents who tend to be far more anxious about their childrens’ safety
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ðiz ɪnˈvaɪrənmənts maɪt lʊk ˈdeɪnʤərəs, əˈspɛʃəli tɪ 21st* ˈsɛnʧəri ˈpɛrənts hu tɛnd tɪ bi fɑr mɔr ˈæŋʃəs əˈbaʊt ðɛr childrens’*’ ˈseɪfti

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: than previous generations.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ðən ˈpriviəs ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃənz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: But research has found these seemingly scary setups teach kids important lessons that standard playgrounds don’t.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: bət ˈrisərʧ həz faʊnd ðiz ˈsimɪŋli ˈskɛri ˈsɛˌtəps tiʧ kɪdz ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ˈlɛsənz ðət ˈstændərd ˈpleɪˌgraʊnz don’t*.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Play is profoundly important for childhood development. Creating games and following rules helps teach problem solving.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: pleɪ ɪz proʊˈfaʊndli ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt fər ˈʧaɪlˌdhʊd dɪˈvɛləpmənt. kriˈeɪtɪŋ geɪmz ənd ˈfɑloʊɪŋ rulz hɛlps tiʧ ˈprɑbləm ˈsɑlvɪŋ.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Moving our bodies through varied terrain helps hone our motor control.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈmuvɪŋ ɑr ˈbɑdiz θru ˈvɛrid təreɪn hɛlps hoʊn ɑr ˈmoʊtər kənˈtroʊl.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: And playing with others lets us practice language and refine our social skills.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ənd pleɪɪŋ wɪθ ˈəðərz lɛts ˈjuˈɛs ˈpræktɪs ˈlæŋgwɪʤ ənd rɪˈfaɪn ɑr ˈsoʊʃəl skɪlz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Since play comes naturally to all of us, children are likely to develop these skills in most environments.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: sɪns pleɪ kəmz ˈnæʧərəli tɪ ɔl əv ˈjuˈɛs, ˈʧɪldrən ər ˈlaɪkli tɪ dɪˈvɛləp ðiz skɪlz ɪn moʊst ɪnˈvaɪrənmənts.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: But researchers have identified a few factors that can maximize these benefits.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: bət ˈrisərʧərz hæv aɪˈdɛntəˌfaɪd ə fju ˈfæktərz ðət kən ˈmæksəˌmaɪz ðiz ˈbɛnəfɪts.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Chief among them is freedom. Play is all about experimentation,
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ʧif əˈməŋ ðɛm ɪz ˈfridəm. pleɪ ɪz ɔl əˈbaʊt ɪkˌspɛrəmənˈteɪʃən,

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: and kids need the agency to explore and shape their surroundings without adults getting in the way.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ənd kɪdz nid ðə ˈeɪʤənsi tɪ ɪkˈsplɔr ənd ʃeɪp ðɛr sərˈaʊndɪŋz wɪˈθaʊt ˈædəlts ˈgɪtɪŋ ɪn ðə weɪ.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: The next factor is novelty—
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ðə nɛkst ˈfæktər ɪz novelty—*—

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: kids are always hungry for new and unpredictable play elements.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: kɪdz ər ˈɔlˌweɪz ˈhəŋgri fər nu ənd ˌənprɪˈdɪktəbəl pleɪ ˈɛləmənts.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Finally, they need time to see their plans through.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈfaɪnəli, ðeɪ nid taɪm tɪ si ðɛr plænz θru.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: While we could all use more time to play, the best play environments offer freedom and novelty
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: waɪl wi kʊd ɔl juz mɔr taɪm tɪ pleɪ, ðə bɛst pleɪ ɪnˈvaɪrənmənts ˈɔfər ˈfridəm ənd ˈnɑvəlti

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: that kids can explore as much or as little as they like.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ðət kɪdz kən ɪkˈsplɔr ɛz məʧ ər ɛz ˈlɪtəl ɛz ðeɪ laɪk.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: But traditional playgrounds don’t offer much flexibility here.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: bət trəˈdɪʃənəl ˈpleɪˌgraʊnz don’t* ˈɔfər məʧ ˌflɛksəˈbɪləti hir.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Swings, slides, and climbing frames are hardly novel structures.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: swɪŋz, slaɪdz, ənd ˈklaɪmɪŋ freɪmz ər ˈhɑrdli ˈnɑvəl ˈstrəkʧərz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: They also have relatively low affordance—
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ðeɪ ˈɔlsoʊ hæv ˈrɛlətɪvli loʊ affordance—*—

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: a term used by play researchers to indicate how open-ended an object’s design is.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ə tərm juzd baɪ pleɪ ˈrisərʧərz tɪ ˈɪndəˌkeɪt haʊ ˈoʊpəˌnɛndəd ən object’s* dɪˈzaɪn ɪz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Items with high affordance, like a sandbox, can be interacted with in many ways.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈaɪtəmz wɪθ haɪ affordance*, laɪk ə ˈsændˌbɑks, kən bi ˌɪnərˈæktəd wɪθ ɪn ˈmɛni weɪz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Meanwhile, objects with low affordance, like a slide, were designed with a single use in mind.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈminˌwaɪl, ˈɑbʤɛkts wɪθ loʊ affordance*, laɪk ə slaɪd, wər dɪˈzaɪnd wɪθ ə ˈsɪŋgəl juz ɪn maɪnd.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Even when kids are creative enough to find new uses for low affordance objects,
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈivɪn wɪn kɪdz ər kriˈeɪtɪv ɪˈnəf tɪ faɪnd nu ˈjuzɪz fər loʊ affordance* ˈɑbʤɛkts,

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: they're still more limited playthings.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ðɛr stɪl mɔr ˈlɪmɪtɪd ˈpleɪˌθɪŋz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: To address these problems, some designers are looking back at Europe's junk playgrounds to create modern adventure playgrounds—
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: tɪ ˈæˌdrɛs ðiz ˈprɑbləmz, səm dɪˈzaɪnərz ər ˈlʊkɪŋ bæk æt ˈjʊrəps ʤəŋk ˈpleɪˌgraʊnz tɪ kriˈeɪt ˈmɑdərn ədˈvɛnʧər playgrounds—*—

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: big, open spaces full of high affordance structures and free play possibilities.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: bɪg, ˈoʊpən ˈspeɪsɪz fʊl əv haɪ affordance* ˈstrəkʧərz ənd fri pleɪ ˌpɑsəˈbɪlətiz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Berlin’s Kolle 37 features scrap and building tools for kids to manipulate.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: berlin’s* kɑl 37 ˈfiʧərz skræp ənd ˈbɪldɪŋ tulz fər kɪdz tɪ məˈnɪpjəˌleɪt.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: In Tokyo’s muddy Harapa Park, children shape water slides, sink holes, and splash pools
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ɪn tokyo’s* ˈmədi harapa* pɑrk, ˈʧɪldrən ʃeɪp ˈwɔtər slaɪdz, sɪŋk hoʊlz, ənd splæʃ pulz

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: out of the natural landscape.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: aʊt əv ðə ˈnæʧərəl ˈlænˌskeɪp.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: And in London’s Glamis Adventure Playground
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ənd ɪn london’s* glamis* ədˈvɛnʧər ˈpleɪˌgraʊnd

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: kids burn things in fire pits, with the help of play worker employees.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: kɪdz bərn θɪŋz ɪn faɪər pɪts, wɪθ ðə hɛlp əv pleɪ ˈwərkər ɪmˈplɔɪiz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: While some parents worry these unpredictable spaces are too dangerous, play researchers like Ellen Sandseter
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: waɪl səm ˈpɛrənts ˈwəri ðiz ˌənprɪˈdɪktəbəl ˈspeɪsɪz ər tu ˈdeɪnʤərəs, pleɪ ˈrisərʧərz laɪk ˈɛlən sandseter*

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: would say this so-called risky play is essential.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: wʊd seɪ ðɪs ˈsoʊˈkɔld ˈrɪski pleɪ ɪz ɛˈsɛnʃəl.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Giving kids the space to experiment with fast speeds, high heights and getting lost
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈgɪvɪŋ kɪdz ðə speɪs tɪ ɪkˈspɛrəmənt wɪθ fæst spidz, haɪ haɪts ənd ˈgɪtɪŋ lɔst

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: helps them develop better judgment and confidence.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: hɛlps ðɛm dɪˈvɛləp ˈbɛtər ˈʤəʤmənt ənd ˈkɑnfədɛns.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: In fact, risky play is how kids learn to manage risk and keep themselves safe.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ɪn fækt, ˈrɪski pleɪ ɪz haʊ kɪdz lərn tɪ ˈmænɪʤ rɪsk ənd kip ðɛmˈsɛlvz seɪf.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: One study even found that kids were more likely to get seriously injured in traditional playgrounds—
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: wən ˈstədi ˈivɪn faʊnd ðət kɪdz wər mɔr ˈlaɪkli tɪ gɪt ˈsɪriəsli ˈɪnʤərd ɪn trəˈdɪʃənəl playgrounds—*—

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: perhaps because adventure playgrounds encourage them to be more aware of their surroundings.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: pərˈhæps bɪˈkəz ədˈvɛnʧər ˈpleɪˌgraʊnz ɪnˈkərəʤ ðɛm tɪ bi mɔr əˈwɛr əv ðɛr sərˈaʊndɪŋz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Risky play also helps develop skills for managing mental health. Experimenting with small doses of uncertainty
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈrɪski pleɪ ˈɔlsoʊ hɛlps dɪˈvɛləp skɪlz fər ˈmænəʤɪŋ ˈmɛntəl hɛlθ. ɛkˈspɛrəˌmɛntɪŋ wɪθ smɔl ˈdoʊsɪz əv ənˈsərtənti

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: gets kids used to life being unpredictable,
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: gɪts kɪdz juzd tɪ laɪf biɪŋ ˌənprɪˈdɪktəbəl,

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: helping them better manage anxiety for years to come.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈhɛlpɪŋ ðɛm ˈbɛtər ˈmænɪʤ æŋˈzaɪəti fər jɪrz tɪ kəm.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Adventure playgrounds aren't the only places for risky play.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ədˈvɛnʧər ˈpleɪˌgraʊnz ˈɑrənt ðə ˈoʊnli ˈpleɪsɪz fər ˈrɪski pleɪ.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: Nature playgrounds encouraging kids to explore creeks, caves, and tree climbing combine the perks of risk-taking
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈneɪʧər ˈpleɪˌgraʊnz ɪnˈkərəʤɪŋ kɪdz tɪ ɪkˈsplɔr kriks, keɪvz, ənd tri ˈklaɪmɪŋ ˈkɑmbaɪn ðə pərks əv risk-taking*

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: with the health benefits of connecting with nature. Meanwhile, other designers are working to bring more freedom and novelty
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: wɪθ ðə hɛlθ ˈbɛnəfɪts əv kəˈnɛktɪŋ wɪθ ˈneɪʧər. ˈminˌwaɪl, ˈəðər dɪˈzaɪnərz ər ˈwərkɪŋ tɪ brɪŋ mɔr ˈfridəm ənd ˈnɑvəlti

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: to traditional designs.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: tɪ trəˈdɪʃənəl dɪˈzaɪnz.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: This Philadelphia park features a 30-person mega-swing, and Nebraska’s Omaha Riverfront Playground
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ðɪs ˌfɪləˈdɛlfiə pɑrk ˈfiʧərz ə 30-person* mega-swing*, ənd nebraska’s* ˈoʊməˌhɑ ˈrɪvərˌfrənt ˈpleɪˌgraʊnd

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: uses a ribbon of multi-layered decking as a play spine,
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈjuzɪz ə ˈrɪbən əv multi-layered* ˈdɛkɪŋ ɛz ə pleɪ spaɪn,

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: warping up and down to reveal secret tunnels and rope forests.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˈwɔrpɪŋ əp ənd daʊn tɪ rɪˈvil ˈsikrɪt ˈtənəlz ənd roʊp ˈfɔrəsts.

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: So the real key is to design a variety of playgrounds, supporting a wide range of activities
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: soʊ ðə ril ki ɪz tɪ dɪˈzaɪn ə vərˈaɪəti əv ˈpleɪˌgraʊnz, səˈpɔrtɪŋ ə waɪd reɪnʤ əv ækˈtɪvɪtiz

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: that promote fun, agency, and a dash of uncertainty—
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ðət prəˈmoʊt fən, ˈeɪʤənsi, ənd ə dæʃ əv uncertainty—*—

kRAl4Xgs_NU.text: inviting kids to fill in the possibilities.
kRAl4Xgs_NU.ipa: ˌɪnˈvaɪtɪŋ kɪdz tɪ fɪl ɪn ðə ˌpɑsəˈbɪlətiz.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: A figure perches, hunched in reflection.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ə ˈfɪgjər ˈpərʧɪz, hənʧt ɪn rɪˈflɛkʃən.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: But this canonical sculpture isn't just contemplation incarnate.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: bət ðɪs kəˈnɑnəkəl ˈskəlpʧər ˈɪzənt ʤɪst ˌkɑntəmˈpleɪʃən ˌɪnˈkɑrˌneɪt.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: French sculptor Auguste Rodin intended it to represent a specific person—
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: frɛnʧ ˈskəlptər ˈɔgəst ˌroʊˈdæn ˌɪnˈtɛndɪd ɪt tɪ ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnt ə spɪˈsɪfɪk person—*—

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: and fit into a much larger piece featuring the fiery pits of Hell—
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ənd fɪt ˈɪntu ə məʧ ˈlɑrʤər pis ˈfiʧərɪŋ ðə ˈfaɪəri pɪts əv hell—*—

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: a project that obsessed him during the last decades of his life.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ə ˈprɑʤɛkt ðət əbˈsɛst ɪm ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə læst ˈdɛkeɪdz əv hɪz laɪf.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: So, who was “The Thinker” and what was he actually thinking?
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: soʊ, hu wɑz ““the* thinker”*” ənd wət wɑz hi ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈθɪŋkɪŋ?

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Rodin's path to renown was rocky.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: rodin's* pæθ tɪ rɪˈnaʊn wɑz ˈrɑki.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: He grew up in a working-class neighborhood in Paris, applied to the esteemed school of fine arts,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: hi gru əp ɪn ə working-class* ˈneɪbərˌhʊd ɪn ˈpɛrɪs, əˈplaɪd tɪ ðə ɛˈstimd skul əv faɪn ɑrts,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: and was rejected three separate times.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ənd wɑz rɪˈʤɛktɪd θri ˈsɛpərˌeɪt taɪmz.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: After several years working as a craftsman, he submitted his first sculpture to Paris’ Salon— and was denied.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˈæftər ˈsɛvərəl jɪrz ˈwərkɪŋ ɛz ə ˈkræftsmən, hi səbˈmɪtəd hɪz fərst ˈskəlpʧər tɪ paris’*’ salon—*— ənd wɑz dɪˈnaɪd.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: It wasn't until 1877, when he was 35 and fresh off a visit to Italy,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ɪt ˈwəzənt ənˈtɪl 1877 wɪn hi wɑz 35 ənd frɛʃ ɔf ə ˈvɪzɪt tɪ ˈɪtəli,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: dazzled by the Renaissance sculptures on display,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˈdæzəld baɪ ðə ˌrɛnəˈsɑns ˈskəlpʧərz ɔn dɪˈspleɪ,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: that Rodin completed his first major work.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ðət ˌroʊˈdæn kəmˈplitɪd hɪz fərst ˈmeɪʤər wərk.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: However, critics accused him of casting the lifelike sculpture directly from the model.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ˈkrɪtɪks əˈkjuzd ɪm əv ˈkæstɪŋ ðə ˈlaɪˌflaɪk ˈskəlpʧər dɪˈrɛkli frəm ðə ˈmɑdəl.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: But he hadn’t, and other artists vouched for him.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: bət hi hadn’t*, ənd ˈəðər ˈɑrtɪsts vaʊʧt fər ɪm.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: As the controversy concluded, however, Rodin drastically shifted his style.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ɛz ðə ˈkɑntrəˌvərsi kənˈkludɪd, ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ˌroʊˈdæn ˈdræstɪkli ˈʃɪftɪd hɪz staɪl.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Rather than render academically realistic forms, he began creating rougher, more expressive surfaces.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˈrəðər ðən ˈrɛndər ˌækəˈdɛmɪkli ˌriəˈlɪstɪk fɔrmz, hi bɪˈgæn kriˈeɪtɪŋ ˈrəfər, mɔr ɪkˈsprɛsɪv ˈsərfəsɪz.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Advances in camera technology had recently made it possible to capture perfect likeness,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ədˈvænsɪz ɪn ˈkæmərə tɛkˈnɑləʤi hæd ˈrisəntli meɪd ɪt ˈpɑsəbəl tɪ ˈkæpʧər ˈpərˌfɪkt ˈlaɪknəs,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: but Rodin argued that artistic renderings, though less precise, were more truthful.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: bət ˌroʊˈdæn ˈɑrgjud ðət ɑrˈtɪstɪk ˈrɛndərɪŋz, ðoʊ lɛs prɪˈsaɪs, wər mɔr ˈtruθfəl.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Like artists helming the burgeoning movements of Cubism, Abstraction, and Impressionism,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: laɪk ˈɑrtɪsts ˈhɛlmɪŋ ðə ˈbərʤənɪŋ ˈmuvmənts əv ˈkjubɪzəm, æbˈstrækʃən, ənd ˌɪmˈprɛʃəˌnɪzəm,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Rodin was poised to modernize sculpture, lending new life to classical forms.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˌroʊˈdæn wɑz pɔɪzd tɪ ˈmɑdərˌnaɪz ˈskəlpʧər, ˈlɛndɪŋ nu laɪf tɪ ˈklæsɪkəl fɔrmz.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: And in 1880, he received his life-defining commission: a monumental doorway for a new French museum
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ənd ɪn 1880 hi rɪˈsivd hɪz life-defining* kəˈmɪʃən: ə ˌmɑnjəˈmɛnəl ˈdɔrˌweɪ fər ə nu frɛnʧ mˈjuziəm

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: intended to echo the “Gates of Paradise” by Renaissance sculptor Lorenzo Ghiberti.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˌɪnˈtɛndɪd tɪ ˈɛkoʊ ðə ““gates* əv paradise”*” baɪ ˌrɛnəˈsɑns ˈskəlptər lərˈɛnzoʊ ghiberti*.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Rodin proposed its antithesis: "The Gates of Hell,”
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˌroʊˈdæn prəˈpoʊzd ɪts ænˈtɪθəsəs: "ðə geɪts əv hell,”*,”

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: a swirling, infernal composition featuring over 200 tormented souls.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ə sˈwərlɪŋ, ˌɪnˈfərnəl ˌkɑmpəˈzɪʃən ˈfiʧərɪŋ ˈoʊvər 200 ˈtɔrˌmɛntɪd soʊlz.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: It was inspired by Dante Alighieri’s “Inferno,”
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ɪt wɑz ˌɪnˈspaɪərd baɪ ˈdɑnteɪ alighieri’s* ““inferno,”*,”

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: a 14th-century poetic journey through the nine circles of Hell
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ə 14th-century* poʊˈɛtɪk ˈʤərni θru ðə naɪn ˈsərkəlz əv hɛl

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: and its doomed inhabitants’ downfalls.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ənd ɪts dumd inhabitants’*’ downfalls*.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Rodin began “The Gates” in clay, sculpting small, interlocking figures,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˌroʊˈdæn bɪˈgæn ““the* gates”*” ɪn kleɪ, ˈskəlptɪŋ smɔl, ˈɪntərˌlɑkɪŋ ˈfɪgjərz,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: his studio filling with fragments to be rearranged, combined, or enlarged as independent works.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: hɪz ˈstudiˌoʊ ˈfɪlɪŋ wɪθ ˈfrægmənts tɪ bi riəreɪnʤd, kəmˈbaɪnd, ər ˌɛnˈlɑrʤd ɛz ˌɪndɪˈpɛndənt wərks.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Breaking with tradition, he left visible traces of the creative process.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˈbreɪkɪŋ wɪθ trəˈdɪʃən, hi lɛft ˈvɪzəbəl ˈtreɪsɪz əv ðə kriˈeɪtɪv ˈprɔˌsɛs.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: However, the museum was never built. And the project became a sprawling obsession of endless revision.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ðə mˈjuziəm wɑz ˈnɛvər bɪlt. ənd ðə ˈprɑʤɛkt bɪˈkeɪm ə ˈsprɔlɪŋ əbˈsɛʃən əv ˈɛndləs riˈvɪʒən.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: But it was one that would yield some of Rodin’s greatest sculptures—
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: bət ɪt wɑz wən ðət wʊd jild səm əv rodin’s* ˈgreɪtəst sculptures—*—

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: individual elements from “The Gates” that were isolated, refined, and scaled up.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəl ˈɛləmənts frəm ““the* gates”*” ðət wər ˈaɪsəˌleɪtɪd, rɪˈfaɪnd, ənd skeɪld əp.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Like many artists, Rodin had a team of studio assistants who were talented in their own right.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: laɪk ˈmɛni ˈɑrtɪsts, ˌroʊˈdæn hæd ə tim əv ˈstudiˌoʊ əˈsɪstənts hu wər ˈtæləntɪd ɪn ðɛr oʊn raɪt.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: For “The Gates,” he favored an ancient technique, the lost-wax method, to go from clay to bronze.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: fər ““the* gates,”*,” hi ˈfeɪvərd ən ˈeɪnʧənt tɛkˈnik, ðə lost-wax* ˈmɛθəd, tɪ goʊ frəm kleɪ tɪ brɑnz.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: For each sculpture, his team made various molds, beginning with plaster and moving into hollow wax replicas they’d coat and heat,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: fər iʧ ˈskəlpʧər, hɪz tim meɪd ˈvɛriəs moʊldz, bɪˈgɪnɪŋ wɪθ ˈplæstər ənd ˈmuvɪŋ ˈɪntu ˈhɑloʊ wæks ˈrɛplɪkəz they’d* koʊt ənd hit,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: melting away the wax, before pouring molten bronze in.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˈmɛltɪŋ əˈweɪ ðə wæks, ˌbiˈfɔr ˈpɔrɪŋ ˈmoʊltən brɑnz ɪn.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Finally, they’d break the outer shell to reveal the solid metal sculpture within.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˈfaɪnəli, they’d* breɪk ðə ˈaʊtər ʃɛl tɪ rɪˈvil ðə ˈsɑləd ˈmɛtəl ˈskəlpʧər wɪˈθɪn.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Complex compositions were cast in sections and soldered together.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˈkɑmplɛks ˌkɑmpəˈzɪʃənz wər kæst ɪn ˈsɛkʃənz ənd soldered* təˈgɛðər.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Then, Rodin’s team would finish the surface, applying a chemical patina.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ðɛn, rodin’s* tim wʊd ˈfɪnɪʃ ðə ˈsərfəs, əˈplaɪɪŋ ə ˈkɛmɪkəl pəˈtinə.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Within “The Gates of Hell,” forms described in Dante’s “Inferno” writhed in sin-struck anguish:
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: wɪˈθɪn ““the* geɪts əv hell,”*,” fɔrmz dɪˈskraɪbd ɪn dante’s* ““inferno”*” writhed* ɪn sin-struck* ˈæŋgwɪʃ:

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: lovers Paolo and Francesca grappling eternally in forbidden lust
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˈləvərz ˈpaʊloʊ ənd frænˈʧɛskə ˈgræpəlɪŋ ɪˈtərnəli ɪn ˈfɔrbɪdən ləst

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: and political traitor Count Ugolino cannibalizing his sons in his final desperate moments.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ənd pəˈlɪtɪkəl ˈtreɪtər kaʊnt ugolino* ˈkænəbəˌlaɪzɪŋ hɪz sənz ɪn hɪz ˈfaɪnəl ˈdɛspərɪt ˈmoʊmənts.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Rodin also found infernal inspiration in other works,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˌroʊˈdæn ˈɔlsoʊ faʊnd ˌɪnˈfərnəl ˌɪnspərˈeɪʃən ɪn ˈəðər wərks,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: like the carnal themes explored in a poetry collection by Charles Baudelaire.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: laɪk ðə ˈkɑrnəl θimz ɪkˈsplɔrd ɪn ə ˈpoʊətri kəˈlɛkʃən baɪ ˈʧɑrəlz baudelaire*.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: But above all of this hellish chaos was to be a single seated figure—
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: bət əˈbəv ɔl əv ðɪs ˈhɛlɪʃ keɪɑs wɑz tɪ bi ə ˈsɪŋgəl ˈsitɪd figure—*—

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: not just any man, but the author of “Inferno,”
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: nɑt ʤɪst ˈɛni mæn, bət ðə ˈɔθər əv ““inferno,”*,”

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Dante, himself, pondering the suffering below,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˈdɑnteɪ, hɪmˈsɛlf, ˈpɑndərɪŋ ðə ˈsəfərɪŋ bɪˈloʊ,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: considering human nature’s great pitfalls, the weight bearing down on his fist.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: kənˈsɪdərɪŋ ˈjumən nature’s* greɪt ˈpɪtˌfɔlz, ðə weɪt ˈbɛrɪŋ daʊn ɔn hɪz fɪst.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Rodin originally called him “The Poet,” then “The Thinker.”
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ˌroʊˈdæn ərˈɪʤənəli kɔld ɪm ““the* poet,”*,” ðɛn ““the* thinker.”*.”

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: First cast on its own in 1888, “The Thinker” became a sensation.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: fərst kæst ɔn ɪts oʊn ɪn 1888 ““the* thinker”*” bɪˈkeɪm ə sɛnˈseɪʃən.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Out of context, the figure came to be seen less as Dante wrestling with sin and damnation,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: aʊt əv ˈkɑntɛkst, ðə ˈfɪgjər keɪm tɪ bi sin lɛs ɛz ˈdɑnteɪ ˈrɛsəlɪŋ wɪθ sɪn ənd dæmˈneɪʃən,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: and more of an everyman;
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ənd mɔr əv ən ˈɛvrimən;

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: a universal symbol of the human mind’s ability to reflect, doubt, and create;
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ə ˌjunəˈvərsəl ˈsɪmbəl əv ðə ˈjumən mind’s* əˈbɪləˌti tɪ rɪˈflɛkt, daʊt, ənd kriˈeɪt;

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: or even France itself, striving to balance its values.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ər ˈivɪn fræns ˌɪtˈsɛlf, ˈstraɪvɪŋ tɪ ˈbæləns ɪts ˈvæljuz.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: In 1904, a life-sized “Thinker” was installed in public—
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ɪn 1904 ə life-sized* ““thinker”*” wɑz ˌɪnˈstɔld ɪn public—*—

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: not overlooking Hell, but crowning a cultural monument.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: nɑt ˈoʊvərˌlʊkɪŋ hɛl, bət ˈkraʊnɪŋ ə ˈkəlʧərəl ˈmɑnjəmənt.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: And it soon became one of the world’s most famous sculptures.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ənd ɪt sun bɪˈkeɪm wən əv ðə world’s* moʊst ˈfeɪməs ˈskəlpʧərz.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: But much as “The Thinker” remains eternally consumed by contemplation,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: bət məʧ ɛz ““the* thinker”*” rɪˈmeɪnz ɪˈtərnəli kənˈsumd baɪ ˌkɑntəmˈpleɪʃən,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Rodin’s “Gates of Hell” remain unfinished.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: rodin’s* ““gates* əv hell”*” rɪˈmeɪn ənˈfɪnɪʃt.

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: Despite 37 years of work,
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: dɪˈspaɪt 37 jɪrz əv wərk,

dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.text: the first bronze cast of “The Gates” was completed nearly a decade after his death.
dOqqBQ5Ha6Q.ipa: ðə fərst brɑnz kæst əv ““the* gates”*” wɑz kəmˈplitɪd ˈnɪrli ə ˈdɛkeɪd ˈæftər hɪz dɛθ.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: How can a bigger tube fit inside a smaller tube?
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: haʊ kən ə ˈbɪgər tub fɪt ˌɪnˈsaɪd ə sˈmɔlər tub?

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: This might sound like a riddle,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðɪs maɪt saʊnd laɪk ə ˈrɪdəl,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: but it’s a practical dilemma in our snake-eat-snake world.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: bət it’s* ə ˈpræktɪkəl dɪˈlɛmə ɪn ɑr snake-eat-snake* wərld.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: This eastern kingsnake, for example, has a mouthful of a Texas rat snake,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðɪs ˈistərn kingsnake*, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, həz ə ˈmaʊθˌfʊl əv ə ˈtɛksəs ræt sneɪk,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: but the rat snake’s longer than it is,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: bət ðə ræt snake’s* ˈlɔŋgər ðən ɪt ɪz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: so how can the kingsnake possibly swallow it whole?
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: soʊ haʊ kən ðə kingsnake* ˈpɑsəbli sˈwɔloʊ ɪt hoʊl?

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: This is just one of countless predatory dilemmas snakes have solved.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ʤɪst wən əv ˈkaʊntləs ˈprɛdəˌtɔri dəˈlɛməz sneɪks hæv sɑlvd.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Since slithering onto the scene some 150 million years ago,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: sɪns sˈlɪðərɪŋ ˈɔntu ðə sin səm 150 ˈmɪljən jɪrz əˈgoʊ,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: evolving length and limblessness out of their ancestral lizard forms,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ɪˈvɑlvɪŋ lɛŋθ ənd limblessness* aʊt əv ðɛr ænˈsɛstrəl ˈlɪzərd fɔrmz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: snakes have diversified rapidly.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: sneɪks hæv dɪˈvərsəˌfaɪd ˈræpədli.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Their noodly bodies and flexible heads granted them access to novel places and prey.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðɛr noodly* ˈbɑdiz ənd ˈflɛksəbəl hɛdz ˈgrænɪd ðɛm ˈækˌsɛs tɪ ˈnɑvəl ˈpleɪsɪz ənd preɪ.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: And today, there are nearly 4,000 snake species,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ənd təˈdeɪ, ðɛr ər ˈnɪrli 4,000* sneɪk ˈspiʃiz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: spanning habitats high and low, wet and dry.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈspænɪŋ ˈhæbəˌtæts haɪ ənd loʊ, wɛt ənd draɪ.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: All are carnivorous, but their diets range from fish eggs to alligators.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ɔl ər kɑrˈnɪvərəs, bət ðɛr daɪɪts reɪnʤ frəm fɪʃ ɛgz tɪ ˈæləˌgeɪtərz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Jaw-dropping anatomy allows most snakes to swallow their meals whole.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: jaw-dropping* əˈnætəmi əˈlaʊz moʊst sneɪks tɪ sˈwɔloʊ ðɛr milz hoʊl.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Because their jaw bones aren’t fused like ours
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: bɪˈkəz ðɛr ʤɔ boʊnz aren’t* fjuzd laɪk ɑrz

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: but connected by an elastic ligament and the bones on the sides of their lower jaws flare apart,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: bət kəˈnɛktɪd baɪ ən ɪˈlæstɪk ˈlɪgəmənt ənd ðə boʊnz ɔn ðə saɪdz əv ðɛr loʊər ʤɔz flɛr əˈpɑrt,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: they can dramatically stretch their maws.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðeɪ kən drəˈmætɪkəli strɛʧ ðɛr maws*.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Reticulated pythons can even achieve 180-degree gapes.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: reticulated* pythons* kən ˈivɪn əˈʧiv 180-degree* gapes*.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Sharp, curved teeth also line many snakes’ jaws, keeping prey from wriggling out.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ʃɑrp, kərvd tiθ ˈɔlsoʊ laɪn ˈmɛni snakes’*’ ʤɔz, ˈkipɪŋ preɪ frəm wriggling* aʊt.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: And to prevent suffocation when choking down a big meal, many snakes shift the position of their airways’ entrance
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ənd tɪ prɪˈvɛnt ˌsəfəˈkeɪʃən wɪn ˈʧoʊkɪŋ daʊn ə bɪg mil, ˈmɛni sneɪks ʃɪft ðə pəˈzɪʃən əv ðɛr airways’*’ ˈɛntrəns

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: and isolate which rib cage regions they inhale with.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ənd ˈaɪsəˌleɪt wɪʧ rɪb keɪʤ ˈriʤənz ðeɪ ˌɪnˈheɪl wɪθ.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Pythons are also equipped with stretchy tissue along their jaws,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: pythons* ər ˈɔlsoʊ ɪkˈwɪpt wɪθ stretchy* ˈtɪʃu əˈlɔŋ ðɛr ʤɔz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: enabling them to spread four times wider than their own skulls.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ɪˈneɪbəlɪŋ ðɛm tɪ sprɛd fɔr taɪmz ˈwaɪdər ðən ðɛr oʊn skəlz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: They’ve been documented using this skill to eat hyenas, alligators,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: they’ve* bɪn ˈdɑkjəˌmɛnəd ˈjuzɪŋ ðɪs skɪl tɪ it haɪˈinəz, ˈæləˌgeɪtərz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: and— yes— even humans, whole.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: and—*— yes—*— ˈivɪn ˈjumənz, hoʊl.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Their skin might sag after all the stretching and it could take weeks to metabolize meals this large,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðɛr skɪn maɪt sæg ˈæftər ɔl ðə ˈstrɛʧɪŋ ənd ɪt kʊd teɪk wiks tɪ məˈtæbəˌlaɪz milz ðɪs lɑrʤ,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: but it seems their intestines have special cells for helping digest bones,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: bət ɪt simz ðɛr ˌɪnˈtɛstənz hæv ˈspɛʃəl sɛlz fər ˈhɛlpɪŋ ˈdaɪʤɛst boʊnz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: and they can sometimes survive for over a year on just one feast.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ənd ðeɪ kən ˈsəmˌtaɪmz sərˈvaɪv fər ˈoʊvər ə jɪr ɔn ʤɪst wən fist.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: African egg-eating snakes, meanwhile, consume large, intact bird eggs,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈæfrɪkɑn egg-eating* sneɪks, ˈminˌwaɪl, kənˈsum lɑrʤ, ˌɪnˈtækt bərd ɛgz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: piercing their shells in their esophagi using inward-facing vertebral spines.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈpɪrsɪŋ ðɛr ʃɛlz ɪn ðɛr esophagi* ˈjuzɪŋ inward-facing* ˈvərtəbrəl spaɪnz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: But while many snakes make a show of swallowing things whole,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: bət waɪl ˈmɛni sneɪks meɪk ə ʃoʊ əv sˈwɑloʊɪŋ θɪŋz hoʊl,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: crab-eating snakes often pry their prey’s limbs off one-by-one
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: crab-eating* sneɪks ˈɔfən praɪ ðɛr prey’s* lɪmz ɔf one-by-one*

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: and blindsnakes decapitate their termite targets,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ənd blindsnakes* diˈkæpəˌteɪt ðɛr ˈtərmaɪt ˈtɑrgəts,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: probably to prioritize their more digestible bodies.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈprɑbəˌbli tɪ praɪˈɔrəˌtaɪz ðɛr mɔr daɪˈʤɛstəbəl ˈbɑdiz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Some blindsnakes actually use chemical secretions to repel their prey,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: səm blindsnakes* ˈæˌkʧuəli juz ˈkɛmɪkəl səˈkriʃənz tɪ rɪˈpɛl ðɛr preɪ,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: because it allows them to hang out in ant colonies,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: bɪˈkəz ɪt əˈlaʊz ðɛm tɪ hæŋ aʊt ɪn ænt ˈkɑləniz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: snacking on their inhabitants without being attacked.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: sˈnækɪŋ ɔn ðɛr ˌɪnˈhæbɪtənts wɪˈθaʊt biɪŋ əˈtækt.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Their skills have even been noticed by other species.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðɛr skɪlz hæv ˈivɪn bɪn ˈnoʊtɪst baɪ ˈəðər ˈspiʃiz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Eastern screech owls sometimes place blindsnakes in their nests,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈistərn skriʧ aʊlz ˈsəmˌtaɪmz pleɪs blindsnakes* ɪn ðɛr nɛsts,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: where they eat the insects that might otherwise harm their young.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: wɛr ðeɪ it ðə ˈɪnˌsɛkts ðət maɪt ˈəðərˌwaɪz hɑrm ðɛr jəŋ.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: In fact, baby owls with a blindsnake nestmate have higher rates
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ɪn fækt, ˈbeɪbi aʊlz wɪθ ə blindsnake* nestmate* hæv haɪər reɪts

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: of growth and survival.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: əv groʊθ ənd sərˈvaɪvəl.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: The same can't be said for other species.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðə seɪm kænt bi sɛd fər ˈəðər ˈspiʃiz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Turtle-headed sea snakes scrape fish eggs off coral reefs.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: turtle-headed* si sneɪks skreɪp fɪʃ ɛgz ɔf ˈkɔrəl rifs.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: And cat-eyed snakes hunt red-eyed tree frog eggs.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ənd cat-eyed* sneɪks hənt red-eyed* tri frɑg ɛgz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: However, even as embryos, the developing frogs can sense an attacking snake’s vibrations
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ˈivɪn ɛz ˈɛmbriˌoʊz, ðə dɪˈvɛləpɪŋ frɑgz kən sɛns ən əˈtækɪŋ snake’s* vaɪˈbreɪʃənz

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: and may prematurely hatch to escape.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ənd meɪ ˌpriməˈʧʊrli hæʧ tɪ ɪˈskeɪp.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: It’s a sacrifice— but at least it’s not certain death by serpent.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: it’s* ə sacrifice—*— bət æt list it’s* nɑt ˈsərtən dɛθ baɪ ˈsərpənt.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Garter snakes, meanwhile, go after western newts
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈgɑrtər sneɪks, ˈminˌwaɪl, goʊ ˈæftər ˈwɛstərn nuts

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: even though their skin is packed with a potent neurotoxin.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈivɪn ðoʊ ðɛr skɪn ɪz pækt wɪθ ə ˈpoʊtənt neurotoxin*.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Just one bite could kill a person,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ʤɪst wən baɪt kʊd kɪl ə ˈpərsən,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: but garter snakes swallow the newts unscathed,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: bət ˈgɑrtər sneɪks sˈwɔloʊ ðə nuts ənˈskeɪðd,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: since modified proteins in their nerve cells prevent the toxin from binding.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: sɪns ˈmɑdəˌfaɪd ˈproʊˌtinz ɪn ðɛr nərv sɛlz prɪˈvɛnt ðə ˈtɑksən frəm ˈbaɪndɪŋ.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Not only that, but since the toxin may remain in their livers for weeks afterwards,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: nɑt ˈoʊnli ðət, bət sɪns ðə ˈtɑksən meɪ rɪˈmeɪn ɪn ðɛr ˈlɪvərz fər wiks ˈæftərwərdz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: it may even end up lending garter snakes protection against their predators.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ɪt meɪ ˈivɪn ɛnd əp ˈlɛndɪŋ ˈgɑrtər sneɪks prəˈtɛkʃən əˈgɛnst ðɛr ˈprɛdətərz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: However, snakes more often make their own toxins,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, sneɪks mɔr ˈɔfən meɪk ðɛr oʊn ˈtɑksənz,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: injecting or spitting venom from specialized glands
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˌɪnˈʤɛktɪŋ ər ˈspɪtɪŋ ˈvɛnəm frəm ˈspɛʃəˌlaɪzd glændz

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: out of grooved or syringe-like fangs.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: aʊt əv grooved* ər syringe-like* fæŋz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Philippine cobra venom is full of fast-acting neurotoxins
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈfɪləˌpin ˈkoʊbrə ˈvɛnəm ɪz fʊl əv fast-acting* neurotoxins*

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: that cause paralysis and respiratory failure.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðət kɔz pərˈælɪsɪs ənd ˈrɛspərəˌtɔri ˈfeɪljər.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: West African saw-scaled viper venom is a cocktail of compounds
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: wɛst ˈæfrɪkɑn saw-scaled* ˈvaɪpər ˈvɛnəm ɪz ə ˈkɑkˌteɪl əv ˈkɑmpaʊndz

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: that cause profuse bleeding and tissue death.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðət kɔz prəfˈjus ˈblidɪŋ ənd ˈtɪʃu dɛθ.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: And while inland taipans are thought to possess the world's most potent venom,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ənd waɪl ˈɪnˌlænd taipans* ər θɔt tɪ pəˈzɛs ðə wərldz moʊst ˈpoʊtənt ˈvɛnəm,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: they tend to reserve it for quickly killing rodents.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ðeɪ tɛnd tɪ rɪˈzərv ɪt fər kˈwɪkli ˈkɪlɪŋ ˈroʊdənts.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: But we still haven’t answered how smaller snakes can consume bigger ones.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: bət wi stɪl haven’t* ˈænsərd haʊ sˈmɔlər sneɪks kən kənˈsum ˈbɪgər wənz.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: It turns out that the kingsnake does this with an elegant trick.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ɪt tərnz aʊt ðət ðə kingsnake* dɪz ðɪs wɪθ ən ˈɛləgənt trɪk.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Once it runs out of space in its flexible stomach,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: wəns ɪt rənz aʊt əv speɪs ɪn ɪts ˈflɛksəbəl ˈstəmək,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: it stretches and compresses its spine,
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ɪt ˈstrɛʧɪz ənd ˈkɑmprɛsəz ɪts spaɪn,

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: shoving the rattlesnake into a kinked, zig-zag shape within its digestive tract.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈʃəvɪŋ ðə ˈrætəlsˌneɪk ˈɪntu ə kinked*, ˈzɪgˌzæg ʃeɪp wɪˈθɪn ɪts daɪˈʤɛstɪv trækt.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: So a post-meal X-ray may look like this.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: soʊ ə post-meal* ˈɛksˌreɪ meɪ lʊk laɪk ðɪs.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Sorry, we know the truth can be hard to swallow.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ˈsɑri, wi noʊ ðə truθ kən bi hɑrd tɪ sˈwɔloʊ.

m2BE9GZJJbs.text: Unless, of course, you're a snake.
m2BE9GZJJbs.ipa: ənˈlɛs, əv kɔrs, jʊr ə sneɪk.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Our bodies are fluid factories, constantly producing, purging, and recycling secretions
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ɑr ˈbɑdiz ər fluɪd ˈfæktəriz, ˈkɑnstəntli prəˈdusɪŋ, ˈpərʤɪŋ, ənd riˈsaɪkəlɪŋ səˈkriʃənz

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: to fulfill all sorts of functions.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: tɪ fʊlˈfɪl ɔl sɔrts əv ˈfəŋkʃənz.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Our reproductive organs are no exception.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ɑr ˌriprəˈdəktɪv ˈɔrgənz ər noʊ ɪkˈsɛpʃən.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Vaginas are engaged in ongoing cycles of fluid discharge.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: vaginas* ər ɪnˈgeɪʤd ɪn ˈɔnˌgoʊɪŋ ˈsaɪkəlz əv fluɪd ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: But it can be hard to know what kind of discharge is “normal” when there’s taboo in talking about it—
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: bət ɪt kən bi hɑrd tɪ noʊ wət kaɪnd əv ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ ɪz ““normal”*” wɪn there’s* tæˈbu ɪn ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt it—*—

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: and absorbency tests for menstrual products tend to show anonymous blue fluids.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ənd əbˈzɔrbənsi tɛsts fər ˈmɛnstrəl ˈprɑdəkts tɛnd tɪ ʃoʊ əˈnɑnəməs blu fluɪdz.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: So, what’s healthy discharge? And when is there cause for concern?
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: soʊ, what’s* ˈhɛlθi ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ? ənd wɪn ɪz ðɛr kɔz fər kənˈsərn?

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Anyone who has a uterus might not experience much discharge early in life.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈɛniˌwən hu həz ə ˈjutərəs maɪt nɑt ɪkˈspɪriəns məʧ ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ ˈərli ɪn laɪf.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: But that changes with puberty, as the ovaries start producing estrogen.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: bət ðət ˈʧeɪnʤɪz wɪθ ˈpjubərti, ɛz ðə ˈoʊˌvəriz stɑrt prəˈdusɪŋ ˈɛstrəʤən.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: This causes the vagina’s inner lining— or epithelium— to thicken and cycle faster.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðɪs ˈkɔzɪz ðə vagina’s* ˈɪnər lining—*— ər epithelium—*— tɪ ˈθɪkən ənd ˈsaɪkəl ˈfæstər.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: These vaginal epithelial cells, in turn, start producing more glycogen,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðiz vəˈʤaɪnəl ɛpɪˈθɛljəl sɛlz, ɪn tərn, stɑrt prəˈdusɪŋ mɔr ˈglaɪkəʤɪn,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: which fuels the growth of lactic-acid- and-hydrogen-peroxide-producing Lactobacillus bacteria,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: wɪʧ fjuəlz ðə groʊθ əv lactic-acid*- and-hydrogen-peroxide-producing* ˌlæktoʊbəˈsɪləs bækˈtɪriə,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: which make the vagina more acidic.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: wɪʧ meɪk ðə vəˈʤaɪnə mɔr əˈsɪdɪk.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Specialized goblet cells in the cervix also begin releasing more mucus,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈspɛʃəˌlaɪzd ˈgɑblət sɛlz ɪn ðə ˈsərvɪks ˈɔlsoʊ bɪˈgɪn riˈlisɪŋ mɔr mˈjukəs,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: which is enriched with immunity-boosting proteins called immunoglobulins.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz ɛnˈrɪʧt wɪθ immunity-boosting* ˈproʊˌtinz kɔld immunoglobulins*.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: The acidic environment, regenerating vaginal epithelium,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðə əˈsɪdɪk ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt, riˈʤɛnərˌeɪtɪŋ vəˈʤaɪnəl epithelium*,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: and cervical mucus all help protect against infections.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ənd ˈsərvɪkəl mˈjukəs ɔl hɛlp prəˈtɛkt əˈgɛnst ˌɪnˈfɛkʃənz.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Vaginal discharge mainly consists of cervical mucus, sloughed epithelial cells, and bacteria,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: vəˈʤaɪnəl ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ ˈmeɪnli kənˈsɪsts əv ˈsərvɪkəl mˈjukəs, sloughed* ɛpɪˈθɛljəl sɛlz, ənd bækˈtɪriə,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: which all exit the vagina with the help of gravity.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: wɪʧ ɔl ˈɛksət ðə vəˈʤaɪnə wɪθ ðə hɛlp əv ˈgrævɪti.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Because of its acidity, discharge can sometimes bleach or leave holes in fabric.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: bɪˈkəz əv ɪts əˈsɪdəti, ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ kən ˈsəmˌtaɪmz bliʧ ər liv hoʊlz ɪn ˈfæbrɪk.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: But, of course, discharge varies considerably with the monthly menstrual cycle,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: bət, əv kɔrs, ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ ˈvɛriz kənˈsɪdərəbli wɪθ ðə ˈmənθli ˈmɛnstrəl ˈsaɪkəl,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: in sync with shifting levels of hormones, like estrogen and progesterone.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ɪn sɪŋk wɪθ ˈʃɪftɪŋ ˈlɛvəlz əv ˈhɔrˌmoʊnz, laɪk ˈɛstrəʤən ənd proʊˈʤɛstərˌoʊn.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: During the first half of the menstrual cycle, known as the follicular phase,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə fərst hæf əv ðə ˈmɛnstrəl ˈsaɪkəl, noʊn ɛz ðə follicular* feɪz,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: estrogen levels gradually increase, as a new egg, or follicle, develops.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈɛstrəʤən ˈlɛvəlz ˈgræʤuəli ˌɪnˈkris, ɛz ə nu ɛg, ər ˈfɑlɪkəl, dɪˈvɛləps.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: The uterus’s inner lining, the endometrium, thickens,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðə uterus’s* ˈɪnər ˈlaɪnɪŋ, ðə endometrium*, ˈθɪkənz,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: preparing for an embryo to embed should an egg get fertilized.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: pərˈpɛrɪŋ fər ən ˈɛmbriˌoʊ tɪ ɪmˈbɛd ʃʊd ən ɛg gɪt ˈfərtəˌlaɪzd.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Early in the follicular phase, cervical goblet cells produce mucus
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈərli ɪn ðə follicular* feɪz, ˈsərvɪkəl ˈgɑblət sɛlz ˈproʊdus mˈjukəs

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: that initially may be whitish, thick, or sticky.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðət ˌɪˈnɪʃəli meɪ bi ˈwaɪtɪʃ, θɪk, ər ˈstɪki.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: In the days prior to ovulation, as estrogen levels further increase and the ovary prepares to release an egg,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ɪn ðə deɪz praɪər tɪ ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən, ɛz ˈɛstrəʤən ˈlɛvəlz ˈfərðər ˌɪnˈkris ənd ðə ˈoʊˌvəri pərˈpɛrz tɪ riˈlis ən ɛg,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: these cervical goblet cells secrete a thinner, clearer mucus, which is easier for sperm to travel through.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðiz ˈsərvɪkəl ˈgɑblət sɛlz sɪˈkrit ə ˈθɪnər, ˈklɪrər mˈjukəs, wɪʧ ɪz ˈiziər fər spərm tɪ ˈtrævəl θru.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: And in the days following ovulation, progesterone levels increase,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ənd ɪn ðə deɪz ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən, proʊˈʤɛstərˌoʊn ˈlɛvəlz ˌɪnˈkris,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: stabilizing the endometrium in preparation for pregnancy.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈsteɪbəˌlaɪzɪŋ ðə endometrium* ɪn ˌprɛpərˈeɪʃən fər ˈprɛgnənsi.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: The cervix produces thicker, cloudier, and sometimes yellowy-colored mucus
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðə ˈsərvɪks prəˈdusɪz ˈθɪkər, cloudier*, ənd ˈsəmˌtaɪmz yellowy-colored* mˈjukəs

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: that now helps form a physical barrier against sperm and pathogens.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðət naʊ hɛlps fɔrm ə ˈfɪzɪkəl ˈbɛriər əˈgɛnst spərm ənd ˈpæθəʤənz.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: If no embryo implants, estrogen and progesterone levels start falling,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ɪf noʊ ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ˌɪmˈplænts, ˈɛstrəʤən ənd proʊˈʤɛstərˌoʊn ˈlɛvəlz stɑrt ˈfɑlɪŋ,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: leading to menstruation.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈlidɪŋ tɪ ˌmɛnstruˈeɪʃən.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: And over the course of around 2 to 7 days, the endometrium clears its excess tissues,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ənd ˈoʊvər ðə kɔrs əv əraʊnd 2 tɪ 7 deɪz, ðə endometrium* klɪrz ɪts ˈɛkˌsɛs ˈtɪʃuz,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: and approximately 20 to 90 milliliters worth of menstrual fluid leaves the vagina.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ənd əˈprɑksəmətli 20 tɪ 90 ˈmɪləˌlitərz wərθ əv ˈmɛnstrəl fluɪd livz ðə vəˈʤaɪnə.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: If a pregnancy does occur, it induces a surge in cervical mucus,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ɪf ə ˈprɛgnənsi dɪz əˈkər, ɪt ˌɪnˈdusɪz ə sərʤ ɪn ˈsərvɪkəl mˈjukəs,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: which forms a thick, jelly-like plug that helps seal off the womb.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: wɪʧ fɔrmz ə θɪk, jelly-like* pləg ðət hɛlps sil ɔf ðə wum.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Oftentimes, this mucus plug is discharged before labor, as the cervix softens and opens.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈɔfənˌtaɪmz, ðɪs mˈjukəs pləg ɪz ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤd ˌbiˈfɔr ˈleɪbər, ɛz ðə ˈsərvɪks ˈsɔfənz ənd ˈoʊpənz.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Meanwhile, factors beyond pregnancy and the usual ebb and flow of the menstrual cycle
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈminˌwaɪl, ˈfæktərz bɪɔnd ˈprɛgnənsi ənd ðə ˈjuʒəwəl ɛb ənd floʊ əv ðə ˈmɛnstrəl ˈsaɪkəl

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: also influence vaginal discharge.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈɔlsoʊ ˈɪnfluəns vəˈʤaɪnəl ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Sexual arousal can trigger fluid to make its way out of tiny blood vessels and across the wall of the vagina.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈsɛkʃuəl ərˈaʊzəl kən ˈtrɪgər fluɪd tɪ meɪk ɪts weɪ aʊt əv ˈtaɪni bləd ˈvɛsəlz ənd əˈkrɔs ðə wɔl əv ðə vəˈʤaɪnə.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: This clear vaginal transudate, comprised mostly of water and proteins, helps provide lubrication
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðɪs klɪr vəˈʤaɪnəl transudate*, kəmˈpraɪzd ˈmoʊstli əv ˈwɔtər ənd ˈproʊˌtinz, hɛlps prəˈvaɪd ˌlubrɪˈkeɪʃən

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: along with secretions from glands at the opening of the vagina.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: əˈlɔŋ wɪθ səˈkriʃənz frəm glændz æt ðə ˈoʊpənɪŋ əv ðə vəˈʤaɪnə.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Hormonal contraceptives can also cause differences in discharge because they tend to cause thicker cervical mucus,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈhɔrˌmoʊnəl ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪvz kən ˈɔlsoʊ kɔz ˈdɪfərənsɪz ɪn ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ bɪˈkəz ðeɪ tɛnd tɪ kɔz ˈθɪkər ˈsərvɪkəl mˈjukəs,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: similar to the kind otherwise associated with the 2-week phase following ovulation.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈsɪmələr tɪ ðə kaɪnd ˈəðərˌwaɪz əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪtəd wɪθ ðə 2-week* feɪz ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Menopause also alters things: as estrogen levels decrease,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈmɛnəˌpaʊz ˈɔlsoʊ ˈɔltərz θɪŋz: ɛz ˈɛstrəʤən ˈlɛvəlz ˈdiˌkris,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: the vaginal epithelium thins and proliferates at a slower rate,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðə vəˈʤaɪnəl epithelium* θɪnz ənd proʊˈlɪfərˌeɪts æt ə sloʊər reɪt,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: the cervix secretes less mucus, and the reproductive microbiome changes, resulting in less discharge overall.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðə ˈsərvɪks secretes* lɛs mˈjukəs, ənd ðə ˌriprəˈdəktɪv microbiome* ˈʧeɪnʤɪz, rɪˈzəltɪŋ ɪn lɛs ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ ˈoʊvərˌɔl.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: In addition to these shifting factors, everyone's baseline production of discharge varies greatly.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ɪn əˈdɪʃən tɪ ðiz ˈʃɪftɪŋ ˈfæktərz, ˈɛvriˌwənz ˈbeɪsˌlaɪn pərˈdəkʃən əv ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ ˈvɛriz ˈgreɪtli.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: What’s important to note is if the baseline changes.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: what’s* ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt tɪ noʊt ɪz ɪf ðə ˈbeɪsˌlaɪn ˈʧeɪnʤɪz.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: For example, clumpy white discharge can occur when more yeast and epithelial cells are being shed than usual—
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ˈkləmpi waɪt ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ kən əˈkər wɪn mɔr jist ənd ɛpɪˈθɛljəl sɛlz ər biɪŋ ʃɛd ðən usual—*—

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: and may indicate an active yeast infection.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ənd meɪ ˈɪndəˌkeɪt ən ˈæktɪv jist ˌɪnˈfɛkʃən.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Thin, gray or white, odorous discharge, on the other hand, might be a sign that certain bacteria are flourishing
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: θɪn, greɪ ər waɪt, ˈoʊdərəs ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ, ɔn ðə ˈəðər hænd, maɪt bi ə saɪn ðət ˈsərtən bækˈtɪriə ər ˈflərɪʃɪŋ

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: and making the vagina less acidic, consistent with an infection called bacterial vaginosis.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ənd ˈmeɪkɪŋ ðə vəˈʤaɪnə lɛs əˈsɪdɪk, kənˈsɪstənt wɪθ ən ˌɪnˈfɛkʃən kɔld bækˈtɪriəl vaginosis*.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: Sexually transmitted infections, polyps, hormonal disorders, and other conditions
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˈsɛkʃuəli trænzˈmɪtəd ˌɪnˈfɛkʃənz, ˈpɑlɪps, ˈhɔrˌmoʊnəl dɪˈsɔrdərz, ənd ˈəðər kənˈdɪʃənz

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: can also cause differences in discharge. Because changes may be caused by a number of things,
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: kən ˈɔlsoʊ kɔz ˈdɪfərənsɪz ɪn ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ. bɪˈkəz ˈʧeɪnʤɪz meɪ bi kɔzd baɪ ə ˈnəmbər əv θɪŋz,

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: instead of self-diagnosing, it’s a good idea to have a doctor weigh in
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ˌɪnˈstɛd əv self-diagnosing*, it’s* ə gʊd aɪˈdiə tɪ hæv ə ˈdɔktər weɪ ɪn

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: in the case of any unusual bleeding, itching, pain, color, or odor.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ɪn ðə keɪs əv ˈɛni ənˈjuˌʒuəl ˈblidɪŋ, ˈɪʧɪŋ, peɪn, ˈkələr, ər ˈoʊdər.

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: The usual variation in vaginal discharge, though, is simply a sign that the fluid factory of the body is at work
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ðə ˈjuʒəwəl ˌvɛriˈeɪʃən ɪn vəˈʤaɪnəl ˈdɪsˌʧɑrʤ, ðoʊ, ɪz ˈsɪmpli ə saɪn ðət ðə fluɪd ˈfæktəri əv ðə ˈbɑdi ɪz æt wərk

FKUc8vg_Lus.text: and operating in good order.
FKUc8vg_Lus.ipa: ənd ˈɔpərˌeɪtɪŋ ɪn gʊd ˈɔrdər.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Pippa.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: hɛˈloʊ, ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm pippa*.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Many of us don't think twice about where the food in our kitchen comes from.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ˈmɛni əv ˈjuˈɛs doʊnt θɪŋk twaɪs əˈbaʊt wɛr ðə fud ɪn ɑr ˈkɪʧən kəmz frəm.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: One trip to the supermarket and we can buy anything we want, whether that's fruit grown in South America,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: wən trɪp tɪ ðə ˈsupərˌmɑrkɪt ənd wi kən baɪ ˈɛniˌθɪŋ wi wɔnt, ˈwɛðər ðæts frut groʊn ɪn saʊθ əˈmɛrɪkə,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: tomatoes from Spain or rice flown in from Asia. Do you know where the food you buy comes from, Neil?
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: tomatoes* frəm speɪn ər raɪs floʊn ɪn frəm ˈeɪʒə. du ju noʊ wɛr ðə fud ju baɪ kəmz frəm, neil?

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: I try to pay attention and buy stuff that's locally produced, but actually, sometimes I don't really look.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: aɪ traɪ tɪ peɪ əˈtɛnʃən ənd baɪ stəf ðæts ˈloʊkəli prəˈdust, bət ˈæˌkʧuəli, ˈsəmˌtaɪmz aɪ doʊnt ˈrɪli lʊk.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: How about you? I actually have a calendar on my kitchen wall, which tells me
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: haʊ əˈbaʊt ju? aɪ ˈæˌkʧuəli hæv ə ˈkæləndər ɔn maɪ ˈkɪʧən wɔl, wɪʧ tɛlz me

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: which fruits and vegetables are grown at what times of year.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: wɪʧ fruts ənd ˈvɛʤtəbəlz ər groʊn æt wət taɪmz əv jɪr.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: The reality is that the supply of fresh food to the UK is fragile. The UK imports around 40% of its food,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ðə ˌriˈæləˌti ɪz ðət ðə səˈplaɪ əv frɛʃ fud tɪ ðə uk* ɪz ˈfræʤəl. ðə uk* ˌɪmˈpɔrts əraʊnd 40 əv ɪts fud,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: and this supply chain can easily be disrupted by wars, shortages and, increasingly, by climate change.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd ðɪs səˈplaɪ ʧeɪn kən ˈizəli bi dɪsˈrəptɪd baɪ wɔrz, ˈʃɔrtɪʤɪz ənd, ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋgli, baɪ ˈklaɪmɪt ʧeɪnʤ.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: In fact, the UK government's Department for Environment, Food
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ɪn fækt, ðə uk* ˈgəvərnmənts dɪˈpɑrtmənt fər ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt, fud

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: and Rural Affairs recently reported on the importance of food security –
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd ˈrʊrəl əˈfɛrz ˈrisəntli ˌriˈpɔrtəd ɔn ðə ˌɪmˈpɔrtəns əv fud sɪˈkjʊrəti –*

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: the term for having a reliable enough supply of affordable, healthy food to feed everyone. In this episode,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ðə tərm fər ˈhævɪŋ ə rɪˈlaɪəbəl ɪˈnəf səˈplaɪ əv əˈfɔrdəbəl, ˈhɛlθi fud tɪ fid ˈɛvriˌwən. ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: we'll be investigating the issues affecting food production in the UK. As usual, we'll learn some useful new vocabulary,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: wɪl bi ˌɪnˈvɛstəˌgeɪtɪŋ ðə ˈɪʃuz əˈfɛktɪŋ fud pərˈdəkʃən ɪn ðə uk*. ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl lərn səm ˈjusfəl nu voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: and remember – there's a quiz, worksheet and transcript of this episode on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd rɪˈmɛmbər –* ðɛrz ə kwɪz, ˈwərkˌʃit ənd ˈtrænˌskrɪpt əv ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: But first, of course, Pippa, I have a quiz question for you.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: bət fərst, əv kɔrs, pippa*, aɪ hæv ə kwɪz kˈwɛʃən fər ju.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: I mentioned that Britain imports 40% of its food from other producers, mostly in Europe.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: aɪ ˈmɛnʃənd ðət ˈbrɪtən ˌɪmˈpɔrts 40 əv ɪts fud frəm ˈəðər prəˈdusərz, ˈmoʊstli ɪn ˈjʊrəp.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: But which European country does the UK import the most food from?
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: but* wɪʧ ˌjʊrəˈpiən ˈkəntri dɪz ðə uk* ˌɪmˈpɔrt ðə moʊst fud from?

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Is it: a) France, b) the Netherlands, or c) Ireland?
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: is* ɪt: ə) fræns, bi) ðə ˈnɛðərləndz, ər si) ireland?

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Hmm. I've seen the Netherlands on a lot of food packaging in the UK, so I'm going to say b) the Netherlands.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: həm. aɪv sin ðə ˈnɛðərləndz ɔn ə lɔt əv fud ˈpækɪʤɪŋ ɪn ðə uk*, soʊ əm goʊɪŋ tɪ seɪ bi) ðə ˈnɛðərləndz.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: OK. Well, we'll find out if you're right later in the programme. According to the UK government's recent report,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ. wɛl, wɪl faɪnd aʊt ɪf jʊr raɪt ˈleɪtər ɪn ðə ˈproʊˌgræm. əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ðə uk* ˈgəvərnmənts ˈrisənt rɪˈpɔrt,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: there are many issues affecting Britain's food supply: geopolitical conflicts like those in Ukraine and Iran, droughts
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ðɛr ər ˈmɛni ˈɪʃuz əˈfɛktɪŋ ˈbrɪtənz fud səˈplaɪ: ˌʤioʊpəˈlɪtɪkəl ˈkɑnflɪkts laɪk ðoʊz ɪn juˈkreɪn ənd ˌɪˈrɑn, draʊts

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: and water shortages, loss of biodiversity caused by climate change... the list goes on and on.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd ˈwɔtər ˈʃɔrtɪʤɪz, lɔs əv ˌbaɪoʊdaɪˈvərsəti kɔzd baɪ ˈklaɪmɪt ʧeɪnʤ... ðə lɪst goʊz ɔn ənd ɔn.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Here, climate reporter Mark Poynting discusses the report's conclusions with BBC Radio 4's The Food Programme.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: hir, ˈklaɪmɪt rɪˈpɔrtər mɑrk poynting* dɪˈskəsɪz ðə rɪˈpɔrts kənˈkluʒənz wɪθ ˌbibiˈsi ˈreɪdiˌoʊ 4's* ðə fud ˈproʊˌgræm.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: And it really draws on scientific research to come up with what it calls a reasonable worst-case scenario
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd ɪt ˈrɪli drɔz ɔn ˌsaɪənˈtɪfɪk ˈrisərʧ tɪ kəm əp wɪθ wət ɪt kɔlz ə ˈrizənəbəl ˈwərstˈkeɪs sɪˈnɛrioʊ

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: for how biodiversity loss could affect UK security. And its conclusions are really quite stark.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: fər haʊ ˌbaɪoʊdaɪˈvərsəti lɔs kʊd əˈfɛkt uk* sɪˈkjʊrəti. ənd ɪts kənˈkluʒənz ər ˈrɪli kwaɪt stɑrk.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: The first line of the report says: Global ecosystem degradation
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ðə fərst laɪn əv ðə rɪˈpɔrt sɪz: ˈgloʊbəl ˈikoʊˌsɪstəm ˌdɛgrəˈdeɪʃən

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: and collapse threatens the UK's national security.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd kəˈlæps θˈrɛtənz ðə uk's* ˈnæʃənəl sɪˈkjʊrəti.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: The report describes a worst-case scenario where Britain runs out of food.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ðə rɪˈpɔrt dɪˈskraɪbz ə ˈwərstˈkeɪs sɪˈnɛrioʊ wɛr ˈbrɪtən rənz aʊt əv fud.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: A worst-case scenario is the worst possible thing that could happen in a particular situation.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ə ˈwərstˈkeɪs sɪˈnɛrioʊ ɪz ðə wərst ˈpɑsəbəl θɪŋ ðət kʊd ˈhæpən ɪn ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Mark says the report's conclusions are stark – an adjective which in this case means obvious or severe.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: mɑrk sɪz ðə rɪˈpɔrts kənˈkluʒənz ər stɑrk –* ən ˈæʤɪktɪv wɪʧ ɪn ðɪs keɪs minz ˈɑbviəs ər səˈvɪr.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Another problem with food security relates to climate change.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: əˈnəðər ˈprɑbləm wɪθ fud sɪˈkjʊrəti rɪˈleɪts tɪ ˈklaɪmɪt ʧeɪnʤ.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Carbon emissions from food production make up around a third of all greenhouse gases,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ˈkɑrbən ɪˈmɪʃənz frəm fud pərˈdəkʃən meɪk əp əraʊnd ə θərd əv ɔl ˈgrinˌhaʊs ˈgæsɪz,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: from the diesel used to drive tractors, to fertilisers, to cows and sheep producing methane.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: frəm ðə ˈdizəl juzd tɪ draɪv ˈtræktərz, tɪ fertilisers*, tɪ kaʊz ənd ʃip prəˈdusɪŋ ˈmɛˌθeɪn.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Emma Pinchbeck is the chief executive of the UK's Climate Change Committee.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ˈɛmə pinchbeck* ɪz ðə ʧif ɪgˈzɛkjətɪv əv ðə uk's* ˈklaɪmɪt ʧeɪnʤ kəˈmɪti.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: She spoke about this problem with BBC Radio 4's The Food Programme. The biggest chunk of emissions from agriculture
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ʃi spoʊk əˈbaʊt ðɪs ˈprɑbləm wɪθ ˌbibiˈsi ˈreɪdiˌoʊ 4's* ðə fud ˈproʊˌgræm. ðə ˈbɪgəst ʧəŋk əv ɪˈmɪʃənz frəm ˈægrɪˌkəlʧər

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: comes from simply having livestock on the land. That's the bit of the puzzle that we need to think about going forward.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: kəmz frəm ˈsɪmpli ˈhævɪŋ ˈlaɪvˌstɑk ɔn ðə lænd. ðæts ðə bɪt əv ðə ˈpəzəl ðət wi nid tɪ θɪŋk əˈbaʊt goʊɪŋ ˈfɔrwərd.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: And in the way that we've thought about it,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd ɪn ðə weɪ ðət wiv θɔt əˈbaʊt ɪt,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: for every cow or sheep we take off the land, we put on something else.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: fər ˈɛvəri kaʊ ər ʃip wi teɪk ɔf ðə lænd, wi pʊt ɔn ˈsəmθɪŋ ɛls.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: And what that does overall is it makes farmers and landowners part of the solution to climate change, not the problem.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd wət ðət dɪz ˈoʊvərˌɔl ɪz ɪt meɪks ˈfɑrmərz ənd ˈlænˌdoʊnərz pɑrt əv ðə səˈluʃən tɪ ˈklaɪmɪt ʧeɪnʤ, nɑt ðə ˈprɑbləm.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Livestock – that's farm animals such as cattle, sheep and pigs – contribute most of the carbon emissions from agriculture.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ˈlaɪvˌstɑk –* ðæts fɑrm ˈænəməlz səʧ ɛz ˈkætəl, ʃip ənd pɪgz –* kənˈtrɪbjut moʊst əv ðə ˈkɑrbən ɪˈmɪʃənz frəm ˈægrɪˌkəlʧər.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Moving towards a sustainable food supply involves reducing the amount of meat
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ˈmuvɪŋ təˈwɔrdz ə səˈsteɪnəbəl fud səˈplaɪ ˌɪnˈvɑlvz rɪˈdusɪŋ ðə əˈmaʊnt əv mit

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: and dairy we eat in favour of non-animal protein such as beans and lentils.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd ˈdɛri wi it ɪn ˈfeɪvər əv non-animal* ˈproʊˌtin səʧ ɛz binz ənd ˈlɛntəlz.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Encouraging farmers and consumers to move away from meat is a piece of the puzzle, according to Emma.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ɪnˈkərəʤɪŋ ˈfɑrmərz ənd kənˈsumərz tɪ muv əˈweɪ frəm mit ɪz ə pis əv ðə ˈpəzəl, əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ˈɛmə.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: A piece of the puzzle means a part of the solution to a problem –
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ə pis əv ðə ˈpəzəl minz ə pɑrt əv ðə səˈluʃən tɪ ə ˈprɑbləm –*

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: in this case, the twin problems of food security and climate change.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ɪn ðɪs keɪs, ðə twɪn ˈprɑbləmz əv fud sɪˈkjʊrəti ənd ˈklaɪmɪt ʧeɪnʤ.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Emma says it's important to think again about how farmland is used.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ˈɛmə sɪz ɪts ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt tɪ θɪŋk əˈgɛn əˈbaʊt haʊ ˈfɑrmˌlænd ɪz juzd.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: For every cow or sheep taken off the land, something else, such as a fruit tree or vegetable crop,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: fər ˈɛvəri kaʊ ər ʃip ˈteɪkən ɔf ðə lænd, ˈsəmθɪŋ ɛls, səʧ ɛz ə frut tri ər ˈvɛʤtəbəl krɑp,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: is put in its place. That way, farmers become part of the solution, not the problem – an idiom meaning that
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ɪz pʊt ɪn ɪts pleɪs. ðət weɪ, ˈfɑrmərz bɪˈkəm pɑrt əv ðə səˈluʃən, nɑt ðə ˈprɑbləm –* ən ˈɪdiəm ˈminɪŋ ðət

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: if you are not actively trying to fix a problem, you are helping it continue.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ɪf ju ər nɑt ˈæktɪvli traɪɪŋ tɪ fɪks ə ˈprɑbləm, ju ər ˈhɛlpɪŋ ɪt kənˈtɪnju.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Lots of ideas to chew over there, Neil. But now, isn't it time you revealed the answer to your question?
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: lots* əv aɪˈdiəz tɪ ʧu ˈoʊvər ðɛr, nil. bət naʊ, ˈɪzənt ɪt taɪm ju rɪˈvild ðə ˈænsər tɪ jʊr question?

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: My question was: Which European country does the UK import the most food from?
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: my* kˈwɛʃən wɑz: wɪʧ ˌjʊrəˈpiən ˈkəntri dɪz ðə uk* ˌɪmˈpɔrt ðə moʊst fud frəm?

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: And you said b) the Netherlands... and you were absolutely correct, Pippa. It is the Netherlands.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd ju sɛd bi) ðə ˈnɛðərləndz... ənd ju wər ˌæbsəˈlutli kərˈɛkt, pippa*. ɪt ɪz ðə ˈnɛðərləndz.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: The UK imports £7.6 billion worth of imports, especially vegetables and dairy.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ðə uk* ˌɪmˈpɔrts £7.6* ˈbɪljən wərθ əv ˌɪmˈpɔrts, əˈspɛʃəli ˈvɛʤtəbəlz ənd ˈdɛri.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: OK. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with food security – the situation where a country has
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv ˈlərnɪd, ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ fud sɪˈkjʊrəti –* ðə ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən wɛr ə ˈkəntri həz

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: a reliable enough supply of food to feed its population.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ə rɪˈlaɪəbəl ɪˈnəf səˈplaɪ əv fud tɪ fid ɪts ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: The worst-case scenario is the worst possible thing that could happen in a particular situation.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ðə ˈwərstˈkeɪs sɪˈnɛrioʊ ɪz ðə wərst ˈpɑsəbəl θɪŋ ðət kʊd ˈhæpən ɪn ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: The adjective stark has several meanings, including obvious and severe.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ðə ˈæʤɪktɪv stɑrk həz ˈsɛvərəl ˈminɪŋz, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ˈɑbviəs ənd səˈvɪr.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Livestock are animals and birds that are kept on a farm, such as cows, sheep or chickens.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ˈlaɪvˌstɑk ər ˈænəməlz ənd bərdz ðət ər kɛpt ɔn ə fɑrm, səʧ ɛz kaʊz, ʃip ər ˈʧɪkənz.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: The phrase a piece of the puzzle means a part of the solution to a problem.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ðə freɪz ə pis əv ðə ˈpəzəl minz ə pɑrt əv ðə səˈluʃən tɪ ə ˈprɑbləm.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: And finally, the idiom be part of the solution,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: ənd ˈfaɪnəli, ðə ˈɪdiəm bi pɑrt əv ðə səˈluʃən,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: not the problem means that if you are not actively trying to fix a problem, you are helping it continue.
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: nɑt ðə ˈprɑbləm minz ðət ɪf ju ər nɑt ˈæktɪvli traɪɪŋ tɪ fɪks ə ˈprɑbləm, ju ər ˈhɛlpɪŋ ɪt kənˈtɪnju.

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but if this topic has made you hungry for more,
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: wəns əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp, bət ɪf ðɪs ˈtɑpɪk həz meɪd ju ˈhəŋgri fər mɔr,

YjwHsdFZPxE.text: why not check out our food topic page on our website, bbclearningenglish.com? Bye for now! Goodbye!
YjwHsdFZPxE.ipa: why* nɑt ʧɛk aʊt ɑr fud ˈtɑpɪk peɪʤ ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*? baɪ fər naʊ! goodbye!

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Becca.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: hɛˈloʊ. ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm ˈbɛkɑ.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Neil, are there any foods that you used to really hate in the past but now don't mind?
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: neil*, ər ðɛr ˈɛni fudz ðət ju juzd tɪ ˈrɪli heɪt ɪn ðə pæst bət naʊ doʊnt maɪnd?

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Yes, actually there's a Japanese food called umeboshi which, when I first tried it, I really didn't like.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: jɛs, ˈæˌkʧuəli ðɛrz ə ˌʤæpəˈniz fud kɔld umeboshi* wɪʧ, wɪn aɪ fərst traɪd ɪt, aɪ ˈrɪli ˈdɪdənt laɪk.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: But after a while, I got used to it and actually now, I really love it. How about you?
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: bət ˈæftər ə waɪl, aɪ gɑt juzd tɪ ɪt ənd ˈæˌkʧuəli naʊ, aɪ ˈrɪli ləv ɪt. haʊ əˈbaʊt you?

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: A similar story to me with olives. I used to really hate olives,
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ə ˈsɪmələr ˈstɔri tɪ mi wɪθ ˈɑlɪvz. aɪ juzd tɪ ˈrɪli heɪt ˈɑlɪvz,

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: but as I've grown older, I've also grown to love them.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: bət ɛz aɪv groʊn ˈoʊldər, aɪv ˈɔlsoʊ groʊn tɪ ləv ðɛm.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Well, in this episode, we'll be learning from food experts about why there are
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: wɛl, ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, wɪl bi ˈlərnɪŋ frəm fud ˈɛkspərts əˈbaʊt waɪ ðɛr ər

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: some foods we just hate and whether it's possible to learn to love them.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: səm fudz wi ʤɪst heɪt ənd ˈwɛðər ɪts ˈpɑsəbəl tɪ lərn tɪ ləv ðɛm.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Yes, and as usual, you can find a transcript for this episode
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: jɛs, ənd ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, ju kən faɪnd ə ˈtrænˌskrɪpt fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: along with all the vocabulary and a worksheet on our website, bbclearningenglish.com. Okay, the question for you, Becca.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: əˈlɔŋ wɪθ ɔl ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri ənd ə ˈwərkˌʃit ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*. ˌoʊˈkeɪ, ðə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, ˈbɛkɑ.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: What word means to have a fear of new things, such as trying new foods.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: wət wərd minz tɪ hæv ə fɪr əv nu θɪŋz, səʧ ɛz traɪɪŋ nu fudz.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Is it a) aerophobia, b) claustrophobia, or c) neophobia?
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: is* ɪt ə) aerophobia*, bi) ˌklɔstrəˈfoʊbiə, ər si) neophobia?

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Well, Neil, I'll go for c) neophobia because neo sounds like 'new'.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: wɛl, nil, aɪl goʊ fər si) neophobia* bɪˈkəz ˈnioʊ saʊnz laɪk 'nu'.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: That's clever thinking but let's see. Now though, back to food.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ðæts ˈklɛvər ˈθɪŋkɪŋ bət lɛts si. naʊ ðoʊ, bæk tɪ fud.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Some experts have said that we can teach ourselves to like new things. Ruth Alexander asks Doctor Dana Small of McGill University,
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: səm ˈɛkspərts hæv sɛd ðət wi kən tiʧ ɑrˈsɛlvz tɪ laɪk nu θɪŋz. ruθ ˌælɪgˈzændər æsks ˈdɔktər ˈdeɪnə smɔl əv məˈgɪl ˌjunəˈvərsəti,

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: where our dislike of certain foods comes from in this BBC World Service programme, The Food Chain.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: wɛr ɑr dɪsˈlaɪk əv ˈsərtən fudz kəmz frəm ɪn ðɪs ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm, ðə fud ʧeɪn.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Are food dislikes learned or genetic, hard-wired in some way?
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: are* fud dɪsˈlaɪks ˈlərnɪd ər ʤəˈnɛtɪk, hard-wired* ɪn səm way?

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Ah, both! So, there are many reasons why you can dislike a food.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ɑ, boʊθ! soʊ, ðɛr ər ˈmɛni ˈrizənz waɪ ju kən dɪsˈlaɪk ə fud.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: For example, you could, via genetics, smell coriander or taste coriander differently. Uh so, that's genetic.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ju kʊd, ˈviə ʤəˈnɛtɪks, smɛl ˌkɔriˈændər ər teɪst ˌkɔriˈændər ˈdɪfərˈɛntli. ə soʊ, ðæts ʤəˈnɛtɪk.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: But there's also a really strong learning component. Dana explains that how we taste or smell something can be different
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: bət ðɛrz ˈɔlsoʊ ə ˈrɪli strɔŋ ˈlərnɪŋ kəmˈpoʊnənt. ˈdeɪnə ɪkˈspleɪnz ðət haʊ wi teɪst ər smɛl ˈsəmθɪŋ kən bi ˈdɪfərənt

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: depending on our genetics. However, how we taste or smell something can also be learnt.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: dɪˈpɛndɪŋ ɔn ɑr ʤəˈnɛtɪks. ˌhaʊˈɛvər, haʊ wi teɪst ər smɛl ˈsəmθɪŋ kən ˈɔlsoʊ bi lərnt.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Ruth asked if a dislike for certain food is hard-wired, and Dana confirmed that this is sometimes the case.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ruθ æst ɪf ə dɪsˈlaɪk fər ˈsərtən fud ɪz hard-wired*, ənd ˈdeɪnə kənˈfərmd ðət ðɪs ɪz ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ðə keɪs.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: The adjective hard-wired describes automatically thinking
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ðə ˈæʤɪktɪv hard-wired* dɪˈskraɪbz ˌɔtəˈmætɪkli ˈθɪŋkɪŋ

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: or behaving in a particular way, for instance, because it's genetic.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ər bɪˈheɪvɪŋ ɪn ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr weɪ, fər ˈɪnstəns, bɪˈkəz ɪts ʤəˈnɛtɪk.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: And Dana says that you could taste food differently to others via genetics.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ənd ˈdeɪnə sɪz ðət ju kʊd teɪst fud ˈdɪfərˈɛntli tɪ ˈəðərz ˈviə ʤəˈnɛtɪks.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: The preposition via means by the way of or by the use of. For example, I get to work via a train.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ðə preposition* ˈviə minz baɪ ðə weɪ əv ər baɪ ðə juz əv. fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, aɪ gɪt tɪ wərk ˈviə ə treɪn.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: So there are lots of different reasons why we dislike some foods, but can we change that?
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: so* ðɛr ər lɑts əv ˈdɪfərənt ˈrizənz waɪ wi dɪsˈlaɪk səm fudz, bət kən wi ʧeɪnʤ that?

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Let's listen to dietitian Claire Thornton Wood explaining on the BBC World Service programme, The Food Chain.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: lɛts ˈlɪsən tɪ ˌdaɪəˈtɪʃən klɛr ˈθɔrntən wʊd ɪkˈspleɪnɪŋ ɔn ðə ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm, ðə fud ʧeɪn.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: One of the the really good techniques that we might use is something called masking,
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: wən əv ðə ðə ˈrɪli gʊd tɛkˈniks ðət wi maɪt juz ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ kɔld ˈmæskɪŋ,
