qI2LxF5sR2c.text: where you dip a food that you don't like into something that you do like.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: wɛr ju dɪp ə fud ðət ju doʊnt laɪk ˈɪntu ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ju du laɪk.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: For those parents who really say they like everything,
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: fər ðoʊz ˈpɛrənts hu ˈrɪli seɪ ðeɪ laɪk ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ,

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: we actually get chocolate covered insects and we offer those
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: wi ˈæˌkʧuəli gɪt ˈʧɔklət ˈkəvərd ˈɪnˌsɛkts ənd wi ˈɔfər ðoʊz

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: and actually people do eat them and try them. And I think it's the concept that it's an insect
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ənd ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈpipəl du it ðɛm ənd traɪ ðɛm. ənd aɪ θɪŋk ɪts ðə ˈkɑnsɛpt ðət ɪts ən ˈɪnˌsɛkt

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: but usually they find that once they eat it, there isn't anything inherently unpleasant
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: bət ˈjuʒəwəli ðeɪ faɪnd ðət wəns ðeɪ it ɪt, ðɛr ˈɪzənt ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ˌɪnˈhɛrəntli ənˈplɛzənt

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: about it. It's a little bit like eating just a bit of crunchy chocolate. Claire uses masking.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: əˈbaʊt ɪt. ɪts ə ˈlɪtəl bɪt laɪk ˈitɪŋ ʤɪst ə bɪt əv ˈkrənʧi ˈʧɔklət. klɛr ˈjuzɪz ˈmæskɪŋ.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Masking is the act of stopping something from being seen. In Claire's clinic,
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ˈmæskɪŋ ɪz ðə ækt əv ˈstɑpɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ frəm biɪŋ sin. ɪn klɛrz ˈklɪnɪk,

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: she masks the disliked foods with something that is liked. Yes, and what parents usually find out is
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ʃi mæsks ðə dɪsˈlaɪkt fudz wɪθ ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ɪz laɪkt. jɛs, ənd wət ˈpɛrənts ˈjuʒəwəli faɪnd aʊt ɪz

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: that the food they dislike isn't inherently bad. The adverb inherently describes something that exists
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ðət ðə fud ðeɪ dɪsˈlaɪk ˈɪzənt ˌɪnˈhɛrəntli bæd. ðə ˈædvərb ˌɪnˈhɛrəntli dɪˈskraɪbz ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ɪgˈzɪsts

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: in a way which is natural or essential. So, insects aren't inherently unpleasant to eat, some of us think they are
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ɪn ə weɪ wɪʧ ɪz ˈnæʧərəl ər ɛˈsɛnʃəl. soʊ, ˈɪnˌsɛkts ˈɑrənt ˌɪnˈhɛrəntli ənˈplɛzənt tɪ it, səm əv ˈjuˈɛs θɪŋk ðeɪ ər

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: because the concept of eating them could be strange to us culturally. We talked about how people sometimes fear trying new food.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: bɪˈkəz ðə ˈkɑnsɛpt əv ˈitɪŋ ðɛm kʊd bi streɪnʤ tɪ ˈjuˈɛs ˈkəlʧərəˌli. wi tɔkt əˈbaʊt haʊ ˈpipəl ˈsəmˌtaɪmz fɪr traɪɪŋ nu fud.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Claire talks about where these fears might come from. For instance, just say that you had eaten prawns in the past
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: klɛr tɔks əˈbaʊt wɛr ðiz fɪrz maɪt kəm frəm. fər ˈɪnstəns, ʤɪst seɪ ðət ju hæd ˈitən prɔnz ɪn ðə pæst

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: and you had become unwell from eating them, you know, you had what you call a dodgy prawn.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ənd ju hæd bɪˈkəm ənˈwɛl frəm ˈitɪŋ ðɛm, ju noʊ, ju hæd wət ju kɔl ə dodgy* prɔn.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: There's a good chance that you might actually associate that with eating the prawn and think,
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ðɛrz ə gʊd ʧæns ðət ju maɪt ˈæˌkʧuəli əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪt ðət wɪθ ˈitɪŋ ðə prɔn ənd θɪŋk,

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: oh, I don't want to eat the prawn again because it's going to make me unwell.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: oʊ, aɪ doʊnt wɔnt tɪ it ðə prɔn əˈgɛn bɪˈkəz ɪts goʊɪŋ tɪ meɪk mi ənˈwɛl.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: So, that's the sort of fear based avoidance. Claire said that you're likely to have a fear of a food
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: soʊ, ðæts ðə sɔrt əv fɪr beɪst əˈvɔɪdəns. klɛr sɛd ðət jʊr ˈlaɪkli tɪ hæv ə fɪr əv ə fud

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: if you've had a bad experience with it. She uses the example of eating a dodgy prawn, which would make you unwell.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ɪf juv hæd ə bæd ɪkˈspɪriəns wɪθ ɪt. ʃi ˈjuzɪz ðə ɪgˈzæmpəl əv ˈitɪŋ ə dodgy* prɔn, wɪʧ wʊd meɪk ju ənˈwɛl.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: If something is dodgy, it's generally bad or has a bad reputation. But when we talk about food, it could mean
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ɪf ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪz dodgy*, ɪts ˈʤɛnərəli bæd ər həz ə bæd ˌrɛpjəˈteɪʃən. bət wɪn wi tɔk əˈbaʊt fud, ɪt kʊd min

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: that it's undercooked, old or has been left out, therefore making you sick. In fact, we may avoid dodgy things.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ðət ɪts əndərˈkʊkt, oʊld ər həz bɪn lɛft aʊt, ˈðɛrˌfɔr ˈmeɪkɪŋ ju sɪk. ɪn fækt, wi meɪ əˈvɔɪd dodgy* θɪŋz.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: To avoid is to keep away from something. So, avoidance is the act of keeping away.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: tɪ əˈvɔɪd ɪz tɪ kip əˈweɪ frəm ˈsəmθɪŋ. soʊ, əˈvɔɪdəns ɪz ðə ækt əv ˈkipɪŋ əˈweɪ.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Now, Neil, That reminds me of the question you asked earlier.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: naʊ, nil, ðət riˈmaɪndz mi əv ðə kˈwɛʃən ju æst ˈərliər.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Ah, yes. I asked you what word means to have a fear of new things,
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ɑ, jɛs. aɪ æst ju wət wərd minz tɪ hæv ə fɪr əv nu θɪŋz,

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: and you answered, c) neophobia. And, Becca, I'm pleased to say your answer was correct!
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ənd ju ˈænsərd, si) neophobia*. ənd, ˈbɛkɑ, əm plizd tɪ seɪ jʊr ˈænsər wɑz kərˈɛkt!

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: So, my thinking was right! That's great. Now let's have a recap of the language we've learnt in this episode,
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: soʊ, maɪ ˈθɪŋkɪŋ wɑz raɪt! ðæts greɪt. naʊ lɛts hæv ə ˈriˌkæp əv ðə ˈlæŋgwɪʤ wiv lərnt ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd,

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: starting with 'hard-wired', which describes automatically thinking
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ 'hard-wired*', wɪʧ dɪˈskraɪbz ˌɔtəˈmætɪkli ˈθɪŋkɪŋ

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: or behaving in a particular way, because of genetics, for example.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ər bɪˈheɪvɪŋ ɪn ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr weɪ, bɪˈkəz əv ʤəˈnɛtɪks, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: 'Via' is a preposition that means by way of or by use of.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: 'ˈviə' ɪz ə preposition* ðət minz baɪ weɪ əv ər baɪ juz əv.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: We had 'masking', that's hiding or stopping something from being seen. The adverb 'inherently' describes something that exists
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: wi hæd 'ˈmæskɪŋ', ðæts ˈhaɪdɪŋ ər ˈstɑpɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ frəm biɪŋ sin. ðə ˈædvərb 'ˌɪnˈhɛrəntli' dɪˈskraɪbz ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ɪgˈzɪsts

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: in a way which is natural or essential. We also had 'avoidance', that is the act of keeping away from something.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ɪn ə weɪ wɪʧ ɪz ˈnæʧərəl ər ɛˈsɛnʃəl. wi ˈɔlsoʊ hæd 'əˈvɔɪdəns', ðət ɪz ðə ækt əv ˈkipɪŋ əˈweɪ frəm ˈsəmθɪŋ.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: And finally, when we talk about food, 'dodgy' means something that can make you unwell.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: ənd ˈfaɪnəli, wɪn wi tɔk əˈbaʊt fud, 'dodgy*' minz ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət kən meɪk ju ənˈwɛl.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but head over to our website, bbclearningenglish.com for a quiz and worksheet for this episode.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: wəns əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp, bət hɛd ˈoʊvər tɪ ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com* fər ə kwɪz ənd ˈwərkˌʃit fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd.

qI2LxF5sR2c.text: See you there soon. But for now, it's goodbye. Goodbye.
qI2LxF5sR2c.ipa: si ju ðɛr sun. bət fər naʊ, ɪts ˌgʊdˈbaɪ. ˌgʊdˈbaɪ.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Becca.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: hɛˈloʊ. ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm ˈbɛkɑ.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: In this episode, we're discussing a food seasoning that's so popular
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, wɪr dɪˈskəsɪŋ ə fud ˈsizənɪŋ ðæts soʊ ˈpɑpjələr

8uFBMD23d5k.text: we eat around three quarters of a million tonnes of it a year. We're talking about pepper.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: wi it əraʊnd θri kˈwɔrtərz əv ə ˈmɪljən tənz əv ɪt ə jɪr. wɪr ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt ˈpɛpər.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Do you add pepper to your food, Becca? I do, Neil. Yes. I think it's an easy way to add some spice.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: do* ju æd ˈpɛpər tɪ jʊr fud, ˈbɛkɑ? aɪ du, nil. jɛs. aɪ θɪŋk ɪts ən ˈizi weɪ tɪ æd səm spaɪs.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Yeah, I love a bit of pepper. I grind pepper onto everything. Well... not everything! But I do like it.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: jæ, aɪ ləv ə bɪt əv ˈpɛpər. aɪ graɪnd ˈpɛpər ˈɔntu ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ. wɛl... nɑt ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ! bət aɪ du laɪk ɪt.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Given its popularity, it's surprising that most people know very little about pepper. Did you know, for example,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ˈgɪvɪn ɪts ˌpɑpjəˈlɛrəti, ɪts səˈpraɪzɪŋ ðət moʊst ˈpipəl noʊ ˈvɛri ˈlɪtəl əˈbaʊt ˈpɛpər. dɪd ju noʊ, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: that peppers are the fruit of vines often growing over ten metres high?
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ðət ˈpɛpərz ər ðə frut əv vaɪnz ˈɔfən groʊɪŋ ˈoʊvər tɛn ˈmitərz high?

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Our ancestors would be surprised how little we know. From ancient Greece onwards, pepper was prized as the black gold of ingredients,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ɑr ˈænˌsɛstərz wʊd bi səˈpraɪzd haʊ ˈlɪtəl wi noʊ. frəm ˈeɪnʧənt gris ˈɑnwərdz, ˈpɛpər wɑz praɪzd ɛz ðə blæk goʊld əv ˌɪnˈgridiənts,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: and explorers crossed oceans in search of it, discovering new continents along the way.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ənd ɪkˈsplɔrərz krɔst ˈoʊʃənz ɪn sərʧ əv ɪt, dɪˈskəvərɪŋ nu ˈkɑntənənts əˈlɔŋ ðə weɪ.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: In this episode, we'll get reacquainted with pepper and learn some useful new words and phrases too.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, wɪl gɪt reacquainted* wɪθ ˈpɛpər ənd lərn səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd ˈfreɪzɪz tu.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: And remember, you'll find a transcript for you to read along
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ənd rɪˈmɛmbər, jul faɪnd ə ˈtrænˌskrɪpt fər ju tɪ rɛd əˈlɔŋ

8uFBMD23d5k.text: with us as you listen, on our website, bbclearningenglish.com. OK. First, I have a question for you, of course, Becca.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: wɪθ ˈjuˈɛs ɛz ju ˈlɪsən, ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*. ˈoʊˈkeɪ. fərst, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, əv kɔrs, ˈbɛkɑ.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Although it's black pepper you're most likely to see in shops and restaurants in the UK,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ˌɔlˈðoʊ ɪts blæk ˈpɛpər jʊr moʊst ˈlaɪkli tɪ si ɪn ʃɑps ənd ˈrɛˌstrɑnts ɪn ðə uk*,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: there are hundreds of different varieties worldwide.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ðɛr ər ˈhənərdz əv ˈdɪfərənt vərˈaɪətiz ˈwərldˈwaɪd.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: But what is unusual about Phu Quoc, a white pepper from Vietnam?
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: but* wət ɪz ənˈjuˌʒuəl əˈbaʊt fu quoc*, ə waɪt ˈpɛpər frəm vietnam?

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Does it: a) make people cry, b) taste like parmesan cheese, or c) cost more than gold?
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: does* ɪt: ə) meɪk ˈpipəl kraɪ, bi) teɪst laɪk ˈpɑrməˌzɑn ʧiz, ər si) kɔst mɔr ðən gold?

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Hmm. Well, I don't think it would be as expensive as gold,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: həm. wɛl, aɪ doʊnt θɪŋk ɪt wʊd bi ɛz ɪkˈspɛnsɪv ɛz goʊld,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: and I kind of want it to taste like parmesan cheese. OK. Well, we'll find out later in the programme.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ənd aɪ kaɪnd əv wɔnt ɪt tɪ teɪst laɪk ˈpɑrməˌzɑn ʧiz. ˈoʊˈkeɪ. wɛl, wɪl faɪnd aʊt ˈleɪtər ɪn ðə ˈproʊˌgræm.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Mathilde Roellinger is the daughter of Olivier Roellinger, an award-winning French chef famous for his use of spices.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ˈmæθɪld roellinger* ɪz ðə ˈdɔtər əv ˌoʊˈlɪˌviˌeɪ roellinger*, ən award-winning* frɛnʧ ʃɛf ˈfeɪməs fər hɪz juz əv ˈspaɪsɪz.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: While other little girls of her age were sprinkling sugar on their breakfast yoghurt,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: waɪl ˈəðər ˈlɪtəl gərlz əv hər eɪʤ wər ˈsprɪŋkəlɪŋ ˈʃʊgər ɔn ðɛr ˈbrɛkfəst yoghurt*,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Mathilde was the only girl in Paris sprinkling pepper. Today, Mathilde runs the Épices Roellinger spice shop
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ˈmæθɪld wɑz ðə ˈoʊnli gərl ɪn ˈpɛrɪs ˈsprɪŋkəlɪŋ ˈpɛpər. təˈdeɪ, ˈmæθɪld rənz ðə éépices* roellinger* spaɪs ʃɑp

8uFBMD23d5k.text: in the Opéra area of the city. Here, customers can find a huge range of peppers,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ɪn ðə opéra* ˈɛriə əv ðə ˈsɪti. hir, ˈkəstəmərz kən faɪnd ə juʤ reɪnʤ əv ˈpɛpərz,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: from fruity red Cambodian Kampot pepper to Borneo's Sarawak black pepper with its woody aroma.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: frəm ˈfruti rɛd ˌkæmˈboʊdiən kampot* ˈpɛpər tɪ borneo's* ˈsɛrəwɑk blæk ˈpɛpər wɪθ ɪts ˈwʊdi ərˈoʊmə.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: These peppers have strong, distinctive tastes, but curiously they enhance rather than overpower the flavour
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ðiz ˈpɛpərz hæv strɔŋ, dɪˈstɪŋktɪv teɪsts, bət ˈkjʊriəsli ðeɪ ɛnˈhæns ˈrəðər ðən ˌoʊvərˈpaʊər ðə flavour*

8uFBMD23d5k.text: of the food you're eating, as Mathilde explained to BBC World Service programme The Food Chain.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: əv ðə fud jʊr ˈitɪŋ, ɛz ˈmæθɪld ɪkˈspleɪnd tɪ ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud ʧeɪn.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: It will give a kick and transform it, but you will still have the savour of the different ingredients.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ɪt wɪl gɪv ə kɪk ənd ˈtrænsfɔrm ɪt, bət ju wɪl stɪl hæv ðə savour* əv ðə ˈdɪfərənt ˌɪnˈgridiənts.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: It will not disguise the other ingredients, but it will push them – it's a flavour catalyst. We can say in punctuation,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ɪt wɪl nɑt dɪsˈgaɪz ðə ˈəðər ˌɪnˈgridiənts, bət ɪt wɪl pʊʃ ðɛm –* ɪts ə flavour* ˈkætəˌlɪst. wi kən seɪ ɪn ˌpəŋkʧuˈeɪʃən,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: like it's an exclamation. It's like an exclamation mark. Exactly. Mathilde says pepper gives food a kick.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: laɪk ɪts ən ˌɛkskləˈmeɪʃən. ɪts laɪk ən ˌɛkskləˈmeɪʃən mɑrk. ɪgˈzæktli. ˈmæθɪld sɪz ˈpɛpər gɪvz fud ə kɪk.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: To give something a kick means to provide it with extra stimulation or excitement.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: tɪ gɪv ˈsəmθɪŋ ə kɪk minz tɪ prəˈvaɪd ɪt wɪθ ˈɛkstrə ˌstɪmjəˈleɪʃən ər ɪkˈsaɪtmənt.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Pepper also makes flavours more intense. Mathilde calls it a catalyst – something that causes another action to start
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ˈpɛpər ˈɔlsoʊ meɪks flavours* mɔr ˌɪnˈtɛns. ˈmæθɪld kɔlz ɪt ə ˈkætəˌlɪst –* ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ˈkɔzɪz əˈnəðər ˈækʃən tɪ stɑrt

8uFBMD23d5k.text: or makes it happen more quickly. In fact, she says pepper is like an exclamation mark. Saying something is like
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ər meɪks ɪt ˈhæpən mɔr kˈwɪkli. ɪn fækt, ʃi sɪz ˈpɛpər ɪz laɪk ən ˌɛkskləˈmeɪʃən mɑrk. seɪɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪz laɪk

8uFBMD23d5k.text: an exclamation mark means it shows strong emotion or excitement –
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ən ˌɛkskləˈmeɪʃən mɑrk minz ɪt ʃoʊz strɔŋ ˈiˌmoʊʃən ər ɪkˈsaɪtmənt –*

8uFBMD23d5k.text: the same thing an exclamation mark does in written punctuation.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ðə seɪm θɪŋ ən ˌɛkskləˈmeɪʃən mɑrk dɪz ɪn ˈrɪtən ˌpəŋkʧuˈeɪʃən.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Mathilde's spice shop holds pepper tasting sessions, where she explains to customers the origin of her peppers and how they grow,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: mathilde's* spaɪs ʃɑp hoʊldz ˈpɛpər ˈteɪstɪŋ ˈsɛʃənz, wɛr ʃi ɪkˈspleɪnz tɪ ˈkəstəmərz ðə ˈɔrəʤən əv hər ˈpɛpərz ənd haʊ ðeɪ groʊ,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: changing colour as they harden in the sun. Reporter John Laurenson attended one of these tasting sessions
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ˈʧeɪnʤɪŋ ˈkələr ɛz ðeɪ ˈhɑrdən ɪn ðə sən. rɪˈpɔrtər ʤɑn laurenson* əˈtɛndəd wən əv ðiz ˈteɪstɪŋ ˈsɛʃənz

8uFBMD23d5k.text: for BBC World Service programme The Food Chain. The different colours of pepper though,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: fər ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud ʧeɪn. ðə ˈdɪfərənt colours* əv ˈpɛpər ðoʊ,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: as Mathilde started to say, do not correspond to the different varieties
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ɛz ˈmæθɪld ˈstɑrtɪd tɪ seɪ, du nɑt ˌkɔrəˈspɑnd tɪ ðə ˈdɪfərənt vərˈaɪətiz

8uFBMD23d5k.text: but to the maturity of the peppercorns and what people do to them.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: bət tɪ ðə məˈʧʊrəti əv ðə peppercorns* ənd wət ˈpipəl du tɪ ðɛm.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: They're green when they're young, black when they're mature and dried, red when they're very mature.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ðɛr grin wɪn ðɛr jəŋ, blæk wɪn ðɛr məˈʧʊr ənd draɪd, rɛd wɪn ðɛr ˈvɛri məˈʧʊr.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Grey pepper is an industrial creation, not a botanical one.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: greɪ ˈpɛpər ɪz ən ˌɪnˈdəstriəl kriˈeɪʃən, nɑt ə bəˈtænɪkəl wən.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Ground to a fine powder, it is, says Mathilde, grey dust. She's not very keen on that one.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: graʊnd tɪ ə faɪn ˈpaʊdər, ɪt ɪz, sɪz ˈmæθɪld, greɪ dəst. ʃiz nɑt ˈvɛri kin ɔn ðət wən.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: The colour of pepper is not determined by the variety, but by its maturity.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ðə ˈkələr əv ˈpɛpər ɪz nɑt dɪˈtərmənd baɪ ðə vərˈaɪəti, bət baɪ ɪts məˈʧʊrəti.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: A food's maturity describes the stage when a food is fully grown and ready to harvest.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ə fudz məˈʧʊrəti dɪˈskraɪbz ðə steɪʤ wɪn ə fud ɪz ˈfʊli groʊn ənd ˈrɛdi tɪ ˈhɑrvəst.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: For peppers, this is when they wrinkle and go black.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: fər ˈpɛpərz, ðɪs ɪz wɪn ðeɪ ˈrɪŋkəl ənd goʊ blæk.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Often a peppermill is used to grind pepper – to crush it into powder by pressing it between two hard surfaces.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ˈɔfən ə peppermill* ɪz juzd tɪ graɪnd ˈpɛpər –* tɪ krəʃ ɪt ˈɪntu ˈpaʊdər baɪ ˈprɛsɪŋ ɪt bɪtˈwin tu hɑrd ˈsərfəsɪz.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: This happens with grey pepper, an artificially produced pepper mix which Mathilde is not keen on – meaning she doesn't like it.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ðɪs ˈhæpənz wɪθ greɪ ˈpɛpər, ən ˌɑrtəˈfɪʃəli prəˈdust ˈpɛpər mɪks wɪʧ ˈmæθɪld ɪz nɑt kin ɔn –* ˈminɪŋ ʃi ˈdəzənt laɪk ɪt.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: We've learned so much about pepper, I'll look at it differently the next time I sprinkle some on my food.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: wiv ˈlərnɪd soʊ məʧ əˈbaʊt ˈpɛpər, aɪl lʊk æt ɪt ˈdɪfərˈɛntli ðə nɛkst taɪm aɪ ˈsprɪŋkəl səm ɔn maɪ fud.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: OK. Neil, I think it's time to reveal the answer to your question.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ. nil, aɪ θɪŋk ɪts taɪm tɪ rɪˈvil ðə ˈænsər tɪ jʊr kˈwɛʃən.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Yes, I asked you what's unusual about Phu Quoc, a white pepper from Vietnam.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: jɛs, aɪ æst ju wəts ənˈjuˌʒuəl əˈbaʊt fu quoc*, ə waɪt ˈpɛpər frəm viˌɛtˈnɑm.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: I answered b) because I want it to taste like parmesan cheese.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: aɪ ˈænsərd bi) bɪˈkəz aɪ wɔnt ɪt tɪ teɪst laɪk ˈpɑrməˌzɑn ʧiz.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Well, you're lucky because it is in fact b) it tastes like parmesan cheese. Well done. OK.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: wɛl, jʊr ˈləki bɪˈkəz ɪt ɪz ɪn fækt bi) ɪt teɪsts laɪk ˈpɑrməˌzɑn ʧiz. wɛl dən. ˈoʊˈkeɪ.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with the phrase
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv ˈlərnɪd, ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ ðə freɪz

8uFBMD23d5k.text: give something a kick – meaning to add extra thrill or excitement.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: gɪv ˈsəmθɪŋ ə kɪk –* ˈminɪŋ tɪ æd ˈɛkstrə θrɪl ər ɪkˈsaɪtmənt.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: A catalyst causes something to start, or speeds it up. If you say something is like an exclamation mark,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ə ˈkætəˌlɪst ˈkɔzɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ tɪ stɑrt, ər spidz ɪt əp. ɪf ju seɪ ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪz laɪk ən ˌɛkskləˈmeɪʃən mɑrk,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: you mean it signifies strong emotion, surprise or excitement, just like an exclamation mark does in writing.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ju min ɪt ˈsɪgnəˌfaɪz strɔŋ ˈiˌmoʊʃən, səˈpraɪz ər ɪkˈsaɪtmənt, ʤɪst laɪk ən ˌɛkskləˈmeɪʃən mɑrk dɪz ɪn ˈraɪtɪŋ.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: A food's maturity refers to the stage when it's fully grown and ready for harvest.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ə fudz məˈʧʊrəti rɪˈfərz tɪ ðə steɪʤ wɪn ɪts ˈfʊli groʊn ənd ˈrɛdi fər ˈhɑrvəst.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: To grind food means to crush it into powder by being pressed between two hard surfaces.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: tɪ graɪnd fud minz tɪ krəʃ ɪt ˈɪntu ˈpaʊdər baɪ biɪŋ prɛst bɪtˈwin tu hɑrd ˈsərfəsɪz.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: And finally, if you're keen on something, you like it and enjoy doing it. Once again, our six minutes are up,
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ənd ˈfaɪnəli, ɪf jʊr kin ɔn ˈsəmθɪŋ, ju laɪk ɪt ənd ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ duɪŋ ɪt. wəns əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp,

8uFBMD23d5k.text: but remember you can find worksheets, quizzes and loads more resources
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: bət rɪˈmɛmbər ju kən faɪnd ˈwərkˌʃits, kˈwɪzɪz ənd loʊdz mɔr ˈrisɔrsɪz

8uFBMD23d5k.text: to improve your English on our website, bbclearningenglish.com. See you there soon, but for now it's goodbye.
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: tɪ ˌɪmˈpruv jʊr ˈɪŋlɪʃ ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*. si ju ðɛr sun, bət fər naʊ ɪts ˌgʊdˈbaɪ.

8uFBMD23d5k.text: Goodbye!
8uFBMD23d5k.ipa: ˌgʊdˈbaɪ!

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Becca. Do you enjoy cooking Neil?
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: hello*, ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm ˈbɛkɑ. du ju ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈkʊkɪŋ nil?

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Yes, I do enjoy cooking. I think it's quite relaxing, especially after a busy day. How about you, Becca?
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: yes*, aɪ du ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈkʊkɪŋ. aɪ θɪŋk ɪts kwaɪt rɪˈlæksɪŋ, əˈspɛʃəli ˈæftər ə ˈbɪzi deɪ. haʊ əˈbaʊt ju, becca?

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: I enjoy cooking when I'm feeding other people, but usually after a busy day at work, I don't enjoy cooking for myself.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: aɪ ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈkʊkɪŋ wɪn əm ˈfidɪŋ ˈəðər ˈpipəl, bət ˈjuʒəwəli ˈæftər ə ˈbɪzi deɪ æt wərk, aɪ doʊnt ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈkʊkɪŋ fər ˌmaɪˈsɛlf.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Well, we're talking about cooking today, and most of us can at least boil an egg, but not everyone could make it
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: wɛl, wɪr ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt ˈkʊkɪŋ təˈdeɪ, ənd moʊst əv ˈjuˈɛs kən æt list bɔɪl ən ɛg, bət nɑt ˈɛvriˌwən kʊd meɪk ɪt

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: as a professional chef in a world-famous restaurant.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ɛz ə prəˈfɛʃənəl ʃɛf ɪn ə world-famous* ˈrɛˌstrɑnt.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Mmm. Being a top chef requires passion, dedication, and hard work.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: mmm*. biɪŋ ə tɔp ʃɛf rikˈwaɪərz ˈpæʃən, ˌdɛdəˈkeɪʃən, ənd hɑrd wərk.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Many chefs work 15 hours a day, six days a week,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ˈmɛni ʃɛfs wərk 15 aʊərz ə deɪ, sɪks deɪz ə wik,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: and busy kitchens cooking expensive food are very stressful places to work. So, why do they do it?
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: and* ˈbɪzi ˈkɪʧənz ˈkʊkɪŋ ɪkˈspɛnsɪv fud ər ˈvɛri ˈstrɛsfəl ˈpleɪsɪz tɪ wərk. soʊ, waɪ du ðeɪ du it?

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: In this episode, we'll hear from one South African chef who started her own award-winning restaurant in the country known
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, wɪl hir frəm wən saʊθ ˈæfrɪkɑn ʃɛf hu ˈstɑrtɪd hər oʊn award-winning* ˈrɛˌstrɑnt ɪn ðə ˈkəntri noʊn

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: as the home of fine dining – France. As usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ɛz ðə hoʊm əv faɪn ˈdaɪnɪŋ –* fræns. ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl bi ˈlərnɪŋ səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd ˈfreɪzɪz.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: And remember, you'll find all this episode's vocabulary, along with a transcript, on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ənd rɪˈmɛmbər, jul faɪnd ɔl ðɪs episode's* voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri, əˈlɔŋ wɪθ ə ˈtrænˌskrɪpt, ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: But first, I have a question for you, Neil. The top prizes for any professional chef are Michelin stars,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: bət fərst, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, nil. ðə tɔp ˈpraɪzɪz fər ˈɛni prəˈfɛʃənəl ʃɛf ər ˈmɪʃələn stɑrz,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: awards given to restaurants offering outstanding cooking. But who is the winner of the most Michelin stars ever?
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: əˈwɔrdz ˈgɪvɪn tɪ ˈrɛˌstrɑnts ˈɔfərɪŋ ˌaʊtˈstændɪŋ ˈkʊkɪŋ. bət hu ɪz ðə ˈwɪnər əv ðə moʊst ˈmɪʃələn stɑrz ever?

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Is it: a) Italian chef Enrico Bartolini, b) French chef Joël Robuchon,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ɪz ɪt: ə) ˌɪˈtæljən ʃɛf ɛnˈrikoʊ bɑrtoʊˈlini, bi) frɛnʧ ʃɛf joël* robuchon*,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: or c) British chef Gordon Ramsay? I'm going to say a) the Italian chef, Enrico Bartolini,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: or* si) ˈbrɪtɪʃ ʃɛf ˈgɔrdən ˈræmˌseɪ? əm goʊɪŋ tɪ seɪ ə) ðə ˌɪˈtæljən ʃɛf, ɛnˈrikoʊ bartolini,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: because I went to Italy recently and the food was amazing! Mmm! OK. We'll find out the answer later in this episode.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: bɪˈkəz aɪ wɛnt tɪ ˈɪtəli ˈrisəntli ənd ðə fud wɑz əˈmeɪzɪŋ! mmm*! ˈoʊˈkeɪ. wɪl faɪnd aʊt ðə ˈænsər ˈleɪtər ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Born in South Africa, chef Chantel Dartnall started her culinary career
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: bɔrn ɪn saʊθ ˈæfrɪkɑ, ʃɛf chantel* dartnall* ˈstɑrtɪd hər ˈkjulɪˌnɛri kərɪr

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: at a three-Michelin-star restaurant in the exclusive Mayfair area of London. Here, she tells Ruth Alexander,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: æt ə three-michelin-star* ˈrɛˌstrɑnt ɪn ðə ɪkˈsklusɪv ˈmeɪˌfɛr ˈɛriə əv ˈləndən. hir, ʃi tɛlz ruθ ˌælɪgˈzændər,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: presenter of BBC World Service programme The Food Chain, how it all got started:
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ˈprɛzəntər əv ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud ʧeɪn, haʊ ɪt ɔl gɑt ˈstɑrtɪd:

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: What happened is I had graduated from the Prue Leith College of Food and Wine
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: wət ˈhæpənd ɪz aɪ hæd ˈgræʤəˌweɪtɪd frəm ðə pru liθ ˈkɑlɪʤ əv fud ənd waɪn

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: and decided to send my CV to all the three-Michelin-star restaurants that I could find at that moment in the Michelin Guide.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ənd ˌdɪˈsaɪdɪd tɪ sɛnd maɪ cv* tɪ ɔl ðə three-michelin-star* ˈrɛˌstrɑnts ðət aɪ kʊd faɪnd æt ðət ˈmoʊmənt ɪn ðə ˈmɪʃələn gaɪd.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: So, you aimed high from the off. I aimed high.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: soʊ, ju eɪmd haɪ frəm ðə ɔf. aɪ eɪmd haɪ.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: The only thing that they could say was no, and what would I lose? I received a call from chef Paul Rhodes
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: the* ˈoʊnli θɪŋ ðət ðeɪ kʊd seɪ wɑz noʊ, ənd wət wʊd aɪ luz? aɪ rɪˈsivd ə kɔl frəm ʃɛf pɔl rhodes

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: to say, When can you start? Wow. And this, sort of, just catapulted my career.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: to* seɪ, wɪn kən ju stɑrt? waʊ. ənd ðɪs, sɔrt əv, ʤɪst ˈkætəˌpəltɪd maɪ career.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Chantel sent her CV to the world's top restaurants. CV is short for curriculum vitae,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: chantel* sɛnt hər cv* tɪ ðə wərldz tɔp ˈrɛˌstrɑnts. cv* ɪz ʃɔrt fər kərˈɪkjələm ˈvaɪtə,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: a document listing someone's education, qualifications and work experience, which they write when looking for a job.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ə ˈdɑkjəmɛnt ˈlɪstɪŋ ˈsəmˌwənz ˌɛʤəˈkeɪʃən, kˌwɑləfəˈkeɪʃənz ənd wərk ɪkˈspɪriəns, wɪʧ ðeɪ raɪt wɪn ˈlʊkɪŋ fər ə ʤɑb.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: In American English, it's called a résumé. Chantel applied to only Michelin-star-winning restaurants.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ɪn əˈmɛrɪkən ˈɪŋlɪʃ, ɪts kɔld ə résumé*é. chantel* əˈplaɪd tɪ ˈoʊnli michelin-star-winning* ˈrɛˌstrɑnts.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: She aimed high, meaning she was ambitious. Chantel says, The worst they could do is say no, a phrase which means
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ʃi eɪmd haɪ, ˈminɪŋ ʃi wɑz æmˈbɪʃəs. chantel* sɪz, ðə wərst ðeɪ kʊd du ɪz seɪ noʊ, ə freɪz wɪʧ minz

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: you shouldn't be afraid of trying to do something because the worst outcome is being rejected.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ju ˈʃʊdənt bi əˈfreɪd əv traɪɪŋ tɪ du ˈsəmθɪŋ bɪˈkəz ðə wərst ˈaʊtˌkəm ɪz biɪŋ rɪˈʤɛktɪd.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Luckily for Chantel, the answer was yes, and learning her trade in a top London restaurant catapulted,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ˈləkəli fər chantel*, ðə ˈænsər wɑz jɛs, ənd ˈlərnɪŋ hər treɪd ɪn ə tɔp ˈləndən ˈrɛˌstrɑnt ˈkætəˌpəltɪd,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: meaning accelerated, her career. At the age of 26,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ˈminɪŋ ækˈsɛlərˌeɪtɪd, hər kərɪr. æt ðə eɪʤ əv 26

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Chantel left London and returned home to open her own restaurant, Mosaic, near Pretoria in South Africa.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: chantel* lɛft ˈləndən ənd rɪˈtərnd hoʊm tɪ ˈoʊpən hər oʊn ˈrɛˌstrɑnt, moʊˈzeɪɪk, nɪr priˈtɔriə ɪn saʊθ ˈæfrɪkɑ.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: She'd been inspired by botanical cuisine, the use of edible flowers and herbs to decorate and flavour food.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ʃid bɪn ˌɪnˈspaɪərd baɪ bəˈtænɪkəl kwɪˈzin, ðə juz əv ˈɛdəbəl flaʊərz ənd ərbz tɪ ˈdɛkərˌeɪt ənd flavour* fud.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Here, Chantel tells more to BBC World Service programme The Food Chain. When I opened Mosaic in South Africa,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: hir, chantel* tɛlz mɔr tɪ ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud ʧeɪn. wɪn aɪ ˈoʊpənd moʊˈzeɪɪk ɪn saʊθ ˈæfrɪkɑ,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: I knew I wanted something entirely different and something which diners had not seen.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: aɪ nu aɪ ˈwɔntɪd ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪnˈtaɪərli ˈdɪfərənt ənd ˈsəmθɪŋ wɪʧ ˈdaɪnərz hæd nɑt sin.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: I wanted to have my own signature and to stand out, and I think especially for botanical cuisine in South Africa,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: aɪ ˈwɔntɪd tɪ hæv maɪ oʊn ˈsɪgnəʧər ənd tɪ stænd aʊt, ənd aɪ θɪŋk əˈspɛʃəli fər bəˈtænɪkəl kwɪˈzin ɪn saʊθ ˈæfrɪkɑ,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: it was very much unknown. Chantel developed her own signature.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ɪt wɑz ˈvɛri məʧ ənˈnoʊn. chantel* dɪˈvɛləpt hər oʊn ˈsɪgnəʧər.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: A signature item is something that is typical of, or strongly associated with, a particular person or place.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ə ˈsɪgnəʧər ˈaɪtəm ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ɪz ˈtɪpɪkəl əv, ər ˈstrɔŋli əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪtəd wɪθ, ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr ˈpərsən ər pleɪs.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: For example, whisky is Scotland's signature drink. Chantel's signature is cooking botanical food.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ˈwɪski ɪz ˈskɑtləndz ˈsɪgnəʧər drɪŋk. chantel's* ˈsɪgnəʧər ɪz ˈkʊkɪŋ bəˈtænɪkəl fud.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Chantel's Mosaic restaurant is currently at the Château des Tesnières in northern France,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: chantel's* moʊˈzeɪɪk ˈrɛˌstrɑnt ɪz ˈkərəntli æt ðə château* dɪ tesnières* ɪn ˈnɔrðərn fræns,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: where it's listed among the top 200 restaurants in the world.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: wɛr ɪts ˈlɪstɪd əˈməŋ ðə tɔp 200 ˈrɛˌstrɑnts ɪn ðə wərld.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: And in 2017 she won the title Best Female Chef in the World.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ənd ɪn 2017 ʃi wən ðə ˈtaɪtəl bɛst ˈfiˌmeɪl ʃɛf ɪn ðə wərld.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: These achievements have helped Chantel stand out – to be more noticeable and better than other chefs.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ðiz əˈʧivmənts hæv hɛlpt chantel* stænd aʊt –* tɪ bi mɔr ˈnoʊtəsəbəl ənd ˈbɛtər ðən ˈəðər ʃɛfs.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Chantel is still waiting, but surely it's only a matter of time until she wins those coveted Michelin stars.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: chantel* ɪz stɪl ˈweɪtɪŋ, bət ˈʃʊrli ɪts ˈoʊnli ə ˈmætər əv taɪm ənˈtɪl ʃi wɪnz ðoʊz ˈkəvətɪd ˈmɪʃələn stɑrz.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Speaking of which, what was the answer to your question, Becca? Ah yes, I asked you earlier,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: speaking* əv wɪʧ, wət wɑz ðə ˈænsər tɪ jʊr kˈwɛʃən, ˈbɛkɑ? ɑ jɛs, aɪ æst ju earlier,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Who is the winner of the most Michelin stars ever?
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: who* ɪz ðə ˈwɪnər əv ðə moʊst ˈmɪʃələn stɑrz ever?

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: I said that it was the Italian chef Enrico Bartolini... ...but unfortunately, it was incorrect.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: aɪ sɛd ðət ɪt wɑz ðə ˌɪˈtæljən ʃɛf ɛnˈrikoʊ bɑrtoʊˈlini... ...bət ənˈfɔrʧənətli, ɪt wɑz ˌɪnkərˈɛkt.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Oh no! The right answer is b) Joël Robuchon,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: oʊ noʊ! ðə raɪt ˈænsər ɪz bi) joël* robuchon*,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: our French chef with 31 stars – the most decorated chef in history.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ɑr frɛnʧ ʃɛf wɪθ 31 stɑrz –* ðə moʊst ˈdɛkərˌeɪtɪd ʃɛf ɪn ˈhɪstəri.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: OK. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned,
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv ˈlərnɪd,

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: starting with curriculum vitae, CV, or résumé in American English –
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ kərˈɪkjələm ˈvaɪtə, cv*, ər résumé*é ɪn əˈmɛrɪkən ˈɪŋlɪʃ –*

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: a document outlining someone's education, qualifications and work experience, which they prepare when looking for a job.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ə ˈdɑkjəmɛnt ˈaʊˌtlaɪnɪŋ ˈsəmˌwənz ˌɛʤəˈkeɪʃən, kˌwɑləfəˈkeɪʃənz ənd wərk ɪkˈspɪriəns, wɪʧ ðeɪ priˈpɛr wɪn ˈlʊkɪŋ fər ə ʤɑb.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: To aim high means to be ambitious and set high goals. The phrase, the worst they can do is say no, means:
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: tɪ eɪm haɪ minz tɪ bi æmˈbɪʃəs ənd sɛt haɪ goʊlz. ðə freɪz, ðə wərst ðeɪ kən du ɪz seɪ noʊ, minz:

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: don't be afraid of trying to do something for the fear of rejection. If something is catapulted, it's suddenly accelerated
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: doʊnt bi əˈfreɪd əv traɪɪŋ tɪ du ˈsəmθɪŋ fər ðə fɪr əv rɪˈʤɛkʃən. ɪf ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪz ˈkætəˌpəltɪd, ɪts ˈsədənli ækˈsɛlərˌeɪtɪd

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: or launched forward. A signature item is something that a person or place is known for.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ər lɔnʧt ˈfɔrwərd. ə ˈsɪgnəʧər ˈaɪtəm ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ə ˈpərsən ər pleɪs ɪz noʊn fər.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: And finally, to stand out means to be more noticeable or better than other similar things.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ənd ˈfaɪnəli, tɪ stænd aʊt minz tɪ bi mɔr ˈnoʊtəsəbəl ər ˈbɛtər ðən ˈəðər ˈsɪmələr θɪŋz.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Once again, our six minutes are up. If you enjoyed this episode, you'll find a quiz and worksheet to practise
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: wəns əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp. ɪf ju ˌɛnˈʤɔɪd ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, jul faɪnd ə kwɪz ənd ˈwərkˌʃit tɪ ˈpræktɪs

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: the vocabulary we've learnt on our website, bbclearningenglish.com. See you again soon. But for now it's goodbye.
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv lərnt ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*. si ju əˈgɛn sun. bət fər naʊ ɪts ˌgʊdˈbaɪ.

nx2BboYL2Y0.text: Goodbye!
nx2BboYL2Y0.ipa: ˌgʊdˈbaɪ!

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Phil. And I'm Becca.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: hɛˈloʊ, ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm fɪl. ənd əm ˈbɛkɑ.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: 6-Minute-English listeners are truly global, coming from all over the world, including some of the coldest countries on Earth –
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: 6-minute-english* ˈlɪsənərz ər ˈtruli ˈgloʊbəl, ˈkəmɪŋ frəm ɔl ˈoʊvər ðə wərld, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ səm əv ðə ˈkoʊldəst ˈkəntriz ɔn ərθ –*

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: places like Finland, where winter temperatures drop to -20°C. Are you good at dealing with the cold, Phil?
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: places* laɪk ˈfɪnˌlænd, wɛr ˈwɪntər ˈtɛmpərəʧərz drɔp tɪ 20°c*. ər ju gʊd æt ˈdilɪŋ wɪθ ðə koʊld, phil?

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: I'm not sure, but I do know that I don't like it. What about you, Becca?
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: əm nɑt ʃʊr, bət aɪ du noʊ ðət aɪ doʊnt laɪk ɪt. wət əˈbaʊt ju, becca?

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Well, I'd rather be too hot and cool down than too cold and try to warm up.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: wɛl, aɪd ˈrəðər bi tu hɑt ənd kul daʊn ðən tu koʊld ənd traɪ tɪ wɔrm əp.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Yes, me too. Here in the UK, it never gets as cold as Finland,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: jɛs, mi tu. hir ɪn ðə uk*, ɪt ˈnɛvər gɪts ɛz koʊld ɛz ˈfɪnˌlænd,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: but it's not unusual to see some people dressed in T-shirts while others are wrapped up in warm clothes.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: bət ɪts nɑt ənˈjuˌʒuəl tɪ si səm ˈpipəl drɛst ɪn ˈtiˌsərts waɪl ˈəðərz ər ræpt əp ɪn wɔrm kloʊðz.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Why do people feel the cold so differently? That's what we'll be discussing in this episode,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: why* du ˈpipəl fil ðə koʊld soʊ ˈdɪfərˈɛntli? ðæts wət wɪl bi dɪˈskəsɪŋ ɪn ðɪs episode,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: as well as learning some useful new words and phrases. And as always you'll find all the vocabulary from this episode,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ɛz wɛl ɛz ˈlərnɪŋ səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd ˈfreɪzɪz. ənd ɛz ˈɔlˌweɪz jul faɪnd ɔl ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri frəm ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: plus a quiz and worksheet, on our website, bbclearningenglish.com. But now I have a question for you, Becca.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: pləs ə kwɪz ənd ˈwərkˌʃit, ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*. bət naʊ aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, ˈbɛkɑ.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: According to the Guinness Book of World Records,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ðə ˈgɪnəs bʊk əv wərld ˈrɛkərdz,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: the lowest temperature ever recorded was a very cold -89°C... but where?
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: the* loʊəst ˈtɛmpərəʧər ˈɛvər rɪˈkɔrdɪd wɑz ə ˈvɛri koʊld 89°c*... bət where?

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Was it: a) the Arctic, b) Antarctica, or c) the top of Mount Everest?
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: was* ɪt: ə) ðə ˈɑrtɪk, bi) ˌæˈnɑrtɪkə, ər si) ðə tɔp əv maʊnt everest?

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Hmm. I'm going to guess b) Antarctica.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: həm. əm goʊɪŋ tɪ gɛs bi) ˌæˈnɑrtɪkə.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: OK. Well, we'll find out the answer at the end of the programme. Our experience of cold depends on many things,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ. wɛl, wɪl faɪnd aʊt ðə ˈænsər æt ðə ɛnd əv ðə ˈproʊˌgræm. ɑr ɪkˈspɪriəns əv koʊld dɪˈpɛndz ɔn ˈmɛni θɪŋz,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: including our genes, culture and place of birth,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ɑr ʤinz, ˈkəlʧər ənd pleɪs əv bərθ,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: but perhaps the most obvious thing is the clothes we wear.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: bət pərˈhæps ðə moʊst ˈɑbviəs θɪŋ ɪz ðə kloʊðz wi wɛr.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Professor Gunhild Sætren lives on the icy island of Svalbard,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: prəˈfɛsər gunhild* sætren* lɪvz ɔn ðə ˈaɪsi ˈaɪlənd əv svalbard*,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: 800 miles inside the Arctic Circle.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: 800 maɪəlz ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə ˈɑrtɪk ˈsərkəl.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Here, she advises presenter Caroline Steel on how to dress against the cold,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: hir, ʃi ædˈvaɪzɪz ˈprɛzəntər ˈkɛrəˌlaɪn stil ɔn haʊ tɪ drɛs əˈgɛnst ðə koʊld,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: for BBC World Service programme CrowdScience. So, what should we consider when choosing our clothes?
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: for* ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm crowdscience*. soʊ, wət ʃʊd wi kənˈsɪdər wɪn ˈʧuzɪŋ ɑr clothes?

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: First and foremost, I would say that perhaps the gender differs.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: fərst ənd ˈfɔrˌmoʊst, aɪ wʊd seɪ ðət pərˈhæps ðə ˈʤɛndər ˈdɪfərz.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: So, males often are more tolerant, perhaps, than females. Then it's of course what you're used to.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: soʊ, meɪlz ˈɔfən ər mɔr ˈtɑlərənt, pərˈhæps, ðən ˈfiˌmeɪlz. ðɛn ɪts əv kɔrs wət jʊr juzd tɪ.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Are you used to dealing with the cold? Are you not?
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: are* ju juzd tɪ ˈdilɪŋ wɪθ ðə koʊld? ər ju not?

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Gunhild says that first and foremost, men and women feel the cold differently.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: gunhild* sɪz ðət fərst ənd ˈfɔrˌmoʊst, mɛn ənd ˈwɪmən fil ðə koʊld ˈdɪfərˈɛntli.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: She uses the phrase 'first and foremost' to mean 'more than anything else'. She wants to emphasise that something –
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ʃi ˈjuzɪz ðə freɪz 'fərst ənd ˈfɔrˌmoʊst' tɪ min 'mɔr ðən ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ɛls'. ʃi wɔnts tɪ emphasise* ðət ˈsəmθɪŋ –*

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: in this case, someone's gender – is the most important thing to consider.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ɪn ðɪs keɪs, ˈsəmˌwənz ˈʤɛndər –* ɪz ðə moʊst ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt θɪŋ tɪ kənˈsɪdər.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: There is some debate about whether men or women are more tolerant of the cold.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ðɛr ɪz səm dəˈbeɪt əˈbaʊt ˈwɛðər mɛn ər ˈwɪmən ər mɔr ˈtɑlərənt əv ðə koʊld.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Being tolerant of something means being able to endure it without getting hurt.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: biɪŋ ˈtɑlərənt əv ˈsəmθɪŋ minz biɪŋ ˈeɪbəl tɪ ɪnˈdʊr ɪt wɪˈθaʊt ˈgɪtɪŋ hərt.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Another important factor is whether you are used to the cold – if being in cold environments is something you're familiar with.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: əˈnəðər ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ˈfæktər ɪz ˈwɛðər ju ər juzd tɪ ðə koʊld –* ɪf biɪŋ ɪn koʊld ɪnˈvaɪrənmənts ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ jʊr fəˈmɪljər wɪθ.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: It might sound obvious that someone born in Arctic Svalbard would feel less cold than someone born in Brazil,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ɪt maɪt saʊnd ˈɑbviəs ðət ˈsəmˌwən bɔrn ɪn ˈɑrtɪk svalbard* wʊd fil lɛs koʊld ðən ˈsəmˌwən bɔrn ɪn brəˈzɪl,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: but apart from environmental factors, are there actual physical differences
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: bət əˈpɑrt frəm ɪnˌvaɪrənˈmɛnəl ˈfæktərz, ər ðɛr ˈækʧəwəl ˈfɪzɪkəl ˈdɪfərənsɪz

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: that allow people to cope better with the cold? Dr Cara Ocobock studies reindeer herders in northern Finland –
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: that* əˈlaʊ ˈpipəl tɪ koʊp ˈbɛtər wɪθ ðə koʊld? ˈdɑktər ˈkɛrə ocobock* ˈstədiz ˈreɪnˌdɪr ˈhərdərz ɪn ˈnɔrðərn ˈfɪnˌlænd –*

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: people who live in cold temperatures every day and have done for centuries. She measures their reaction to extreme cold
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ˈpipəl hu lɪv ɪn koʊld ˈtɛmpərəʧərz ˈɛvəri deɪ ənd hæv dən fər ˈsɛnʧəriz. ʃi ˈmɛʒərz ðɛr riˈækʃən tɪ ɪkˈstrim koʊld

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: and compares it to ordinary Finns from warmer parts of the country. Here, Cara shares her findings with Caroline Steel
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ənd kəmˈpɛrz ɪt tɪ ˈɔrdəˌnɛri fɪnz frəm ˈwɔrmər pɑrts əv ðə ˈkəntri. hir, ˈkɛrə ʃɛrz hər ˈfaɪndɪŋz wɪθ ˈkɛrəˌlaɪn stil

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: from BBC World Service's CrowdScience.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: frəm ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪsɪz crowdscience*.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: OK. My guess is the reindeer herders deal better in the cold. Yes and no.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ. maɪ gɛs ɪz ðə ˈreɪnˌdɪr ˈhərdərz dil ˈbɛtər ɪn ðə koʊld. jɛs ənd noʊ.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: The more data we collect within this area, the more confusing the picture gets.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ðə mɔr ˈdætə wi kəˈlɛkt wɪˈθɪn ðɪs ˈɛriə, ðə mɔr kənfˈjuzɪŋ ðə ˈpɪkʧər gɪts.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: I can say that subjectively, at this point, the reindeer herders,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: aɪ kən seɪ ðət subjectively*, æt ðɪs pɔɪnt, ðə ˈreɪnˌdɪr ˈhərdərz,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: at least kind of mentally, handle the cold far better. They are far less likely to shiver.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: æt list kaɪnd əv ˈmɛnəli, ˈhændəl ðə koʊld fɑr ˈbɛtər. ðeɪ ər fɑr lɛs ˈlaɪkli tɪ ˈʃɪvər.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: So, do the reindeer herders deal better with the cold?
The answer is yes and no – a phrase meaning partly and partly not,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ðə ˈænsər ɪz jɛs ənd noʊ –* ə freɪz ˈminɪŋ ˈpɑrtli ənd ˈpɑrtli nɑt,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: used when there's no clear answer to a question.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: juzd wɪn ðɛrz noʊ klɪr ˈænsər tɪ ə kˈwɛʃən.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: However, Cara does say subjectively, the herders manage better.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ˈkɛrə dɪz seɪ subjectively*, ðə ˈhərdərz ˈmænɪʤ ˈbɛtər.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: They don't feel so cold. Subjectively means based on your personal inner experience rather
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ðeɪ doʊnt fil soʊ koʊld. subjectively* minz beɪst ɔn jʊr ˈpərsɪnəl ˈɪnər ɪkˈspɪriəns ˈrəðər

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: than objective facts. And physically there are differences too.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ðən əˈbʤɛktɪv fækts. ənd ˈfɪzɪkəli ðɛr ər ˈdɪfərənsɪz tu.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Reindeer herders are less likely to shiver – the shaky movement that cold muscles make to try and warm them up.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ˈreɪnˌdɪr ˈhərdərz ər lɛs ˈlaɪkli tɪ ˈʃɪvər –* ðə ˈʃeɪki ˈmuvmənt ðət koʊld ˈməsəlz meɪk tɪ traɪ ənd wɔrm ðɛm əp.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: In fact, how we experience the cold is probably a combination of everything we've discussed,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ɪn fækt, haʊ wi ɪkˈspɪriəns ðə koʊld ɪz ˈprɑbəˌbli ə ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ wiv dɪˈskəst,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: including genetic adaptations passed on from parents to children. Right, I'm off to find my gloves and woolly hat,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ʤəˈnɛtɪk ˌædəpˈteɪʃənz pæst ɔn frəm ˈpɛrənts tɪ ˈʧɪldrən. raɪt, əm ɔf tɪ faɪnd maɪ gləvz ənd ˈwʊli hæt,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: so why don't you reveal the answer to the question, Phil?
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: so* waɪ doʊnt ju rɪˈvil ðə ˈænsər tɪ ðə kˈwɛʃən, phil?

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Yes, I asked where the lowest temperature on Earth was ever recorded. You said b) Antarctica.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: jɛs, aɪ æst wɛr ðə loʊəst ˈtɛmpərəʧər ɔn ərθ wɑz ˈɛvər rɪˈkɔrdɪd. ju sɛd bi) ˌæˈnɑrtɪkə.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: And that is... the right answer. The lowest temperature ever recorded on Earth
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ənd ðət ɪz... ðə raɪt ˈænsər. ðə loʊəst ˈtɛmpərəʧər ˈɛvər rɪˈkɔrdɪd ɔn ərθ

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: was at the Vostok Research Station in Antarctica in 1983. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: wɑz æt ðə vostok* ˈrisərʧ ˈsteɪʃən ɪn ˌæˈnɑrtɪkə ɪn 1983 lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv ˈlərnɪd,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: starting with the phrase, 'first and foremost', meaning more than anything else.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ ðə freɪz, 'fərst ənd ˈfɔrˌmoʊst', ˈminɪŋ mɔr ðən ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ɛls.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: A person who is tolerant of something is able to endure it without being hurt.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ə ˈpərsən hu ɪz ˈtɑlərənt əv ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪz ˈeɪbəl tɪ ɪnˈdʊr ɪt wɪˈθaʊt biɪŋ hərt.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: If you're used to something, you're familiar with it. The idiom 'yes and no' means partly and partly not,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ɪf jʊr juzd tɪ ˈsəmθɪŋ, jʊr fəˈmɪljər wɪθ ɪt. ðə ˈɪdiəm 'jɛs ənd noʊ' minz ˈpɑrtli ənd ˈpɑrtli nɑt,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: and is used when you can't give a clear answer to a question. The adverb 'subjectively' means in a way that's based
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ənd ɪz juzd wɪn ju kænt gɪv ə klɪr ˈænsər tɪ ə kˈwɛʃən. ðə ˈædvərb 'subjectively*' minz ɪn ə weɪ ðæts beɪst

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: on your personal experience rather than objective facts.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ɔn jʊr ˈpərsɪnəl ɪkˈspɪriəns ˈrəðər ðən əˈbʤɛktɪv fækts.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: And finally, a shiver is the shaking movement made by your muscles when you feel cold or afraid.
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: ənd ˈfaɪnəli, ə ˈʃɪvər ɪz ðə ˈʃeɪkɪŋ ˈmuvmənt meɪd baɪ jʊr ˈməsəlz wɪn ju fil koʊld ər əˈfreɪd.

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but remember you'll find a quiz and a worksheet for this episode on our website,
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: wəns əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp, bət rɪˈmɛmbər jul faɪnd ə kwɪz ənd ə ˈwərkˌʃit fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt,

GPbPAC0xS1s.text: bbclearningenglish.com. See you there soon, but for now, it's goodbye. Bye!
GPbPAC0xS1s.ipa: bbclearningenglish.com*. si ju ðɛr sun, bət fər naʊ, ɪts ˌgʊdˈbaɪ. baɪ!

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Phil. And I'm Pippa.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: hɛˈloʊ, ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm fɪl. ənd əm pippa*.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Are you a big reader, Phil? Sometimes yes, sometimes no.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ər ju ə bɪg ˈridər, fɪl? ˈsəmˌtaɪmz jɛs, ˈsəmˌtaɪmz no.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Sometimes I go a while without reading anything. What about you? Well, I really love to read.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: sometimes* aɪ goʊ ə waɪl wɪˈθaʊt ˈrɛdɪŋ ˈɛniˌθɪŋ. wət əˈbaʊt ju? wɛl, aɪ ˈrɪli ləv tɪ read.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: I read on the train to work all the time.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: aɪ rɛd ɔn ðə treɪn tɪ wərk ɔl ðə taɪm.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Ah, well, that is a great way to get some reading in, I guess.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɑ, wɛl, ðət ɪz ə greɪt weɪ tɪ gɪt səm ˈrɛdɪŋ ɪn, aɪ gɛs.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Yes and reading, of course, is a life skill – an essential skill which is useful or important in everyone's life.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: jɛs ənd ˈrɛdɪŋ, əv kɔrs, ɪz ə laɪf skɪl –* ən ɛˈsɛnʃəl skɪl wɪʧ ɪz ˈjusfəl ər ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ɪn ˈɛvriˌwənz laɪf.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: But reading can be a struggle for people with dyslexia –
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: bət ˈrɛdɪŋ kən bi ə ˈstrəgəl fər ˈpipəl wɪθ dɪsˈlɛksiə –*

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: a learning difficulty making it hard for people to read, write or spell.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ə ˈlərnɪŋ ˈdɪfɪˌkəlti ˈmeɪkɪŋ ɪt hɑrd fər ˈpipəl tɪ rɛd, raɪt ər spɛl.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: It's thought that around one in ten people in the UK are dyslexic, and many dyslexic children struggle at school,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɪts θɔt ðət əraʊnd wən ɪn tɛn ˈpipəl ɪn ðə uk* ər dɪsˈlɛksɪk, ənd ˈmɛni dɪsˈlɛksɪk ˈʧɪldrən ˈstrəgəl æt skul,

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: where the focus is on reading and writing. But in this episode, we'll be hearing how dyslexics
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: wɛr ðə ˈfoʊkɪs ɪz ɔn ˈrɛdɪŋ ənd ˈraɪtɪŋ. bət ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, wɪl bi ˈhirɪŋ haʊ dyslexics*

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: have other skills which allow them to excel in the workplace. As usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: hæv ˈəðər skɪlz wɪʧ əˈlaʊ ðɛm tɪ ɪkˈsɛl ɪn ðə ˈwərkˌpleɪs. ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl bi ˈlərnɪŋ səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd ˈfreɪzɪz,

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: and remember, you'll find all the vocabulary for this episode on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ənd rɪˈmɛmbər, jul faɪnd ɔl ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: But first, I have a question for you, Phil. Studies have shown that people with dyslexia have enhanced abilities
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: bət fərst, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, fɪl. ˈstədiz hæv ʃoʊn ðət ˈpipəl wɪθ dɪsˈlɛksiə hæv ɛnˈhænst əˈbɪləˌtiz

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: in certain areas and can excel in creative thinking, problem solving and inventiveness.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɪn ˈsərtən ˈɛriəz ənd kən ɪkˈsɛl ɪn kriˈeɪtɪv ˈθɪŋkɪŋ, ˈprɑbləm ˈsɑlvɪŋ ənd ˌɪnˈvɛntɪvnəs.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: So, which famous scientist was dyslexic?
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: so*, wɪʧ ˈfeɪməs ˈsaɪəntɪst wɑz dyslexic?

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Was it: a) Stephen Hawking, b) Albert Einstein, or c) Marie Curie?
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: was* ɪt: ə) ˈstivən ˈhɔkɪŋ, bi) ˈælbərt ˈaɪnstaɪn, ər si) mərˈi curie?

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Oh, I think... I think it's b) – I think it's Albert Einstein.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: oʊ, aɪ θɪŋk... aɪ θɪŋk ɪts bi) –* aɪ θɪŋk ɪts ˈælbərt ˈaɪnstaɪn.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Well, we'll find out the answer at the end of the programme.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: wɛl, wɪl faɪnd aʊt ðə ˈænsər æt ðə ɛnd əv ðə ˈproʊˌgræm.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Now, the British charity Made By Dyslexia shares online learning tools
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: naʊ, ðə ˈbrɪtɪʃ ˈʧɛrɪti meɪd baɪ dɪsˈlɛksiə ʃɛrz ˈɔnˌlaɪn ˈlərnɪŋ tulz

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: and works with schools and teachers to raise awareness about the condition.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ənd wərks wɪθ skulz ənd ˈtiʧərz tɪ reɪz əˈwɛrnəs əˈbaʊt ðə kənˈdɪʃən.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: The charity recently collaborated with British celebrities who have dyslexia for this promotional video:
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ðə ˈʧɛrɪti ˈrisəntli kəˈlæbərˌeɪtɪd wɪθ ˈbrɪtɪʃ səˈlɛbrɪtiz hu hæv dɪsˈlɛksiə fər ðɪs pərˈmoʊʃənəl ˈvɪdioʊ:

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: If you're dyslexic, it's kind of your superpower. It's, like, the way that you think.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɪf jʊr dɪsˈlɛksɪk, ɪts kaɪnd əv jʊr ˌsupərˈpaʊər. ɪts, laɪk, ðə weɪ ðət ju θɪŋk.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Our brains – they're wired to, I think, process information differently.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɑr breɪnz –* ðɛr waɪərd tɪ, aɪ θɪŋk, ˈprɔˌsɛs ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ˈdɪfərˈɛntli.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: The way I see the world might be different from somebody else, but that's valid. In fact, it's vital.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ðə weɪ aɪ si ðə wərld maɪt bi ˈdɪfərənt frəm ˈsəmˌbɑdi ɛls, bət ðæts ˈvælɪd. ɪn fækt, ɪts ˈvaɪtəl.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: You might recognise some of those voices, including actors Orlando Bloom and Keira Knightley
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ju maɪt ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪz səm əv ðoʊz ˈvɔɪsɪz, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ˈæktərz ɔˈrlænˌdoʊ blum ənd keira* knightley*

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: and businessman Richard Branson. The message is that, far from being a disability, dyslexia is
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ənd ˈbɪznɪsˌmæn ˈrɪʧərd ˈbrænsən. ðə ˈmɛsɪʤ ɪz ðət, fɑr frəm biɪŋ ə ˌdɪsəˈbɪlɪti, dɪsˈlɛksiə ɪz

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: their superpower – the ability to do something other people can't.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ðɛr ˌsupərˈpaʊər –* ðə əˈbɪləˌti tɪ du ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈəðər ˈpipəl kænt.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Made By Dyslexia was founded by social entrepreneur Kate Griggs, and Kate co-authored a report showing
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: meɪd baɪ dɪsˈlɛksiə wɑz ˈfaʊndɪd baɪ ˈsoʊʃəl ˌɑntrəprəˈnʊr keɪt grɪgz, ənd keɪt co-authored* ə rɪˈpɔrt ʃoʊɪŋ

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: that empowering dyslexic workers could boost the global economy by billions.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ðət ɪmˈpaʊərɪŋ dɪsˈlɛksɪk ˈwərkərz kʊd bust ðə ˈgloʊbəl ɪˈkɑnəmi baɪ ˈbɪljənz.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: BBC World Service programme People Fixing the World met Kate at the report's London launch event, where she explained
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ˈpipəl ˈfɪksɪŋ ðə wərld mɛt keɪt æt ðə rɪˈpɔrts ˈləndən lɔnʧ ɪˈvɛnt, wɛr ʃi ɪkˈspleɪnd

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: why being dyslexic can actually give advantages in life, including some exciting job prospects.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: waɪ biɪŋ dɪsˈlɛksɪk kən ˈæˌkʧuəli gɪv ædˈvæntɪʤɪz ɪn laɪf, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ səm ɪkˈsaɪtɪŋ ʤɑb ˈprɑspɛkts.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: If you think about what you have to do as a spy,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɪf ju θɪŋk əˈbaʊt wət ju hæv tɪ du ɛz ə spaɪ,

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: you have to be reading people really quickly, spotting complex patterns and solving problems really quickly,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ju hæv tɪ bi ˈrɛdɪŋ ˈpipəl ˈrɪli kˈwɪkli, ˈspɑtɪŋ ˈkɑmplɛks ˈpætərnz ənd ˈsɑlvɪŋ ˈprɑbləmz ˈrɪli kˈwɪkli,

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: so you can see whether there's a terror threat. It's natural that dyslexics are brilliant spies.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: soʊ ju kən si ˈwɛðər ðɛrz ə ˈtɛrər θrɛt. ɪts ˈnæʧərəl ðət dyslexics* ər ˈbrɪljənt spaɪz.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Kate thinks dyslexics make good spies – people who secretly collect information
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: keɪt θɪŋks dyslexics* meɪk gʊd spaɪz –* ˈpipəl hu ˈsikrɪtli kəˈlɛkt ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: about another country's government or organisations. Why? Because they're good at reading people.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: about* əˈnəðər ˈkəntriz ˈgəvərnmənt ər ˌɔrgənɪˈzeɪʃənz. waɪ? bɪˈkəz ðɛr gʊd æt ˈrɛdɪŋ ˈpipəl.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: To read people means to be able to understand someone's true intentions
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: tɪ rɛd ˈpipəl minz tɪ bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd ˈsəmˌwənz tru ˌɪnˈtɛnʧənz

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: through their body language and behaviour, rather than their words.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: θru ðɛr ˈbɑdi ˈlæŋgwɪʤ ənd bɪˈheɪvjər, ˈrəðər ðən ðɛr wərdz.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Also present at the London charity event was space scientist and dyslexic herself, Dr Maggie Aderin-Pocock.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ˈɔlsoʊ ˈprɛzənt æt ðə ˈləndən ˈʧɛrɪti ɪˈvɛnt wɑz speɪs ˈsaɪəntɪst ənd dɪsˈlɛksɪk hərˈsɛlf, ˈdɑktər ˈmæˌgi aderin-pocock*.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Here, Maggie tells BBC World Service's People Fixing the World
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: hir, ˈmæˌgi tɛlz ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪsɪz ˈpipəl ˈfɪksɪŋ ðə wərld

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: how being dyslexic contributes different skills and ideas to her project teams:
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: haʊ biɪŋ dɪsˈlɛksɪk kənˈtrɪbjuts ˈdɪfərənt skɪlz ənd aɪˈdiəz tɪ hər ˈprɑʤɛkt timz:

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Well, I think it's recognising people's skills and differences. It's about teamwork and collaboration.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: wɛl, aɪ θɪŋk ɪts ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪzɪŋ ˈpipəlz skɪlz ənd ˈdɪfərənsɪz. ɪts əˈbaʊt ˈtimˌwərk ənd kəˌlæbərˈeɪʃən.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: We do it in science and that's when we reach for the stars,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: wi du ɪt ɪn saɪəns ənd ðæts wɪn wi riʧ fər ðə stɑrz,

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: but to do that, we need to know our skills and value all the skills.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: bət tɪ du ðət, wi nid tɪ noʊ ɑr skɪlz ənd ˈvælju ɔl ðə skɪlz.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Maggie thinks that everyone's skills should be valued – or considered important.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ˈmæˌgi θɪŋks ðət ˈɛvriˌwənz skɪlz ʃʊd bi ˈvæljud –* ər kənˈsɪdərd ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Dyslexics may struggle to read or spell, but their skills in problem-solving and communication,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: dyslexics* meɪ ˈstrəgəl tɪ rɛd ər spɛl, bət ðɛr skɪlz ɪn problem-solving* ənd kəmˌjunəˈkeɪʃən,

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: for example, can be essential for scientific success. By valuing everyone's contribution,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, kən bi ɛˈsɛnʃəl fər ˌsaɪənˈtɪfɪk səkˈsɛs. baɪ ˈvæljuɪŋ ˈɛvriˌwənz ˌkɑntrəˈbjuʃən,

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: business and science teams can reach for the stars – an idiom meaning to be ambitious and try to achieve something difficult,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ˈbɪznɪs ənd saɪəns timz kən riʧ fər ðə stɑrz –* ən ˈɪdiəm ˈminɪŋ tɪ bi æmˈbɪʃəs ənd traɪ tɪ əˈʧiv ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈdɪfəkəlt,

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: even if it seems impossible. And talking of reaching for the stars, Pippa, I think
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ˈivɪn ɪf ɪt simz ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl. ənd ˈtɔkɪŋ əv ˈriʧɪŋ fər ðə stɑrz, pippa*, aɪ θɪŋk

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: it's time you revealed the answer to your question about successful scientists.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɪts taɪm ju rɪˈvild ðə ˈænsər tɪ jʊr kˈwɛʃən əˈbaʊt səkˈsɛsfəl ˈsaɪəntɪsts.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Yes, I asked you which famous scientist was dyslexic.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: jɛs, aɪ æst ju wɪʧ ˈfeɪməs ˈsaɪəntɪst wɑz dɪsˈlɛksɪk.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Was it a) Stephen Hawking, b) Albert Einstein, or c) Marie Curie? And you were right, Phil.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: was* ɪt ə) ˈstivən ˈhɔkɪŋ, bi) ˈælbərt ˈaɪnstaɪn, ər si) mərˈi ˈkjʊri? ənd ju wər raɪt, phil.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: It was Albert Einstein. OK. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɪt wɑz ˈælbərt ˈaɪnstaɪn. ˈoʊˈkeɪ. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv ˈlərnɪd,

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: starting with life skill – an essential skill, such as reading or problem-solving, which is useful in everyone's life.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ laɪf skɪl –* ən ɛˈsɛnʃəl skɪl, səʧ ɛz ˈrɛdɪŋ ər problem-solving*, wɪʧ ɪz ˈjusfəl ɪn ˈɛvriˌwənz laɪf.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: A superpower is the ability to do something that other people can't.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ə ˌsupərˈpaʊər ɪz ðə əˈbɪləˌti tɪ du ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ˈəðər ˈpipəl kænt.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: A spy is someone who secretly collects information about other countries' governments, or organisations.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ə spaɪ ɪz ˈsəmˌwən hu ˈsikrɪtli kəˈlɛkts ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən əˈbaʊt ˈəðər ˈkəntriz' ˈgəvərnmənts, ər ˌɔrgənɪˈzeɪʃənz.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: If you're good at reading people, you can understand people's real intentions
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɪf jʊr gʊd æt ˈrɛdɪŋ ˈpipəl, ju kən ˌəndərˈstænd ˈpipəlz ril ˌɪnˈtɛnʧənz

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: through how they act and behave, instead of what they say.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: θru haʊ ðeɪ ækt ənd bɪˈheɪv, ˌɪnˈstɛd əv wət ðeɪ seɪ.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: If you value something or someone, you consider them to be important.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ɪf ju ˈvælju ˈsəmθɪŋ ər ˈsəmˌwən, ju kənˈsɪdər ðɛm tɪ bi ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: And finally, the idiom to reach for the stars means to have high
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ənd ˈfaɪnəli, ðə ˈɪdiəm tɪ riʧ fər ðə stɑrz minz tɪ hæv haɪ

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: or ambitious aims and try to achieve something difficult, even if it seems impossible.
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: ər æmˈbɪʃəs eɪmz ənd traɪ tɪ əˈʧiv ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈdɪfəkəlt, ˈivɪn ɪf ɪt simz ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl.

GiQZAXCjsf4.text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but remember, you can find many more trending topics,
GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: wəns əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp, bət rɪˈmɛmbər, ju kən faɪnd ˈmɛni mɔr ˈtrɛndɪŋ ˈtɑpɪks,

GiQZAXCjsf4.ipa: pləs ə kwɪz ənd ˈwərkˌʃit fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*. hoʊp tɪ si ju ðɛr sun,

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Georgie.
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: hɛˈloʊ. ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm ˈʤɔrʤi.

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: In winter, people find different ways of keeping warm, like wearing extra clothes or staying indoors,
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: ɪn ˈwɪntər, ˈpipəl faɪnd ˈdɪfərənt weɪz əv ˈkipɪŋ wɔrm, laɪk ˈwɛrɪŋ ˈɛkstrə kloʊðz ər steɪɪŋ ˈɪnˌdɔrz,

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: but in some countries there's another way of keeping the cold out – going for a sauna.
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: bət ɪn səm ˈkəntriz ðɛrz əˈnəðər weɪ əv ˈkipɪŋ ðə koʊld aʊt –* goʊɪŋ fər ə ˈsɔnə.

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: Yes, Nordic countries like Sweden and Finland have a long history
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: jɛs, ˈnɔrdɪk ˈkəntriz laɪk sˈwidən ənd ˈfɪnˌlænd hæv ə lɔŋ ˈhɪstəri

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: of taking saunas, but now they're popping up in Britain too. Have you ever been for a sauna, Georgie? I have, yes.
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: of* ˈteɪkɪŋ ˈsɔnəz, bət naʊ ðɛr ˈpɑpɪŋ əp ɪn ˈbrɪtən tu. hæv ju ˈɛvər bɪn fər ə ˈsɔnə, ˈʤɔrʤi? aɪ hæv, yes.

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: And if I'm honest, I'm not a huge fan. I don't really like being too hot. It's uncomfortable. What about you, Neil?
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: and* ɪf əm ˈɑnəst, əm nɑt ə juʤ fæn. aɪ doʊnt ˈrɪli laɪk biɪŋ tu hɑt. ɪts ənˈkəmfərtəbəl. wət əˈbaʊt ju, neil?

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: Oh really? I quite like a sauna. You feel really refreshed afterwards. So, what is a sauna?
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: oh* ˈrɪli? aɪ kwaɪt laɪk ə ˈsɔnə. ju fil ˈrɪli riˈfrɛʃt ˈæftərwərdz. soʊ, wət ɪz ə sauna?

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: Well, a typical sauna is a small wooden room heated with steam to
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: wɛl, ə ˈtɪpɪkəl ˈsɔnə ɪz ə smɔl ˈwʊdən rum ˈhitɪd wɪθ stim tɪ

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: around 80°C. It's hot enough to make anyone sweat, but are saunas good for your health as well?
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: əraʊnd 80°c*. ɪts hɑt ɪˈnəf tɪ meɪk ˈɛniˌwən swɛt, bət ər ˈsɔnəz gʊd fər jʊr hɛlθ ɛz well?

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: That's what James Gallagher wanted to find out for BBC Radio 4 programme Inside Health.
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: ðæts wət ʤeɪmz ˈgæləgər ˈwɔntɪd tɪ faɪnd aʊt fər ˌbibiˈsi ˈreɪdiˌoʊ 4 ˈproʊˌgræm ˌɪnˈsaɪd hɛlθ.

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: We're seeing what saunas do to the human body. They're popping up all over the country

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: with claims that high temperatures boost your health and well-being, but are they all they're cracked up to be?
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: with* kleɪmz ðət haɪ ˈtɛmpərəʧərz bust jʊr hɛlθ ənd ˌwɛlˈbiɪŋ, bət ər ðeɪ ɔl ðɛr krækt əp tɪ be?

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: James wants to find out if saunas are all they're cracked up to be.
28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: ʤeɪmz wɔnts tɪ faɪnd aʊt ɪf ˈsɔnəz ər ɔl ðɛr krækt əp tɪ bi.

28EFRJaA2JQ.ipa: he* minz, ər ðeɪ ɛz gʊd ɛz ˈpipəl seɪ? ənd ðæts ɪgˈzæktli wət wɪl bi ˈfaɪndɪŋ aʊt ɪn ðɪs episode,

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: along with some useful new words and phrases. And remember, you can practise all the new vocabulary from this episode

28EFRJaA2JQ.text: with the quiz and worksheet on our website, bbclearningenglish.com. But now I have a question for you, Georgie.

a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: right*! bət du ju noʊ waɪ ðiz ˈænəməlz ɪˈvɑlvd tɪ bi blæk ənd waɪt? həm. əm nɑt soʊ sure.

a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: aɪ laɪk jʊr staɪl! səm ˈsaɪəntɪsts θɪŋk ˈzibrəz' dɪˈstɪŋktɪv blæk-

a5UADG2c0sw.text: Here, Martin discusses his experiment with Caroline Steel from BBC World Service programme CrowdScience:
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: hir, ˈmɑrtɪn dɪˈskəsɪz hɪz ɪkˈspɛrəmənt wɪθ ˈkɛrəˌlaɪn stil frəm ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm crowdscience*:

a5UADG2c0sw.text: Do we know what it is about the stripes that's putting them off? Is it somehow, like, visually confusing?
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: do* wi noʊ wət ɪt ɪz əˈbaʊt ðə straɪps ðæts ˈpʊtɪŋ ðɛm ɔf? ɪz ɪt ˈsəmˌhaʊ, laɪk, ˈvɪʒwəli kənfˈjuzɪŋ?

a5UADG2c0sw.text: We originally set out to test whether maybe there were these optical illusions at play,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: wi ərˈɪʤənəli sɛt aʊt tɪ tɛst ˈwɛðər ˈmeɪbi ðɛr wər ðiz ˈɑptɪkəl ˌɪˈluʒənz æt pleɪ,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: so we did a whole bunch of tests where we tried different patterns of rugs,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: soʊ wi dɪd ə hoʊl bənʧ əv tɛsts wɛr wi traɪd ˈdɪfərənt ˈpætərnz əv rəgz,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: but it turned out that practically every high-contrast pattern, every black-and-white pattern we used,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: bət ɪt tərnd aʊt ðət ˈpræktɪkəli ˈɛvəri high-contrast* ˈpætərn, ˈɛvəri black-and-white* ˈpætərn wi juzd,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: almost every pattern was really effective at stopping flies. Martin conducted a whole bunch of –
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˈɛvəri ˈpætərn wɑz ˈrɪli ˈifɛktɪv æt ˈstɑpɪŋ flaɪz. ˈmɑrtɪn kənˈdəktəd ə hoʊl bənʧ əv –*

a5UADG2c0sw.text: meaning a lot of – tests using different colours and patterns.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ˈminɪŋ ə lɔt əv –* tɛsts ˈjuzɪŋ ˈdɪfərənt colours* ənd ˈpætərnz.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: Black-and-white stripes created an optical illusion, a visual trick that makes your eyes see things differently from how they really are.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: black-and-white* straɪps kriˈeɪtɪd ən ˈɑptɪkəl ˌɪˈluʒən, ə ˈvɪʒəwəl trɪk ðət meɪks jʊr aɪz si θɪŋz ˈdɪfərˈɛntli frəm haʊ ðeɪ ˈrɪli ər.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: As a result, the flies were put off. They lost interest and didn't bite.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ɛz ə rɪˈzəlt, ðə flaɪz wər pʊt ɔf. ðeɪ lɔst ˈɪntəˌrɛst ənd ˈdɪdənt baɪt.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: So zebras' stripes confuse insects, telling them to look elsewhere for food.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: soʊ ˈzibrəz' straɪps kənfˈjuz ˈɪnˌsɛkts, ˈtɛlɪŋ ðɛm tɪ lʊk ˈɛlsˌwɛr fər fud.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: But are there other reasons animals are black and white?
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: but* ər ðɛr ˈəðər ˈrizənz ˈænəməlz ər blæk ənd white?

a5UADG2c0sw.text: Nobody who's seen giant pandas in a zoo would find them hard to spot. Against a grassy background, they stand out easily.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ˈnoʊˌbɑˌdi huz sin ʤaɪənt ˈpændəz ɪn ə zu wʊd faɪnd ðɛm hɑrd tɪ spɑt. əˈgɛnst ə ˈgræsi ˈbækˌgraʊnd, ðeɪ stænd aʊt ˈizəli.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: But seen from a distance in the snowy mountain forests where they live, black and white makes the perfect disguise.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: bət sin frəm ə ˈdɪstəns ɪn ðə snoʊi ˈmaʊntən ˈfɔrəsts wɛr ðeɪ lɪv, blæk ənd waɪt meɪks ðə ˈpərˌfɪkt dɪsˈgaɪz.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: Here's Professor Tim Caro, a world authority on animal behaviour,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: hɪrz prəˈfɛsər tɪm ˈkɑroʊ, ə wərld əˈθɔrəti ɔn ˈænəməl bɪˈheɪvjər,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: explaining more to Caroline Steel for BBC World Service's CrowdScience:
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ɪkˈspleɪnɪŋ mɔr tɪ ˈkɛrəˌlaɪn stil fər ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪsɪz crowdscience*:

a5UADG2c0sw.text: The giveaway for me was 15 photos of giant pandas,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ðə ˈgɪvəˌweɪ fər mi wɑz 15 ˈfoʊˌtoʊz əv ʤaɪənt ˈpændəz,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: taken either fairly close up or at a distance, and in two of those photos,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ˈteɪkən ˈiðər ˈfɛrli kloʊz əp ər æt ə ˈdɪstəns, ənd ɪn tu əv ðoʊz ˈfoʊˌtoʊz,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: I was unable to see anything in the picture other than rocks and snow. And then later on, I realised that,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: aɪ wɑz əˈneɪbəl tɪ si ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ɪn ðə ˈpɪkʧər ˈəðər ðən rɑks ənd snoʊ. ənd ðɛn ˈleɪtər ɔn, aɪ ˈriəˌlaɪzd ðət,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: in fact, there was a giant panda in the centre of that picture some distance away.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ɪn fækt, ðɛr wɑz ə ʤaɪənt ˈpændə ɪn ðə ˈsɛntər əv ðət ˈpɪkʧər səm ˈdɪstəns əˈweɪ.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: Immediately that... that clicked in my mind. The pandas were so well camouflaged Tim couldn't spot them in the photos.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ˌɪˈmiˌdiətli ðət... ðət klɪkt ɪn maɪ maɪnd. ðə ˈpændəz wər soʊ wɛl ˈkæməˌflɑʒd tɪm ˈkʊdənt spɑt ðɛm ɪn ðə ˈfoʊˌtoʊz.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: That's when something clicked in his mind – a phrase used when you suddenly understand something after trying for a long time.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ðæts wɪn ˈsəmθɪŋ klɪkt ɪn hɪz maɪnd –* ə freɪz juzd wɪn ju ˈsədənli ˌəndərˈstænd ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈæftər traɪɪŋ fər ə lɔŋ taɪm.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: Tim understood that pandas use colour to hide. He calls this a giveaway – something that makes it easy
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: tɪm ˌəndərˈstʊd ðət ˈpændəz juz ˈkələr tɪ haɪd. hi kɔlz ðɪs ə ˈgɪvəˌweɪ –* ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət meɪks ɪt ˈizi

a5UADG2c0sw.text: for people to discover something secret or unknown. So, are animals black and white to repel insects, like zebras,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: fər ˈpipəl tɪ dɪˈskəvər ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈsikrɪt ər ənˈnoʊn. soʊ, ər ˈænəməlz blæk ənd waɪt tɪ rɪˈpɛl ˈɪnˌsɛkts, laɪk ˈzibrəz,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: or for camouflage, like pandas? Let's give the final word to Hannah Rowland, a researcher
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: or* fər ˈkæməˌflɑʒ, laɪk ˈpændəz? lɛts gɪv ðə ˈfaɪnəl wərd tɪ ˈhænə ˈroʊlənd, ə researcher

a5UADG2c0sw.text: into another black-and-white animal – penguins. Really, science never has a black-and-white answer –
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ˈɪntu əˈnəðər black-and-white* ˈænəməl –* ˈpɛŋgwənz. ˈrɪli, saɪəns ˈnɛvər həz ə black-and-white* ˈænsər –*

a5UADG2c0sw.text: excuse the pun – about the... with penguins being black and white.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ɪkˈskjuz ðə pən –* əˈbaʊt ðə... wɪθ ˈpɛŋgwənz biɪŋ blæk ənd waɪt.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: It's often a very complex mixture of reasons.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ɪts ˈɔfən ə ˈvɛri ˈkɑmplɛks ˈmɪksʧər əv ˈrizənz.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: The answer to why animals are black and white is not black and white –
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ðə ˈænsər tɪ waɪ ˈænəməlz ər blæk ənd waɪt ɪz nɑt blæk ənd waɪt –*

a5UADG2c0sw.text: a situation described as black and white means one where it's easy to understand what's right and wrong.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ə ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən dɪˈskraɪbd ɛz blæk ənd waɪt minz wən wɛr ɪts ˈizi tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd wəts raɪt ənd rɔŋ.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: In fact, there's no one reason why animals are black and white.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ɪn fækt, ðɛrz noʊ wən ˈrizən waɪ ˈænəməlz ər blæk ənd waɪt.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: Zebras avoid bites, pandas hide from predators and some animals, like skunks, warn other animals they're dangerous.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ˈzibrəz əˈvɔɪd baɪts, ˈpændəz haɪd frəm ˈprɛdətərz ənd səm ˈænəməlz, laɪk skəŋks, wɔrn ˈəðər ˈænəməlz ðɛr ˈdeɪnʤərəs.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: It all depends on the specific animal. And talking of skunks reminds me of my question, Becca.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ɪt ɔl dɪˈpɛndz ɔn ðə spɪˈsɪfɪk ˈænəməl. ənd ˈtɔkɪŋ əv skəŋks riˈmaɪndz mi əv maɪ kˈwɛʃən, ˈbɛkɑ.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: According to one theory, some animals are black and white to warn potential predators they're dangerous or taste bad.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ wən ˈθɪri, səm ˈænəməlz ər blæk ənd waɪt tɪ wɔrn pəˈtɛnʃəl ˈprɛdətərz ðɛr ˈdeɪnʤərəs ər teɪst bæd.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: But what is the scientific term? And I chose c) aposematism because I'd never heard of it before.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: but* wət ɪz ðə ˌsaɪənˈtɪfɪk tərm? ənd aɪ ʧoʊz si) aposematism* bɪˈkəz aɪd ˈnɛvər hərd əv ɪt before.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: Well, that's a great way of choosing. And it's done well for you because you're correct. Woohoo!
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: wɛl, ðæts ə greɪt weɪ əv ˈʧuzɪŋ. ənd ɪts dən wɛl fər ju bɪˈkəz jʊr kərˈɛkt. woohoo*!

a5UADG2c0sw.text: OK. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with 'optical illusion' – a visual trick
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv ˈlərnɪd, ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ 'ˈɑptɪkəl ˌɪˈluʒən' –* ə ˈvɪʒəwəl trɪk

a5UADG2c0sw.text: where your eyes perceive things differently from how they really are. 'A whole bunch of something' means 'a lot of something'.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: wɛr jʊr aɪz pərˈsiv θɪŋz ˈdɪfərˈɛntli frəm haʊ ðeɪ ˈrɪli ər. 'ə hoʊl bənʧ əv ˈsəmθɪŋ' minz 'ə lɔt əv ˈsəmθɪŋ'.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: If something puts you off, it causes you to dislike it or lose interest. A giveaway is something that makes it easy
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ɪf ˈsəmθɪŋ pʊts ju ɔf, ɪt ˈkɔzɪz ju tɪ dɪsˈlaɪk ɪt ər luz ˈɪntəˌrɛst. ə ˈgɪvəˌweɪ ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət meɪks ɪt ˈizi

a5UADG2c0sw.text: for people to discover something secret or unknown. The phrase 'something clicked in my mind' is used
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: fər ˈpipəl tɪ dɪˈskəvər ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈsikrɪt ər ənˈnoʊn. ðə freɪz 'ˈsəmθɪŋ klɪkt ɪn maɪ maɪnd' ɪz juzd

a5UADG2c0sw.text: when you suddenly understand something at last. And finally, if you describe something as black and white,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: wɪn ju ˈsədənli ˌəndərˈstænd ˈsəmθɪŋ æt læst. ənd ˈfaɪnəli, ɪf ju dɪˈskraɪb ˈsəmθɪŋ ɛz blæk ənd waɪt,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: you mean it's easy to understand what is right and wrong. Once again, our six minutes are up,
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: ju min ɪts ˈizi tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd wət ɪz raɪt ənd rɔŋ. wəns əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp,

a5UADG2c0sw.text: but head over to our website, bbclearningenglish.com, for a quiz and worksheet for this episode. See you there soon.
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: bət hɛd ˈoʊvər tɪ ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*, fər ə kwɪz ənd ˈwərkˌʃit fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd. si ju ðɛr sun.

a5UADG2c0sw.text: But for now, it's goodbye. Goodbye!
a5UADG2c0sw.ipa: bət fər naʊ, ɪts ˌgʊdˈbaɪ. ˌgʊdˈbaɪ!

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Phil. And I'm Becca.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: hɛˈloʊ, ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm fɪl. ənd əm ˈbɛkɑ.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Have you ever done something physically challenging, Phil, like running a marathon maybe, or climbing a mountain?
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: have* ju ˈɛvər dən ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈfɪzɪkəli ˈʧælənʤɪŋ, fɪl, laɪk ˈrənɪŋ ə ˈmɛrəˌθɑn ˈmeɪbi, ər ˈklaɪmɪŋ ə mountain?

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: I've climbed some mountains, but only very little ones, so it wasn't that challenging. If physical challenges are something you enjoy,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: aɪv klaɪmd səm ˈmaʊntənz, bət ˈoʊnli ˈvɛri ˈlɪtəl wənz, soʊ ɪt ˈwəzənt ðət ˈʧælənʤɪŋ. ɪf ˈfɪzɪkəl ˈʧælənʤɪz ər ˈsəmθɪŋ ju ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: maybe you should try artistic swimming, or synchronised swimming as it used to be called – a combination of dance
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ˈmeɪbi ju ʃʊd traɪ ɑrˈtɪstɪk sˈwɪmɪŋ, ər synchronised* sˈwɪmɪŋ ɛz ɪt juzd tɪ bi kɔld –* ə ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv dæns

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: and gymnastics with a difference.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ənd ʤɪmˈnæstɪks wɪθ ə ˈdɪfərəns.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: In artistic swimming, athletes dance underwater while holding their breath!
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ɪn ɑrˈtɪstɪk sˈwɪmɪŋ, ˈæθˌlits dæns ˈəndərˌwɔtər waɪl ˈhoʊldɪŋ ðɛr brɛθ!

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Did you say... dance underwater?
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: dɪd ju seɪ... dæns underwater?

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Yes, swimmers dance upside down in the water, holding their breath.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: jɛs, sˈwɪmərz dæns ˈəpˈsaɪd daʊn ɪn ðə ˈwɔtər, ˈhoʊldɪŋ ðɛr brɛθ.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: As we'll hear, it's an extremely demanding sport
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ɛz wɪl hir, ɪts ən ɪkˈstrimli dɪˈmændɪŋ spɔrt

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: which requires discipline, stamina and years of training.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: wɪʧ rikˈwaɪərz ˈdɪsəplən, ˈstæmənə ənd jɪrz əv ˈtreɪnɪŋ.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: As usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl bi ˈlərnɪŋ səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd ˈfreɪzɪz,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: and you can practise these with the quiz and worksheet on our website. That's bbclearningenglish.com.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ənd ju kən ˈpræktɪs ðiz wɪθ ðə kwɪz ənd ˈwərkˌʃit ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt. ðæts bbclearningenglish.com*.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Great. But first, Phil, I have a question for you.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: greɪt. bət fərst, fɪl, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Artistic swimming became an Olympic sport in 1984,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ɑrˈtɪstɪk sˈwɪmɪŋ bɪˈkeɪm ən oʊˈlɪmpɪk spɔrt ɪn 1984

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: but which country has won the most gold medals since then?
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: but* wɪʧ ˈkəntri həz wən ðə moʊst goʊld ˈmɛdəlz sɪns then?

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Is it: a) Russia, b) China, or c) the USA?
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: is* ɪt: ə) ˈrəʃə, bi) ˈʧaɪnə, ər si) ðə usa?

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Ooh, I think it's b) China. Well, we'll reveal the correct answer later in this episode.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: u, aɪ θɪŋk ɪts bi) ˈʧaɪnə. wɛl, wɪl rɪˈvil ðə kərˈɛkt ˈænsər ˈleɪtər ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Silvia Solymosyová from Slovakia is a European Championship medallist. She's also the first artistic swimmer
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ˈsɪlviə solymosyová*á frəm sˌloʊˈvɑˌkiə ɪz ə ˌjʊrəˈpiən ˈʧæmpiənˌʃɪp medallist*. ʃiz ˈɔlsoʊ ðə fərst ɑrˈtɪstɪk sˈwɪmər

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: with over a million social media followers, after videos of her walking upside down underwater went viral.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: wɪθ ˈoʊvər ə ˈmɪljən ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə ˈfɑloʊərz, ˈæftər ˈvɪdioʊz əv hər ˈwɔkɪŋ ˈəpˈsaɪd daʊn ˈəndərˌwɔtər wɛnt ˈvaɪrəl.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Here, she explains the different types of swimming dances, called routines, to Datshiane Navanayagam, presenter
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: hir, ʃi ɪkˈspleɪnz ðə ˈdɪfərənt taɪps əv sˈwɪmɪŋ ˈdænsɪz, kɔld ruˈtinz, tɪ datshiane* navanayagam*, ˈprɛzəntər

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: of BBC World Service programme The Conversation. If you had to explain artistic swimming to someone who has never seen it,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: əv ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən. ɪf ju hæd tɪ ɪkˈspleɪn ɑrˈtɪstɪk sˈwɪmɪŋ tɪ ˈsəmˌwən hu həz ˈnɛvər sin ɪt,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: how would you describe it? So, I would say it depends if you're watching, like, a solo or duet or team routine,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: how* wʊd ju dɪˈskraɪb ɪt? soʊ, aɪ wʊd seɪ ɪt dɪˈpɛndz ɪf jʊr ˈwɑʧɪŋ, laɪk, ə ˈsoʊˌloʊ ər duˈɛt ər tim ruˈtin,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: because in solo there's one person, in duet there's two people,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: bɪˈkəz ɪn ˈsoʊˌloʊ ðɛrz wən ˈpərsən, ɪn duˈɛt ðɛrz tu ˈpipəl,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: and in team routines there can be eight people or ten people. And in the team routines, there's a lot of lifts.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ənd ɪn tim ruˈtinz ðɛr kən bi eɪt ˈpipəl ər tɛn ˈpipəl. ənd ɪn ðə tim ruˈtinz, ðɛrz ə lɔt əv lɪfts.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: And actually in the routines we are smiling, but
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ənd ˈæˌkʧuəli ɪn ðə ruˈtinz wi ər sˈmaɪlɪŋ, bət

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: we are hiding behind the smile because we are actually, maybe, dying...! We don't have oxygen, so...
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: wi ər ˈhaɪdɪŋ bɪˈhaɪnd ðə smaɪl bɪˈkəz wi ər ˈæˌkʧuəli, ˈmeɪbi, daɪɪŋ...! wi doʊnt hæv ˈɑksɪʤən, soʊ...

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: but we are smiling and people think that it's easy when it's actually not.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: bət wi ər sˈmaɪlɪŋ ənd ˈpipəl θɪŋk ðət ɪts ˈizi wɪn ɪts ˈæˌkʧuəli nɑt.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Artistic swimming routines are performed solo, meaning by just one person,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ɑrˈtɪstɪk sˈwɪmɪŋ ruˈtinz ər pərˈfɔrmd ˈsoʊˌloʊ, ˈminɪŋ baɪ ʤɪst wən ˈpərsən,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: or as a duet, which means by two people.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ər ɛz ə duˈɛt, wɪʧ minz baɪ tu ˈpipəl.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: There are also team routines where one swimmer is lifted up out of the water.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ðɛr ər ˈɔlsoʊ tim ruˈtinz wɛr wən sˈwɪmər ɪz ˈlɪftɪd əp aʊt əv ðə ˈwɔtər.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: What all these routines have in common is that, physically, they are incredibly hard.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: wət ɔl ðiz ruˈtinz hæv ɪn ˈkɑmən ɪz ðət, ˈfɪzɪkəli, ðeɪ ər ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli hɑrd.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Yes, artistic routines are done upside down, underwater, without oxygen.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: jɛs, ɑrˈtɪstɪk ruˈtinz ər dən ˈəpˈsaɪd daʊn, ˈəndərˌwɔtər, wɪˈθaʊt ˈɑksɪʤən.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: The swimmer's movements need to be as graceful as a ballet dancer, and above all, it has to look effortless
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ðə swimmer's* ˈmuvmənts nid tɪ bi ɛz ˈgreɪsfəl ɛz ə bæˈleɪ ˈdænsər, ənd əˈbəv ɔl, ɪt həz tɪ lʊk ˈɛfərtləs

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: because judges award points for artistic expression.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: bɪˈkəz ˈʤəʤɪz əˈwɔrd pɔɪnts fər ɑrˈtɪstɪk ɪkˈsprɛʃən.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Silvia says she hides behind a smile – an idiom meaning to hide pain by presenting a happy outward appearance.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ˈsɪlviə sɪz ʃi haɪdz bɪˈhaɪnd ə smaɪl –* ən ˈɪdiəm ˈminɪŋ tɪ haɪd peɪn baɪ prɪˈzɛntɪŋ ə ˈhæpi ˈaʊtwərd əˈpɪrəns.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: To succeed at the top level of any sport requires a huge amount of dedication.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: tɪ səkˈsid æt ðə tɔp ˈlɛvəl əv ˈɛni spɔrt rikˈwaɪərz ə juʤ əˈmaʊnt əv ˌdɛdəˈkeɪʃən.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Silvia trains eight hours a day, six days a week, under the guidance of her coach,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ˈsɪlviə treɪnz eɪt aʊərz ə deɪ, sɪks deɪz ə wik, ˈəndər ðə ˈgaɪdəns əv hər koʊʧ,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: who's also her mum and a former artistic swimming champion herself.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: huz ˈɔlsoʊ hər məm ənd ə ˈfɔrmər ɑrˈtɪstɪk sˈwɪmɪŋ ˈʧæmpiən hərˈsɛlf.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Here, Silvia discusses the sacrifices she's made to succeed,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: hir, ˈsɪlviə dɪˈskəsɪz ðə ˈsækrəˌfaɪsɪz ʃiz meɪd tɪ səkˈsid,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: with Datshiane Navanayagam for BBC World Service's The Conversation:
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: wɪθ datshiane* navanayagam* fər ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪsɪz ðə ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən:

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: How easy was it being trained and coached by your mum?
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: how* ˈizi wɑz ɪt biɪŋ treɪnd ənd koʊʧt baɪ jʊr mum?

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: I think it's good on one side because you can practise more at home, but on the other hand,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk ɪts gʊd ɔn wən saɪd bɪˈkəz ju kən ˈpræktɪs mɔr æt hoʊm, bət ɔn ðə ˈəðər hænd,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: it's not sometimes good because you're training when you're at home, when you're supposed to be, like, chillin'.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ɪts nɑt ˈsəmˌtaɪmz gʊd bɪˈkəz jʊr ˈtreɪnɪŋ wɪn jʊr æt hoʊm, wɪn jʊr səˈpoʊzd tɪ bi, laɪk, chillin*'.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: There's no respite from it, in a way, I guess. Yeah, I didn't have time for anything else.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ðɛrz noʊ ˈrɛspɪt frəm ɪt, ɪn ə weɪ, aɪ gɛs. jæ, aɪ ˈdɪdənt hæv taɪm fər ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ɛls.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Silvia thinks there are good and bad things about being coached by her mum. It's good because she can practise at home,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ˈsɪlviə θɪŋks ðɛr ər gʊd ənd bæd θɪŋz əˈbaʊt biɪŋ koʊʧt baɪ hər məm. ɪts gʊd bɪˈkəz ʃi kən ˈpræktɪs æt hoʊm,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: but on the other hand, it feels like she's training all the time.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: bət ɔn ðə ˈəðər hænd, ɪt filz laɪk ʃiz ˈtreɪnɪŋ ɔl ðə taɪm.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: She uses the phrase, on the other hand, to show this contrasting opinion.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ʃi ˈjuzɪz ðə freɪz, ɔn ðə ˈəðər hænd, tɪ ʃoʊ ðɪs kənˈtræstɪŋ əˈpɪnjən.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: What Silvia really looks forward to is chillin' – a slang word meaning relaxing or hanging out.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: wət ˈsɪlviə ˈrɪli lʊks ˈfɔrwərd tɪ ɪz chillin*' –* ə slæŋ wərd ˈminɪŋ rɪˈlæksɪŋ ər ˈhæŋɪŋ aʊt.
