KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: For young athletes, balancing sport with school and friends is difficult, and it's hard to get a respite from training.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: fər jəŋ ˈæθˌlits, ˈbælənsɪŋ spɔrt wɪθ skul ənd frɛndz ɪz ˈdɪfəkəlt, ənd ɪts hɑrd tɪ gɪt ə ˈrɛspɪt frəm ˈtreɪnɪŋ.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: A respite is a temporary break from doing something difficult. Do you know what, Becca?
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: a* ˈrɛspɪt ɪz ə ˈtɛmpərˌɛri breɪk frəm duɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈdɪfəkəlt. du ju noʊ wət, becca?

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: I think I need a respite from all this talk about training. Now, what was the answer to your question?
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: i* θɪŋk aɪ nid ə ˈrɛspɪt frəm ɔl ðɪs tɔk əˈbaʊt ˈtreɪnɪŋ. naʊ, wət wɑz ðə ˈænsər tɪ jʊr question?

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Ah yes, Phil! I asked you which country had won the most gold medals for artistic swimming
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ɑ jɛs, fɪl! aɪ æst ju wɪʧ ˈkəntri hæd wən ðə moʊst goʊld ˈmɛdəlz fər ɑrˈtɪstɪk sˈwɪmɪŋ

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: since the beginning of the Olympic sport? And I said I thought it was China. Well, you're very close geographically.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: since* ðə bɪˈgɪnɪŋ əv ðə oʊˈlɪmpɪk spɔrt? ənd aɪ sɛd aɪ θɔt ɪt wɑz ˈʧaɪnə. wɛl, jʊr ˈvɛri kloʊz geographically.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: It is, in fact, Russia. OK. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learnt, starting with solo –
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ɪt ɪz, ɪn fækt, ˈrəʃə. ˈoʊˈkeɪ. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv lərnt, ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ ˈsoʊˌloʊ –*

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: something done alone. And duet – something done by two people.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ˈsəmθɪŋ dən əˈloʊn. ənd duˈɛt –* ˈsəmθɪŋ dən baɪ tu ˈpipəl.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: The idiom, hide behind a smile, means to hide painful feelings by presenting a happy external appearance.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ðə ˈɪdiəm, haɪd bɪˈhaɪnd ə smaɪl, minz tɪ haɪd ˈpeɪnfəl ˈfilɪŋz baɪ prɪˈzɛntɪŋ ə ˈhæpi ɪkˈstərnəl əˈpɪrəns.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: The phrase, on the other hand, is used to introduce a contrasting opinion or statement.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ðə freɪz, ɔn ðə ˈəðər hænd, ɪz juzd tɪ ˌɪntrəˈdus ə kənˈtræstɪŋ əˈpɪnjən ər ˈsteɪtmənt.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Chillin' is slang for relaxing. And finally, a respite is a break from doing something difficult.
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: chillin*' ɪz slæŋ fər rɪˈlæksɪŋ. ənd ˈfaɪnəli, ə ˈrɛspɪt ɪz ə breɪk frəm duɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈdɪfəkəlt.

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but if you'd like to practise the vocabulary you've learnt from this
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: wəns əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp, bət ɪf jʊd laɪk tɪ ˈpræktɪs ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri juv lərnt frəm ðɪs

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: or any of our other episodes, then head over to our website, bbclearningenglish.com,
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: ər ˈɛni əv ɑr ˈəðər ˈɛpɪˌsoʊdz, ðɛn hɛd ˈoʊvər tɪ ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*,

KLWrAJ5YSwE.text: for quizzes, pronunciation tips and much more. Thanks for joining us and goodbye for now. Bye!
KLWrAJ5YSwE.ipa: fər kˈwɪzɪz, prəˌnənsiˈeɪʃən tɪps ənd məʧ mɔr. θæŋks fər ˈʤɔɪnɪŋ ˈjuˈɛs ənd ˌgʊdˈbaɪ fər naʊ. baɪ!

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Hello. This is 6 minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Becca.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: hɛˈloʊ. ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm ˈbɛkɑ.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Worrying about work or exams, War and climate change in the news? It's no wonder that modern life is stressful.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: worrying* əˈbaʊt wərk ər ɪgˈzæmz, wɔr ənd ˈklaɪmɪt ʧeɪnʤ ɪn ðə nuz? ɪts noʊ ˈwəndər ðət ˈmɑdərn laɪf ɪz stressful.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: What makes you stress, Neil? Oh, good question.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: what* meɪks ju strɛs, nil? oʊ, gʊd question.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: I think not having enough time to do something, that really makes me stressed out. How about you?
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: i* θɪŋk nɑt ˈhævɪŋ ɪˈnəf taɪm tɪ du ˈsəmθɪŋ, ðət ˈrɪli meɪks mi strɛst aʊt. haʊ əˈbaʊt you?

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Um, when I'm trying to do something that I've never tried before
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: əm, wɪn əm traɪɪŋ tɪ du ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət aɪv ˈnɛvər traɪd ˌbiˈfɔr

QdE63sYqwd8.text: and I'm not very good at it, that makes me feel really stressed. I find that stressful too.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ənd əm nɑt ˈvɛri gʊd æt ɪt, ðət meɪks mi fil ˈrɪli strɛst. aɪ faɪnd ðət ˈstrɛsfəl tu.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: And how do you cope with stressful situations, Neil?
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ənd haʊ du ju koʊp wɪθ ˈstrɛsfəl ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃənz, neil?

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Well, I try to slow down, uh, pause, take a breath and think
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: wɛl, aɪ traɪ tɪ sloʊ daʊn, ə, pɔz, teɪk ə brɛθ ənd θɪŋk

QdE63sYqwd8.text: 'at some point, probably later today, everything will be okay again.' How about you? Yeah, same.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: 'at* səm pɔɪnt, ˈprɑbəˌbli ˈleɪtər təˈdeɪ, ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ wɪl bi ˌoʊˈkeɪ əˈgɛn.' haʊ əˈbaʊt ju? jæ, same.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: But in my mind, I'm telling myself that 'I will be better at what I'm doing. I just need to practice more.'
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: bət ɪn maɪ maɪnd, əm ˈtɛlɪŋ ˌmaɪˈsɛlf ðət 'aɪ wɪl bi ˈbɛtər æt wət əm duɪŋ. aɪ ʤɪst nid tɪ ˈpræktɪs mɔr.'

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Hmm. Many young people who feel stressed about the modern world use the word overwhelm to describe their feelings.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: həm. ˈmɛni jəŋ ˈpipəl hu fil strɛst əˈbaʊt ðə ˈmɑdərn wərld juz ðə wərd ˌoʊvərˈwɛlm tɪ dɪˈskraɪb ðɛr ˈfilɪŋz.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: And overwhelm was also the topic of a recent BBC World Service programme, What in the World.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ənd ˌoʊvərˈwɛlm wɑz ˈɔlsoʊ ðə ˈtɑpɪk əv ə ˈrisənt ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm, wət ɪn ðə wərld.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Here's Iqra Farooq and Claudia Hammond. So overwhelm is an emotional state where you are struggling
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: hɪrz iqra* farooq* ənd ˈklɔdiə ˈhæmənd. soʊ ˌoʊvərˈwɛlm ɪz ən ˈiˌmoʊʃənəl steɪt wɛr ju ər ˈstrəgəlɪŋ

QdE63sYqwd8.text: with your current situation. So, you might feel overloaded,
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: wɪθ jʊr ˈkɑrənt ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən. soʊ, ju maɪt fil ˈoʊvərˌloʊdɪd,

QdE63sYqwd8.text: you might feel you can't cope and that might be a temporary thing hopefully. But if it's not in the longer term,
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ju maɪt fil ju kænt koʊp ənd ðət maɪt bi ə ˈtɛmpərˌɛri θɪŋ ˈhoʊpfəli. bət ɪf ɪts nɑt ɪn ðə ˈlɔŋgər tərm,

QdE63sYqwd8.text: if that becomes more of a kind of chronic kind of stress, then that that might lead to burnout in the end.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ɪf ðət bɪˈkəmz mɔr əv ə kaɪnd əv ˈkrɑnɪk kaɪnd əv strɛs, ðɛn ðət ðət maɪt lɛd tɪ ˈbərˌnaʊt ɪn ðə ɛnd.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Feeling overwhelmed and unable to cope with life can become chronic, meaning that it lasts for a long time.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ˈfilɪŋ ˌoʊvərˈwɛlmd ənd əˈneɪbəl tɪ koʊp wɪθ laɪf kən bɪˈkəm ˈkrɑnɪk, ˈminɪŋ ðət ɪt læsts fər ə lɔŋ taɪm.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Overwhelm can lead to burnout, mental or physical exhaustion caused by working too hard.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ˌoʊvərˈwɛlm kən lɛd tɪ ˈbərˌnaʊt, ˈmɛntəl ər ˈfɪzɪkəl ɪgˈzɔsʧən kɔzd baɪ ˈwərkɪŋ tu hɑrd.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: So how can we manage the stress in our lives and feel happier and healthier?
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: so* haʊ kən wi ˈmænɪʤ ðə strɛs ɪn ɑr lɪvz ənd fil ˈhæpiər ənd healthier?

QdE63sYqwd8.text: That's what we'll be discussing in this episode, along with some useful new words and phrases.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ðæts wət wɪl bi dɪˈskəsɪŋ ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, əˈlɔŋ wɪθ səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd ˈfreɪzɪz.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: You'll find all the vocabulary from this episode on our website bbclearningenglish.com.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: jul faɪnd ɔl ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri frəm ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt bbclearningenglish.com*.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: But before we start, I have a question for you, Neil. One simple way of combating stress is taking time to rest.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: bət ˌbiˈfɔr wi stɑrt, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, nil. wən ˈsɪmpəl weɪ əv kəmˈbætɪŋ strɛs ɪz ˈteɪkɪŋ taɪm tɪ rɛst.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: So, according to research conducted by BBC presenter and psychologist Claudia Hammond,
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: soʊ, əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ˈrisərʧ kənˈdəktəd baɪ ˌbibiˈsi ˈprɛzəntər ənd saɪˈkɑləʤəst ˈklɔdiə ˈhæmənd,

QdE63sYqwd8.text: which activity is thought to be the most restful? Is it a) reading for pleasure,
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: which* ækˈtɪvɪti ɪz θɔt tɪ bi ðə moʊst ˈrɛstfəl? ɪz ɪt ə) ˈrɛdɪŋ fər pleasure,

QdE63sYqwd8.text: b) walking in nature, or c) listening to music? Well, I think they're all quite relaxing,
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: b*) ˈwɔkɪŋ ɪn ˈneɪʧər, ər si) ˈlɪsənɪŋ tɪ mˈjuzɪk? wɛl, aɪ θɪŋk ðɛr ɔl kwaɪt rɪˈlæksɪŋ,

QdE63sYqwd8.text: but I'm going to guess b) walking in nature. I'm surprised, Neil!
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: bət əm goʊɪŋ tɪ gɛs bi) ˈwɔkɪŋ ɪn ˈneɪʧər. əm səˈpraɪzd, nil!

QdE63sYqwd8.text: I know you love music. And we'll find out the answer later.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: aɪ noʊ ju ləv mˈjuzɪk. ənd wɪl faɪnd aʊt ðə ˈænsər ˈleɪtər.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: One of the most stressful things facing many young people is exams.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: wən əv ðə moʊst ˈstrɛsfəl θɪŋz ˈfeɪsɪŋ ˈmɛni jəŋ ˈpipəl ɪz ɪgˈzæmz.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Here, Claudia Hammond offers some advice for managing exam stress to BBC World Service's What in the World:
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: hir, ˈklɔdiə ˈhæmənd ˈɔfərz səm ədˈvaɪs fər ˈmænəʤɪŋ ɪgˈzæm strɛs tɪ ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪsɪz wət ɪn ðə wərld:

QdE63sYqwd8.text: ... particularly in the exam itself, if you feel stressed, then just knowing that you well may well perform better
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ˌpɑrˈtɪkjələrli ɪn ðə ɪgˈzæm ˌɪtˈsɛlf, ɪf ju fil strɛst, ðɛn ʤɪst noʊɪŋ ðət ju wɛl meɪ wɛl pərˈfɔrm ˈbɛtər

QdE63sYqwd8.text: while you're stressed that this... it concentrates the mind, it sharpens
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: waɪl jʊr strɛst ðət ðɪs... ɪt ˈkɑnsənˌtreɪts ðə maɪnd, ɪt ˈʃɑrpənz

QdE63sYqwd8.text: and your nerves sharpen your thinking and allow you to really do your best. It's normal to feel nerves before an exam.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ənd jʊr nərvz ˈʃɑrpən jʊr ˈθɪŋkɪŋ ənd əˈlaʊ ju tɪ ˈrɪli du jʊr bɛst. ɪts ˈnɔrməl tɪ fil nərvz ˌbiˈfɔr ən ɪgˈzæm.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Nerves is an informal word for worry or anxiety, but according to Claudia., nerves aren't necessarily bad.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: nərvz ɪz ən ˌɪnˈfɔrməl wərd fər ˈwəri ər æŋˈzaɪəti, bət əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ˈklɔdiə., nərvz ˈɑrənt ˌnɛsəˈsɛrəli bæd.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Yes, nerves can help concentrate the mind. The phrase concentrate or sharpen the mind means to think more clearly
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: jɛs, nərvz kən hɛlp ˈkɑnsənˌtreɪt ðə maɪnd. ðə freɪz ˈkɑnsənˌtreɪt ər ˈʃɑrpən ðə maɪnd minz tɪ θɪŋk mɔr ˈklɪrli

QdE63sYqwd8.text: about something like an actor or an athlete feeling nerves before a big performance can help them do their best.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: əˈbaʊt ˈsəmθɪŋ laɪk ən ˈæktər ər ən ˈæθˌlit ˈfilɪŋ nərvz ˌbiˈfɔr ə bɪg pərˈfɔrməns kən hɛlp ðɛm du ðɛr bɛst.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Claudia recommends other ways to reduce stress as well.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ˈklɔdiə ˌrɛkəˈmɛndz ˈəðər weɪz tɪ rɪˈdus strɛs ɛz wɛl.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: These include slowing down and accepting that it's okay to be good enough, rather than always trying for perfection.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ðiz ˌɪnˈklud sloʊɪŋ daʊn ənd əkˈsɛptɪŋ ðət ɪts ˌoʊˈkeɪ tɪ bi gʊd ɪˈnəf, ˈrəðər ðən ˈɔlˌweɪz traɪɪŋ fər pərˈfɛkʃən.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Here, she shares more tips with Iqra Farooq of BBC World Service's What in the World:
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: hir, ʃi ʃɛrz mɔr tɪps wɪθ iqra* farooq* əv ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪsɪz wət ɪn ðə wərld:

QdE63sYqwd8.text: I guess there's no one-size-fits-all as well when it comes to this.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: aɪ gɛs ðɛrz noʊ one-size-fits-all* ɛz wɛl wɪn ɪt kəmz tɪ ðɪs.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: So, if someone's listening and thinking, I want some top tips
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: soʊ, ɪf ˈsəmˌwənz ˈlɪsənɪŋ ənd ˈθɪŋkɪŋ, aɪ wɔnt səm tɔp tɪps

QdE63sYqwd8.text: on just feeling overwhelmed generally in my life, what would you say to them?
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: on* ʤɪst ˈfilɪŋ ˌoʊvərˈwɛlmd ˈʤɛnərəli ɪn maɪ laɪf, wət wʊd ju seɪ tɪ them?

QdE63sYqwd8.text: So, first I would say except that your to-do-list is never going to end, it's always going to be there.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: soʊ, fərst aɪ wʊd seɪ ɪkˈsɛpt ðət jʊr to-do-list* ɪz ˈnɛvər goʊɪŋ tɪ ɛnd, ɪts ˈɔlˌweɪz goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ðɛr.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: So, even I know that might sound defeatist, but even if you get to the end of it today,
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: soʊ, ˈivɪn aɪ noʊ ðət maɪt saʊnd dɪˈfitɪst, bət ˈivɪn ɪf ju gɪt tɪ ðə ɛnd əv ɪt təˈdeɪ,

QdE63sYqwd8.text: tomorrow there will be something else on it. And that's just life, and that is just signs of a busy life.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: təˈmɑˌroʊ ðɛr wɪl bi ˈsəmθɪŋ ɛls ɔn ɪt. ənd ðæts ʤɪst laɪf, ənd ðət ɪz ʤɪst saɪnz əv ə ˈbɪzi laɪf.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: There are many ways to combat stress, not a one-size-fits-all solution.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ðɛr ər ˈmɛni weɪz tɪ ˈkɑmbæt strɛs, nɑt ə one-size-fits-all* səˈluʃən.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: The phrase one-size-fits-all describes trying to apply the same solution to many different problems.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ðə freɪz one-size-fits-all* dɪˈskraɪbz traɪɪŋ tɪ əˈplaɪ ðə seɪm səˈluʃən tɪ ˈmɛni ˈdɪfərənt ˈprɑbləmz.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Claudia's final piece of advice is don't worry too much about your to-do-list,
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: claudia's* ˈfaɪnəl pis əv ədˈvaɪs ɪz doʊnt ˈwəri tu məʧ əˈbaʊt jʊr to-do-list*,

QdE63sYqwd8.text: the list of all the items you have to do. Why? Because it never ends.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: the* lɪst əv ɔl ðə ˈaɪtəmz ju hæv tɪ du. waɪ? bɪˈkəz ɪt ˈnɛvər ends.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Every day there will be something new to add to the list. So, why stress? Some good advice there, Neil.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: every* deɪ ðɛr wɪl bi ˈsəmθɪŋ nu tɪ æd tɪ ðə lɪst. soʊ, waɪ strɛs? səm gʊd ədˈvaɪs ðɛr, neil.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: I think I'll try these techniques next time I feel stressed. And speaking of ways to de-stress reminds me
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk aɪl traɪ ðiz tɛkˈniks nɛkst taɪm aɪ fil strɛst. ənd ˈspikɪŋ əv weɪz tɪ de-stress* riˈmaɪndz mi

QdE63sYqwd8.text: of my question I asked you earlier. According to research conducted by BBC presenter
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: əv maɪ kˈwɛʃən aɪ æst ju ˈərliər. əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ˈrisərʧ kənˈdəktəd baɪ ˌbibiˈsi ˈprɛzəntər

QdE63sYqwd8.text: and psychologist Claudia Hammond, which activity is thought of to be the most restful? And you answered b) walking in nature.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: and* saɪˈkɑləʤəst ˈklɔdiə ˈhæmənd, wɪʧ ækˈtɪvɪti ɪz θɔt əv tɪ bi ðə moʊst ˈrɛstfəl? ənd ju ˈænsərd bi) ˈwɔkɪŋ ɪn nature.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Unfortunately, Neil, the answer is a) reading. Ah well, reading is really relaxing as well.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ənˈfɔrʧənətli, nil, ðə ˈænsər ɪz ə) ˈrɛdɪŋ. ɑ wɛl, ˈrɛdɪŋ ɪz ˈrɪli rɪˈlæksɪŋ ɛz wɛl.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: OK, let's recap the vocabulary from this episode, beginning with the adjective chronic, which means continuing for a long time.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ, lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri frəm ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, bɪˈgɪnɪŋ wɪθ ðə ˈæʤɪktɪv ˈkrɑnɪk, wɪʧ minz kənˈtɪnjuɪŋ fər ə lɔŋ taɪm.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Burnout is a feeling of physical or mental exhaustion caused by working too hard.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ˈbərˌnaʊt ɪz ə ˈfilɪŋ əv ˈfɪzɪkəl ər ˈmɛntəl ɪgˈzɔsʧən kɔzd baɪ ˈwərkɪŋ tu hɑrd.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: If you say that a difficult situation sharpens or concentrates someone's mind, you mean that it makes them think clearly.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ɪf ju seɪ ðət ə ˈdɪfəkəlt ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən ˈʃɑrpənz ər ˈkɑnsənˌtreɪts ˈsəmˌwənz maɪnd, ju min ðət ɪt meɪks ðɛm θɪŋk ˈklɪrli.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Nerves are an informal word for worry or anxiety.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: nərvz ər ən ˌɪnˈfɔrməl wərd fər ˈwəri ər æŋˈzaɪəti.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: A one-size-fits-all approach tries to apply the same solution to all problems, rather than solving them individually.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ə one-size-fits-all* əˈproʊʧ traɪz tɪ əˈplaɪ ðə seɪm səˈluʃən tɪ ɔl ˈprɑbləmz, ˈrəðər ðən ˈsɑlvɪŋ ðɛm ˌɪndɪˈvɪʤəli.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: And finally, a to-do-list is a list of all the tasks you have to do.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ənd ˈfaɪnəli, ə to-do-list* ɪz ə lɪst əv ɔl ðə tæsks ju hæv tɪ du.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: Of course, another way to beat stress is doing something you love, and if that's improving your English,
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: əv kɔrs, əˈnəðər weɪ tɪ bit strɛs ɪz duɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ ju ləv, ənd ɪf ðæts ˌɪmˈpruvɪŋ jʊr ˈɪŋlɪʃ,

QdE63sYqwd8.text: then why not visit our website bbclearningenglish.com where you'll find loads more activities for doing just that.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: ðɛn waɪ nɑt ˈvɪzɪt ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt bbclearningenglish.com* wɛr jul faɪnd loʊdz mɔr ækˈtɪvɪtiz fər duɪŋ ʤɪst ðət.

QdE63sYqwd8.text: See you again next time, but for now it's goodbye. Bye.
QdE63sYqwd8.ipa: si ju əˈgɛn nɛkst taɪm, bət fər naʊ ɪts ˌgʊdˈbaɪ. baɪ.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: There is a riddle that is so counterintuitive, it still seems wrong even if you know the answer.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɛr ɪz ə ˈrɪdəl ðət ɪz soʊ counterintuitive*, ɪt stɪl simz rɔŋ ˈivɪn ɪf ju noʊ ðə ˈænsər.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - You'd think it's an almost impossible number. - I feel like you probably hit me with some truth bomb.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: jʊd θɪŋk ɪts ən ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl ˈnəmbər.  aɪ fil laɪk ju ˈprɑbəˌbli hɪt mi wɪθ səm truθ bɔm.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - I mean, if you're trying to create controversy and you're trying to confuse people, you're gonna succeed. (both laughs)
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: aɪ min, ɪf jʊr traɪɪŋ tɪ kriˈeɪt ˈkɑntrəˌvərsi ənd jʊr traɪɪŋ tɪ kənfˈjuz ˈpipəl, jʊr ˈgɑnə səkˈsid. (boʊθ læfs)

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - There are a bunch of YouTube videos about it, but I find all of them either incorrect or incomplete.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɛr ər ə bənʧ əv ˈjuˌtub ˈvɪdioʊz əˈbaʊt ɪt, bət aɪ faɪnd ɔl əv ðɛm ˈiðər ˌɪnkərˈɛkt ər ˌɪnkəmˈplit.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So in this video, I'm going to dive deeper and explain it fully. Here is the setup.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ɪn ðɪs ˈvɪdioʊ, əm goʊɪŋ tɪ daɪv ˈdipər ənd ɪkˈspleɪn ɪt ˈfʊli. hir ɪz ðə ˈsɛˌtəp.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: (suspenseful music) Say there are 100 prisoners numbered 1 to 100.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: (səˈspɛnsfəl mˈjuzɪk) seɪ ðɛr ər 100 ˈprɪzənərz ˈnəmbərd 1 tɪ 100

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Slips of paper containing each of their numbers are randomly placed in 100 boxes in a sealed room.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: slɪps əv ˈpeɪpər kənˈteɪnɪŋ iʧ əv ðɛr ˈnəmbərz ər ˈrændəmli pleɪst ɪn 100 ˈbɑksɪz ɪn ə sild rum.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: One at a time, each prisoner is allowed to enter the room and open any 50 of the 100 boxes,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wən æt ə taɪm, iʧ ˈprɪzənər ɪz əˈlaʊd tɪ ˈɛnər ðə rum ənd ˈoʊpən ˈɛni 50 əv ðə 100 ˈbɑksɪz,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: searching for their number. And afterwards, they must leave the room exactly as they found it,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ˈsərʧɪŋ fər ðɛr ˈnəmbər. ənd ˈæftərwərdz, ðeɪ məst liv ðə rum ɪgˈzæktli ɛz ðeɪ faʊnd ɪt,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and they can't communicate in any way with the other prisoners. If all 100 prisoners find their own number
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ðeɪ kænt kəmˈjunəˌkeɪt ɪn ˈɛni weɪ wɪθ ðə ˈəðər ˈprɪzənərz. ɪf ɔl 100 ˈprɪzənərz faɪnd ðɛr oʊn ˈnəmbər

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: during their turn in the room, they will all be freed. But if even one of them fails to find their number,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ˈdʊrɪŋ ðɛr tərn ɪn ðə rum, ðeɪ wɪl ɔl bi frid. bət ɪf ˈivɪn wən əv ðɛm feɪlz tɪ faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbər,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: they will all be executed. The prisoners are allowed to strategize before any of them goes into the room.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðeɪ wɪl ɔl bi ˈɛksəˌkjutɪd. ðə ˈprɪzənərz ər əˈlaʊd tɪ ˈstrætəʤaɪz ˌbiˈfɔr ˈɛni əv ðɛm goʊz ˈɪntu ðə rum.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So what is their best strategy? If they each search for their own number randomly,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ wət ɪz ðɛr bɛst ˈstrætəʤi? ɪf ðeɪ iʧ sərʧ fər ðɛr oʊn ˈnəmbər ˈrændəmli,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: then each prisoner has a 50% chance of finding it. So the probability that all 100 prisoners find their numbers
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɛn iʧ ˈprɪzənər həz ə 50 ʧæns əv ˈfaɪndɪŋ ɪt. soʊ ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti ðət ɔl 100 ˈprɪzənərz faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbərz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: is 1/2 times 1/2 times 1/2 a hundred times or 1/2 to the power of 100.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪz 1/2* taɪmz 1/2* taɪmz 1/2* ə ˈhənərd taɪmz ər 1/2* tɪ ðə paʊər əv 100

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: This is equal to 0.00000000 30 zeros,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ˈikwəl tɪ 0.00000000* 30 ˈzɪroʊz,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and then an eight. To put this probability into perspective, two people have a better chance of picking out
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ðɛn ən eɪt. tɪ pʊt ðɪs ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti ˈɪntu pərˈspɛktɪv, tu ˈpipəl hæv ə ˈbɛtər ʧæns əv ˈpɪkɪŋ aʊt

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: the same grain of sand from all the beaches and deserts on earth than by escaping this way.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə seɪm greɪn əv sænd frəm ɔl ðə ˈbiʧɪz ənd ˈdɛzərts ɔn ərθ ðən baɪ ɪˈskeɪpɪŋ ðɪs weɪ.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: But what have I told you that with the right strategy,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bət wət hæv aɪ toʊld ju ðət wɪθ ðə raɪt ˈstrætəʤi,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: there's a way to raise their chances to nearly one in three. It improves their odds of a random chance
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɛrz ə weɪ tɪ reɪz ðɛr ˈʧænsɪz tɪ ˈnɪrli wən ɪn θri. ɪt ˌɪmˈpruvz ðɛr ɑdz əv ə ˈrændəm ʧæns

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: by nearly 30 orders of magnitude. That's like taking a millimeter and scaling it up
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: baɪ ˈnɪrli 30 ˈɔrdərz əv ˈmægnəˌtud. ðæts laɪk ˈteɪkɪŋ ə ˈmɪləˌmitər ənd ˈskeɪlɪŋ ɪt əp

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: to the diameter of the observable universe.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: tɪ ðə daɪˈæmətər əv ðə əbˈzərvəbəl ˈjunəˌvərs.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - But they can only coordinate this strategy beforehand. - Correct? - Is this true? - Yes.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bət ðeɪ kən ˈoʊnli koʊˈɔrdəˌneɪt ðɪs ˈstrætəʤi bɪˈfɔˌrhænd.  kərˈɛkt?  ɪz ðɪs tru?  jɛs.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - Teach me.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: tiʧ mi.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - This is not a trick question. The solution just involves an incredible feature of math.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɪs ɪz nɑt ə trɪk kˈwɛʃən. ðə səˈluʃən ʤɪst ˌɪnˈvɑlvz ən ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbəl ˈfiʧər əv mæθ.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So what is this mathematical strategy? Well, if you don't already know the answer,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ wət ɪz ðɪs ˌmæθəˈmætɪkəl ˈstrætəʤi? wɛl, ɪf ju doʊnt ɔˈrɛdi noʊ ðə ˈænsər,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: feel free to pause the video here and try it for yourself.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: fil fri tɪ pɔz ðə ˈvɪdioʊ hir ənd traɪ ɪt fər ˈjɔrsɛlf.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: And if you don't come up with it, don't worry, you're in good company.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ɪf ju doʊnt kəm əp wɪθ ɪt, doʊnt ˈwəri, jʊr ɪn gʊd ˈkəmpəˌni.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Even the person who came up with this riddle, computer scientist Peter Bro Miltersen, he didn't even think of this strategy
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ˈivɪn ðə ˈpərsən hu keɪm əp wɪθ ðɪs ˈrɪdəl, kəmˈpjutər ˈsaɪəntɪst ˈpitər broʊ miltersen*, hi ˈdɪdənt ˈivɪn θɪŋk əv ðɪs ˈstrætəʤi

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: until a colleague pointed it out. Miltersen, ultimately published this problem in a paper where he generously left the solution
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənˈtɪl ə ˈkɑlig ˈpɔɪntɪd ɪt aʊt. miltersen*, ˈəltəmətli ˈpəblɪʃt ðɪs ˈprɑbləm ɪn ə ˈpeɪpər wɛr hi ˈʤɛnərəsli lɛft ðə səˈluʃən

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: as an exercise for the reader.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɛz ən ˈɛksərˌsaɪz fər ðə ˈridər.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So here is the solution. Pretend you are one of the prisoners, when you go into the room,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ hir ɪz ðə səˈluʃən. priˈtɛnd ju ər wən əv ðə ˈprɪzənərz, wɪn ju goʊ ˈɪntu ðə rum,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: open the box with your number on it, the number on the slip inside probably won't be yours, but that's okay.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ˈoʊpən ðə bɑks wɪθ jʊr ˈnəmbər ɔn ɪt, ðə ˈnəmbər ɔn ðə slɪp ˌɪnˈsaɪd ˈprɑbəˌbli woʊnt bi jʊrz, bət ðæts ˌoʊˈkeɪ.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Go to the box with that number on it. look at the number inside,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: goʊ tɪ ðə bɑks wɪθ ðət ˈnəmbər ɔn ɪt. lʊk æt ðə ˈnəmbər ˌɪnˈsaɪd,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: then go to the box with that number on it, and so on.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɛn goʊ tɪ ðə bɑks wɪθ ðət ˈnəmbər ɔn ɪt, ənd soʊ ɔn.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Keep doing this until you find the slip with your number. If you find your number, that essentially tells you
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: kip duɪŋ ðɪs ənˈtɪl ju faɪnd ðə slɪp wɪθ jʊr ˈnəmbər. ɪf ju faɪnd jʊr ˈnəmbər, ðət ɛˈsɛnʃəli tɛlz ju

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: to go back to the box where you started. It closes the loop of numbers you've been following.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: tɪ goʊ bæk tɪ ðə bɑks wɛr ju ˈstɑrtɪd. ɪt ˈkloʊzɪz ðə lup əv ˈnəmbərz juv bɪn ˈfɑloʊɪŋ.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: But if you've found your number, then you're done. You can stop and leave the room.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bət ɪf juv faʊnd jʊr ˈnəmbər, ðɛn jʊr dən. ju kən stɑp ənd liv ðə rum.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: This simple strategy gives over a 30% chance that all the prisoners will find their number.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɪs ˈsɪmpəl ˈstrætəʤi gɪvz ˈoʊvər ə 30 ʧæns ðət ɔl ðə ˈprɪzənərz wɪl faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbər.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - The entire pool has a 30... - Everyone can find their number 31% of the time.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə ɪnˈtaɪər pul həz ə 30  ˈɛvriˌwən kən faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbər 31 əv ðə taɪm.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - What?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wət?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - But how does it work?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bət haʊ dɪz ɪt wərk?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: The first thing to notice is that all boxes become part of a closed loop.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə fərst θɪŋ tɪ ˈnoʊtɪs ɪz ðət ɔl ˈbɑksɪz bɪˈkəm pɑrt əv ə kloʊzd lup.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: The simplest loop would be a box that contains its own number.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə ˈsɪmpləst lup wʊd bi ə bɑks ðət kənˈteɪnz ɪts oʊn ˈnəmbər.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: If you're prisoner number one and you go to box one, it contains slip one, then you're done.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪf jʊr ˈprɪzənər ˈnəmbər wən ənd ju goʊ tɪ bɑks wən, ɪt kənˈteɪnz slɪp wən, ðɛn jʊr dən.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Your number was part of a loop of one, but you could also have a loop of two.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: jʊr ˈnəmbər wɑz pɑrt əv ə lup əv wən, bət ju kʊd ˈɔlsoʊ hæv ə lup əv tu.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Say box one points to box seven and box seven points back to box one.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: seɪ bɑks wən pɔɪnts tɪ bɑks ˈsɛvən ənd bɑks ˈsɛvən pɔɪnts bæk tɪ bɑks wən.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Or you could have a loop of three, or four, or five,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ər ju kʊd hæv ə lup əv θri, ər fɔr, ər faɪv,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: or any length all the way up to 100. The longest loop you could have would connect
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ər ˈɛni lɛŋθ ɔl ðə weɪ əp tɪ 100 ðə ˈlɔŋgɪst lup ju kʊd hæv wʊd kəˈnɛkt

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: all the numbers in a single loop. But more generally, any random arrangement of the slips in these boxes
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɔl ðə ˈnəmbərz ɪn ə ˈsɪŋgəl lup. bət mɔr ˈʤɛnərəli, ˈɛni ˈrændəm ərˈeɪnʤmənt əv ðə slɪps ɪn ðiz ˈbɑksɪz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: will result in a mixture of some shorter and some longer loops.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪl rɪˈzəlt ɪn ə ˈmɪksʧər əv səm ˈʃɔrtər ənd səm ˈlɔŋgər lups.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: When you start with a box labeled with your number, you are guaranteed to be on the loop that includes your slip.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪn ju stɑrt wɪθ ə bɑks ˈleɪbəld wɪθ jʊr ˈnəmbər, ju ər ˌgɛrənˈtid tɪ bi ɔn ðə lup ðət ˌɪnˈkludz jʊr slɪp.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So the thing that determines whether or not you find your slip is the length of the loop.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ðə θɪŋ ðət dɪˈtərmənz ˈwɛðər ər nɑt ju faɪnd jʊr slɪp ɪz ðə lɛŋθ əv ðə lup.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: If your number is part of a loop that is shorter than 50, then you will definitely find your slip.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪf jʊr ˈnəmbər ɪz pɑrt əv ə lup ðət ɪz ˈʃɔrtər ðən 50 ðɛn ju wɪl ˈdɛfənətli faɪnd jʊr slɪp.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: But if your number is part of a loop that is 51 or longer, you are in trouble.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bət ɪf jʊr ˈnəmbər ɪz pɑrt əv ə lup ðət ɪz 51 ər ˈlɔŋgər, ju ər ɪn ˈtrəbəl.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: You won't find it before you've exhausted the 50 boxes you're allowed to search.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ju woʊnt faɪnd ɪt ˌbiˈfɔr juv ɪgˈzɔstɪd ðə 50 ˈbɑksɪz jʊr əˈlaʊd tɪ sərʧ.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: When you open the box labeled with your number, you are in fact starting
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪn ju ˈoʊpən ðə bɑks ˈleɪbəld wɪθ jʊr ˈnəmbər, ju ər ɪn fækt ˈstɑrtɪŋ

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: at the farthest point on the loop from your slip. You wanna know where is the slip that points to this box,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: æt ðə ˈfɑrðəst pɔɪnt ɔn ðə lup frəm jʊr slɪp. ju ˈwɑnə noʊ wɛr ɪz ðə slɪp ðət pɔɪnts tɪ ðɪs bɑks,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: but to find it, you have to follow the loop of numbers all the way around to the end.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bət tɪ faɪnd ɪt, ju hæv tɪ ˈfɑloʊ ðə lup əv ˈnəmbərz ɔl ðə weɪ əraʊnd tɪ ðə ɛnd.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: That means if the prisoners follow this strategy and the longest loop is 51, not just one or two prisoners
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðət minz ɪf ðə ˈprɪzənərz ˈfɑloʊ ðɪs ˈstrætəʤi ənd ðə ˈlɔŋgɪst lup ɪz 51 nɑt ʤɪst wən ər tu ˈprɪzənərz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: will fail to find their number, but all 51 on this loop won't make it.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪl feɪl tɪ faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbər, bət ɔl 51 ɔn ðɪs lup woʊnt meɪk ɪt.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: They make it to the box just before the box with their slip, but they have to stop searching there.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðeɪ meɪk ɪt tɪ ðə bɑks ʤɪst ˌbiˈfɔr ðə bɑks wɪθ ðɛr slɪp, bət ðeɪ hæv tɪ stɑp ˈsərʧɪŋ ðɛr.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: (both laughs)
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: (boʊθ læfs)

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So the probability that all of the prisoners succeed is just the probability that a random arrangement
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti ðət ɔl əv ðə ˈprɪzənərz səkˈsid ɪz ʤɪst ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti ðət ə ˈrændəm ərˈeɪnʤmənt

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: of a hundred numbers contains no loops longer than 50. Now I promised that this probability would come out
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: əv ə ˈhənərd ˈnəmbərz kənˈteɪnz noʊ lups ˈlɔŋgər ðən 50 naʊ aɪ ˈprɑməst ðət ðɪs ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti wʊd kəm aʊt

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: to around one in three, but how do we calculate it? Well, imagine writing down all the different ways
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: tɪ əraʊnd wən ɪn θri, bət haʊ du wi ˈkælkjəˌleɪt ɪt? wɛl, ˌɪˈmæʤən ˈraɪtɪŋ daʊn ɔl ðə ˈdɪfərənt weɪz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: that you could connect 100 boxes to form a loop of length 100.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðət ju kʊd kəˈnɛkt 100 ˈbɑksɪz tɪ fɔrm ə lup əv lɛŋθ 100

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So you could have box one points to box two, box two points to box three to box four, and so on,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ju kʊd hæv bɑks wən pɔɪnts tɪ bɑks tu, bɑks tu pɔɪnts tɪ bɑks θri tɪ bɑks fɔr, ənd soʊ ɔn,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: all the way to 100, and then box 100 would point back to box one,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɔl ðə weɪ tɪ 100 ənd ðɛn bɑks 100 wʊd pɔɪnt bæk tɪ bɑks wən,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: or you could have something random. Box five points to box 99 to box 17 and so on,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ər ju kʊd hæv ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈrændəm. bɑks faɪv pɔɪnts tɪ bɑks 99 tɪ bɑks 17 ənd soʊ ɔn,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and let's pick the last one, is 63.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd lɛts pɪk ðə læst wən, ɪz 63

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: And box 63 points back to box five. So how many different arrangements of these a hundred boxes
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd bɑks 63 pɔɪnts bæk tɪ bɑks faɪv. soʊ haʊ ˈmɛni ˈdɪfərənt ərˈeɪnʤmənts əv ðiz ə ˈhənərd ˈbɑksɪz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: or permutations could you have? Well, for the first box, I have 100 different boxes that I could choose from.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ər ˌpərmjuˈteɪʃənz kʊd ju hæv? wɛl, fər ðə fərst bɑks, aɪ hæv 100 ˈdɪfərənt ˈbɑksɪz ðət aɪ kʊd ʧuz frəm.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: The second box, because I've already used one, I can only pick from 99 boxes,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə ˈsɛkənd bɑks, bɪˈkəz aɪv ɔˈrɛdi juzd wən, aɪ kən ˈoʊnli pɪk frəm 99 ˈbɑksɪz,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and the next one, I can pick from 98 boxes, and so on, down to the very last box.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ðə nɛkst wən, aɪ kən pɪk frəm 98 ˈbɑksɪz, ənd soʊ ɔn, daʊn tɪ ðə ˈvɛri læst bɑks.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: I don't really have a choice. There's only one box left I could put in the last position.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: aɪ doʊnt ˈrɪli hæv ə ʧɔɪs. ðɛrz ˈoʊnli wən bɑks lɛft aɪ kʊd pʊt ɪn ðə læst pəˈzɪʃən.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So the total number of different permutations would be 100 times 99, times 98, times 97, all the way down to one.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ðə ˈtoʊtəl ˈnəmbər əv ˈdɪfərənt ˌpərmjuˈteɪʃənz wʊd bi 100 taɪmz 99 taɪmz 98 taɪmz 97 ɔl ðə weɪ daʊn tɪ wən.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: That is just 100 factorial. There are 100 factorial different ways
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðət ɪz ʤɪst 100 factorial*. ðɛr ər 100 factorial* ˈdɪfərənt weɪz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: that you could create a loop of a hundred boxes. But what we can't forget
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðət ju kʊd kriˈeɪt ə lup əv ə ˈhənərd ˈbɑksɪz. bət wət wi kænt fərˈgɛt

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: is that these are not just lines of numbers. They are loops.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪz ðət ðiz ər nɑt ʤɪst laɪnz əv ˈnəmbərz. ðeɪ ər lups.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So some of these lines that look different are actually the same loop.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ səm əv ðiz laɪnz ðət lʊk ˈdɪfərənt ər ˈæˌkʧuəli ðə seɪm lup.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: For example, two, three, four, five, and so on up to 100 and then 1
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, tu, θri, fɔr, faɪv, ənd soʊ ɔn əp tɪ 100 ənd ðɛn 1

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: is the same thing as 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 to 100. You can rearrange the way you write these numbers
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪz ðə seɪm θɪŋ ɛz 1 2 3 4 5 tɪ 100 ju kən riəreɪnʤ ðə weɪ ju raɪt ðiz ˈnəmbərz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: a hundred different ways, but they all represent the same loop.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ə ˈhənərd ˈdɪfərənt weɪz, bət ðeɪ ɔl ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnt ðə seɪm lup.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So the total number of unique loops of length 100 is 100 factorial divided by 100.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ðə ˈtoʊtəl ˈnəmbər əv juˈnik lups əv lɛŋθ 100 ɪz 100 factorial* dɪˈvaɪdɪd baɪ 100

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So what is the probability that any random arrangement of 100 boxes
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ wət ɪz ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti ðət ˈɛni ˈrændəm ərˈeɪnʤmənt əv 100 ˈbɑksɪz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: will contain a loop of length 100? Well, it's just equal to the number of unique such loops that we just calculated,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪl kənˈteɪn ə lup əv lɛŋθ 100 wɛl, ɪts ʤɪst ˈikwəl tɪ ðə ˈnəmbər əv juˈnik səʧ lups ðət wi ʤɪst ˈkælkjəˌleɪtɪd,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: 100 factorial over 100, divided by the total number of ways that you could put a hundred slips in 100 boxes,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: 100 factorial* ˈoʊvər 100 dɪˈvaɪdɪd baɪ ðə ˈtoʊtəl ˈnəmbər əv weɪz ðət ju kʊd pʊt ə ˈhənərd slɪps ɪn 100 ˈbɑksɪz,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: which is 100 factorial.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz 100 factorial*.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So the answer is 1 over 100. So there is a 1% chance that a random arrangement of slips
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ðə ˈænsər ɪz 1 ˈoʊvər 100 soʊ ðɛr ɪz ə 1 ʧæns ðət ə ˈrændəm ərˈeɪnʤmənt əv slɪps

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: results in a loop of length 100, and this is a general result.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: rɪˈzəlts ɪn ə lup əv lɛŋθ 100 ənd ðɪs ɪz ə ˈʤɛnərəl rɪˈzəlt.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: The probability that you get a loop of length 99 is 1 over 99.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti ðət ju gɪt ə lup əv lɛŋθ 99 ɪz 1 ˈoʊvər 99

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: The probability that you get a loop of length 98 is 1 over 98.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti ðət ju gɪt ə lup əv lɛŋθ 98 ɪz 1 ˈoʊvər 98

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So the probability that there is a loop longer than 50 is 1 over 51 plus 1 over 52
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti ðət ðɛr ɪz ə lup ˈlɔŋgər ðən 50 ɪz 1 ˈoʊvər 51 pləs 1 ˈoʊvər 52

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: plus one over 53, et cetera. Add all these up, and it equals .69.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: pləs wən ˈoʊvər 53 ɛt ˈsɛtərə. æd ɔl ðiz əp, ənd ɪt ˈikwəlz 69

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: There is a 69% chance of failure for the prisoners, meaning a 31% chance of success
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɛr ɪz ə 69 ʧæns əv ˈfeɪljər fər ðə ˈprɪzənərz, ˈminɪŋ ə 31 ʧæns əv səkˈsɛs

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: where the longest loop is 50 or shorter.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɛr ðə ˈlɔŋgɪst lup ɪz 50 ər ˈʃɔrtər.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - I still find it difficult to believe. - [Derek] This feels a bit like magic. Using the loop strategy,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: aɪ stɪl faɪnd ɪt ˈdɪfəkəlt tɪ bɪˈliv.  [ˈdɛrɪk] ðɪs filz ə bɪt laɪk ˈmæʤɪk. ˈjuzɪŋ ðə lup ˈstrætəʤi,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: all the prisoners are more likely to find their numbers than even just two prisoners choosing at random.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɔl ðə ˈprɪzənərz ər mɔr ˈlaɪkli tɪ faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbərz ðən ˈivɪn ʤɪst tu ˈprɪzənərz ˈʧuzɪŋ æt ˈrændəm.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So using the loop strategy, what is the probability that each prisoner alone finds their number?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ˈjuzɪŋ ðə lup ˈstrætəʤi, wət ɪz ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti ðət iʧ ˈprɪzənər əˈloʊn faɪndz ðɛr ˈnəmbər?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: It is still 50%. Each prisoner can still only open half the boxes,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪt ɪz stɪl 50 iʧ ˈprɪzənər kən stɪl ˈoʊnli ˈoʊpən hæf ðə ˈbɑksɪz,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: so their individual chance is still 1/2,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ðɛr ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəl ʧæns ɪz stɪl 1/2*

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: but these probabilities are no longer independent of each other.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bət ðiz ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌtiz ər noʊ ˈlɔŋgər ˌɪndɪˈpɛndənt əv iʧ ˈəðər.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Imagine running this experiment a thousand times over. If everyone is guessing randomly,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ˌɪˈmæʤən ˈrənɪŋ ðɪs ɪkˈspɛrəmənt ə ˈθaʊzənd taɪmz ˈoʊvər. ɪf ˈɛvriˌwən ɪz ˈgɛsɪŋ ˈrændəmli,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: you'd expect that on most runs around 50 prisoners would find their number.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: jʊd ɪkˈspɛkt ðət ɔn moʊst rənz əraʊnd 50 ˈprɪzənərz wʊd faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbər.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: On lucky runs, the number would be a bit higher, on unlucky runs, a bit lower. But using the loop strategy,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɔn ˈləki rənz, ðə ˈnəmbər wʊd bi ə bɪt haɪər, ɔn ənˈləki rənz, ə bɪt loʊər. bət ˈjuzɪŋ ðə lup ˈstrætəʤi,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: all of the prisoners would find their numbers 31% of the time.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɔl əv ðə ˈprɪzənərz wʊd faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbərz 31 əv ðə taɪm.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: And 69% of the time, fewer than 50 find their number. The prisoners all win together
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd 69 əv ðə taɪm, fjuər ðən 50 faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbər. ðə ˈprɪzənərz ɔl wɪn təˈgɛðər

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: or the majority loses together. That's how this strategy works.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ər ðə məˈʤɔrəti ˈluzɪz təˈgɛðər. ðæts haʊ ðɪs ˈstrætəʤi wərks.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - Why are you assuming that your number will always be on the loop that you're on?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: waɪ ər ju əˈsumɪŋ ðət jʊr ˈnəmbər wɪl ˈɔlˌweɪz bi ɔn ðə lup ðət jʊr ɔn?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - I feel like- - I don't understand that. - This is a key question, right?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: aɪ fil laɪk-  aɪ doʊnt ˌəndərˈstænd ðət.  ðɪs ɪz ə ki kˈwɛʃən, raɪt?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - 'Cause I feel like it's possible to start and go on a complete loop
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: 'kɔz aɪ fil laɪk ɪts ˈpɑsəbəl tɪ stɑrt ənd goʊ ɔn ə kəmˈplit lup

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and not come back to your own number because you got on the wrong loop
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd nɑt kəm bæk tɪ jʊr oʊn ˈnəmbər bɪˈkəz ju gɑt ɔn ðə rɔŋ lup

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and then you'd have to get on another loop, so I don't know that I'd buy this.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ðɛn jʊd hæv tɪ gɪt ɔn əˈnəðər lup, soʊ aɪ doʊnt noʊ ðət aɪd baɪ ðɪs.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - Right, right, right, right, right, right. Now, the big question everyone asks is how do you know that if you start
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: raɪt, raɪt, raɪt, raɪt, raɪt, raɪt. naʊ, ðə bɪg kˈwɛʃən ˈɛvriˌwən æsks ɪz haʊ du ju noʊ ðət ɪf ju stɑrt

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: with a box with your number on it you are guaranteed to be on the loop that contains your slip?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪθ ə bɑks wɪθ jʊr ˈnəmbər ɔn ɪt ju ər ˌgɛrənˈtid tɪ bi ɔn ðə lup ðət kənˈteɪnz jʊr slɪp?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Well, if you think about it, the slip that says 73, if anyone sees that,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɛl, ɪf ju θɪŋk əˈbaʊt ɪt, ðə slɪp ðət sɪz 73 ɪf ˈɛniˌwən siz ðət,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: they will definitely go to the box with the number 73. So the slip and box with the same number
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðeɪ wɪl ˈdɛfənətli goʊ tɪ ðə bɑks wɪθ ðə ˈnəmbər 73 soʊ ðə slɪp ənd bɑks wɪθ ðə seɪm ˈnəmbər

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: essentially form a unit. They're like a little Lego brick.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɛˈsɛnʃəli fɔrm ə ˈjunɪt. ðɛr laɪk ə ˈlɪtəl ˈlɛgoʊ brɪk.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: And then every slip is hidden inside another box.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ðɛn ˈɛvəri slɪp ɪz ˈhɪdən ˌɪnˈsaɪd əˈnəðər bɑks.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So as I start laying out slips and boxes randomly, you can see that there's no way
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ɛz aɪ stɑrt leɪɪŋ aʊt slɪps ənd ˈbɑksɪz ˈrændəmli, ju kən si ðət ðɛrz noʊ weɪ

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: that we can end up with a dead end. It's not like you can just get to a box and then stop
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðət wi kən ɛnd əp wɪθ ə dɛd ɛnd. ɪts nɑt laɪk ju kən ʤɪst gɪt tɪ ə bɑks ənd ðɛn stɑp

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: because every box contains a slip and that points at another box.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bɪˈkəz ˈɛvəri bɑks kənˈteɪnz ə slɪp ənd ðət pɔɪnts æt əˈnəðər bɑks.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So the only way for you to see only boxes when you walk into the room
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ ðə ˈoʊnli weɪ fər ju tɪ si ˈoʊnli ˈbɑksɪz wɪn ju wɔk ˈɪntu ðə rum

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: is for every slip to be contained within a box,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪz fər ˈɛvəri slɪp tɪ bi kənˈteɪnd wɪˈθɪn ə bɑks,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and that necessarily will mean that we are forming loops. So when I start with box 73,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ðət ˌnɛsəˈsɛrəli wɪl min ðət wi ər ˈfɔrmɪŋ lups. soʊ wɪn aɪ stɑrt wɪθ bɑks 73

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: I must eventually find slip 73, because then and only then will I be directed to go back to box 73
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: aɪ məst ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli faɪnd slɪp 73 bɪˈkəz ðɛn ənd ˈoʊnli ðɛn wɪl aɪ bi dɪˈrɛktɪd tɪ goʊ bæk tɪ bɑks 73

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: which closes the loop.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪʧ ˈkloʊzɪz ðə lup.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - Who is the warden to this prison? And what kind of sadistic mathematical warden are you dealing with here?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: hu ɪz ðə ˈwɔrdən tɪ ðɪs ˈprɪzən? ənd wət kaɪnd əv səˈdɪstɪk ˌmæθəˈmætɪkəl ˈwɔrdən ər ju ˈdilɪŋ wɪθ hir?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: This is awful. - Now, what if there is a sympathetic prison guard
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ˈɔfəl.  naʊ, wət ɪf ðɛr ɪz ə ˌsɪmpəˈθɛtɪk ˈprɪzən gɑrd

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: who sneaks into the room before any of the prisoners go in? Well, then they can guarantee success for the prisoners
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: hu sniks ˈɪntu ðə rum ˌbiˈfɔr ˈɛni əv ðə ˈprɪzənərz goʊ ɪn? wɛl, ðɛn ðeɪ kən ˌgɛrənˈti səkˈsɛs fər ðə ˈprɪzənərz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: by swapping the contents of just two boxes. That's because there can be at most one loop
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: baɪ sˈwɑpɪŋ ðə ˈkɑntɛnts əv ʤɪst tu ˈbɑksɪz. ðæts bɪˈkəz ðɛr kən bi æt moʊst wən lup

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: that is longer than 50, and you can break it in half
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðət ɪz ˈlɔŋgər ðən 50 ənd ju kən breɪk ɪt ɪn hæf

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: just by swapping the contents of two boxes. And now I have two separate loops that are each shorter than 50.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ʤɪst baɪ sˈwɑpɪŋ ðə ˈkɑntɛnts əv tu ˈbɑksɪz. ənd naʊ aɪ hæv tu ˈsɛpərˌeɪt lups ðət ər iʧ ˈʃɔrtər ðən 50

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: But what if there was a malicious guard who figured out that the prisoners were going to use this loop strategy?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bət wət ɪf ðɛr wɑz ə məˈlɪʃəs gɑrd hu ˈfɪgjərd aʊt ðət ðə ˈprɪzənərz wər goʊɪŋ tɪ juz ðɪs lup ˈstrætəʤi?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Well, then they could put the numbers in boxes to ensure they formed a loop longer than 50.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɛl, ðɛn ðeɪ kʊd pʊt ðə ˈnəmbərz ɪn ˈbɑksɪz tɪ ɪnˈʃʊr ðeɪ fɔrmd ə lup ˈlɔŋgər ðən 50

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: In this case, are the prisoners doomed? Surprisingly, no.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪn ðɪs keɪs, ər ðə ˈprɪzənərz dumd? səˈpraɪzɪŋli, noʊ.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: They can counter by arbitrarily renumbering the boxes. They could, for example, add five to each box number.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðeɪ kən ˈkaʊntər baɪ ˈɑrbɪˌtrɛrəli renumbering* ðə ˈbɑksɪz. ðeɪ kʊd, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, æd faɪv tɪ iʧ bɑks ˈnəmbər.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: The loops are set both by the locations of the slips and by the box numbers.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə lups ər sɛt boʊθ baɪ ðə loʊˈkeɪʃənz əv ðə slɪps ənd baɪ ðə bɑks ˈnəmbərz.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Renumbering the boxes is essentially the same as redistributing the slips. So the problem is back to a random arrangement of loops,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: renumbering* ðə ˈbɑksɪz ɪz ɛˈsɛnʃəli ðə seɪm ɛz ˌridɪˈstrɪbjətɪŋ ðə slɪps. soʊ ðə ˈprɑbləm ɪz bæk tɪ ə ˈrændəm ərˈeɪnʤmənt əv lups,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: meaning the prisoners are back to their 31% chance of survival.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ˈminɪŋ ðə ˈprɪzənərz ər bæk tɪ ðɛr 31 ʧæns əv sərˈvaɪvəl.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Now, what happens if you increase the number of prisoners? - Fun fact, nobody knows if
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: naʊ, wət ˈhæpənz ɪf ju ˌɪnˈkris ðə ˈnəmbər əv ˈprɪzənərz?  fən fækt, ˈnoʊˌbɑˌdi noʊz ɪf

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: as you have more and more prisoners it's going towards a limit, or if it'll eventually go down to zero, or what?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɛz ju hæv mɔr ənd mɔr ˈprɪzənərz ɪts goʊɪŋ təˈwɔrdz ə ˈlɪmət, ər ɪf ˈɪtəl ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli goʊ daʊn tɪ ˈziroʊ, ər wət?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: - That is my friend Matt Parker, and I think what he meant to say is we know exactly what happens
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðət ɪz maɪ frɛnd mæt ˈpɑrkər, ənd aɪ θɪŋk wət hi mɛnt tɪ seɪ ɪz wi noʊ ɪgˈzæktli wət ˈhæpənz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: as you increase the number of prisoners. With a thousand prisoners each allowed to check 500 boxes,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɛz ju ˌɪnˈkris ðə ˈnəmbər əv ˈprɪzənərz. wɪθ ə ˈθaʊzənd ˈprɪzənərz iʧ əˈlaʊd tɪ ʧɛk 500 ˈbɑksɪz,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: you might expect their chance of success to drop dramatically, but you can calculate it like we did before,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ju maɪt ɪkˈspɛkt ðɛr ʧæns əv səkˈsɛs tɪ drɔp drəˈmætɪkəli, bət ju kən ˈkælkjəˌleɪt ɪt laɪk wi dɪd ˌbiˈfɔr,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and it comes out to 30.74%, only half a percentage point lower than for 100 prisoners.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ɪt kəmz aʊt tɪ 30.74* ˈoʊnli hæf ə pərˈsɛnɪʤ pɔɪnt loʊər ðən fər 100 ˈprɪzənərz.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: For 1 million prisoners, the probability of success is 30.685%,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: fər 1 ˈmɪljən ˈprɪzənərz, ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti əv səkˈsɛs ɪz 30.685*

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: which is only a little higher than for 1 billion. Of course, their bigger problem would be
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz ˈoʊnli ə ˈlɪtəl haɪər ðən fər 1 ˈbɪljən. əv kɔrs, ðɛr ˈbɪgər ˈprɑbləm wʊd bi

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: the time it takes to open all the boxes. So your probability of winning this game does indeed approach a limit.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə taɪm ɪt teɪks tɪ ˈoʊpən ɔl ðə ˈbɑksɪz. soʊ jʊr ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti əv ˈwɪnɪŋ ðɪs geɪm dɪz ˌɪnˈdid əˈproʊʧ ə ˈlɪmət.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: So what is that limit?
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: soʊ wət ɪz ðət ˈlɪmət?

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: The formula we've been using is one minus the chance of failure,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə ˈfɔrmjələ wiv bɪn ˈjuzɪŋ ɪz wən ˈmaɪnəs ðə ʧæns əv ˈfeɪljər,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: which is the series 1 over 51 plus 1 over 52, and so on, up to 1 over 100.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz ðə ˈsɪriz 1 ˈoʊvər 51 pləs 1 ˈoʊvər 52 ənd soʊ ɔn, əp tɪ 1 ˈoʊvər 100

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: We can depict this series as the sum of areas of rectangles, and there is a curve that follows
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wi kən dɪˈpɪkt ðɪs ˈsɪriz ɛz ðə səm əv ˈɛriəz əv ˈrɛkˌtæŋgəlz, ənd ðɛr ɪz ə kərv ðət ˈfɑloʊz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: the heights of these blocks. That curve is one over X. The area under that curve from 50 to 100
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə haɪts əv ðiz blɑks. ðət kərv ɪz wən ˈoʊvər ɛks. ðə ˈɛriə ˈəndər ðət kərv frəm 50 tɪ 100

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: approximates the area of all the rectangles. And as the number of prisoners goes to infinity,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: əˈprɑksəˌmeɪts ðə ˈɛriə əv ɔl ðə ˈrɛkˌtæŋgəlz. ənd ɛz ðə ˈnəmbər əv ˈprɪzənərz goʊz tɪ ˌɪnˈfɪnɪti,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: it becomes a better and better approximation. So to find the probability of failure,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪt bɪˈkəmz ə ˈbɛtər ənd ˈbɛtər əˌprɑksəˈmeɪʃən. soʊ tɪ faɪnd ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti əv ˈfeɪljər,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: we can just take the integral of one over X from n to 2n.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wi kən ʤɪst teɪk ðə ˈɪnəgrəl əv wən ˈoʊvər ɛks frəm ɛn tɪ 2n*.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: And we find that it's equal to the natural logarithm of two. This gives a probability of success
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd wi faɪnd ðət ɪts ˈikwəl tɪ ðə ˈnæʧərəl ˈlɑgərˌɪðəm əv tu. ðɪs gɪvz ə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti əv səkˈsɛs

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: of one minus the natural log of two, which is about 30.7%. The bottom line is
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: əv wən ˈmaɪnəs ðə ˈnæʧərəl lɔg əv tu, wɪʧ ɪz əˈbaʊt 30.7* ðə ˈbɑtəm laɪn ɪz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: that no matter how many prisoners you have, you'll always end up with above a 30% chance of escaping using this strategy.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðət noʊ ˈmætər haʊ ˈmɛni ˈprɪzənərz ju hæv, jul ˈɔlˌweɪz ɛnd əp wɪθ əˈbəv ə 30 ʧæns əv ɪˈskeɪpɪŋ ˈjuzɪŋ ðɪs ˈstrætəʤi.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: And that feels really wrong. I mean, at first it seemed essentially impossible for all 100 prisoners to find their numbers.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ðət filz ˈrɪli rɔŋ. aɪ min, æt fərst ɪt simd ɛˈsɛnʃəli ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl fər ɔl 100 ˈprɪzənərz tɪ faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbərz.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: But now we're seeing that you could have a hundred, a million, or any arbitrarily large number of prisoners
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: bət naʊ wɪr siɪŋ ðət ju kʊd hæv ə ˈhənərd, ə ˈmɪljən, ər ˈɛni ˈɑrbɪˌtrɛrəli lɑrʤ ˈnəmbər əv ˈprɪzənərz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: with at least a 30% chance that they all find their numbers. The beauty of the loop strategy
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪθ æt list ə 30 ʧæns ðət ðeɪ ɔl faɪnd ðɛr ˈnəmbərz. ðə ˈbjuti əv ðə lup ˈstrætəʤi

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: is linking everyone's outcomes together, instead of each prisoner walking in with their own 50-50 shot.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ɪz ˈlɪŋkɪŋ ˈɛvriˌwənz ˈaʊtˌkəmz təˈgɛðər, ˌɪnˈstɛd əv iʧ ˈprɪzənər ˈwɔkɪŋ ɪn wɪθ ðɛr oʊn 50-50* ʃɑt.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Following the same loops means that they have the exact same chance of finding their number as everyone else in their loop.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ðə seɪm lups minz ðət ðeɪ hæv ðə ɪgˈzækt seɪm ʧæns əv ˈfaɪndɪŋ ðɛr ˈnəmbər ɛz ˈɛvriˌwən ɛls ɪn ðɛr lup.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: And once the boxes and slips are arranged, that chance is set at either 100% or 0%.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd wəns ðə ˈbɑksɪz ənd slɪps ər əreɪnʤd, ðət ʧæns ɪz sɛt æt ˈiðər 100 ər 0

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: With this strategy, you can't ever get close to winning with only a few people missing their numbers.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: wɪθ ðɪs ˈstrætəʤi, ju kænt ˈɛvər gɪt kloʊz tɪ ˈwɪnɪŋ wɪθ ˈoʊnli ə fju ˈpipəl ˈmɪsɪŋ ðɛr ˈnəmbərz.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: You can only fail hard or succeed completely.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ju kən ˈoʊnli feɪl hɑrd ər səkˈsid kəmˈplitli.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Now, if you like solving puzzles even outside of life-threatening prison situations, well, you'll love Brilliant, these sponsor of this video.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: naʊ, ɪf ju laɪk ˈsɑlvɪŋ ˈpəzəlz ˈivɪn ˈaʊtˈsaɪd əv ˈlaɪfθˌrɛtnɪŋ ˈprɪzən ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃənz, wɛl, jul ləv ˈbrɪljənt, ðiz ˈspɑnsər əv ðɪs ˈvɪdioʊ.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Brilliant is a website and app that builds problem-solving skills and guides you through engaging interactive lessons in math and science.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ˈbrɪljənt ɪz ə ˈwɛbˌsaɪt ənd æp ðət bɪldz problem-solving* skɪlz ənd gaɪdz ju θru ɪnˈgeɪʤɪŋ ˌɪnərˈæktɪv ˈlɛsənz ɪn mæθ ənd saɪəns.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: They have great courses on tons of topics, from statistics to astrophysics to logic. Now, if you liked this riddle
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðeɪ hæv greɪt ˈkɔrsɪz ɔn tənz əv ˈtɑpɪks, frəm stəˈtɪstɪks tɪ ˌæstroʊˈfɪzɪks tɪ ˈlɑʤɪk. naʊ, ɪf ju laɪkt ðɪs ˈrɪdəl

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and you want more just like it, I'd recommend their perplexing probability course, featuring other seemingly impossible odds
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ju wɔnt mɔr ʤɪst laɪk ɪt, aɪd ˌrɛkəˈmɛnd ðɛr pərˈplɛksɪŋ ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti kɔrs, ˈfiʧərɪŋ ˈəðər ˈsimɪŋli ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl ɑdz

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and even another mathematically inclined prison warden. They've also got a joy of problem solving course
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ˈivɪn əˈnəðər ˌmæθəˈmætɪkəli ˌɪnˈklaɪnd ˈprɪzən ˈwɔrdən. ðeɪv ˈɔlsoʊ gɑt ə ʤɔɪ əv ˈprɑbləm ˈsɑlvɪŋ kɔrs

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: to take you through some of their most delightful math puzzles. Every lesson builds on what you learned previously
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: tɪ teɪk ju θru səm əv ðɛr moʊst dɪˈlaɪtfəl mæθ ˈpəzəlz. ˈɛvəri ˈlɛsən bɪldz ɔn wət ju ˈlərnɪd ˈpriviəsli

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: to give you an in-depth understanding with any course you take. Your knowledge is constantly developed
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: tɪ gɪv ju ən in-depth* ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ wɪθ ˈɛni kɔrs ju teɪk. jʊr ˈnɑlɪʤ ɪz ˈkɑnstəntli dɪˈvɛləpt

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and tested through interactive experiments and quizzes. And if you get stuck, there's always a helpful hint.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd ˈtɛstɪd θru ˌɪnərˈæktɪv ɪkˈspɛrəmənts ənd kˈwɪzɪz. ənd ɪf ju gɪt stək, ðɛrz ˈɔlˌweɪz ə ˈhɛlpfəl hɪnt.

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: Head over to brilliant.org/veritasium to check out all these courses and test your instincts after learning about
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: hɛd ˈoʊvər tɪ brilliant.org/veritasium* tɪ ʧɛk aʊt ɔl ðiz ˈkɔrsɪz ənd tɛst jʊr ˈɪnstɪŋkts ˈæftər ˈlərnɪŋ əˈbaʊt

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: the 100 prisoners riddle. If you click through right now, Brilliant are offering 20% off an annual premium subscription
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ðə 100 ˈprɪzənərz ˈrɪdəl. ɪf ju klɪk θru raɪt naʊ, ˈbrɪljənt ər ˈɔfərɪŋ 20 ɔf ən ˈænjuəl ˈprimiəm səbˈskrɪpʃən

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: to the first 200 people to sign up. So I wanna thank brilliant for supporting Veritasium,
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: tɪ ðə fərst 200 ˈpipəl tɪ saɪn əp. soʊ aɪ ˈwɑnə θæŋk ˈbrɪljənt fər səˈpɔrtɪŋ veritasium*,

iSNsgj1OCLA.text: and I want to thank you for watching.
iSNsgj1OCLA.ipa: ənd aɪ wɔnt tɪ θæŋk ju fər ˈwɑʧɪŋ.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: What makes for a great live performance is when you walk away, and that air hits you in the parking lot,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: wət meɪks fər ə greɪt lɪv pərˈfɔrməns ɪz wɪn ju wɔk əˈweɪ, ənd ðət ɛr hɪts ju ɪn ðə ˈpɑrkɪŋ lɔt,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: and you’re kind of wondering, what just happened?
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd you’re* kaɪnd əv ˈwəndərɪŋ, wət ʤɪst ˈhæpənd?

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: Hey, you. Yes, you! Is there music inside of you? We’ve recruited working musicians from throughout the industry
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: heɪ, ju. jɛs, ju! ɪz ðɛr mˈjuzɪk ˌɪnˈsaɪd əv ju? we’ve* rɪˈkrutɪd ˈwərkɪŋ mjuˈzɪʃənz frəm θruaʊt ðə ˈɪndəstri

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: to help you hear it, hold it, and share it with this wild and wonderful world.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: tɪ hɛlp ju hir ɪt, hoʊld ɪt, ənd ʃɛr ɪt wɪθ ðɪs waɪld ənd ˈwəndərfəl wərld.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: A great live performer is someone who is at one with their sound,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ə greɪt lɪv pərˈfɔrmər ɪz ˈsəmˌwən hu ɪz æt wən wɪθ ðɛr saʊnd,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: and there's no separation between themselves and the sound they're making.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd ðɛrz noʊ ˌsɛpərˈeɪʃən bɪtˈwin ðɛmˈsɛlvz ənd ðə saʊnd ðɛr ˈmeɪkɪŋ.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: I like to just feel really present, really in my body,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: aɪ laɪk tɪ ʤɪst fil ˈrɪli ˈprɛzənt, ˈrɪli ɪn maɪ ˈbɑdi,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: in this state of bliss that lifts up people around me
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ɪn ðɪs steɪt əv blɪs ðət lɪfts əp ˈpipəl əraʊnd mi

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: and makes them want to tune in.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd meɪks ðɛm wɔnt tɪ tun ɪn.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: For me, what makes for a compelling performance is when you are comfortable. If people are coming to see me perform,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: fər mi, wət meɪks fər ə kəmˈpɛlɪŋ pərˈfɔrməns ɪz wɪn ju ər ˈkəmfərtəbəl. ɪf ˈpipəl ər ˈkəmɪŋ tɪ si mi pərˈfɔrm,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: in essence they’re coming to see me do something they could never imagine doing. They want to watch me be comfortable
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ɪn ˈɛsəns they’re* ˈkəmɪŋ tɪ si mi du ˈsəmθɪŋ ðeɪ kʊd ˈnɛvər ˌɪˈmæʤən duɪŋ. ðeɪ wɔnt tɪ wɔʧ mi bi ˈkəmfərtəbəl

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: because the thought makes them uncomfortable. Part of my exercise is being myself and being comfortable
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: bɪˈkəz ðə θɔt meɪks ðɛm ənˈkəmfərtəbəl. pɑrt əv maɪ ˈɛksərˌsaɪz ɪz biɪŋ ˌmaɪˈsɛlf ənd biɪŋ ˈkəmfərtəbəl

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: because that's the beginning of performing in a genuine way.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: bɪˈkəz ðæts ðə bɪˈgɪnɪŋ əv pərˈfɔrmɪŋ ɪn ə ˈʤɛnjuˌaɪn weɪ.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: Me having a great show is not always going to be me having my best vocal night,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: mi ˈhævɪŋ ə greɪt ʃoʊ ɪz nɑt ˈɔlˌweɪz goʊɪŋ tɪ bi mi ˈhævɪŋ maɪ bɛst ˈvoʊkəl naɪt,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: but it's going to be whether or not I was able to move the people in the crowd.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: bət ɪts goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ˈwɛðər ər nɑt aɪ wɑz ˈeɪbəl tɪ muv ðə ˈpipəl ɪn ðə kraʊd.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: The stage is a magnifying glass.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ðə steɪʤ ɪz ə ˈmægnəˌfaɪɪŋ glæs.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: We can tell if you're not telling the truth.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: wi kən tɛl ɪf jʊr nɑt ˈtɛlɪŋ ðə truθ.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: The audience’s experience watching you is going to be a reflection
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ðə audience’s* ɪkˈspɪriəns ˈwɑʧɪŋ ju ɪz goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ə rɪˈflɛkʃən

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: of your experience on stage. If you're having a good time on stage, they're going to have a good time watching you.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: əv jʊr ɪkˈspɪriəns ɔn steɪʤ. ɪf jʊr ˈhævɪŋ ə gʊd taɪm ɔn steɪʤ, ðɛr goʊɪŋ tɪ hæv ə gʊd taɪm ˈwɑʧɪŋ ju.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: I like to treat the audience as a couple of my friends in my living room.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: aɪ laɪk tɪ trit ðə ˈɔdiəns ɛz ə ˈkəpəl əv maɪ frɛndz ɪn maɪ ˈlɪvɪŋ rum.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: My friend off to the right, my friend down the middle, and my friend on the left.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: maɪ frɛnd ɔf tɪ ðə raɪt, maɪ frɛnd daʊn ðə ˈmɪdəl, ənd maɪ frɛnd ɔn ðə lɛft.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: I always tried to make sure everybody is engaged on every side.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: aɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz traɪd tɪ meɪk ʃʊr ˈɛvriˌbɑdi ɪz ɪnˈgeɪʤd ɔn ˈɛvəri saɪd.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: And more importantly, it’s like,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd mɔr ˌɪmˈpɔrtəntli, it’s* laɪk,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: don't do anything except the way you would do it for your best friends in your living room.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: doʊnt du ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ɪkˈsɛpt ðə weɪ ju wʊd du ɪt fər jʊr bɛst frɛndz ɪn jʊr ˈlɪvɪŋ rum.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: And one of the things about being a professional musician is you have to play well, no matter if the audience is
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd wən əv ðə θɪŋz əˈbaʊt biɪŋ ə prəˈfɛʃənəl mjuˈzɪʃən ɪz ju hæv tɪ pleɪ wɛl, noʊ ˈmætər ɪf ðə ˈɔdiəns ɪz

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: giving you a bunch of energy or not. Sometimes you have a great audience,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ˈgɪvɪŋ ju ə bənʧ əv ˈɛnərʤi ər nɑt. ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ju hæv ə greɪt ˈɔdiəns,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: sometimes maybe it’s a Sunday night and people are a little sleepy and you don’t have a great audience.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ˈmeɪbi it’s* ə ˈsənˌdi naɪt ənd ˈpipəl ər ə ˈlɪtəl sˈlipi ənd ju don’t* hæv ə greɪt ˈɔdiəns.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: But that shouldn’t matter to the professional musician— you have to play. We’re in an era of rampant price gouging—
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: bət ðət shouldn’t* ˈmætər tɪ ðə prəˈfɛʃənəl musician—*— ju hæv tɪ pleɪ. we’re* ɪn ən ˈɪrə əv ˈræmpənt praɪs gouging—*—

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: people are really spending a lot of money on tickets to go and see people play—
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ˈpipəl ər ˈrɪli ˈspɛndɪŋ ə lɔt əv ˈməni ɔn ˈtɪkɪts tɪ goʊ ənd si ˈpipəl play—*—

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: so you want to make sure they get their money’s worth
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: soʊ ju wɔnt tɪ meɪk ʃʊr ðeɪ gɪt ðɛr money’s* wərθ

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: because it’s not really fair to go out there and phone it in.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: bɪˈkəz it’s* nɑt ˈrɪli fɛr tɪ goʊ aʊt ðɛr ənd foʊn ɪt ɪn.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: I really think music is where you really connect to people,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: aɪ ˈrɪli θɪŋk mˈjuzɪk ɪz wɛr ju ˈrɪli kəˈnɛkt tɪ ˈpipəl,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: but it's okay that it's also show business and you are going to be performing.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: bət ɪts ˌoʊˈkeɪ ðət ɪts ˈɔlsoʊ ʃoʊ ˈbɪznɪs ənd ju ər goʊɪŋ tɪ bi pərˈfɔrmɪŋ.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: You’re going to be on stage and you want to create something visual
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: you’re* goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ɔn steɪʤ ənd ju wɔnt tɪ kriˈeɪt ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈvɪʒəwəl

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: that people want to look at while they’re listening to you. They will listen better
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ðət ˈpipəl wɔnt tɪ lʊk æt waɪl they’re* ˈlɪsənɪŋ tɪ ju. ðeɪ wɪl ˈlɪsən ˈbɛtər

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: if there’s something in your energy that feels good. You have to have a visual component and a musical component
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ɪf there’s* ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪn jʊr ˈɛnərʤi ðət filz gʊd. ju hæv tɪ hæv ə ˈvɪʒəwəl kəmˈpoʊnənt ənd ə mˈjuzɪkəl kəmˈpoʊnənt

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: to make a good show, I feel like. Sometimes it’s an exploration in identity that is just as important
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: tɪ meɪk ə gʊd ʃoʊ, aɪ fil laɪk. ˈsəmˌtaɪmz it’s* ən ˌɛksplərˈeɪʃən ɪn aɪˈdɛntəˌti ðət ɪz ʤɪst ɛz ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: as exploration of technique.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ɛz ˌɛksplərˈeɪʃən əv tɛkˈnik.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: When you look at performers, like Billie Eilish’s colored hair and her baggy clothes,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: wɪn ju lʊk æt pərˈfɔrmərz, laɪk ˈbɪli eilish’s* ˈkələrd hɛr ənd hər ˈbægi kloʊðz,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: makes people feel an identity.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: meɪks ˈpipəl fil ən aɪˈdɛntəˌti.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: How I connect with fans in a live setting is the music first.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: haʊ aɪ kəˈnɛkt wɪθ fænz ɪn ə lɪv ˈsɛtɪŋ ɪz ðə mˈjuzɪk fərst.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: The trust that the music is going to be to be where it’s supposed to be on a nightly basis, second.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ðə trəst ðət ðə mˈjuzɪk ɪz goʊɪŋ tɪ bi tɪ bi wɛr it’s* səˈpoʊzd tɪ bi ɔn ə ˈnaɪtli ˈbeɪsɪs, ˈsɛkənd.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: And the rest is left to beautiful chance.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd ðə rɛst ɪz lɛft tɪ ˈbjutəfəl ʧæns.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: So, let’s get really clear.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: soʊ, let’s* gɪt ˈrɪli klɪr.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: Six grown men aren’t meant to be traveling on a bus together for a month.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: sɪks groʊn mɛn aren’t* mɛnt tɪ bi ˈtrævəlɪŋ ɔn ə bəs təˈgɛðər fər ə mənθ.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: For it to work, that in and of itself is going to be a bond. Chemistry on stage is incredibly important.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: fər ɪt tɪ wərk, ðət ɪn ənd əv ˌɪtˈsɛlf ɪz goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ə bɑnd. ˈkɛmɪstri ɔn steɪʤ ɪz ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: The audience feels it if you guys are all on the same page.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ðə ˈɔdiəns filz ɪt ɪf ju gaɪz ər ɔl ɔn ðə seɪm peɪʤ.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: And I think that also extends to the front of house engineer and the person who's mixing your ears.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd aɪ θɪŋk ðət ˈɔlsoʊ ɪkˈstɛndz tɪ ðə frənt əv haʊs ˈɛnʤəˈnɪr ənd ðə ˈpərsən huz ˈmɪksɪŋ jʊr ɪrz.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: That person is incredibly important. If I can't hear myself and if I can't hear my band,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ðət ˈpərsən ɪz ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt. ɪf aɪ kænt hir ˌmaɪˈsɛlf ənd ɪf aɪ kænt hir maɪ bænd,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: that has everything to do with the chemistry and the level of show that I’m going to be able to put on.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ðət həz ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ tɪ du wɪθ ðə ˈkɛmɪstri ənd ðə ˈlɛvəl əv ʃoʊ ðət i’m* goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ pʊt ɔn.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: Trusting each other on stage, runs across many different kinds of ways of performing,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ˈtrəstɪŋ iʧ ˈəðər ɔn steɪʤ, rənz əˈkrɔs ˈmɛni ˈdɪfərənt kaɪnz əv weɪz əv pərˈfɔrmɪŋ,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: you have to trust what everyone else is doing. and have the utmost confidence that they’re doing their utmost
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ju hæv tɪ trəst wət ˈɛvriˌwən ɛls ɪz duɪŋ. ənd hæv ðə ˈətˌmoʊst ˈkɑnfədɛns ðət they’re* duɪŋ ðɛr ˈətˌmoʊst

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: to perform their role to a high standard. The people that I’ve surrounded myself with who are in my band
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: tɪ pərˈfɔrm ðɛr roʊl tɪ ə haɪ ˈstændərd. ðə ˈpipəl ðət i’ve* sərˈaʊndɪd ˌmaɪˈsɛlf wɪθ hu ər ɪn maɪ bænd

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: we’re also connected musically. And everyone kind of comes from a different musical background,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: we’re* ˈɔlsoʊ kəˈnɛktɪd mˈjuzɪkli. ənd ˈɛvriˌwən kaɪnd əv kəmz frəm ə ˈdɪfərənt mˈjuzɪkəl ˈbækˌgraʊnd,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: which is cool because all of these different ideas can come together in such a perfect way.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz kul bɪˈkəz ɔl əv ðiz ˈdɪfərənt aɪˈdiəz kən kəm təˈgɛðər ɪn səʧ ə ˈpərˌfɪkt weɪ.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: So if you have a great sense of rhythm and a strong pocket,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: soʊ ɪf ju hæv ə greɪt sɛns əv ˈrɪðəm ənd ə strɔŋ ˈpɑkət,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: I love you, that makes all the difference to me.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: aɪ ləv ju, ðət meɪks ɔl ðə ˈdɪfərəns tɪ mi.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: How do you get to the pocket? How do you be in the pocket? Wow, that's a great question.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: haʊ du ju gɪt tɪ ðə ˈpɑkət? haʊ du ju bi ɪn ðə ˈpɑkət? waʊ, ðæts ə greɪt kˈwɛʃən.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: Essentially, pocket is groove or feel. If someone says, “oh, they’re in the pocket”
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ɛˈsɛnʃəli, ˈpɑkət ɪz gruv ər fil. ɪf ˈsəmˌwən sɪz, ““oh*, they’re* ɪn ðə pocket”*”

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: it means that they’re in time with each other, they’re in sync with one another.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ɪt minz ðət they’re* ɪn taɪm wɪθ iʧ ˈəðər, they’re* ɪn sɪŋk wɪθ wən əˈnəðər.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: The pocket is between a bass player and a drummer. When the groove feels very effortless,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ðə ˈpɑkət ɪz bɪtˈwin ə bæs pleɪər ənd ə ˈdrəmər. wɪn ðə gruv filz ˈvɛri ˈɛfərtləs,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: and there is, it’s breathing in a way, you can say about a musician they have great pocket, right?
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd ðɛr ɪz, it’s* ˈbriðɪŋ ɪn ə weɪ, ju kən seɪ əˈbaʊt ə mjuˈzɪʃən ðeɪ hæv greɪt ˈpɑkət, raɪt?

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: People that just naturally lay down,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ˈpipəl ðət ʤɪst ˈnæʧərəli leɪ daʊn,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: they play and you’re like, oh, I have to move when they play.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ðeɪ pleɪ ənd you’re* laɪk, oʊ, aɪ hæv tɪ muv wɪn ðeɪ pleɪ.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: You can’t get there without listening to what other people are doing. You need to be listening to the drums, bass,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ju can’t* gɪt ðɛr wɪˈθaʊt ˈlɪsənɪŋ tɪ wət ˈəðər ˈpipəl ər duɪŋ. ju nid tɪ bi ˈlɪsənɪŋ tɪ ðə drəmz, bæs,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: anyone else who is on stage with you— you need to be listening to them
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ˈɛniˌwən ɛls hu ɪz ɔn steɪʤ wɪθ you—*— ju nid tɪ bi ˈlɪsənɪŋ tɪ ðɛm

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: and figuring out how you can make your sound fit in with their sound.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd ˈfɪgjərɪŋ aʊt haʊ ju kən meɪk jʊr saʊnd fɪt ɪn wɪθ ðɛr saʊnd.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: We're not robots, right? And that's where the pocket comes in, because it's not about perfect time.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: wɪr nɑt ˈroʊˌbəts, raɪt? ənd ðæts wɛr ðə ˈpɑkət kəmz ɪn, bɪˈkəz ɪts nɑt əˈbaʊt ˈpərˌfɪkt taɪm.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: It’s about the feel, right?
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: it’s* əˈbaʊt ðə fil, raɪt?

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: And so that comes with time.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd soʊ ðət kəmz wɪθ taɪm.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: Comes with time and just experience and playing with the right guys that have the pocket.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: kəmz wɪθ taɪm ənd ʤɪst ɪkˈspɪriəns ənd pleɪɪŋ wɪθ ðə raɪt gaɪz ðət hæv ðə ˈpɑkət.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: And then at one particular moment, I remember when it happened to me,
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ənd ðɛn æt wən ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr ˈmoʊmənt, aɪ rɪˈmɛmbər wɪn ɪt ˈhæpənd tɪ mi,

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: I finally found the pocket and I was like, thank the Lord! I’ve been looking for this moment all my life.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: aɪ ˈfaɪnəli faʊnd ðə ˈpɑkət ənd aɪ wɑz laɪk, θæŋk ðə lɔrd! i’ve* bɪn ˈlʊkɪŋ fər ðɪs ˈmoʊmənt ɔl maɪ laɪf.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: You need to be willing to make mistakes. A lot of people say you need to dare to suck.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ju nid tɪ bi ˈwɪlɪŋ tɪ meɪk mɪˈsteɪks. ə lɔt əv ˈpipəl seɪ ju nid tɪ dɛr tɪ sək.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: You have to find your way through the clunkiness to get to a point of where, “oh, this feels right!”
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ju hæv tɪ faɪnd jʊr weɪ θru ðə clunkiness* tɪ gɪt tɪ ə pɔɪnt əv wɛr, ““oh*, ðɪs filz right!”*!”

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: Each person has to find their own survival system.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: iʧ ˈpərsən həz tɪ faɪnd ðɛr oʊn sərˈvaɪvəl ˈsɪstəm.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: What works for you? What works for you? What works for your voice? What works for your family?
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: wət wərks fər ju? wət wərks fər ju? wət wərks fər jʊr vɔɪs? wət wərks fər jʊr ˈfæməli?

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: What works for your heart? What do you need? Because I think that life also is important.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: wət wərks fər jʊr hɑrt? wət du ju nid? bɪˈkəz aɪ θɪŋk ðət laɪf ˈɔlsoʊ ɪz ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt.

zrjhaYc-9pg.text: It's important to live life, to be able to have something to sing about.
zrjhaYc-9pg.ipa: ɪts ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt tɪ lɪv laɪf, tɪ bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ hæv ˈsəmθɪŋ tɪ sɪŋ əˈbaʊt.

MBir652KZPE.text: My lyric process is, first and foremost, to overthink,
MBir652KZPE.ipa: maɪ ˈlɪrɪk ˈprɔˌsɛs ɪz, fərst ənd ˈfɔrˌmoʊst, tɪ overthink*,

MBir652KZPE.text: and then say, now how do we say that like we’re not nerds?
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ənd ðɛn seɪ, naʊ haʊ du wi seɪ ðət laɪk we’re* nɑt nərdz?

MBir652KZPE.text: Hey, you! Yes, you. Is there music inside of you? We’ve recruited working musicians from throughout the industry
MBir652KZPE.ipa: heɪ, ju! jɛs, ju. ɪz ðɛr mˈjuzɪk ˌɪnˈsaɪd əv ju? we’ve* rɪˈkrutɪd ˈwərkɪŋ mjuˈzɪʃənz frəm θruaʊt ðə ˈɪndəstri

MBir652KZPE.text: to help you hear it, hold it, and share it with this wild and wonderful world.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: tɪ hɛlp ju hir ɪt, hoʊld ɪt, ənd ʃɛr ɪt wɪθ ðɪs waɪld ənd ˈwəndərfəl wərld.

MBir652KZPE.text: I think a great lyric, it does two things: one, I think it helps if your lyric is unique,
MBir652KZPE.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk ə greɪt ˈlɪrɪk, ɪt dɪz tu θɪŋz: wən, aɪ θɪŋk ɪt hɛlps ɪf jʊr ˈlɪrɪk ɪz juˈnik,

MBir652KZPE.text: its really important where someone can listen to it and be like,
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ɪts ˈrɪli ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt wɛr ˈsəmˌwən kən ˈlɪsən tɪ ɪt ənd bi laɪk,

MBir652KZPE.text: okay, this is a lyric that I have not heard before,
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ˌoʊˈkeɪ, ðɪs ɪz ə ˈlɪrɪk ðət aɪ hæv nɑt hərd ˌbiˈfɔr,

MBir652KZPE.text: or a take on something that I have not considered in this way.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ər ə teɪk ɔn ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət aɪ hæv nɑt kənˈsɪdərd ɪn ðɪs weɪ.

MBir652KZPE.text: But then I also think a great lyric is honest.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: bət ðɛn aɪ ˈɔlsoʊ θɪŋk ə greɪt ˈlɪrɪk ɪz ˈɑnəst.

MBir652KZPE.text: When it comes to lyric writing I really love, I love a noun, I love a picture.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: wɪn ɪt kəmz tɪ ˈlɪrɪk ˈraɪtɪŋ aɪ ˈrɪli ləv, aɪ ləv ə naʊn, aɪ ləv ə ˈpɪkʧər.

MBir652KZPE.text: I feel like people really hold on to nouns and they can be really sticky. I like colors and textures and smells,
MBir652KZPE.ipa: aɪ fil laɪk ˈpipəl ˈrɪli hoʊld ɔn tɪ naʊnz ənd ðeɪ kən bi ˈrɪli ˈstɪki. aɪ laɪk ˈkələrz ənd ˈtɛksʧərz ənd smɛlz,

MBir652KZPE.text: and I like to feel like when you see the title, you can imagine the music video.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ənd aɪ laɪk tɪ fil laɪk wɪn ju si ðə ˈtaɪtəl, ju kən ˌɪˈmæʤən ðə mˈjuzɪk ˈvɪdioʊ.

MBir652KZPE.text: That being said, you can also write something that’s a little headier, that’s a little more just emotional or internal.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ðət biɪŋ sɛd, ju kən ˈɔlsoʊ raɪt ˈsəmθɪŋ that’s* ə ˈlɪtəl headier*, that’s* ə ˈlɪtəl mɔr ʤɪst ˈiˌmoʊʃənəl ər ˌɪnˈtərnəl.

MBir652KZPE.text: And that can also be super, super powerful. So there’s not only one way to do it.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ənd ðət kən ˈɔlsoʊ bi ˈsupər, ˈsupər ˈpaʊərfəl. soʊ there’s* nɑt ˈoʊnli wən weɪ tɪ du ɪt.

MBir652KZPE.text: To make the personal universal is so good.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: tɪ meɪk ðə ˈpərsɪnəl ˌjunəˈvərsəl ɪz soʊ gʊd.

MBir652KZPE.text: I would say my favorite example of that is a Stevie reference—
MBir652KZPE.ipa: aɪ wʊd seɪ maɪ ˈfeɪvərɪt ɪgˈzæmpəl əv ðət ɪz ə ˈstivi reference—*—

MBir652KZPE.text: “Living For The City.” He says, “to find a job is like a haystack needle.”
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ““living* fər ðə city.”*.” hi sɪz, ““to* faɪnd ə ʤɑb ɪz laɪk ə ˈheɪˌstæk needle.”*.”

MBir652KZPE.text: They took that colloquialism, crunched it up, flipped it over:
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ðeɪ tʊk ðət colloquialism*, krənʧt ɪt əp, flɪpt ɪt ˈoʊvər:

MBir652KZPE.text: “To find a job is like a haystack needle.”
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ““to* faɪnd ə ʤɑb ɪz laɪk ə ˈheɪˌstæk needle.”*.”

MBir652KZPE.text: But it makes perfect sense, and it makes universal sense, though it's a really quirky way of doing it.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: bət ɪt meɪks ˈpərˌfɪkt sɛns, ənd ɪt meɪks ˌjunəˈvərsəl sɛns, ðoʊ ɪts ə ˈrɪli kˈwərki weɪ əv duɪŋ ɪt.

MBir652KZPE.text: I do feel like I have to tell the truth. So if there’s something that feels really resonant,
MBir652KZPE.ipa: aɪ du fil laɪk aɪ hæv tɪ tɛl ðə truθ. soʊ ɪf there’s* ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət filz ˈrɪli ˈrɛzənənt,

MBir652KZPE.text: it’s really hard not to write about it.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: it’s* ˈrɪli hɑrd nɑt tɪ raɪt əˈbaʊt ɪt.

MBir652KZPE.text: I find it really cathartic and therapeutic to write about what it is that I’m going through,
MBir652KZPE.ipa: aɪ faɪnd ɪt ˈrɪli kəˈθɑrtɪk ənd ˌθɛrəˈpjutɪk tɪ raɪt əˈbaʊt wət ɪt ɪz ðət i’m* goʊɪŋ θru,

MBir652KZPE.text: because there’s always something I learn about my own inner experience by writing about it.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: bɪˈkəz there’s* ˈɔlˌweɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ aɪ lərn əˈbaʊt maɪ oʊn ˈɪnər ɪkˈspɪriəns baɪ ˈraɪtɪŋ əˈbaʊt ɪt.

MBir652KZPE.text: It depends on the genre, and it depends on the topic.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ɪt dɪˈpɛndz ɔn ðə ˈʒɑnrə, ənd ɪt dɪˈpɛndz ɔn ðə ˈtɑpɪk.

MBir652KZPE.text: Party time songs, for example, usually are going to be more rhythmic, and you want it to be something that everyone

MBir652KZPE.text: I think when it’s more introspective, I like to have more emotional detail.

MBir652KZPE.text: Olivia Rodrigo’s ”Driver’s License” is a great example of a heartbreak song that has really specific little things.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: oʊˈlɪviə rodrigo’s* ””driver’s* license”*” ɪz ə greɪt ɪgˈzæmpəl əv ə ˈhɑrtˌbreɪk sɔŋ ðət həz ˈrɪli spɪˈsɪfɪk ˈlɪtəl θɪŋz.

MBir652KZPE.text: The details of the things that you miss about someone or the things that remind you of someone.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ðə ˈditeɪlz əv ðə θɪŋz ðət ju mɪs əˈbaʊt ˈsəmˌwən ər ðə θɪŋz ðət riˈmaɪnd ju əv ˈsəmˌwən.

MBir652KZPE.text: When you’re talking about heartbreak or something more introspective, it's nice to kind of find the everyday things

MBir652KZPE.text: and think back to a time in your life when you were feeling that way.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ənd θɪŋk bæk tɪ ə taɪm ɪn jʊr laɪf wɪn ju wər ˈfilɪŋ ðət weɪ.

MBir652KZPE.text: I try to stay away from being overly cryptic.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: aɪ traɪ tɪ steɪ əˈweɪ frəm biɪŋ ˈoʊvərli ˈkrɪptɪk.

MBir652KZPE.text: You can be poetic and and vague even, but I think it has to be something that anyone could listen to
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ju kən bi poʊˈɛtɪk ənd ənd veɪg ˈivɪn, bət aɪ θɪŋk ɪt həz tɪ bi ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ˈɛniˌwən kʊd ˈlɪsən tɪ

MBir652KZPE.text: and be like, I know what that means. There’s not an original feeling under the sun, you know,
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ənd bi laɪk, aɪ noʊ wət ðət minz. there’s* nɑt ən ərˈɪʤənəl ˈfilɪŋ ˈəndər ðə sən, ju noʊ,

MBir652KZPE.text: if you felt it, there’s billions of other people who have felt that same exact thing.

MBir652KZPE.text: But what does become original is your way of expressing that feeling.

MBir652KZPE.text: How one person says I love you is very different than the next person.

MBir652KZPE.text: Experience, sincerity, imagination are what make lyrics great, as far as I’m concerned.

MBir652KZPE.text: I’ve got to be genuine in the emotion that I’m trying to communicate, if not transmit.

MBir652KZPE.text: I think I know I’m onto something when it strikes a chord in me emotionally,
MBir652KZPE.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk aɪ noʊ i’m* ˈɔntu ˈsəmθɪŋ wɪn ɪt straɪks ə kɔrd ɪn mi ˈiˌmoʊʃnəli,

MBir652KZPE.text: However, the ultimate test is the 24 hour test— because I’ve had that feeling, relisten the next day,

MBir652KZPE.text: There has been work that I’ve questioned— the entire time I’m questioning, and I’m writing, I’m playing going—back and forth.

MBir652KZPE.text: I put it away, I listen the next day, and I’m shocked at how connected I am to it.

MBir652KZPE.text: That cuts both ways— so it’s a matter of trusting the process.

MBir652KZPE.text: And know that no one is going to say what you have to say, the way you have to say it.

MBir652KZPE.text: And lean into that with everything you’ve got.

MBir652KZPE.text: When I’m evaluating a song, the first thing that I’m looking for is authenticity.

MBir652KZPE.text: and tried to write the most poetic thing.

MBir652KZPE.text: I think lyrics are great when they feel like natural poetry;

MBir652KZPE.text: when they feel like you didn’t craft them, they just were.

MBir652KZPE.text: I've always felt that life was as much being lived as being inscribed.

MBir652KZPE.text: That recognition means that the song’s always being written.

MBir652KZPE.text: It's just whether or not I'm paying attention. I try to be ready and worthy of whatever sound

MBir652KZPE.text: I would say that I have a lot of different processes to write a song.

MBir652KZPE.text: One of my go-to tools is I have a word bank, so I always have phrases and words that I'm thinking about.

MBir652KZPE.text: And then when we actually go to the studio and go to sit down and write a song,

MBir652KZPE.text: I have this bank of phrases and ideas and titles. My process is funny because I start with collecting ideas.
