MBir652KZPE.text: And it’s really fun to just make music and then see what happens.
MBir652KZPE.ipa: ənd it’s* ˈrɪli fən tɪ ʤɪst meɪk mˈjuzɪk ənd ðɛn si wət ˈhæpənz.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I couldn't possibly recognize that it was going to be as important a moment as it’s become.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ ˈkʊdənt ˈpɑsəbli ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪz ðət ɪt wɑz goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ɛz ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ə ˈmoʊmənt ɛz it’s* bɪˈkəm.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: But I knew that nothing else along my life’s journey had affected me in that way.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: bət aɪ nu ðət ˈnəθɪŋ ɛls əˈlɔŋ maɪ life’s* ˈʤərni hæd əˈfɛktɪd mi ɪn ðət weɪ.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: And that was it— I heard the lap steel guitar and there was no turning back.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ðət wɑz it—*— aɪ hərd ðə læp stil gɪˈtɑr ənd ðɛr wɑz noʊ ˈtərnɪŋ bæk.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: Hey, you! Yes, you. What’s that light inside of you? Is it a dream? A beat?
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: heɪ, ju! jɛs, ju. what’s* ðət laɪt ˌɪnˈsaɪd əv ju? ɪz ɪt ə drim? ə bit?

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: A beautiful sound? A heartbreaking song? Whatever it is, we’re here with working musicians
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ə ˈbjutəfəl saʊnd? ə ˈhɑrtˌbreɪkɪŋ sɔŋ? ˌwəˈtɛvər ɪt ɪz, we’re* hir wɪθ ˈwərkɪŋ mjuˈzɪʃənz

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: to help you grow and share that gift with this wild and wonderful world.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: tɪ hɛlp ju groʊ ənd ʃɛr ðət gɪft wɪθ ðɪs waɪld ənd ˈwəndərfəl wərld.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: The guitar. First of all, it's something that you can carry around with you. I really, really love that about it,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ðə gɪˈtɑr. fərst əv ɔl, ɪts ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ju kən ˈkɛri əraʊnd wɪθ ju. aɪ ˈrɪli, ˈrɪli ləv ðət əˈbaʊt ɪt,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: because I had a lot of feelings about not belonging
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: bɪˈkəz aɪ hæd ə lɔt əv ˈfilɪŋz əˈbaʊt nɑt bɪˈlɔŋɪŋ

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and trying to find my place in the world.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd traɪɪŋ tɪ faɪnd maɪ pleɪs ɪn ðə wərld.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: So having the guitar as this very travelable companion made a lot of sense to me.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: soʊ ˈhævɪŋ ðə gɪˈtɑr ɛz ðɪs ˈvɛri travelable* kəmˈpænjən meɪd ə lɔt əv sɛns tɪ mi.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: It accompanies the voice so well. So for me it was about the songwriting.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ɪt əˈkəmpəniz ðə vɔɪs soʊ wɛl. soʊ fər mi ɪt wɑz əˈbaʊt ðə ˈsɔŋˌraɪtɪŋ.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: It was about the singing and the guitar was just the vehicle to do that.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ɪt wɑz əˈbaʊt ðə ˈsɪŋɪŋ ənd ðə gɪˈtɑr wɑz ʤɪst ðə ˈviɪkəl tɪ du ðət.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: So, I don’t think the clarinet would have worked for me.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: soʊ, aɪ don’t* θɪŋk ðə ˌklɛrəˈnɛt wʊd hæv wərkt fər mi.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I started on, when everybody starts their instrument in the 5th grade, I played the clarinet.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ ˈstɑrtɪd ɔn, wɪn ˈɛvriˌbɑdi stɑrts ðɛr ˈɪnstrəmənt ɪn ðə 5th* greɪd, aɪ pleɪd ðə ˌklɛrəˈnɛt.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I loved playing music with other people, played the flute for a little while,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ ləvd pleɪɪŋ mˈjuzɪk wɪθ ˈəðər ˈpipəl, pleɪd ðə flut fər ə ˈlɪtəl waɪl,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and then eventually moved on to the alto saxophone. I think I just wanted to try them all.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ðɛn ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli muvd ɔn tɪ ðə ˈæltoʊ ˈsæksəˌfoʊn. aɪ θɪŋk aɪ ʤɪst ˈwɔntɪd tɪ traɪ ðɛm ɔl.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: But finally I wanted to be in a symphony orchestra. So I had heard of the oboe.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: bət ˈfaɪnəli aɪ ˈwɔntɪd tɪ bi ɪn ə ˈsɪmfəni ˈɔrkəstrə. soʊ aɪ hæd hərd əv ðə ˈoʊboʊ.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I didn't know anybody that played it. I went on YouTube and just watched video after video
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ ˈdɪdənt noʊ ˈɛnibədi ðət pleɪd ɪt. aɪ wɛnt ɔn ˈjuˌtub ənd ʤɪst wɔʧt ˈvɪdioʊ ˈæftər ˈvɪdioʊ

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and player after player of this interesting instrument. I mean, the rest is history— I loved it.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd pleɪər ˈæftər pleɪər əv ðɪs ˈɪntəˌrɛstɪŋ ˈɪnstrəmənt. aɪ min, ðə rɛst ɪz history—*— aɪ ləvd ɪt.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I am obviously an oboist and I've been playing for probably 14 years now.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ æm ˈɑbviəsli ən ˈoʊboʊəst ənd aɪv bɪn pleɪɪŋ fər ˈprɑbəˌbli 14 jɪrz naʊ.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I heard the lap steel played when I was very young,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ hərd ðə læp stil pleɪd wɪn aɪ wɑz ˈvɛri jəŋ,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and it was the sound that pulled me in in a way that was a mystery that to this day
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ɪt wɑz ðə saʊnd ðət pʊld mi ɪn ɪn ə weɪ ðət wɑz ə ˈmɪstəri ðət tɪ ðɪs deɪ

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I’ve been in pursuit of. It's always difficult to put words to what and why an instrument pulls you in,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: i’ve* bɪn ɪn pərˈsut əv. ɪts ˈɔlˌweɪz ˈdɪfəkəlt tɪ pʊt wərdz tɪ wət ənd waɪ ən ˈɪnstrəmənt pʊlz ju ɪn,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: that mystery is part of what makes it special to the person playing that instrument.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ðət ˈmɪstəri ɪz pɑrt əv wət meɪks ɪt ˈspɛʃəl tɪ ðə ˈpərsən pleɪɪŋ ðət ˈɪnstrəmənt.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I have very clear memories of music finding me.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ hæv ˈvɛri klɪr ˈmɛməriz əv mˈjuzɪk ˈfaɪndɪŋ mi.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: And I think as a as a child I was maybe I was struggling sometimes and I needed to escape.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd aɪ θɪŋk ɛz ə ɛz ə ʧaɪld aɪ wɑz ˈmeɪbi aɪ wɑz ˈstrəgəlɪŋ ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ənd aɪ ˈnidɪd tɪ ɪˈskeɪp.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: And I had this shelter, which was music.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd aɪ hæd ðɪs ˈʃɛltər, wɪʧ wɑz mˈjuzɪk.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: Over the years— so as a very young child, I played different instruments. I end up playing guitar and then bass,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ˈoʊvər ðə years—*— soʊ ɛz ə ˈvɛri jəŋ ʧaɪld, aɪ pleɪd ˈdɪfərənt ˈɪnstrəmənts. aɪ ɛnd əp pleɪɪŋ gɪˈtɑr ənd ðɛn bæs,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and for me it works really well with my personality.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd fər mi ɪt wərks ˈrɪli wɛl wɪθ maɪ ˌpərsəˈnælɪti.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: Bass is more about sort of a combination of rhythm and harmony. You have to love groove,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: bæs ɪz mɔr əˈbaʊt sɔrt əv ə ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv ˈrɪðəm ənd ˈhɑrməni. ju hæv tɪ ləv gruv,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and you have to sort of appreciate the power of repeating a bassline basically. I always loved meditating,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ju hæv tɪ sɔrt əv əˈpriʃiˌeɪt ðə paʊər əv rɪˈpitɪŋ ə bassline* ˈbeɪsɪkli. aɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz ləvd ˈmɛdəˌteɪtɪŋ,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and I think from early age I found playing bass somewhat like a meditation.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd aɪ θɪŋk frəm ˈərli eɪʤ aɪ faʊnd pleɪɪŋ bæs ˈsəmˈwət laɪk ə ˌmɛdəˈteɪʃən.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: First answer, real honest: I chose the piano because this girl I had a crush on in 3rd grade played piano.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: fərst ˈænsər, ril ˈɑnəst: aɪ ʧoʊz ðə piˈænə bɪˈkəz ðɪs gərl aɪ hæd ə krəʃ ɔn ɪn 3rd* greɪd pleɪd piˈænə.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: So I was like, oh shoot, let’s go! I also fell in love with the piano, so that worked out well.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: soʊ aɪ wɑz laɪk, oʊ ʃut, let’s* goʊ! aɪ ˈɔlsoʊ fɛl ɪn ləv wɪθ ðə piˈænə, soʊ ðət wərkt aʊt wɛl.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I was 8 or 9 when I started playing piano.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ wɑz 8 ər 9 wɪn aɪ ˈstɑrtɪd pleɪɪŋ piˈænə.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I was 13 when I started telling people I was going to be a songwriter for a living.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ wɑz 13 wɪn aɪ ˈstɑrtɪd ˈtɛlɪŋ ˈpipəl aɪ wɑz goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ə ˈsɔŋˌraɪtər fər ə ˈlɪvɪŋ.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: And it never really stopped from there.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ɪt ˈnɛvər ˈrɪli stɑpt frəm ðɛr.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I played alto saxophone for probably 10 years because I love jazz.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ pleɪd ˈæltoʊ ˈsæksəˌfoʊn fər ˈprɑbəˌbli 10 jɪrz bɪˈkəz aɪ ləv ʤæz.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: There's a part of me that had to realize you only have a certain amount of hours in a day,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ðɛrz ə pɑrt əv mi ðət hæd tɪ ˈriəˌlaɪz ju ˈoʊnli hæv ə ˈsərtən əˈmaʊnt əv aʊərz ɪn ə deɪ,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and if you're going to play something and play it to a professional level, you gotta triage, right?
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ɪf jʊr goʊɪŋ tɪ pleɪ ˈsəmθɪŋ ənd pleɪ ɪt tɪ ə prəˈfɛʃənəl ˈlɛvəl, ju ˈgɑtə traɪɪʤ, raɪt?

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: So saxophone was awesome and I love it, but it just wasn't doing it, you know what I mean?
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: soʊ ˈsæksəˌfoʊn wɑz ˈɔsəm ənd aɪ ləv ɪt, bət ɪt ʤɪst ˈwəzənt duɪŋ ɪt, ju noʊ wət aɪ min?

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I think the piano is a super power instrument because all of music theory is laid out before you
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk ðə piˈænə ɪz ə ˈsupər paʊər ˈɪnstrəmənt bɪˈkəz ɔl əv mˈjuzɪk ˈθɪri ɪz leɪd aʊt ˌbiˈfɔr ju

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: once you understand the piano. Now, I also play guitar. It’s not as linear.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: wəns ju ˌəndərˈstænd ðə piˈænə. naʊ, aɪ ˈɔlsoʊ pleɪ gɪˈtɑr. it’s* nɑt ɛz ˈlɪniər.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I can bring theory structure to the guitar because I understand it on piano first.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ kən brɪŋ ˈθɪri ˈstrəkʧər tɪ ðə gɪˈtɑr bɪˈkəz aɪ ˌəndərˈstænd ɪt ɔn piˈænə fərst.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I will give credit to one of my first piano teachers. Her name is Florence.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ wɪl gɪv ˈkrɛdɪt tɪ wən əv maɪ fərst piˈænə ˈtiʧərz. hər neɪm ɪz ˈflɔrəns.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I would bring her a song and I'd say, I want to learn this song.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ wʊd brɪŋ hər ə sɔŋ ənd aɪd seɪ, aɪ wɔnt tɪ lərn ðɪs sɔŋ.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: And she'd say, okay, first try it on your own with your ears,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ʃid seɪ, ˌoʊˈkeɪ, fərst traɪ ɪt ɔn jʊr oʊn wɪθ jʊr ɪrz,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and then come back and I’ll show you what you should adjust.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ðɛn kəm bæk ənd i’ll* ʃoʊ ju wət ju ʃʊd əˈʤəst.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: And so I fell in love with the sheer process of
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd soʊ aɪ fɛl ɪn ləv wɪθ ðə ʃɪr ˈprɔˌsɛs əv

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: realizing that all of these keys in front of me mean something,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ˈriəˌlaɪzɪŋ ðət ɔl əv ðiz kiz ɪn frənt əv mi min ˈsəmθɪŋ,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and if I sat here long enough, I could sound like people that I look up to. Music. I love it.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ɪf aɪ sæt hir lɔŋ ɪˈnəf, aɪ kʊd saʊnd laɪk ˈpipəl ðət aɪ lʊk əp tɪ. mˈjuzɪk. aɪ ləv ɪt.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: How did I get from a love of music to the drums? I think that there’s an element of patience
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: haʊ dɪd aɪ gɪt frəm ə ləv əv mˈjuzɪk tɪ ðə drəmz? aɪ θɪŋk ðət there’s* ən ˈɛləmənt əv ˈpeɪʃəns

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: that’s required in learning music. I think drumming didn't inflame my lack of patience.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: that’s* rikˈwaɪərd ɪn ˈlərnɪŋ mˈjuzɪk. aɪ θɪŋk ˈdrəmɪŋ ˈdɪdənt ɪnˈfleɪm maɪ læk əv ˈpeɪʃəns.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I had plenty of opportunities to learn piano and I felt disconnected from it. I had plenty of opportunities to learn guitar—
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ hæd ˈplɛnti əv ˌɑpərˈtunətiz tɪ lərn piˈænə ənd aɪ fɛlt ˌdɪskəˈnɛktɪd frəm ɪt. aɪ hæd ˈplɛnti əv ˌɑpərˈtunətiz tɪ lərn guitar—*—

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I felt totally intimidated by it. Drumming, it seemed to be initially simple enough
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ fɛlt ˈtoʊtəli ˌɪnˈtɪmɪˌdeɪtɪd baɪ ɪt. ˈdrəmɪŋ, ɪt simd tɪ bi ˌɪˈnɪʃəli ˈsɪmpəl ɪˈnəf

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and also loud enough
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ˈɔlsoʊ laʊd ɪˈnəf

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: to where I felt an immediate shock of power and excitement.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: tɪ wɛr aɪ fɛlt ən ˌɪˈmiˌdiət ʃɑk əv paʊər ənd ɪkˈsaɪtmənt.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: And I didn’t feel intimidated by the complexities. I felt a balance of gratification and satisfaction
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd aɪ didn’t* fil ˌɪnˈtɪmɪˌdeɪtɪd baɪ ðə kəmˈplɛksɪtiz. aɪ fɛlt ə ˈbæləns əv ˌgrætəfəˈkeɪʃən ənd ˌsætɪsˈfækʃən

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: that the fact that it gave me that so quickly, it then kind of circumvented my tendency to be impatient
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ðət ðə fækt ðət ɪt geɪv mi ðət soʊ kˈwɪkli, ɪt ðɛn kaɪnd əv ˌsərkəmˈvɛntɪd maɪ ˈtɛndənsi tɪ bi ˌɪmˈpeɪʃənt

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: and unwilling to learn. I think when you pick an instrument,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ənd ənˈwɪlɪŋ tɪ lərn. aɪ θɪŋk wɪn ju pɪk ən ˈɪnstrəmənt,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: you shouldn't skip the part where you think, what kind of person am I?
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ju ˈʃʊdənt skɪp ðə pɑrt wɛr ju θɪŋk, wət kaɪnd əv ˈpərsən æm aɪ?

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: Is this going to be a good avenue to express myself?
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ɪz ðɪs goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ə gʊd ˈævəˌnu tɪ ɪkˈsprɛs ˌmaɪˈsɛlf?

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: Or am I going to be just frustrated?
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ər æm aɪ goʊɪŋ tɪ bi ʤɪst ˈfrəˌstreɪtəd?

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: If you need constant attention and you want to be in the front of the band all the time, try the guitar.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ɪf ju nid ˈkɑnstənt əˈtɛnʃən ənd ju wɔnt tɪ bi ɪn ðə frənt əv ðə bænd ɔl ðə taɪm, traɪ ðə gɪˈtɑr.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I think when you want to make that choice,
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk wɪn ju wɔnt tɪ meɪk ðət ʧɔɪs,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: let's say you want to get into a band or you want to pick an instrument and there’s guitar and there’s bass
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: lɛts seɪ ju wɔnt tɪ gɪt ˈɪntu ə bænd ər ju wɔnt tɪ pɪk ən ˈɪnstrəmənt ənd there’s* gɪˈtɑr ənd there’s* bæs

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: In the beginning, I just wasn't really very passionate about it.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: Now, I pick up my guitar and I can’t imagine doing anything else.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: It’s an outlet for me. It’s like my form of self-expression. The same way I love art and photography,

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: music is just another way for me to express myself. There are guitars with nylon strings.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: mˈjuzɪk ɪz ʤɪst əˈnəðər weɪ fər mi tɪ ɪkˈsprɛs ˌmaɪˈsɛlf. ðɛr ər gɪˈtɑrz wɪθ ˈnaɪˌlɑn strɪŋz.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: There are guitars with steel strings, all different shapes and sizes. Electric, acoustic.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: ðɛr ər gɪˈtɑrz wɪθ stil strɪŋz, ɔl ˈdɪfərənt ʃeɪps ənd ˈsaɪzɪz. ɪˈlɛktrɪk, əˈkustɪk.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: Acoustic and electric guitar. They feel so different and you definitely do have to cater your playing style to it.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: əˈkustɪk ənd ɪˈlɛktrɪk gɪˈtɑr. ðeɪ fil soʊ ˈdɪfərənt ənd ju ˈdɛfənətli du hæv tɪ ˈkeɪtər jʊr pleɪɪŋ staɪl tɪ ɪt.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: And I actually find that playing acoustic transfers over to electric better than playing electric transfers over to an acoustic guitar.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: Yeah, it’s an exciting venture, the guitar.
ybftH3XSd0Q.ipa: jæ, it’s* ən ɪkˈsaɪtɪŋ ˈvɛnʧər, ðə gɪˈtɑr.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I mean, every time I pick it up, I’m learning something.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: I’m still learning new things every day and trying to improve myself.

ybftH3XSd0Q.text: It’s the connection. Music is a language. It's a way to connect.

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: Easy English Conversations.
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: easy* ˈɪŋlɪʃ conversations.

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: What did you do at the weekend?

kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: what* bʊk dɪd ju rɛd? aɪ rɛd ə dɪˈtɛktɪv story.

kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: click* ɔn ðə lɪŋk tɪ ˈdaʊnˌloʊd ə fri worksheet.

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: Now, back to the lesson. How was your weekend? What did you do?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: now*, bæk tɪ ðə ˈlɛsən. haʊ wɑz jʊr ˈwiˌkɪnd? wət dɪd ju do?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: I went to the cinema. What else did you do?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: i* wɛnt tɪ ðə ˈsɪnəmə. wət ɛls dɪd ju do?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: I had lunch with my friends. What did you eat?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: i* hæd lənʧ wɪθ maɪ frɛndz. wət dɪd ju eat?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: We ate pasta. What else did you do?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: we* eɪt ˈpɑstə. wət ɛls dɪd ju do?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: I made a cake.
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: aɪ meɪd ə cake.

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: How is Georgie feeling?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: how* ɪz ˈʤɔrʤi feeling?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: Find out in the next episode. Now, back to the lesson.
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: find* aʊt ɪn ðə nɛkst ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd. naʊ, bæk tɪ ðə lesson.

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: What did Tim do at the weekend?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: what* dɪd tɪm du æt ðə weekend?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: He went hiking. That's right. What did Sian do at the weekend?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: he* wɛnt ˈhaɪkɪŋ. ðæts raɪt. wət dɪd siɑn du æt ðə weekend?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: She made a chocolate cake. Correct. What did Buli do at the weekend?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: she* meɪd ə ˈʧɔklət keɪk. kərˈɛkt. wət dɪd buli* du æt ðə weekend?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: He went to the beach. That's right. What did Georgie do at the weekend?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: he* wɛnt tɪ ðə biʧ. ðæts raɪt. wət dɪd ˈʤɔrʤi du æt ðə weekend?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: She read a book. Correct! What did you do at the weekend?
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: she* rɛd ə bʊk. kərˈɛkt! wət dɪd ju du æt ðə ˈwiˌkɪnd?

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: Let us know in the comments. Click here to find out what we do every day.
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: lɛt ˈjuˈɛs noʊ ɪn ðə ˈkɑmɛnts. klɪk hir tɪ faɪnd aʊt wət wi du ˈɛvəri day.

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: And click here to subscribe to our channel! See you in the next video.
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: and* klɪk hir tɪ səbˈskraɪb tɪ ɑr ˈʧænəl! si ju ɪn ðə nɛkst video.

kTf0V4HyFtM.text: Bye!
kTf0V4HyFtM.ipa: bye!

rdEC52iCQNk.text: In the 1st century CE, Roman emperor Tiberius made an unprecedented decision:
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ɪn ðə 1st* ˈsɛnʧəri ce*, ˈroʊmən ˈɛmpərər tiberius* meɪd ən ənˈprɛsɪˌdɛntɪd dɪˈsɪʒən:

rdEC52iCQNk.text: banning kissing at all public events.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ˈbænɪŋ ˈkɪsɪŋ æt ɔl ˈpəblɪk ɪˈvɛnts.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: This move was aimed at thwarting a puzzling outbreak,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ðɪs muv wɑz eɪmd æt θˈwɔrtɪŋ ə ˈpəzəlɪŋ ˈaʊtˌbreɪk,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: which many historians believe was an epidemic of oral herpes.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: wɪʧ ˈmɛni hɪˈstɔriənz bɪˈliv wɑz ən ˌɛpɪˈdɛmɪk əv ˈɔrəl ˈhərpiz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Fast forward to today, and more than half the population is infected with herpes simplex virus,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: fæst ˈfɔrwərd tɪ təˈdeɪ, ənd mɔr ðən hæf ðə ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən ɪz ˌɪnˈfɛktɪd wɪθ ˈhərpiz ˈsɪmˌplɛks ˈvaɪrəs,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: though as many as 90% of those infected don't actually know they have it.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ðoʊ ɛz ˈmɛni ɛz 90 əv ðoʊz ˌɪnˈfɛktɪd doʊnt ˈæˌkʧuəli noʊ ðeɪ hæv ɪt.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: The term “herpesvirus” refers to a whole family of viruses.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ðə tərm ““herpesvirus”*” rɪˈfərz tɪ ə hoʊl ˈfæməli əv ˈvaɪrəsɪz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: But what we typically call the condition of herpes is caused by two viruses in particular:
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: bət wət wi ˈtɪpɪkəli kɔl ðə kənˈdɪʃən əv ˈhərpiz ɪz kɔzd baɪ tu ˈvaɪrəsɪz ɪn ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr:

rdEC52iCQNk.text: herpes simplex 1 and 2, or HSV-1 and HSV-2.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ˈhərpiz ˈsɪmˌplɛks 1 ənd 2 ər hsv-1* ənd hsv-2*

rdEC52iCQNk.text: While similar, they tend to infect different parts of the body.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: waɪl ˈsɪmələr, ðeɪ tɛnd tɪ ˌɪnˈfɛkt ˈdɪfərənt pɑrts əv ðə ˈbɑdi.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: HSV-1 is typically associated with oral herpes, which causes cold sores on the mouth.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: hsv-1* ɪz ˈtɪpɪkəli əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪtəd wɪθ ˈɔrəl ˈhərpiz, wɪʧ ˈkɔzɪz koʊld sɔrz ɔn ðə maʊθ.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Genital herpes can be caused by either virus, though it’s more often caused by HSV-2.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ˈʤɛnətəl ˈhərpiz kən bi kɔzd baɪ ˈiðər ˈvaɪrəs, ðoʊ it’s* mɔr ˈɔfən kɔzd baɪ hsv-2*

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Other body parts, such as fingers and eyes, can also be infected by either virus.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ˈəðər ˈbɑdi pɑrts, səʧ ɛz ˈfɪŋgərz ənd aɪz, kən ˈɔlsoʊ bi ˌɪnˈfɛktɪd baɪ ˈiðər ˈvaɪrəs.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Regardless of location or type,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: rəˈgɑrdləs əv loʊˈkeɪʃən ər taɪp,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: HSV enters the body through microscopic breaks in the skin or mucous membranes,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: hsv* ˈɛnərz ðə ˈbɑdi θru ˌmaɪkrəˈskɑpɪk breɪks ɪn ðə skɪn ər mˈjukəs ˈmɛmˌbreɪnz,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: like the mouth and genitals.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: laɪk ðə maʊθ ənd ˈʤɛnətəlz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Once inside, viral proteins hijack the cellular machinery to replicate and spread.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: wəns ˌɪnˈsaɪd, ˈvaɪrəl ˈproʊˌtinz ˈhaɪˌʤæk ðə ˈsɛljələr məˈʃinəri tɪ ˈrɛplɪˌkeɪt ənd sprɛd.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: For many people, this initial infection won’t result in any symptoms.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: fər ˈmɛni ˈpipəl, ðɪs ˌɪˈnɪʃəl ˌɪnˈfɛkʃən won’t* rɪˈzəlt ɪn ˈɛni ˈsɪmptəmz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: But for others, the virus causes enough damage that fluid-filled blisters develop and rupture,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: bət fər ˈəðərz, ðə ˈvaɪrəs ˈkɔzɪz ɪˈnəf ˈdæmɪʤ ðət fluid-filled* ˈblɪstərz dɪˈvɛləp ənd ˈrəpʧər,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: causing painful lesions.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ˈkɔzɪŋ ˈpeɪnfəl ˈliʒənz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: With more severe initial infections, the virus can enter the bloodstream
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: wɪθ mɔr səˈvɪr ˌɪˈnɪʃəl ˌɪnˈfɛkʃənz, ðə ˈvaɪrəs kən ˈɛnər ðə ˈblədˌstrim

rdEC52iCQNk.text: and lead to fevers, muscle aches, and headaches. This viral onslaught alerts the immune system,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ənd lɛd tɪ ˈfivərz, ˈməsəl eɪks, ənd ˈhɛˌdeɪks. ðɪs ˈvaɪrəl ˈɔnsˌlɔt əˈlərts ðə ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: which gets to work attempting to eliminate the HSV virus, just as it would when faced with other common viral pathogens.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: wɪʧ gɪts tɪ wərk əˈtɛmptɪŋ tɪ ɪˈlɪməˌneɪt ðə hsv* ˈvaɪrəs, ʤɪst ɛz ɪt wʊd wɪn feɪst wɪθ ˈəðər ˈkɑmən ˈvaɪrəl ˈpæθəʤənz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: But what makes HSV particularly difficult to clear is that some virus hides itself away,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: bət wət meɪks hsv* ˌpɑrˈtɪkjələrli ˈdɪfəkəlt tɪ klɪr ɪz ðət səm ˈvaɪrəs haɪdz ˌɪtˈsɛlf əˈweɪ,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: evading detection by the immune system.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ɪˈveɪdɪŋ dɪˈtɛkʃən baɪ ðə ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: You see, in addition to penetrating skin and mucosal cells,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ju si, ɪn əˈdɪʃən tɪ ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtɪŋ skɪn ənd mjuˈkoʊsəl sɛlz,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: HSV also infiltrates nearby nerve cells in the skin.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: hsv* ˈɔlsoʊ infiltrates* ˈnɪrˈbaɪ nərv sɛlz ɪn ðə skɪn.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: They then travel along the stretch of axons until they reach nerve clusters called ganglia.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ðeɪ ðɛn ˈtrævəl əˈlɔŋ ðə strɛʧ əv ˈækˌsɑnz ənˈtɪl ðeɪ riʧ nərv ˈkləstərz kɔld ˈgæŋgliə.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: HSV-1 thrives in the trigeminal ganglia, located in the skull,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: hsv-1* θraɪvz ɪn ðə trigeminal* ˈgæŋgliə, ˈloʊˌkeɪtəd ɪn ðə skəl,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: and HSV-2 prefers the sacral ganglia near the base of the spinal cord.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ənd hsv-2* prɪˈfərz ðə sacral* ˈgæŋgliə nɪr ðə beɪs əv ðə ˈspaɪnəl kɔrd.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Here, the virus goes dormant, turning off its replication machinery, which helps it avoid attacks from the immune system.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: hir, ðə ˈvaɪrəs goʊz ˈdɔrmənt, ˈtərnɪŋ ɔf ɪts ˌrɛplɪˈkeɪʃən məˈʃinəri, wɪʧ hɛlps ɪt əˈvɔɪd əˈtæks frəm ðə ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: It doesn't cause any damage or disease symptoms, but it doesn't disappear either.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ɪt ˈdəzənt kɔz ˈɛni ˈdæmɪʤ ər dɪˈziz ˈsɪmptəmz, bət ɪt ˈdəzənt ˌdɪsəˈpɪr ˈiðər.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: While living in the ganglia, the virus can reawaken periodically, often triggered by sickness or stress,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: waɪl ˈlɪvɪŋ ɪn ðə ˈgæŋgliə, ðə ˈvaɪrəs kən ˌriəˈweɪkən ˌpiriˈɑdɪkəli, ˈɔfən ˈtrɪgərd baɪ ˈsɪknəs ər strɛs,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: though the cause isn’t always clear.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ðoʊ ðə kɔz isn’t* ˈɔlˌweɪz klɪr.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: No matter the reason, the virus travels back down along the nerve,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: noʊ ˈmætər ðə ˈrizən, ðə ˈvaɪrəs ˈtrævəlz bæk daʊn əˈlɔŋ ðə nərv,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: which may cause itching or tingling, often the first symptom of recurrence.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: wɪʧ meɪ kɔz ˈɪʧɪŋ ər ˈtɪŋgəlɪŋ, ˈɔfən ðə fərst ˈsɪmptəm əv rɪˈkərəns.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Once back at the surface, the virus begins to replicate, potentially causing a new bout of symptoms.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: wəns bæk æt ðə ˈsərfəs, ðə ˈvaɪrəs bɪˈgɪnz tɪ ˈrɛplɪˌkeɪt, pəˈtɛnʃəli ˈkɔzɪŋ ə nu baʊt əv ˈsɪmptəmz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: The frequency of these recurrences can vary and happen as often as monthly,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ðə ˈfrikwənsi əv ðiz recurrences* kən ˈvɛri ənd ˈhæpən ɛz ˈɔfən ɛz ˈmənθli,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: or as infrequently as every few years.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ər ɛz ˌɪnˈfrikwəntli ɛz ˈɛvəri fju jɪrz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: And when the infection is active and shedding from the skin or mucosa,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ənd wɪn ðə ˌɪnˈfɛkʃən ɪz ˈæktɪv ənd ˈʃɛdɪŋ frəm ðə skɪn ər mjuˈkoʊsə,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: it's possible to spread to others via direct contact.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ɪts ˈpɑsəbəl tɪ sprɛd tɪ ˈəðərz ˈviə dɪˈrɛkt ˈkɑnˌtækt.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Yet since most infections are asymptomatic or only cause mild flare-ups,
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: jɛt sɪns moʊst ˌɪnˈfɛkʃənz ər ˌeɪˌsɪmptəˈmætɪk ər ˈoʊnli kɔz maɪld flare-ups*,

rdEC52iCQNk.text: many people don’t know they have HSV.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ˈmɛni ˈpipəl don’t* noʊ ðeɪ hæv hsv*.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: And herpes is surprisingly difficult to diagnose.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ənd ˈhərpiz ɪz səˈpraɪzɪŋli ˈdɪfəkəlt tɪ ˌdaɪəgˈnoʊs.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Blood tests aren’t normally recommended as they’re often inaccurate
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: bləd tɛsts aren’t* ˈnɔrməli ˌrɛkəˈmɛndɪd ɛz they’re* ˈɔfən ˌɪˈnækjərət

rdEC52iCQNk.text: and can’t reliably distinguish between a genital or oral HSV-1 infection.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ənd can’t* rɪˈlaɪəbli dɪˈstɪŋgwɪʃ bɪtˈwin ə ˈʤɛnətəl ər ˈɔrəl hsv-1* ˌɪnˈfɛkʃən.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: If you do have symptoms, the best way to get a clear diagnosis is to see a health care provider
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ɪf ju du hæv ˈsɪmptəmz, ðə bɛst weɪ tɪ gɪt ə klɪr ˌdaɪəgˈnoʊsəs ɪz tɪ si ə hɛlθ kɛr prəˈvaɪdər

rdEC52iCQNk.text: when a lesion is present.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: wɪn ə ˈliʒən ɪz ˈprɛzənt.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Aside from the pain caused by sores during a flare-up, for most people, herpes doesn’t lead to serious health problems.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: əˈsaɪd frəm ðə peɪn kɔzd baɪ sɔrz ˈdʊrɪŋ ə flare-up*, fər moʊst ˈpipəl, ˈhərpiz doesn’t* lɛd tɪ ˈsɪriəs hɛlθ ˈprɑbləmz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: And there are treatments to improve symptoms: antiviral medications work by mimicking a component of viral DNA
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ənd ðɛr ər ˈtritmənts tɪ ˌɪmˈpruv ˈsɪmptəmz: ˌæntiˈvaɪrəl ˌmɛdəˈkeɪʃənz wərk baɪ ˈmɪmɪkɪŋ ə kəmˈpoʊnənt əv ˈvaɪrəl ˈdiˌɛˈneɪ

rdEC52iCQNk.text: and inhibiting viral replication.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ənd ˌɪnˈhɪbətɪŋ ˈvaɪrəl ˌrɛplɪˈkeɪʃən.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: These medications can also reduce the risk of transmission, in the case of HSV-2, by about 50%.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ðiz ˌmɛdəˈkeɪʃənz kən ˈɔlsoʊ rɪˈdus ðə rɪsk əv trænzˈmɪʃən, ɪn ðə keɪs əv hsv-2* baɪ əˈbaʊt 50

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Over time, symptoms and shedding usually decline as the body produces immunity against the virus.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ˈoʊvər taɪm, ˈsɪmptəmz ənd ˈʃɛdɪŋ ˈjuʒəwəli dɪˈklaɪn ɛz ðə ˈbɑdi prəˈdusɪz ˌɪmˈjunɪti əˈgɛnst ðə ˈvaɪrəs.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Though, there is always some risk of flare-ups and transmission.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ðoʊ, ðɛr ɪz ˈɔlˌweɪz səm rɪsk əv flare-ups* ənd trænzˈmɪʃən.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: That’s why for decades, scientists have searched for treatments that can prevent or cure herpes.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: that’s* waɪ fər ˈdɛkeɪdz, ˈsaɪəntɪsts hæv sərʧt fər ˈtritmənts ðət kən prɪˈvɛnt ər kjʊr ˈhərpiz.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: But the virus's unique ability to establish silent infections and evade the immune system has made progress difficult.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: bət ðə ˈvaɪrəsɪz juˈnik əˈbɪləˌti tɪ ɪˈstæblɪʃ ˈsaɪlənt ˌɪnˈfɛkʃənz ənd ɪˈveɪd ðə ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm həz meɪd ˈprɑˌgrɛs ˈdɪfəkəlt.

rdEC52iCQNk.text: Still, there’s hope. Breakthroughs in emerging technologies like gene editing and novel vaccines
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: stɪl, there’s* hoʊp. ˈbreɪkθˌruz ɪn ˈimərʤɪŋ tɛkˈnɑləʤiz laɪk ʤin ˈɛdɪtɪŋ ənd ˈnɑvəl ˌvækˈsinz

rdEC52iCQNk.text: show promise in the fight against this stealthy virus.
rdEC52iCQNk.ipa: ʃoʊ ˈprɑməs ɪn ðə faɪt əˈgɛnst ðɪs ˈstɛlθi ˈvaɪrəs.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Ah, yes: the Viking Age. When medieval horn-helmeted Scandinavian men ravaged Europe,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ɑ, jɛs: ðə ˈvaɪkɪŋ eɪʤ. wɪn mɪˈdjivəl horn-helmeted* ˌskændɪˈneɪviən mɛn ˈrævɪʤd ˈjʊrəp,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: scribbling mysterious runes and toasting their victories in goblets forged from enemy skulls
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈskrɪbəlɪŋ mɪˈstɪriəs runz ənd ˈtoʊstɪŋ ðɛr ˈvɪktəriz ɪn goblets* fɔrʤd frəm ˈɛnəmi skəlz

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: before bidding farewell in fiery funerals.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˌbiˈfɔr ˈbɪdɪŋ ˌfɛrˈwɛl ɪn ˈfaɪəri fˈjunərəlz.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Except, that’s not quite how it went...
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ɪkˈsɛpt, that’s* nɑt kwaɪt haʊ ɪt wɛnt...

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: It’s unclear where the word “Viking” came from, but in Old Norse it apparently referred to an act of maritime raiding.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: it’s* ənˈklɪr wɛr ðə wərd ““viking”*” keɪm frəm, bət ɪn oʊld nɔrs ɪt əˈpɛrəntli rɪˈfərd tɪ ən ækt əv ˈmærəˌtaɪm ˈreɪdɪŋ.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Historians long pinned the start of the Viking Age to a brutal ambush on an English monastery in 793 CE,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: hɪˈstɔriənz lɔŋ pɪnd ðə stɑrt əv ðə ˈvaɪkɪŋ eɪʤ tɪ ə ˈbrutəl ˈæmˌbʊʃ ɔn ən ˈɪŋlɪʃ ˈmɑnəˌstɛri ɪn 793 ce*,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: which the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle described as an onslaught of lightning,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: wɪʧ ðə anglo-saxon* ˈkrɑnɪkəl dɪˈskraɪbd ɛz ən ˈɔnsˌlɔt əv ˈlaɪtnɪŋ,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: “fiery dragons,” and “heathen men.”
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ““fiery* dragons,”*,” ənd ““heathen* men.”*.”

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: The more recent discovery of Scandinavian artifacts at a site in Estonia suggests that Viking activity began decades earlier.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ðə mɔr ˈrisənt ˌdɪˈskəvri əv ˌskændɪˈneɪviən ˈɑrtəˌfækts æt ə saɪt ɪn ɛˈstoʊniə səˈʤɛsts ðət ˈvaɪkɪŋ ækˈtɪvɪti bɪˈgæn ˈdɛkeɪdz ˈərliər.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: But whatever the precise 8th-century start, it persisted for the next 300 years.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: bət ˌwəˈtɛvər ðə prɪˈsaɪs 8th-century* stɑrt, ɪt pərˈsɪstɪd fər ðə nɛkst 300 jɪrz.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: We don’t know exactly what prompted all this Viking-ing. But volcanic activity in the mid-500s triggered a mini Ice Age
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: wi don’t* noʊ ɪgˈzæktli wət ˈprɑmptɪd ɔl ðɪs viking-ing*. bət vɑlˈkænɪk ækˈtɪvɪti ɪn ðə mid-500s* ˈtrɪgərd ə ˈmɪni aɪs eɪʤ

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: that destabilized Scandinavia.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ðət dɪˈsteɪbəˌlaɪzd ˌskændɪˈneɪviə.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Its society grew increasingly militarized, and like many warrior-based peoples,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ɪts soʊˈsaɪɪti gru ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋgli ˈmɪlətərˌaɪzd, ənd laɪk ˈmɛni warrior-based* ˈpipəlz,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: local chieftains legitimized their rule through gift giving.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈloʊkəl ˈʧiftənz lɪˈʤɪtəˌmaɪzd ðɛr rul θru gɪft ˈgɪvɪŋ.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: So, wealth-acquiring expeditions helped maintain authority.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: soʊ, wealth-acquiring* ˌɛkspəˈdɪʃənz hɛlpt meɪnˈteɪn əˈθɔrəti.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Meanwhile, new seafaring technology enabled ships to navigate both shallow rivers and open oceans.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈminˌwaɪl, nu seafaring* tɛkˈnɑləʤi ɪˈneɪbəld ʃɪps tɪ ˈnævəˌgeɪt boʊθ ˈʃæloʊ ˈrɪvərz ənd ˈoʊpən ˈoʊʃənz.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Most Scandinavians didn't participate directly,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: moʊst ˌskændɪˈneɪviənz ˈdɪdənt pɑrˈtɪsəˌpeɪt dɪˈrɛkli,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: but Viking raids could offer young men the opportunity to amass riches to afford land and marriage.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: bət ˈvaɪkɪŋ reɪdz kʊd ˈɔfər jəŋ mɛn ðə ˌɑpərˈtunəti tɪ əˈmæs ˈrɪʧɪz tɪ əˈfɔrd lænd ənd ˈmɛrɪʤ.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: These raids were unquestionably violent.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ðiz reɪdz wər ənkˈwɛsʧənəbli ˈvaɪələnt.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: But Vikings didn’t use their victims’ skulls as goblets,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: bət ˈvaɪkɪŋz didn’t* juz ðɛr victims’*’ skəlz ɛz goblets*,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: though this has been recorded in history— looking at you, Shah Ismail.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ðoʊ ðɪs həz bɪn rɪˈkɔrdɪd ɪn history—*— ˈlʊkɪŋ æt ju, ʃɑ ˈɪsmeɪl.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: This myth likely arose when a 17th century Danish scholar called Ole Worm
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ðɪs mɪθ ˈlaɪkli əroʊz wɪn ə 17th* ˈsɛnʧəri ˈdeɪnɪʃ ˈskɑlər kɔld ˌoʊˈleɪ wərm

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: mistranslated a reference to animal horns.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: mistranslated* ə ˈrɛfərəns tɪ ˈænəməl hɔrnz.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Beyond killing, Viking invasions also involved mass capture.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: bɪɔnd ˈkɪlɪŋ, ˈvaɪkɪŋ ˌɪnˈveɪʒənz ˈɔlsoʊ ˌɪnˈvɑlvd mæs ˈkæpʧər.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Vikings enslaved and sold people into human trafficking networks, or sent them to labor in Scandinavia and its colonies,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈvaɪkɪŋz ɛnsˈleɪvd ənd soʊld ˈpipəl ˈɪntu ˈjumən ˈtræfɪkɪŋ ˈnɛtˌwərks, ər sɛnt ðɛm tɪ ˈleɪbər ɪn ˌskændɪˈneɪviə ənd ɪts ˈkɑləniz,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: as was the case for many Irish captives in Iceland.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ɛz wɑz ðə keɪs fər ˈmɛni ˈaɪrɪʃ ˈkæptɪvz ɪn ˈaɪslənd.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Over time, small-scale attacks on vulnerable coastal communities evolved into sieges on cities;
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈoʊvər taɪm, small-scale* əˈtæks ɔn ˈvəlnərəbəl ˈkoʊstəl kəmˈjunɪtiz ɪˈvɑlvd ˈɪntu ˈsiʤɪz ɔn ˈsɪtiz;

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: temporary camps grew into permanent communities; and trade networks spread even farther.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈtɛmpərˌɛri kæmps gru ˈɪntu ˈpərmɑˌnɛnt kəmˈjunɪtiz; ənd treɪd ˈnɛtˌwərks sprɛd ˈivɪn ˈfɑrðər.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Viking-era Scandinavian chieftains may have drunk German wine from Egyptian glass,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: viking-era* ˌskændɪˈneɪviən ˈʧiftənz meɪ hæv drəŋk ˈʤərmən waɪn frəm ɪˈʤɪpʃən glæs,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: while clad in Chinese silks and Indian gemstones.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: waɪl klæd ɪn ʧaɪˈniz sɪlks ənd ˈɪndiən ˈʤɛmˌstoʊnz.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: But they did not wear horned helmets.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: bət ðeɪ dɪd nɑt wɛr hɔrnd ˈhɛlməts.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: This image was popularized in 19th-century stage productions, specifically Richard Wagner’s “The Ring Cycle,”
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ðɪs ˈɪmɪʤ wɑz ˈpɑpjələrˌaɪzd ɪn 19th-century* steɪʤ pərˈdəkʃənz, spəˈsɪfɪkli ˈrɪʧərd wagner’s* ““the* rɪŋ cycle,”*,”

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: an epic, mythologized account of Germanic history.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ən ˈɛpɪk, mythologized* əˈkaʊnt əv ʤərˈmænɪk ˈhɪstəri.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: While archaeologists have found Scandinavian horned helmets, they preceded Vikings by 2,000 years,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: waɪl ˌɑrkiˈɑləʤɪsts hæv faʊnd ˌskændɪˈneɪviən hɔrnd ˈhɛlməts, ðeɪ prɪˈsidɪd ˈvaɪkɪŋz baɪ 2,000* jɪrz,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: and were likely used in rituals, not battle.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ənd wər ˈlaɪkli juzd ɪn ˈrɪʧuəlz, nɑt ˈbætəl.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Instead, high-status Vikings might have worn iron helmets with brow bridges,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˌɪnˈstɛd, high-status* ˈvaɪkɪŋz maɪt hæv wɔrn aɪərn ˈhɛlməts wɪθ braʊ ˈbrɪʤɪz,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: while others donned leather caps.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: waɪl ˈəðərz dɑnd ˈlɛðər kæps.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: And we aren’t sure these warriors were always men.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ənd wi aren’t* ʃʊr ðiz ˈwɔrjərz wər ˈɔlˌweɪz mɛn.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: In 1878, researchers excavated gravesite remains since interpreted as belonging to a high-status warrior,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ɪn 1878 ˈrisərʧərz ˈɛkskəˌveɪtɪd ˈgreɪvˌsaɪt rɪˈmeɪnz sɪns ˌɪnˈtərprətəd ɛz bɪˈlɔŋɪŋ tɪ ə high-status* ˈwɔrjər,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: which a 2017 analysis found to be female.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: wɪʧ ə 2017 æˈnælɪsɪs faʊnd tɪ bi ˈfiˌmeɪl.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Viking society was undeniably patriarchal, but discoveries like this hint at our incomplete understanding
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈvaɪkɪŋ soʊˈsaɪɪti wɑz ˌəndɪˈnaɪəbli ˌpeɪtriˈɑrkəl, bət dɪˈskəvəriz laɪk ðɪs hɪnt æt ɑr ˌɪnkəmˈplit ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: of the social complexities at play.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: əv ðə ˈsoʊʃəl kəmˈplɛksɪtiz æt pleɪ.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: As for funerary customs, 10th-century Arab traveler Ibn Fadlan recorded witnessing
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ɛz fər fˈjunərˌɛri ˈkəstəmz, 10th-century* ˈærəb ˈtrævələr ˈɪbən fadlan* rɪˈkɔrdɪd ˈwɪtnəsɪŋ

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: a burning boat funeral along the Volga—
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ə ˈbərnɪŋ boʊt fˈjunərəl əˈlɔŋ ðə volga—*—

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: however, it’s unclear if these were, in fact, Vikings, and it occurred on land—
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, it’s* ənˈklɪr ɪf ðiz wər, ɪn fækt, ˈvaɪkɪŋz, ənd ɪt əˈkərd ɔn land—*—

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: without flaming arrows and with sexual violence and human sacrifice.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: wɪˈθaʊt ˈfleɪmɪŋ ˈɛroʊz ənd wɪθ ˈsɛkʃuəl ˈvaɪələns ənd ˈjumən ˈsækrəˌfaɪs.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Popular Viking funeral depictions are likely modern inventions.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈpɑpjələr ˈvaɪkɪŋ fˈjunərəl dɪˈpɪkʃənz ər ˈlaɪkli ˈmɑdərn ˌɪnˈvɛnʃənz.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Vikings didn't appear to have one funerary practice, and both buried and cremated their dead.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈvaɪkɪŋz ˈdɪdənt əˈpɪr tɪ hæv wən fˈjunərˌɛri ˈpræktɪs, ənd boʊθ ˈbɛrid ənd ˈkrimeɪtɪd ðɛr dɛd.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Regarding runes, Germanic peoples began writing with them around the 2nd century.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: rɪˈgɑrdɪŋ runz, ʤərˈmænɪk ˈpipəlz bɪˈgæn ˈraɪtɪŋ wɪθ ðɛm əraʊnd ðə 2nd* ˈsɛnʧəri.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: They came to be used to bridge the physical and spiritual worlds, and for more mundane matters.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ðeɪ keɪm tɪ bi juzd tɪ brɪʤ ðə ˈfɪzɪkəl ənd ˈspɪrɪˌʧuəl wərldz, ənd fər mɔr mənˈdeɪn ˈmætərz.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: While some reference past events, Viking Age runes typically didn't record detailed narratives.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: waɪl səm ˈrɛfərəns pæst ɪˈvɛnts, ˈvaɪkɪŋ eɪʤ runz ˈtɪpɪkəli ˈdɪdənt ˈrɛkərd dɪˈteɪld ˈnɛrətɪvz.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Old Norse only became a literary language after Christianized Icelandic scholars adopted Latin script
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: oʊld nɔrs ˈoʊnli bɪˈkeɪm ə ˈlɪtərˌɛri ˈlæŋgwɪʤ ˈæftər ˈkrɪsʧəˌnaɪzd aɪsˈlændɪk ˈskɑlərz əˈdɑptəd ˈlætən skrɪpt

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: around the 12th century.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: əraʊnd ðə 12th* ˈsɛnʧəri.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: So, actual Viking Age accounts generally originate from traumatized victims and cultural outsiders,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: soʊ, ˈækʧəwəl ˈvaɪkɪŋ eɪʤ əˈkaʊnts ˈʤɛnərəli ərˈɪʤəˌneɪt frəm ˈtrɔməˌtaɪzd ˈvɪktɪmz ənd ˈkəlʧərəl aʊtˈsaɪdərz,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: restricting our insights into how Vikings understood themselves and their world.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: riˈstrɪktɪŋ ɑr ˈɪnˌsaɪts ˈɪntu haʊ ˈvaɪkɪŋz ˌəndərˈstʊd ðɛmˈsɛlvz ənd ðɛr wərld.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: The Viking Age is traditionally said to have ended in 1066,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ðə ˈvaɪkɪŋ eɪʤ ɪz trəˈdɪʃənəli sɛd tɪ hæv ˈɛndɪd ɪn 1066

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: with a battle won by English troops, though some incursions continued.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: wɪθ ə ˈbætəl wən baɪ ˈɪŋlɪʃ trups, ðoʊ səm ˌɪnˈkərʒənz kənˈtɪnjud.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: In Scandinavia, power shifted towards centralized Christian monarchies
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ɪn ˌskændɪˈneɪviə, paʊər ˈʃɪftɪd təˈwɔrdz ˈsɛntrəˌlaɪzd ˈkrɪsʧɪn ˈmɑnɑrkiz

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: and state-sponsored conquest, like Crusading.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ənd state-sponsored* ˈkɑŋkwɛst, laɪk kruˈseɪdɪŋ.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: Meanwhile, the Scandinavian diaspora flourished in Iceland and integrated into other European societies.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈminˌwaɪl, ðə ˌskændɪˈneɪviən daɪˈæspərə flərɪʃt ɪn ˈaɪslənd ənd ˈɪnəˌgreɪtɪd ˈɪntu ˈəðər ˌjʊrəˈpiən səˈsaɪɪtiz.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: For centuries, the term “Viking” went unused, until the 1800s, when nationalism swept Europe,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: fər ˈsɛnʧəriz, ðə tərm ““viking”*” wɛnt ənˈjuzd, ənˈtɪl ðə 1800s*, wɪn ˈnæʃənəˌlɪzəm swɛpt ˈjʊrəp,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: and people began invoking it to gesture at a romanticized, shared Germanic past.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ənd ˈpipəl bɪˈgæn ˌɪnˈvoʊkɪŋ ɪt tɪ ˈʤɛsʧər æt ə roʊˈmæntəˌsaɪzd, ʃɛrd ʤərˈmænɪk pæst.

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: This mythos persists today—
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ðɪs mythos* pərˈsɪsts today—*—

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: even as new discoveries reveal just how little we know of the Viking world,
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ˈivɪn ɛz nu dɪˈskəvəriz rɪˈvil ʤɪst haʊ ˈlɪtəl wi noʊ əv ðə ˈvaɪkɪŋ wərld,

2gPzWP3dcOQ.text: and shapes that once seemed certain recede into the thick mist of time.
2gPzWP3dcOQ.ipa: ənd ʃeɪps ðət wəns simd ˈsərtən rɪˈsid ˈɪntu ðə θɪk mɪst əv taɪm.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: In 1856, quarriers working a cave in Germany’s Neander Valley
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɪn 1856 quarriers* ˈwərkɪŋ ə keɪv ɪn germany’s* neander* ˈvæli

EtiC0DVewa4.text: discovered several mysterious fossils.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: dɪˈskəvərd ˈsɛvərəl mɪˈstɪriəs ˈfɑsəlz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: The remains changed hands until being identified as the skullcap and femur bones
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ðə rɪˈmeɪnz ʧeɪnʤd hænz ənˈtɪl biɪŋ aɪˈdɛntəˌfaɪd ɛz ðə ˈskəlˌkæp ənd ˈfimər boʊnz

EtiC0DVewa4.text: of something ancient and human, but not quite us.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: əv ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈeɪnʧənt ənd ˈjumən, bət nɑt kwaɪt ˈjuˈɛs.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: It soon became clear they belonged to an extinct human species— the first ever known to science:
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɪt sun bɪˈkeɪm klɪr ðeɪ bɪˈlɔŋd tɪ ən ɪkˈstɪŋkt ˈjumən species—*— ðə fərst ˈɛvər noʊn tɪ saɪəns:

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Homo neanderthalensis, or simply, Neanderthals.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈhoʊmoʊ neanderthalensis*, ər ˈsɪmpli, niˈændərˌθɔlz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Not long before the discovery, many believed the world was only about 6,000 years old.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: nɑt lɔŋ ˌbiˈfɔr ðə ˌdɪˈskəvri, ˈmɛni bɪˈlivd ðə wərld wɑz ˈoʊnli əˈbaʊt 6,000* jɪrz oʊld.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: However, by the mid-1800s, naturalists were more comfortable with geological timelines
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, baɪ ðə mid-1800s*, ˈnæʧərəlɪsts wər mɔr ˈkəmfərtəbəl wɪθ ˌʤiəˈlɑʤɪkəl ˈtaɪmlaɪnz

EtiC0DVewa4.text: and evolutionary theory.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd ˌɛvəˈluʃəˌnɛri ˈθɪri.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: The idea that ancient remains could belong to another human species
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ðə aɪˈdiə ðət ˈeɪnʧənt rɪˈmeɪnz kʊd bɪˈlɔŋ tɪ əˈnəðər ˈjumən ˈspiʃiz

EtiC0DVewa4.text: was finally becoming conceivable.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: wɑz ˈfaɪnəli bɪˈkəmɪŋ kənˈsivəbəl.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: But misconceptions persisted, and much “Neander slander” ensued.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: bət mɪskənˈsɛpʃənz pərˈsɪstɪd, ənd məʧ ““neander* slander”*” ɪnˈsud.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Early on, Neanderthals were depicted as dim-witted oafs
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈərli ɔn, niˈændərˌθɔlz wər dɪˈpɪktɪd ɛz dim-witted* oafs*

EtiC0DVewa4.text: who died out because of their inferiority to modern humans, aka Homo sapiens.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: hu daɪd aʊt bɪˈkəz əv ðɛr ˌɪnfɪriˈɔrɪˌti tɪ ˈmɑdərn ˈjumənz, ˈɑkə ˈhoʊmoʊ ˈseɪpiənz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: One popular illustration suggested that, in addition to being hairy and club-carrying,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: wən ˈpɑpjələr ˌɪləˈstreɪʃən səˈʤɛstɪd ðət, ɪn əˈdɪʃən tɪ biɪŋ ˈhɛri ənd club-carrying*,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Neanderthals stooped and had ape-like opposable toes,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: niˈændərˌθɔlz stupt ənd hæd ape-like* opposable* toʊz,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: even though this wasn’t reflected in skeletal findings.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈivɪn ðoʊ ðɪs wasn’t* rɪˈflɛktɪd ɪn ˈskɛlətəl ˈfaɪndɪŋz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Over the next century, however, archaeologists discovered more hominin species,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈoʊvər ðə nɛkst ˈsɛnʧəri, ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ˌɑrkiˈɑləʤɪsts dɪˈskəvərd mɔr hominin* ˈspiʃiz,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: as well as the remains of over 300 Neanderthal individuals.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɛz wɛl ɛz ðə rɪˈmeɪnz əv ˈoʊvər 300 niˈændərˌθɔl ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəlz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Compared to modern humans, Neanderthals had larger, deeper-set eyes;
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: kəmˈpɛrd tɪ ˈmɑdərn ˈjumənz, niˈændərˌθɔlz hæd ˈlɑrʤər, deeper-set* aɪz;

EtiC0DVewa4.text: were shorter and more muscular,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: wər ˈʃɔrtər ənd mɔr ˈməskjələr,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: had higher caloric needs with slightly bigger bodies, including their hearts and brains.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: hæd haɪər kæˈlɔrɪk nidz wɪθ sˈlaɪtli ˈbɪgər ˈbɑdiz, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ðɛr hɑrts ənd breɪnz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: For over 350,000 years, they lived across Europe and Western Asia.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: fər ˈoʊvər 350,000* jɪrz, ðeɪ lɪvd əˈkrɔs ˈjʊrəp ənd ˈwɛstərn ˈeɪʒə.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: For at least their final 10,000 years, they spatially overlapped with Homo sapiens.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: fər æt list ðɛr ˈfaɪnəl 10,000* jɪrz, ðeɪ spatially* ˈoʊvərˌlæpt wɪθ ˈhoʊmoʊ ˈseɪpiənz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: And archaeological finds have suggested that they led much more complex lives
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd ˌɑrkiəˈlɑʤɪkəl faɪndz hæv səˈʤɛstɪd ðət ðeɪ lɛd məʧ mɔr ˈkɑmplɛks lɪvz

EtiC0DVewa4.text: than initially suspected.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ðən ˌɪˈnɪʃəli səˈspɛktɪd.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: In the 1950s, scientists unearthed the remains of a Neanderthal
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɪn ðə 1950s*, ˈsaɪəntɪsts əˈnərθt ðə rɪˈmeɪnz əv ə niˈændərˌθɔl

EtiC0DVewa4.text: in a cave in northern Iraq who had a severed right arm, injured leg,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɪn ə keɪv ɪn ˈnɔrðərn ˌɪˈrɑk hu hæd ə ˈsɛvərd raɪt ɑrm, ˈɪnʤərd lɛg,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: and was likely partially blind and deaf. Yet, he survived long after these injuries,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd wɑz ˈlaɪkli ˈpɑrʃəli blaɪnd ənd dɛf. jɛt, hi sərˈvaɪvd lɔŋ ˈæftər ðiz ˈɪnʤəriz,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: suggesting his community provided him extensive, daily support.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: səˈʤɛstɪŋ hɪz kəmˈjunɪti prəˈvaɪdɪd ɪm ɪkˈstɛnsɪv, ˈdeɪli səˈpɔrt.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: And another skeleton from a French cave belonged to an older Neanderthal who probably had trouble walking
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd əˈnəðər ˈskɛlətən frəm ə frɛnʧ keɪv bɪˈlɔŋd tɪ ən ˈoʊldər niˈændərˌθɔl hu ˈprɑbəˌbli hæd ˈtrəbəl ˈwɔkɪŋ

EtiC0DVewa4.text: and had lost so many teeth he may have needed his food ground up.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd hæd lɔst soʊ ˈmɛni tiθ hi meɪ hæv ˈnidɪd hɪz fud graʊnd əp.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Both excavation sites also showed signs of burial,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: boʊθ ˌɛkskəˈveɪʃən saɪts ˈɔlsoʊ ʃoʊd saɪnz əv ˈbɛriəl,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: all suggesting that Neanderthals went beyond immediate, practical concerns for survival
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɔl səˈʤɛstɪŋ ðət niˈændərˌθɔlz wɛnt bɪɔnd ˌɪˈmiˌdiət, ˈpræktɪkəl kənˈsərnz fər sərˈvaɪvəl

EtiC0DVewa4.text: and cared for their ailing and dead.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd kɛrd fər ðɛr ˈeɪlɪŋ ənd dɛd.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Teeth analyses also indicate that Neanderthals
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: tiθ æˈnælɪˌsiz ˈɔlsoʊ ˈɪndəˌkeɪt ðət niˈændərˌθɔlz

EtiC0DVewa4.text: might have used anti-inflammatory and antibiotic plants medicinally.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: maɪt hæv juzd anti-inflammatory* ənd ˌæntibaɪˈɑtɪk plænts məˈdɪsənəli.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: For a while, researchers assumed that Neanderthals hunted big game
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: fər ə waɪl, ˈrisərʧərz əˈsumd ðət niˈændərˌθɔlz ˈhənɪd bɪg geɪm

EtiC0DVewa4.text: with unsophisticated, brute force ambush attacks.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: wɪθ ˌənsəˈfɪstɪˌkeɪtɪd, brut fɔrs ˈæmˌbʊʃ əˈtæks.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: But cut marks on rabbit bones and tools with apparent traces of scales and feathers
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: bət kət mɑrks ɔn ˈræbɪt boʊnz ənd tulz wɪθ əˈpɛrənt ˈtreɪsɪz əv skeɪlz ənd ˈfɛðərz

EtiC0DVewa4.text: suggest Neanderthals were capable of the skill required to capture small game,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: səˈʤɛst niˈændərˌθɔlz wər ˈkeɪpəbəl əv ðə skɪl rikˈwaɪərd tɪ ˈkæpʧər smɔl geɪm,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: perhaps utilizing snares and fast-moving projectiles.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: pərˈhæps ˈjutəˌlaɪzɪŋ snɛrz ənd fast-moving* prɑˈʤɛktəlz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: In fact, we know they fashioned and wielded many types of tools,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɪn fækt, wi noʊ ðeɪ ˈfæʃənd ənd ˈwildɪd ˈmɛni taɪps əv tulz,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: sometimes affixing them with glue they made from heated birch bark.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈsəmˌtaɪmz əˈfɪksɪŋ ðɛm wɪθ glu ðeɪ meɪd frəm ˈhitɪd bərʧ bɑrk.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: They also shaped hardwood digging sticks with fire
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ðeɪ ˈɔlsoʊ ʃeɪpt ˈhɑrdˌwʊd ˈdɪgɪŋ stɪks wɪθ faɪər

EtiC0DVewa4.text: and created reinforced, 3-ply string using bark fibers.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd kriˈeɪtɪd ˌriɪnˈfɔrst, 3-ply* strɪŋ ˈjuzɪŋ bɑrk ˈfaɪbərz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Other findings have also raised questions about whether Neanderthals thought symbolically,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈəðər ˈfaɪndɪŋz hæv ˈɔlsoʊ reɪzd kˈwɛsʧənz əˈbaʊt ˈwɛðər niˈændərˌθɔlz θɔt sɪmˈbɑlɪkəli,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: seeing beyond direct utility, into realms like art and language,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: siɪŋ bɪɔnd dɪˈrɛkt juˈtɪləti, ˈɪntu rɛlmz laɪk ɑrt ənd ˈlæŋgwɪʤ,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: long considered a hallmark of Homo sapiens.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: lɔŋ kənˈsɪdərd ə ˈhɑlˌmɑrk əv ˈhoʊmoʊ ˈseɪpiənz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Potentially for personal adornment, there’s evidence of Neanderthals selectively removing bird’s flight feathers,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: pəˈtɛnʃəli fər ˈpərsɪnəl əˈdɔrnmənt, there’s* ˈɛvədəns əv niˈændərˌθɔlz səˈlɛktɪvli riˈmuvɪŋ bird’s* flaɪt ˈfɛðərz,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: painting and perforating shells,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈpeɪnɪŋ ənd ˈpərfərˌeɪtɪŋ ʃɛlz,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: and wrapping eagle talons in what seems to have been another kind of animal tissue.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd ˈræpɪŋ ˈigəl ˈtælənz ɪn wət simz tɪ hæv bɪn əˈnəðər kaɪnd əv ˈænəməl ˈtɪʃu.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: They likewise made markings on a giant elk’s toe bone
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ðeɪ ˈlaɪkˌwaɪz meɪd ˈmɑrkɪŋz ɔn ə ʤaɪənt elk’s* toʊ boʊn

EtiC0DVewa4.text: and created three cave paintings in Spain,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd kriˈeɪtɪd θri keɪv ˈpeɪnɪŋz ɪn speɪn,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: if the original 65,000-year-old dating estimate holds.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɪf ðə ərˈɪʤənəl 65,000-year-old* ˈdeɪtɪŋ ˈɛstəˌmeɪt hoʊldz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Meanwhile, broken, rearranged, and burned stalagmites deep within a French cave
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈminˌwaɪl, ˈbroʊkən, riəreɪnʤd, ənd bərnd stalagmites* dip wɪˈθɪn ə frɛnʧ keɪv

EtiC0DVewa4.text: left scientists wondering if the mysterious structures
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: lɛft ˈsaɪəntɪsts ˈwəndərɪŋ ɪf ðə mɪˈstɪriəs ˈstrəkʧərz

EtiC0DVewa4.text: held spiritual or ceremonial significance to Neanderthals.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: hɛld ˈspɪrɪˌʧuəl ər ˌsɛrəˈmoʊniəl sɪgˈnɪfɪkəns tɪ niˈændərˌθɔlz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Clearly, they weren’t as different as originally believed.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈklɪrli, ðeɪ weren’t* ɛz ˈdɪfərənt ɛz ərˈɪʤənəli bɪˈlivd.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: And following the first fully sequenced Neanderthal genome in 2010,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ðə fərst ˈfʊli sequenced* niˈændərˌθɔl ˈʤiˌnoʊm ɪn 2010

EtiC0DVewa4.text: researchers realized our species interbred.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈrisərʧərz ˈriəˌlaɪzd ɑr ˈspiʃiz ˌɪntərˈbrɛd.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: All modern humans retain some Neanderthal DNA— up to about 4%—
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɔl ˈmɑdərn ˈjumənz rɪˈteɪn səm niˈændərˌθɔl dna—*— əp tɪ əˈbaʊt 4%—*

EtiC0DVewa4.text: the result of hundreds of discrete interbreeding events.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ðə rɪˈzəlt əv ˈhənərdz əv dɪˈskrit interbreeding* ɪˈvɛnts.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: So, what happened to Neanderthals?
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: soʊ, wət ˈhæpənd tɪ niˈændərˌθɔlz?

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Theories from rampant cannibalism, to disease and climate change,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈθɪriz frəm ˈræmpənt ˈkænəbəˌlɪzəm, tɪ dɪˈziz ənd ˈklaɪmɪt ʧeɪnʤ,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: to aggressive Homo sapiens have been floated.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: tɪ əˈgrɛsɪv ˈhoʊmoʊ ˈseɪpiənz hæv bɪn ˈfloʊtɪd.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: But another idea is that, because Neanderthals lived in smaller groups,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: bət əˈnəðər aɪˈdiə ɪz ðət, bɪˈkəz niˈændərˌθɔlz lɪvd ɪn sˈmɔlər grups,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: as they interbred with larger populations of Homo sapiens,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɛz ðeɪ ˌɪntərˈbrɛd wɪθ ˈlɑrʤər ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃənz əv ˈhoʊmoʊ ˈseɪpiənz,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: they just sort of got gradually absorbed.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ðeɪ ʤɪst sɔrt əv gɑt ˈgræʤuəli əbˈzɔrbd.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: Overall, consensus has begun shifting away from representing human evolution as tree-like
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ˈoʊvərˌɔl, kənˈsɛnsəs həz ˈbeɪgən ˈʃɪftɪŋ əˈweɪ frəm ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnɪŋ ˈjumən ˌɛvəˈluʃən ɛz tree-like*

EtiC0DVewa4.text: and more towards a kind of braided stream,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ənd mɔr təˈwɔrdz ə kaɪnd əv ˈbreɪdɪd strim,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: with different hominin groups separating and rejoining at various junctures.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: wɪθ ˈdɪfərənt hominin* grups ˈsɛpərˌeɪtɪŋ ənd riˈʤɔɪnɪŋ æt ˈvɛriəs ˈʤəŋkʧərz.

EtiC0DVewa4.text: As we continue to learn more about our evolutionary cousins,
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: ɛz wi kənˈtɪnju tɪ lərn mɔr əˈbaʊt ɑr ˌɛvəˈluʃəˌnɛri ˈkəzənz,

EtiC0DVewa4.text: each discovery raises questions about just how much we share.
EtiC0DVewa4.ipa: iʧ ˌdɪˈskəvri ˈreɪzɪz kˈwɛsʧənz əˈbaʊt ʤɪst haʊ məʧ wi ʃɛr.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Way before the first selfie, the ancient Greeks and Romans had a myth
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: weɪ ˌbiˈfɔr ðə fərst ˈsɛlˌfi, ðə ˈeɪnʧənt griks ənd ˈroʊmənz hæd ə mɪθ

arJLy3hX1E8.text: about someone a little too obsessed with his own image. In one telling,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: əˈbaʊt ˈsəmˌwən ə ˈlɪtəl tu əbˈsɛst wɪθ hɪz oʊn ˈɪmɪʤ. ɪn wən ˈtɛlɪŋ,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Narcissus was a handsome guy wandering the world in search of someone to love. After rejecting a nymph named Echo,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: nɑrˈsɪsəs wɑz ə ˈhænsəm gaɪ ˈwɑndərɪŋ ðə wərld ɪn sərʧ əv ˈsəmˌwən tɪ ləv. ˈæftər rɪˈʤɛktɪŋ ə nɪmf neɪmd ˈɛkoʊ,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: he caught a glimpse of his own reflection in a river, and fell in love with it. Unable to tear himself away,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: hi kɔt ə glɪmps əv hɪz oʊn rɪˈflɛkʃən ɪn ə ˈrɪvər, ənd fɛl ɪn ləv wɪθ ɪt. əˈneɪbəl tɪ tɪr hɪmˈsɛlf əˈweɪ,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Narcissus drowned. A flower marked the spot of where he died, and we call that flower the Narcissus.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: nɑrˈsɪsəs draʊnd. ə flaʊər mɑrkt ðə spɑt əv wɛr hi daɪd, ənd wi kɔl ðət flaʊər ðə nɑrˈsɪsəs.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: The myth captures the basic idea of narcissism, elevated and sometimes detrimental self-involvement.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ðə mɪθ ˈkæpʧərz ðə ˈbeɪsɪk aɪˈdiə əv ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm, ˈɛləˌveɪtɪd ənd ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ˌdɛtrəˈmɛnəl self-involvement*.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: But it's not just a personality type that shows up in advice columns.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: bət ɪts nɑt ʤɪst ə ˌpərsəˈnælɪti taɪp ðət ʃoʊz əp ɪn ədˈvaɪs ˈkɑləmz.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: It's actually a set of traits classified and studied by psychologists.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ɪts ˈæˌkʧuəli ə sɛt əv treɪts ˈklæsəˌfaɪd ənd ˈstədid baɪ saɪˈkɑləʤəsts.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: The psychological definition of narcissism is an inflated, grandiose self-image.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ðə ˌsaɪkəˈlɑʤɪkəl ˌdɛfəˈnɪʃən əv ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm ɪz ən ˌɪnˈfleɪtɪd, ˌgrændiˈoʊs self-image*.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: To varying degrees, narcissists think they're better looking, smarter, and more important than other people,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: tɪ ˈvɛriɪŋ dɪˈgriz, narcissists* θɪŋk ðɛr ˈbɛtər ˈlʊkɪŋ, sˈmɑrtər, ənd mɔr ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ðən ˈəðər ˈpipəl,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: and that they deserve special treatment. Psychologists recognize two forms of narcissism as a personality trait:
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ənd ðət ðeɪ dɪˈzərv ˈspɛʃəl ˈtritmənt. saɪˈkɑləʤəsts ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪz tu fɔrmz əv ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm ɛz ə ˌpərsəˈnælɪti treɪt:

arJLy3hX1E8.text: grandiose and vulnerable narcissism. There's also narcissistic personality disorder,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ˌgrændiˈoʊs ənd ˈvəlnərəbəl ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm. ðɛrz ˈɔlsoʊ ˌnɑrsɪˈsɪstɪk ˌpərsəˈnælɪti dɪˈsɔrdər,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: a more extreme form, which we'll return to shortly. Grandiose narcissism is the most familiar kind,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ə mɔr ɪkˈstrim fɔrm, wɪʧ wɪl rɪˈtərn tɪ ˈʃɔrtli. ˌgrændiˈoʊs ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm ɪz ðə moʊst fəˈmɪljər kaɪnd,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: characterized by extroversion, dominance, and attention seeking.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ˈkɛrɪktərˌaɪzd baɪ extroversion*, ˈdɑmənəns, ənd əˈtɛnʃən ˈsikɪŋ.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Grandiose narcissists pursue attention and power, sometimes as politicians, celebrities, or cultural leaders.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ˌgrændiˈoʊs narcissists* pərˈsu əˈtɛnʃən ənd paʊər, ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ɛz ˌpɑləˈtɪʃənz, səˈlɛbrɪtiz, ər ˈkəlʧərəl ˈlidərz.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Of course, not everyone who pursues these positions of power is narcissistic. Many do it for very positive reasons,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: əv kɔrs, nɑt ˈɛvriˌwən hu pərˈsuz ðiz pəˈzɪʃənz əv paʊər ɪz ˌnɑrsɪˈsɪstɪk. ˈmɛni du ɪt fər ˈvɛri ˈpɑzətɪv ˈrizənz,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: like reaching their full potential, or helping make people's lives better. But narcissistic individuals seek power
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: laɪk ˈriʧɪŋ ðɛr fʊl pəˈtɛnʃəl, ər ˈhɛlpɪŋ meɪk ˈpipəlz lɪvz ˈbɛtər. bət ˌnɑrsɪˈsɪstɪk ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəlz sik paʊər

arJLy3hX1E8.text: for the status and attention that goes with it. Meanwhile, vulnerable narcissists can be quiet and reserved.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: fər ðə ˈstætəs ənd əˈtɛnʃən ðət goʊz wɪθ ɪt. ˈminˌwaɪl, ˈvəlnərəbəl narcissists* kən bi kwaɪət ənd rɪˈzərvd.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: They have a strong sense of entitlement, but are easily threatened or slighted.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ðeɪ hæv ə strɔŋ sɛns əv ɛnˈtaɪtəlmənt, bət ər ˈizəli θˈrɛtənd ər sˈlaɪtɪd.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: In either case, the dark side of narcissism shows up over the long term. Narcissists tend to act selfishly,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ɪn ˈiðər keɪs, ðə dɑrk saɪd əv ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm ʃoʊz əp ˈoʊvər ðə lɔŋ tərm. narcissists* tɛnd tɪ ækt selfishly*,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: so narcissistic leaders may make risky or unethical decisions,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: soʊ ˌnɑrsɪˈsɪstɪk ˈlidərz meɪ meɪk ˈrɪski ər əˈnɛθɪkəl dɪˈsɪʒənz,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: and narcissistic partners may be dishonest or unfaithful. When their rosy view of themselves is challenged,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ənd ˌnɑrsɪˈsɪstɪk ˈpɑrtnərz meɪ bi dɪˈsɑnəst ər ənˈfeɪθfəl. wɪn ðɛr ˈroʊzi vju əv ðɛmˈsɛlvz ɪz ˈʧælənʤd,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: they can become resentful and aggressive. It's like a disease where the sufferers feel pretty good, but the people around them suffer.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ðeɪ kən bɪˈkəm rɪˈzɛntfəl ənd əˈgrɛsɪv. ɪts laɪk ə dɪˈziz wɛr ðə ˈsəfərərz fil ˈprɪti gʊd, bət ðə ˈpipəl əraʊnd ðɛm ˈsəfər.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Taken to the extreme, this behavior is classified as a psychological disorder called narcissistic personality disorder.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ˈteɪkən tɪ ðə ɪkˈstrim, ðɪs bɪˈheɪvjər ɪz ˈklæsəˌfaɪd ɛz ə ˌsaɪkəˈlɑʤɪkəl dɪˈsɔrdər kɔld ˌnɑrsɪˈsɪstɪk ˌpərsəˈnælɪti dɪˈsɔrdər.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: It affects one to two percent of the population, more commonly men. It is also a diagnosis reserved for adults.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ɪt əˈfɛkts wən tɪ tu pərˈsɛnt əv ðə ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən, mɔr ˈkɑmənli mɛn. ɪt ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ ə ˌdaɪəgˈnoʊsəs rɪˈzərvd fər ˈædəlts.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Young people, especially children, can be very self-centered, but this might just be a normal part of development.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: jəŋ ˈpipəl, əˈspɛʃəli ˈʧɪldrən, kən bi ˈvɛri ˈsɛlfˈsɛntərd, bət ðɪs maɪt ʤɪst bi ə ˈnɔrməl pɑrt əv dɪˈvɛləpmənt.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: The fifth edition of the American Psychiatric Association's Diagnostic and Statistical Manual
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ðə fɪθ ɪˈdɪʃən əv ðə əˈmɛrɪkən ˌsaɪkiˈætrɪk əˌsoʊʃiˈeɪʃənz ˌdaɪəgˈnɑstɪk ənd stəˈtɪstɪkəl ˈmænjuəl

arJLy3hX1E8.text: describes several traits associated with narcissistic personality disorder. They include a grandiose view of oneself,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: dɪˈskraɪbz ˈsɛvərəl treɪts əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪtəd wɪθ ˌnɑrsɪˈsɪstɪk ˌpərsəˈnælɪti dɪˈsɔrdər. ðeɪ ˌɪnˈklud ə ˌgrændiˈoʊs vju əv ˌwənˈsɛlf,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: problems with empathy, a sense of entitlement, and a need for admiration or attention.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ˈprɑbləmz wɪθ ˈɛmpəθi, ə sɛns əv ɛnˈtaɪtəlmənt, ənd ə nid fər ˌædmərˈeɪʃən ər əˈtɛnʃən.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: What makes these trait a true personality disorder is that they take over people's lives and cause significant problems.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: wət meɪks ðiz treɪt ə tru ˌpərsəˈnælɪti dɪˈsɔrdər ɪz ðət ðeɪ teɪk ˈoʊvər ˈpipəlz lɪvz ənd kɔz sɪgˈnɪfɪkənt ˈprɑbləmz.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Imagine that instead of caring for your spouse or children, you used them as a source of attention or admiration.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ˌɪˈmæʤən ðət ˌɪnˈstɛd əv ˈkɛrɪŋ fər jʊr spaʊs ər ˈʧɪldrən, ju juzd ðɛm ɛz ə sɔrs əv əˈtɛnʃən ər ˌædmərˈeɪʃən.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Or imagine that instead of seeking constructive feedback about your performance, you instead told everyone who tried to help you
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ər ˌɪˈmæʤən ðət ˌɪnˈstɛd əv ˈsikɪŋ kənˈstrəktɪv ˈfidˌbæk əˈbaʊt jʊr pərˈfɔrməns, ju ˌɪnˈstɛd toʊld ˈɛvriˌwən hu traɪd tɪ hɛlp ju

arJLy3hX1E8.text: that they were wrong. So what causes narcissism? Twin studies show a strong genetic component,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ðət ðeɪ wər rɔŋ. soʊ wət ˈkɔzɪz ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm? twɪn ˈstədiz ʃoʊ ə strɔŋ ʤəˈnɛtɪk kəmˈpoʊnənt,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: although we don't know which genes are involved. But environment matters, too. Parents who put their child on a pedestal
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ˌɔlˈðoʊ wi doʊnt noʊ wɪʧ ʤinz ər ˌɪnˈvɑlvd. bət ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt ˈmætərz, tu. ˈpɛrənts hu pʊt ðɛr ʧaɪld ɔn ə ˈpɛdəstəl

arJLy3hX1E8.text: can foster grandiose narcissism. And cold, controlling parents can contribute to vulnerable narcissism.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: kən ˈfɑstər ˌgrændiˈoʊs ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm. ənd koʊld, kənˈtroʊlɪŋ ˈpɛrənts kən kənˈtrɪbjut tɪ ˈvəlnərəbəl ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Narcissism also seems to be higher in cultures that value individuality and self-promotion.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm ˈɔlsoʊ simz tɪ bi haɪər ɪn ˈkəlʧərz ðət ˈvælju ˌɪnˌdɪvɪʤuˈælɪti ənd self-promotion*.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: In the United States, for example, narcissism as a personality trait has been rising since the 1970s,
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ɪn ðə juˈnaɪtɪd steɪts, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm ɛz ə ˌpərsəˈnælɪti treɪt həz bɪn ˈraɪzɪŋ sɪns ðə 1970s*,

arJLy3hX1E8.text: when the communal focus of the 60s gave way to the self-esteem movement and a rise in materialism.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: wɪn ðə kəmˈjunəl ˈfoʊkɪs əv ðə 60s* geɪv weɪ tɪ ðə self-esteem* ˈmuvmənt ənd ə raɪz ɪn məˈtɪriəˌlɪzəm.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: More recently, social media has multiplied the possibilities for self-promotion, though it's worth noting
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: mɔr ˈrisəntli, ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə həz ˈməltəˌplaɪd ðə ˌpɑsəˈbɪlətiz fər self-promotion*, ðoʊ ɪts wərθ ˈnoʊtɪŋ

arJLy3hX1E8.text: that there's no clear evidence that social media causes narcissism.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ðət ðɛrz noʊ klɪr ˈɛvədəns ðət ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə ˈkɔzɪz ˈnɑrsɪˌsɪzəm.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: Rather, it provides narcissists a means to seek social status and attention.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ˈrəðər, ɪt prəˈvaɪdz narcissists* ə minz tɪ sik ˈsoʊʃəl ˈstætəs ənd əˈtɛnʃən.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: So can narcissists improve on those negative traits? Yes! Anything that promotes honest reflection on their own behavior
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: soʊ kən narcissists* ˌɪmˈpruv ɔn ðoʊz ˈnɛgətɪv treɪts? jɛs! ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ðət prəˈmoʊts ˈɑnəst rɪˈflɛkʃən ɔn ðɛr oʊn bɪˈheɪvjər

arJLy3hX1E8.text: and caring for others, like psychotherapy or practicing compassion towards others, can be helpful.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ənd ˈkɛrɪŋ fər ˈəðərz, laɪk ˌsaɪkoʊˈθɛrəpi ər ˈpræktɪsɪŋ kəmˈpæʃən təˈwɔrdz ˈəðərz, kən bi ˈhɛlpfəl.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: The difficulty is it can be challenging for people with narcissistic personality disorder to keep working at self-betterment.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: ðə ˈdɪfɪˌkəlti ɪz ɪt kən bi ˈʧælənʤɪŋ fər ˈpipəl wɪθ ˌnɑrsɪˈsɪstɪk ˌpərsəˈnælɪti dɪˈsɔrdər tɪ kip ˈwərkɪŋ æt self-betterment*.

arJLy3hX1E8.text: For a narcissist, self-reflection is hard from an unflattering angle.
arJLy3hX1E8.ipa: fər ə ˈnɑrsɪsɪst, self-reflection* ɪz hɑrd frəm ən ənˈflætərɪŋ ˈæŋgəl.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: In a 2021 study, researchers asked over 500 participants a simple question:
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ɪn ə 2021 ˈstədi, ˈrisərʧərz æst ˈoʊvər 500 pɑrˈtɪsəpənts ə ˈsɪmpəl kˈwɛʃən:

P5pmMe3EarI.text: how does the morning-after pill work?
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: haʊ dɪz ðə morning-after* pɪl wərk?

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Despite these pills widespread use, 60% answered incorrectly.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: dɪˈspaɪt ðiz pɪlz ˈwaɪdˈsprɛd juz, 60 ˈænsərd ˌɪnkərˈɛktli.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Emergency contraceptives are often misunderstood. In fact, even the phrase “the morning-after pill” is a misnomer.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪvz ər ˈɔfən ˌmɪsəndərˈstʊd. ɪn fækt, ˈivɪn ðə freɪz ““the* morning-after* pill”*” ɪz ə mɪsˈnoʊmər.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: So, how do they work?
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: soʊ, haʊ du ðeɪ wərk?

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Each month, the brain and the ovaries prepare an egg to be released. First, a group of immature eggs start to grow
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: iʧ mənθ, ðə breɪn ənd ðə ˈoʊˌvəriz priˈpɛr ən ɛg tɪ bi riˈlist. fərst, ə grup əv ˌɪməˈtjʊr ɛgz stɑrt tɪ groʊ

P5pmMe3EarI.text: protective, fluid-filled sacs called follicles. Around the eighth day, one of these follicles becomes mature or dominant,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: prəˈtɛktɪv, fluid-filled* sæks kɔld ˈfɑlɪkəlz. əraʊnd ðə eɪθ deɪ, wən əv ðiz ˈfɑlɪkəlz bɪˈkəmz məˈʧʊr ər ˈdɑmənənt,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: and the others are reabsorbed.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ənd ðə ˈəðərz ər ˌriəbˈzɔrbd.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: At this point, the brain signals the pituitary gland to produce luteinizing hormone, often just called LH.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: æt ðɪs pɔɪnt, ðə breɪn ˈsɪgnəlz ðə pɪˈtuɪˌtɛri glænd tɪ ˈproʊdus luteinizing* ˈhɔrˌmoʊn, ˈɔfən ʤɪst kɔld lh*.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: A big surge in LH tells the dominant follicle to rupture, releasing the egg.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ə bɪg sərʤ ɪn lh* tɛlz ðə ˈdɑmənənt ˈfɑlɪkəl tɪ ˈrəpʧər, riˈlisɪŋ ðə ɛg.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: This is ovulation. The egg travels to the fallopian tube, where it stays for about 24 hours,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən. ðə ɛg ˈtrævəlz tɪ ðə fəˈloʊpiən tub, wɛr ɪt steɪz fər əˈbaʊt 24 aʊərz,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: and it’s only in this very short window that it can be fertilized by sperm.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ənd it’s* ˈoʊnli ɪn ðɪs ˈvɛri ʃɔrt ˈwɪndoʊ ðət ɪt kən bi ˈfərtəˌlaɪzd baɪ spərm.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: The rise of LH also stimulates the production of a hormone called progesterone. When progesterone binds at specific receptors,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ðə raɪz əv lh* ˈɔlsoʊ ˈstɪmjəˌleɪts ðə pərˈdəkʃən əv ə ˈhɔrˌmoʊn kɔld proʊˈʤɛstərˌoʊn. wɪn proʊˈʤɛstərˌoʊn baɪndz æt spɪˈsɪfɪk rəˈsɛptərz,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: this also helps to signal ovulation. And high levels of progesterone are vital for preparing the uterine lining
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ðɪs ˈɔlsoʊ hɛlps tɪ ˈsɪgnəl ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən. ənd haɪ ˈlɛvəlz əv proʊˈʤɛstərˌoʊn ər ˈvaɪtəl fər pərˈpɛrɪŋ ðə ˈjutərən ˈlaɪnɪŋ

P5pmMe3EarI.text: to support the possible implantation of a fertilized egg.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: tɪ səˈpɔrt ðə ˈpɑsəbəl ˌɪmplænˈteɪʃən əv ə ˈfərtəˌlaɪzd ɛg.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: So, where do emergency contraceptive pills come into play?
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: soʊ, wɛr du ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪv pɪlz kəm ˈɪntu pleɪ?

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Emergency contraceptives are not abortion pills. Instead, they work much earlier in the process before pregnancy,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪvz ər nɑt əˈbɔrʃən pɪlz. ˌɪnˈstɛd, ðeɪ wərk məʧ ˈərliər ɪn ðə ˈprɔˌsɛs ˌbiˈfɔr ˈprɛgnənsi,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: by disrupting or delaying ovulation.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: baɪ dɪsˈrəptɪŋ ər dɪˈleɪɪŋ ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: There are two main types of pills.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ðɛr ər tu meɪn taɪps əv pɪlz.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: The first type, sometimes called Plan B, contains a molecule called levonorgestrel,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ðə fərst taɪp, ˈsəmˌtaɪmz kɔld plæn bi, kənˈteɪnz ə ˈmɑləˌkjul kɔld levonorgestrel*,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: which is structurally very similar to the body’s hormone, progesterone.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz ˈstrəkʧərəli ˈvɛri ˈsɪmələr tɪ ðə body’s* ˈhɔrˌmoʊn, proʊˈʤɛstərˌoʊn.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: When taken, levonorgestrel interferes with the production of LH.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: wɪn ˈteɪkən, levonorgestrel* ˌɪnərˈfɪrz wɪθ ðə pərˈdəkʃən əv lh*.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Because LH doesn’t surge, there’s no ovulation.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: bɪˈkəz lh* doesn’t* sərʤ, there’s* noʊ ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: That said, Plan B needs to be taken before an LH surge.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ðət sɛd, plæn bi nidz tɪ bi ˈteɪkən ˌbiˈfɔr ən lh* sərʤ.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: It can’t prevent ovulation once LH starts rising.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ɪt can’t* prɪˈvɛnt ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən wəns lh* stɑrts ˈraɪzɪŋ.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: The second type of pill contains a molecule called ulipristal acetate.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ðə ˈsɛkənd taɪp əv pɪl kənˈteɪnz ə ˈmɑləˌkjul kɔld ulipristal* ˈæsəˌteɪt.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: It works by blocking progesterone from binding at receptors, thereby delaying ovulation.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ɪt wərks baɪ ˈblɑkɪŋ proʊˈʤɛstərˌoʊn frəm ˈbaɪndɪŋ æt rəˈsɛptərz, ˈðɛrˈbaɪ dɪˈleɪɪŋ ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Ulipristal acetate works even if LH has already begun to rise,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ulipristal* ˈæsəˌteɪt wərks ˈivɪn ɪf lh* həz ɔˈrɛdi ˈbeɪgən tɪ raɪz,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: which means it’s effective in a wider window of time than Plan B.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: wɪʧ minz it’s* ˈifɛktɪv ɪn ə ˈwaɪdər ˈwɪndoʊ əv taɪm ðən plæn bi.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Since both pills work by blocking or delaying ovulation, the name “the morning-after pill” can be misleading.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: sɪns boʊθ pɪlz wərk baɪ ˈblɑkɪŋ ər dɪˈleɪɪŋ ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən, ðə neɪm ““the* morning-after* pill”*” kən bi mɪsˈlidɪŋ.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Emergency contraceptives are most effective when taken as soon as possible after sex,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪvz ər moʊst ˈifɛktɪv wɪn ˈteɪkən ɛz sun ɛz ˈpɑsəbəl ˈæftər sɛks,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: to better the chance of disrupting ovulation before it occurs.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: tɪ ˈbɛtər ðə ʧæns əv dɪsˈrəptɪŋ ˌoʊvjəˈleɪʃən ˌbiˈfɔr ɪt əˈkərz.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: There is no need to wait until morning.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ðɛr ɪz noʊ nid tɪ weɪt ənˈtɪl ˈmɔrnɪŋ.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: For this reason, many healthcare providers suggest having them on hand.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: fər ðɪs ˈrizən, ˈmɛni ˈhɛlθˌkɛr prəˈvaɪdərz səˈʤɛst ˈhævɪŋ ðɛm ɔn hænd.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Yet emergency contraceptives may still prevent pregnancy even if they’re taken 3 to 5 days after sex,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: jɛt ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪvz meɪ stɪl prɪˈvɛnt ˈprɛgnənsi ˈivɪn ɪf they’re* ˈteɪkən 3 tɪ 5 deɪz ˈæftər sɛks,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: depending on the pill.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: dɪˈpɛndɪŋ ɔn ðə pɪl.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: That’s because sperm can live in the reproductive tract for about five days.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: that’s* bɪˈkəz spərm kən lɪv ɪn ðə ˌriprəˈdəktɪv trækt fər əˈbaʊt faɪv deɪz.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: So, for example, if a person ovulates three days after having unprotected sex, there's still a chance of pregnancy.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: soʊ, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ɪf ə ˈpərsən ovulates* θri deɪz ˈæftər ˈhævɪŋ ˌənprəˈtɛktɪd sɛks, ðɛrz stɪl ə ʧæns əv ˈprɛgnənsi.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Since it's difficult to know exactly where someone is in their cycle, it’s best to take emergency contraceptives as soon as possible.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: sɪns ɪts ˈdɪfəkəlt tɪ noʊ ɪgˈzæktli wɛr ˈsəmˌwən ɪz ɪn ðɛr ˈsaɪkəl, it’s* bɛst tɪ teɪk ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪvz ɛz sun ɛz ˈpɑsəbəl.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: But perhaps the most widely held misconception about these pills is that they can harm a person’s future fertility.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: bət pərˈhæps ðə moʊst ˈwaɪdli hɛld mɪskənˈsɛpʃən əˈbaʊt ðiz pɪlz ɪz ðət ðeɪ kən hɑrm ə person’s* fˈjuʧər fərˈtɪlɪti.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Decades of research have shown that this isn’t the case, even if emergency contraceptives are taken multiple times.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ˈdɛkeɪdz əv ˈrisərʧ hæv ʃoʊn ðət ðɪs isn’t* ðə keɪs, ˈivɪn ɪf ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪvz ər ˈteɪkən ˈməltəpəl taɪmz.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: In fact, the effects of both pills are short lived, and they don't provide continued protection.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ɪn fækt, ðə ˈifɛkts əv boʊθ pɪlz ər ʃɔrt lɪvd, ənd ðeɪ doʊnt prəˈvaɪd kənˈtɪnjud prəˈtɛkʃən.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: It's recommended that a person uses condoms for the remainder of their cycle,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ɪts ˌrɛkəˈmɛndɪd ðət ə ˈpərsən ˈjuzɪz ˈkɑndəmz fər ðə rɪˈmeɪndər əv ðɛr ˈsaɪkəl,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: and if they have unprotected sex again, then repeat use.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ənd ɪf ðeɪ hæv ˌənprəˈtɛktɪd sɛks əˈgɛn, ðɛn rɪˈpit juz.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Of course, emergency contraceptive pills aren’t failproof. As they neither prevent fertilization nor the implantation of a fertilized egg,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: əv kɔrs, ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪv pɪlz aren’t* failproof*. ɛz ðeɪ ˈniðər prɪˈvɛnt ˌfərtəlɪˈzeɪʃən nɔr ðə ˌɪmplænˈteɪʃən əv ə ˈfərtəˌlaɪzd ɛg,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: these pills are ineffective if someone has already ovulated, or the LH surge has already peaked.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ðiz pɪlz ər ˌɪnɪˈfɛktɪv ɪf ˈsəmˌwən həz ɔˈrɛdi ovulated*, ər ðə lh* sərʤ həz ɔˈrɛdi pikt.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: However, there is a third option that can protect against pregnancy, even if a person has already ovulated.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ðɛr ɪz ə θərd ˈɔpʃən ðət kən prəˈtɛkt əˈgɛnst ˈprɛgnənsi, ˈivɪn ɪf ə ˈpərsən həz ɔˈrɛdi ovulated*.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Many people know copper intrauterine devices, or IUDs, as a form of long-term birth control,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ˈmɛni ˈpipəl noʊ ˈkɑpər ˌɪntrəˈjutərɪn dɪˈvaɪsɪz, ər iuds*, ɛz ə fɔrm əv ˈlɔŋˈtərm bərθ kənˈtroʊl,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: but they also work to prevent pregnancy if inserted soon after unprotected sex.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: bət ðeɪ ˈɔlsoʊ wərk tɪ prɪˈvɛnt ˈprɛgnənsi ɪf ˌɪnˈsərtɪd sun ˈæftər ˌənprəˈtɛktɪd sɛks.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Unlike emergency contraceptive pills, a copper IUD can prevent pregnancy before and after fertilization.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ənˈlaɪk ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪv pɪlz, ə ˈkɑpər iud* kən prɪˈvɛnt ˈprɛgnənsi ˌbiˈfɔr ənd ˈæftər ˌfərtəlɪˈzeɪʃən.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Copper is toxic to sperm and creates a uterine environment that makes implantation unlikely.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: ˈkɑpər ɪz ˈtɑksɪk tɪ spərm ənd kriˈeɪts ə ˈjutərən ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt ðət meɪks ˌɪmplænˈteɪʃən ənˈlaɪkli.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Of course, copper IUDs might be less accessible in an emergency situation— they must be inserted by a trained healthcare provider.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: əv kɔrs, ˈkɑpər iuds* maɪt bi lɛs ækˈsɛsəbəl ɪn ən ˈimərʤənsi situation—*— ðeɪ məst bi ˌɪnˈsərtɪd baɪ ə treɪnd ˈhɛlθˌkɛr prəˈvaɪdər.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: But on the upside, they continue to prevent pregnancy for at least 10 years while in place.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: bət ɔn ðə ˈəpˈsaɪd, ðeɪ kənˈtɪnju tɪ prɪˈvɛnt ˈprɛgnənsi fər æt list 10 jɪrz waɪl ɪn pleɪs.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: Most healthcare providers agree that it's more effective, as well as less expensive,
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: moʊst ˈhɛlθˌkɛr prəˈvaɪdərz əˈgri ðət ɪts mɔr ˈifɛktɪv, ɛz wɛl ɛz lɛs ɪkˈspɛnsɪv,

P5pmMe3EarI.text: to use a regular form of birth control when trying to avoid pregnancy.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: tɪ juz ə ˈrɛgjələr fɔrm əv bərθ kənˈtroʊl wɪn traɪɪŋ tɪ əˈvɔɪd ˈprɛgnənsi.

P5pmMe3EarI.text: But if or when you need it, emergency contraceptives are a safe option to use.
P5pmMe3EarI.ipa: bət ɪf ər wɪn ju nid ɪt, ˈimərʤənsi ˌkɑntrəˈsɛptɪvz ər ə seɪf ˈɔpʃən tɪ juz.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: In the late 1700s, German physician Anton Mesmer was enthralling Europe,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ɪn ðə leɪt 1700s*, ˈʤərmən fəˈzɪʃən ˈænˌtɔn ˈmɛzmər wɑz enthralling* ˈjʊrəp,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: curing conditions from nervous disorders to blindness— or so he claimed.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˈkjʊrɪŋ kənˈdɪʃənz frəm ˈnərvəs dɪˈsɔrdərz tɪ blindness—*— ər soʊ hi kleɪmd.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: According to Mesmer, an invisible, magnetic fluid animated all living beings,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ˈmɛzmər, ən ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl, mægˈnɛtɪk fluɪd ˈænəˌmeɪtɪd ɔl ˈlɪvɪŋ biɪŋz,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: and disturbances to it caused illness.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ənd dɪˈstərbənsɪz tɪ ɪt kɔzd ˈɪlnəs.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: For so-called “treatment,” Mesmer had his clientele form a chain around a contraption filled with water
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: fər ˈsoʊˈkɔld ““treatment,”*,” ˈmɛzmər hæd hɪz ˌklaɪənˈtɛl fɔrm ə ʧeɪn əraʊnd ə kənˈtræpʃən fɪld wɪθ ˈwɔtər

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: he allegedly magnetized.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: hi əˈlɛʤədli ˈmægnɪˌtaɪzd.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Then, with waves of his hands,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðɛn, wɪθ weɪvz əv hɪz hænz,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: patients would fall into fits that would supposedly restore their health.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˈpeɪʃənz wʊd fɔl ˈɪntu fɪts ðət wʊd səˈpoʊzədli rɪˈstɔr ðɛr hɛlθ.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Some, however, were skeptical— including King Louis XVI,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: səm, ˌhaʊˈɛvər, wər skeptical—*— ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ kɪŋ luɪs xvi*,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: who tasked renowned researchers with interrogating Mesmer's methods in 1784.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: hu tæskt rɪˈnaʊnd ˈrisərʧərz wɪθ ˌɪnˈtɛrəˌgeɪtɪŋ mesmer's* ˈmɛθədz ɪn 1784

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: They conducted science’s first-ever blind placebo trial,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðeɪ kənˈdəktəd science’s* first-ever* blaɪnd pləˈsiboʊ traɪəl,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: informing participants that they were receiving the magnetized treatment though they weren’t, and vice versa.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˌɪnˈfɔrmɪŋ pɑrˈtɪsəpənts ðət ðeɪ wər rɪˈsivɪŋ ðə ˈmægnɪˌtaɪzd ˈtritmənt ðoʊ ðeɪ weren’t*, ənd vaɪs ˈvərsə.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: The committee ultimately concluded that any positive outcomes weren't attributable to magnetism.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðə kəˈmɪti ˈəltəmətli kənˈkludɪd ðət ˈɛni ˈpɑzətɪv ˈaʊtˌkəmz wərənt əˈtrɪbjətəbəl tɪ ˈmægnəˌtɪzəm.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Instead, they were results of the participants’ own imaginations,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˌɪnˈstɛd, ðeɪ wər rɪˈzəlts əv ðə participants’*’ oʊn ˌɪˌmæʤəˈneɪʃənz,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: responding to suggestions about the treatment's effectiveness.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: rɪˈspɑndɪŋ tɪ səˈʤɛsʧənz əˈbaʊt ðə treatment's* ˈifɛktɪvnəs.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Mesmer disappeared, but the tantalizing takeaway remained.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˈmɛzmər ˌdɪsəˈpɪrd, bət ðə ˈtænəˌlaɪzɪŋ takeaway* rɪˈmeɪnd.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: And decades later, Scottish surgeon James Braid
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ənd ˈdɛkeɪdz ˈleɪtər, ˈskɑtɪʃ ˈsərʤɪn ʤeɪmz breɪd

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: began utilizing aspects of Mesmerism, minus the magnetism.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: bɪˈgæn ˈjutəˌlaɪzɪŋ ˈæˌspɛkts əv ˈmɛsmərˌɪzəm, ˈmaɪnəs ðə ˈmægnəˌtɪzəm.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Braid thought it induced a sleep-like state, so he called it hypnotism.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: breɪd θɔt ɪt ˌɪnˈdust ə sleep-like* steɪt, soʊ hi kɔld ɪt ˈhɪpnəˌtɪzəm.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: The practice today holds an entrancing place in popular fantasy.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðə ˈpræktɪs təˈdeɪ hoʊldz ən entrancing* pleɪs ɪn ˈpɑpjələr ˈfænəsi.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: But is there any truth to what it can accomplish? Or is it just illusion?
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: bət ɪz ðɛr ˈɛni truθ tɪ wət ɪt kən əˈkɑmplɪʃ? ər ɪz ɪt ʤɪst ˌɪˈluʒən?

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: While stage hypnosis has contributed to the conception that hypnotized people lose control over their actions,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: waɪl steɪʤ hɪpˈnoʊsəs həz kənˈtrɪbjutɪd tɪ ðə kənˈsɛpʃən ðət ˈhɪpnəˌtaɪzd ˈpipəl luz kənˈtroʊl ˈoʊvər ðɛr ˈækʃənz,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: this doesn’t seem accurate.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðɪs doesn’t* sim ˈækjərət.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Whether or not they were hypnotized, one study found that participants were just as likely
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˈwɛðər ər nɑt ðeɪ wər ˈhɪpnəˌtaɪzd, wən ˈstədi faʊnd ðət pɑrˈtɪsəpənts wər ʤɪst ɛz ˈlaɪkli

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: to dip their hands into acid.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: tɪ dɪp ðɛr hænz ˈɪntu ˈæsəd.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: This might be because they felt motivated to take risks within the presumably safe confines of a psychology experiment.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðɪs maɪt bi bɪˈkəz ðeɪ fɛlt ˈmoʊtəˌveɪtəd tɪ teɪk rɪsks wɪˈθɪn ðə prɪˈzuməbli seɪf ˈkɑnˌfaɪnz əv ə saɪˈkɑləʤi ɪkˈspɛrəmənt.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Similarly, onstage participants might comply in the context of social pressures and fun,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˈsɪmələrli, ˌɑnˈsteɪʤ pɑrˈtɪsəpənts maɪt kəmˈplaɪ ɪn ðə ˈkɑntɛkst əv ˈsoʊʃəl ˈprɛʃərz ənd fən,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: with perhaps some hypnosis at play.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: wɪθ pərˈhæps səm hɪpˈnoʊsəs æt pleɪ.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: But hypnosis used in medicine today looks different;
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: bət hɪpˈnoʊsəs juzd ɪn ˈmɛdəsən təˈdeɪ lʊks ˈdɪfərənt;

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: no props are necessary.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: noʊ prɑps ər ˈnɛsəˌsɛri.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: And while hypnosis was initially considered a trance-like state,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ənd waɪl hɪpˈnoʊsəs wɑz ˌɪˈnɪʃəli kənˈsɪdərd ə trance-like* steɪt,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: it’s now understood more as a set of procedures focused on suggestions.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: it’s* naʊ ˌəndərˈstʊd mɔr ɛz ə sɛt əv prəˈsiʤərz ˈfoʊkɪst ɔn səˈʤɛsʧənz.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: No matter how informal, suggestions are natural features of doctor-patient interactions,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: noʊ ˈmætər haʊ ˌɪnˈfɔrməl, səˈʤɛsʧənz ər ˈnæʧərəl ˈfiʧərz əv doctor-patient* ˌɪnərˈækʃənz,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: and we now know they can influence a patient’s experience of their treatment.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ənd wi naʊ noʊ ðeɪ kən ˈɪnfluəns ə patient’s* ɪkˈspɪriəns əv ðɛr ˈtritmənt.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Research-backed hypnosis sessions generally begin with a hypnotic induction,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: research-backed* hɪpˈnoʊsəs ˈsɛʃənz ˈʤɛnərəli bɪˈgɪn wɪθ ə hɪpˈnɑtɪk ˌɪnˈdəkʃən,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: where a trained professional primes a patient for suggestions by drawing their attention to simple tasks,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: wɛr ə treɪnd prəˈfɛʃənəl praɪmz ə ˈpeɪʃənt fər səˈʤɛsʧənz baɪ drɔɪŋ ðɛr əˈtɛnʃən tɪ ˈsɪmpəl tæsks,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: like relaxing certain muscles.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: laɪk rɪˈlæksɪŋ ˈsərtən ˈməsəlz.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Induction is supposed to help facilitate a more receptive mindset.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˌɪnˈdəkʃən ɪz səˈpoʊzd tɪ hɛlp fəˈsɪləˌteɪt ə mɔr rɪˈsɛptɪv ˈmaɪndˌsɛt.
