JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Then, professionals make suggestions aimed at altering an aspect of the patient's perception,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðɛn, prəˈfɛʃənəlz meɪk səˈʤɛsʧənz eɪmd æt ˈɔltərɪŋ ən ˈæˌspɛkt əv ðə ˈpeɪʃənts pərˈsɛpʃən,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: often describing something as though it will happen and be passively observed.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˈɔfən dɪˈskraɪbɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ ɛz ðoʊ ɪt wɪl ˈhæpən ənd bi ˈpæsɪvli əbˈzərvd.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: The session may close with a de-induction to ensure the patient is fully alert.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðə ˈsɛʃən meɪ kloʊz wɪθ ə de-induction* tɪ ɪnˈʃʊr ðə ˈpeɪʃənt ɪz ˈfʊli əˈlərt.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Most people seem moderately responsive to hypnotic suggestion,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: moʊst ˈpipəl sim ˈmɑdərətli rɪˈspɑnsɪv tɪ hɪpˈnɑtɪk səˈʤɛsʧən,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: while small percentages are extremely responsive or unresponsive.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: waɪl smɔl pərˈsɛnɪʤɪz ər ɪkˈstrimli rɪˈspɑnsɪv ər ˌənriˈspɑnsɪv.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: A person’s hypnotizability doesn’t tend to change much over time,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ə person’s* hypnotizability* doesn’t* tɛnd tɪ ʧeɪnʤ məʧ ˈoʊvər taɪm,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: though certain drugs and brain stimulation may increase it temporarily.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðoʊ ˈsərtən drəgz ənd breɪn ˌstɪmjəˈleɪʃən meɪ ˌɪnˈkris ɪt ˌtɛmpərˈɛrəli.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: It’s unclear what determines hypnotizability, but certain traits seem to be associated.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: it’s* ənˈklɪr wət dɪˈtərmənz hypnotizability*, bət ˈsərtən treɪts sim tɪ bi əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪtəd.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Highly hypnotizable people tend to be open minded toward hypnosis,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˈhaɪli hypnotizable* ˈpipəl tɛnd tɪ bi ˈoʊpən ˈmaɪndɪd təˈwɔrd hɪpˈnoʊsəs,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: more prone to fantasy, and likely to become absorbed in activities.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: mɔr proʊn tɪ ˈfænəsi, ənd ˈlaɪkli tɪ bɪˈkəm əbˈzɔrbd ɪn ækˈtɪvɪtiz.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: They also report more unusual perceptual and dissociative experiences overall.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðeɪ ˈɔlsoʊ rɪˈpɔrt mɔr ənˈjuˌʒuəl pərˈsɛpʧəwəl ənd dissociative* ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz ˈoʊvərˌɔl.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: When a hypnotic suggestion works, the effects can be considerable— and traceable.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: wɪn ə hɪpˈnɑtɪk səˈʤɛsʧən wərks, ðə ˈifɛkts kən bi considerable—*— ənd ˈtreɪsəbəl.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: In some studies, researchers have prompted highly hypnotizable participants
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ɪn səm ˈstədiz, ˈrisərʧərz hæv ˈprɑmptɪd ˈhaɪli hypnotizable* pɑrˈtɪsəpənts

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: to perceive things that were otherwise absent.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: tɪ pərˈsiv θɪŋz ðət wər ˈəðərˌwaɪz ˈæbsənt.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: In response, the hypnotized participants’ brain activity has been shown to change
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ɪn rɪˈspɑns, ðə ˈhɪpnəˌtaɪzd participants’*’ breɪn ækˈtɪvɪti həz bɪn ʃoʊn tɪ ʧeɪnʤ

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: in ways different from what’s observed in imaginative processes,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ɪn weɪz ˈdɪfərənt frəm what’s* əbˈzərvd ɪn ˌɪˈmæʤənətɪv ˈprɑsɛsəz,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: to instead more closely resemble actual perception.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: tɪ ˌɪnˈstɛd mɔr ˈkloʊsli rɪˈzɛmbəl ˈækʧəwəl pərˈsɛpʃən.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: For those it works with, hypnosis can be a helpful medical tool, though its scope is generally limited to psychiatry.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: fər ðoʊz ɪt wərks wɪθ, hɪpˈnoʊsəs kən bi ə ˈhɛlpfəl ˈmɛdɪkəl tul, ðoʊ ɪts skoʊp ɪz ˈʤɛnərəli ˈlɪmɪtɪd tɪ sɪˈkaɪətri.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: In one study, hypnotizable patients who received pain-reducing suggestions during surgery
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ɪn wən ˈstədi, hypnotizable* ˈpeɪʃənz hu rɪˈsivd pain-reducing* səˈʤɛsʧənz ˈdʊrɪŋ ˈsərʤəri

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: reported less pain and required fewer opioids afterwards.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ˌriˈpɔrtəd lɛs peɪn ənd rikˈwaɪərd fjuər opioids* ˈæftərwərdz.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: Among patients with brain injuries, hypnosis has been observed to improve their working memory—
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: əˈməŋ ˈpeɪʃənz wɪθ breɪn ˈɪnʤəriz, hɪpˈnoʊsəs həz bɪn əbˈzərvd tɪ ˌɪmˈpruv ðɛr ˈwərkɪŋ memory—*—

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: that is, their ability to hold information like sequences of numbers for short periods.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðət ɪz, ðɛr əˈbɪləˌti tɪ hoʊld ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən laɪk ˈsikwənsɪz əv ˈnəmbərz fər ʃɔrt ˈpɪriədz.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: This might be because the suggestions somehow ease cognitive deficits
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðɪs maɪt bi bɪˈkəz ðə səˈʤɛsʧənz ˈsəmˌhaʊ iz ˈkɑgnɪtɪv ˈdɛfɪsɪts

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: that are driven by the patient’s own beliefs about their post-injury abilities.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ðət ər ˈdrɪvən baɪ ðə patient’s* oʊn bɪˈlifs əˈbaʊt ðɛr post-injury* əˈbɪləˌtiz.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: And for certain things, including quitting smoking, hypnosis seems to be similarly effective to treatments
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: ənd fər ˈsərtən θɪŋz, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ kˈwɪtɪŋ sˈmoʊkɪŋ, hɪpˈnoʊsəs simz tɪ bi ˈsɪmələrli ˈifɛktɪv tɪ ˈtritmənts

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: like counseling and exercise.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: laɪk ˈkaʊnsəlɪŋ ənd ˈɛksərˌsaɪz.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: But while hypnosis can help some people manage conditions like anxiety and depression,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: bət waɪl hɪpˈnoʊsəs kən hɛlp səm ˈpipəl ˈmænɪʤ kənˈdɪʃənz laɪk æŋˈzaɪəti ənd dɪˈprɛʃən,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: there’s little evidence it can treat things like schizophrenia.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: there’s* ˈlɪtəl ˈɛvədəns ɪt kən trit θɪŋz laɪk ˌskɪtsəˈfriniə.

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: When hypnosis does help, though, it’s certainly no thanks to magnetized fluids,
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: wɪn hɪpˈnoʊsəs dɪz hɛlp, ðoʊ, it’s* ˈsərtənli noʊ θæŋks tɪ ˈmægnɪˌtaɪzd fluɪdz,

JJdoAMiaLZo.text: but rather the mesmerizing power of the mind.
JJdoAMiaLZo.ipa: bət ˈrəðər ðə ˈmɛzmərˌaɪzɪŋ paʊər əv ðə maɪnd.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: When I was in the fifth grade, I bought an issue of DC Comics Presents number 57
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: wɪn aɪ wɑz ɪn ðə fɪθ greɪd, aɪ bɔt ən ˈɪʃu əv ˌdiˈsi ˈkɑmɪks ˈprɛzənts ˈnəmbər 57

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: off of a spinner rack at my local bookstore. And that comic book changed my life.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ɔf əv ə ˈspɪnər ræk æt maɪ ˈloʊkəl ˈbʊkˌstɔr. ənd ðət ˈkɑmɪk bʊk ʧeɪnʤd maɪ laɪf.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: I immediately fell in love with the medium of comics. But I never brought them to school.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: aɪ ˌɪˈmiˌdiətli fɛl ɪn ləv wɪθ ðə ˈmidiəm əv ˈkɑmɪks. bət aɪ ˈnɛvər brɔt ðɛm tɪ skul.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Instinctively, I knew that comic books didn’t belong in the classroom.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˌɪnˈstɪŋktɪvˌli, aɪ nu ðət ˈkɑmɪk bʊks didn’t* bɪˈlɔŋ ɪn ðə ˈklæsˌrum.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Eventually, I became a published cartoonist,
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli, aɪ bɪˈkeɪm ə ˈpəblɪʃt kɑrˈtunəst,

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: writing and drawing comic books for a living.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˈraɪtɪŋ ənd drɔɪŋ ˈkɑmɪk bʊks fər ə ˈlɪvɪŋ.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: I also became a high school teacher. This is where I taught: Bishop O’Dowd High School in Oakland, California.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: aɪ ˈɔlsoʊ bɪˈkeɪm ə haɪ skul ˈtiʧər. ðɪs ɪz wɛr aɪ tɔt: ˈbɪʃəp o’dowd* haɪ skul ɪn ˈoʊklənd, ˌkæləˈfɔrnjə.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: I remember telling my students on the first day of every class that I was also a cartoonist.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: aɪ rɪˈmɛmbər ˈtɛlɪŋ maɪ ˈstudənts ɔn ðə fərst deɪ əv ˈɛvəri klæs ðət aɪ wɑz ˈɔlsoʊ ə kɑrˈtunəst.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: I was hoping comics would make them think that I was cool.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: aɪ wɑz ˈhoʊpɪŋ ˈkɑmɪks wʊd meɪk ðɛm θɪŋk ðət aɪ wɑz kul.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: I was wrong. They thought I was kind of a dork.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: aɪ wɑz rɔŋ. ðeɪ θɔt aɪ wɑz kaɪnd əv ə dork*.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: They would use comic books as a way of distracting me. They would raise their hands and ask me questions like,
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ðeɪ wʊd juz ˈkɑmɪk bʊks ɛz ə weɪ əv dɪˈstræktɪŋ mi. ðeɪ wʊd reɪz ðɛr hænz ənd æsk mi kˈwɛsʧənz laɪk,

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: “Mr. Yang, who do you think would win in a fight: Superman or the Hulk?”
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ““mr*. jæŋ, hu du ju θɪŋk wʊd wɪn ɪn ə faɪt: ˈsupərˌmæn ər ðə hulk?”*?”

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: It seemed like my instincts in fifth grade were correct— comic books didn’t belong in the classroom— but again I was wrong.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ɪt simd laɪk maɪ ˈɪnstɪŋkts ɪn fɪθ greɪd wər correct—*— ˈkɑmɪk bʊks didn’t* bɪˈlɔŋ ɪn ðə classroom—*— bət əˈgɛn aɪ wɑz rɔŋ.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: One semester I was asked to sub for this Algebra 2 class. But there was a problem.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: wən səˈmɛstər aɪ wɑz æst tɪ səb fər ðɪs ˈælʤəbrə 2 klæs. bət ðɛr wɑz ə ˈprɑbləm.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Every couple of weeks, I had to miss 1 or 2 periods.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˈɛvəri ˈkəpəl əv wiks, aɪ hæd tɪ mɪs 1 ər 2 ˈpɪriədz.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: In an effort to provide some sort of consistency for my students, I began videotaping myself giving lectures.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ɪn ən ˈɛfərt tɪ prəˈvaɪd səm sɔrt əv kənˈsɪstənsi fər maɪ ˈstudənts, aɪ bɪˈgæn ˈvɪdioʊˌteɪpɪŋ ˌmaɪˈsɛlf ˈgɪvɪŋ ˈlɛkʧərz.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: I tried to make these videos as engaging as possible.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: aɪ traɪd tɪ meɪk ðiz ˈvɪdioʊz ɛz ɪnˈgeɪʤɪŋ ɛz ˈpɑsəbəl.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: These video lectures were a disaster. I had students coming up to me and saying things like,
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ðiz ˈvɪdioʊ ˈlɛkʧərz wər ə dɪˈzæstər. aɪ hæd ˈstudənts ˈkəmɪŋ əp tɪ mi ənd seɪɪŋ θɪŋz laɪk,

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: “Mr. Yang, we thought you were boring in person, but on video you are just unbearable.”
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ““mr*. jæŋ, wi θɔt ju wər ˈbɔrɪŋ ɪn ˈpərsən, bət ɔn ˈvɪdioʊ ju ər ʤɪst unbearable.”*.”

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: So as a desperate second attempt, I began drawing these lectures as comics.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: soʊ ɛz ə ˈdɛspərɪt ˈsɛkənd əˈtɛmpt, aɪ bɪˈgæn drɔɪŋ ðiz ˈlɛkʧərz ɛz ˈkɑmɪks.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Much to my surprise, these comics lectures were a hit. My students would ask me to make these for them,
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: məʧ tɪ maɪ səˈpraɪz, ðiz ˈkɑmɪks ˈlɛkʧərz wər ə hɪt. maɪ ˈstudənts wʊd æsk mi tɪ meɪk ðiz fər ðɛm,

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: even when I could be there in person. it was like they liked cartoon me more than actual me.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˈivɪn wɪn aɪ kʊd bi ðɛr ɪn ˈpərsən. ɪt wɑz laɪk ðeɪ laɪkt kɑrˈtun mi mɔr ðən ˈækʧəwəl mi.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: I began to understand the educational potential of comics.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: aɪ bɪˈgæn tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd ðə ˌɛʤəˈkeɪʃənəl pəˈtɛnʃəl əv ˈkɑmɪks.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Comics lectures taught visually.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˈkɑmɪks ˈlɛkʧərz tɔt ˈvɪʒwəli.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Our students grow up in a visual culture— so they’re used to taking in information that way.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ɑr ˈstudənts groʊ əp ɪn ə ˈvɪʒəwəl culture—*— soʊ they’re* juzd tɪ ˈteɪkɪŋ ɪn ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ðət weɪ.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: In a comic, past, present, and future all sit side by side on the same page.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ɪn ə ˈkɑmɪk, pæst, ˈprɛzənt, ənd fˈjuʧər ɔl sɪt saɪd baɪ saɪd ɔn ðə seɪm peɪʤ.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: This means that the rate of information flow is firmly in the hands of the reader.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ðɪs minz ðət ðə reɪt əv ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən floʊ ɪz ˈfərmli ɪn ðə hænz əv ðə ˈridər.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: When my students didn’t understand something in my comics lecture,
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: wɪn maɪ ˈstudənts didn’t* ˌəndərˈstænd ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪn maɪ ˈkɑmɪks ˈlɛkʧər,

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: they could just reread that passage as quickly or as slowly as they needed.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ðeɪ kʊd ʤɪst ˈriˈrid ðət ˈpæsɪʤ ɛz kˈwɪkli ər ɛz sˈloʊli ɛz ðeɪ ˈnidɪd.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Comic books first became a mass medium in the 1940s, with millions of copies selling every month.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˈkɑmɪk bʊks fərst bɪˈkeɪm ə mæs ˈmidiəm ɪn ðə 1940s*, wɪθ ˈmɪljənz əv ˈkɑpiz ˈsɛlɪŋ ˈɛvəri mənθ.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: A lot of innovative teachers began bringing comics into their classrooms to experiment.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ə lɔt əv ˈɪnəˌveɪtɪv ˈtiʧərz bɪˈgæn ˈbrɪŋɪŋ ˈkɑmɪks ˈɪntu ðɛr ˈklæsˌrumz tɪ ɪkˈspɛrəmənt.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: But then along comes this guy.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: bət ðɛn əˈlɔŋ kəmz ðɪs gaɪ.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: This is child psychologist Dr. Fredrick Wortham, and in 1954, he wrote a book called “Seduction of the innocent,”
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ʧaɪld saɪˈkɑləʤəst ˈdɑktər. ˈfrɛdrɪk ˈwərθəm, ənd ɪn 1954 hi roʊt ə bʊk kɔld ““seduction* əv ðə innocent,”*,”

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: where he argues that comic books cause juvenile delinquency.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: wɛr hi ˈɑrgjuz ðət ˈkɑmɪk bʊks kɔz ˈʤuvəˌnaɪl dɪˈlɪŋkwənsi.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: He was wrong. In the 1940s and 50s, almost every kid in America read comic books.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: hi wɑz rɔŋ. ɪn ðə 1940s* ənd 50s*, ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˈɛvəri kɪd ɪn əˈmɛrɪkə rɛd ˈkɑmɪk bʊks.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Dr. Wortham does a pretty dubious job of proving his case, but his book does inspire the Senate of the United States
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˈdɑktər. ˈwərθəm dɪz ə ˈprɪti ˈdubiəs ʤɑb əv ˈpruvɪŋ hɪz keɪs, bət hɪz bʊk dɪz ˌɪnˈspaɪr ðə ˈsɛnɪt əv ðə juˈnaɪtɪd steɪts

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: to hold a series of hearings to see if, in fact, comic books caused juvenile delinquency.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: tɪ hoʊld ə ˈsɪriz əv ˈhirɪŋz tɪ si ɪf, ɪn fækt, ˈkɑmɪk bʊks kɔzd ˈʤuvəˌnaɪl dɪˈlɪŋkwənsi.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: These hearings lasted for almost two months.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ðiz ˈhirɪŋz ˈlæstɪd fər ˈɔlˌmoʊst tu mənθs.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: They ended inconclusively, but not before doing tremendous damage to the reputation of comic books
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ðeɪ ˈɛndɪd ˌɪŋˈkɑnklusɪvli, bət nɑt ˌbiˈfɔr duɪŋ trɪˈmɛndəs ˈdæmɪʤ tɪ ðə ˌrɛpjəˈteɪʃən əv ˈkɑmɪk bʊks

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: in the eyes of the American public.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ɪn ðə aɪz əv ðə əˈmɛrɪkən ˈpəblɪk.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: It wasn’t until the 1970s that a few brave souls started making their way back in
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ɪt wasn’t* ənˈtɪl ðə 1970s* ðət ə fju breɪv soʊlz ˈstɑrtɪd ˈmeɪkɪŋ ðɛr weɪ bæk ɪn

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: and it really wasn’t until pretty recently— maybe the last decade or so—
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ənd ɪt ˈrɪli wasn’t* ənˈtɪl ˈprɪti recently—*— ˈmeɪbi ðə læst ˈdɛkeɪd ər so—*—

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: that comics have seen more widespread acceptance among American educators.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ðət ˈkɑmɪks hæv sin mɔr ˈwaɪdˈsprɛd əkˈsɛptəns əˈməŋ əˈmɛrɪkən ˈɛʤəˌkeɪtərz.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: And this is even happening at Bishop O’Dowd, where I used to teach. Mr. Smith, one of my former colleagues,
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ənd ðɪs ɪz ˈivɪn ˈhæpənɪŋ æt ˈbɪʃəp o’dowd*, wɛr aɪ juzd tɪ tiʧ. ˈmɪstər. smɪθ, wən əv maɪ ˈfɔrmər ˈkɑligz,

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: uses Scott McCloud’s “Understanding Comics” in his literature and film class.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˈjuzɪz skɑt mccloud’s* ““understanding* comics”*” ɪn hɪz ˈlɪtərəʧər ənd fɪlm klæs.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Mr. Burns assigns a comics essay to his students every year by asking his students to process a prose novel using images.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˈmɪstər. bərnz əˈsaɪnz ə ˈkɑmɪks ˈɛˌseɪ tɪ hɪz ˈstudənts ˈɛvəri jɪr baɪ ˈæskɪŋ hɪz ˈstudənts tɪ ˈprɔˌsɛs ə proʊz ˈnɑvəl ˈjuzɪŋ ˈɪmɪʤɪz.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: And Miss Murac uses my own “American Born Chinese” with her English one students.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ənd mɪs murac* ˈjuzɪz maɪ oʊn ““american* bɔrn chinese”*” wɪθ hər ˈɪŋlɪʃ wən ˈstudənts.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: Over in the library, Miss Counts has built a pretty impressive graphic novel collection for Bishop O’Dowd.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ˈoʊvər ɪn ðə ˈlaɪbrɛˌri, mɪs kaʊnts həz bɪlt ə ˈprɪti ˌɪmˈprɛsɪv ˈgræfɪk ˈnɑvəl kəˈlɛkʃən fər ˈbɪʃəp o’dowd*.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: A school library journal article stated that the mere presence of graphic novels
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ə skul ˈlaɪbrɛˌri ˈʤərnəl ˈɑrtɪkəl ˈsteɪtɪd ðət ðə mɪr ˈprɛzəns əv ˈgræfɪk ˈnɑvəlz

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: in a library increased usage by about 80%,
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ɪn ə ˈlaɪbrɛˌri ˌɪnˈkrist ˈjusɪʤ baɪ əˈbaʊt 80

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: and increased the circulation of non-comics material by about 30%.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ənd ˌɪnˈkrist ðə ˈsərkjəˌleɪʃən əv non-comics* məˈtɪriəl baɪ əˈbaʊt 30

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: American cartoonists are now producing more explicitly educational content for the K-12 market than ever before.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: əˈmɛrɪkən kɑrˈtunɪsts ər naʊ prəˈdusɪŋ mɔr ɪkˈsplɪsətli ˌɛʤəˈkeɪʃənəl ˈkɑntɛnt fər ðə k-12* ˈmɑrkɪt ðən ˈɛvər ˌbiˈfɔr.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: More comics and graphic novels are starting to tackle math and science topics.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: mɔr ˈkɑmɪks ənd ˈgræfɪk ˈnɑvəlz ər ˈstɑrtɪŋ tɪ ˈtækəl mæθ ənd saɪəns ˈtɑpɪks.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: STEM comics and graphic novels really are like this uncharted territory ready to be explored.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: stɛm ˈkɑmɪks ənd ˈgræfɪk ˈnɑvəlz ˈrɪli ər laɪk ðɪs ənˈʧɑrtɪd ˈtɛrɪˌtɔri ˈrɛdi tɪ bi ɪkˈsplɔrd.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: America is finally waking up to the fact that comic books really do belong in every educator’s toolkit.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: əˈmɛrɪkə ɪz ˈfaɪnəli ˈweɪkɪŋ əp tɪ ðə fækt ðət ˈkɑmɪk bʊks ˈrɪli du bɪˈlɔŋ ɪn ˈɛvəri educator’s* toolkit*.

ofk-aw5G-FQ.text: The educational potential is there just waiting to be tapped by creative people like you.
ofk-aw5G-FQ.ipa: ðə ˌɛʤəˈkeɪʃənəl pəˈtɛnʃəl ɪz ðɛr ʤɪst ˈweɪtɪŋ tɪ bi tæpt baɪ kriˈeɪtɪv ˈpipəl laɪk ju.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: If any of his wives ever birthed a son, he'd be killed immediately.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ɪf ˈɛni əv hɪz waɪvz ˈɛvər birthed* ə sən, hid bi kɪld ˌɪˈmiˌdiətli.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: That was the threat Shemwindo, ruler of the village of Tubondo, had delivered,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ðət wɑz ðə θrɛt shemwindo*, ˈrulər əv ðə ˈvɪlɪʤ əv tubondo*, hæd dɪˈlɪvərd,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: for he wanted all the power to himself.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: fər hi ˈwɔntɪd ɔl ðə paʊər tɪ hɪmˈsɛlf.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: But he was about to meet his match— and enter a rivalry that would take him to the underworld and back.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: bət hi wɑz əˈbaʊt tɪ mit hɪz match—*— ənd ˈɛnər ə ˈraɪvəlri ðət wʊd teɪk ɪm tɪ ðə ˈəndərˌwərld ənd bæk.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Six of Shemwindo’s wives had daughters, but when Nyamwindo finally went into labor,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: sɪks əv shemwindo’s* waɪvz hæd ˈdɔtərz, bət wɪn nyamwindo* ˈfaɪnəli wɛnt ˈɪntu ˈleɪbər,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: the midwives witnessed a birth unlike any they'd ever seen.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ðə ˈmɪdˌwaɪvz ˈwɪtnəst ə bərθ ənˈlaɪk ˈɛni ðeɪd ˈɛvər sin.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Nyamwindo’s baby, Mwindo, materialized from her belly button,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: nyamwindo’s* ˈbeɪbi, mwindo*, məˈtɪriəˌlaɪzd frəm hər ˈbɛli ˈbətən,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: wielding a magical conga scepter, already laughing, talking, and walking.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ˈwildɪŋ ə ˈmæʤɪkəl ˈkɔŋgə scepter*, ɔˈrɛdi ˈlæfɪŋ, ˈtɔkɪŋ, ənd ˈwɔkɪŋ.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: The women agreed to keep the birth a secret.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ðə ˈwɪmən əˈgrid tɪ kip ðə bərθ ə ˈsikrɪt.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: But a stealthy cricket saw everything and carried the news to Shemwindo.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: bət ə ˈstɛlθi ˈkrɪkɪt sɔ ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ənd ˈkɛrid ðə nuz tɪ shemwindo*.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Outraged, he hurled spears at his newborn, but it was like they couldn't touch him.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ˈaʊˌtreɪʤd, hi hərld spɪrz æt hɪz ˈnubɔrn, bət ɪt wɑz laɪk ðeɪ ˈkʊdənt təʧ ɪm.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: The tyrannical chief ordered his soldiers to construct a wooden drum,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ðə tərˈænɪkəl ʧif ˈɔrdərd hɪz ˈsoʊlʤərz tɪ ˈkɑnstrəkt ə ˈwʊdən drəm,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: wrestle Mwindo into it, and toss it into the river.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ˈrɛsəl mwindo* ˈɪntu ɪt, ənd tɔs ɪt ˈɪntu ðə ˈrɪvər.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: From inside, Mwindo cursed his father, vowing to return.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: frəm ˌɪnˈsaɪd, mwindo* kərst hɪz ˈfɑðər, vaʊɪŋ tɪ rɪˈtərn.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Once he was out of sight, Mwindo raised the drum to the water's surface and made for safer shores,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: wəns hi wɑz aʊt əv saɪt, mwindo* reɪzd ðə drəm tɪ ðə ˈwɔtərz ˈsərfəs ənd meɪd fər ˈseɪfər ʃɔrz,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: where his paternal aunt, Iyangura, lived, wedded to a water serpent.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: wɛr hɪz pəˈtərnəl ɔnt, iyangura*, lɪvd, ˈwɛdɪd tɪ ə ˈwɔtər ˈsərpənt.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: She was far wiser than her brother.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ʃi wɑz fɑr ˈwaɪzər ðən hər ˈbrəðər.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: And as Mwindo laid out his plan for revenge, she resolved to counsel him on his journey.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd ɛz mwindo* leɪd aʊt hɪz plæn fər riˈvɛnʤ, ʃi riˈzɑlvd tɪ ˈkaʊnsəl ɪm ɔn hɪz ˈʤərni.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: But they wouldn’t make the trek alone:
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: bət ðeɪ wouldn’t* meɪk ðə trɛk əˈloʊn:

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: throughout their quest, they’d be accompanied by different friends and foes.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: θruaʊt ðɛr kwɛst, they’d* bi əˈkəmpənid baɪ ˈdɪfərənt frɛndz ənd foʊz.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: By the time they reached Tubondo, Shemwindo had already fled.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: baɪ ðə taɪm ðeɪ riʧt tubondo*, shemwindo* hæd ɔˈrɛdi flɛd.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: And though Iyangura advised Mwindo to be merciful, his rage led him instead,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd ðoʊ iyangura* ədˈvaɪzd mwindo* tɪ bi ˈmərsɪfəl, hɪz reɪʤ lɛd ɪm ˌɪnˈstɛd,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: and he summoned lightning that decimated the village.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd hi ˈsəmənd ˈlaɪtnɪŋ ðət ˈdɛsəˌmeɪtɪd ðə ˈvɪlɪʤ.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: In the midst of the chaos, an uprooted kikoka plant caught Iyangura’s eye,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ɪn ðə mɪst əv ðə keɪɑs, ən əˈprutɪd kikoka* plænt kɔt iyangura’s* aɪ,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: and she realized that her brother had used the plant’s roots to escape into the underworld.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd ʃi ˈriəˌlaɪzd ðət hər ˈbrəðər hæd juzd ðə plant’s* ruts tɪ ɪˈskeɪp ˈɪntu ðə ˈəndərˌwərld.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Iyangura held fast to one end of a great rope,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: iyangura* hɛld fæst tɪ wən ɛnd əv ə greɪt roʊp,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: and Mwindo descended into the frigid, subterranean realm after his father.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd mwindo* dɪˈsɛndɪd ˈɪntu ðə ˈfrɪʤəd, səbtərˈeɪniən rɛlm ˈæftər hɪz ˈfɑðər.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: First, Mwindo reached the lair of Muisa, god of the underworld. With a glint of cruel mischief,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: fərst, mwindo* riʧt ðə lɛr əv muisa*, gɑd əv ðə ˈəndərˌwərld. wɪθ ə glɪnt əv kruəl ˈmɪsʧəf,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Muisa said he’d reveal Shemwindo’s hiding place—
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: muisa* sɛd he’d* rɪˈvil shemwindo’s* ˈhaɪdɪŋ place—*—

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: only if Mwindo planted thousands of banana trees on an arid mountainside.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ˈoʊnli ɪf mwindo* ˈplænɪd ˈθaʊzənz əv bəˈnænə triz ɔn ən ˈɛrəd ˈmaʊntənˌsaɪd.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Unintimidated, Mwindo bewitched countless axes into tilling the soil.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: unintimidated*, mwindo* bɪˈwɪʧt ˈkaʊntləs ˈækˌsɪz ˈɪntu ˈtɪlɪŋ ðə sɔɪl.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: But before he could celebrate his victory, Muisa retaliated, sending an enchanted weapon to pin him down.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: bət ˌbiˈfɔr hi kʊd ˈsɛləˌbreɪt hɪz ˈvɪktəri, muisa* rɪˈtæliˌeɪtɪd, ˈsɛndɪŋ ən ɛnˈʧænɪd ˈwɛpən tɪ pɪn ɪm daʊn.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: In turn, Mwindo ordered his scepter to strike back,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ɪn tərn, mwindo* ˈɔrdərd hɪz scepter* tɪ straɪk bæk,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: and Muisa lay defeated.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd muisa* leɪ dɪˈfitɪd.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: But Mwindo was no closer to finding his father—
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: bət mwindo* wɑz noʊ ˈkloʊzər tɪ ˈfaɪndɪŋ hɪz father—*—

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: until Hawk appeared and divulged his whereabouts:
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənˈtɪl hɔk əˈpɪrd ənd dɪˈvəlʤd hɪz ˈwɛrəˌbaʊts:

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: a dark cave guarded by Ntumba, a fearsome aardvark.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ə dɑrk keɪv ˈgɑrdɪd baɪ ntumba*, ə ˈfɪrsəm ˈɑrdˌvɑrk.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: But before Mwindo defeated the beast, Shemwindo fled again,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: bət ˌbiˈfɔr mwindo* dɪˈfitɪd ðə bist, shemwindo* flɛd əˈgɛn,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: and the chase persisted through the depths of the underworld.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd ðə ʧeɪs pərˈsɪstɪd θru ðə dɛpθs əv ðə ˈəndərˌwərld.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Soon, Sheburungu, the supreme god of fire,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: sun, sheburungu*, ðə səˈprim gɑd əv faɪər,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: materialized before Mwindo and challenged him to a betting game.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: məˈtɪriəˌlaɪzd ˌbiˈfɔr mwindo* ənd ˈʧælənʤd ɪm tɪ ə ˈbɛtɪŋ geɪm.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Round after round, Mwindo gambled everything— Tubondo’s land, people, and property— and lost.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: raʊnd ˈæftər raʊnd, mwindo* ˈgæmbəld everything—*— tubondo’s* lænd, ˈpipəl, ənd property—*— ənd lɔst.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: When he thought he had nothing left to wager,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: wɪn hi θɔt hi hæd ˈnəθɪŋ lɛft tɪ ˈweɪʤər,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Mwindo realized these things weren't his to give.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: mwindo* ˈriəˌlaɪzd ðiz θɪŋz wərənt hɪz tɪ gɪv.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: The next round, he pledged his own cherished conga scepter.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ðə nɛkst raʊnd, hi plɛʤd hɪz oʊn ˈʧɛrɪʃt ˈkɔŋgə scepter*.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: And it was this sacrifice that turned his luck and won him the game.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd ɪt wɑz ðɪs ˈsækrəˌfaɪs ðət tərnd hɪz lək ənd wən ɪm ðə geɪm.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: In the same moment, Hawk and Sparrow sounded the alarm,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ɪn ðə seɪm ˈmoʊmənt, hɔk ənd ˈspɛroʊ ˈsaʊndɪd ðə əˈlɑrm,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: warning Mwindo that his father was attempting to escape again.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ˈwɔrnɪŋ mwindo* ðət hɪz ˈfɑðər wɑz əˈtɛmptɪŋ tɪ ɪˈskeɪp əˈgɛn.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: But this time, Mwindo managed to capture the runaway ruler.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: bət ðɪs taɪm, mwindo* ˈmænɪʤd tɪ ˈkæpʧər ðə ˈrənəˌweɪ ˈrulər.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: And yet, while Mwindo had thought that seeing his father begging for mercy would delight him,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd jɛt, waɪl mwindo* hæd θɔt ðət siɪŋ hɪz ˈfɑðər ˈbɛgɪŋ fər ˈmərsi wʊd dɪˈlaɪt ɪm,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: his thirst for revenge had all but evaporated.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: hɪz θərst fər riˈvɛnʤ hæd ɔl bət ɪˈvæpərˌeɪtɪd.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: He remembered his aunt's advice, and all the people of Tubondo who’d long endured his father’s cruelty,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: hi rɪˈmɛmbərd hɪz ɔnts ədˈvaɪs, ənd ɔl ðə ˈpipəl əv tubondo* who’d* lɔŋ ɪnˈdʊrd hɪz father’s* ˈkruəlti,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: then his own.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ðɛn hɪz oʊn.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Shemwindo in tow, Mwindo retraced his path with a new resolve.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: shemwindo* ɪn toʊ, mwindo* riˈtreɪst hɪz pæθ wɪθ ə nu riˈzɑlv.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: First he yielded all that Sheburungu lost in their gambling game, receiving his blessing in return.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: fərst hi ˈjildɪd ɔl ðət sheburungu* lɔst ɪn ðɛr ˈgæmbəlɪŋ geɪm, rɪˈsivɪŋ hɪz ˈblɛsɪŋ ɪn rɪˈtərn.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Then, upon seeing that Ntumba had reconstructed the cave he destroyed,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ðɛn, əˈpɑn siɪŋ ðət ntumba* hæd ˌrikənˈstrəktɪd ðə keɪv hi dɪˈstrɔɪd,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Mwindo offered his sympathy and earned Ntumba’s well wishes too.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: mwindo* ˈɔfərd hɪz ˈsɪmpəθi ənd ərnd ntumba’s* wɛl ˈwɪʃɪz tu.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Mwindo and his father reemerged from the kikoka root transformed.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: mwindo* ənd hɪz ˈfɑðər riɪˈmərʤd frəm ðə kikoka* rut trænsˈfɔrmd.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: Mwindo had won the gods’ respect and Shemwindo’s atonement.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: mwindo* hæd wən ðə gods’*’ rɪˈspɛkt ənd shemwindo’s* əˈtoʊnmənt.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: With his aunt's guidance, he restored the village and pardoned his father,
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: wɪθ hɪz ɔnts ˈgaɪdəns, hi rɪˈstɔrd ðə ˈvɪlɪʤ ənd ˈpɑrdənd hɪz ˈfɑðər,

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: who officially apologized to Tubondo.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: hu əˈfɪʃəli əˈpɑləˌʤaɪzd tɪ tubondo*.

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: And over time, Mwindo gained the people’s trust
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ənd ˈoʊvər taɪm, mwindo* geɪnd ðə people’s* trəst

QFbQkZT1EEk.text: as a kind, just leader who ended age-old cycles of despotism.
QFbQkZT1EEk.ipa: ɛz ə kaɪnd, ʤɪst ˈlidər hu ˈɛndɪd age-old* ˈsaɪkəlz əv ˈdɛspəˌtɪzəm.

lEXBxijQREo.text: Picture warm, gooey cookies, crunchy candies, velvety cakes, waffle cones piled high with ice cream.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ˈpɪkʧər wɔrm, gui ˈkʊkiz, ˈkrənʧi ˈkændiz, ˈvɛlvəti keɪks, ˈwɑfəl koʊnz paɪld haɪ wɪθ aɪs krim.

lEXBxijQREo.text: Is your mouth watering? Are you craving dessert? Why?
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ɪz jʊr maʊθ ˈwɔtərɪŋ? ər ju ˈkreɪvɪŋ dɪˈzərt? waɪ?

lEXBxijQREo.text: What happens in the brain that makes sugary foods so hard to resist?
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: wət ˈhæpənz ɪn ðə breɪn ðət meɪks ˈʃʊgəri fudz soʊ hɑrd tɪ rɪˈzɪst?

lEXBxijQREo.text: Sugar is a general term used to describe a class of molecules called carbohydrates,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ˈʃʊgər ɪz ə ˈʤɛnərəl tərm juzd tɪ dɪˈskraɪb ə klæs əv ˈmɑləˌkjulz kɔld ˌkɑrboʊˈhaɪdreɪts,

lEXBxijQREo.text: and it's found in a wide variety of food and drink. Just check the labels on sweet products you buy.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ənd ɪts faʊnd ɪn ə waɪd vərˈaɪəti əv fud ənd drɪŋk. ʤɪst ʧɛk ðə ˈleɪbəlz ɔn swit ˈprɑdəkts ju baɪ.

lEXBxijQREo.text: Glucose, fructose, sucrose, maltose, lactose, dextrose, and starch are all forms of sugar.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ˈgluˌkoʊs, ˈfrəkˌtoʊs, ˈsukroʊs, ˈmɔltoʊs, ˈlæktoʊs, ˈdɛkstroʊs, ənd stɑrʧ ər ɔl fɔrmz əv ˈʃʊgər.

lEXBxijQREo.text: So are high-fructose corn syrup, fruit juice, raw sugar, and honey.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: soʊ ər high-fructose* kɔrn ˈsɪrəp, frut ʤus, rɑ ˈʃʊgər, ənd ˈhəni.

lEXBxijQREo.text: And sugar isn't just in candies and desserts, it's also added to tomato sauce,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ənd ˈʃʊgər ˈɪzənt ʤɪst ɪn ˈkændiz ənd dɪˈzərts, ɪts ˈɔlsoʊ ˈædɪd tɪ təˈmɑˌtoʊ sɔs,

lEXBxijQREo.text: yogurt, dried fruit, flavored waters, or granola bars. Since sugar is everywhere, it's important to understand
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ˈjoʊgərt, draɪd frut, ˈfleɪvərd ˈwɔtərz, ər grəˈnoʊlə bɑrz. sɪns ˈʃʊgər ɪz ˈɛvriˌwɛr, ɪts ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd

lEXBxijQREo.text: how it affects the brain. What happens when sugar hits your tongue?
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: haʊ ɪt əˈfɛkts ðə breɪn. wət ˈhæpənz wɪn ˈʃʊgər hɪts jʊr təŋ?

lEXBxijQREo.text: And does eating a little bit of sugar make you crave more?
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ənd dɪz ˈitɪŋ ə ˈlɪtəl bɪt əv ˈʃʊgər meɪk ju kreɪv mɔr?

lEXBxijQREo.text: You take a bite of cereal. The sugars it contains activate the sweet-taste receptors, part of the taste buds on the tongue.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ju teɪk ə baɪt əv ˈsɪriəl. ðə ˈʃʊgərz ɪt kənˈteɪnz ˈæktəˌveɪt ðə sweet-taste* rəˈsɛptərz, pɑrt əv ðə teɪst bədz ɔn ðə təŋ.

lEXBxijQREo.text: These receptors send a signal up to the brain stem, and from there, it forks off into many areas of the forebrain,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ðiz rəˈsɛptərz sɛnd ə ˈsɪgnəl əp tɪ ðə breɪn stɛm, ənd frəm ðɛr, ɪt fɔrks ɔf ˈɪntu ˈmɛni ˈɛriəz əv ðə ˈfɔrˌbreɪn,

lEXBxijQREo.text: one of which is the cerebral cortex. Different sections of the cerebral cortex process different tastes:
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: wən əv wɪʧ ɪz ðə ˈsɛrəbrəl ˈkɔrtɛks. ˈdɪfərənt ˈsɛkʃənz əv ðə ˈsɛrəbrəl ˈkɔrtɛks ˈprɔˌsɛs ˈdɪfərənt teɪsts:

lEXBxijQREo.text: bitter, salty, umami, and, in our case, sweet. From here, the signal activates the brain's reward system.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ˈbɪtər, ˈsɔlti, umami*, ənd, ɪn ɑr keɪs, swit. frəm hir, ðə ˈsɪgnəl ˈæktəˌveɪts ðə breɪnz rɪˈwɔrd ˈsɪstəm.

lEXBxijQREo.text: This reward system is a series of electrical and chemical pathways across several different regions of the brain. It's a complicated network,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ðɪs rɪˈwɔrd ˈsɪstəm ɪz ə ˈsɪriz əv ɪˈlɛktrɪkəl ənd ˈkɛmɪkəl ˈpæθˌweɪz əˈkrɔs ˈsɛvərəl ˈdɪfərənt ˈriʤənz əv ðə breɪn. ɪts ə ˈkɑmpləˌkeɪtəd ˈnɛtˌwərk,

lEXBxijQREo.text: but it helps answer a single, subconscious question: should I do that again?
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: bət ɪt hɛlps ˈænsər ə ˈsɪŋgəl, səbˈkɑnʃəs kˈwɛʃən: ʃʊd aɪ du ðət əˈgɛn?

lEXBxijQREo.text: That warm, fuzzy feeling you get when you taste Grandma's chocolate cake? That's your reward system saying, "Mmm, yes!"
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ðət wɔrm, ˈfəzi ˈfilɪŋ ju gɪt wɪn ju teɪst ˈgrændˌmɑz ˈʧɔklət keɪk? ðæts jʊr rɪˈwɔrd ˈsɪstəm seɪɪŋ, "mmm*, jɛs!"

lEXBxijQREo.text: And it's not just activated by food. Socializing, sexual behavior, and drugs are just a few examples of things and experiences
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ənd ɪts nɑt ʤɪst ˈæktɪˌveɪtɪd baɪ fud. ˈsoʊʃəˌlaɪzɪŋ, ˈsɛkʃuəl bɪˈheɪvjər, ənd drəgz ər ʤɪst ə fju ɪgˈzæmpəlz əv θɪŋz ənd ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz

lEXBxijQREo.text: that also activate the reward system. But overactivating this reward system kickstarts a series of unfortunate events:
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ðət ˈɔlsoʊ ˈæktəˌveɪt ðə rɪˈwɔrd ˈsɪstəm. bət overactivating* ðɪs rɪˈwɔrd ˈsɪstəm kickstarts* ə ˈsɪriz əv ənˈfɔrʧənət ɪˈvɛnts:

lEXBxijQREo.text: loss of control, craving, and increased tolerance to sugar.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: lɔs əv kənˈtroʊl, ˈkreɪvɪŋ, ənd ˌɪnˈkrist ˈtɑlərəns tɪ ˈʃʊgər.

lEXBxijQREo.text: Let's get back to our bite of cereal. It travels down into your stomach and eventually into your gut. And guess what?
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: lɛts gɪt bæk tɪ ɑr baɪt əv ˈsɪriəl. ɪt ˈtrævəlz daʊn ˈɪntu jʊr ˈstəmək ənd ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli ˈɪntu jʊr gət. ənd gɛs wət?

lEXBxijQREo.text: There are sugar receptors here, too. They are not taste buds, but they do send signals telling your brain that you're full
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ðɛr ər ˈʃʊgər rəˈsɛptərz hir, tu. ðeɪ ər nɑt teɪst bədz, bət ðeɪ du sɛnd ˈsɪgnəlz ˈtɛlɪŋ jʊr breɪn ðət jʊr fʊl

lEXBxijQREo.text: or that your body should produce more insulin to deal with the extra sugar you're eating.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ər ðət jʊr ˈbɑdi ʃʊd ˈproʊdus mɔr ˈɪnsələn tɪ dil wɪθ ðə ˈɛkstrə ˈʃʊgər jʊr ˈitɪŋ.

lEXBxijQREo.text: The major currency of our reward system is dopamine, an important chemical or neurotransmitter.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ðə ˈmeɪʤər ˈkərənsi əv ɑr rɪˈwɔrd ˈsɪstəm ɪz ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn, ən ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ˈkɛmɪkəl ər neurotransmitter*.

lEXBxijQREo.text: There are many dopamine receptors in the forebrain, but they're not evenly distributed. Certain areas contain dense clusters of receptors,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ðɛr ər ˈmɛni ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn rəˈsɛptərz ɪn ðə ˈfɔrˌbreɪn, bət ðɛr nɑt ˈivənli dɪˈstrɪbjətəd. ˈsərtən ˈɛriəz kənˈteɪn dɛns ˈkləstərz əv rəˈsɛptərz,

lEXBxijQREo.text: and these dopamine hot spots are a part of our reward system.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ənd ðiz ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn hɑt spɑts ər ə pɑrt əv ɑr rɪˈwɔrd ˈsɪstəm.

lEXBxijQREo.text: Drugs like alcohol, nicotine, or heroin send dopamine into overdrive, leading some people to constantly seek that high,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: drəgz laɪk ˈælkəˌhɑl, ˈnɪkəˌtin, ər ˈhɛroʊən sɛnd ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn ˈɪntu ˈoʊvərˌdraɪv, ˈlidɪŋ səm ˈpipəl tɪ ˈkɑnstəntli sik ðət haɪ,

lEXBxijQREo.text: in other words, to be addicted.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ɪn ˈəðər wərdz, tɪ bi əˈdɪktɪd.

lEXBxijQREo.text: Sugar also causes dopamine to be released, though not as violently as drugs.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ˈʃʊgər ˈɔlsoʊ ˈkɔzɪz ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn tɪ bi riˈlist, ðoʊ nɑt ɛz ˈvaɪələntli ɛz drəgz.

lEXBxijQREo.text: And sugar is rare among dopamine-inducing foods. Broccoli, for example, has no effect, which probably explains
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ənd ˈʃʊgər ɪz rɛr əˈməŋ dopamine-inducing* fudz. ˈbrɑkəli, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, həz noʊ ˈifɛkt, wɪʧ ˈprɑbəˌbli ɪkˈspleɪnz

lEXBxijQREo.text: why it's so hard to get kids to eat their veggies.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: waɪ ɪts soʊ hɑrd tɪ gɪt kɪdz tɪ it ðɛr ˈvɛʤiz.

lEXBxijQREo.text: Speaking of healthy foods, let's say you're hungry and decide to eat a balanced meal.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ˈspikɪŋ əv ˈhɛlθi fudz, lɛts seɪ jʊr ˈhəŋgri ənd ˌdɪˈsaɪd tɪ it ə ˈbælənst mil.

lEXBxijQREo.text: You do, and dopamine levels spike in the reward system hot spots.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ju du, ənd ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn ˈlɛvəlz spaɪk ɪn ðə rɪˈwɔrd ˈsɪstəm hɑt spɑts.

lEXBxijQREo.text: But if you eat that same dish many days in a row, dopamine levels will spike less and less, eventually leveling out.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: bət ɪf ju it ðət seɪm dɪʃ ˈmɛni deɪz ɪn ə roʊ, ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn ˈlɛvəlz wɪl spaɪk lɛs ənd lɛs, ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli ˈlɛvəlɪŋ aʊt.

lEXBxijQREo.text: That's because when it comes to food, the brain evolved to pay special attention to new or different tastes. Why?
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ðæts bɪˈkəz wɪn ɪt kəmz tɪ fud, ðə breɪn ɪˈvɑlvd tɪ peɪ ˈspɛʃəl əˈtɛnʃən tɪ nu ər ˈdɪfərənt teɪsts. waɪ?

lEXBxijQREo.text: Two reasons: first, to detect food that's gone bad. And second, because the more variety we have in our diet,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: tu ˈrizənz: fərst, tɪ dɪˈtɛkt fud ðæts gɔn bæd. ənd ˈsɛkənd, bɪˈkəz ðə mɔr vərˈaɪəti wi hæv ɪn ɑr daɪət,

lEXBxijQREo.text: the more likely we are to get all the nutrients we need. To keep that variety up,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ðə mɔr ˈlaɪkli wi ər tɪ gɪt ɔl ðə ˈnutriənts wi nid. tɪ kip ðət vərˈaɪəti əp,

lEXBxijQREo.text: we need to be able to recognize a new food, and more importantly, we need to want to keep eating new foods.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: wi nid tɪ bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪz ə nu fud, ənd mɔr ˌɪmˈpɔrtəntli, wi nid tɪ wɔnt tɪ kip ˈitɪŋ nu fudz.

lEXBxijQREo.text: And that's why the dopamine levels off when a food becomes boring. Now, back to that meal.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ənd ðæts waɪ ðə ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn ˈlɛvəlz ɔf wɪn ə fud bɪˈkəmz ˈbɔrɪŋ. naʊ, bæk tɪ ðət mil.

lEXBxijQREo.text: What happens if in place of the healthy, balanced dish, you eat sugar-rich food instead?
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: wət ˈhæpənz ɪf ɪn pleɪs əv ðə ˈhɛlθi, ˈbælənst dɪʃ, ju it sugar-rich* fud ˌɪnˈstɛd?

lEXBxijQREo.text: If you rarely eat sugar or don't eat much at a time, the effect is similar to that of the balanced meal.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ɪf ju ˈrɛrli it ˈʃʊgər ər doʊnt it məʧ æt ə taɪm, ðə ˈifɛkt ɪz ˈsɪmələr tɪ ðət əv ðə ˈbælənst mil.

lEXBxijQREo.text: But if you eat too much, the dopamine response does not level out.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: bət ɪf ju it tu məʧ, ðə ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn rɪˈspɑns dɪz nɑt ˈlɛvəl aʊt.

lEXBxijQREo.text: In other words, eating lots of sugar will continue to feel rewarding.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ɪn ˈəðər wərdz, ˈitɪŋ lɑts əv ˈʃʊgər wɪl kənˈtɪnju tɪ fil rɪˈwɔrdɪŋ.

lEXBxijQREo.text: In this way, sugar behaves a little bit like a drug. It's one reason people seem to be hooked on sugary foods.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ɪn ðɪs weɪ, ˈʃʊgər bɪˈheɪvz ə ˈlɪtəl bɪt laɪk ə drəg. ɪts wən ˈrizən ˈpipəl sim tɪ bi hʊkt ɔn ˈʃʊgəri fudz.

lEXBxijQREo.text: So, think back to all those different kinds of sugar. Each one is unique, but every time any sugar is consumed,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: soʊ, θɪŋk bæk tɪ ɔl ðoʊz ˈdɪfərənt kaɪnz əv ˈʃʊgər. iʧ wən ɪz juˈnik, bət ˈɛvəri taɪm ˈɛni ˈʃʊgər ɪz kənˈsumd,

lEXBxijQREo.text: it kickstarts a domino effect in the brain that sparks a rewarding feeling.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: ɪt kickstarts* ə ˈdɑmɪˌnoʊ ˈifɛkt ɪn ðə breɪn ðət spɑrks ə rɪˈwɔrdɪŋ ˈfilɪŋ.

lEXBxijQREo.text: Too much, too often, and things can go into overdrive. So, yes, overconsumption of sugar can have addictive effects on the brain,
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: tu məʧ, tu ˈɔfən, ənd θɪŋz kən goʊ ˈɪntu ˈoʊvərˌdraɪv. soʊ, jɛs, ˈoʊvərkənˌsəmpʃən əv ˈʃʊgər kən hæv əˈdɪktɪv ˈifɛkts ɔn ðə breɪn,

lEXBxijQREo.text: but a wedge of cake once in a while won't hurt you.
lEXBxijQREo.ipa: bət ə wɛʤ əv keɪk wəns ɪn ə waɪl woʊnt hərt ju.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: After waking up alone in a locked room, two documents are slipped under your door:
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ˈæftər ˈweɪkɪŋ əp əˈloʊn ɪn ə lɑkt rum, tu ˈdɑkjəmənts ər slɪpt ˈəndər jʊr dɔr:

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: a note in an alien language and a detailed instruction manual in your language.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ə noʊt ɪn ən ˈeɪliən ˈlæŋgwɪʤ ənd ə dɪˈteɪld ˌɪnˈstrəkʃən ˈmænjuəl ɪn jʊr ˈlæŋgwɪʤ.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: The manual explains that for each alien character in the note,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðə ˈmænjuəl ɪkˈspleɪnz ðət fər iʧ ˈeɪliən ˈkɛrɪktər ɪn ðə noʊt,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: you should write an indicated corresponding symbol.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ju ʃʊd raɪt ən ˈɪndəˌkeɪtɪd ˌkɔrəˈspɑndɪŋ ˈsɪmbəl.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Following this chart, you write a response that you slip out the door.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ðɪs ʧɑrt, ju raɪt ə rɪˈspɑns ðət ju slɪp aʊt ðə dɔr.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: And for the next several days, this exchange continues. Outside the room,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ənd fər ðə nɛkst ˈsɛvərəl deɪz, ðɪs ɪksˈʧeɪnʤ kənˈtɪnjuz. ˈaʊtˈsaɪd ðə rum,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: alien scientists are thrilled because they believe you’re conversing with them.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ˈeɪliən ˈsaɪəntɪsts ər θrɪld bɪˈkəz ðeɪ bɪˈliv you’re* kənˈvərsɪŋ wɪθ ðɛm.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: But you still have no idea what these characters mean.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bət ju stɪl hæv noʊ aɪˈdiə wət ðiz ˈkɛrɪktərz min.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: This scenario isn’t just a bizarre misunderstanding—
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðɪs sɪˈnɛrioʊ isn’t* ʤɪst ə bɪˈzɑr misunderstanding—*—

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: it’s a valuable thought experiment for understanding artificial intelligence.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: it’s* ə ˈvæljəbəl θɔt ɪkˈspɛrəmənt fər ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ ˌɑrtəˈfɪʃəl ˌɪnˈtɛləʤəns.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Philosopher John Searle developed the original version of this premise in 1980,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: fəˈlɑsəfər ʤɑn sərl dɪˈvɛləpt ðə ərˈɪʤənəl ˈvərʒən əv ðɪs ˈprɛmɪs ɪn 1980

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: as a response to some of the AI work being done at the time.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ɛz ə rɪˈspɑns tɪ səm əv ðə eɪaɪ wərk biɪŋ dən æt ðə taɪm.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: But while modern AI models don't work like those outdated machines or the prisoner in Searle’s hypothetical,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bət waɪl ˈmɑdərn eɪaɪ ˈmɑdəlz doʊnt wərk laɪk ðoʊz ˈaʊtˌdeɪtɪd məˈʃinz ər ðə ˈprɪzənər ɪn searle’s* ˌhaɪpəˈθɛtɪkəl,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: the question motivating his thought experiment is still relevant.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðə kˈwɛʃən ˈmoʊtəˌveɪtɪŋ hɪz θɔt ɪkˈspɛrəmənt ɪz stɪl ˈrɛləvənt.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: To quote Searle, he wanted to interrogate whether an
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: tɪ kwoʊt sərl, hi ˈwɔntɪd tɪ ˌɪnˈtɛrəˌgeɪt ˈwɛðər ən

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: “appropriately programmed computer literally has cognitive states.”
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ““appropriately* ˈproʊˌgræmd kəmˈpjutər ˈlɪtərəli həz ˈkɑgnɪtɪv states.”*.”

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: In other words, if a computer looks like it understands something,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ɪn ˈəðər wərdz, ɪf ə kəmˈpjutər lʊks laɪk ɪt ˌəndərˈstændz ˈsəmθɪŋ,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: does that mean it actually understands the way a human does?
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: dɪz ðət min ɪt ˈæˌkʧuəli ˌəndərˈstændz ðə weɪ ə ˈjumən dɪz?

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Searle’s question falls into a long tradition of exploring whether or not
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: searle’s* kˈwɛʃən fɔlz ˈɪntu ə lɔŋ trəˈdɪʃən əv ɪkˈsplɔrɪŋ ˈwɛðər ər nɑt

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: AI could have a mind like ours.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: eɪaɪ kʊd hæv ə maɪnd laɪk ɑrz.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: But answering these inquiries is incredibly difficult because, as philosophers and cognitive scientists will tell you,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bət ˈænsərɪŋ ðiz ˌɪnkˈwaɪəriz ɪz ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli ˈdɪfəkəlt bɪˈkəz, ɛz fəˈlɑsəfərz ənd ˈkɑgnɪtɪv ˈsaɪəntɪsts wɪl tɛl ju,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: we still don’t know how our minds work.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: wi stɪl don’t* noʊ haʊ ɑr maɪndz wərk.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Even our fundamental definitions are slippery!
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ˈivɪn ɑr ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl ˌdɛfəˈnɪʃənz ər sˈlɪpəri!

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Theorists generally agree that concepts like understanding, sentience, and consciousness are all different,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: θiərɪsts ˈʤɛnərəli əˈgri ðət ˈkɑnsɛpts laɪk ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ, sentience*, ənd ˈkɑnʃəsnəs ər ɔl ˈdɪfərənt,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: but also that they’re related, and we don’t know how.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bət ˈɔlsoʊ ðət they’re* rɪˈleɪtɪd, ənd wi don’t* noʊ haʊ.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Worse still, our usual scientific tools struggle to help us understand these experiences.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: wərs stɪl, ɑr ˈjuʒəwəl ˌsaɪənˈtɪfɪk tulz ˈstrəgəl tɪ hɛlp ˈjuˈɛs ˌəndərˈstænd ðiz ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Consider drinking a cup of coffee.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: kənˈsɪdər ˈdrɪŋkɪŋ ə kəp əv ˈkɔfi.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Scientists can observe the physical process of ingesting the coffee,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ˈsaɪəntɪsts kən əbˈzərv ðə ˈfɪzɪkəl ˈprɔˌsɛs əv ˌɪnˈʤɛstɪŋ ðə ˈkɔfi,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: and we can measure the chemical impacts of caffeine on your body.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ənd wi kən ˈmɛʒər ðə ˈkɛmɪkəl ˌɪmˈpækts əv kæˈfin ɔn jʊr ˈbɑdi.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: These things are objective realities.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðiz θɪŋz ər əˈbʤɛktɪv ˌriˈæləˌtiz.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: But your collective sensation of smelling, sipping, evaluating, and experiencing
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bət jʊr kəˈlɛktɪv sɛnˈseɪʃən əv sˈmɛlɪŋ, ˈsɪpɪŋ, ɪˈvæljuˌeɪtɪŋ, ənd ɪkˈspɪriənsɪŋ

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: a morning routine is more than the sum of its parts.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ə ˈmɔrnɪŋ ruˈtin ɪz mɔr ðən ðə səm əv ɪts pɑrts.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: This is consciousness— your subjective experience of being alive.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðɪs ɪz consciousness—*— jʊr səˈbʤɛktɪv ɪkˈspɪriəns əv biɪŋ əˈlaɪv.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: And despite major leaps in psychology, cognitive science, and neurology,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ənd dɪˈspaɪt ˈmeɪʤər lips ɪn saɪˈkɑləʤi, ˈkɑgnɪtɪv saɪəns, ənd nʊˈrɑləʤi,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: researchers still don’t know how various firing neurons bring about this experience.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ˈrisərʧərz stɪl don’t* noʊ haʊ ˈvɛriəs ˈfaɪərrɪŋ ˈnʊrɑnz brɪŋ əˈbaʊt ðɪs ɪkˈspɪriəns.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: So if we can't define consciousness and understanding or identify what's uniquely human about them,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: soʊ ɪf wi kænt dɪˈfaɪn ˈkɑnʃəsnəs ənd ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ ər aɪˈdɛntəˌfaɪ wəts juˈnikli ˈjumən əˈbaʊt ðɛm,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: how can we possibly test for these states in computers?
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: haʊ kən wi ˈpɑsəbli tɛst fər ðiz steɪts ɪn kəmˈpjutərz?

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Assessments like the Turing Test propose that if a human can't tell they're conversing with a computer,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: əˈsɛsmənts laɪk ðə ˈtʊrɪŋ tɛst prəˈpoʊz ðət ɪf ə ˈjumən kænt tɛl ðɛr kənˈvərsɪŋ wɪθ ə kəmˈpjutər,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: that computer could be seen as having some internal cognition.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðət kəmˈpjutər kʊd bi sin ɛz ˈhævɪŋ səm ˌɪnˈtərnəl kɑgˈnɪʃən.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: But this scenario is exactly what Searle was criticizing!
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bət ðɪs sɪˈnɛrioʊ ɪz ɪgˈzæktli wət sərl wɑz ˈkrɪtɪˌsaɪzɪŋ!

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: This computer might just have the appearance of understanding.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðɪs kəmˈpjutər maɪt ʤɪst hæv ðə əˈpɪrəns əv ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: And just like cognitive scientists struggling to map consciousness onto brain activity,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ənd ʤɪst laɪk ˈkɑgnɪtɪv ˈsaɪəntɪsts ˈstrəgəlɪŋ tɪ mæp ˈkɑnʃəsnəs ˈɔntu breɪn ækˈtɪvɪti,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: today’s AI researchers know how they trained their creations,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: today’s* eɪaɪ ˈrisərʧərz noʊ haʊ ðeɪ treɪnd ðɛr kriˈeɪʃənz,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: but not how AIs reach their exact conclusions.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bət nɑt haʊ aɪz riʧ ðɛr ɪgˈzækt kənˈkluʒənz.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: There are some ways in which modern machine learning models are less mysterious than their predecessors.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðɛr ər səm weɪz ɪn wɪʧ ˈmɑdərn məˈʃin ˈlərnɪŋ ˈmɑdəlz ər lɛs mɪˈstɪriəs ðən ðɛr ˈprɛdəˌsɛsərz.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Approaches like neural networks and deep learning are designed to mimic known elements of human cognition.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: əˈproʊʧɪz laɪk ˈnʊrəl ˈnɛtˌwərks ənd dip ˈlərnɪŋ ər dɪˈzaɪnd tɪ ˈmɪmɪk noʊn ˈɛləmənts əv ˈjumən kɑgˈnɪʃən.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Like us, these models excel at pattern recognition—
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: laɪk ˈjuˈɛs, ðiz ˈmɑdəlz ɪkˈsɛl æt ˈpætərn recognition—*—

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: they learn by becoming familiar with information and forming connections across data sets.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðeɪ lərn baɪ bɪˈkəmɪŋ fəˈmɪljər wɪθ ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ənd ˈfɔrmɪŋ kəˈnɛkʃənz əˈkrɔs ˈdætə sɛts.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: This kind of processing arguably approaches Searle’s definition of understanding—
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðɪs kaɪnd əv ˈprɑsɛsɪŋ ˈɑrgjuəbli əˈproʊʧɪz searle’s* ˌdɛfəˈnɪʃən əv understanding—*—

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: but it also reveals a bias in his original question.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bət ɪt ˈɔlsoʊ rɪˈvilz ə baɪəs ɪn hɪz ərˈɪʤənəl kˈwɛʃən.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Since humans learn through pattern recognition and we believe ourselves to be conscious,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: sɪns ˈjumənz lərn θru ˈpætərn ˌrɛkɪgˈnɪʃən ənd wi bɪˈliv ɑrˈsɛlvz tɪ bi ˈkɑnʃəs,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: we might also be predisposed to think other beings who learn the same way
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: wi maɪt ˈɔlsoʊ bi ˌpridɪˈspoʊzd tɪ θɪŋk ˈəðər biɪŋz hu lərn ðə seɪm weɪ

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: are somehow closer to consciousness as well.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ər ˈsəmˌhaʊ ˈkloʊzər tɪ ˈkɑnʃəsnəs ɛz wɛl.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: To combat this bias, some theorists have developed a different metric;
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: tɪ ˈkɑmbæt ðɪs baɪəs, səm θiərɪsts hæv dɪˈvɛləpt ə ˈdɪfərənt ˈmɛtrɪk;

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: specifically, that a fully conscious AI could draw connections
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: spəˈsɪfɪkli, ðət ə ˈfʊli ˈkɑnʃəs eɪaɪ kʊd drɔ kəˈnɛkʃənz

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: beyond the information in its data set.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bɪɔnd ðə ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ɪn ɪts ˈdætə sɛt.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: For example, one lab’s “artificial consciousness test”
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, wən lab’s* ““artificial* ˈkɑnʃəsnəs test”*”

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: probes AIs that have no data about consciousness
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: proʊbz aɪz ðət hæv noʊ ˈdætə əˈbaʊt ˈkɑnʃəsnəs

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: for information they could only acquire from being conscious.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: fər ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ðeɪ kʊd ˈoʊnli əkˈwaɪər frəm biɪŋ ˈkɑnʃəs.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: This might involve asking an AI if it understands dreaming,
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðɪs maɪt ˌɪnˈvɑlv ˈæskɪŋ ən eɪaɪ ɪf ɪt ˌəndərˈstændz ˈdrimɪŋ,

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: or can report having had dreams itself.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ər kən rɪˈpɔrt ˈhævɪŋ hæd drimz ˌɪtˈsɛlf.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: Can it understand a story about body swapping, where consciousnesses are shuffled between physical forms?
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: kən ɪt ˌəndərˈstænd ə ˈstɔri əˈbaʊt ˈbɑdi sˈwɑpɪŋ, wɛr consciousnesses* ər ˈʃəfəld bɪtˈwin ˈfɪzɪkəl fɔrmz?

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: It’s unclear when or if an AI will be able to understand us
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: it’s* ənˈklɪr wɪn ər ɪf ən eɪaɪ wɪl bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd ˈjuˈɛs

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: the way we understand each other.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: ðə weɪ wi ˌəndərˈstænd iʧ ˈəðər.

3ezPMAoxSbw.text: But whatever happens, it’s up to us already conscious creatures to chart the path forward.
3ezPMAoxSbw.ipa: bət ˌwəˈtɛvər ˈhæpənz, it’s* əp tɪ ˈjuˈɛs ɔˈrɛdi ˈkɑnʃəs ˈkriʧərz tɪ ʧɑrt ðə pæθ ˈfɔrwərd.

uooR4293p_4.text: In 1864, French zoologist August Duméril was baffled while investigating the axolotl.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ɪn 1864 frɛnʧ zoʊˈɑləʤəst ˈɔgəst duméril* wɑz ˈbæfəld waɪl ˌɪnˈvɛstəˌgeɪtɪŋ ðə axolotl*.

uooR4293p_4.text: Unlike many other amphibians, which transform into terrestrial adults,
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ənˈlaɪk ˈmɛni ˈəðər æmˈfɪbiənz, wɪʧ ˈtrænsfɔrm ˈɪntu tərˈɛstriəl ˈædəlts,

uooR4293p_4.text: axolotls retain their juvenile characteristics and never leave the water.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: axolotls* rɪˈteɪn ðɛr ˈʤuvəˌnaɪl ˌkɛrɪktərˈɪstɪks ənd ˈnɛvər liv ðə ˈwɔtər.

uooR4293p_4.text: In an attempt to induce metamorphosis, Duméril spent months removing their gills.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ɪn ən əˈtɛmpt tɪ ˌɪnˈdus ˌmɛtəˈmɔrfəsəs, duméril* spɛnt mənθs riˈmuvɪŋ ðɛr gɪlz.

uooR4293p_4.text: But in most instances, the axolotls simply... grew them back. Indeed, axolotls are masters of regeneration:
uooR4293p_4.ipa: bət ɪn moʊst ˈɪnstənsɪz, ðə axolotls* ˈsɪmpli... gru ðɛm bæk. ˌɪnˈdid, axolotls* ər ˈmæstərz əv riˈʤɛnərˈeɪʃən:

uooR4293p_4.text: they can flawlessly regenerate body parts
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðeɪ kən ˈflɔləsli riˈʤɛnərˌeɪt ˈbɑdi pɑrts

uooR4293p_4.text: ranging from amputated limbs and crushed spines to parts of their eyes and brains.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˈreɪnʤɪŋ frəm ˈæmpjəˌteɪtɪd lɪmz ənd krəʃt spaɪnz tɪ pɑrts əv ðɛr aɪz ənd breɪnz.

uooR4293p_4.text: So how do they do it? And what other secrets are they keeping?
uooR4293p_4.ipa: soʊ haʊ du ðeɪ du ɪt? ənd wət ˈəðər ˈsikrɪts ər ðeɪ ˈkipɪŋ?

uooR4293p_4.text: This extraordinary salamander is native to the wetlands in Mexico City.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðɪs ˌɛkstrəˈɔrdəˌnɛri ˌsæləˈmændər ɪz ˈneɪtɪv tɪ ðə ˈwɛˌtlændz ɪn ˈmɛksəˌkoʊ ˈsɪti.

uooR4293p_4.text: Ancient Aztec people considered it the incarnation of a God named Xolotl—
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˈeɪnʧənt ˈæzˌtɛk ˈpipəl kənˈsɪdərd ɪt ðə ˌɪnˈkɑrˈneɪʃən əv ə gɑd neɪmd xolotl—*—

uooR4293p_4.text: hence the axolotl’s name, roughly meaning “water monster.”
uooR4293p_4.ipa: hɛns ðə axolotl’s* neɪm, ˈrəfli ˈminɪŋ ““water* monster.”*.”

uooR4293p_4.text: Axolotls reach sexual maturity with gills and a tadpole-like dorsal fin.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: axolotls* riʧ ˈsɛkʃuəl məˈʧʊrəti wɪθ gɪlz ənd ə tadpole-like* ˈdɔrsəl fɪn.

uooR4293p_4.text: Scientists think their forever-young condition, called “neoteny,” evolved because of their stable habitat.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˈsaɪəntɪsts θɪŋk ðɛr forever-young* kənˈdɪʃən, kɔld ““neoteny,”*,” ɪˈvɑlvd bɪˈkəz əv ðɛr ˈsteɪbəl ˈhæbəˌtæt.

uooR4293p_4.text: For salamanders that develop in waters that dry up, efficiently transitioning to land is essential.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: fər ˌsæləˈmændərz ðət dɪˈvɛləp ɪn ˈwɔtərz ðət draɪ əp, ɪˈfɪʃəntli trænˈzɪʃənɪŋ tɪ lænd ɪz ɛˈsɛnʃəl.

uooR4293p_4.text: But the lakes axolotls evolved in were unchanging year-round and didn't host many aquatic predators.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: bət ðə leɪks axolotls* ɪˈvɑlvd ɪn wər ənˈʧeɪnʤɪŋ year-round* ənd ˈdɪdənt hoʊst ˈmɛni əkˈwɑtɪk ˈprɛdətərz.

uooR4293p_4.text: So, scientists think it was advantageous for axolotls to forgo the demands of metamorphosis.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: soʊ, ˈsaɪəntɪsts θɪŋk ɪt wɑz ˌædvənˈteɪʤəs fər axolotls* tɪ fɔrˈgoʊ ðə dɪˈmændz əv ˌmɛtəˈmɔrfəsəs.

uooR4293p_4.text: However, they haven’t completely lost this ability.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ðeɪ haven’t* kəmˈplitli lɔst ðɪs əˈbɪləˌti.

uooR4293p_4.text: If exposed to certain substances, axolotls will turn into adults.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ɪf ɪkˈspoʊzd tɪ ˈsərtən ˈsəbstənsɪz, axolotls* wɪl tərn ˈɪntu ˈædəlts.

uooR4293p_4.text: But they’ll often experience shorter lifespans and lose some of their self-healing abilities.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: bət they’ll* ˈɔfən ɪkˈspɪriəns ˈʃɔrtər ˈlaɪfˌspænz ənd luz səm əv ðɛr self-healing* əˈbɪləˌtiz.

uooR4293p_4.text: These regenerative talents may seem like crazy superpowers to begin with, but axolotls have good use for them.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðiz regenerative* ˈtælənts meɪ sim laɪk ˈkreɪzi ˌsupərˈpaʊərz tɪ bɪˈgɪn wɪθ, bət axolotls* hæv gʊd juz fər ðɛm.

uooR4293p_4.text: As babies, they’re in direct competition. So, they snack on each other.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ɛz ˈbeɪbiz, they’re* ɪn dɪˈrɛkt ˌkɑmpəˈtɪʃən. soʊ, ðeɪ snæk ɔn iʧ ˈəðər.

uooR4293p_4.text: This is usually not a huge problem thanks to how quickly they can regenerate body parts.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ˈjuʒəwəli nɑt ə juʤ ˈprɑbləm θæŋks tɪ haʊ kˈwɪkli ðeɪ kən riˈʤɛnərˌeɪt ˈbɑdi pɑrts.

uooR4293p_4.text: When an axolotl loses a limb, tissues stimulate growth in the area. Skin cells divide and cover the wound.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: wɪn ən axolotl* ˈluzɪz ə lɪm, ˈtɪʃuz ˈstɪmjəˌleɪt groʊθ ɪn ðə ˈɛriə. skɪn sɛlz dɪˈvaɪd ənd ˈkəvər ðə wund.

uooR4293p_4.text: Then, progenitor cells, which can develop into various bodily tissues, form a mass at the site of injury
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðɛn, proʊˈʤɛnɪtər sɛlz, wɪʧ kən dɪˈvɛləp ˈɪntu ˈvɛriəs ˈbɑdəli ˈtɪʃuz, fɔrm ə mæs æt ðə saɪt əv ˈɪnʤəri

uooR4293p_4.text: and nearby nerves secrete growth-promoting proteins.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ənd ˈnɪrˈbaɪ nərvz sɪˈkrit growth-promoting* ˈproʊˌtinz.

uooR4293p_4.text: Over the next few weeks, a new limb emerges as cells proliferate and differentiate in coordination.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˈoʊvər ðə nɛkst fju wiks, ə nu lɪm ˈimərʤɪz ɛz sɛlz proʊˈlɪfərˌeɪt ənd ˌdɪfərˈɛnʧiˌeɪt ɪn koʊˌɔrdəˈneɪʃən.

uooR4293p_4.text: This process could potentially lead to uncontrolled growth and tumor formation.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðɪs ˈprɔˌsɛs kʊd pəˈtɛnʃəli lɛd tɪ ˌənkənˈtroʊld groʊθ ənd ˈtumər fɔrˈmeɪʃən.

uooR4293p_4.text: But axolotls are remarkably resistant to cancer.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: bət axolotls* ər rɪˈmɑrkəbli rɪˈzɪstənt tɪ ˈkænsər.

uooR4293p_4.text: They have a system in place that tightly controls cellular proliferation.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðeɪ hæv ə ˈsɪstəm ɪn pleɪs ðət ˈtaɪtli kənˈtroʊlz ˈsɛljələr ˌproʊlɪfərˈeɪʃən.

uooR4293p_4.text: To better understand the axolotl’s baffling biology, scientists sequenced its genome.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: tɪ ˈbɛtər ˌəndərˈstænd ðə axolotl’s* ˈbæfəlɪŋ baɪˈɑləʤi, ˈsaɪəntɪsts sequenced* ɪts ˈʤiˌnoʊm.

uooR4293p_4.text: They found it to be more than ten times longer than a human’s.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðeɪ faʊnd ɪt tɪ bi mɔr ðən tɛn taɪmz ˈlɔŋgər ðən ə human’s*.

uooR4293p_4.text: Mutations can change the length of any animal’s genome. For whatever reason, salamanders have much more DNA than other vertebrates
uooR4293p_4.ipa: mjuˈteɪʃənz kən ʧeɪnʤ ðə lɛŋθ əv ˈɛni animal’s* ˈʤiˌnoʊm. fər ˌwəˈtɛvər ˈrizən, ˌsæləˈmændərz hæv məʧ mɔr ˈdiˌɛˈneɪ ðən ˈəðər ˈvərtəˌbreɪts

uooR4293p_4.text: because they lose parts of it less frequently.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: bɪˈkəz ðeɪ luz pɑrts əv ɪt lɛs ˈfrikwɛntli.

uooR4293p_4.text: Investigating the axolotl’s genome, scientists saw many repeated sequences,
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˌɪnˈvɛstəˌgeɪtɪŋ ðə axolotl’s* ˈʤiˌnoʊm, ˈsaɪəntɪsts sɔ ˈmɛni rɪˈpitɪd ˈsikwənsɪz,

uooR4293p_4.text: most of which don't code for proteins and have no known function.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: moʊst əv wɪʧ doʊnt koʊd fər ˈproʊˌtinz ənd hæv noʊ noʊn ˈfəŋkʃən.

uooR4293p_4.text: They also found genes that are key in regeneration.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðeɪ ˈɔlsoʊ faʊnd ʤinz ðət ər ki ɪn riˈʤɛnərˈeɪʃən.

uooR4293p_4.text: However, the biggest factor that sets axolotl regeneration apart
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ðə ˈbɪgəst ˈfæktər ðət sɛts axolotl* riˈʤɛnərˈeɪʃən əˈpɑrt

uooR4293p_4.text: may not be a set of unique genes, but how they regulate their genes.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: meɪ nɑt bi ə sɛt əv juˈnik ʤinz, bət haʊ ðeɪ ˈrɛgjəˌleɪt ðɛr ʤinz.

uooR4293p_4.text: It’s no wonder that axolotls are one of science’s most studied animals. But their population in the wild has plummeted.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: it’s* noʊ ˈwəndər ðət axolotls* ər wən əv science’s* moʊst ˈstədid ˈænəməlz. bət ðɛr ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən ɪn ðə waɪld həz ˈpləmətɪd.

uooR4293p_4.text: Hundreds of years ago, axolotls thrived under the Aztec capital.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˈhənərdz əv jɪrz əˈgoʊ, axolotls* θraɪvd ˈəndər ðə ˈæzˌtɛk ˈkæpɪtəl.

uooR4293p_4.text: Within the surrounding lakes, Aztec people built islands called chinampas for growing crops.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: wɪˈθɪn ðə sərˈaʊndɪŋ leɪks, ˈæzˌtɛk ˈpipəl bɪlt ˈaɪləndz kɔld chinampas* fər groʊɪŋ krɑps.

uooR4293p_4.text: This highly productive form of agriculture created a vast system of canals,
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ðɪs ˈhaɪli pərˈdəktɪv fɔrm əv ˈægrɪˌkəlʧər kriˈeɪtɪd ə væst ˈsɪstəm əv kəˈnælz,

uooR4293p_4.text: expanding the lake system’s shallow, sheltered habitat— the axolotls’ ideal environment.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ɪkˈspændɪŋ ðə leɪk system’s* ˈʃæloʊ, ˈʃɛltərd habitat—*— ðə axolotls’*’ aɪˈdil ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt.

uooR4293p_4.text: But when Spanish invaders arrived, they began draining the lakes. And even more water has been diverted in recent years.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: bət wɪn ˈspænɪʃ ˌɪnˈveɪdərz əraɪvd, ðeɪ bɪˈgæn ˈdreɪnɪŋ ðə leɪks. ənd ˈivɪn mɔr ˈwɔtər həz bɪn dɪˈvərtɪd ɪn ˈrisənt jɪrz.

uooR4293p_4.text: Today, the entire population of wild axolotls is found in just one place,
uooR4293p_4.ipa: təˈdeɪ, ðə ɪnˈtaɪər ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən əv waɪld axolotls* ɪz faʊnd ɪn ʤɪst wən pleɪs,

uooR4293p_4.text: Lake Xochimilco, where it’s threatened by pollution and invasive fishes.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: leɪk xochimilco*, wɛr it’s* θˈrɛtənd baɪ pəˈluʃən ənd ˌɪnˈveɪsɪv ˈfɪʃɪz.

uooR4293p_4.text: People are working to regenerate the ecosystem and strengthen the 2,000 year old chinampa farming tradition.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˈpipəl ər ˈwərkɪŋ tɪ riˈʤɛnərˌeɪt ðə ˈikoʊˌsɪstəm ənd ˈstrɛŋθən ðə 2,000* jɪr oʊld chinampa* ˈfɑrmɪŋ trəˈdɪʃən.

uooR4293p_4.text: If interest grows, farmers could recover abandoned chinampas and support the local community— along with the axolotl.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ɪf ˈɪntəˌrɛst groʊz, ˈfɑrmərz kʊd rɪˈkəvər əˈbændənd chinampas* ənd səˈpɔrt ðə ˈloʊkəl community—*— əˈlɔŋ wɪθ ðə axolotl*.

uooR4293p_4.text: Eventually, the benefits of saving this salamander might be even greater.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli, ðə ˈbɛnəfɪts əv ˈseɪvɪŋ ðɪs ˌsæləˈmændər maɪt bi ˈivɪn ˈgreɪtər.

uooR4293p_4.text: Scientists hope that one day we’ll be able to apply the axolotl’s masterful tumor suppression and regenerative abilities
uooR4293p_4.ipa: ˈsaɪəntɪsts hoʊp ðət wən deɪ we’ll* bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ əˈplaɪ ðə axolotl’s* ˈmæstərfəl ˈtumər səˈprɛʃən ənd regenerative* əˈbɪləˌtiz

uooR4293p_4.text: to the human body. Perhaps its secrets are the real reason for the slimy god monster’s smile.
uooR4293p_4.ipa: tɪ ðə ˈjumən ˈbɑdi. pərˈhæps ɪts ˈsikrɪts ər ðə ril ˈrizən fər ðə sˈlaɪmi gɑd monster’s* smaɪl.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Over 100,000 metric tons of caffeine are consumed around the world every year.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ˈoʊvər 100,000* ˈmɛtrɪk tənz əv kæˈfin ər kənˈsumd əraʊnd ðə wərld ˈɛvəri jɪr.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: That's equivalent to the weight of 14 Eiffel Towers.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ðæts ɪkˈwɪvələnt tɪ ðə weɪt əv 14 ˈaɪfəl taʊərz.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Most of this caffeine is consumed in coffee and tea, but it's also ingested in some sodas,
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: moʊst əv ðɪs kæˈfin ɪz kənˈsumd ɪn ˈkɔfi ənd ti, bət ɪts ˈɔlsoʊ ˌɪnˈʤɛstəd ɪn səm ˈsoʊdəz,

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: chocolate, caffeine pills, and even beverages labeled decaf.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ˈʧɔklət, kæˈfin pɪlz, ənd ˈivɪn ˈbɛvrɪʤɪz ˈleɪbəld ˈdikæf.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Caffeine helps us feel alert, focused, happy, and energetic, even if we haven't had enough sleep.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: kæˈfin hɛlps ˈjuˈɛs fil əˈlərt, ˈfoʊkɪst, ˈhæpi, ənd ˌɛnərˈʤɛtɪk, ˈivɪn ɪf wi ˈhævənt hæd ɪˈnəf slip.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: But it can also raise our blood pressure, and make us feel anxious.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: bət ɪt kən ˈɔlsoʊ reɪz ɑr bləd ˈprɛʃər, ənd meɪk ˈjuˈɛs fil ˈæŋʃəs.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: It's the world most widely used drug. So how does it keep us awake?
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ɪts ðə wərld moʊst ˈwaɪdli juzd drəg. soʊ haʊ dɪz ɪt kip ˈjuˈɛs əˈweɪk?

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Caffeine evolved in plants where it serves a few purposes.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: kæˈfin ɪˈvɑlvd ɪn plænts wɛr ɪt sərvz ə fju ˈpərpəsɪz.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: In high doses, as it's found in the leaves and seeds of certain species, it's toxic to insects.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ɪn haɪ ˈdoʊsɪz, ɛz ɪts faʊnd ɪn ðə livz ənd sidz əv ˈsərtən ˈspiʃiz, ɪts ˈtɑksɪk tɪ ˈɪnˌsɛkts.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: But when they consume it in lower doses, as it's found in nectar, it can actually help them remember and revisit flowers.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: bət wɪn ðeɪ kənˈsum ɪt ɪn loʊər ˈdoʊsɪz, ɛz ɪts faʊnd ɪn ˈnɛktər, ɪt kən ˈæˌkʧuəli hɛlp ðɛm rɪˈmɛmbər ənd riˈvɪzɪt flaʊərz.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: In the human body, caffeine acts as a stimulant for the central nervous system.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ɪn ðə ˈjumən ˈbɑdi, kæˈfin ækts ɛz ə ˈstɪmjələnt fər ðə ˈsɛntrəl ˈnərvəs ˈsɪstəm.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: It keeps us awake by blocking one of the body's key sleep-inducing molecules, a substance called adenosine.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ɪt kips ˈjuˈɛs əˈweɪk baɪ ˈblɑkɪŋ wən əv ðə ˈbɑdiz ki sleep-inducing* ˈmɑləˌkjulz, ə ˈsəbstəns kɔld əˈdɛnəˌsin.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Your body needs a constant supply of energy, which it gets by breaking down a high-energy molecule called ATP.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: jʊr ˈbɑdi nidz ə ˈkɑnstənt səˈplaɪ əv ˈɛnərʤi, wɪʧ ɪt gɪts baɪ ˈbreɪkɪŋ daʊn ə high-energy* ˈmɑləˌkjul kɔld ˈeɪˈtiˈpi.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: In the process, it liberates adenosine, ATP's chemical backbone.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ɪn ðə ˈprɔˌsɛs, ɪt liberates* əˈdɛnəˌsin, atp's* ˈkɛmɪkəl ˈbækˌboʊn.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Neurons in your brain have receptors perfectly tailored to this molecule.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ˈnʊrɑnz ɪn jʊr breɪn hæv rəˈsɛptərz ˈpərfəktli ˈteɪlərd tɪ ðɪs ˈmɑləˌkjul.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: When adenosine docks to these receptors, it activates a cascade of biochemical reactions
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: wɪn əˈdɛnəˌsin dɑks tɪ ðiz rəˈsɛptərz, ɪt ˈæktəˌveɪts ə kæˈskeɪd əv ˌbaɪoʊˈkɛmɪkəl riˈækʃənz

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: that cause neurons to fire more sluggishly and slow the release of important brain-signaling molecules.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ðət kɔz ˈnʊrɑnz tɪ faɪər mɔr sˈləgɪʃli ənd sloʊ ðə riˈlis əv ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt brain-signaling* ˈmɑləˌkjulz.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: In other words, you get sleepy.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ɪn ˈəðər wərdz, ju gɪt sˈlipi.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Caffeine is what's called an adenosine receptor antagonist.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: kæˈfin ɪz wəts kɔld ən əˈdɛnəˌsin riˈsɛptər ænˈtægənəst.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: That means it derails this process of slowing your neurons down by blocking adenosine receptors.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ðət minz ɪt dɪˈreɪlz ðɪs ˈprɔˌsɛs əv sloʊɪŋ jʊr ˈnʊrɑnz daʊn baɪ ˈblɑkɪŋ əˈdɛnəˌsin rəˈsɛptərz.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Caffeine and adenosine have a similar molecular structure, close enough that caffeine can wedge into the adenosine receptors,
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: kæˈfin ənd əˈdɛnəˌsin hæv ə ˈsɪmələr məˈlɛkjələr ˈstrəkʧər, kloʊz ɪˈnəf ðət kæˈfin kən wɛʤ ˈɪntu ðə əˈdɛnəˌsin rəˈsɛptərz,

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: but not close enough to activate them.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: bət nɑt kloʊz ɪˈnəf tɪ ˈæktəˌveɪt ðɛm.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: To summarize, adenosine inhibits your neurons. Caffeine inhibits the inhibitor, so it stimulates you.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: tɪ ˈsəmərˌaɪz, əˈdɛnəˌsin ˌɪnˈhɪbəts jʊr ˈnʊrɑnz. kæˈfin ˌɪnˈhɪbəts ðə ˌɪnˈhɪbətər, soʊ ɪt ˈstɪmjəˌleɪts ju.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Caffeine can also boost positive feelings. In some neurons, the adenosine receptors
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: kæˈfin kən ˈɔlsoʊ bust ˈpɑzətɪv ˈfilɪŋz. ɪn səm ˈnʊrɑnz, ðə əˈdɛnəˌsin rəˈsɛptərz

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: are linked to receptors for another molecule called dopamine.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ər lɪŋkt tɪ rəˈsɛptərz fər əˈnəðər ˈmɑləˌkjul kɔld ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: One of dopamine's roles in the brain is to promote feelings of pleasure.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: wən əv dopamine's* roʊlz ɪn ðə breɪn ɪz tɪ prəˈmoʊt ˈfilɪŋz əv ˈplɛʒər.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: When adenosine docks in one of these paired receptors, that can make it harder for dopamine to fit in its own spot,
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: wɪn əˈdɛnəˌsin dɑks ɪn wən əv ðiz pɛrd rəˈsɛptərz, ðət kən meɪk ɪt ˈhɑrdər fər ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn tɪ fɪt ɪn ɪts oʊn spɑt,

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: interrupting its mood-lifting work.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ˌɪntərˈəptɪŋ ɪts mood-lifting* wərk.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: But when caffeine takes adenosine's place, it doesn't have the same effect, and dopamine can slide in.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: bət wɪn kæˈfin teɪks adenosine's* pleɪs, ɪt ˈdəzənt hæv ðə seɪm ˈifɛkt, ənd ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn kən slaɪd ɪn.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: There's evidence that caffeine's effects on adenosine and dopamine receptors
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ðɛrz ˈɛvədəns ðət caffeine's* ˈifɛkts ɔn əˈdɛnəˌsin ənd ˈdɑpəˌmaɪn rəˈsɛptərz

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: can have long-term benefits, too, reducing the risk of diseases
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: kən hæv ˈlɔŋˈtərm ˈbɛnəfɪts, tu, rɪˈdusɪŋ ðə rɪsk əv dɪˈzizɪz

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: like Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, and some types of cancer.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: laɪk ˈpɑrkɪnsənz, ˈɑtszˌhaɪmərz, ənd səm taɪps əv ˈkænsər.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Caffeine can also ramp up the body's ability to burn fat.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: kæˈfin kən ˈɔlsoʊ ræmp əp ðə ˈbɑdiz əˈbɪləˌti tɪ bərn fæt.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: In fact, some sports organizations think that caffeine gives athletes an unfair advantage
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ɪn fækt, səm spɔrts ˌɔrgənəˈzeɪʃənz θɪŋk ðət kæˈfin gɪvz ˈæθˌlits ən ˌɔnˈfɛr ædˈvæntɪʤ

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: and have placed limits on its consumption.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ənd hæv pleɪst ˈlɪmɪts ɔn ɪts kənˈsəmʃən.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: From 1972 until 2004, Olympic athletes had to stay below a certain blood-caffeine concentration
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: frəm 1972 ənˈtɪl 2004 oʊˈlɪmpɪk ˈæθˌlits hæd tɪ steɪ bɪˈloʊ ə ˈsərtən blood-caffeine* ˌkɑnsənˈtreɪʃən

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: to compete.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: tɪ kəmˈpit.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Of course, not all of caffeine's effects are so helpful. It might make you feel better and more alert,
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: əv kɔrs, nɑt ɔl əv caffeine's* ˈifɛkts ər soʊ ˈhɛlpfəl. ɪt maɪt meɪk ju fil ˈbɛtər ənd mɔr əˈlərt,

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: but it can also raise your heart rate and blood pressure,
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: bət ɪt kən ˈɔlsoʊ reɪz jʊr hɑrt reɪt ənd bləd ˈprɛʃər,

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: cause increased urination or diarrhea, and contribute to insomnia and anxiety.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: kɔz ˌɪnˈkrist urination* ər ˌdaɪərˈiə, ənd kənˈtrɪbjut tɪ ˌɪnˈsɑmniə ənd æŋˈzaɪəti.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Plus, the foods and beverages caffeine is found in
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: pləs, ðə fudz ənd ˈbɛvrɪʤɪz kæˈfin ɪz faʊnd ɪn

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: have their own impacts on your body that have to be taken into account.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: hæv ðɛr oʊn ˌɪmˈpækts ɔn jʊr ˈbɑdi ðət hæv tɪ bi ˈteɪkən ˈɪntu əˈkaʊnt.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: Your brain can adapt to regular consumption of caffeine. If your adenosine receptors are perpetually clogged,
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: jʊr breɪn kən əˈdæpt tɪ ˈrɛgjələr kənˈsəmʃən əv kæˈfin. ɪf jʊr əˈdɛnəˌsin rəˈsɛptərz ər pərˈpɛʧuəli klɔgd,

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: your body will manufacture extra ones. That way, even with caffeine around,
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: jʊr ˈbɑdi wɪl ˌmænjəˈfækʧər ˈɛkstrə wənz. ðət weɪ, ˈivɪn wɪθ kæˈfin əraʊnd,

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: adenosine can still do its job of signaling the brain to power down.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: əˈdɛnəˌsin kən stɪl du ɪts ʤɑb əv ˈsɪgnəlɪŋ ðə breɪn tɪ paʊər daʊn.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: That's why you may find you need to consume more and more caffeine to feel as alert.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ðæts waɪ ju meɪ faɪnd ju nid tɪ kənˈsum mɔr ənd mɔr kæˈfin tɪ fil ɛz əˈlərt.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: There are more and more adenosine receptors to block.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ðɛr ər mɔr ənd mɔr əˈdɛnəˌsin rəˈsɛptərz tɪ blɑk.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: It's also why if you suddenly quit caffeine, you may experience an unpleasant withdrawal.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ɪts ˈɔlsoʊ waɪ ɪf ju ˈsədənli kwɪt kæˈfin, ju meɪ ɪkˈspɪriəns ən ənˈplɛzənt wɪθˈdrɔəl.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: With plenty of receptors and no competition, adenosine can work overtime, causing symptoms like headaches, tiredness,
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: wɪθ ˈplɛnti əv rəˈsɛptərz ənd noʊ ˌkɑmpəˈtɪʃən, əˈdɛnəˌsin kən wərk ˈoʊvərˌtaɪm, ˈkɔzɪŋ ˈsɪmptəmz laɪk ˈhɛˌdeɪks, ˈtaɪərdnɪs,

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: and depressed moods.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ənd dɪˈprɛst mudz.

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: But in a few days, the extra adenosine receptors will disappear, your body will readjust,
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: bət ɪn ə fju deɪz, ðə ˈɛkstrə əˈdɛnəˌsin rəˈsɛptərz wɪl ˌdɪsəˈpɪr, jʊr ˈbɑdi wɪl ˌriəˈʤəst,

foLf5Bi9qXs.text: and you'll feel just as alert as ever, even without an infusion of the world's most popular stimulant.
foLf5Bi9qXs.ipa: ənd jul fil ʤɪst ɛz əˈlərt ɛz ˈɛvər, ˈivɪn wɪˈθaʊt ən ˌɪnfˈjuʒən əv ðə wərldz moʊst ˈpɑpjələr ˈstɪmjələnt.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: In January 2016, the US Powerball made history
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɪn ˈʤænjuˌɛri 2016 ðə ˈjuˈɛs ˈpaʊərˌbɔl meɪd ˈhɪstəri

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: by awarding the first jackpot to exceed $1 billion.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: baɪ əˈwɔrdɪŋ ðə fərst ˈʤækˌpɑt tɪ ɪkˈsid 1 ˈbɪljən.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: The three winning ticket holders were incredibly lucky,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ðə θri ˈwɪnɪŋ ˈtɪkɪt ˈhoʊldərz wər ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli ˈləki,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: with odds of roughly 1 in 300 million.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: wɪθ ɑdz əv ˈrəfli 1 ɪn 300 ˈmɪljən.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: By some estimates, you’re statistically more likely to be killed by a nearby meteor strike.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: baɪ səm ˈɛstəˌmeɪts, you’re* stəˈtɪstɪkəli mɔr ˈlaɪkli tɪ bi kɪld baɪ ə ˈnɪrˈbaɪ ˈmitiər straɪk.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: After winning such a huge jackpot, you might assume your life would immediately become simpler.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˈæftər ˈwɪnɪŋ səʧ ə juʤ ˈʤækˌpɑt, ju maɪt əˈsum jʊr laɪf wʊd ˌɪˈmiˌdiətli bɪˈkəm ˈsɪmpələr.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: But there are plenty of new challenges to navigate.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: bət ðɛr ər ˈplɛnti əv nu ˈʧælənʤɪz tɪ ˈnævəˌgeɪt.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: First, most experts advise hiring a lawyer
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: fərst, moʊst ˈɛkspərts ədˈvaɪz ˈhaɪrɪŋ ə ˈlɔjər

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: to protect you from fraud, harassment, and other legal risks.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: tɪ prəˈtɛkt ju frəm frɔd, hərˈæsmənt, ənd ˈəðər ˈligəl rɪsks.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: It’s also critical to safeguard yourself against impulsive spending.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: it’s* ˈɔlsoʊ ˈkrɪtɪkəl tɪ ˈseɪfˌgɑrd ˈjɔrsɛlf əˈgɛnst ˌɪmˈpəlsɪv ˈspɛndɪŋ.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: a trap many lottery winners fall into.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ə træp ˈmɛni ˈlɑtəri ˈwɪnərz fɔl ˈɪntu.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Take one Pennsylvania lotto winner, who, after winning $16.2 million,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: teɪk wən ˌpɛnsəlˈveɪnjə ˈlɑˌtoʊ ˈwɪnər, hu, ˈæftər ˈwɪnɪŋ 16.2* ˈmɪljən,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: made so many extravagant purchases that he ended up in $500,000 of debt within three months.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: meɪd soʊ ˈmɛni ɛkˈstrævəgənt ˈpərʧəsɪz ðət hi ˈɛndɪd əp ɪn 500,000* əv dɛt wɪˈθɪn θri mənθs.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: And you’ll need to make one more decision— one that could dramatically shape the course of your financial future.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ənd you’ll* nid tɪ meɪk wən mɔr decision—*— wən ðət kʊd drəˈmætɪkəli ʃeɪp ðə kɔrs əv jʊr ˌfaɪˈnænʃəl fˈjuʧər.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Lottery winnings are typically paid out in one of two ways:
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˈlɑtəri ˈwɪnɪŋz ər ˈtɪpɪkəli peɪd aʊt ɪn wən əv tu weɪz:

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: you can choose the full jackpot amount, paid out in annual installments over 30 years.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ju kən ʧuz ðə fʊl ˈʤækˌpɑt əˈmaʊnt, peɪd aʊt ɪn ˈænjuəl ˌɪnˈstɔlmənts ˈoʊvər 30 jɪrz.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: This is known as the annuity option.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ðɪs ɪz noʊn ɛz ðə əˈnuɪti ˈɔpʃən.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Or you can take a much smaller lump sum paid out immediately.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ər ju kən teɪk ə məʧ sˈmɔlər ləmp səm peɪd aʊt ˌɪˈmiˌdiətli.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: To better understand the financial implications of this choice,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: tɪ ˈbɛtər ˌəndərˈstænd ðə ˌfaɪˈnænʃəl ˌɪmpləˈkeɪʃənz əv ðɪs ʧɔɪs,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: let’s walk through both scenarios, assuming you’re the sole winner of a $3 million jackpot.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: let’s* wɔk θru boʊθ sɪˈnɛrioʊz, əˈsumɪŋ you’re* ðə soʊl ˈwɪnər əv ə 3 ˈmɪljən ˈʤækˌpɑt.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: The annuity would pay roughly $50,000 the first year.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ðə əˈnuɪti wʊd peɪ ˈrəfli 50,000* ðə fərst jɪr.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: You'd then receive increasing annual payments for the next 30 years, reaching a grand total of 3 million.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: jʊd ðɛn rɪˈsiv ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋ ˈænjuəl ˈpeɪmənts fər ðə nɛkst 30 jɪrz, ˈriʧɪŋ ə grænd ˈtoʊtəl əv 3 ˈmɪljən.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: In the short term, it might make sense to pay off any high-interest debts,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɪn ðə ʃɔrt tərm, ɪt maɪt meɪk sɛns tɪ peɪ ɔf ˈɛni high-interest* dɛts,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: like credit cards, auto loans, student loans, or mortgages.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: laɪk ˈkrɛdɪt kɑrdz, ˈɔtoʊ loʊnz, ˈstudənt loʊnz, ər ˈmɔrgɪʤɪz.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: In 2024, the average American owed around $100,000 across these categories—
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɪn 2024 ðə ˈævərɪʤ əˈmɛrɪkən oʊd əraʊnd 100,000* əˈkrɔs ðiz categories—*—

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: so the first few annuity payments could clear much of it.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: soʊ ðə fərst fju əˈnuɪti ˈpeɪmənts kʊd klɪr məʧ əv ɪt.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Alternatively, initial payments could go towards building an emergency fund,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɔlˈtərnətɪvli, ˌɪˈnɪʃəl ˈpeɪmənts kʊd goʊ təˈwɔrdz ˈbɪldɪŋ ən ˈimərʤənsi fənd,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: which is typically a savings account that could cover 3 to 6 months of expenses.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz ˈtɪpɪkəli ə ˈseɪvɪŋz əˈkaʊnt ðət kʊd ˈkəvər 3 tɪ 6 mənθs əv ɪkˈspɛnsɪz.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: This would put you ahead of most Americans:
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ðɪs wʊd pʊt ju əˈhɛd əv moʊst əˈmɛrɪkənz:

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: a 2024 survey found that only 46% of adults had enough saved funds
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ə 2024 ˈsərˌveɪ faʊnd ðət ˈoʊnli 46 əv ˈædəlts hæd ɪˈnəf seɪvd fəndz

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: to pay three months of bills.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: tɪ peɪ θri mənθs əv bɪlz.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Receiving your winnings in a slow drip might not be as thrilling as an immediate tidal wave of cash.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: rɪˈsivɪŋ jʊr ˈwɪnɪŋz ɪn ə sloʊ drɪp maɪt nɑt bi ɛz θˈrɪlɪŋ ɛz ən ˌɪˈmiˌdiət ˈtaɪdəl weɪv əv kæʃ.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: However, when used wisely, the annuity can provide financial stability from the start,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, wɪn juzd ˈwaɪzli, ðə əˈnuɪti kən prəˈvaɪd ˌfaɪˈnænʃəl stəˈbɪlɪti frəm ðə stɑrt,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: while ensuring a steady income stream for the future.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: waɪl ɪnˈʃʊrɪŋ ə ˈstɛdi ˈɪnˌkəm strim fər ðə fˈjuʧər.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: But maybe your goal is strictly to maximize your winnings.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: bət ˈmeɪbi jʊr goʊl ɪz ˈstrɪktli tɪ ˈmæksəˌmaɪz jʊr ˈwɪnɪŋz.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: In that case, the upfront lump sum might actually be the better choice,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɪn ðət keɪs, ðə ˈəpˌfrənt ləmp səm maɪt ˈæˌkʧuəli bi ðə ˈbɛtər ʧɔɪs,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: even though you’ll forfeit about half the advertised jackpot
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˈivɪn ðoʊ you’ll* ˈfɔrfɪt əˈbaʊt hæf ðə ˌædvərˈtaɪzd ˈʤækˌpɑt

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: and it’s often subject to a higher tax rate.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ənd it’s* ˈɔfən ˈsəbʤɪkt tɪ ə haɪər tæks reɪt.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: A $3 million jackpot might leave you with just $850,000 after all these deductions.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ə 3 ˈmɪljən ˈʤækˌpɑt maɪt liv ju wɪθ ʤɪst 850,000* ˈæftər ɔl ðiz dɪˈdəkʃənz.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Still, with some patience,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: stɪl, wɪθ səm ˈpeɪʃəns,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: you could end up with more money if you utilize the power of compound interest.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ju kʊd ɛnd əp wɪθ mɔr ˈməni ɪf ju ˈjutəˌlaɪz ðə paʊər əv ˈkɑmpaʊnd ˈɪntəˌrɛst.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Compound interest builds like a snowball.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˈkɑmpaʊnd ˈɪntəˌrɛst bɪldz laɪk ə sˈnoʊˌbɔl.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: You earn interest on your original investment and then start earning interest on that interest.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ju ərn ˈɪntəˌrɛst ɔn jʊr ərˈɪʤənəl ˌɪnˈvɛstmənt ənd ðɛn stɑrt ˈərnɪŋ ˈɪntəˌrɛst ɔn ðət ˈɪntəˌrɛst.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Benjamin Franklin famously illustrated this by leaving just £1,000
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˈbɛnʤəmən ˈfræŋklɪn ˈfeɪməsli ˈɪləˌstreɪtɪd ðɪs baɪ ˈlivɪŋ ʤɪst £1,000*

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: in two accounts in 1790, with instructions to let it compound.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɪn tu əˈkaʊnts ɪn 1790 wɪθ ˌɪnˈstrəkʃənz tɪ lɛt ɪt ˈkɑmpaʊnd.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: After 200 years, these funds grew to $6.5 million.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˈæftər 200 jɪrz, ðiz fəndz gru tɪ 6.5* ˈmɪljən.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: So if you took the $850,000 lump sum and invested it in the US stock market,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: soʊ ɪf ju tʊk ðə 850,000* ləmp səm ənd ˌɪnˈvɛstɪd ɪt ɪn ðə ˈjuˈɛs stɑk ˈmɑrkɪt,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: it could grow exponentially over time.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɪt kʊd groʊ ˌɛkspoʊˈnɛnʃəli ˈoʊvər taɪm.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Take the S&P 500, an index fund that includes 500 leading companies
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: teɪk ðə s&p* 500 ən ˈɪndɛks fənd ðət ˌɪnˈkludz 500 ˈlidɪŋ ˈkəmpəˌniz

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: and is typically considered a stable long-term investment.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ənd ɪz ˈtɪpɪkəli kənˈsɪdərd ə ˈsteɪbəl ˈlɔŋˈtərm ˌɪnˈvɛstmənt.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Historically, it's returned an average of 10.5% per year.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: hɪˈstɔrɪkəli, ɪts rɪˈtərnd ən ˈævərɪʤ əv 10.5* pər jɪr.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: If you'd won 30 years ago and invested the lump sum in the S&P 500,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɪf jʊd wən 30 jɪrz əˈgoʊ ənd ˌɪnˈvɛstɪd ðə ləmp səm ɪn ðə s&p* 500

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: it'd be worth over $19 million today.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˈɪtəd bi wərθ ˈoʊvər 19 ˈmɪljən təˈdeɪ.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Ultimately, choosing between the lump sum and the annuity comes down to knowing your own goals and priorities.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˈəltəmətli, ˈʧuzɪŋ bɪtˈwin ðə ləmp səm ənd ðə əˈnuɪti kəmz daʊn tɪ noʊɪŋ jʊr oʊn goʊlz ənd praɪˈɔrətiz.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Are you content to wait decades as your fortune builds?
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ər ju ˈkɑntɛnt tɪ weɪt ˈdɛkeɪdz ɛz jʊr ˈfɔrʧən bɪldz?

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Does a lifetime of steady paychecks feel more reassuring than one big payout?
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: dɪz ə ˈlaɪfˌtaɪm əv ˈstɛdi ˈpeɪˌʧɛks fil mɔr ˌriəˈʃʊrɪŋ ðən wən bɪg peɪaʊt?

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: What kind of life do you want to live?
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: wət kaɪnd əv laɪf du ju wɔnt tɪ lɪv?

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Understanding your values also makes it easier to spend with intention and avoid impulsive buys.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ jʊr ˈvæljuz ˈɔlsoʊ meɪks ɪt ˈiziər tɪ spɛnd wɪθ ˌɪnˈtɛnʧən ənd əˈvɔɪd ˌɪmˈpəlsɪv baɪz.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: And this mindset isn't just for lottery winners.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ənd ðɪs ˈmaɪndˌsɛt ˈɪzənt ʤɪst fər ˈlɑtəri ˈwɪnərz.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: Consider this: say you spend $3 per day, or around $1,000 per year,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: kənˈsɪdər ðɪs: seɪ ju spɛnd 3 pər deɪ, ər əraʊnd 1,000* pər jɪr,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: on lottery tickets.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɔn ˈlɑtəri ˈtɪkɪts.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: If at 18, you had instead chosen to invest that money consistently,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɪf æt 18 ju hæd ˌɪnˈstɛd ˈʧoʊzən tɪ ˌɪnˈvɛst ðət ˈməni kənˈsɪstəntli,
