wxf_pKCOCBo.text: you could likely retire with over $1 million.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ju kʊd ˈlaɪkli ˌriˈtaɪər wɪθ ˈoʊvər 1 ˈmɪljən.

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: In other words, while lottery winners are rare,
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ɪn ˈəðər wərdz, waɪl ˈlɑtəri ˈwɪnərz ər rɛr,

wxf_pKCOCBo.text: building up your own million-dollar jackpot doesn’t have to be.
wxf_pKCOCBo.ipa: ˈbɪldɪŋ əp jʊr oʊn ˈmɪljənˌdɔlər ˈʤækˌpɑt doesn’t* hæv tɪ bi.

BT93bRX2t68.text: Jo the First is a ruthless dictator who rules his country with an iron fist.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ʤoʊ ðə fərst ɪz ə ˈruθləs ˈdɪkteɪtər hu rulz hɪz ˈkəntri wɪθ ən aɪərn fɪst.

BT93bRX2t68.text: Whenever he wants something, everyone hurries to appease him.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: wɛˈnɛvər hi wɔnts ˈsəmθɪŋ, ˈɛvriˌwən ˈhəriz tɪ əˈpiz ɪm.

BT93bRX2t68.text: And since complainers are permanently banished,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ənd sɪns ˈkɑmˌpleɪnərz ər ˈpərmɑˌnɛnˌtli ˈbænɪʃt,

BT93bRX2t68.text: Jo’s terrified servants treat his every whim as a wise and noble decision.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: jo’s* ˈtɛrəˌfaɪd ˈsərvənts trit hɪz ˈɛvəri wɪm ɛz ə waɪz ənd ˈnoʊbəl dɪˈsɪʒən.

BT93bRX2t68.text: To most onlookers, his rule appears cruel and unjust. But his beloved son JoJo doesn’t see any issues.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: tɪ moʊst ˈɔnˌlʊkərz, hɪz rul əˈpɪrz kruəl ənd ənˈʤəst. bət hɪz bɪˈləvəd sən jojo* doesn’t* si ˈɛni ˈɪʃuz.

BT93bRX2t68.text: This life is all JoJo's ever known,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ðɪs laɪf ɪz ɔl jojo's* ˈɛvər noʊn,

BT93bRX2t68.text: and he never witnesses any fear from Jo’s subjects or violence against them.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ənd hi ˈnɛvər ˈwɪtnəsɪz ˈɛni fɪr frəm jo’s* ˈsəbʤɪkts ər ˈvaɪələns əˈgɛnst ðɛm.

BT93bRX2t68.text: All JoJo sees is his father’s strength, and his subjects’ obedience and praise.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ɔl jojo* siz ɪz hɪz father’s* strɛŋθ, ənd hɪz subjects’*’ oʊˈbidiəns ənd preɪz.

BT93bRX2t68.text: After spending his entire childhood in the castle,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ˈæftər ˈspɛndɪŋ hɪz ɪnˈtaɪər ˈʧaɪlˌdhʊd ɪn ðə ˈkæsəl,

BT93bRX2t68.text: JoJo grows up to inherit his father’s role and his style of rule.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: jojo* groʊz əp tɪ ˌɪnˈhɛrət hɪz father’s* roʊl ənd hɪz staɪl əv rul.

BT93bRX2t68.text: And just as with Jo the First, it’s clear that JoJo’s actions are wrong.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ənd ʤɪst ɛz wɪθ ʤoʊ ðə fərst, it’s* klɪr ðət jojo’s* ˈækʃənz ər rɔŋ.

BT93bRX2t68.text: But his case raises a question about morality that can also shed light on less extreme cases:
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: bət hɪz keɪs ˈreɪzɪz ə kˈwɛʃən əˈbaʊt ˌmɔˈræləˌti ðət kən ˈɔlsoʊ ʃɛd laɪt ɔn lɛs ɪkˈstrim ˈkeɪsɪz:

BT93bRX2t68.text: given JoJo’s unusual upbringing, does he bear full moral responsibility for his actions?
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ˈgɪvɪn jojo’s* ənˈjuˌʒuəl ˈəpˌbrɪŋɪŋ, dɪz hi bɛr fʊl ˈmɔrəl riˌspɑnsəˈbɪləti fər hɪz ˈækʃənz?

BT93bRX2t68.text: Philosophers Gary Watson and Harry Frankfurt would say yes.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: fəˈlɑsəfərz ˈgɛri ˈwɑtsən ənd ˈhɛri ˈfræŋkfərt wʊd seɪ jɛs.

BT93bRX2t68.text: According to their Deep Self View,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ðɛr dip sɛlf vju,

BT93bRX2t68.text: people are morally responsible for actions that stem from their true self,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ˈpipəl ər ˈmɔrəli riˈspɑnsəbəl fər ˈækʃənz ðət stɛm frəm ðɛr tru sɛlf,

BT93bRX2t68.text: meaning actions that reflect their deepest values and commitments.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ˈminɪŋ ˈækʃənz ðət rɪˈflɛkt ðɛr ˈdipəst ˈvæljuz ənd kəˈmɪtmənts.

BT93bRX2t68.text: They believe people are less morally responsible for actions performed under the influence of external forces,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ðeɪ bɪˈliv ˈpipəl ər lɛs ˈmɔrəli riˈspɑnsəbəl fər ˈækʃənz pərˈfɔrmd ˈəndər ðə ˈɪnfluəns əv ɪkˈstərnəl ˈfɔrsɪz,

BT93bRX2t68.text: such as committing a crime under duress or while intoxicated.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: səʧ ɛz kəˈmɪtɪŋ ə kraɪm ˈəndər ˈdʊrɛs ər waɪl ˌɪnˈtɑksɪˌkeɪtɪd.

BT93bRX2t68.text: But JoJo has no such excuse.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: bət jojo* həz noʊ səʧ ɪkˈskjuz.

BT93bRX2t68.text: His actions are the product of his values, and as such,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: hɪz ˈækʃənz ər ðə ˈprɑdəkt əv hɪz ˈvæljuz, ənd ɛz səʧ,

BT93bRX2t68.text: Deep Self theorists would argue that he’s responsible for them.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: dip sɛlf θiərɪsts wʊd ˈɑrgju ðət he’s* riˈspɑnsəbəl fər ðɛm.

BT93bRX2t68.text: But what if JoJo’s values have been compromised?
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: bət wət ɪf jojo’s* ˈvæljuz hæv bɪn ˈkɑmprəˌmaɪzd?

BT93bRX2t68.text: JoJo might be a monster, but he was raised in a very atypical environment.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: jojo* maɪt bi ə ˈmɑnstər, bət hi wɑz reɪzd ɪn ə ˈvɛri ˌeɪˈtɪpɪkəl ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt.

BT93bRX2t68.text: Is he really just as responsible for his crimes as someone who had a normal childhood and then became a cruel dictator?
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ɪz hi ˈrɪli ʤɪst ɛz riˈspɑnsəbəl fər hɪz kraɪmz ɛz ˈsəmˌwən hu hæd ə ˈnɔrməl ˈʧaɪlˌdhʊd ənd ðɛn bɪˈkeɪm ə kruəl ˈdɪkteɪtər?

BT93bRX2t68.text: And if JoJo isn’t to blame for who he is, how can we blame him for what he does?
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ənd ɪf jojo* isn’t* tɪ bleɪm fər hu hi ɪz, haʊ kən wi bleɪm ɪm fər wət hi dɪz?

BT93bRX2t68.text: This is the argument of philosopher Susan Wolf,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ðə ˈɑrgjəmənt əv fəˈlɑsəfər ˈsuzən wʊlf,

BT93bRX2t68.text: who invented JoJo’s case.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: hu ˌɪnˈvɛntɪd jojo’s* keɪs.

BT93bRX2t68.text: Wolf believes that even though JoJo’s actions do reflect his deep self,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: wʊlf bɪˈlivz ðət ˈivɪn ðoʊ jojo’s* ˈækʃənz du rɪˈflɛkt hɪz dip sɛlf,

BT93bRX2t68.text: when determining moral responsibility, we also need to consider how someone’s deep self came to be.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: wɪn dɪˈtərmənɪŋ ˈmɔrəl riˌspɑnsəˈbɪləti, wi ˈɔlsoʊ nid tɪ kənˈsɪdər haʊ someone’s* dip sɛlf keɪm tɪ bi.

BT93bRX2t68.text: And in JoJo's situation, Wolf believes that even if JoJo is acting on his own values,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ənd ɪn jojo's* ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən, wʊlf bɪˈlivz ðət ˈivɪn ɪf jojo* ɪz ˈæktɪŋ ɔn hɪz oʊn ˈvæljuz,

BT93bRX2t68.text: his upbringing makes him less responsible for his cruelty.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: hɪz ˈəpˌbrɪŋɪŋ meɪks ɪm lɛs riˈspɑnsəbəl fər hɪz ˈkruəlti.

BT93bRX2t68.text: Wolf’s logic seems pretty reasonable in JoJo’s case.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: wolf’s* ˈlɑʤɪk simz ˈprɪti ˈrizənəbəl ɪn jojo’s* keɪs.

BT93bRX2t68.text: But if everyone’s deepest values are shaped by their upbringing,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: bət ɪf everyone’s* ˈdipəst ˈvæljuz ər ʃeɪpt baɪ ðɛr ˈəpˌbrɪŋɪŋ,

BT93bRX2t68.text: does that mean nobody is responsible for their actions?
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: dɪz ðət min ˈnoʊˌbɑˌdi ɪz riˈspɑnsəbəl fər ðɛr ˈækʃənz?

BT93bRX2t68.text: This is the attitude held by incompatibilists.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ðə ˈætəˌtud hɛld baɪ incompatibilists*.

BT93bRX2t68.text: These philosophers believe that if everything is predetermined by factors like our environment or biology,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ðiz fəˈlɑsəfərz bɪˈliv ðət ɪf ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ɪz ˌpridiˈtərmɪnd baɪ ˈfæktərz laɪk ɑr ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt ər baɪˈɑləʤi,

BT93bRX2t68.text: then no one is truly morally responsible for anything. By contrast, compatibilist philosophers
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ðɛn noʊ wən ɪz ˈtruli ˈmɔrəli riˈspɑnsəbəl fər ˈɛniˌθɪŋ. baɪ ˈkɑntræst, compatibilist* fəˈlɑsəfərz

BT93bRX2t68.text: argue that even if our decisions are the inevitable result of past events,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ˈɑrgju ðət ˈivɪn ɪf ɑr dɪˈsɪʒənz ər ðə ˌɪˈnɛvətəbəl rɪˈzəlt əv pæst ɪˈvɛnts,

BT93bRX2t68.text: we can still be held responsible for them.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: wi kən stɪl bi hɛld riˈspɑnsəbəl fər ðɛm.

BT93bRX2t68.text: The debate between these factions has been raging for centuries,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ðə dəˈbeɪt bɪtˈwin ðiz ˈfækʃənz həz bɪn ˈreɪʤɪŋ fər ˈsɛnʧəriz,

BT93bRX2t68.text: but Wolf created JoJo’s case to focus on a different question.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: bət wʊlf kriˈeɪtɪd jojo’s* keɪs tɪ ˈfoʊkɪs ɔn ə ˈdɪfərənt kˈwɛʃən.

BT93bRX2t68.text: Unlike most people, JoJo had no meaningful opportunity to learn right from wrong.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ənˈlaɪk moʊst ˈpipəl, jojo* hæd noʊ ˈminɪŋfəl ˌɑpərˈtunəti tɪ lərn raɪt frəm rɔŋ.

BT93bRX2t68.text: And since he continues to be cruel even as an adult,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ənd sɪns hi kənˈtɪnjuz tɪ bi kruəl ˈivɪn ɛz ən ˈædəlt,

BT93bRX2t68.text: it would seem JoJo also lacks the capacity to self-reflect and change his values.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ɪt wʊd sim jojo* ˈɔlsoʊ læks ðə kəˈpæsɪti tɪ self-reflect* ənd ʧeɪnʤ hɪz ˈvæljuz.

BT93bRX2t68.text: To Wolf, this indicates that JoJo lacks basic moral competence.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: tɪ wʊlf, ðɪs ˈɪndɪˌkeɪts ðət jojo* læks ˈbeɪsɪk ˈmɔrəl ˈkɑmpətɪns.

BT93bRX2t68.text: If he doesn’t know right from wrong and can no longer learn the difference,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ɪf hi doesn’t* noʊ raɪt frəm rɔŋ ənd kən noʊ ˈlɔŋgər lərn ðə ˈdɪfərəns,

BT93bRX2t68.text: surely it's misplaced to fully blame him for his actions.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ˈʃʊrli ɪts mɪˈspleɪst tɪ ˈfʊli bleɪm ɪm fər hɪz ˈækʃənz.

BT93bRX2t68.text: But this argument raises another important question:
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: bət ðɪs ˈɑrgjəmənt ˈreɪzɪz əˈnəðər ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt kˈwɛʃən:

BT93bRX2t68.text: even with this horrible upbringing, was JoJo’s moral incompetence truly inevitable?
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ˈivɪn wɪθ ðɪs ˈhɔrəbəl ˈəpˌbrɪŋɪŋ, wɑz jojo’s* ˈmɔrəl ˌɪnˈkɑmpətəns ˈtruli ˌɪˈnɛvətəbəl?

BT93bRX2t68.text: Let’s imagine JoJo had a sister who was raised in the same environment but developed different values.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: let’s* ˌɪˈmæʤən jojo* hæd ə ˈsɪstər hu wɑz reɪzd ɪn ðə seɪm ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt bət dɪˈvɛləpt ˈdɪfərənt ˈvæljuz.

BT93bRX2t68.text: If JoJa rejected their father’s tyranny,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ɪf joja* rɪˈʤɛktɪd ðɛr father’s* ˈtɪrəni,

BT93bRX2t68.text: this suggests that JoJo could have done the same—
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ðɪs səˈʤɛsts ðət jojo* kʊd hæv dən ðə same—*—

BT93bRX2t68.text: an outcome that seemingly increases his moral responsibility.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ən ˈaʊtˌkəm ðət ˈsimɪŋli ˌɪnˈkrisɪz hɪz ˈmɔrəl riˌspɑnsəˈbɪləti.

BT93bRX2t68.text: Similarly, what if a rogue court member tried to teach young JoJo
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ˈsɪmələrli, wət ɪf ə roʊg kɔrt ˈmɛmbər traɪd tɪ tiʧ jəŋ jojo*

BT93bRX2t68.text: about justice and morality, but he still chose tyranny?
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: əˈbaʊt ˈʤəstɪs ənd ˌmɔˈræləˌti, bət hi stɪl ʧoʊz ˈtɪrəni?

BT93bRX2t68.text: Just as Wolf argues, it seems like the more chances JoJo has to develop moral competence,
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ʤɪst ɛz wʊlf ˈɑrgjuz, ɪt simz laɪk ðə mɔr ˈʧænsɪz jojo* həz tɪ dɪˈvɛləp ˈmɔrəl ˈkɑmpətɪns,

BT93bRX2t68.text: the less we can excuse his actions. Ultimately, it's up to you to decide.
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ðə lɛs wi kən ɪkˈskjuz hɪz ˈækʃənz. ˈəltəmətli, ɪts əp tɪ ju tɪ ˌdɪˈsaɪd.

BT93bRX2t68.text: How should we determine moral responsibility?
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: haʊ ʃʊd wi dɪˈtərmən ˈmɔrəl riˌspɑnsəˈbɪləti?

BT93bRX2t68.text: And is JoJo’s tyrannical nature truly inevitable—
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ənd ɪz jojo’s* tərˈænɪkəl ˈneɪʧər ˈtruli inevitable—*—

BT93bRX2t68.text: or can our understanding of right and wrong transcend our upbringing?
BT93bRX2t68.ipa: ər kən ɑr ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ əv raɪt ənd rɔŋ trænˈsɛnd ɑr ˈəpˌbrɪŋɪŋ?

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: In the 1970s, psychoanalyst Samuel Dunkell proposed a curious,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ɪn ðə 1970s*, ˌsaɪkoʊˈænələst ˈsæmjul dunkell* prəˈpoʊzd ə ˈkjʊriəs,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: albeit almost completely unsubstantiated, theory that the position you sleep in reflects your personality.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ɔlˈbiɪt ˈɔlˌmoʊst kəmˈplitli ˌənsəbˈstænʃiˌeɪtɪd, ˈθɪri ðət ðə pəˈzɪʃən ju slip ɪn rɪˈflɛkts jʊr ˌpərsəˈnælɪti.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: For example, preferring to snooze in the so-called royal position indicated self-confidence,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, prɪˈfərɪŋ tɪ snuz ɪn ðə ˈsoʊˈkɔld rɔɪəl pəˈzɪʃən ˈɪndəˌkeɪtɪd ˌsɛlfˈkɑnfədəns,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: while sleeping semi-fetal meant you’re sensible and well adjusted.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: waɪl sˈlipɪŋ semi-fetal* mɛnt you’re* ˈsɛnsəbəl ənd wɛl əˈʤəstɪd.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: While Dunkell’s ideas lack any definitive proof, how we sleep can impact our health.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: waɪl dunkell’s* aɪˈdiəz læk ˈɛni dɪˈfɪnɪtɪv pruf, haʊ wi slip kən ˌɪmˈpækt ɑr hɛlθ.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: So, what’s the best position to sleep in?
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: soʊ, what’s* ðə bɛst pəˈzɪʃən tɪ slip ɪn?

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Sleep positions go by countless creative names—
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: slip pəˈzɪʃənz goʊ baɪ ˈkaʊntləs kriˈeɪtɪv names—*—

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: the zombie, mountain climber, free faller, and soldier—
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ðə ˈzɑmbi, ˈmaʊntən ˈklaɪmər, fri ˈfɔlər, ənd soldier—*—

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: yet sleep experts typically simplify them into four basic types:
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: jɛt slip ˈɛkspərts ˈtɪpɪkəli ˈsɪmpləˌfaɪ ðɛm ˈɪntu fɔr ˈbeɪsɪk taɪps:

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: left side, right side, stomach, or back.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: lɛft saɪd, raɪt saɪd, ˈstəmək, ər bæk.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Until the age of one, it is recommended that babies sleep on their backs to lower their risk of choking and suffocation.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ənˈtɪl ðə eɪʤ əv wən, ɪt ɪz ˌrɛkəˈmɛndɪd ðət ˈbeɪbiz slip ɔn ðɛr bæks tɪ loʊər ðɛr rɪsk əv ˈʧoʊkɪŋ ənd ˌsəfəˈkeɪʃən.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: As young children, we start to move around more in our sleep, usually ending up on our sides and backs.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ɛz jəŋ ˈʧɪldrən, wi stɑrt tɪ muv əraʊnd mɔr ɪn ɑr slip, ˈjuʒəwəli ˈɛndɪŋ əp ɔn ɑr saɪdz ənd bæks.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: And this tendency to avoid sleeping on our stomachs only grows with age.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ənd ðɪs ˈtɛndənsi tɪ əˈvɔɪd sˈlipɪŋ ɔn ɑr ˈstəməks ˈoʊnli groʊz wɪθ eɪʤ.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: By adulthood, most people prefer side-sleeping, while fewer favor back-sleeping,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: baɪ əˈdəlˌthʊd, moʊst ˈpipəl prɪˈfər side-sleeping*, waɪl fjuər ˈfeɪvər back-sleeping*,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: and stomach-sleeping is the least preferred overall. But of course, individual sleep styles are highly personal:
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ənd stomach-sleeping* ɪz ðə list prɪˈfərd ˈoʊvərˌɔl. bət əv kɔrs, ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəl slip staɪlz ər ˈhaɪli ˈpərsɪnəl:

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: some people are sworn belly sleepers, while others can't nod off unless they're sprawled out on their backs.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: səm ˈpipəl ər swɔrn ˈbɛli sˈlipərz, waɪl ˈəðərz kænt nɑd ɔf ənˈlɛs ðɛr sprɔld aʊt ɔn ðɛr bæks.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: How we spend the night affects our bodies in several ways. Different sleep postures shift the relative positions of our internal organs,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: haʊ wi spɛnd ðə naɪt əˈfɛkts ɑr ˈbɑdiz ɪn ˈsɛvərəl weɪz. ˈdɪfərənt slip ˈpɑsʧərz ʃɪft ðə ˈrɛlətɪv pəˈzɪʃənz əv ɑr ˌɪnˈtərnəl ˈɔrgənz,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: which can either relieve or worsen certain health issues.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: wɪʧ kən ˈiðər rɪˈliv ər ˈwərsən ˈsərtən hɛlθ ˈɪʃuz.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Others can leave the spine unsupported, straining nearby tissue,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ˈəðərz kən liv ðə spaɪn ənsəˈpɔrtɪd, ˈstreɪnɪŋ ˈnɪrˈbaɪ ˈtɪʃu,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: and preventing the muscles in the back, hips, or neck from fully relaxing.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ənd prɪˈvɛnɪŋ ðə ˈməsəlz ɪn ðə bæk, hɪps, ər nɛk frəm ˈfʊli rɪˈlæksɪŋ.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: This can then lead to tension and stiffness in the morning.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ðɪs kən ðɛn lɛd tɪ ˈtɛnʃən ənd ˈstɪfnəs ɪn ðə ˈmɔrnɪŋ.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Take, for example, the least popular position: sleeping prone.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: teɪk, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ðə list ˈpɑpjələr pəˈzɪʃən: sˈlipɪŋ proʊn.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Of the four, this also tends to be the least recommended by sleep experts,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: əv ðə fɔr, ðɪs ˈɔlsoʊ tɛndz tɪ bi ðə list ˌrɛkəˈmɛndɪd baɪ slip ˈɛkspərts,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: as it's thought to stretch the natural curvature of your spine.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ɛz ɪts θɔt tɪ strɛʧ ðə ˈnæʧərəl ˈkərvəʧər əv jʊr spaɪn.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Yet there are unique situations when lying on your stomach may be beneficial.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: jɛt ðɛr ər juˈnik ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃənz wɪn laɪɪŋ ɔn jʊr ˈstəmək meɪ bi ˌbɛnəˈfɪʃəl.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: For patients with certain respiratory conditions, for example, it allows the back of the lungs to expand more freely.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: fər ˈpeɪʃənz wɪθ ˈsərtən ˈrɛspərəˌtɔri kənˈdɪʃənz, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ɪt əˈlaʊz ðə bæk əv ðə ləŋz tɪ ɪkˈspænd mɔr ˈfrili.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Sleeping on your back, on the other hand, is often preferred by those with lower back pain.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: sˈlipɪŋ ɔn jʊr bæk, ɔn ðə ˈəðər hænd, ɪz ˈɔfən prɪˈfərd baɪ ðoʊz wɪθ loʊər bæk peɪn.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: When paired with a small pillow under the knees, this position allows the spine to maintain its natural curve.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: wɪn pɛrd wɪθ ə smɔl ˈpɪloʊ ˈəndər ðə niz, ðɪs pəˈzɪʃən əˈlaʊz ðə spaɪn tɪ meɪnˈteɪn ɪts ˈnæʧərəl kərv.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: However, it may be less than ideal for your sleep partner.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ɪt meɪ bi lɛs ðən aɪˈdil fər jʊr slip ˈpɑrtnər.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: For some people, lying on their back can cause the soft tissues of the throat and tongue to collapse into the airway.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: fər səm ˈpipəl, laɪɪŋ ɔn ðɛr bæk kən kɔz ðə sɔft ˈtɪʃuz əv ðə θroʊt ənd təŋ tɪ kəˈlæps ˈɪntu ðə ˈɛrˌweɪ.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Then, as the body tries to pull air through, these tissues vibrate, creating the sound we call snoring.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ðɛn, ɛz ðə ˈbɑdi traɪz tɪ pʊl ɛr θru, ðiz ˈtɪʃuz ˈvaɪbreɪt, kriˈeɪtɪŋ ðə saʊnd wi kɔl sˈnɔrɪŋ.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: This is also why people with sleep apnea are often told to avoid this position.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ waɪ ˈpipəl wɪθ slip ˈæpniə ər ˈɔfən toʊld tɪ əˈvɔɪd ðɪs pəˈzɪʃən.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: This condition already involves pauses in breathing, and back sleeping can increase the likelihood
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ðɪs kənˈdɪʃən ɔˈrɛdi ˌɪnˈvɑlvz ˈpɔzɪz ɪn ˈbriðɪŋ, ənd bæk sˈlipɪŋ kən ˌɪnˈkris ðə ˈlaɪkliˌhʊd

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: that the airway collapses completely, blocking airflow.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ðət ðə ˈɛrˌweɪ kəˈlæpsɪz kəmˈplitli, ˈblɑkɪŋ ˈɛrfloʊ.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Today, most experts generally recommend side-sleeping, particularly when combined with a pillow between the knees,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: təˈdeɪ, moʊst ˈɛkspərts ˈʤɛnərəli ˌrɛkəˈmɛnd side-sleeping*, ˌpɑrˈtɪkjələrli wɪn kəmˈbaɪnd wɪθ ə ˈpɪloʊ bɪtˈwin ðə niz,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: to better align the spine.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: tɪ ˈbɛtər əˈlaɪn ðə spaɪn.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: But as it turns out, which side you lie on may make a difference.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: bət ɛz ɪt tərnz aʊt, wɪʧ saɪd ju laɪ ɔn meɪ meɪk ə ˈdɪfərəns.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: For example, studies show that sleeping on the left side often reduces heartburn symptoms for those with acid reflux.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ˈstədiz ʃoʊ ðət sˈlipɪŋ ɔn ðə lɛft saɪd ˈɔfən rɪˈdusɪz ˈhɑrtˌbərn ˈsɪmptəmz fər ðoʊz wɪθ ˈæsəd reflux*.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Your stomach is primarily located on the left side of your body, so this position situates it below the esophagus,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: jʊr ˈstəmək ɪz praɪˈmɛrəli ˈloʊˌkeɪtəd ɔn ðə lɛft saɪd əv jʊr ˈbɑdi, soʊ ðɪs pəˈzɪʃən situates* ɪt bɪˈloʊ ðə ɪˈsɑfəgəs,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: allowing gravity to naturally keep stomach acid from flowing back up.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: əˈlaʊɪŋ ˈgrævɪti tɪ ˈnæʧərəli kip ˈstəmək ˈæsəd frəm floʊɪŋ bæk əp.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: However, for people with congestive heart failure,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, fər ˈpipəl wɪθ kənˈʤɛstɪv hɑrt ˈfeɪljər,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: who often have an enlarged left ventricle,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: hu ˈɔfən hæv ən ˌɛnˈlɑrʤd lɛft ventricle*,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: sleeping on the right side can help relieve chest pressure and improve their overall quality of sleep.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: sˈlipɪŋ ɔn ðə raɪt saɪd kən hɛlp rɪˈliv ʧɛst ˈprɛʃər ənd ˌɪmˈpruv ðɛr ˈoʊvərˌɔl kˈwɑləti əv slip.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Clearly, there’s no one-size-fits-all answer.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ˈklɪrli, there’s* noʊ one-size-fits-all* ˈænsər.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: And attempting to reap the benefits of any one position can be tough,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ənd əˈtɛmptɪŋ tɪ rip ðə ˈbɛnəfɪts əv ˈɛni wən pəˈzɪʃən kən bi təf,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: since most people shift positions 20 to 30 times per night without even knowing it.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: sɪns moʊst ˈpipəl ʃɪft pəˈzɪʃənz 20 tɪ 30 taɪmz pər naɪt wɪˈθaʊt ˈivɪn noʊɪŋ ɪt.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: Pillows and other sleep training devices can help you stay in one place. But fortunately, unless you have a specific concern,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ˈpɪloʊz ənd ˈəðər slip ˈtreɪnɪŋ dɪˈvaɪsɪz kən hɛlp ju steɪ ɪn wən pleɪs. bət ˈfɔrʧənətli, ənˈlɛs ju hæv ə spɪˈsɪfɪk kənˈsərn,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: sleep movement isn't always a bad thing.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: slip ˈmuvmənt ˈɪzənt ˈɔlˌweɪz ə bæd θɪŋ.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: It's how your body naturally relieves pressure, responds to discomfort, and ultimately prevents injury.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ɪts haʊ jʊr ˈbɑdi ˈnæʧərəli riˈlivz ˈprɛʃər, rɪˈspɑndz tɪ dɪˈskəmfərt, ənd ˈəltəmətli prɪˈvɛnts ˈɪnʤəri.

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: So, the next time you find yourself drifting to the free faller, soldier, or zombie,
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: soʊ, ðə nɛkst taɪm ju faɪnd ˈjɔrsɛlf ˈdrɪftɪŋ tɪ ðə fri ˈfɔlər, ˈsoʊlʤər, ər ˈzɑmbi,

_GI9-J-sE5k.text: you can rest assured.
_GI9-J-sE5k.ipa: ju kən rɛst əˈʃʊrd.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Ever since she can remember, Penny's legs have been to different lengths.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˈɛvər sɪns ʃi kən rɪˈmɛmbər, ˈpɛniz lɛgz hæv bɪn tɪ ˈdɪfərənt lɛŋθs.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Initially, the discrepancy was small enough to ignore, and later it could be fixed with shoe lifts.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˌɪˈnɪʃəli, ðə dɪˈskrɛpənsi wɑz smɔl ɪˈnəf tɪ ˌɪgˈnɔr, ənd ˈleɪtər ɪt kʊd bi fɪkst wɪθ ʃu lɪfts.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: But this difference only increased over time,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: bət ðɪs ˈdɪfərəns ˈoʊnli ˌɪnˈkrist ˈoʊvər taɪm,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: and after years of difficulty walking and misalignments in posture,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ənd ˈæftər jɪrz əv ˈdɪfɪˌkəlti ˈwɔkɪŋ ənd misalignments* ɪn ˈpɑsʧər,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: she's now dealing with scoliosis alongside chronic back and joint pain.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ʃiz naʊ ˈdilɪŋ wɪθ scoliosis* əˈlɔŋˈsaɪd ˈkrɑnɪk bæk ənd ʤɔɪnt peɪn.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Penny is far from the only person with this problem.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˈpɛni ɪz fɑr frəm ðə ˈoʊnli ˈpərsən wɪθ ðɪs ˈprɑbləm.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Differences in leg length can emerge for various reasons,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˈdɪfərənsɪz ɪn lɛg lɛŋθ kən ˈimərʤ fər ˈvɛriəs ˈrizənz,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: from genetic and hormonal conditions to injuries that impact bone growth.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: frəm ʤəˈnɛtɪk ənd ˈhɔrˌmoʊnəl kənˈdɪʃənz tɪ ˈɪnʤəriz ðət ˌɪmˈpækt boʊn groʊθ.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: But while we still don’t know exactly what degree of difference causes health issues,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: bət waɪl wi stɪl don’t* noʊ ɪgˈzæktli wət dɪˈgri əv ˈdɪfərəns ˈkɔzɪz hɛlθ ˈɪʃuz,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: doctors have made great strides in figuring out how to even out these mismatched limbs.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˈdɑktərz hæv meɪd greɪt straɪdz ɪn ˈfɪgjərɪŋ aʊt haʊ tɪ ˈivɪn aʊt ðiz mɪsˈmæʧt lɪmz.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: To lengthen a bone you need to grow new bone tissue,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: tɪ ˈlɛŋθən ə boʊn ju nid tɪ groʊ nu boʊn ˈtɪʃu,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: and a great way to get the body to do that is to break an existing bone.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ənd ə greɪt weɪ tɪ gɪt ðə ˈbɑdi tɪ du ðət ɪz tɪ breɪk ən ɪgˈzɪstɪŋ boʊn.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Within 5 to 7 days of a bone break or fracture,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: wɪˈθɪn 5 tɪ 7 deɪz əv ə boʊn breɪk ər ˈfrækʧər,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: the body will form a fracture callus at the damaged site.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ðə ˈbɑdi wɪl fɔrm ə ˈfrækʧər ˈkæləs æt ðə ˈdæmɪʤd saɪt.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Over several weeks, this jelly-like blob of scab tissue will solidify into cartilage,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˈoʊvər ˈsɛvərəl wiks, ðɪs jelly-like* blɑb əv skæb ˈtɪʃu wɪl səˈlɪdəˌfaɪ ˈɪntu ˈkɑrtəlɪʤ,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: which will harden into standard bone over 2 to 3 months.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: wɪʧ wɪl ˈhɑrdən ˈɪntu ˈstændərd boʊn ˈoʊvər 2 tɪ 3 mənθs.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: But with the proper tools, surgeons can control and extend this process,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: bət wɪθ ðə ˈprɑpər tulz, ˈsərʤənz kən kənˈtroʊl ənd ɪkˈstɛnd ðɪs ˈprɔˌsɛs,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: using the fracture callus like a bone printer to extend a limb’s length.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˈjuzɪŋ ðə ˈfrækʧər ˈkæləs laɪk ə boʊn ˈprɪnər tɪ ɪkˈstɛnd ə limb’s* lɛŋθ.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: While doctors throughout the early 20th century
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: waɪl ˈdɑktərz θruaʊt ðə ˈərli 20th* ˈsɛnʧəri

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: attempted to nail, pull, clamp, and pin bones to guide this process,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: əˈtɛmptəd tɪ neɪl, pʊl, klæmp, ənd pɪn boʊnz tɪ gaɪd ðɪs ˈprɔˌsɛs,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: a monumental breakthrough came in the 1950s
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ə ˌmɑnjəˈmɛnəl ˈbreɪkθˌru keɪm ɪn ðə 1950s*

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: from Soviet orthopedic surgeon Gavriil Ilizarov.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: frəm ˈsoʊviˌɛt ˌɔrθəˈpidɪk ˈsərʤɪn gavriil* ilizarov*.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: He developed a technique called distraction osteogenesis,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: hi dɪˈvɛləpt ə tɛkˈnik kɔld dɪˈstrækʃən osteogenesis*,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: which begins with a controlled bone break called an osteotomy.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: wɪʧ bɪˈgɪnz wɪθ ə kənˈtroʊld boʊn breɪk kɔld ən osteotomy*.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: To do this, doctors cut a small nick in the skin
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: tɪ du ðɪs, ˈdɑktərz kət ə smɔl nɪk ɪn ðə skɪn

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: through which they cut the bone with a chisel-like tool.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: θru wɪʧ ðeɪ kət ðə boʊn wɪθ ə chisel-like* tul.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Then they hooked a kind of circular frame called an external fixator
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ðɛn ðeɪ hʊkt ə kaɪnd əv ˈsərkjələr freɪm kɔld ən ɪkˈstərnəl fixator*

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: above and below the break.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: əˈbəv ənd bɪˈloʊ ðə breɪk.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: The posable fixator allowed doctors to precisely pull the healing bone apart,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ðə posable* fixator* əˈlaʊd ˈdɑktərz tɪ prɪˈsaɪsli pʊl ðə ˈhilɪŋ boʊn əˈpɑrt,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: and the device’s modern-day descendants are still used for correcting complex bone deformities.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ənd ðə device’s* modern-day* dɪˈsɛnɪnts ər stɪl juzd fər kərˈɛktɪŋ ˈkɑmplɛks boʊn dɪˈfɔrmətiz.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: But since their wires and pins puncture the skin, they often pull on muscles, scar soft tissue,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: bət sɪns ðɛr waɪərz ənd pɪnz ˈpəŋkʧər ðə skɪn, ðeɪ ˈɔfən pʊl ɔn ˈməsəlz, skɑr sɔft ˈtɪʃu,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: and create opportunities for infection. So when possible,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ənd kriˈeɪt ˌɑpərˈtunətiz fər ˌɪnˈfɛkʃən. soʊ wɪn ˈpɑsəbəl,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: modern surgeons typically typically rely on a more recent breakthrough: implantable lengthening nails.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˈmɑdərn ˈsərʤənz ˈtɪpɪkəli ˈtɪpɪkəli rɪˈlaɪ ɔn ə mɔr ˈrisənt ˈbreɪkθˌru: ˈɪmˌplæntəbəl ˈlɛŋθənɪŋ neɪlz.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: To see these in action, let's check back in with Penny.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: tɪ si ðiz ɪn ˈækʃən, lɛts ʧɛk bæk ɪn wɪθ ˈpɛni.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Her right femur is five centimeters shorter than its counterpart.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: hər raɪt ˈfimər ɪz faɪv ˈsɛntəˌmitərz ˈʃɔrtər ðən ɪts ˈkaʊntərˌpɑrt.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: To increase its length with an internal nail, the surgeon first places rotation marker pins
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: tɪ ˌɪnˈkris ɪts lɛŋθ wɪθ ən ˌɪnˈtərnəl neɪl, ðə ˈsərʤɪn fərst ˈpleɪsɪz roʊˈteɪʃən ˈmɑrkər pɪnz

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: above and below the site of the eventual bone cut.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: əˈbəv ənd bɪˈloʊ ðə saɪt əv ðə əˈvɛnʧuəl boʊn kət.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Then, they perform an osteotomy,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ðɛn, ðeɪ pərˈfɔrm ən osteotomy*,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: and the surgeon threads the nail through the broken bone,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ənd ðə ˈsərʤɪn θrɛdz ðə neɪl θru ðə ˈbroʊkən boʊn,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: securing it on both sides of the cut before removing the rotation markers and closing the skin.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: sɪˈkjʊrɪŋ ɪt ɔn boʊθ saɪdz əv ðə kət ˌbiˈfɔr riˈmuvɪŋ ðə roʊˈteɪʃən ˈmɑrkərz ənd ˈkloʊzɪŋ ðə skɪn.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Once the fracture callus forms, the nail can get to work in one of several ways.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: wəns ðə ˈfrækʧər ˈkæləs fɔrmz, ðə neɪl kən gɪt tɪ wərk ɪn wən əv ˈsɛvərəl weɪz.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Some allow the patient to twist their leg back and forth to rotate gears along a track.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: səm əˈlaʊ ðə ˈpeɪʃənt tɪ twɪst ðɛr lɛg bæk ənd fɔrθ tɪ ˈroʊˌteɪt gɪrz əˈlɔŋ ə træk.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Others have a controller that can gradually extend the nail,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˈəðərz hæv ə kənˈtroʊlər ðət kən ˈgræʤuəli ɪkˈstɛnd ðə neɪl,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: either via a cord routed through the skin,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ˈiðər ˈviə ə kɔrd ˈrutɪd θru ðə skɪn,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: or wirelessly controlled magnets that rotate gears inside the nail.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ər wirelessly* kənˈtroʊld ˈmægnəts ðət ˈroʊˌteɪt gɪrz ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə neɪl.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: In a given day, the bone is gradually lengthened several times, rarely exceeding one millimeter daily.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ɪn ə ˈgɪvɪn deɪ, ðə boʊn ɪz ˈgræʤuəli ˈlɛŋθənd ˈsɛvərəl taɪmz, ˈrɛrli ɪkˈsidɪŋ wən ˈmɪləˌmitər ˈdeɪli.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: This slow speed is essential, both for bone formation and nearby soft tissue.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ðɪs sloʊ spid ɪz ɛˈsɛnʃəl, boʊθ fər boʊn fɔrˈmeɪʃən ənd ˈnɪrˈbaɪ sɔft ˈtɪʃu.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Since our bodies can’t make new muscle or nerve cells,
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: sɪns ɑr ˈbɑdiz can’t* meɪk nu ˈməsəl ər nərv sɛlz,

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: limb lengthening requires our existing cells to stretch.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: lɪm ˈlɛŋθənɪŋ rikˈwaɪərz ɑr ɪgˈzɪstɪŋ sɛlz tɪ strɛʧ.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: And if they’re stretched too quickly, it can lead to joint tightness or nerve pain.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ənd ɪf they’re* strɛʧt tu kˈwɪkli, ɪt kən lɛd tɪ ʤɔɪnt ˈtaɪtnəs ər nərv peɪn.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Once the desired length is reached, the new bone is left to heal and solidify.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: wəns ðə dɪˈzaɪərd lɛŋθ ɪz riʧt, ðə nu boʊn ɪz lɛft tɪ hil ənd səˈlɪdəˌfaɪ.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Like any surgery, bone lengthening has its risks and limitations.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: laɪk ˈɛni ˈsərʤəri, boʊn ˈlɛŋθənɪŋ həz ɪts rɪsks ənd ˌlɪmɪˈteɪʃənz.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: In rare cases, implanted nails can break, requiring further surgery to remove or replace.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ɪn rɛr ˈkeɪsɪz, ˌɪmˈplæntɪd neɪlz kən breɪk, rikˈwaɪərɪŋ ˈfərðər ˈsərʤəri tɪ riˈmuv ər ˌriˈpleɪs.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: And when limb length differences are especially large, patients may require multiple surgeries.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ənd wɪn lɪm lɛŋθ ˈdɪfərənsɪz ər əˈspɛʃəli lɑrʤ, ˈpeɪʃənz meɪ ˌrikˈwaɪər ˈməltəpəl ˈsərʤəriz.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: But the biggest limitations are access and awareness.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: bət ðə ˈbɪgəst ˌlɪmɪˈteɪʃənz ər ˈækˌsɛs ənd əˈwɛrnəs.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: Since this technology is new, the surgery can be expensive.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: sɪns ðɪs tɛkˈnɑləʤi ɪz nu, ðə ˈsərʤəri kən bi ɪkˈspɛnsɪv.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: And many doctors and hospitals don’t yet have the tools or expertise to perform lengthening surgeries.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: ənd ˈmɛni ˈdɑktərz ənd ˈhɑˌspɪtəlz don’t* jɛt hæv ðə tulz ər ˌɛkspərˈtiz tɪ pərˈfɔrm ˈlɛŋθənɪŋ ˈsərʤəriz.

ul-c6a4QkiU.text: But hopefully it won't take long for these surgeries to become accessible to people from all walks of life.
ul-c6a4QkiU.ipa: bət ˈhoʊpfəli ɪt woʊnt teɪk lɔŋ fər ðiz ˈsərʤəriz tɪ bɪˈkəm ækˈsɛsəbəl tɪ ˈpipəl frəm ɔl wɔks əv laɪf.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: In 1900, Greek divers stumbled upon a 2,000-year-old shipwreck
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ɪn 1900 grik ˈdaɪvərz ˈstəmbəld əˈpɑn ə 2,000-year-old* ˈʃɪprɛk

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: whose contents would shake our understanding of the ancient world.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: huz ˈkɑntɛnts wʊd ʃeɪk ɑr ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ əv ðə ˈeɪnʧənt wərld.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Among the remains were fragments of mangled wood and corroded metal,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: əˈməŋ ðə rɪˈmeɪnz wər ˈfrægmənts əv ˈmæŋgəld wʊd ənd kərˈoʊdɪd ˈmɛtəl,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: which archaeologists soon realized contained an intricate series of gears,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: wɪʧ ˌɑrkiˈɑləʤɪsts sun ˈriəˌlaɪzd kənˈteɪnd ən ˈɪntrəkət ˈsɪriz əv gɪrz,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: resembling technology not believed to exist until centuries later.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: rɪˈzɛmbəlɪŋ tɛkˈnɑləʤi nɑt bɪˈlivd tɪ ɪgˈzɪst ənˈtɪl ˈsɛnʧəriz ˈleɪtər.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: By the 1950s, scientists began to understand what they'd found.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: baɪ ðə 1950s*, ˈsaɪəntɪsts bɪˈgæn tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd wət ðeɪd faʊnd.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: These fragments were part of a device that could calculate the movements of the Sun, Moon, and planets.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðiz ˈfrægmənts wər pɑrt əv ə dɪˈvaɪs ðət kʊd ˈkælkjəˌleɪt ðə ˈmuvmənts əv ðə sən, mun, ənd ˈplænəts.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Now known as the Antikythera mechanism,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: naʊ noʊn ɛz ðə antikythera* ˈmɛkəˌnɪzəm,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: this instrument appears to be the oldest geared device ever discovered
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðɪs ˈɪnstrəmənt əˈpɪrz tɪ bi ðə ˈoʊldəst gɪrd dɪˈvaɪs ˈɛvər dɪˈskəvərd

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: and humankind’s first computer.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd humankind’s* fərst kəmˈpjutər.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Most modern devices, like your phone or laptop, are examples of digital computers.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: moʊst ˈmɑdərn dɪˈvaɪsɪz, laɪk jʊr foʊn ər ˈlæpˌtɑp, ər ɪgˈzæmpəlz əv ˈdɪʤɪtəl kəmˈpjutərz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: These systems store information in strings of zeros and ones that they can then convert into images on a screen
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðiz ˈsɪstəmz stɔr ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ɪn strɪŋz əv ˈzɪroʊz ənd wənz ðət ðeɪ kən ðɛn ˈkɑnvərt ˈɪntu ˈɪmɪʤɪz ɔn ə skrin

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: or sounds in a speaker.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ər saʊnz ɪn ə ˈspikər.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: But more generally, a computer refers to any device that stores information
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: bət mɔr ˈʤɛnərəli, ə kəmˈpjutər rɪˈfərz tɪ ˈɛni dɪˈvaɪs ðət stɔrz ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: and can convert it from one format into another.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd kən ˈkɑnvərt ɪt frəm wən ˈfɔrˌmæt ˈɪntu əˈnəðər.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: And long before the rise of digital computing,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd lɔŋ ˌbiˈfɔr ðə raɪz əv ˈdɪʤɪtəl kəmˈpjutɪŋ,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: human technology was dominated by analog computers.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ˈjumən tɛkˈnɑləʤi wɑz ˈdɑməˌneɪtəd baɪ ˈænəˌlɔg kəmˈpjutərz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Rather than using binary bits, analog computers receive and store signals from the real world,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ˈrəðər ðən ˈjuzɪŋ ˈbaɪnəˌri bɪts, ˈænəˌlɔg kəmˈpjutərz rɪˈsiv ənd stɔr ˈsɪgnəlz frəm ðə ril wərld,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: such as light or mechanical motion,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: səʧ ɛz laɪt ər məˈkænɪkəl ˈmoʊʃən,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: and convert that information into something “readable.”
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd ˈkɑnvərt ðət ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ˈɪntu ˈsəmθɪŋ ““readable.”*.”

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Think of how a vinyl record stores impressions of sound.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: θɪŋk əv haʊ ə ˈvaɪnəl ˈrɛkərd stɔrz ˌɪmˈprɛʃənz əv saʊnd.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: The recording device— a vinyl cutting system— writes mechanical analogs of the world:
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðə rɪˈkɔrdɪŋ device—*— ə ˈvaɪnəl ˈkətɪŋ system—*— raɪts məˈkænɪkəl analogs* əv ðə wərld:

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: the physical force of sound waves carves grooves into a disc,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðə ˈfɪzɪkəl fɔrs əv saʊnd weɪvz kɑrvz gruvz ˈɪntu ə dɪsk,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: which a record player then reads back into sound.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: wɪʧ ə ˈrɛkərd pleɪər ðɛn ridz bæk ˈɪntu saʊnd.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: The Antikythera is the first known example of analog computing.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðə antikythera* ɪz ðə fərst noʊn ɪgˈzæmpəl əv ˈænəˌlɔg kəmˈpjutɪŋ.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: So how did it work?
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: soʊ haʊ dɪd ɪt wərk?

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Ancient Greek astronomers knew that celestial bodies zoom predictably around the solar system.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ˈeɪnʧənt grik əˈstrɑnəmərz nu ðət səˈlɛsʧəl ˈbɑdiz zum prɪˈdɪktəbli əraʊnd ðə ˈsoʊlər ˈsɪstəm.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: But to forecast events like solar eclipses, they'd need to perform calculations by hand.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: bət tɪ ˈfɔrˌkæst ɪˈvɛnts laɪk ˈsoʊlər ɪˈklɪpsɪz, ðeɪd nid tɪ pərˈfɔrm ˌkælkjəˈleɪʃənz baɪ hænd.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: This device likely made that process much easier.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðɪs dɪˈvaɪs ˈlaɪkli meɪd ðət ˈprɔˌsɛs məʧ ˈiziər.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Setting the dial to a specific day drove a series of gears
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ˈsɛtɪŋ ðə daɪəl tɪ ə spɪˈsɪfɪk deɪ droʊv ə ˈsɪriz əv gɪrz

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: to calculate that day's astronomical personality.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: tɪ ˈkælkjəˌleɪt ðət deɪz ˌæstrəˈnɑmɪkəl ˌpərsəˈnælɪti.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Metallic hands pointed to the positions of the Sun, Moon,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: məˈtælɪk hænz ˈpɔɪntɪd tɪ ðə pəˈzɪʃənz əv ðə sən, mun,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: and what seemed to be the five then-known planets relative to the 12 constellations of the zodiac.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd wət simd tɪ bi ðə faɪv then-known* ˈplænəts ˈrɛlətɪv tɪ ðə 12 ˌkɑnstəˈleɪʃənz əv ðə ˈzoʊdiˌæk.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: And the Antikythera’s features didn’t end there:
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd ðə antikythera’s* ˈfiʧərz didn’t* ɛnd ðɛr:

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: another gear system likely spun a colored sphere representing the lunar phase.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: əˈnəðər gɪr ˈsɪstəm ˈlaɪkli spən ə ˈkələrd sfɪr ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnɪŋ ðə ˈlunər feɪz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: On another face, one of the five dials revolved according to the Saros cycle—
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ɔn əˈnəðər feɪs, wən əv ðə faɪv daɪəlz riˈvɑlvd əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ðə ˈsɑroʊs cycle—*—

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: the approximately 6,585.3 days it takes for the Sun, Moon, and Earth
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðə əˈprɑksəmətli 6,585.3* deɪz ɪt teɪks fər ðə sən, mun, ənd ərθ

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: to return to their exact relative positions.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: tɪ rɪˈtərn tɪ ðɛr ɪgˈzækt ˈrɛlətɪv pəˈzɪʃənz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: There was even a dial that indicated the timing of Ancient Greek festivals.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðɛr wɑz ˈivɪn ə daɪəl ðət ˈɪndəˌkeɪtɪd ðə ˈtaɪmɪŋ əv ˈeɪnʧənt grik ˈfɛstɪvəlz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: For the Antikythera, computation lived in the connections between gears.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: fər ðə antikythera*, ˌkɑmpjəˈteɪʃən lɪvd ɪn ðə kəˈnɛkʃənz bɪtˈwin gɪrz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Each celestial body follows specific rules that govern its motion, positions, and quirks—
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: iʧ səˈlɛsʧəl ˈbɑdi ˈfɑloʊz spɪˈsɪfɪk rulz ðət ˈgəvərn ɪts ˈmoʊʃən, pəˈzɪʃənz, ənd quirks—*—

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: like changing speeds and the Moon’s wobbly orbit.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: laɪk ˈʧeɪnʤɪŋ spidz ənd ðə moon’s* ˈwɑbəli ˈɔrbət.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: The fact that these ancient gears were precisely designed
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðə fækt ðət ðiz ˈeɪnʧənt gɪrz wər prɪˈsaɪsli dɪˈzaɪnd

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: to compute not just one, but many, of these rules simultaneously has astounded researchers.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: tɪ kəmˈpjut nɑt ʤɪst wən, bət ˈmɛni, əv ðiz rulz ˌsaɪməlˈteɪniəsli həz əˈstaʊndɪd ˈrisərʧərz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: For example, the handshake between 13 gears automatically
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ðə ˈhændˌʃeɪk bɪtˈwin 13 gɪrz ˌɔtəˈmætɪkli

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: that recreated our Moon’s orbit was only seconds off from reality.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðət ˈrɛkriˌeɪtɪd ɑr moon’s* ˈɔrbət wɑz ˈoʊnli ˈsɛkəndz ɔf frəm ˌriˈæləˌti.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: Analog computers, like the Antikythera, are, in fact, more efficient than digital computers.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ˈænəˌlɔg kəmˈpjutərz, laɪk ðə antikythera*, ər, ɪn fækt, mɔr ɪˈfɪʃənt ðən ˈdɪʤɪtəl kəmˈpjutərz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: For example, take a digital 4K movie, represented by 100 billion binary digits.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, teɪk ə ˈdɪʤɪtəl 4k* ˈmuvi, ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnɪd baɪ 100 ˈbɪljən ˈbaɪnəˌri ˈdɪʤɪts.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: These bits are fundamentally devoid of an identity.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðiz bɪts ər ˌfəndəˈmɛnəli dɪˈvɔɪd əv ən aɪˈdɛntəˌti.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: They must be assembled by software on your TV or laptop,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðeɪ məst bi əˈsɛmbəld baɪ ˈsɔfˌwɛr ɔn jʊr ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən ər ˈlæpˌtɑp,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: and converted into the colors, motion, and sound that simulate the real world.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd kənˈvərtɪd ˈɪntu ðə ˈkələrz, ˈmoʊʃən, ənd saʊnd ðət ˈsɪmjəˌleɪt ðə ril wərld.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: While the exactness of binary code makes it reliable,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: waɪl ðə exactness* əv ˈbaɪnəˌri koʊd meɪks ɪt rɪˈlaɪəbəl,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: digital logic requires abundance at the expense of efficiency.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ˈdɪʤɪtəl ˈlɑʤɪk rikˈwaɪərz əˈbəndəns æt ðə ɪkˈspɛns əv ɪˈfɪʃənsi.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: And the output is always just an approximation of reality.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd ðə ˈaʊtˌpʊt ɪz ˈɔlˌweɪz ʤɪst ən əˌprɑksəˈmeɪʃən əv ˌriˈæləˌti.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: In contrast, consider an analog scale—
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ɪn ˈkɑntræst, kənˈsɪdər ən ˈænəˌlɔg scale—*—

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: when you step on this device, the force of your body weight presses down on a spring
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: wɪn ju stɛp ɔn ðɪs dɪˈvaɪs, ðə fɔrs əv jʊr ˈbɑdi weɪt ˈprɛsɪz daʊn ɔn ə spərɪŋ

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: which rotates a dial to reveal a number.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: wɪʧ ˈroʊˌteɪts ə daɪəl tɪ rɪˈvil ə ˈnəmbər.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: This “computer” converts mechanical force directly into numbers we can read.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðɪs ““computer”*” ˈkɑnvərts məˈkænɪkəl fɔrs dɪˈrɛkli ˈɪntu ˈnəmbərz wi kən rɛd.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: It’s a continuous input:
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: it’s* ə kənˈtɪnjuəs ˈɪnˌpʊt:

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: weighing 150 pounds moves the spring more than weighing 149 pounds does.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: weɪɪŋ 150 paʊnz muvz ðə spərɪŋ mɔr ðən weɪɪŋ 149 paʊnz dɪz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: And by acting continuously rather than discretely,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd baɪ ˈæktɪŋ kənˈtɪnjuəsli ˈrəðər ðən discretely*,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: many experts argue that analog computers are more real than digital ones.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ˈmɛni ˈɛkspərts ˈɑrgju ðət ˈænəˌlɔg kəmˈpjutərz ər mɔr ril ðən ˈdɪʤɪtəl wənz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: This elegance of faithfully representing real information has renewed interest in analog tech,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ðɪs ˈɛləgəns əv ˈfeɪθfəli ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnɪŋ ril ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən həz rɪˈnud ˈɪntəˌrɛst ɪn ˈænəˌlɔg tɛk,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: and is even inspiring new technologies.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ənd ɪz ˈivɪn ˌɪnˈspaɪərɪŋ nu tɛkˈnɑləʤiz.

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: In the future, analog computing may improve the efficiency of digital systems,
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ɪn ðə fˈjuʧər, ˈænəˌlɔg kəmˈpjutɪŋ meɪ ˌɪmˈpruv ðə ɪˈfɪʃənsi əv ˈdɪʤɪtəl ˈsɪstəmz,

XNEHP6qFeCs.text: proving what's old can be new again.
XNEHP6qFeCs.ipa: ˈpruvɪŋ wəts oʊld kən bi nu əˈgɛn.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: In 1910, the Antarctic Nimrod Expedition was in trouble. Led by Ernest Shackleton,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ɪn 1910 ðə æˈnɑrtɪk ˈnɪmrɑd ˌɛkspəˈdɪʃən wɑz ɪn ˈtrəbəl. lɛd baɪ ˈərnɪst ˈʃækəltən,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: the team had set the record for the southernmost point ever reached.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ðə tim hæd sɛt ðə ˈrɛkərd fər ðə ˈsəðərnˌmoʊst pɔɪnt ˈɛvər riʧt.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: But after miscalculating how long it would take to return to the nearest supply depot, the crew had run out of food.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: bət ˈæftər miscalculating* haʊ lɔŋ ɪt wʊd teɪk tɪ rɪˈtərn tɪ ðə ˈnɪrəst səˈplaɪ ˈdipoʊ, ðə kru hæd rən aʊt əv fud.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Fatigued and desperate, they turned to their medical kit. It contained a drug called “Forced March tablets,”
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: fəˈtigd ənd ˈdɛspərɪt, ðeɪ tərnd tɪ ðɛr ˈmɛdɪkəl kɪt. ɪt kənˈteɪnd ə drəg kɔld ““forced* mɑrʧ tablets,”*,”

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: which promised to mitigate hunger and prolong endurance,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: wɪʧ ˈprɑməst tɪ ˈmɪtəˌgeɪt ˈhəŋgər ənd prəˈlɔŋ ˈɛndərəns,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: thanks to the powerful combination of its two ingredients:
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: θæŋks tɪ ðə ˈpaʊərfəl ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv ɪts tu ˌɪnˈgridiənts:

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: cocaine, and the extract of the African kola nut.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: koʊˈkeɪn, ənd ðə ˈɛkˌstrækt əv ðə ˈæfrɪkɑn kola* nət.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: After taking the pills, the team was able to push through and reach safety.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ˈæftər ˈteɪkɪŋ ðə pɪlz, ðə tim wɑz ˈeɪbəl tɪ pʊʃ θru ənd riʧ ˈseɪfti.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: So how did the kola nut, a sacred ingredient from West Africa, end up in Shackleton's emergency kit?
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: soʊ haʊ dɪd ðə kola* nət, ə ˈseɪkrɪd ˌɪnˈgridiənt frəm wɛst ˈæfrɪkɑ, ɛnd əp ɪn ˈʃækəltənz ˈimərʤənsi kɪt?

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: And how did this same combination of kola nut and cocaine make its way into the original recipe of a drink
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ənd haʊ dɪd ðɪs seɪm ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv kola* nət ənd koʊˈkeɪn meɪk ɪts weɪ ˈɪntu ðə ərˈɪʤənəl ˈrɛsəpi əv ə drɪŋk

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: that billions of people consume every day?
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ðət ˈbɪljənz əv ˈpipəl kənˈsum ˈɛvəri deɪ?

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: The kola nut is the seed of the kola tree,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ðə kola* nət ɪz ðə sid əv ðə kola* tri,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: a small evergreen native to the tropical forests of West Africa,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ə smɔl ˈɛvərˌgrin ˈneɪtɪv tɪ ðə ˈtrɑpɪkəl ˈfɔrəsts əv wɛst ˈæfrɪkɑ,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: where it has long been integral to the cultural fabric of many communities.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: wɛr ɪt həz lɔŋ bɪn ˈɪnəgrəl tɪ ðə ˈkəlʧərəl ˈfæbrɪk əv ˈmɛni kəmˈjunɪtiz.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Among the Igbo, the bitter nut is known as the food of the gods.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: əˈməŋ ðə igbo*, ðə ˈbɪtər nət ɪz noʊn ɛz ðə fud əv ðə gɑdz.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: In Igbo homes, its custom to greet and honor guests by breaking a seed and sharing the lobes.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ɪn igbo* hoʊmz, ɪts ˈkəstəm tɪ grit ənd ˈɑnər gɛsts baɪ ˈbreɪkɪŋ ə sid ənd ˈʃɛrɪŋ ðə loʊbz.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Within Yoruba communities, kola plays a central role in many religious ceremonies,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: wɪˈθɪn jəˈrubə kəmˈjunɪtiz, kola* pleɪz ə ˈsɛntrəl roʊl ɪn ˈmɛni rɪˈlɪʤəs ˈsɛrəˌmoʊniz,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: such as weddings, funerals, and divination.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: səʧ ɛz ˈwɛdɪŋz, fˈjunərəlz, ənd ˌdɪvəˈneɪʃən.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Kola nuts can contain up to 2.5% caffeine,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: kola* nəts kən kənˈteɪn əp tɪ 2.5* kæˈfin,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: or about twice the concentration found in coffee beans, along with trace amounts of theobromine, a structurally similar compound.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ər əˈbaʊt twaɪs ðə ˌkɑnsənˈtreɪʃən faʊnd ɪn ˈkɔfi binz, əˈlɔŋ wɪθ treɪs əˈmaʊnts əv theobromine*, ə ˈstrəkʧərəli ˈsɪmələr ˈkɑmpaʊnd.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Both molecules stimulate the central nervous system, causing effects like alertness and increased blood flow to the muscles and lungs.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: boʊθ ˈmɑləˌkjulz ˈstɪmjəˌleɪt ðə ˈsɛntrəl ˈnərvəs ˈsɪstəm, ˈkɔzɪŋ ˈifɛkts laɪk əˈlərtnəs ənd ˌɪnˈkrist bləd floʊ tɪ ðə ˈməsəlz ənd ləŋz.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: And it’s these properties that likely led to kola’s long-standing use in traditional West African medicine.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ənd it’s* ðiz ˈprɑpərtiz ðət ˈlaɪkli lɛd tɪ kola’s* long-standing* juz ɪn trəˈdɪʃənəl wɛst ˈæfrɪkɑn ˈmɛdəsən.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: But there’s also a bitter side to the kola nut’s history.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: bət there’s* ˈɔlsoʊ ə ˈbɪtər saɪd tɪ ðə kola* nut’s* ˈhɪstəri.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: As early as the 10th century, it was used by various groups in West Africa as currency
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ɛz ˈərli ɛz ðə 10th* ˈsɛnʧəri, ɪt wɑz juzd baɪ ˈvɛriəs grups ɪn wɛst ˈæfrɪkɑ ɛz ˈkərənsi

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: to purchase captives across the Sahara.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: tɪ ˈpərʧəs ˈkæptɪvz əˈkrɔs ðə səˈhɛrə.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: And in the 16th century, Portuguese and Afro-Portuguese traders in the region
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ənd ɪn ðə 16th* ˈsɛnʧəri, ˈpɔrʧəˌgiz ənd afro-portuguese* ˈtreɪdərz ɪn ðə ˈriʤən

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: similarly exchanged the seeds for people, whom they then sold into transatlantic slavery.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ˈsɪmələrli ɪksˈʧeɪnʤd ðə sidz fər ˈpipəl, hum ðeɪ ðɛn soʊld ˈɪntu ˌtrænzəˈtlænɪk sˈleɪvəri.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: By the 17th century, the kola nut had made its way to the Americas.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: baɪ ðə 17th* ˈsɛnʧəri, ðə kola* nət hæd meɪd ɪts weɪ tɪ ðə əˈmɛrɪkəz.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Brazilian oral narratives tell of enslaved peoples smuggling the seeds across the Atlantic in their clothing and hair.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: brəˈzɪljən ˈɔrəl ˈnɛrətɪvz tɛl əv ɛnsˈleɪvd ˈpipəlz sˈməgəlɪŋ ðə sidz əˈkrɔs ðə əˈtlæntɪk ɪn ðɛr ˈkloʊðɪŋ ənd hɛr.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Enslavers who learned of the nut’s energizing properties often weaponized it,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: enslavers* hu ˈlərnɪd əv ðə nut’s* ˈɛnərˌʤaɪzɪŋ ˈprɑpərtiz ˈɔfən weaponized* ɪt,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: supplying it to subjugated workers to induce more back-breaking labor.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: səˈplaɪɪŋ ɪt tɪ ˈsəbʤəˌgeɪtɪd ˈwərkərz tɪ ˌɪnˈdus mɔr back-breaking* ˈleɪbər.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: However, in places like Jamaica, Cuba, and Brazil, many enslaved communities managed to cultivate kola on their own,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ɪn ˈpleɪsɪz laɪk ʤəˈmeɪkə, ˈkjubə, ənd brəˈzɪl, ˈmɛni ɛnsˈleɪvd kəmˈjunɪtiz ˈmænɪʤd tɪ ˈkəltəˌveɪt kola* ɔn ðɛr oʊn,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: allowing them to preserve and continue practicing their cultural and religious traditions.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: əˈlaʊɪŋ ðɛm tɪ prɪˈzərv ənd kənˈtɪnju ˈpræktɪsɪŋ ðɛr ˈkəlʧərəl ənd rɪˈlɪʤəs trəˈdɪʃənz.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: In the late 19th century, the kola nut gained the attention of pharmacists in Europe and North America.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ɪn ðə leɪt 19th* ˈsɛnʧəri, ðə kola* nət geɪnd ðə əˈtɛnʃən əv ˈfɑrməsɪsts ɪn ˈjʊrəp ənd nɔrθ əˈmɛrɪkə.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Many claimed, without much evidence, that the nut contained countless medicinal and even mind-altering properties.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ˈmɛni kleɪmd, wɪˈθaʊt məʧ ˈɛvədəns, ðət ðə nət kənˈteɪnd ˈkaʊntləs məˈdɪsənəl ənd ˈivɪn mind-altering* ˈprɑpərtiz.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Soon, kola-containing tonics, chocolates, and lozenges lined pharmacy shelves.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: sun, kola-containing* ˈtɑnɪks, ˈʧɔkləts, ənd ˈlɔzənʤəz laɪnd ˈfɑrməsi ʃɛlvz.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: It was combined with extracts from the coca leaf, the source of cocaine, to create Shackleton’s Forced March tablets,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ɪt wɑz kəmˈbaɪnd wɪθ ˈɛkˌstrækts frəm ðə ˈkoʊkə lif, ðə sɔrs əv koʊˈkeɪn, tɪ kriˈeɪt shackleton’s* fɔrst mɑrʧ ˈtæbləts,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: which were also supplied to British soldiers during World War I.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: wɪʧ wər ˈɔlsoʊ səˈplaɪd tɪ ˈbrɪtɪʃ ˈsoʊlʤərz ˈdʊrɪŋ wərld wɔr aɪ.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: In 1886, Atlanta pharmacist John Pemberton used this same potent combo
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ɪn 1886 æˈtlæntə ˈfɑrməsɪst ʤɑn ˈpɛmbərtən juzd ðɪs seɪm ˈpoʊtənt ˈkɑmˌboʊ

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: to create the original recipe for Coca-Cola,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: tɪ kriˈeɪt ðə ərˈɪʤənəl ˈrɛsəpi fər ˌkoʊkəˈkoʊlɑ,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: which he claimed could treat ailments like headaches, indigestion, and impotence.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: wɪʧ hi kleɪmd kʊd trit ˈeɪlmənts laɪk ˈhɛˌdeɪks, ˌɪndaɪˈʤɛsʧən, ənd ˈɪmpətəns.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: By the 1920s, African export of the kola nut slowed,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: baɪ ðə 1920s*, ˈæfrɪkɑn ˈɛkspɔrt əv ðə kola* nət sloʊd,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: thanks in part to the introduction of another energizing seed from the region: the cocoa bean.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: θæŋks ɪn pɑrt tɪ ðə ˌɪntrəˈdəkʃən əv əˈnəðər ˈɛnərˌʤaɪzɪŋ sid frəm ðə ˈriʤən: ðə ˈkoʊkoʊ bin.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Around this time, the Coca-Cola company is also said to have significantly reduced the kola nut in its recipe,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: əraʊnd ðɪs taɪm, ðə ˌkoʊkəˈkoʊlɑ ˈkəmpəˌni ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ sɛd tɪ hæv sɪgˈnɪfɪkəntli rɪˈdust ðə kola* nət ɪn ɪts ˈrɛsəpi,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: but no one knows for sure— the exact ingredients remain a closely guarded trade secret.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: bət noʊ wən noʊz fər sure—*— ðə ɪgˈzækt ˌɪnˈgridiənts rɪˈmeɪn ə ˈkloʊsli ˈgɑrdɪd treɪd ˈsikrɪt.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: However, we do know that the company removed all traces of cocaine by 1903.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, wi du noʊ ðət ðə ˈkəmpəˌni riˈmuvd ɔl ˈtreɪsɪz əv koʊˈkeɪn baɪ 1903

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Today, the kola nut can still be found in various drinks and supplements, like Bissy tea, a staple in many Jamaican homes.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: təˈdeɪ, ðə kola* nət kən stɪl bi faʊnd ɪn ˈvɛriəs drɪŋks ənd ˌsəpləˈmɛnts, laɪk bissy* ti, ə ˈsteɪpəl ɪn ˈmɛni ʤəˈmeɪkən hoʊmz.

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: Believed to have been first brought to the region on slave ships in the 17th century,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: bɪˈlivd tɪ hæv bɪn fərst brɔt tɪ ðə ˈriʤən ɔn sleɪv ʃɪps ɪn ðə 17th* ˈsɛnʧəri,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: this tea is just one example of how the kola nut, despite its bitter past,
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: ðɪs ti ɪz ʤɪst wən ɪgˈzæmpəl əv haʊ ðə kola* nət, dɪˈspaɪt ɪts ˈbɪtər pæst,

IpaVpMFBwAE.text: continues to serve as a powerful symbol of cultural preservation and remembrance.
IpaVpMFBwAE.ipa: kənˈtɪnjuz tɪ sərv ɛz ə ˈpaʊərfəl ˈsɪmbəl əv ˈkəlʧərəl ˌprɛzərˈveɪʃən ənd riˈmɛmbrəns.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: In 1796, the scientist Edward Jenner
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ɪn 1796 ðə ˈsaɪəntɪst ˈɛdwərd ˈʤɛnər

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: injected material from a cowpox virus into an eight-year-old boy with a hunch that this would provide the protection needed
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ˌɪnˈʤɛktɪd məˈtɪriəl frəm ə cowpox* ˈvaɪrəs ˈɪntu ən eight-year-old* bɔɪ wɪθ ə hənʧ ðət ðɪs wʊd prəˈvaɪd ðə prəˈtɛkʃən ˈnidɪd

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: to save people from deadly outbreaks of the related smallpox virus. It was a success.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: tɪ seɪv ˈpipəl frəm ˈdɛdli ˈaʊtˌbreɪks əv ðə rɪˈleɪtɪd sˈmɔlˌpɑks ˈvaɪrəs. ɪt wɑz ə səkˈsɛs.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: The eight-year-old was inoculated against the disease and this became the first ever vaccine. But why did it work?
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ðə eight-year-old* wɑz ˌɪˈnɑkjəˌleɪtɪd əˈgɛnst ðə dɪˈziz ənd ðɪs bɪˈkeɪm ðə fərst ˈɛvər ˌvækˈsin. bət waɪ dɪd ɪt wərk?

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: To understand how vaccines function, we need to know how the immune system defends us against contagious diseases
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd haʊ ˌvækˈsinz ˈfəŋkʃən, wi nid tɪ noʊ haʊ ðə ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm dɪˈfɛndz ˈjuˈɛs əˈgɛnst kənˈteɪʤəs dɪˈzizɪz

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: in the first place. When foreign microbes invade us, the immune system triggers a series of responses
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ɪn ðə fərst pleɪs. wɪn ˈfɔrən ˈmaɪˌkroʊbz ˌɪnˈveɪd ˈjuˈɛs, ðə ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm ˈtrɪgərz ə ˈsɪriz əv rɪˈspɑnsɪz

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: in an attempt to identify and remove them from our bodies. The signs that this immune response is working
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ɪn ən əˈtɛmpt tɪ aɪˈdɛntəˌfaɪ ənd riˈmuv ðɛm frəm ɑr ˈbɑdiz. ðə saɪnz ðət ðɪs ˌɪmˈjun rɪˈspɑns ɪz ˈwərkɪŋ

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: are the coughing, sneezing, inflammation and fever we experience,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ər ðə ˈkɑfɪŋ, sˈnizɪŋ, ˌɪnfləˈmeɪʃən ənd ˈfivər wi ɪkˈspɪriəns,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: which work to trap, deter and rid the body of threatening things, like bacteria.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: wɪʧ wərk tɪ træp, dɪˈtər ənd rɪd ðə ˈbɑdi əv θˈrɛtənɪŋ θɪŋz, laɪk bækˈtɪriə.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: These innate immune responses also trigger our second line of defense, called adaptive immunity.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ðiz ˌɪˈneɪt ˌɪmˈjun rɪˈspɑnsɪz ˈɔlsoʊ ˈtrɪgər ɑr ˈsɛkənd laɪn əv dɪˈfɛns, kɔld əˈdæptɪv ˌɪmˈjunɪti.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: Special cells called B cells and T cells are recruited to fight microbes,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ˈspɛʃəl sɛlz kɔld bi sɛlz ənd ti sɛlz ər rɪˈkrutɪd tɪ faɪt ˈmaɪˌkroʊbz,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: and also record information about them, creating a memory of what the invaders look like,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ənd ˈɔlsoʊ ˈrɛkərd ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən əˈbaʊt ðɛm, kriˈeɪtɪŋ ə ˈmɛməri əv wət ðə ˌɪnˈveɪdərz lʊk laɪk,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: and how best to fight them. This know-how becomes handy if the same pathogen invades the body again.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ənd haʊ bɛst tɪ faɪt ðɛm. ðɪs ˈnoʊˌhaʊ bɪˈkəmz ˈhændi ɪf ðə seɪm ˈpæθəʤən ˌɪnˈveɪdz ðə ˈbɑdi əˈgɛn.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: But despite this smart response, there's still a risk involved. The body takes time to learn how to respond to pathogens
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: bət dɪˈspaɪt ðɪs smɑrt rɪˈspɑns, ðɛrz stɪl ə rɪsk ˌɪnˈvɑlvd. ðə ˈbɑdi teɪks taɪm tɪ lərn haʊ tɪ rɪˈspɑnd tɪ ˈpæθəʤənz

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: and to build up these defenses. And even then,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ənd tɪ bɪld əp ðiz dɪˈfɛnsɪz. ənd ˈivɪn ðɛn,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: if a body is too weak or young to fight back when it's invaded,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ɪf ə ˈbɑdi ɪz tu wik ər jəŋ tɪ faɪt bæk wɪn ɪts ˌɪnˈveɪdɪd,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: it might face very serious risk if the pathogen is particularly severe.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ɪt maɪt feɪs ˈvɛri ˈsɪriəs rɪsk ɪf ðə ˈpæθəʤən ɪz ˌpɑrˈtɪkjələrli səˈvɪr.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: But what if we could prepare the body's immune response, readying it before someone even got ill?
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: bət wət ɪf wi kʊd priˈpɛr ðə ˈbɑdiz ˌɪmˈjun rɪˈspɑns, ˈrɛdiɪŋ ɪt ˌbiˈfɔr ˈsəmˌwən ˈivɪn gɑt ɪl?

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: This is where vaccines come in. Using the same principles that the body uses to defend itself,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ðɪs ɪz wɛr ˌvækˈsinz kəm ɪn. ˈjuzɪŋ ðə seɪm ˈprɪnsəpəlz ðət ðə ˈbɑdi ˈjuzɪz tɪ dɪˈfɛnd ˌɪtˈsɛlf,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: scientists use vaccines to trigger the body's adaptive immune system,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ˈsaɪəntɪsts juz ˌvækˈsinz tɪ ˈtrɪgər ðə ˈbɑdiz əˈdæptɪv ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: without exposing humans to the full strength disease. This has resulted in many vaccines, which each work uniquely,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: wɪˈθaʊt ɪkˈspoʊzɪŋ ˈjumənz tɪ ðə fʊl strɛŋθ dɪˈziz. ðɪs həz rɪˈzəltɪd ɪn ˈmɛni ˌvækˈsinz, wɪʧ iʧ wərk juˈnikli,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: separated into many different types. First, we have live attenuated vaccines.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ˈsɛpərˌeɪtɪd ˈɪntu ˈmɛni ˈdɪfərənt taɪps. fərst, wi hæv lɪv əˈtɛnjuˌeɪtɪd ˌvækˈsinz.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: These are made of the pathogen itself but a much weaker and tamer version.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ðiz ər meɪd əv ðə ˈpæθəʤən ˌɪtˈsɛlf bət ə məʧ ˈwikər ənd ˈteɪmər ˈvərʒən.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: Next, we have inactive vaccines, in which the pathogens have been killed. The weakening and inactivation in both types of vaccine
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: nɛkst, wi hæv ˌɪˈnæktɪv ˌvækˈsinz, ɪn wɪʧ ðə ˈpæθəʤənz hæv bɪn kɪld. ðə ˈwikənɪŋ ənd ˌɪˌnæktɪˈveɪʃən ɪn boʊθ taɪps əv ˌvækˈsin

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: ensures that pathogens don't develop into the full blown disease. But just like a disease, they trigger an immune response,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ɪnˈʃʊrz ðət ˈpæθəʤənz doʊnt dɪˈvɛləp ˈɪntu ðə fʊl bloʊn dɪˈziz. bət ʤɪst laɪk ə dɪˈziz, ðeɪ ˈtrɪgər ən ˌɪmˈjun rɪˈspɑns,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: teaching the body to recognize an attack by making a profile of pathogens in preparation.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ˈtiʧɪŋ ðə ˈbɑdi tɪ ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪz ən əˈtæk baɪ ˈmeɪkɪŋ ə ˈproʊˌfaɪl əv ˈpæθəʤənz ɪn ˌprɛpərˈeɪʃən.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: The downside is that live attenuated vaccines can be difficult to make, and because they're live and quite powerful,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ðə ˈdaʊnˌsaɪd ɪz ðət lɪv əˈtɛnjuˌeɪtɪd ˌvækˈsinz kən bi ˈdɪfəkəlt tɪ meɪk, ənd bɪˈkəz ðɛr lɪv ənd kwaɪt ˈpaʊərfəl,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: people with weaker immune systems can't have them, while inactive vaccines don't create long-lasting immunity.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ˈpipəl wɪθ ˈwikər ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəmz kænt hæv ðɛm, waɪl ˌɪˈnæktɪv ˌvækˈsinz doʊnt kriˈeɪt long-lasting* ˌɪmˈjunɪti.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: Another type, the subunit vaccine, is only made from one part of the pathogen, called an antigen,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: əˈnəðər taɪp, ðə subunit* ˌvækˈsin, ɪz ˈoʊnli meɪd frəm wən pɑrt əv ðə ˈpæθəʤən, kɔld ən ˈæntəʤən,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: the ingredient that actually triggers the immune response. By even further isolating specific components of antigens,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ðə ˌɪnˈgridiənt ðət ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈtrɪgərz ðə ˌɪmˈjun rɪˈspɑns. baɪ ˈivɪn ˈfərðər ˈaɪsəˌleɪtɪŋ spɪˈsɪfɪk kəmˈpoʊnənts əv ˈæntɪʤənz,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: like proteins or polysaccharides, these vaccines can prompt specific responses.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: laɪk ˈproʊˌtinz ər ˌpɑˌliˈsækərˌaɪdz, ðiz ˌvækˈsinz kən prɑmpt spɪˈsɪfɪk rɪˈspɑnsɪz.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: Scientists are now building a whole new range of vaccines called DNA vaccines.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ˈsaɪəntɪsts ər naʊ ˈbɪldɪŋ ə hoʊl nu reɪnʤ əv ˌvækˈsinz kɔld ˈdiˌɛˈneɪ ˌvækˈsinz.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: For this variety, they isolate the very genes that make the specific antigens
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: fər ðɪs vərˈaɪəti, ðeɪ ˈaɪsəˌleɪt ðə ˈvɛri ʤinz ðət meɪk ðə spɪˈsɪfɪk ˈæntɪʤənz

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: the body needs to trigger its immune response to specific pathogens. When injected into the human body,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ðə ˈbɑdi nidz tɪ ˈtrɪgər ɪts ˌɪmˈjun rɪˈspɑns tɪ spɪˈsɪfɪk ˈpæθəʤənz. wɪn ˌɪnˈʤɛktɪd ˈɪntu ðə ˈjumən ˈbɑdi,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: those genes instruct cells in the body to make the antigens. This causes a stronger immune response,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ðoʊz ʤinz ˌɪnˈstrəkt sɛlz ɪn ðə ˈbɑdi tɪ meɪk ðə ˈæntɪʤənz. ðɪs ˈkɔzɪz ə ˈstrɔŋgər ˌɪmˈjun rɪˈspɑns,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: and prepares the body for any future threats, and because the vaccine only includes specific genetic material,
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ənd pərˈpɛrz ðə ˈbɑdi fər ˈɛni fˈjuʧər θrɛts, ənd bɪˈkəz ðə ˌvækˈsin ˈoʊnli ˌɪnˈkludz spɪˈsɪfɪk ʤəˈnɛtɪk məˈtɪriəl,

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: it doesn't contain any other ingredients from the rest of the pathogen that could develop into the disease and harm the patient.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ɪt ˈdəzənt kənˈteɪn ˈɛni ˈəðər ˌɪnˈgridiənts frəm ðə rɛst əv ðə ˈpæθəʤən ðət kʊd dɪˈvɛləp ˈɪntu ðə dɪˈziz ənd hɑrm ðə ˈpeɪʃənt.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: If these vaccines become a success, we might be able to build more effective treatments for invasive pathogens in years to come.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ɪf ðiz ˌvækˈsinz bɪˈkəm ə səkˈsɛs, wi maɪt bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ bɪld mɔr ˈifɛktɪv ˈtritmənts fər ˌɪnˈveɪsɪv ˈpæθəʤənz ɪn jɪrz tɪ kəm.

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: Just like Edward Jenner's amazing discovery spurred on modern medicine all those decades ago, continuing the development of vaccines
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: ʤɪst laɪk ˈɛdwərd jenner's* əˈmeɪzɪŋ ˌdɪˈskəvri spərd ɔn ˈmɑdərn ˈmɛdəsən ɔl ðoʊz ˈdɛkeɪdz əˈgoʊ, kənˈtɪnjuɪŋ ðə dɪˈvɛləpmənt əv ˌvækˈsinz

rb7TVW77ZCs.text: might even allow us to treat diseases like HIV, malaria, or Ebola, one day.
rb7TVW77ZCs.ipa: maɪt ˈivɪn əˈlaʊ ˈjuˈɛs tɪ trit dɪˈzizɪz laɪk hiv*, məˈlɛriə, ər iˈboʊlə, wən deɪ.

mzREA81VQ34.text: It's just before dawn on a Saturday in summer 1906,
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ɪts ʤɪst ˌbiˈfɔr dɔn ɔn ə ˈsæˌtɪˌdeɪ ɪn ˈsəmər 1906

mzREA81VQ34.text: and 16-year-old Alice Sutton’s mind is already spinning— tonight’s plans could cost her her job.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ənd 16-year-old* ˈælɪs sutton’s* maɪnd ɪz ɔˈrɛdi spinning—*— tonight’s* plænz kʊd kɔst hər hər ʤɑb.

mzREA81VQ34.text: But for now, there's work to do.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: bət fər naʊ, ðɛrz wərk tɪ du.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Alice gets ready slowly, thinking about how her life has changed.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈælɪs gɪts ˈrɛdi sˈloʊli, ˈθɪŋkɪŋ əˈbaʊt haʊ hər laɪf həz ʧeɪnʤd.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Only a week ago, she was the lone servant in a modest home.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈoʊnli ə wik əˈgoʊ, ʃi wɑz ðə loʊn ˈsərvənt ɪn ə ˈmɑdəst hoʊm.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Now she’s an under-housemaid at Wroxton Abbey in Oxfordshire, England,
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: naʊ she’s* ən under-housemaid* æt wroxton* ˈæbi ɪn oxfordshire*, ˈɪŋglənd,

mzREA81VQ34.text: the grand estate of Sir John and Lady Ann Ashby-Blythe.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ðə grænd ɛˈsteɪt əv sər ʤɑn ənd ˈleɪdi æn ashby-blythe*.

mzREA81VQ34.text: As one of the lower-ranking indoor servants, she reports to the housemaids
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ɛz wən əv ðə lower-ranking* ˈɪnˌdɔr ˈsərvənts, ʃi rɪˈpɔrts tɪ ðə housemaids*

mzREA81VQ34.text: and, ultimately, the housekeeper, Mrs. Fletcher.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ənd, ˈəltəmətli, ðə ˈhaʊˌskipər, ˈmɪsɪz. ˈflɛʧər.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Her responsibilities include scrubbing floors, sweeping fireplaces, and other cleaning tasks.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: hər riˌspɑnsəˈbɪlətiz ˌɪnˈklud ˈskrəbɪŋ flɔrz, sˈwipɪŋ ˈfaɪərˌpleɪsəz, ənd ˈəðər ˈklinɪŋ tæsks.

mzREA81VQ34.text: She’s one of over a million women in domestic service in England— making up almost 30% of the female workforce.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: she’s* wən əv ˈoʊvər ə ˈmɪljən ˈwɪmən ɪn dəˈmɛstɪk ˈsərvɪs ɪn england—*— ˈmeɪkɪŋ əp ˈɔlˌmoʊst 30 əv ðə ˈfiˌmeɪl ˈwərkˌfɔrs.

mzREA81VQ34.text: All but the poorest households have at least one servant,
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ɔl bət ðə ˈpurɪst ˈhaʊsˌhoʊldz hæv æt list wən ˈsərvənt,

mzREA81VQ34.text: while estates like Wroxton employ dozens.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: waɪl ɛˈsteɪts laɪk wroxton* ɪmˈplɔɪ ˈdəzənz.

mzREA81VQ34.text: She waits for Roberta, her nosy roommate, to leave.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ʃi weɪts fər rəˈbərtə, hər ˈnoʊzi ˈruˌmeɪt, tɪ liv.

mzREA81VQ34.text: When all’s clear, Alice slips a stick of chalk into her pocket.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: wɪn all’s* klɪr, ˈælɪs slɪps ə stɪk əv ʧɔk ˈɪntu hər ˈpɑkət.

mzREA81VQ34.text: A buzz of excitement fills the kitchen as everyone discusses tonight’s guest:
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ə bəz əv ɪkˈsaɪtmənt fɪlz ðə ˈkɪʧən ɛz ˈɛvriˌwən dɪˈskəsɪz tonight’s* gɛst:

mzREA81VQ34.text: Prince Arthur, the king’s brother.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: prɪns ˈɑrθər, ðə king’s* ˈbrəðər.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Tonight is Alice’s chance to prove herself to her new employers.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: təˈnaɪt ɪz alice’s* ʧæns tɪ pruv hərˈsɛlf tɪ hər nu ɪmˈplɔɪərz.

mzREA81VQ34.text: As she finishes her breakfast, Alice watches as a kitchen maid carefully stirs eggs,
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ɛz ʃi ˈfɪnɪʃɪz hər ˈbrɛkfəst, ˈælɪs ˈwɑʧɪz ɛz ə ˈkɪʧən meɪd ˈkɛrfəli stərz ɛgz,

mzREA81VQ34.text: ensuring the yolks stay perfectly centered.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ɪnˈʃʊrɪŋ ðə joʊks steɪ ˈpərfəktli ˈsɛntərd.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Like many of the tasks servants perform, it requires painstaking precision.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: laɪk ˈmɛni əv ðə tæsks ˈsərvənts pərˈfɔrm, ɪt rikˈwaɪərz ˈpeɪnˌsteɪkɪŋ priˈsɪʒən.

mzREA81VQ34.text: And Alice thinks it’s the sort of pointless demand only someone who has never had to do it could dream up.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ənd ˈælɪs θɪŋks it’s* ðə sɔrt əv ˈpɔɪntləs dɪˈmænd ˈoʊnli ˈsəmˌwən hu həz ˈnɛvər hæd tɪ du ɪt kʊd drim əp.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Alice and another under-housemaid, Louisa, head to Sir John and Lady Ann’s rooms to perform their morning chores:
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈælɪs ənd əˈnəðər under-housemaid*, luˈizə, hɛd tɪ sər ʤɑn ənd ˈleɪdi ann’s* rumz tɪ pərˈfɔrm ðɛr ˈmɔrnɪŋ ʧɔrz:

mzREA81VQ34.text: filling the water basins, opening the curtains, and carrying out the chamber pots.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈfɪlɪŋ ðə ˈwɔtər ˈbeɪsənz, ˈoʊpənɪŋ ðə ˈkərtənz, ənd ˈkɛriɪŋ aʊt ðə ˈʧeɪmbər pɑts.

mzREA81VQ34.text: The two girls had become fast friends after meeting months ago at a gathering of the Women’s Social and Political Union.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ðə tu gərlz hæd bɪˈkəm fæst frɛndz ˈæftər ˈmitɪŋ mənθs əˈgoʊ æt ə ˈgæðərɪŋ əv ðə women’s* ˈsoʊʃəl ənd pəˈlɪtɪkəl ˈjunjən.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Since 1869, British women who owned property had been allowed to vote in local elections—
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: sɪns 1869 ˈbrɪtɪʃ ˈwɪmən hu oʊnd ˈprɑpərti hæd bɪn əˈlaʊd tɪ voʊt ɪn ˈloʊkəl elections—*—

mzREA81VQ34.text: but not in parliamentary ones.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: bət nɑt ɪn ˌpɑrləˈmɛntəri wənz.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Many hoped the Liberal Party, back in power since December, would expand voting rights for all women,
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈmɛni hoʊpt ðə ˈlɪˌbərəl ˈpɑrti, bæk ɪn paʊər sɪns dɪˈsɛmbər, wʊd ɪkˈspænd ˈvoʊtɪŋ raɪts fər ɔl ˈwɪmən,

mzREA81VQ34.text: but they kept deferring the issue.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: bət ðeɪ kɛpt dɪˈfərɪŋ ðə ˈɪʃu.

mzREA81VQ34.text: This included Alice’s employer, Sir John, who, while a critic of Britain’s imperial project,
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ðɪs ˌɪnˈkludɪd alice’s* ɪmˈplɔɪər, sər ʤɑn, hu, waɪl ə ˈkrɪtɪk əv britain’s* ˌɪmˈpɪriəl ˈprɑʤɛkt,

mzREA81VQ34.text: had yet to voice an opinion on women's suffrage.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: hæd jɛt tɪ vɔɪs ən əˈpɪnjən ɔn ˈwɪmənz ˈsəfrɪʤ.

mzREA81VQ34.text: But now, women of all classes were demanding deeds, not words, from the government—
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: bət naʊ, ˈwɪmən əv ɔl ˈklæsɪz wər dɪˈmændɪŋ didz, nɑt wərdz, frəm ðə government—*—

mzREA81VQ34.text: chaining themselves to railings, smashing windows, and defacing churches.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈʧeɪnɪŋ ðɛmˈsɛlvz tɪ ˈreɪlɪŋz, sˈmæʃɪŋ ˈwɪndoʊz, ənd dɪˈfeɪsɪŋ ˈʧərʧɪz.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Alice and Louisa hadn't gone that far. Not yet.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈælɪs ənd luˈizə ˈhædənt gɔn ðət fɑr. nɑt jɛt.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Alice's afternoon is spent in the downstairs entryway, washing each tile in the grand mosaic with milk
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈælɪsəz ˌæftərˈnun ɪz spɛnt ɪn ðə ˈdaʊnˈstɛrz ˈɛntriweɪ, ˈwɑʃɪŋ iʧ taɪl ɪn ðə grænd moʊˈzeɪɪk wɪθ mɪlk

mzREA81VQ34.text: so it would gleam in the candlelight as the guests arrived.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: soʊ ɪt wʊd glim ɪn ðə ˈkændəˌllaɪt ɛz ðə gɛsts əraɪvd.

mzREA81VQ34.text: As the dinner hour nears, she guides footmen carrying intricately carved butter sculptures to the dining room,
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ɛz ðə ˈdɪnər aʊər nɪrz, ʃi gaɪdz footmen* ˈkɛriɪŋ ˈɪntrəkətli kɑrvd ˈbətər ˈskəlpʧərz tɪ ðə ˈdaɪnɪŋ rum,

mzREA81VQ34.text: and notices Roberta pulling Luisa aside.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ənd ˈnoʊtɪsɪz rəˈbərtə ˈpʊlɪŋ luˈizə əˈsaɪd.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Alice lingers, trying to listen in on their conversation, but Mrs. Fletcher admonishes her for dawdling.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈælɪs ˈlɪŋgərz, traɪɪŋ tɪ ˈlɪsən ɪn ɔn ðɛr ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən, bət ˈmɪsɪz. ˈflɛʧər ædˈmɑnɪʃɪz hər fər ˈdɔdlɪŋ.

mzREA81VQ34.text: By the time Prince Arthur's motorcar finally arrives, Alice has spent over 12 hours on her feet.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: baɪ ðə taɪm prɪns ˈɑrθərz ˈmoʊtərˌkɑr ˈfaɪnəli əraɪvz, ˈælɪs həz spɛnt ˈoʊvər 12 aʊərz ɔn hər fit.

mzREA81VQ34.text: As the dinner bell rings out, she realizes it's now or never.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ɛz ðə ˈdɪnər bɛl rɪŋz aʊt, ʃi ˈriəˌlaɪzɪz ɪts naʊ ər ˈnɛvər.

mzREA81VQ34.text: She and Louisa slip out and run to the nearby church. Alice scrawls “Votes For Women” across the wall
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ʃi ənd luˈizə slɪp aʊt ənd rən tɪ ðə ˈnɪrˈbaɪ ʧərʧ. ˈælɪs scrawls* ““votes* fər women”*” əˈkrɔs ðə wɔl

mzREA81VQ34.text: while Louisa strews pamphlets about.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: waɪl luˈizə strews* ˈpæmfləts əˈbaʊt.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Suddenly, Roberta emerges from the shadows. Before Alice can stammer an excuse, Roberta hurls a rock at one of the windows.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈsədənli, rəˈbərtə ˈimərʤɪz frəm ðə ˈʃæˌdoʊz. ˌbiˈfɔr ˈælɪs kən ˈstæmər ən ɪkˈskjuz, rəˈbərtə hurls* ə rɑk æt wən əv ðə ˈwɪndoʊz.

mzREA81VQ34.text: The three girls stand back and smile, imagining Sir John and the prince’s reactions when they arrive for church the next morning.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ðə θri gərlz stænd bæk ənd smaɪl, ˌɪˈmæʤənɪŋ sər ʤɑn ənd ðə prince’s* riˈækʃənz wɪn ðeɪ əraɪv fər ʧərʧ ðə nɛkst ˈmɔrnɪŋ.

mzREA81VQ34.text: With the prince there, the act might even make the newspapers.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: wɪθ ðə prɪns ðɛr, ðə ækt maɪt ˈivɪn meɪk ðə ˈnuzˌpeɪpərz.

mzREA81VQ34.text: They rush back to the Abbey, arriving just in time to help clean up after dinner.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ðeɪ rəʃ bæk tɪ ðə ˈæbi, ərˈaɪvɪŋ ʤɪst ɪn taɪm tɪ hɛlp klin əp ˈæftər ˈdɪnər.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Alice heads to bed, exhilarated and exhausted,
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈælɪs hɛdz tɪ bɛd, ɪgˈzɪlərˌeɪtɪd ənd ɪgˈzɔstɪd,

mzREA81VQ34.text: unaware of how much would change in the years to come.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˌənəˈwɛr əv haʊ məʧ wʊd ʧeɪnʤ ɪn ðə jɪrz tɪ kəm.

mzREA81VQ34.text: From 1914, the First World War fundamentally altered the rhythms of domestic life.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: frəm 1914 ðə fərst wərld wɔr ˌfəndəˈmɛnəli ˈɔltərd ðə ˈrɪðəmz əv dəˈmɛstɪk laɪf.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Working-class women like Alice took on roles in factories, offices, and civil service.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: working-class* ˈwɪmən laɪk ˈælɪs tʊk ɔn roʊlz ɪn ˈfæktəriz, ˈɔfəsɪz, ənd ˈsɪvəl ˈsərvɪs.

mzREA81VQ34.text: Many never returned to domestic work.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈmɛni ˈnɛvər rɪˈtərnd tɪ dəˈmɛstɪk wərk.

mzREA81VQ34.text: In 1918, some women over 30 were finally allowed to vote in parliamentary elections.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ɪn 1918 səm ˈwɪmən ˈoʊvər 30 wər ˈfaɪnəli əˈlaʊd tɪ voʊt ɪn ˌpɑrləˈmɛntəri ɪˈlɛkʃənz.

mzREA81VQ34.text: But another decade would pass before all women could vote on equal terms with men.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: bət əˈnəðər ˈdɛkeɪd wʊd pæs ˌbiˈfɔr ɔl ˈwɪmən kʊd voʊt ɔn ˈikwəl tərmz wɪθ mɛn.

mzREA81VQ34.text: By the mid-20th century, rising taxes, falling agricultural income,
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: baɪ ðə mid-20th* ˈsɛnʧəri, ˈraɪzɪŋ ˈtæksɪz, ˈfɑlɪŋ ˌægrɪˈkəlʧərəl ˈɪnˌkəm,

mzREA81VQ34.text: and a shrinking workforce led many grand estates to be sold off—
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ənd ə ʃˈrɪŋkɪŋ ˈwərkˌfɔrs lɛd ˈmɛni grænd ɛˈsteɪts tɪ bi soʊld off—*—

mzREA81VQ34.text: relics of a system whose grandeur depended on deep inequalities and the invisibility of the laborers who sustained it.
mzREA81VQ34.ipa: ˈrɛlɪks əv ə ˈsɪstəm huz grænˈdur dɪˈpɛndɪd ɔn dip ˌɪnɪkˈwɑlɪtiz ənd ðə ˌɪnˌvɪzəˈbɪləti əv ðə ˈleɪbərərz hu səˈsteɪnd ɪt.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: You’re sure you know your third grade teacher’s name— it’s like you’re hovering over it in your mind,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: you’re* ʃʊr ju noʊ jʊr θərd greɪd teacher’s* name—*— it’s* laɪk you’re* ˈhəvərɪŋ ˈoʊvər ɪt ɪn jʊr maɪnd,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: but it just won’t materialize. Researchers call this tantalizing torment a “tip-of-the-tongue state,”
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: bət ɪt ʤɪst won’t* məˈtɪriəˌlaɪz. ˈrisərʧərz kɔl ðɪs ˈtænəˌlaɪzɪŋ ˈtɔrˌmɛnt ə ““tip-of-the-tongue* state,”*,”

wtovy-1-QUM.text: and it's something everyone experiences.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ənd ɪts ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈɛvriˌwən ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: But what's actually happening when a word gets caught here? And how can you best get it unstuck?
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: bət wəts ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈhæpənɪŋ wɪn ə wərd gɪts kɔt hir? ənd haʊ kən ju bɛst gɪt ɪt ənˈstək?

wtovy-1-QUM.text: Most of the time, our brains seamlessly summon words from vast stores of knowledge,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: moʊst əv ðə taɪm, ɑr breɪnz ˈsimləsli ˈsəmən wərdz frəm væst stɔrz əv ˈnɑlɪʤ,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: paring their meaning and sounds, and stringing them into sentences.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ˈpɛrɪŋ ðɛr ˈminɪŋ ənd saʊnz, ənd ˈstrɪŋɪŋ ðɛm ˈɪntu ˈsɛntənsɪz.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: But in a tip-of-the-tongue moment, this retrieval process derails, and there's the sensation of remembering the word
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: bət ɪn ə tip-of-the-tongue* ˈmoʊmənt, ðɪs rɪˈtrivəl ˈprɔˌsɛs dɪˈreɪlz, ənd ðɛrz ðə sɛnˈseɪʃən əv rɪˈmɛmbərɪŋ ðə wərd

wtovy-1-QUM.text: but the struggle to recall it. In these moments, on top of the usual brain activity associated with word retrieval,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: bət ðə ˈstrəgəl tɪ ˈriˌkɔl ɪt. ɪn ðiz ˈmoʊmənts, ɔn tɔp əv ðə ˈjuʒəwəl breɪn ækˈtɪvɪti əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪtəd wɪθ wərd rɪˈtrivəl,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: we also see other brain regions light up, like the conflict-detecting anterior cingulate,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: wi ˈɔlsoʊ si ˈəðər breɪn ˈriʤənz laɪt əp, laɪk ðə conflict-detecting* ænˈtɪriər cingulate*,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: which generates that urgently frustrating feeling. It's unclear whether the target word is directly detected
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: wɪʧ ˈʤɛnərˌeɪts ðət ˈərʤəntli ˈfrəˌstreɪtɪŋ ˈfilɪŋ. ɪts ənˈklɪr ˈwɛðər ðə ˈtərgət wərd ɪz dɪˈrɛkli dɪˈtɛktɪd

wtovy-1-QUM.text: and just not successfully recalled,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ənd ʤɪst nɑt səkˈsɛsfəli rɪˈkɔld,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: or associations are simply helping the brain infer that it has the word.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ər əˌsoʊʃiˈeɪʃənz ər ˈsɪmpli ˈhɛlpɪŋ ðə breɪn ˌɪnˈfər ðət ɪt həz ðə wərd.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: The reality could also be some combination of these hypotheses. But the experience is pretty consistent
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ðə ˌriˈæləˌti kʊd ˈɔlsoʊ bi səm ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv ðiz haɪˈpɑθəˌsiz. bət ðə ɪkˈspɪriəns ɪz ˈprɪti kənˈsɪstənt

wtovy-1-QUM.text: with how psycholinguistic theorists think we mentally organize language-related information,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: wɪθ haʊ psycholinguistic* θiərɪsts θɪŋk wi ˈmɛnəli ˈɔrgəˌnaɪz language-related* ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: going from the word's meaning and associations to how it sounds.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: goʊɪŋ frəm ðə wərdz ˈminɪŋ ənd əˌsoʊʃiˈeɪʃənz tɪ haʊ ɪt saʊnz.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: Some researchers think that the last layer of information— sound— might be the most vulnerable to glitches.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: səm ˈrisərʧərz θɪŋk ðət ðə læst leɪər əv information—*— sound—*— maɪt bi ðə moʊst ˈvəlnərəbəl tɪ ˈglɪʧɪz.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: Lots of things seem to influence how likely you are to enter a tip-of-the-tongue state,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: lɑts əv θɪŋz sim tɪ ˈɪnfluəns haʊ ˈlaɪkli ju ər tɪ ˈɛnər ə tip-of-the-tongue* steɪt,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: all the way from what kinds of words are involved, to your experiences and environment.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ɔl ðə weɪ frəm wət kaɪnz əv wərdz ər ˌɪnˈvɑlvd, tɪ jʊr ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz ənd ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: For example, tip-of-the-tongue states occur most frequently with proper nouns,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, tip-of-the-tongue* steɪts əˈkər moʊst ˈfrikwɛntli wɪθ ˈprɑpər naʊnz,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: infrequently used words, and ones that are more abstract and less visual, like “idiosyncrasy” or “revelation.”
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ˌɪnˈfrikwəntli juzd wərdz, ənd wənz ðət ər mɔr ˈæbˌstrækt ənd lɛs ˈvɪʒəwəl, laɪk ““idiosyncrasy”*” ər ““revelation.”*.”

wtovy-1-QUM.text: Cognitively, recognizing a face and associating it with general information,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: cognitively*, ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪzɪŋ ə feɪs ənd əˈsoʊsiˌeɪtɪŋ ɪt wɪθ ˈʤɛnərəl ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: like someone’s profession, is often easy, while names tend to trip us up.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: laɪk someone’s* prəˈfɛʃən, ɪz ˈɔfən ˈizi, waɪl neɪmz tɛnd tɪ trɪp ˈjuˈɛs əp.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: And retrieval of unintended words is also a common problem.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ənd rɪˈtrivəl əv ˌənɪnˈtɛndɪd wərdz ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ ə ˈkɑmən ˈprɑbləm.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: Like if you’re trying to summon the star of “The Wizard of Oz’s” name
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: laɪk ɪf you’re* traɪɪŋ tɪ ˈsəmən ðə stɑr əv ““the* ˈwɪzərd əv oz’s”*” neɪm

wtovy-1-QUM.text: and keep getting stuck on Dorothy instead of Judy Garland.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ənd kip ˈgɪtɪŋ stək ɔn ˈdɔrθi ˌɪnˈstɛd əv ˈʤudi ˈgɑrlənd.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: In these situations, the words seem to work
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ɪn ðiz ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃənz, ðə wərdz sim tɪ wərk

wtovy-1-QUM.text: as distracting, red herring-like blockers in the retrieval pathway.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ɛz dɪˈstræktɪŋ, rɛd herring-like* ˈblɑkərz ɪn ðə rɪˈtrivəl ˈpæθˌweɪ.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: And one study found that stressful test conditions made participants report more tip-of-the-tongue experiences,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ənd wən ˈstədi faʊnd ðət ˈstrɛsfəl tɛst kənˈdɪʃənz meɪd pɑrˈtɪsəpənts rɪˈpɔrt mɔr tip-of-the-tongue* ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: suggesting that environmental conditions, including stress, can exacerbate them.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: səˈʤɛstɪŋ ðət ɪnˌvaɪrənˈmɛnəl kənˈdɪʃənz, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ strɛs, kən ɪgˈzæsərˌbeɪt ðɛm.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: But some people tend to have more tip-of-the-tongue moments than others.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: bət səm ˈpipəl tɛnd tɪ hæv mɔr tip-of-the-tongue* ˈmoʊmənts ðən ˈəðərz.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: This seems to be true for people who speak multiple languages—
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ðɪs simz tɪ bi tru fər ˈpipəl hu spik ˈməltəpəl languages—*—

wtovy-1-QUM.text: perhaps because they know more than one word to describe the same thing.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: pərˈhæps bɪˈkəz ðeɪ noʊ mɔr ðən wən wərd tɪ dɪˈskraɪb ðə seɪm θɪŋ.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: So a word in one language might become an interfering blocker for the target word in another.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: soʊ ə wərd ɪn wən ˈlæŋgwɪʤ maɪt bɪˈkəm ən ˌɪnərˈfɪrɪŋ ˈblɑkər fər ðə ˈtərgət wərd ɪn əˈnəðər.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: This seems to be especially true when someone’s switching between languages— but it might also depend on their fluency:
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ðɪs simz tɪ bi əˈspɛʃəli tru wɪn someone’s* sˈwɪʧɪŋ bɪtˈwin languages—*— bət ɪt maɪt ˈɔlsoʊ dɪˈpɛnd ɔn ðɛr ˈfluənsi:

wtovy-1-QUM.text: one study suggested that people who learned their second language before age five had fewer tip-of-the-tongue stumbles.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: wən ˈstədi səˈʤɛstɪd ðət ˈpipəl hu ˈlərnɪd ðɛr ˈsɛkənd ˈlæŋgwɪʤ ˌbiˈfɔr eɪʤ faɪv hæd fjuər tip-of-the-tongue* ˈstəmbəlz.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: And sometimes even just hearing a language participants didn’t know
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ənd ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ˈivɪn ʤɪst ˈhirɪŋ ə ˈlæŋgwɪʤ pɑrˈtɪsəpənts didn’t* noʊ

wtovy-1-QUM.text: has proven to be enough to increase their tip-of-the-tongue incidents.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: həz ˈpruvən tɪ bi ɪˈnəf tɪ ˌɪnˈkris ðɛr tip-of-the-tongue* ˈɪnsədənts.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: They also seem to rise with age, perhaps from a cognitive decline in speech-related brain areas,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ðeɪ ˈɔlsoʊ sim tɪ raɪz wɪθ eɪʤ, pərˈhæps frəm ə ˈkɑgnɪtɪv dɪˈklaɪn ɪn speech-related* breɪn ˈɛriəz,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: greater accumulation of knowledge to search through, or a combination of both.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ˈgreɪtər əˌkjumjəˈleɪʃən əv ˈnɑlɪʤ tɪ sərʧ θru, ər ə ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv boʊθ.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: But while tip-of-the-tongue states may feel like the brain is failing,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: bət waɪl tip-of-the-tongue* steɪts meɪ fil laɪk ðə breɪn ɪz ˈfeɪlɪŋ,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: they seem to have a positive function.
