wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ðeɪ sim tɪ hæv ə ˈpɑzətɪv ˈfəŋkʃən.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: Words don't usually go past the point of no return and get permanently forgotten
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: wərdz doʊnt ˈjuʒəwəli goʊ pæst ðə pɔɪnt əv noʊ rɪˈtərn ənd gɪt ˈpərmɑˌnɛnˌtli fərˈgɑtən

wtovy-1-QUM.text: so much as they tend to get tougher to access.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: soʊ məʧ ɛz ðeɪ tɛnd tɪ gɪt ˈtəfər tɪ ˈækˌsɛs.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: That tough-to-shake, tip-of-the-tongue feeling of impending success just around the corner
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ðət tough-to-shake*, tip-of-the-tongue* ˈfilɪŋ əv ˌɪmˈpɛndɪŋ səkˈsɛs ʤɪst əraʊnd ðə ˈkɔrnər

wtovy-1-QUM.text: may help motivate us and make us more likely to remember.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: meɪ hɛlp ˈmoʊtəˌveɪt ˈjuˈɛs ənd meɪk ˈjuˈɛs mɔr ˈlaɪkli tɪ rɪˈmɛmbər.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: And researchers have observed that participants in tip-of-the-tongue states
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ənd ˈrisərʧərz hæv əbˈzərvd ðət pɑrˈtɪsəpənts ɪn tip-of-the-tongue* steɪts

wtovy-1-QUM.text: are more likely to spend more time and effort searching for answers.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ər mɔr ˈlaɪkli tɪ spɛnd mɔr taɪm ənd ˈɛfərt ˈsərʧɪŋ fər ˈænsərz.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: One method for getting something off the tip of your tongue is cueing.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: wən ˈmɛθəd fər ˈgɪtɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ ɔf ðə tɪp əv jʊr təŋ ɪz cueing*.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: This means leaning into the clues your brain surfaces, like associated memories and letters.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ðɪs minz ˈlinɪŋ ˈɪntu ðə kluz jʊr breɪn ˈsərfəsɪz, laɪk əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪtəd ˈmɛməriz ənd ˈlɛtərz.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: For example, if you're trying to remember the name of the ancient city with the legendary Hanging Gardens,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ɪf jʊr traɪɪŋ tɪ rɪˈmɛmbər ðə neɪm əv ðə ˈeɪnʧənt ˈsɪti wɪθ ðə ˈlɛʤənˌdɛri ˈhæŋɪŋ ˈgɑrdənz,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: you might recall they’re one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ju maɪt ˈriˌkɔl they’re* wən əv ðə ˈsɛvən ˈwəndərz əv ðə ˈeɪnʧənt wərld

wtovy-1-QUM.text: and run through the alphabet to see if any letters stand out.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: ənd rən θru ðə ˈælfəˌbɛt tɪ si ɪf ˈɛni ˈlɛtərz stænd aʊt.

wtovy-1-QUM.text: Because tip-of-the-tongue moments don’t seem to be just your brain babbling,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: bɪˈkəz tip-of-the-tongue* ˈmoʊmənts don’t* sim tɪ bi ʤɪst jʊr breɪn ˈbæbəlɪŋ,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: but perhaps trying to jog its own extensive memory,
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: bət pərˈhæps traɪɪŋ tɪ ʤɑg ɪts oʊn ɪkˈstɛnsɪv ˈmɛməri,

wtovy-1-QUM.text: where your third grade teacher’s name is surely stored... somewhere.
wtovy-1-QUM.ipa: wɛr jʊr θərd greɪd teacher’s* neɪm ɪz ˈʃʊrli stɔrd... ˈsəmˌwɛr.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Many of you here have probably heard of the 10,000 hours rule.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ˈmɛni əv ju hir hæv ˈprɑbəˌbli hərd əv ðə 10,000* aʊərz rul.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: It’s the idea that to become great in anything takes 10,000 hours of focused practice.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: it’s* ðə aɪˈdiə ðət tɪ bɪˈkəm greɪt ɪn ˈɛniˌθɪŋ teɪks 10,000* aʊərz əv ˈfoʊkɪst ˈpræktɪs.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: So you’d better get started as early as possible. The poster child for this story is Tiger Woods.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: soʊ you’d* ˈbɛtər gɪt ˈstɑrtɪd ɛz ˈərli ɛz ˈpɑsəbəl. ðə ˈpoʊstər ʧaɪld fər ðɪs ˈstɔri ɪz ˈtaɪgər wʊdz.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: His father famously gave him a putter when he was seven months old.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: hɪz ˈfɑðər ˈfeɪməsli geɪv ɪm ə ˈpətər wɪn hi wɑz ˈsɛvən mənθs oʊld.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Fast forward to the age of 21— he’s the greatest golfer in the world. Quintessential 10,000 hours story.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: fæst ˈfɔrwərd tɪ ðə eɪʤ əv 21—* he’s* ðə ˈgreɪtəst ˈgɑlfər ɪn ðə wərld. kˌwɪntɪˈsɛnʃəl 10,000* aʊərz ˈstɔri.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Another is that of the three Polgar sisters, whose father decided to teach them chess in a very technical manner
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: əˈnəðər ɪz ðət əv ðə θri ˈpoʊlgər ˈsɪstərz, huz ˈfɑðər ˌdɪˈsaɪdɪd tɪ tiʧ ðɛm ʧɛs ɪn ə ˈvɛri ˈtɛknɪkəl ˈmænər

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: from a very early age. Two of his daughters went on to become grandmaster chess players
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: frəm ə ˈvɛri ˈərli eɪʤ. tu əv hɪz ˈdɔtərz wɛnt ɔn tɪ bɪˈkəm ˈgrændˈmæstər ʧɛs pleɪərz

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: I got curious: if this 10,000 hours rule is correct, then we should see that elite athletes get a head start
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: aɪ gɑt ˈkjʊriəs: ɪf ðɪs 10,000* aʊərz rul ɪz kərˈɛkt, ðɛn wi ʃʊd si ðət ɪˈlit ˈæθˌlits gɪt ə hɛd stɑrt

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: in so-called deliberate practice. And in fact, when scientists study elite athletes, they see that they spend more time in deliberate practice.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ɪn ˈsoʊˈkɔld dɪˈlɪbərˌeɪt ˈpræktɪs. ənd ɪn fækt, wɪn ˈsaɪəntɪsts ˈstədi ɪˈlit ˈæθˌlits, ðeɪ si ðət ðeɪ spɛnd mɔr taɪm ɪn dɪˈlɪbərˌeɪt ˈpræktɪs.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Not a big surprise. When they actually track athletes over the course of their development, the pattern looks like this:
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: nɑt ə bɪg səˈpraɪz. wɪn ðeɪ ˈæˌkʧuəli træk ˈæθˌlits ˈoʊvər ðə kɔrs əv ðɛr dɪˈvɛləpmənt, ðə ˈpætərn lʊks laɪk ðɪs:

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: the future elites tend to have what scientists call a sampling period, where they try a variety of physical activities.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðə fˈjuʧər ɪˈlits tɛnd tɪ hæv wət ˈsaɪəntɪsts kɔl ə ˈsæmplɪŋ ˈpɪriəd, wɛr ðeɪ traɪ ə vərˈaɪəti əv ˈfɪzɪkəl ækˈtɪvɪtiz.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: They gain broad general skills and delay specializing until later than peers who plateau at lower levels.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðeɪ geɪn brɔd ˈʤɛnərəl skɪlz ənd dɪˈleɪ ˈspɛʃəˌlaɪzɪŋ ənˈtɪl ˈleɪtər ðən pɪrz hu plæˈtoʊ æt loʊər ˈlɛvəlz.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: That doesn’t really comport with the 10,000 hours rule, does it? So I started to wonder about other domains that we associate
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðət doesn’t* ˈrɪli kəmˈpɔrt wɪθ ðə 10,000* aʊərz rul, dɪz ɪt? soʊ aɪ ˈstɑrtɪd tɪ ˈwəndər əˈbaʊt ˈəðər doʊˈmeɪnz ðət wi əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪt

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: with obligatory early specialization, like music. Turns out the pattern is often similar.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: wɪθ əˈblɪgəˌtɔri ˈərli ˌspɛʃələˈzeɪʃən, laɪk mˈjuzɪk. tərnz aʊt ðə ˈpætərn ɪz ˈɔfən ˈsɪmələr.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: The exceptional musicians didn’t start spending more time in deliberate practice than the average musicians until their third instrument.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðə ɪkˈsɛpʃənəl mjuˈzɪʃənz didn’t* stɑrt ˈspɛndɪŋ mɔr taɪm ɪn dɪˈlɪbərˌeɪt ˈpræktɪs ðən ðə ˈævərɪʤ mjuˈzɪʃənz ənˈtɪl ðɛr θərd ˈɪnstrəmənt.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: They too tended to have a sampling period. Even musicians we think of as famously precocious, like Yo-Yo Ma.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðeɪ tu ˈtɛndɪd tɪ hæv ə ˈsæmplɪŋ ˈpɪriəd. ˈivɪn mjuˈzɪʃənz wi θɪŋk əv ɛz ˈfeɪməsli prɪˈkoʊʃəs, laɪk yo-yo* mɑ.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: So this got me interested in exploring the developmental backgrounds of people whose work I had long admired.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: soʊ ðɪs gɑt mi ˈɪntəˌrɛstɪd ɪn ɪkˈsplɔrɪŋ ðə dɪˌvɛləpˈmɛnəl ˈbækˌgraʊndz əv ˈpipəl huz wərk aɪ hæd lɔŋ ədˈmaɪərd.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Duke Ellington shunned music lessons as a kid to focus on baseball and painting and drawing.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: duk ˈɛlɪŋtən ʃənd mˈjuzɪk ˈlɛsənz ɛz ə kɪd tɪ ˈfoʊkɪs ɔn ˈbeɪsˈbɔl ənd ˈpeɪnɪŋ ənd drɔɪŋ.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Mariam Mirzakhani wasn’t interested in math as a girl, dreamed of becoming a novelist,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ˈmɑriəm mirzakhani* wasn’t* ˈɪntəˌrɛstɪd ɪn mæθ ɛz ə gərl, drimd əv bɪˈkəmɪŋ ə ˈnɑvələst,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: and went on to become the first and so far only woman to win the Fields Medal,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ənd wɛnt ɔn tɪ bɪˈkəm ðə fərst ənd soʊ fɑr ˈoʊnli ˈwʊmən tɪ wɪn ðə fildz ˈmɛdəl,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: the most prestigious prize in the world in math. Vincent van Gogh had five different careers before flaming out spectacularly,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðə moʊst pərˈstiʤəs praɪz ɪn ðə wərld ɪn mæθ. ˈvɪnsɪnt væn goʊ hæd faɪv ˈdɪfərənt kərɪrz ˌbiˈfɔr ˈfleɪmɪŋ aʊt spɛkˈtækjələrli,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: and, in his late 20s, picked up a book called “The Guide to the ABCs of Drawing.”
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ənd, ɪn hɪz leɪt 20s*, pɪkt əp ə bʊk kɔld ““the* gaɪd tɪ ðə ˈeɪˌbiˌsiz əv drawing.”*.”

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Claude Shannon was an electrical engineer at the University of Michigan who took a philosophy course just to fulfill a requirement.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: klɔd ˈʃænən wɑz ən ɪˈlɛktrɪkəl ˈɛnʤəˈnɪr æt ðə ˌjunəˈvərsəti əv ˈmɪʃɪgən hu tʊk ə fəˈlɑsəfi kɔrs ʤɪst tɪ fʊlˈfɪl ə rɪkˈwaɪrmənt.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: And in it he learned about a near century-old system of logic
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ənd ɪn ɪt hi ˈlərnɪd əˈbaʊt ə nɪr century-old* ˈsɪstəm əv ˈlɑʤɪk

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: by which true and false statements could be coded as ones and zeros and solved like math problems.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: baɪ wɪʧ tru ənd fɔls ˈsteɪtmənts kʊd bi ˈkoʊdɪd ɛz wənz ənd ˈzɪroʊz ənd sɑlvd laɪk mæθ ˈprɑbləmz.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: This led to the development of binary code, which underlies all of our digital computers today.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðɪs lɛd tɪ ðə dɪˈvɛləpmənt əv ˈbaɪnəˌri koʊd, wɪʧ ˌəndərˈlaɪz ɔl əv ɑr ˈdɪʤɪtəl kəmˈpjutərz təˈdeɪ.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Frances Hesselbein took her first professional job at the age of 54,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ˈfrænsɪs hesselbein* tʊk hər fərst prəˈfɛʃənəl ʤɑb æt ðə eɪʤ əv 54

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: and went on to become the CEO of the Girl Scouts. Here’s an athlete I’ve followed.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ənd wɛnt ɔn tɪ bɪˈkəm ðə ˈsiˌiˈoʊ əv ðə gərl skaʊts. here’s* ən ˈæθˌlit i’ve* ˈfɑloʊd.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: He tried some tennis, some skiing, wrestling. His mother was actually a tennis coach,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: hi traɪd səm ˈtɛnɪs, səm skiɪŋ, ˈrɛsəlɪŋ. hɪz ˈməðər wɑz ˈæˌkʧuəli ə ˈtɛnɪs koʊʧ,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: but she declined to coach him because he wouldn’t return balls normally. And he kept trying more sports:
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: bət ʃi dɪˈklaɪnd tɪ koʊʧ ɪm bɪˈkəz hi wouldn’t* rɪˈtərn bɔlz ˈnɔrməli. ənd hi kɛpt traɪɪŋ mɔr spɔrts:

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: handball, volleyball, soccer, badminton, skateboarding. So who is this dabbler? This is Roger Federer.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ˈhændˌbɔl, ˈvɑliˌbɔl, ˈsɑkər, ˈbædˌmɪntən, ˈskeɪtˌbɔrdɪŋ. soʊ hu ɪz ðɪs dabbler*? ðɪs ɪz ˈrɑʤər ˈfɛdərər.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Every bit as famous as an adult as Tiger Woods. And yet even tennis enthusiasts don't usually know anything
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ˈɛvəri bɪt ɛz ˈfeɪməs ɛz ən ˈædəlt ɛz ˈtaɪgər wʊdz. ənd jɛt ˈivɪn ˈtɛnɪs ɛnˈθuziˌæsts doʊnt ˈjuʒəwəli noʊ ˈɛniˌθɪŋ

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: about his developmental story. Why is that? I think it’s partly because the Tiger story is very dramatic,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: əˈbaʊt hɪz dɪˌvɛləpˈmɛnəl ˈstɔri. waɪ ɪz ðət? aɪ θɪŋk it’s* ˈpɑrtli bɪˈkəz ðə ˈtaɪgər ˈstɔri ɪz ˈvɛri drəˈmætɪk,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: but also because it seems like this tidy narrative
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: bət ˈɔlsoʊ bɪˈkəz ɪt simz laɪk ðɪs ˈtaɪdi ˈnɛrətɪv

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: that we can extrapolate to anything that we want to be good at in our own lives.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðət wi kən ɛkˈstræpəˌleɪt tɪ ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ðət wi wɔnt tɪ bi gʊd æt ɪn ɑr oʊn lɪvz.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: But it turns out that in many ways, golf is a uniquely horrible model of almost everything that humans want to learn.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: bət ɪt tərnz aʊt ðət ɪn ˈmɛni weɪz, gɔlf ɪz ə juˈnikli ˈhɔrəbəl ˈmɑdəl əv ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ðət ˈjumənz wɔnt tɪ lərn.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Golf is the epitome of what the psychologist Robin Hogarth called a kind learning environment.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: gɔlf ɪz ðə ɪˈpɪtəmi əv wət ðə saɪˈkɑləʤəst ˈrɑbɪn ˈhoʊˌgɑrθ kɔld ə kaɪnd ˈlərnɪŋ ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Next steps and goals are clear; rules that are clear and never change.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: nɛkst stɛps ənd goʊlz ər klɪr; rulz ðət ər klɪr ənd ˈnɛvər ʧeɪnʤ.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: When you do something, you get feedback that is quick and accurate.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: wɪn ju du ˈsəmθɪŋ, ju gɪt ˈfidˌbæk ðət ɪz kwɪk ənd ˈækjərət.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Chess, also a kind learning environment. On the other end of the spectrum are wicked learning environments
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ʧɛs, ˈɔlsoʊ ə kaɪnd ˈlərnɪŋ ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt. ɔn ðə ˈəðər ɛnd əv ðə ˈspɛktrəm ər ˈwɪkəd ˈlərnɪŋ ɪnˈvaɪrənmənts

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: where next steps and goals may not be clear— rules may change.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: wɛr nɛkst stɛps ənd goʊlz meɪ nɑt bi clear—*— rulz meɪ ʧeɪnʤ.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: You may or may not get feedback when you do something, it may be delayed, it may be inaccurate.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ju meɪ ər meɪ nɑt gɪt ˈfidˌbæk wɪn ju du ˈsəmθɪŋ, ɪt meɪ bi dɪˈleɪd, ɪt meɪ bi ˌɪˈnækjərət.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: Which one of these sounds like the world we're increasingly living in?
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: wɪʧ wən əv ðiz saʊnz laɪk ðə wərld wɪr ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋgli ˈlɪvɪŋ ɪn?

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: So if hyper-specialization isn’t always the trick in a wicked world, what is? That can be difficult to talk about,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: soʊ ɪf hyper-specialization* isn’t* ˈɔlˌweɪz ðə trɪk ɪn ə ˈwɪkəd wərld, wət ɪz? ðət kən bi ˈdɪfəkəlt tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: because sometimes it looks like meandering or zigzagging or keeping a broader view. It can look like getting behind.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: bɪˈkəz ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ɪt lʊks laɪk miˈændərɪŋ ər ˈzɪgzægɪŋ ər ˈkipɪŋ ə ˈbrɔdər vju. ɪt kən lʊk laɪk ˈgɪtɪŋ bɪˈhaɪnd.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: But if we look at research on technological innovation, it shows that increasingly the most impactful patents are authored
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: bət ɪf wi lʊk æt ˈrisərʧ ɔn ˌtɛknəˈlɑʤɪkəl ˌɪnəˈveɪʃən, ɪt ʃoʊz ðət ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋgli ðə moʊst impactful* ˈpætənts ər ˈɔθərd

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: by teams that include individuals who have worked across a large number of different technology classes
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: baɪ timz ðət ˌɪnˈklud ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəlz hu hæv wərkt əˈkrɔs ə lɑrʤ ˈnəmbər əv ˈdɪfərənt tɛkˈnɑləʤi ˈklæsɪz

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: and often merge things from different domains. Someone whose work I've admired, who was sort of on the forefront of this,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ənd ˈɔfən mərʤ θɪŋz frəm ˈdɪfərənt doʊˈmeɪnz. ˈsəmˌwən huz wərk aɪv ədˈmaɪərd, hu wɑz sɔrt əv ɔn ðə ˈfɔrˌfrənt əv ðɪs,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: is a Japanese man named Junpei Yokoi. Yokoi didn't score well in his electronics exams at school,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ɪz ə ˌʤæpəˈniz mæn neɪmd junpei* yokoi*. yokoi* ˈdɪdənt skɔr wɛl ɪn hɪz ˌɪˌlɛkˈtrɑnɪks ɪgˈzæmz æt skul,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: so he had to settle for a low-tier job as a machine maintenance worker at a playing card company in Kyoto.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: soʊ hi hæd tɪ ˈsɛtəl fər ə low-tier* ʤɑb ɛz ə məˈʃin ˈmeɪntənəns ˈwərkər æt ə pleɪɪŋ kɑrd ˈkəmpəˌni ɪn ˈkjoʊtoʊ.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: He combined some well-known technology from the calculator industry, with some well-known technology from the credit card industry, and made handheld games.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: hi kəmˈbaɪnd səm ˈwɛlˈnoʊn tɛkˈnɑləʤi frəm ðə ˈkælkjəˌleɪtər ˈɪndəstri, wɪθ səm ˈwɛlˈnoʊn tɛkˈnɑləʤi frəm ðə ˈkrɛdɪt kɑrd ˈɪndəstri, ənd meɪd ˈhænˈdhɛld geɪmz.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: And it turned this playing card company, which was founded in a wooden storefront in the 19th century,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ənd ɪt tərnd ðɪs pleɪɪŋ kɑrd ˈkəmpəˌni, wɪʧ wɑz ˈfaʊndɪd ɪn ə ˈwʊdən ˈstɔrˌfrɑnt ɪn ðə 19th* ˈsɛnʧəri,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: into a toy and game operation. You may have heard of it, it’s called Nintendo. His magnum opus was the Game Boy.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ˈɪntu ə tɔɪ ənd geɪm ˌɑpərˈeɪʃən. ju meɪ hæv hərd əv ɪt, it’s* kɔld nɪnˈtɛndoʊ. hɪz ˈmægnəm ˈoʊpəs wɑz ðə geɪm bɔɪ.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: We probably don't make as many of those people as we could, because we don't tend to incentivize anything that doesn't
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: wi ˈprɑbəˌbli doʊnt meɪk ɛz ˈmɛni əv ðoʊz ˈpipəl ɛz wi kʊd, bɪˈkəz wi doʊnt tɛnd tɪ incentivize* ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ðət ˈdəzənt

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: look like a head start or specialization. And naturally, I think there are as many ways to succeed as there are people,
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: lʊk laɪk ə hɛd stɑrt ər ˌspɛʃələˈzeɪʃən. ənd ˈnæʧərəli, aɪ θɪŋk ðɛr ər ɛz ˈmɛni weɪz tɪ səkˈsid ɛz ðɛr ər ˈpipəl,

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: but I think we tend only to incentivize and encourage the Tiger path, when increasingly, in a wicked world, we need people
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: bət aɪ θɪŋk wi tɛnd ˈoʊnli tɪ incentivize* ənd ɪnˈkərəʤ ðə ˈtaɪgər pæθ, wɪn ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋgli, ɪn ə ˈwɪkəd wərld, wi nid ˈpipəl

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: who travel the Roger path as well. Or as the eminent physicist and mathematician and writer Freeman Dyson put it:
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: hu ˈtrævəl ðə ˈrɑʤər pæθ ɛz wɛl. ər ɛz ðə ˈɛmənənt ˈfɪzɪsɪst ənd ˌmæθəməˈtɪʃən ənd ˈraɪtər ˈfrimən ˈdaɪsən pʊt ɪt:

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: “For a healthy ecosystem, we need both birds and frogs. Frogs are down in the mud seeing all the granular details.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ““for* ə ˈhɛlθi ˈikoʊˌsɪstəm, wi nid boʊθ bərdz ənd frɑgz. frɑgz ər daʊn ɪn ðə məd siɪŋ ɔl ðə ˈgrænjələr ˈditeɪlz.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: The birds are soaring up above, not seeing those details, but integrating the knowledge of the frogs.” And we need both.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðə bərdz ər ˈsɔrɪŋ əp əˈbəv, nɑt siɪŋ ðoʊz ˈditeɪlz, bət ˈɪnəˌgreɪtɪŋ ðə ˈnɑlɪʤ əv ðə frogs.”*.” ənd wi nid boʊθ.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: The problem, Dyson said, is that we’re telling everyone to become frogs.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ðə ˈprɑbləm, ˈdaɪsən sɛd, ɪz ðət we’re* ˈtɛlɪŋ ˈɛvriˌwən tɪ bɪˈkəm frɑgz.

jLOuMXnM5wk.text: And I think in a wicked world, that's increasingly shortsighted.
jLOuMXnM5wk.ipa: ənd aɪ θɪŋk ɪn ə ˈwɪkəd wərld, ðæts ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋgli ˈʃɔrtˈsaɪtɪd.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: 400 years ago, modern-day Tokyo was known as Edo,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: 400 jɪrz əˈgoʊ, modern-day* ˈtoʊkiˌoʊ wɑz noʊn ɛz ˈidoʊ,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: and it was already one of the biggest cities in the world, with a population approaching 1 million.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ənd ɪt wɑz ɔˈrɛdi wən əv ðə ˈbɪgəst ˈsɪtiz ɪn ðə wərld, wɪθ ə ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən əˈproʊʧɪŋ 1 ˈmɪljən.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: However, it was on the verge of ecological collapse.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ɪt wɑz ɔn ðə vərʤ əv ɛkəˈlɑʤɪkəl kəˈlæps.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Years of intense logging had led to massive deforestation.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: jɪrz əv ˌɪnˈtɛns ˈlɔgɪŋ hæd lɛd tɪ ˈmæsɪv dɪˌfɔrɪˈsteɪʃən.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: The city now faced timber shortages and the impacts of severe erosion
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðə ˈsɪti naʊ feɪst ˈtɪmbər ˈʃɔrtɪʤɪz ənd ðə ˌɪmˈpækts əv səˈvɪr ɪˈroʊʒən

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: and frequent flooding that threatened food supplies.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ənd ˈfrikˌwɛnt ˈflədɪŋ ðət θˈrɛtənd fud səˈplaɪz.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: But over just a few decades, Edo will transform and become one of the most sustainable
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: bət ˈoʊvər ʤɪst ə fju ˈdɛkeɪdz, ˈidoʊ wɪl ˈtrænsfɔrm ənd bɪˈkəm wən əv ðə moʊst səˈsteɪnəbəl

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: and efficient cities in history.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ənd ɪˈfɪʃənt ˈsɪtiz ɪn ˈhɪstəri.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: So, how did this city that wasted nothing come about?
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: soʊ, haʊ dɪd ðɪs ˈsɪti ðət ˈweɪstɪd ˈnəθɪŋ kəm əˈbaʊt?

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: In 1467, Japan entered a series of continuous bloody civil wars
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ɪn 1467 ʤəˈpæn ˈɛnərd ə ˈsɪriz əv kənˈtɪnjuəs ˈblədi ˈsɪvəl wɔrz

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: that would last over a century.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðət wʊd læst ˈoʊvər ə ˈsɛnʧəri.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: But in 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu defeated his warlord rivals
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: bət ɪn 1600 tokugawa* ieyasu* dɪˈfitɪd hɪz ˈwɔˌrlɔrd ˈraɪvəlz

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: and unified Japan under his rule.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ənd ˈjunəˌfaɪd ʤəˈpæn ˈəndər hɪz rul.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: This marked the beginning of the Tokugawa shogunate’s reign,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðɪs mɑrkt ðə bɪˈgɪnɪŋ əv ðə tokugawa* shogunate’s* reɪn,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: which lasted from 1603 through 1867.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: wɪʧ ˈlæstɪd frəm 1603 θru 1867

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: During this time, Ieyasu and the shoguns who succeeded him enacted a series of sweeping reforms
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˈdʊrɪŋ ðɪs taɪm, ieyasu* ənd ðə shoguns* hu səkˈsidɪd ɪm ɛˈnæktəd ə ˈsɪriz əv sˈwipɪŋ rɪˈfɔrmz

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: that reshaped the country’s economy, landscape, and culture.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðət riˈʃeɪpt ðə country’s* ɪˈkɑnəmi, ˈlænˌskeɪp, ənd ˈkəlʧər.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Concerned over the growing influence of outside powers,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: kənˈsərnd ˈoʊvər ðə groʊɪŋ ˈɪnfluəns əv ˈaʊtˈsaɪd paʊərz,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: the shoguns imposed severe limitations on foreign relations.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðə shoguns* ˌɪmˈpoʊzd səˈvɪr ˌlɪmɪˈteɪʃənz ɔn ˈfɔrən riˈleɪʃənz.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: They banned foreign traders from entering the country, and heavily restricted citizens from leaving.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðeɪ bænd ˈfɔrən ˈtreɪdərz frəm ˈɛnərɪŋ ðə ˈkəntri, ənd ˈhɛvəli riˈstrɪktɪd ˈsɪtɪzənz frəm ˈlivɪŋ.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: To make up for the sudden decrease in imported goods,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: tɪ meɪk əp fər ðə ˈsədən ˈdiˌkris ɪn ˌɪmˈpɔrtɪd gʊdz,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: the Tokugawa shoguns invested heavily in increasing internal production
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðə tokugawa* shoguns* ˌɪnˈvɛstɪd ˈhɛvəli ɪn ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋ ˌɪnˈtərnəl pərˈdəkʃən

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: while decreasing consumption and waste.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: waɪl ˈdiˌkrisɪŋ kənˈsəmʃən ənd weɪst.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: These practices were not entirely new— they drew upon the Buddhist concept of mottainai,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðiz ˈpræktɪsɪz wər nɑt ɪnˈtaɪərli new—*— ðeɪ dru əˈpɑn ðə ˈbudəst ˈkɑnsɛpt əv mottainai*,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: which emphasized not wasting resources and being satisfied with “just enough.”
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: wɪʧ ˈɛmfəˌsaɪzd nɑt ˈweɪstɪŋ ˈrisɔrsɪz ənd biɪŋ ˈsætɪsˌfaɪd wɪθ ““just* enough.”*.”

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Realizing the extent of ecological destruction impacting the capital city, Edo,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˈriəˌlaɪzɪŋ ðə ɪkˈstɛnt əv ɛkəˈlɑʤɪkəl dɪˈstrəkʃən ˌɪmˈpæktɪŋ ðə ˈkæpɪtəl ˈsɪti, ˈidoʊ,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: the Tokugawa shoguns introduced limitations on logging and launched sprawling reforestation programs.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðə tokugawa* shoguns* ˌɪntrəˈdust ˌlɪmɪˈteɪʃənz ɔn ˈlɔgɪŋ ənd lɔnʧt ˈsprɔlɪŋ ˌrifɔrəˈsteɪʃən ˈproʊˌgræmz.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Local villagers were required— and later paid— to plant millions of trees.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˈloʊkəl ˈvɪlɪʤərz wər required—*— ənd ˈleɪtər paid—*— tɪ plænt ˈmɪljənz əv triz.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: To curb demands for timber, strict rationing rules were introduced,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: tɪ kərb dɪˈmændz fər ˈtɪmbər, strɪkt ˈræʃənɪŋ rulz wər ˌɪntrəˈdust,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: and houses were built with standardized wooden components that could be disassembled and reused.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ənd ˈhaʊsɪz wər bɪlt wɪθ ˈstændərˌdaɪzd ˈwʊdən kəmˈpoʊnənts ðət kʊd bi ˌdɪsəˈsɛmbəld ənd riuzd.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Soon, entire industries grew around eliminating the city’s waste.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: sun, ɪnˈtaɪər ˈɪndəstriz gru əraʊnd ɪˈlɪməˌneɪtɪŋ ðə city’s* weɪst.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Residents used agricultural byproducts, like rice straw, to make rope and packaging material.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˈrɛzɪdənts juzd ˌægrɪˈkəlʧərəl ˈbaɪˌprɔdəkts, laɪk raɪs strɔ, tɪ meɪk roʊp ənd ˈpækɪʤɪŋ məˈtɪriəl.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Candle wax drippings were saved and remolded.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˈkændəl wæks drippings* wər seɪvd ənd riˈmoʊldɪd.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Craftsmen repaired old umbrellas and worn-out sandals rather than tossing them.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˈkræftsmɛn rɪˈpɛrd oʊld əmˈbrɛləz ənd worn-out* ˈsændəlz ˈrəðər ðən ˈtɔsɪŋ ðɛm.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Even human waste was collected and turned into fertilizer.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˈivɪn ˈjumən weɪst wɑz kəˈlɛktəd ənd tərnd ˈɪntu ˈfərtəˌlaɪzər.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Within households, families developed techniques to reuse or repurpose items rather than discarding them.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: wɪˈθɪn ˈhaʊsˌhoʊldz, ˈfæməliz dɪˈvɛləpt tɛkˈniks tɪ riˈjuz ər repurpose* ˈaɪtəmz ˈrəðər ðən dɪˈskɑrdɪŋ ðɛm.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Kimonos were continuously mended.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: kəˈmoʊnəz wər kənˈtɪnjuəsli ˈmɛndɪd.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: When the fabric was too frayed for repair, it was used to cover futons,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: wɪn ðə ˈfæbrɪk wɑz tu freɪd fər rɪˈpɛr, ɪt wɑz juzd tɪ ˈkəvər ˈfutɑnz,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: then cut into diapers or cleaning cloths, before finally being burned as fuel.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ðɛn kət ˈɪntu ˈdaɪəpərz ər ˈklinɪŋ klɔθs, ˌbiˈfɔr ˈfaɪnəli biɪŋ bərnd ɛz fjuəl.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Artisans deftly repaired ceramics using kintsugi,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˈɑrtəzənz ˈdɛftli rɪˈpɛrd sərˈæmɪks ˈjuzɪŋ kintsugi*,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: where broken pieces are plastered together, and the fracture lines brushed with gold lacquer.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: wɛr ˈbroʊkən ˈpisɪz ər ˈplæstərd təˈgɛðər, ənd ðə ˈfrækʧər laɪnz brəʃt wɪθ goʊld ˈlækər.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: A tradition known as boro developed, in which fragments of old cloth were sewn together
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ə trəˈdɪʃən noʊn ɛz bəroʊ dɪˈvɛləpt, ɪn wɪʧ ˈfrægmənts əv oʊld klɔθ wər soʊn təˈgɛðər

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: to create beautifully patchworked garments that were then passed down for generations.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: tɪ kriˈeɪt ˈbjutəfli patchworked* ˈgɑrmənts ðət wər ðɛn pæst daʊn fər ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃənz.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Meanwhile, poetry, theater, and literature flourished,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ˈminˌwaɪl, ˈpoʊətri, ˈθieɪtər, ənd ˈlɪtərəʧər flərɪʃt,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: as did the study of Chinese Confucianism and European scientific texts—
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ɛz dɪd ðə ˈstədi əv ʧaɪˈniz kənfˈjuʃəˌnɪzəm ənd ˌjʊrəˈpiən ˌsaɪənˈtɪfɪk texts—*—

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: which were welcomed in the country even as their authors were not.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: wɪʧ wər ˈwɛlkəmd ɪn ðə ˈkəntri ˈivɪn ɛz ðɛr ˈɔθərz wər nɑt.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Yet, while the Tokugawa period was marked by achievements in sustainability and art, it wasn't without its flaws.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: jɛt, waɪl ðə tokugawa* ˈpɪriəd wɑz mɑrkt baɪ əˈʧivmənts ɪn səˌsteɪnəˈbɪlɪti ənd ɑrt, ɪt ˈwəzənt wɪˈθaʊt ɪts flɔz.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Strict laws and regulations were enforced by harsh authoritarian rule.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: strɪkt lɔz ənd ˌrɛgjəˈleɪʃənz wər ɛnˈfɔrst baɪ hɑrʃ əˌθɔrəˈtɛriən rul.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: It was a feudal society, rife with social and economic inequality. The majority peasant population, for example,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ɪt wɑz ə fˈjudəl soʊˈsaɪɪti, raɪf wɪθ ˈsoʊʃəl ənd ˌɛkəˈnɑmɪk ˌɪnɪkˈwɑləti. ðə məˈʤɔrəti ˈpɛzənt ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: was forced to pay an often-crippling rice tax, called nengu, to regional lords known as daimyo.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: wɑz fɔrst tɪ peɪ ən often-crippling* raɪs tæks, kɔld nengu*, tɪ ˈriʤənəl lɔrdz noʊn ɛz daimyo*.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: And by the second half of the 19th century, the country faced rising inflation,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ənd baɪ ðə ˈsɛkənd hæf əv ðə 19th* ˈsɛnʧəri, ðə ˈkəntri feɪst ˈraɪzɪŋ ˌɪnˈfleɪʃən,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: destabilizing its economy and increasing external pressure, including the United States’ use of military power.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: dɪˈsteɪbəˌlaɪzɪŋ ɪts ɪˈkɑnəmi ənd ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋ ɪkˈstərnəl ˈprɛʃər, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ðə juˈnaɪtɪd states’*’ juz əv ˈmɪlɪˌtɛri paʊər.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: All of this forced Japan to reopen its doors to foreign trade and influence,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ɔl əv ðɪs fɔrst ʤəˈpæn tɪ riˈoʊpən ɪts dɔrz tɪ ˈfɔrən treɪd ənd ˈɪnfluəns,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: and ultimately led to the end of the Tokugawa shogunate's reign.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ənd ˈəltəmətli lɛd tɪ ðə ɛnd əv ðə tokugawa* shogunate's* reɪn.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Despite these challenges, this period can serve as a powerful lesson.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: dɪˈspaɪt ðiz ˈʧælənʤɪz, ðɪs ˈpɪriəd kən sərv ɛz ə ˈpaʊərfəl ˈlɛsən.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Edo's transformation from a city teetering on ecological collapse to a thriving cultural epicenter
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: edo's* ˌtrænsfərˈmeɪʃən frəm ə ˈsɪti ˈtitərɪŋ ɔn ɛkəˈlɑʤɪkəl kəˈlæps tɪ ə θˈraɪvɪŋ ˈkəlʧərəl ˈɛpəˌsɛntər

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: shows us that what’s broken can be repaired, and by doing so, we can create something even more beautiful.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: ʃoʊz ˈjuˈɛs ðət what’s* ˈbroʊkən kən bi rɪˈpɛrd, ənd baɪ duɪŋ soʊ, wi kən kriˈeɪt ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈivɪn mɔr ˈbjutəfəl.

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Today, at a time when only 7% of the global economy is circular,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: təˈdeɪ, æt ə taɪm wɪn ˈoʊnli 7 əv ðə ˈgloʊbəl ɪˈkɑnəmi ɪz ˈsərkjələr,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: Edo's history also reminds us that we can create economies driven not by consumption and waste,
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: edo's* ˈhɪstəri ˈɔlsoʊ riˈmaɪndz ˈjuˈɛs ðət wi kən kriˈeɪt ɪˈkɑnəmiz ˈdrɪvən nɑt baɪ kənˈsəmʃən ənd weɪst,

t0h8Dmni8g8.text: but rather by making the most of limited resources.
t0h8Dmni8g8.ipa: bət ˈrəðər baɪ ˈmeɪkɪŋ ðə moʊst əv ˈlɪmɪtɪd ˈrisɔrsɪz.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: It's 6:00 in the morning, pitch black outside. My 14-year-old son is fast asleep in his bed.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ɪts sɪks æt ðə ˈmɔːrnɪŋ, pɪtʃ blæk ˌaʊtˈsaɪd. maɪ ˌfɔːrˈtiːn jɪər oʊld sʌn ɪz fæst əˈsliːp ɪn hɪz bed.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: I flip on the light and physically shake the poor boy awake.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: aɪ flɪp ɒn ðə laɪt ænd ˈfɪzɪkli ʃeɪk ðə pʊər bɔɪ əˈweɪk.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: because I know that, like ripping off a Band-Aid, it's better to get it over with quickly.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: bɪˈkɒz aɪ noʊ ðæt, laɪk ˈrɪpɪŋ ɒf ə ˈbændeɪd, ɪts ˈbetər tuː ɡet ɪt ˈoʊvər wɪð ˈkwɪkli.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Sound brutal, but perhaps familiar?
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: saʊnd ˈbruːtl, bʌt pərˈhæps fəˈmɪliər?

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Every morning I ask myself, how can I, knowing what I know,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈevri ˈmɔːrnɪŋ aɪ æsk maɪˈself, haʊ kæn aɪ, ˈnoʊɪŋ wɒt aɪ noʊ,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: be doing this to my own son?
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: biː ˈduːɪŋ ðɪs tuː maɪ oʊn sʌn?

WJhCa12UTsg.text: You see, I’m a sleep researcher.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: juː siː, aɪm ə sliːp rɪˈsɜːrtʃər.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: So I know that I’m depriving my son of sleep he desperately needs as a rapidly growing teenager.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: soʊ aɪ noʊ ðæt aɪm dɪˈpraɪvɪŋ maɪ sʌn əv sliːp hiː ˈdespərətli niːdz æz ə ˈræpɪdli ˈɡroʊɪŋ ˈtiːneɪdʒər.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: I also know that by waking him up hours before his natural biological clock tells him he’s ready,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: aɪ ˈɔːlsoʊ noʊ ðæt baɪ ˈweɪkɪŋ hɪm ʌp ˈaʊərz bɪˈfɔːr hɪz ˈnætʃrəl ˌbaɪəˈlɒdʒɪkl klɒk telz hɪm hiːz ˈredi,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: I’m literally robbing him of the type of sleep
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: aɪm ˈlɪtərəli ˈrɒbɪŋ hɪm əv ðə taɪp əv sliːp

WJhCa12UTsg.text: most associated with learning, memory consolidation, and emotional processing.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: moʊst əˈsoʊʃiˌeɪtəd wɪθ ˈlərnɪŋ, ˈmɛməri kənˌsɑləˈdeɪʃən, ənd ˈiˌmoʊʃənəl ˈprɑsɛsɪŋ.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: But it's not just my kid that's being deprived of sleep. Sleep deprivation among American teenagers is an epidemic.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: bət ɪts nɑt ʤɪst maɪ kɪd ðæts biɪŋ dɪˈpraɪvd əv slip. slip ˌdɛprəˈveɪʃən əˈməŋ əˈmɛrɪkən ˈtiˌneɪʤərz ɪz ən ˌɛpɪˈdɛmɪk.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Only about 1 in 10 gets the 8 to 10 hours of sleep per night recommended by sleep scientists and pediatricians.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈoʊnli əˈbaʊt 1 ɪn 10 gɪts ðə 8 tɪ 10 aʊərz əv slip pər naɪt ˌrɛkəˈmɛndɪd baɪ slip ˈsaɪəntɪsts ənd ˌpidiəˈtrɪʃənz.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: The major factor preventing teens from getting the sleep they need is a matter of public policy.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðə ˈmeɪʤər ˈfæktər prɪˈvɛnɪŋ tinz frəm ˈgɪtɪŋ ðə slip ðeɪ nid ɪz ə ˈmætər əv ˈpəblɪk ˈpɑləsi.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Across the country, many schools are starting around 7:30 a.m. or earlier,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: əˈkrɔs ðə ˈkəntri, ˈmɛni skulz ər ˈstɑrtɪŋ əraʊnd 7:30* a.m*. ər ˈərliər,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: despite the fact that major medical organizations recommend
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: dɪˈspaɪt ðə fækt ðət ˈmeɪʤər ˈmɛdɪkəl ˌɔrgənəˈzeɪʃənz ˌrɛkəˈmɛnd

WJhCa12UTsg.text: that middle and high schools start no earlier than 8:30 a.m.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðət ˈmɪdəl ənd haɪ skulz stɑrt noʊ ˈərliər ðən 8:30* a.m*.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: These early start policies have a direct effect on how little sleep American teenagers are getting.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðiz ˈərli stɑrt ˈpɑləsiz hæv ə dɪˈrɛkt ˈifɛkt ɔn haʊ ˈlɪtəl slip əˈmɛrɪkən ˈtiˌneɪʤərz ər ˈgɪtɪŋ.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: They’re also pitting teenagers in a fundamentally unwinnable fight against their own bodies.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: they’re* ˈɔlsoʊ ˈpɪtɪŋ ˈtiˌneɪʤərz ɪn ə ˌfəndəˈmɛnəli ənˈwɪnəbəl faɪt əˈgɛnst ðɛr oʊn ˈbɑdiz.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Around the time of puberty, teenagers experience a delay in their biological clock,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: əraʊnd ðə taɪm əv ˈpjubərti, ˈtiˌneɪʤərz ɪkˈspɪriəns ə dɪˈleɪ ɪn ðɛr ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl klɑk,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: which determines when we feel most awake and when we feel most sleepy.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: wɪʧ dɪˈtərmənz wɪn wi fil moʊst əˈweɪk ənd wɪn wi fil moʊst sˈlipi.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: This is driven in part by a shift in the release of the hormone melatonin.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ˈdrɪvən ɪn pɑrt baɪ ə ʃɪft ɪn ðə riˈlis əv ðə ˈhɔrˌmoʊn ˌmɛləˈtoʊnɪn.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Teenagers’ bodies wait to start releasing melatonin until around 11 p.m.,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: teenagers’*’ ˈbɑdiz weɪt tɪ stɑrt riˈlisɪŋ ˌmɛləˈtoʊnɪn ənˈtɪl əraʊnd 11 p.m*.,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: which is two hours later than what we see in adults or younger children.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz tu aʊərz ˈleɪtər ðən wət wi si ɪn ˈædəlts ər ˈjəŋgər ˈʧɪldrən.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: This means that waking a teenager up at 6 a.m.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðɪs minz ðət ˈweɪkɪŋ ə ˈtiˌneɪʤər əp æt 6 a.m*.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: is the biological equivalent of waking an adult up at 4 a.m.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ɪz ðə ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl ɪkˈwɪvələnt əv ˈweɪkɪŋ ən ˈædəlt əp æt 4 a.m*.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Now, on the unfortunate days when I have to wake up at 4 a.m., I’m a zombie. Functionally useless.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: naʊ, ɔn ðə ənˈfɔrʧənət deɪz wɪn aɪ hæv tɪ weɪk əp æt 4 a.m*., i’m* ə ˈzɑmbi. ˈfəŋkʃənəli ˈjusləs.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: I can't think straight. I’m irritable, and I probably shouldn’t be driving a car.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: aɪ kænt θɪŋk streɪt. i’m* ˈɪrətəbəl, ənd aɪ ˈprɑbəˌbli shouldn’t* bi ˈdraɪvɪŋ ə kɑr.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: But this is how many American teenagers feel every single school day.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: bət ðɪs ɪz haʊ ˈmɛni əˈmɛrɪkən ˈtiˌneɪʤərz fil ˈɛvəri ˈsɪŋgəl skul deɪ.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Many of the, shall we say, unpleasant characteristics that we chalk up to being a teenager—
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈmɛni əv ðə, ʃæl wi seɪ, ənˈplɛzənt ˌkɛrɪktərˈɪstɪks ðət wi ʧɔk əp tɪ biɪŋ ə teenager—*—

WJhCa12UTsg.text: moodiness, irritability, laziness, depression—
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: moodiness*, ˌɪrɪtəˈbɪləti, ˈleɪzinəs, depression—*—

WJhCa12UTsg.text: could be a product of chronic sleep deprivation. For many teens battling chronic sleep loss,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: kʊd bi ə ˈprɑdəkt əv ˈkrɑnɪk slip ˌdɛprəˈveɪʃən. fər ˈmɛni tinz ˈbætəlɪŋ ˈkrɑnɪk slip lɔs,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: their go-to strategy to compensate is consuming large quantities of caffeine
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðɛr go-to* ˈstrætəʤi tɪ ˈkɑmpənˌseɪt ɪz kənˈsumɪŋ lɑrʤ kˈwɑntətiz əv kæˈfin

WJhCa12UTsg.text: in the form of venti Frappuccinos, or energy drinks and shots.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ɪn ðə fɔrm əv ˈvɛnti frappuccinos*, ər ˈɛnərʤi drɪŋks ənd ʃɑts.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: So essentially, we've got an entire population of tired but wired youth.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: soʊ ɛˈsɛnʃəli, wiv gɑt ən ɪnˈtaɪər ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən əv taɪərd bət waɪərd juθ.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Advocates of sleep-friendly start times know that adolescence is a period of dramatic brain development,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈædvəˌkeɪts əv sleep-friendly* stɑrt taɪmz noʊ ðət ˌædəˈlɛsəns ɪz ə ˈpɪriəd əv drəˈmætɪk breɪn dɪˈvɛləpmənt,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: particularly in the parts of the brain that are responsible for those higher order thinking processes,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˌpɑrˈtɪkjələrli ɪn ðə pɑrts əv ðə breɪn ðət ər riˈspɑnsəbəl fər ðoʊz haɪər ˈɔrdər ˈθɪŋkɪŋ ˈprɑsɛsəz,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: including reasoning, problem solving, and good judgment.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ˈrizənɪŋ, ˈprɑbləm ˈsɑlvɪŋ, ənd gʊd ˈʤəʤmənt.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: In other words, the very type of brain activity that’s responsible for reining in
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ɪn ˈəðər wərdz, ðə ˈvɛri taɪp əv breɪn ækˈtɪvɪti that’s* riˈspɑnsəbəl fər ˈreɪnɪŋ ɪn

WJhCa12UTsg.text: those impulsive and often risky behaviors that are so characteristic of adolescence.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðoʊz ˌɪmˈpəlsɪv ənd ˈɔfən ˈrɪski bɪˈheɪvjərz ðət ər soʊ ˌkɛrɪktərˈɪstɪk əv ˌædəˈlɛsəns.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: They know that, like the rest of us, when teenagers don’t get the sleep they need,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðeɪ noʊ ðət, laɪk ðə rɛst əv ˈjuˈɛs, wɪn ˈtiˌneɪʤərz don’t* gɪt ðə slip ðeɪ nid,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: their brains, their bodies, and behavior suffer. They can't concentrate.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðɛr breɪnz, ðɛr ˈbɑdiz, ənd bɪˈheɪvjər ˈsəfər. ðeɪ kænt ˈkɑnsənˌtreɪt.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Their attention plummets,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðɛr əˈtɛnʃən ˈpləməts,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: and many will even show behavioral signs that mimic ADHD.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ənd ˈmɛni wɪl ˈivɪn ʃoʊ bɪˈheɪvjərəl saɪnz ðət ˈmɪmɪk adhd*.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: But the consequences of teen sleep loss go well beyond the classroom,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: bət ðə ˈkɑnsəkˌwɛnsəz əv tin slip lɔs goʊ wɛl bɪɔnd ðə ˈklæsˌrum,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: sadly contributing to many of the mental health problems that skyrocket during adolescence.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈsædli kənˈtrɪbjutɪŋ tɪ ˈmɛni əv ðə ˈmɛntəl hɛlθ ˈprɑbləmz ðət ˈskaɪˌrɑkət ˈdʊrɪŋ ˌædəˈlɛsəns.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: In our work with teens from LA Unified School District, we found that teens with sleep problems were 55% more likely
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ɪn ɑr wərk wɪθ tinz frəm lɑ ˈjunəˌfaɪd skul ˈdɪstrɪkt, wi faʊnd ðət tinz wɪθ slip ˈprɑbləmz wər 55 mɔr ˈlaɪkli

WJhCa12UTsg.text: to have used alcohol in the past month.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: tɪ hæv juzd ˈælkəˌhɑl ɪn ðə pæst mənθ.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: In another study with over 30,000 high school students, they found that for each hour of lost sleep,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ɪn əˈnəðər ˈstədi wɪθ ˈoʊvər 30,000* haɪ skul ˈstudənts, ðeɪ faʊnd ðət fər iʧ aʊər əv lɔst slip,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: there was a 38% increase in feeling sad or hopeless,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðɛr wɑz ə 38 ˌɪnˈkris ɪn ˈfilɪŋ sæd ər ˈhoʊpləs,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: and a 58% increase in teen suicide attempts.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ənd ə 58 ˌɪnˈkris ɪn tin ˈsuɪˌsaɪd əˈtɛmpts.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: And if that's not enough, teens who skimp out on sleep are at increased risk
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ənd ɪf ðæts nɑt ɪˈnəf, tinz hu skɪmp aʊt ɔn slip ər æt ˌɪnˈkrist rɪsk

WJhCa12UTsg.text: for a host of physical health problems that plague our country, including obesity, heart disease, and diabetes.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: fər ə hoʊst əv ˈfɪzɪkəl hɛlθ ˈprɑbləmz ðət pleɪg ɑr ˈkəntri, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ əˈbisəti, hɑrt dɪˈziz, ənd ˌdaɪəˈbitiz.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Then there’s the risk of putting a sleep-deprived teen behind the wheel.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðɛn there’s* ðə rɪsk əv ˈpʊtɪŋ ə sleep-deprived* tin bɪˈhaɪnd ðə wil.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Studies have shown that getting five hours or less of sleep per night
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈstədiz hæv ʃoʊn ðət ˈgɪtɪŋ faɪv aʊərz ər lɛs əv slip pər naɪt

WJhCa12UTsg.text: is the equivalent of driving with a blood alcohol content above the legal limit.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ɪz ðə ɪkˈwɪvələnt əv ˈdraɪvɪŋ wɪθ ə bləd ˈælkəˌhɑl ˈkɑntɛnt əˈbəv ðə ˈligəl ˈlɪmət.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Researchers in this area have produced tremendous science showing the benefits of later start times.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈrisərʧərz ɪn ðɪs ˈɛriə hæv prəˈdust trɪˈmɛndəs saɪəns ʃoʊɪŋ ðə ˈbɛnəfɪts əv ˈleɪtər stɑrt taɪmz.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: The findings are unequivocal. Teens from districts with later start times get more sleep.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðə ˈfaɪndɪŋz ər ˌənɪkˈwɪvəkəl. tinz frəm ˈdɪstrɪkts wɪθ ˈleɪtər stɑrt taɪmz gɪt mɔr slip.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: They're more likely to show up for school. School absences dropped by 25% in one district,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðɛr mɔr ˈlaɪkli tɪ ʃoʊ əp fər skul. skul ˈæbsənsɪz drɑpt baɪ 25 ɪn wən ˈdɪstrɪkt,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: and they're less likely to drop out.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ənd ðɛr lɛs ˈlaɪkli tɪ drɔp aʊt.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Not surprisingly, they do better academically.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: nɑt səˈpraɪzɪŋli, ðeɪ du ˈbɛtər ˌækəˈdɛmɪkli.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Standardized test scores in math and reading go up by 2 to 3 percentage points.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈstændərˌdaɪzd tɛst skɔrz ɪn mæθ ənd ˈrɛdɪŋ goʊ əp baɪ 2 tɪ 3 pərˈsɛnɪʤ pɔɪnts.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: That's as powerful as reducing class sizes by one third fewer students.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðæts ɛz ˈpaʊərfəl ɛz rɪˈdusɪŋ klæs ˈsaɪzɪz baɪ wən θərd fjuər ˈstudənts.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Their mental and physical health improves, and their families are happier.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðɛr ˈmɛntəl ənd ˈfɪzɪkəl hɛlθ ˌɪmˈpruvz, ənd ðɛr ˈfæməliz ər ˈhæpiər.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Even their communities are safer because car crash rates go down— a 70% reduction in one district.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈivɪn ðɛr kəmˈjunɪtiz ər ˈseɪfər bɪˈkəz kɑr kræʃ reɪts goʊ down—*— ə 70 rɪˈdəkʃən ɪn wən ˈdɪstrɪkt.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Now, given these tremendous benefits, you might think, well, this is a no brainer, right?
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: naʊ, ˈgɪvɪn ðiz trɪˈmɛndəs ˈbɛnəfɪts, ju maɪt θɪŋk, wɛl, ðɪs ɪz ə noʊ ˈbreɪnər, raɪt?

WJhCa12UTsg.text: So why have we as a society failed to heed this call?
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: soʊ waɪ hæv wi ɛz ə soʊˈsaɪɪti feɪld tɪ hid ðɪs kɔl?

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Delaying start times presents many logistical challenges.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: dɪˈleɪɪŋ stɑrt taɪmz ˈprɛzənts ˈmɛni ləˈʤɪstɪkəl ˈʧælənʤɪz.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Updating bus routes, increased transportation costs, impact on sports.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈəpˌdeɪtɪŋ bəs rʊts, ˌɪnˈkrist ˌtrænspərˈteɪʃən kɔsts, ˌɪmˈpækt ɔn spɔrts.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Care before or after school.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: kɛr ˌbiˈfɔr ər ˈæftər skul.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: But these are problems we have to work through.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: bət ðiz ər ˈprɑbləmz wi hæv tɪ wərk θru.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: They are not valid excuses for failing to do the right thing for our children,
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ðeɪ ər nɑt ˈvælɪd ɪkˈskjuzɪz fər ˈfeɪlɪŋ tɪ du ðə raɪt θɪŋ fər ɑr ˈʧɪldrən,

WJhCa12UTsg.text: which is to start middle and high schools no earlier than 8:30 a.m.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz tɪ stɑrt ˈmɪdəl ənd haɪ skulz noʊ ˈərliər ðən 8:30* a.m*.

WJhCa12UTsg.text: Districts around the country, big and small, who have made this change, they’ve found that these fears are often unfounded
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ˈdɪstrɪkts əraʊnd ðə ˈkəntri, bɪg ənd smɔl, hu hæv meɪd ðɪs ʧeɪnʤ, they’ve* faʊnd ðət ðiz fɪrz ər ˈɔfən ənˈfaʊndɪd

WJhCa12UTsg.text: and far outweighed by the tremendous benefits for our student health and performance and our collective public safety.
WJhCa12UTsg.ipa: ənd fɑr aʊtˈweɪd baɪ ðə trɪˈmɛndəs ˈbɛnəfɪts fər ɑr ˈstudənt hɛlθ ənd pərˈfɔrməns ənd ɑr kəˈlɛktɪv ˈpəblɪk ˈseɪfti.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: If you can't imagine life without chocolate,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ɪf ju kænt ˌɪˈmæʤən laɪf wɪˈθaʊt ˈʧɔklət,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: you're lucky you weren't born before the 16th century.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: jʊr ˈləki ju wərənt bɔrn ˌbiˈfɔr ðə 16th* ˈsɛnʧəri.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: Until then, chocolate only existed in Mesoamerica in a form quite different from what we know.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ənˈtɪl ðɛn, ˈʧɔklət ˈoʊnli ɪgˈzɪstəd ɪn mesoamerica* ɪn ə fɔrm kwaɪt ˈdɪfərənt frəm wət wi noʊ.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: As far back as 1900 BCE, the people of that region had learned to prepare the beans
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ɛz fɑr bæk ɛz 1900 bce*, ðə ˈpipəl əv ðət ˈriʤən hæd ˈlərnɪd tɪ priˈpɛr ðə binz

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: of the native cacao tree. The earliest records tell us the beans were ground
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: əv ðə ˈneɪtɪv kəˈkeɪoʊ tri. ðə ˈərliəst ˈrɛkərdz tɛl ˈjuˈɛs ðə binz wər graʊnd

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: and mixed with cornmeal and chili peppers to create a drink - not a relaxing cup of hot cocoa,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ənd mɪkst wɪθ ˈkɔrnˌmil ənd ˈʧɪli ˈpɛpərz tɪ kriˈeɪt ə drɪŋk  nɑt ə rɪˈlæksɪŋ kəp əv hɑt ˈkoʊkoʊ,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: but a bitter, invigorating concoction frothing with foam. And if you thought we make a big deal about chocolate today,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: bət ə ˈbɪtər, ˌɪnˈvɪgərˌeɪtɪŋ kənˈkɑkʃən frothing* wɪθ foʊm. ənd ɪf ju θɔt wi meɪk ə bɪg dil əˈbaʊt ˈʧɔklət təˈdeɪ,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: the Mesoamericans had us beat. They believed that cacao was a heavenly food
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ðə mesoamericans* hæd ˈjuˈɛs bit. ðeɪ bɪˈlivd ðət kəˈkeɪoʊ wɑz ə ˈhɛvənli fud

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: gifted to humans by a feathered serpent god, known to the Maya as Kukulkan
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ˈgɪftɪd tɪ ˈjumənz baɪ ə ˈfɛðərd ˈsərpənt gɑd, noʊn tɪ ðə maɪə ɛz kukulkan*

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: and to the Aztecs as Quetzalcoatl. Aztecs used cacao beans as currency
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ənd tɪ ðə ˈæzˌtɛks ɛz quetzalcoatl*. ˈæzˌtɛks juzd kəˈkeɪoʊ binz ɛz ˈkərənsi

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: and drank chocolate at royal feasts, gave it to soldiers as a reward for success in battle,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ənd dræŋk ˈʧɔklət æt rɔɪəl fists, geɪv ɪt tɪ ˈsoʊlʤərz ɛz ə rɪˈwɔrd fər səkˈsɛs ɪn ˈbætəl,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: and used it in rituals. The first transatlantic chocolate encounter occurred in 1519
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ənd juzd ɪt ɪn ˈrɪʧuəlz. ðə fərst ˌtrænzəˈtlænɪk ˈʧɔklət ɪnˈkaʊnər əˈkərd ɪn 1519

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: when Hernán Cortés visited the court of Moctezuma at Tenochtitlan. As recorded by Cortés's lieutenant,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: wɪn hernán* cortés* ˈvɪzɪtɪd ðə kɔrt əv moctezuma* æt tenochtitlan*. ɛz rɪˈkɔrdɪd baɪ cortés's* luˈtɛnənt,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: the king had 50 jugs of the drink brought out and poured into golden cups.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ðə kɪŋ hæd 50 ʤəgz əv ðə drɪŋk brɔt aʊt ənd pɔrd ˈɪntu ˈgoʊldən kəps.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: When the colonists returned with shipments of the strange new bean, missionaries' salacious accounts of native customs
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: wɪn ðə ˈkɑlənɪsts rɪˈtərnd wɪθ ˈʃɪpmənts əv ðə streɪnʤ nu bin, ˈmɪʃəˌnɛriz' səˈleɪʃəs əˈkaʊnts əv ˈneɪtɪv ˈkəstəmz

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: gave it a reputation as an aphrodisiac.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: geɪv ɪt ə ˌrɛpjəˈteɪʃən ɛz ən ˌæfroʊˈdiziæk.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: At first, its bitter taste made it suitable as a medicine for ailments, like upset stomachs,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: æt fərst, ɪts ˈbɪtər teɪst meɪd ɪt ˈsutəbəl ɛz ə ˈmɛdəsən fər ˈeɪlmənts, laɪk ˈəpˌsɛt ˈstəməks,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: but sweetening it with honey, sugar, or vanilla quickly made chocolate a popular delicacy in the Spanish court.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: bət sˈwitənɪŋ ɪt wɪθ ˈhəni, ˈʃʊgər, ər vəˈnɪlə kˈwɪkli meɪd ˈʧɔklət ə ˈpɑpjələr ˈdɛlɪkəsi ɪn ðə ˈspænɪʃ kɔrt.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: And soon, no aristocratic home was complete without dedicated chocolate ware.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ənd sun, noʊ ərˌɪstəˈkrætɪk hoʊm wɑz kəmˈplit wɪˈθaʊt ˈdɛdəkeɪtəd ˈʧɔklət wɛr.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: The fashionable drink was difficult and time consuming to produce on a large scale.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ðə ˈfæʃənəbəl drɪŋk wɑz ˈdɪfəkəlt ənd taɪm kənˈsumɪŋ tɪ ˈproʊdus ɔn ə lɑrʤ skeɪl.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: That involved using plantations and imported slave labor in the Caribbean and on islands off the coast of Africa.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ðət ˌɪnˈvɑlvd ˈjuzɪŋ ˌplænˈteɪʃənz ənd ˌɪmˈpɔrtɪd sleɪv ˈleɪbər ɪn ðə ˌkɛrɪˈbiən ənd ɔn ˈaɪləndz ɔf ðə koʊst əv ˈæfrɪkɑ.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: The world of chocolate would change forever in 1828
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ðə wərld əv ˈʧɔklət wʊd ʧeɪnʤ fərˈɛvər ɪn 1828

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: with the introduction of the cocoa press by Coenraad van Houten of Amsterdam.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: wɪθ ðə ˌɪntrəˈdəkʃən əv ðə ˈkoʊkoʊ prɛs baɪ coenraad* væn ˈhaʊtɛn əv ˈæmstərˌdæm.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: Van Houten's invention could separate the cocoa's natural fat, or cocoa butter.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: væn ˈhaʊtɛnz ˌɪnˈvɛnʃən kʊd ˈsɛpərˌeɪt ðə cocoa's* ˈnæʧərəl fæt, ər ˈkoʊkoʊ ˈbətər.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: This left a powder that could be mixed into a drinkable solution or recombined with the cocoa butter
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ðɪs lɛft ə ˈpaʊdər ðət kʊd bi mɪkst ˈɪntu ə ˈdrɪnkəbəl səˈluʃən ər recombined* wɪθ ðə ˈkoʊkoʊ ˈbətər

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: to create the solid chocolate we know today. Not long after, a Swiss chocolatier named Daniel Peter
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: tɪ kriˈeɪt ðə ˈsɑləd ˈʧɔklət wi noʊ təˈdeɪ. nɑt lɔŋ ˈæftər, ə swɪs chocolatier* neɪmd ˈdænjəl ˈpitər

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: added powdered milk to the mix, thus inventing milk chocolate.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ˈædɪd ˈpaʊdərd mɪlk tɪ ðə mɪks, ðəs ˌɪnˈvɛntɪŋ mɪlk ˈʧɔklət.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: By the 20th century, chocolate was no longer an elite luxury but had become a treat for the public.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: baɪ ðə 20th* ˈsɛnʧəri, ˈʧɔklət wɑz noʊ ˈlɔŋgər ən ɪˈlit ˈləgʒəri bət hæd bɪˈkəm ə trit fər ðə ˈpəblɪk.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: Meeting the massive demand required more cultivation of cocoa, which can only grow near the equator.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ˈmitɪŋ ðə ˈmæsɪv dɪˈmænd rikˈwaɪərd mɔr ˌkəltɪˈveɪʃən əv ˈkoʊkoʊ, wɪʧ kən ˈoʊnli groʊ nɪr ðə ɪkˈweɪtər.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: Now, instead of African slaves being shipped to South American cocoa plantations,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: naʊ, ˌɪnˈstɛd əv ˈæfrɪkɑn sleɪvz biɪŋ ʃɪpt tɪ saʊθ əˈmɛrɪkən ˈkoʊkoʊ ˌplænˈteɪʃənz,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: cocoa production itself would shift to West Africa
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ˈkoʊkoʊ pərˈdəkʃən ˌɪtˈsɛlf wʊd ʃɪft tɪ wɛst ˈæfrɪkɑ

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: with Cote d'Ivoire providing two-fifths of the world's cocoa as of 2015. Yet along with the growth of the industry,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: wɪθ koʊt divˈwɑr prəˈvaɪdɪŋ two-fifths* əv ðə wərldz ˈkoʊkoʊ ɛz əv 2015 jɛt əˈlɔŋ wɪθ ðə groʊθ əv ðə ˈɪndəstri,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: there have been horrific abuses of human rights. Many of the plantations throughout West Africa, which supply Western companies,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ðɛr hæv bɪn hɔˈrɪfɪk əˈbjuzɪz əv ˈjumən raɪts. ˈmɛni əv ðə ˌplænˈteɪʃənz θruaʊt wɛst ˈæfrɪkɑ, wɪʧ səˈplaɪ ˈwɛstərn ˈkəmpəˌniz,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: use slave and child labor, with an estimation of more than 2 million children affected.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: juz sleɪv ənd ʧaɪld ˈleɪbər, wɪθ ən ˌɛstəˈmeɪʃən əv mɔr ðən 2 ˈmɪljən ˈʧɪldrən əˈfɛktɪd.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: This is a complex problem that persists despite efforts from major chocolate companies to partner with African nations
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ðɪs ɪz ə ˈkɑmplɛks ˈprɑbləm ðət pərˈsɪsts dɪˈspaɪt ˈɛfərts frəm ˈmeɪʤər ˈʧɔklət ˈkəmpəˌniz tɪ ˈpɑrtnər wɪθ ˈæfrɪkɑn ˈneɪʃənz

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: to reduce child and indentured labor practices.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: tɪ rɪˈdus ʧaɪld ənd ˌɪnˈdɛnʧərd ˈleɪbər ˈpræktɪsɪz.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: Today, chocolate has established itself in the rituals of our modern culture.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: təˈdeɪ, ˈʧɔklət həz ɪˈstæblɪʃt ˌɪtˈsɛlf ɪn ðə ˈrɪʧuəlz əv ɑr ˈmɑdərn ˈkəlʧər.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: Due to its colonial association with native cultures, combined with the power of advertising,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: du tɪ ɪts kəˈloʊniəl əˌsoʊʃiˈeɪʃən wɪθ ˈneɪtɪv ˈkəlʧərz, kəmˈbaɪnd wɪθ ðə paʊər əv ˈædvərˌtaɪzɪŋ,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: chocolate retains an aura of something sensual, decadent, and forbidden.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: ˈʧɔklət rɪˈteɪnz ən ˈɔrə əv ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈsɛnʃəwəl, ˈdɛkədənt, ənd ˈfɔrbɪdən.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: Yet knowing more about its fascinating and often cruel history, as well as its production today,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: jɛt noʊɪŋ mɔr əˈbaʊt ɪts ˈfæsəˌneɪtɪŋ ənd ˈɔfən kruəl ˈhɪstəri, ɛz wɛl ɛz ɪts pərˈdəkʃən təˈdeɪ,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: tells us where these associations originate and what they hide.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: tɛlz ˈjuˈɛs wɛr ðiz əˌsoʊʃiˈeɪʃənz ərˈɪʤəˌneɪt ənd wət ðeɪ haɪd.

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: So as you unwrap your next bar of chocolate,
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: soʊ ɛz ju ənˈræp jʊr nɛkst bɑr əv ˈʧɔklət,

ibjUpk9Iagk.text: take a moment to consider that not everything about chocolate is sweet.
ibjUpk9Iagk.ipa: teɪk ə ˈmoʊmənt tɪ kənˈsɪdər ðət nɑt ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ əˈbaʊt ˈʧɔklət ɪz swit.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Every year, people leave their doctor's office with an unusual prescription.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ˈɛvəri jɪr, ˈpipəl liv ðɛr ˈdɑktərz ˈɔfəs wɪθ ən ənˈjuˌʒuəl prəˈskrɪpʃən.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Instead of traditional treatments like a pill, shot, or cream, they’re sent home with a recommendation for light—
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ˌɪnˈstɛd əv trəˈdɪʃənəl ˈtritmənts laɪk ə pɪl, ʃɑt, ər krim, they’re* sɛnt hoʊm wɪθ ə ˌrɛkəmənˈdeɪʃən fər light—*—

0RiDPisQAzM.text: or more specifically, light therapy.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ər mɔr spəˈsɪfɪkli, laɪt ˈθɛrəpi.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: To understand how this works, let’s step out of the office and into this valley.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd haʊ ðɪs wərks, let’s* stɛp aʊt əv ðə ˈɔfəs ənd ˈɪntu ðɪs ˈvæli.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Here, as winter approaches, shorter days spark a cascade of changes within the brains of its inhabitants.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: hir, ɛz ˈwɪntər əˈproʊʧɪz, ˈʃɔrtər deɪz spɑrk ə kæˈskeɪd əv ˈʧeɪnʤɪz wɪˈθɪn ðə breɪnz əv ɪts ˌɪnˈhæbɪtənts.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: As a result, groundhogs retreat to their burrows for hibernation.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ɛz ə rɪˈzəlt, groundhogs* riˈtrit tɪ ðɛr bəroʊz fər ˌhaɪbərˈneɪʃən.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Weasels’ coats turn from brown to white, and birds cease their singing.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: weasels’*’ koʊts tərn frəm braʊn tɪ waɪt, ənd bərdz sis ðɛr ˈsɪŋɪŋ.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: And scientists are finding that seasonal behavioral changes similarly happen for some humans.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ənd ˈsaɪəntɪsts ər ˈfaɪndɪŋ ðət ˈsizənəl bɪˈheɪvjərəl ˈʧeɪnʤɪz ˈsɪmələrli ˈhæpən fər səm ˈjumənz.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Around 1% of people experience seasonal affective disorder, or SAD,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: əraʊnd 1 əv ˈpipəl ɪkˈspɪriəns ˈsizənəl əˈfɛktɪv dɪˈsɔrdər, ər sæd,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: a mood disorder marked by episodes of depression that recur at certain times of the year.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ə mud dɪˈsɔrdər mɑrkt baɪ ˈɛpɪˌsoʊdz əv dɪˈprɛʃən ðət rɪˈkər æt ˈsərtən taɪmz əv ðə jɪr.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Most SAD cases appear during the fall and winter months when days are shortest.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: moʊst sæd ˈkeɪsɪz əˈpɪr ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə fɔl ənd ˈwɪntər mənθs wɪn deɪz ər ˈʃɔrtɪst.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: And while many people experience mild changes in energy or appetite during winter, SAD is much more serious.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ənd waɪl ˈmɛni ˈpipəl ɪkˈspɪriəns maɪld ˈʧeɪnʤɪz ɪn ˈɛnərʤi ər ˈæpəˌtaɪt ˈdʊrɪŋ ˈwɪntər, sæd ɪz məʧ mɔr ˈsɪriəs.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Its symptoms largely overlap with non-seasonal depression, and can include pervasive feelings of sadness,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ɪts ˈsɪmptəmz ˈlɑrʤli ˈoʊvərˌlæp wɪθ non-seasonal* dɪˈprɛʃən, ənd kən ˌɪnˈklud pərˈveɪsɪv ˈfilɪŋz əv ˈsædnəs,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: loss of interest in activities, unshakable fatigue, dramatic changes in sleep and weight, and suicidal thoughts.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: lɔs əv ˈɪntəˌrɛst ɪn ækˈtɪvɪtiz, ənˈʃeɪkəbəl fəˈtig, drəˈmætɪk ˈʧeɪnʤɪz ɪn slip ənd weɪt, ənd ˌsuəˈsaɪdəl θɔts.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: There’s still a lot we don’t understand about SAD,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: there’s* stɪl ə lɔt wi don’t* ˌəndərˈstænd əˈbaʊt sæd,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: but researchers have two main theories on what may trigger it— both of which involve changes to our body's daily rhythms,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: bət ˈrisərʧərz hæv tu meɪn ˈθɪriz ɔn wət meɪ ˈtrɪgər it—*— boʊθ əv wɪʧ ˌɪnˈvɑlv ˈʧeɪnʤɪz tɪ ɑr ˈbɑdiz ˈdeɪli ˈrɪðəmz,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: also known as circadian rhythms.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ˈɔlsoʊ noʊn ɛz sərˈkeɪdiən ˈrɪðəmz.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: When sunlight reaches our eyes, light sensitive cells in the retina send a signal that travel along the optic nerve
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: wɪn ˈsənˌlaɪt ˈriʧɪz ɑr aɪz, laɪt ˈsɛnsɪtɪv sɛlz ɪn ðə ˈrɛtənə sɛnd ə ˈsɪgnəl ðət ˈtrævəl əˈlɔŋ ðə ˈɑptɪk nərv

0RiDPisQAzM.text: to our suprachiasmatic nucleus.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: tɪ ɑr suprachiasmatic* ˈnukliəs.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: This part of the brain is sometimes referred to as our biological clock
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ðɪs pɑrt əv ðə breɪn ɪz ˈsəmˌtaɪmz rɪˈfərd tɪ ɛz ɑr ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl klɑk

0RiDPisQAzM.text: because it regulates our daily rhythms of alertness, body temperature, hormone production, and cell growth.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: bɪˈkəz ɪt ˈrɛgjəˌleɪts ɑr ˈdeɪli ˈrɪðəmz əv əˈlərtnəs, ˈbɑdi ˈtɛmpərəʧər, ˈhɔrˌmoʊn pərˈdəkʃən, ənd sɛl groʊθ.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Each night after sunset, this biological clock signals the release of melatonin, a hormone that triggers sleep.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: iʧ naɪt ˈæftər ˈsənˌsɛt, ðɪs ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl klɑk ˈsɪgnəlz ðə riˈlis əv ˌmɛləˈtoʊnɪn, ə ˈhɔrˌmoʊn ðət ˈtrɪgərz slip.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: For most people, this melatonin release is consistent regardless of the time of year.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: fər moʊst ˈpipəl, ðɪs ˌmɛləˈtoʊnɪn riˈlis ɪz kənˈsɪstənt rəˈgɑrdləs əv ðə taɪm əv jɪr.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: However, one theory posits that this isn’t the case for some people with SAD. Instead, as the days get shorter,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, wən ˈθɪri ˈpɑzəts ðət ðɪs isn’t* ðə keɪs fər səm ˈpipəl wɪθ sæd. ˌɪnˈstɛd, ɛz ðə deɪz gɪt ˈʃɔrtər,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: their biological clocks signal melatonin to be released for longer each night.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ðɛr ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl klɑks ˈsɪgnəl ˌmɛləˈtoʊnɪn tɪ bi riˈlist fər ˈlɔŋgər iʧ naɪt.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: This kind of circadian change isn’t entirely unheard of— weasels and groundhogs change their appearance and behavior in winter
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ðɪs kaɪnd əv sərˈkeɪdiən ʧeɪnʤ isn’t* ɪnˈtaɪərli ənˈhərd of—*— ˈwizəlz ənd groundhogs* ʧeɪnʤ ðɛr əˈpɪrəns ənd bɪˈheɪvjər ɪn ˈwɪntər

0RiDPisQAzM.text: due to a prolonged release of melatonin.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: du tɪ ə prəˈlɔŋd riˈlis əv ˌmɛləˈtoʊnɪn.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: But it’s unclear why this would happen in certain humans.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: bət it’s* ənˈklɪr waɪ ðɪs wʊd ˈhæpən ɪn ˈsərtən ˈjumənz.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: One idea is that, unlike most people,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: wən aɪˈdiə ɪz ðət, ənˈlaɪk moʊst ˈpipəl,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: some individuals with SAD have retinas that fail to adjust to be more sensitive to light in winter.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: səm ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəlz wɪθ sæd hæv retinas* ðət feɪl tɪ əˈʤəst tɪ bi mɔr ˈsɛnsɪtɪv tɪ laɪt ɪn ˈwɪntər.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: A second hypothesis proposes that SAD isn’t related to the duration of melatonin release,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ə ˈsɛkənd haɪˈpɑθəsəs prəˈpoʊzɪz ðət sæd isn’t* rɪˈleɪtɪd tɪ ðə ˈdʊˈreɪʃən əv ˌmɛləˈtoʊnɪn riˈlis,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: but rather to a complete shift in the timing of circadian rhythms during winter.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: bət ˈrəðər tɪ ə kəmˈplit ʃɪft ɪn ðə ˈtaɪmɪŋ əv sərˈkeɪdiən ˈrɪðəmz ˈdʊrɪŋ ˈwɪntər.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: This could manifest as a phase-advance, a fast-running biological clock,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ðɪs kʊd ˈmænəˌfɛst ɛz ə phase-advance*, ə fast-running* ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl klɑk,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: or a phase-delay, a slow-running biological clock.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ər ə phase-delay*, ə slow-running* ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl klɑk.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: A phase-delay could explain why many people with SAD struggle to wake up in the morning.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ə phase-delay* kʊd ɪkˈspleɪn waɪ ˈmɛni ˈpipəl wɪθ sæd ˈstrəgəl tɪ weɪk əp ɪn ðə ˈmɔrnɪŋ.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Essentially, their hormones are telling them that it’s still nighttime. But since it’s unclear how any of these circadian changes
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ɛˈsɛnʃəli, ðɛr ˈhɔrˌmoʊnz ər ˈtɛlɪŋ ðɛm ðət it’s* stɪl ˈnaɪtˌtaɪm. bət sɪns it’s* ənˈklɪr haʊ ˈɛni əv ðiz sərˈkeɪdiən ˈʧeɪnʤɪz

0RiDPisQAzM.text: could cause such significant shifts in mood, most researchers believe other factors are at play.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: kʊd kɔz səʧ sɪgˈnɪfɪkənt ʃɪfts ɪn mud, moʊst ˈrisərʧərz bɪˈliv ˈəðər ˈfæktərz ər æt pleɪ.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: For example, as with non-seasonal depression, people with SAD are more prone to repetitive negative thinking called rumination.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ɛz wɪθ non-seasonal* dɪˈprɛʃən, ˈpipəl wɪθ sæd ər mɔr proʊn tɪ rɪˈpɛtɪtɪv ˈnɛgətɪv ˈθɪŋkɪŋ kɔld ˌrumɪˈneɪʃən.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: They also tend to be more pessimistic about wintertime.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ðeɪ ˈɔlsoʊ tɛnd tɪ bi mɔr ˌpɛsəˈmɪstɪk əˈbaʊt ˈwɪntərˌtaɪm.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: So much so, that some experts believe living in cultures that embrace the winter season may offer some protection against SAD.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: soʊ məʧ soʊ, ðət səm ˈɛkspərts bɪˈliv ˈlɪvɪŋ ɪn ˈkəlʧərz ðət ɪmˈbreɪs ðə ˈwɪntər ˈsizən meɪ ˈɔfər səm prəˈtɛkʃən əˈgɛnst sæd.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: And while there is no single SAD gene, there are multiple genes that put you at higher risk,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ənd waɪl ðɛr ɪz noʊ ˈsɪŋgəl sæd ʤin, ðɛr ər ˈməltəpəl ʤinz ðət pʊt ju æt haɪər rɪsk,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: which may be why SAD can run in families.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: wɪʧ meɪ bi waɪ sæd kən rən ɪn ˈfæməliz.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Another mystery is that some people with SAD experience the opposite seasonal phenomenon: summertime depression.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: əˈnəðər ˈmɪstəri ɪz ðət səm ˈpipəl wɪθ sæd ɪkˈspɪriəns ðə ˈɑpəzɪt ˈsizənəl fəˈnɑməˌnɑn: ˈsəmərˌtaɪm dɪˈprɛʃən.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: This may be triggered by too much light, but perhaps also heat and humidity.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ðɪs meɪ bi ˈtrɪgərd baɪ tu məʧ laɪt, bət pərˈhæps ˈɔlsoʊ hit ənd hjuˈmɪdəti.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Thankfully, despite these mysteries, doctors do know how to help with this condition. Treatments used for non-seasonal depression,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ˈθæŋkfəli, dɪˈspaɪt ðiz ˈmɪstəriz, ˈdɑktərz du noʊ haʊ tɪ hɛlp wɪθ ðɪs kənˈdɪʃən. ˈtritmənts juzd fər non-seasonal* dɪˈprɛʃən,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: like antidepressants and cognitive-behavioral talk therapy, can improve SAD’s symptoms and prevent future episodes.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: laɪk ˌænˌtidɪˈprɛsənts ənd cognitive-behavioral* tɔk ˈθɛrəpi, kən ˌɪmˈpruv sad’s* ˈsɪmptəmz ənd prɪˈvɛnt fˈjuʧər ˈɛpɪˌsoʊdz.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: There's also light therapy, which typically involves spending 30 minutes or more each morning using a device that emits full-spectrum light
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ðɛrz ˈɔlsoʊ laɪt ˈθɛrəpi, wɪʧ ˈtɪpɪkəli ˌɪnˈvɑlvz ˈspɛndɪŋ 30 ˈmɪnəts ər mɔr iʧ ˈmɔrnɪŋ ˈjuzɪŋ ə dɪˈvaɪs ðət ɪˈmɪts full-spectrum* laɪt

0RiDPisQAzM.text: while filtering out harmful UV rays.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: waɪl ˈfɪltərɪŋ aʊt ˈhɑrmfəl uv* reɪz.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: Light therapy should be started under the guidance of a healthcare expert,
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: laɪt ˈθɛrəpi ʃʊd bi ˈstɑrtɪd ˈəndər ðə ˈgaɪdəns əv ə ˈhɛlθˌkɛr ˈɛkspərt,

0RiDPisQAzM.text: as it can be difficult to figure out dosage and timing, and it can cause side effects.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: ɛz ɪt kən bi ˈdɪfəkəlt tɪ ˈfɪgjər aʊt ˈdoʊsɪʤ ənd ˈtaɪmɪŋ, ənd ɪt kən kɔz saɪd ˈifɛkts.

0RiDPisQAzM.text: But with regular use, it’s thought to help shift circadian rhythms back to their sunnier, summer-like position.
0RiDPisQAzM.ipa: bət wɪθ ˈrɛgjələr juz, it’s* θɔt tɪ hɛlp ʃɪft sərˈkeɪdiən ˈrɪðəmz bæk tɪ ðɛr sunnier*, summer-like* pəˈzɪʃən.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Even after writing eleven books and winning several prestigious awards, Maya Angelou couldn’t escape the nagging doubt
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ˈivɪn ˈæftər ˈraɪtɪŋ ˈilɛvən bʊks ənd ˈwɪnɪŋ ˈsɛvərəl pərˈstiʤəs əˈwɔrdz, maɪə ˈænʤəˌlu couldn’t* ɪˈskeɪp ðə ˈnægɪŋ daʊt

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: that she hadn’t really earned her accomplishments. Albert Einstein experienced something similar:
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ðət ʃi hadn’t* ˈrɪli ərnd hər əˈkɑmplɪʃmənts. ˈælbərt ˈaɪnstaɪn ɪkˈspɪriənst ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈsɪmələr:

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: he described himself as an “involuntary swindler” whose work didn’t deserve as much attention as it had received.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: hi dɪˈskraɪbd hɪmˈsɛlf ɛz ən ““involuntary* swindler”*” huz wərk didn’t* dɪˈzərv ɛz məʧ əˈtɛnʃən ɛz ɪt hæd rɪˈsivd.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Accomplishments at the level of Angelou’s or Einstein’s are rare, but their feeling of fraudulence is extremely common.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: əˈkɑmplɪʃmənts æt ðə ˈlɛvəl əv angelou’s* ər einstein’s* ər rɛr, bət ðɛr ˈfilɪŋ əv fraudulence* ɪz ɪkˈstrimli ˈkɑmən.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Why can’t so many of us shake feelings that we haven’t earned our accomplishments,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: waɪ can’t* soʊ ˈmɛni əv ˈjuˈɛs ʃeɪk ˈfilɪŋz ðət wi haven’t* ərnd ɑr əˈkɑmplɪʃmənts,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: or that our ideas and skills aren’t worthy of others’ attention? Psychologist Pauline Rose Clance was the first to study
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ər ðət ɑr aɪˈdiəz ənd skɪlz aren’t* ˈwərði əv others’*’ əˈtɛnʃən? saɪˈkɑləʤəst pɔˈlin roʊz clance* wɑz ðə fərst tɪ ˈstədi

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: this unwarranted sense of insecurity. In her work as a therapist,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ðɪs ənˈwɔrəntɪd sɛns əv ˌɪnsɪˈkjʊrɪti. ɪn hər wərk ɛz ə ˈθɛrəpɪst,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: she noticed many of her undergraduate patients shared a concern: though they had high grades,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ʃi ˈnoʊtɪst ˈmɛni əv hər ˌəndərˈgræʤəwət ˈpeɪʃənz ʃɛrd ə kənˈsərn: ðoʊ ðeɪ hæd haɪ greɪdz,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: they didn’t believe they deserved their spots at the university. Some even believed their acceptance had been an admissions error.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ðeɪ didn’t* bɪˈliv ðeɪ dɪˈzərvd ðɛr spɑts æt ðə ˌjunəˈvərsəti. səm ˈivɪn bɪˈlivd ðɛr əkˈsɛptəns hæd bɪn ən ədˈmɪʃənz ˈɛrər.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: While Clance knew these fears were unfounded, she could also remember feeling the exact same way in graduate school.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: waɪl clance* nu ðiz fɪrz wər ənˈfaʊndɪd, ʃi kʊd ˈɔlsoʊ rɪˈmɛmbər ˈfilɪŋ ðə ɪgˈzækt seɪm weɪ ɪn ˈgræʤəˌweɪt skul.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: She and her patients experienced something that goes by a number of names-- imposter phenomenon, imposter experience,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ʃi ənd hər ˈpeɪʃənz ɪkˈspɪriənst ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət goʊz baɪ ə ˈnəmbər əv neɪmz-- ˌɪmˈpɔstər fəˈnɑməˌnɑn, ˌɪmˈpɔstər ɪkˈspɪriəns,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: and imposter syndrome. Together with colleague Suzanne Imes,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ənd ˌɪmˈpɔstər ˈsɪnˌdroʊm. təˈgɛðər wɪθ ˈkɑlig suˈzæn aɪmz,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Clance first studied imposterism in female college students and faculty.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: clance* fərst ˈstədid imposterism* ɪn ˈfiˌmeɪl ˈkɑlɪʤ ˈstudənts ənd ˈfækəlti.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Their work established pervasive feelings of fraudulence in this group. Since that first study, the same thing has been established across gender,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ðɛr wərk ɪˈstæblɪʃt pərˈveɪsɪv ˈfilɪŋz əv fraudulence* ɪn ðɪs grup. sɪns ðət fərst ˈstədi, ðə seɪm θɪŋ həz bɪn ɪˈstæblɪʃt əˈkrɔs ˈʤɛndər,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: race, age, and a huge range of occupations, though it may be more prevalent and disproportionately affect
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: reɪs, eɪʤ, ənd ə juʤ reɪnʤ əv ˌɑkjəˈpeɪʃənz, ðoʊ ɪt meɪ bi mɔr ˈprɛvələnt ənd ˌdɪsprəˈpɔrʃənətli əˈfɛkt

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: the experiences of underrepresented or disadvantaged groups. To call it a syndrome is to downplay how universal it is.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ðə ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz əv əndərˌrɛprɪˈzɛntɪd ər ˌdɪsədˈvænɪʤd grups. tɪ kɔl ɪt ə ˈsɪnˌdroʊm ɪz tɪ ˈdaʊnˌpleɪ haʊ ˌjunəˈvərsəl ɪt ɪz.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: It's not a disease or an abnormality, and it isn’t necessarily tied to depression, anxiety,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ɪts nɑt ə dɪˈziz ər ən ˌæbnɔrˈmæləti, ənd ɪt isn’t* ˌnɛsəˈsɛrəli taɪd tɪ dɪˈprɛʃən, æŋˈzaɪəti,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: or self-esteem. Where do these feelings of fraudulence come from? People who are highly skilled or accomplished
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ər self-esteem*. wɛr du ðiz ˈfilɪŋz əv fraudulence* kəm frəm? ˈpipəl hu ər ˈhaɪli skɪld ər əˈkɑmplɪʃt

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: tend to think others are just as skilled. This can spiral into feelings that they don’t deserve accolades
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: tɛnd tɪ θɪŋk ˈəðərz ər ʤɪst ɛz skɪld. ðɪs kən ˈspaɪrəl ˈɪntu ˈfilɪŋz ðət ðeɪ don’t* dɪˈzərv ˈækəˌleɪdz

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: and opportunities over other people. And as Angelou and Einstein experienced, there’s often no threshold of accomplishment
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ənd ˌɑpərˈtunətiz ˈoʊvər ˈəðər ˈpipəl. ənd ɛz ˈænʤəˌlu ənd ˈaɪnstaɪn ɪkˈspɪriənst, there’s* ˈɔfən noʊ θˈrɛˌʃoʊld əv əˈkɑmplɪʃmənt

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: that puts these feelings to rest. Feelings of imposterism aren’t restricted to highly skilled individuals, either.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ðət pʊts ðiz ˈfilɪŋz tɪ rɛst. ˈfilɪŋz əv imposterism* aren’t* riˈstrɪktɪd tɪ ˈhaɪli skɪld ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəlz, ˈiðər.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Everyone is susceptible to a phenomenon known as pluralistic ignorance, where we each doubt ourselves privately,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ˈɛvriˌwən ɪz səˈsɛptəbəl tɪ ə fəˈnɑməˌnɑn noʊn ɛz ˌplʊrəˈlɪstɪk ˈɪgnərəns, wɛr wi iʧ daʊt ɑrˈsɛlvz ˈpraɪvətli,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: but believe we’re alone in thinking that way because no one else voices their doubts.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: bət bɪˈliv we’re* əˈloʊn ɪn ˈθɪŋkɪŋ ðət weɪ bɪˈkəz noʊ wən ɛls ˈvɔɪsɪz ðɛr daʊts.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Since it’s tough to really know how hard our peers work, how difficult they find certain tasks,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: sɪns it’s* təf tɪ ˈrɪli noʊ haʊ hɑrd ɑr pɪrz wərk, haʊ ˈdɪfəkəlt ðeɪ faɪnd ˈsərtən tæsks,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: or how much they doubt themselves, there’s no easy way to dismiss feelings that we’re less capable than the people around us.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ər haʊ məʧ ðeɪ daʊt ðɛmˈsɛlvz, there’s* noʊ ˈizi weɪ tɪ dɪsˈmɪs ˈfilɪŋz ðət we’re* lɛs ˈkeɪpəbəl ðən ðə ˈpipəl əraʊnd ˈjuˈɛs.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Intense feelings of imposterism can prevent people from sharing their great ideas or applying for jobs and programs where they’d excel.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ˌɪnˈtɛns ˈfilɪŋz əv imposterism* kən prɪˈvɛnt ˈpipəl frəm ˈʃɛrɪŋ ðɛr greɪt aɪˈdiəz ər əˈplaɪɪŋ fər ʤɑbz ənd ˈproʊˌgræmz wɛr they’d* ɪkˈsɛl.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: At least so far, the most surefire way to combat imposter syndrome is to talk about it.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: æt list soʊ fɑr, ðə moʊst surefire* weɪ tɪ ˈkɑmbæt ˌɪmˈpɔstər ˈsɪnˌdroʊm ɪz tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt ɪt.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Many people suffering from imposter syndrome are afraid that if they ask about their performance, their fears will be confirmed.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ˈmɛni ˈpipəl ˈsəfərɪŋ frəm ˌɪmˈpɔstər ˈsɪnˌdroʊm ər əˈfreɪd ðət ɪf ðeɪ æsk əˈbaʊt ðɛr pərˈfɔrməns, ðɛr fɪrz wɪl bi kənˈfərmd.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: And even when they receive positive feedback, it often fails to ease feelings of fraudulence. But on the other hand,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ənd ˈivɪn wɪn ðeɪ rɪˈsiv ˈpɑzətɪv ˈfidˌbæk, ɪt ˈɔfən feɪlz tɪ iz ˈfilɪŋz əv fraudulence*. bət ɔn ðə ˈəðər hænd,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: hearing that an advisor or mentor has experienced feelings of imposterism can help relieve those feelings. The same goes for peers.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ˈhirɪŋ ðət ən ædˈvaɪzər ər ˈmɛnˌtɔr həz ɪkˈspɪriənst ˈfilɪŋz əv imposterism* kən hɛlp rɪˈliv ðoʊz ˈfilɪŋz. ðə seɪm goʊz fər pɪrz.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Even simply finding out there’s a term for these feelings can be an incredible relief. Once you’re aware of the phenomenon,
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ˈivɪn ˈsɪmpli ˈfaɪndɪŋ aʊt there’s* ə tərm fər ðiz ˈfilɪŋz kən bi ən ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbəl rɪˈlif. wəns you’re* əˈwɛr əv ðə fəˈnɑməˌnɑn,

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: you can combat your own imposter syndrome by collecting and revisiting positive feedback.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ju kən ˈkɑmbæt jʊr oʊn ˌɪmˈpɔstər ˈsɪnˌdroʊm baɪ kəˈlɛktɪŋ ənd riˈvɪzɪtɪŋ ˈpɑzətɪv ˈfidˌbæk.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: One scientist who kept blaming herself for problems in her lab started to document the causes every time something went wrong.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: wən ˈsaɪəntɪst hu kɛpt ˈbleɪmɪŋ hərˈsɛlf fər ˈprɑbləmz ɪn hər læb ˈstɑrtɪd tɪ ˈdɑkjəmɛnt ðə ˈkɔzɪz ˈɛvəri taɪm ˈsəmθɪŋ wɛnt rɔŋ.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: Eventually, she realized most of the problems came from equipment failure, and came to recognize her own competence.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli, ʃi ˈriəˌlaɪzd moʊst əv ðə ˈprɑbləmz keɪm frəm ɪkˈwɪpmənt ˈfeɪljər, ənd keɪm tɪ ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪz hər oʊn ˈkɑmpətɪns.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: We may never be able to banish these feelings entirely, but we can have open conversations about academic or professional challenges.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: wi meɪ ˈnɛvər bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ ˈbænɪʃ ðiz ˈfilɪŋz ɪnˈtaɪərli, bət wi kən hæv ˈoʊpən ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz əˈbaʊt ˌækəˈdɛmɪk ər prəˈfɛʃənəl ˈʧælənʤɪz.

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: With increasing awareness of how common these experiences are, perhaps we can feel freer to be frank about our feelings
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: wɪθ ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋ əˈwɛrnəs əv haʊ ˈkɑmən ðiz ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz ər, pərˈhæps wi kən fil friər tɪ bi fræŋk əˈbaʊt ɑr ˈfilɪŋz

ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.text: and build confidence in some simple truths: you have talent, you are capable, and you belong.
ZQUxL4Jm1Lo.ipa: ənd bɪld ˈkɑnfədɛns ɪn səm ˈsɪmpəl truθs: ju hæv ˈtælənt, ju ər ˈkeɪpəbəl, ənd ju bɪˈlɔŋ.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Mastering any physical skill, be it performing a pirouette, playing an instrument, or throwing a baseball,
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˈmæstərɪŋ ˈɛni ˈfɪzɪkəl skɪl, bi ɪt pərˈfɔrmɪŋ ə ˌpɪruˈɛ, pleɪɪŋ ən ˈɪnstrəmənt, ər θroʊɪŋ ə ˈbeɪsˈbɔl,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: takes practice. Practice is the repetition of an action with the goal of improvement,
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: teɪks ˈpræktɪs. ˈpræktɪs ɪz ðə ˌrɛpəˈtɪʃən əv ən ˈækʃən wɪθ ðə goʊl əv ˌɪmˈpruvmənt,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: and it helps us perform with more ease, speed, and confidence.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ənd ɪt hɛlps ˈjuˈɛs pərˈfɔrm wɪθ mɔr iz, spid, ənd ˈkɑnfədɛns.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: So what does practice do in our brains to make us better at things? Our brains have two kinds of neural tissue:
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: soʊ wət dɪz ˈpræktɪs du ɪn ɑr breɪnz tɪ meɪk ˈjuˈɛs ˈbɛtər æt θɪŋz? ɑr breɪnz hæv tu kaɪnz əv ˈnʊrəl ˈtɪʃu:

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: grey matter and white matter. The grey matter processes information in the brain,
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: greɪ ˈmætər ənd waɪt ˈmætər. ðə greɪ ˈmætər ˈprɑsɛsəz ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ɪn ðə breɪn,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: directing signals and sensory stimuli to nerve cells,
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: dɪˈrɛktɪŋ ˈsɪgnəlz ənd ˈsɛnsəri ˈstɪmjəˌlaɪ tɪ nərv sɛlz,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: while white matter is mostly made up of fatty tissue and nerve fibers. In order for our bodies to move,
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: waɪl waɪt ˈmætər ɪz ˈmoʊstli meɪd əp əv ˈfæti ˈtɪʃu ənd nərv ˈfaɪbərz. ɪn ˈɔrdər fər ɑr ˈbɑdiz tɪ muv,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: information needs to travel from the brain's grey matter, down the spinal cord, through a chain of nerve fibers called axons
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən nidz tɪ ˈtrævəl frəm ðə breɪnz greɪ ˈmætər, daʊn ðə ˈspaɪnəl kɔrd, θru ə ʧeɪn əv nərv ˈfaɪbərz kɔld ˈækˌsɑnz

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: to our muscles. So how does practice or repetition affect the inner workings of our brains?
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: tɪ ɑr ˈməsəlz. soʊ haʊ dɪz ˈpræktɪs ər ˌrɛpəˈtɪʃən əˈfɛkt ðə ˈɪnər ˈwərkɪŋz əv ɑr breɪnz?

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: The axons that exist in the white matter are wrapped with a fatty substance called myelin.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ðə ˈækˌsɑnz ðət ɪgˈzɪst ɪn ðə waɪt ˈmætər ər ræpt wɪθ ə ˈfæti ˈsəbstəns kɔld ˈmaɪɪlɪn.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: And it's this myelin covering, or sheath, that seems to change with practice.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ənd ɪts ðɪs ˈmaɪɪlɪn ˈkəvərɪŋ, ər ʃiθ, ðət simz tɪ ʧeɪnʤ wɪθ ˈpræktɪs.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Myelin is similar to insulation on electrical cables. It prevents energy loss from electrical signals that the brain uses,
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˈmaɪɪlɪn ɪz ˈsɪmələr tɪ ˌɪnsəˈleɪʃən ɔn ɪˈlɛktrɪkəl ˈkeɪbəlz. ɪt prɪˈvɛnts ˈɛnərʤi lɔs frəm ɪˈlɛktrɪkəl ˈsɪgnəlz ðət ðə breɪn ˈjuzɪz,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: moving them more efficiently along neural pathways.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˈmuvɪŋ ðɛm mɔr ɪˈfɪʃəntli əˈlɔŋ ˈnʊrəl ˈpæθˌweɪz.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Some recent studies in mice suggest that the repetition of a physical motion
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: səm ˈrisənt ˈstədiz ɪn maɪs səˈʤɛst ðət ðə ˌrɛpəˈtɪʃən əv ə ˈfɪzɪkəl ˈmoʊʃən

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: increases the layers of myelin sheath that insulates the axons.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˌɪnˈkrisɪz ðə leɪərz əv ˈmaɪɪlɪn ʃiθ ðət insulates* ðə ˈækˌsɑnz.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: And the more layers, the greater the insulation around the axon chains, forming a sort of superhighway for information
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ənd ðə mɔr leɪərz, ðə ˈgreɪtər ðə ˌɪnsəˈleɪʃən əraʊnd ðə ˈækˌsɑn ʧeɪnz, ˈfɔrmɪŋ ə sɔrt əv ˌsupərˈhaɪˌweɪ fər ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: connecting your brain to your muscles. So while many athletes and performers attribute their successes to muscle memory,
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: kəˈnɛktɪŋ jʊr breɪn tɪ jʊr ˈməsəlz. soʊ waɪl ˈmɛni ˈæθˌlits ənd pərˈfɔrmərz əˈtrɪˌbjut ðɛr səkˈsɛsɪz tɪ ˈməsəl ˈmɛməri,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: muscles themselves don't really have memory. Rather, it may be the myelination of neural pathways
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˈməsəlz ðɛmˈsɛlvz doʊnt ˈrɪli hæv ˈmɛməri. ˈrəðər, ɪt meɪ bi ðə myelination* əv ˈnʊrəl ˈpæθˌweɪz

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: that gives these athletes and performers their edge with faster and more efficient neural pathways.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ðət gɪvz ðiz ˈæθˌlits ənd pərˈfɔrmərz ðɛr ɛʤ wɪθ ˈfæstər ənd mɔr ɪˈfɪʃənt ˈnʊrəl ˈpæθˌweɪz.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: There are many theories that attempt to quantify the number of hours, days, and even years of practice
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ðɛr ər ˈmɛni ˈθɪriz ðət əˈtɛmpt tɪ kˈwɑntɪˌfaɪ ðə ˈnəmbər əv aʊərz, deɪz, ənd ˈivɪn jɪrz əv ˈpræktɪs

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: that it takes to master a skill. While we don't yet have a magic number,
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ðət ɪt teɪks tɪ ˈmæstər ə skɪl. waɪl wi doʊnt jɛt hæv ə ˈmæʤɪk ˈnəmbər,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: we do know that mastery isn't simply about the amount of hours of practice.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: wi du noʊ ðət ˈmæstəri ˈɪzənt ˈsɪmpli əˈbaʊt ðə əˈmaʊnt əv aʊərz əv ˈpræktɪs.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: It's also the quality and effectiveness of that practice. Effective practice is consistent, intensely focused,
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ɪts ˈɔlsoʊ ðə kˈwɑləti ənd ˈifɛktɪvnəs əv ðət ˈpræktɪs. ˈifɛktɪv ˈpræktɪs ɪz kənˈsɪstənt, ˌɪnˈtɛnsli ˈfoʊkɪst,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: and targets content or weaknesses that lie at the edge of one's current abilities.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ənd ˈtɑrgəts ˈkɑntɛnt ər ˈwiknəsɪz ðət laɪ æt ðə ɛʤ əv wənz ˈkɑrənt əˈbɪləˌtiz.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: So if effective practice is the key, how can we get the most out of our practice time? Try these tips.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: soʊ ɪf ˈifɛktɪv ˈpræktɪs ɪz ðə ki, haʊ kən wi gɪt ðə moʊst aʊt əv ɑr ˈpræktɪs taɪm? traɪ ðiz tɪps.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Focus on the task at hand. Minimize potential distractions by turning off the computer or TV
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˈfoʊkɪs ɔn ðə tæsk æt hænd. ˈmɪnəˌmaɪz pəˈtɛnʃəl dɪˈstrækʃənz baɪ ˈtərnɪŋ ɔf ðə kəmˈpjutər ər ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: and putting your cell phone on airplane mode. In one study, researchers observed 260 students studying.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ənd ˈpʊtɪŋ jʊr sɛl foʊn ɔn ˈɛrˌpleɪn moʊd. ɪn wən ˈstədi, ˈrisərʧərz əbˈzərvd 260 ˈstudənts ˈstədiɪŋ.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: On average, those students were able to stay on task for only six minutes at a time.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ɔn ˈævərɪʤ, ðoʊz ˈstudənts wər ˈeɪbəl tɪ steɪ ɔn tæsk fər ˈoʊnli sɪks ˈmɪnəts æt ə taɪm.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Laptops, smartphones, and particularly Facebook were the root of most distractions. Start out slowly or in slow-motion.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˈlæpˌtɑps, smartphones*, ənd ˌpɑrˈtɪkjələrli ˈfeɪsˌbʊk wər ðə rut əv moʊst dɪˈstrækʃənz. stɑrt aʊt sˈloʊli ər ɪn slow-motion*.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Coordination is built with repetitions, whether correct or incorrect.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: koʊˌɔrdəˈneɪʃən ɪz bɪlt wɪθ ˌrɛpəˈtɪʃənz, ˈwɛðər kərˈɛkt ər ˌɪnkərˈɛkt.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: If you gradually increase the speed of the quality repetitons, you have a better chance of doing them correctly.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ɪf ju ˈgræʤuəli ˌɪnˈkris ðə spid əv ðə kˈwɑləti repetitons*, ju hæv ə ˈbɛtər ʧæns əv duɪŋ ðɛm kərˈɛktli.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Next, frequent repetitions with allotted breaks are common practice habits of elite performers.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: nɛkst, ˈfrikˌwɛnt ˌrɛpəˈtɪʃənz wɪθ əˈlɑtɪd breɪks ər ˈkɑmən ˈpræktɪs ˈhæbəts əv ɪˈlit pərˈfɔrmərz.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Studies have shown that many top athletes, musicians, and dancers
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˈstədiz hæv ʃoʊn ðət ˈmɛni tɔp ˈæθˌlits, mjuˈzɪʃənz, ənd ˈdænsərz

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: spend 50-60 hours per week on activities related to their craft.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: spɛnd 50-60* aʊərz pər wik ɔn ækˈtɪvɪtiz rɪˈleɪtɪd tɪ ðɛr kræft.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Many divide their time used for effective practice into multiple daily practice sessions of limited duration.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ˈmɛni dɪˈvaɪd ðɛr taɪm juzd fər ˈifɛktɪv ˈpræktɪs ˈɪntu ˈməltəpəl ˈdeɪli ˈpræktɪs ˈsɛʃənz əv ˈlɪmɪtɪd ˈdʊˈreɪʃən.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: And finally, practice in your brain in vivid detail. It's a bit surprising, but a number of studies suggest
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ənd ˈfaɪnəli, ˈpræktɪs ɪn jʊr breɪn ɪn ˈvɪvɪd ˈditeɪl. ɪts ə bɪt səˈpraɪzɪŋ, bət ə ˈnəmbər əv ˈstədiz səˈʤɛst

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: that once a physical motion has been established, it can be reinforced just by imagining it.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ðət wəns ə ˈfɪzɪkəl ˈmoʊʃən həz bɪn ɪˈstæblɪʃt, ɪt kən bi ˌriɪnˈfɔrst ʤɪst baɪ ˌɪˈmæʤənɪŋ ɪt.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: In one study, 144 basketball players were divided into two groups.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ɪn wən ˈstədi, 144 ˈbæskətˌbɔl pleɪərz wər dɪˈvaɪdɪd ˈɪntu tu grups.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: Group A physically practiced one-handed free throws while Group B only mentally practiced them.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: grup ə ˈfɪzɪkəli ˈpræktɪst one-handed* fri θroʊz waɪl grup bi ˈoʊnli ˈmɛnəli ˈpræktɪst ðɛm.

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: When they were tested at the end of the two week experiment, the intermediate and experienced players in both groups
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: wɪn ðeɪ wər ˈtɛstɪd æt ðə ɛnd əv ðə tu wik ɪkˈspɛrəmənt, ðə ˌɪnərˈmidiɪt ənd ɪkˈspɪriənst pleɪərz ɪn boʊθ grups

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: had improved by nearly the same amount. As scientists get closer to unraveling the secrets of our brains,
