f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: hæd ˌɪmˈpruvd baɪ ˈnɪrli ðə seɪm əˈmaʊnt. ɛz ˈsaɪəntɪsts gɪt ˈkloʊzər tɪ ənˈrævəlɪŋ ðə ˈsikrɪts əv ɑr breɪnz,

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: our understanding of effective practice will only improve. In the meantime, effective practice is the best way we have
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: ɑr ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ əv ˈifɛktɪv ˈpræktɪs wɪl ˈoʊnli ˌɪmˈpruv. ɪn ðə ˈminˌtaɪm, ˈifɛktɪv ˈpræktɪs ɪz ðə bɛst weɪ wi hæv

f2O6mQkFiiw.text: of pushing our individual limits, achieving new heights, and maximizing our potential.
f2O6mQkFiiw.ipa: əv ˈpʊʃɪŋ ɑr ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəl ˈlɪmɪts, əˈʧivɪŋ nu haɪts, ənd ˈmæksəˌmaɪzɪŋ ɑr pəˈtɛnʃəl.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: In 1937, renowned fashion designer Elsa Schiaparelli unveiled one of her most famous creations:
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ɪn 1937 rɪˈnaʊnd ˈfæʃən dɪˈzaɪnər ˈɛlsə schiaparelli* ənˈveɪld wən əv hər moʊst ˈfeɪməs kriˈeɪʃənz:

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: a black stiletto with a pink heel.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ə blæk stəˈlɛtoʊ wɪθ ə pɪŋk hil.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: What made this seemingly ordinary piece so noteworthy was that it was designed to sit on top of a woman’s head.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: wət meɪd ðɪs ˈsimɪŋli ˈɔrdəˌnɛri pis soʊ ˈnoʊtˌwərði wɑz ðət ɪt wɑz dɪˈzaɪnd tɪ sɪt ɔn tɔp əv ə woman’s* hɛd.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Created in collaboration with Salvador Dalí, Schiaparelli’s bizarre shoe hat was celebrated as an iconic contribution
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: kriˈeɪtɪd ɪn kəˌlæbərˈeɪʃən wɪθ ˈsælvəˌdɔr dalí*í, schiaparelli’s* bɪˈzɑr ʃu hæt wɑz ˈsɛləˌbreɪtɪd ɛz ən ˌaɪˈkɑnɪk ˌkɑntrəˈbjuʃən

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: to the Surrealist art movement.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: tɪ ðə surrealist* ɑrt ˈmuvmənt.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Humans have been fashioning caps since the dawn of civilization.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ˈjumənz hæv bɪn ˈfæʃənɪŋ kæps sɪns ðə dɔn əv ˌsɪvəlɪˈzeɪʃən.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: A simple bearskin cap from the Bronze Age is one of the earliest surviving hats,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ə ˈsɪmpəl bearskin* kæp frəm ðə brɑnz eɪʤ ɪz wən əv ðə ˈərliəst sərˈvaɪvɪŋ hæts,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: discovered in the Alps alongside Europe’s oldest mummy, Ötzi the Iceman.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: dɪˈskəvərd ɪn ðə ælps əˈlɔŋˈsaɪd europe’s* ˈoʊldəst ˈməmi, öötzi* ðə ˈaɪsmæn.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: But we’ve found images of hats that are much older:
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: bət we’ve* faʊnd ˈɪmɪʤɪz əv hæts ðət ər məʧ ˈoʊldər:

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: some believe that the “Venus of Willendorf,” a Paleolithic figurine made around 30,000 years ago,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: səm bɪˈliv ðət ðə ““venus* əv willendorf,”*,” ə paleolithic* ˌfɪgjərˈin meɪd əraʊnd 30,000* jɪrz əˈgoʊ,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: is the earliest known depiction of woven headgear.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ɪz ðə ˈərliəst noʊn dɪˈpɪkʃən əv ˈwoʊvən ˈhɛdˌgɪr.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Hats aren’t just for keeping heads warm; they can also be used to make political statements.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: hæts aren’t* ʤɪst fər ˈkipɪŋ hɛdz wɔrm; ðeɪ kən ˈɔlsoʊ bi juzd tɪ meɪk pəˈlɪtɪkəl ˈsteɪtmənts.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Take the pileus cap, a favorite among ancient Romans and Greeks.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: teɪk ðə pileus* kæp, ə ˈfeɪvərɪt əˈməŋ ˈeɪnʧənt ˈroʊmənz ənd griks.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: In the Roman Republic, pileus caps were given to previously enslaved people as a public sign of their freedom
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ɪn ðə ˈroʊmən riˈpəblɪk, pileus* kæps wər ˈgɪvɪn tɪ ˈpriviəsli ɛnsˈleɪvd ˈpipəl ɛz ə ˈpəblɪk saɪn əv ðɛr ˈfridəm

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: and came to symbolize the ideals of personal and political liberty.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ənd keɪm tɪ ˈsɪmbəˌlaɪz ðə aɪˈdilz əv ˈpərsɪnəl ənd pəˈlɪtɪkəl ˈlɪbərˌti.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: In the 18th century, American and French revolutionaries seemed to have conflated the pileus
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ɪn ðə 18th* ˈsɛnʧəri, əˈmɛrɪkən ənd frɛnʧ ˌrɛvəˈluʃəˌnɛriz simd tɪ hæv conflated* ðə pileus*

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: with the very similar-looking Phrygian cap, embracing the Phrygian as an emblem of their cause.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: wɪθ ðə ˈvɛri similar-looking* ˈfrɪʤiən kæp, ɛmˈbreɪsɪŋ ðə ˈfrɪʤiən ɛz ən ˈɛmbləm əv ðɛr kɔz.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: This is reflected in renderings of Marianne, a personification of the French Republic, who often wears a red Phrygian.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ðɪs ɪz rɪˈflɛktɪd ɪn ˈrɛndərɪŋz əv ˌmɑriˈæn, ə pərˌsɑnəfəˈkeɪʃən əv ðə frɛnʧ riˈpəblɪk, hu ˈɔfən wɛrz ə rɛd ˈfrɪʤiən.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Hats have also been an essential accessory for projecting status and power. In Western Europe in the 15th century,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: hæts hæv ˈɔlsoʊ bɪn ən ɛˈsɛnʃəl ækˈsɛsəri fər prɑˈʤɛktɪŋ ˈstætəs ənd paʊər. ɪn ˈwɛstərn ˈjʊrəp ɪn ðə 15th* ˈsɛnʧəri,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: nothing conveyed royal excess quite like the hennin.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ˈnəθɪŋ kənˈveɪd rɔɪəl ˈɛkˌsɛs kwaɪt laɪk ðə hennin*.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Popular among the women of the Burgundy Court,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ˈpɑpjələr əˈməŋ ðə ˈwɪmən əv ðə ˈbərgəndi kɔrt,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: these bejeweled headdresses were crafted from expensive silks and included variations like a flat-topped design
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ðiz bejeweled* ˈhɛdˌdrɛsəz wər ˈkræftɪd frəm ɪkˈspɛnsɪv sɪlks ənd ˌɪnˈkludɪd ˌvɛriˈeɪʃənz laɪk ə flat-topped* dɪˈzaɪn

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: and the daring double-coned escoffion.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ənd ðə ˈdɛrɪŋ double-coned* escoffion*.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Hat height was restricted according to social rank: while princesses could sport meter-high hennins,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: hæt haɪt wɑz riˈstrɪktɪd əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ˈsoʊʃəl ræŋk: waɪl ˈprɪnsɛsɪz kʊd spɔrt meter-high* hennins*,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: noblewomen were limited to much shorter styles. And they weren’t without their critics;
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: noblewomen* wər ˈlɪmɪtɪd tɪ məʧ ˈʃɔrtər staɪlz. ənd ðeɪ weren’t* wɪˈθaʊt ðɛr ˈkrɪtɪks;

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: hennins were often targeted by clerics and moralists who found their flashy designs impious.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: hennins* wər ˈɔfən ˈtɑrgətɪd baɪ ˈklɛrɪks ənd ˈmɔrəˌlɪsts hu faʊnd ðɛr ˈflæʃi dɪˈzaɪnz ˌɪmˈpaɪəs.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Nearly a century later, Suleiman the Magnificent, the longest-reigning sultan of the Ottoman Empire,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ˈnɪrli ə ˈsɛnʧəri ˈleɪtər, suleiman* ðə mægˈnɪfɪsənt, ðə longest-reigning* ˈsəltən əv ðə ˈɑtəmən ˈɛmpaɪər,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: became known for his bold military campaigns and even more audacious headgear.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: bɪˈkeɪm noʊn fər hɪz boʊld ˈmɪlɪˌtɛri kæmˈpeɪnz ənd ˈivɪn mɔr ɑˈdeɪʃəs ˈhɛdˌgɪr.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: His grand bulb-shaped turbans were adorned with sparkling sprays of diamonds or feathers.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: hɪz grænd bulb-shaped* ˈtərbənz wər əˈdɔrnd wɪθ ˈspɑrkəlɪŋ spreɪz əv ˈdaɪməndz ər ˈfɛðərz.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: His collection also included a grand four-tiered golden helmet, though some historians suspect it wasn’t actually designed for wearing.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: hɪz kəˈlɛkʃən ˈɔlsoʊ ˌɪnˈkludɪd ə grænd four-tiered* ˈgoʊldən ˈhɛlmət, ðoʊ səm hɪˈstɔriənz ˈsəˌspɛkt ɪt wasn’t* ˈæˌkʧuəli dɪˈzaɪnd fər ˈwɛrɪŋ.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Rather, the crown was crafted to flaunt his immense power and wealth to his rivals.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ˈrəðər, ðə kraʊn wɑz ˈkræftɪd tɪ flɔnt hɪz ˌɪˈmɛns paʊər ənd wɛlθ tɪ hɪz ˈraɪvəlz.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: For other caps, the real story lies in their making.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: fər ˈəðər kæps, ðə ril ˈstɔri laɪz ɪn ðɛr ˈmeɪkɪŋ.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: The sleek British top hat was first introduced in the early 19th century as powdered wigs fell out of fashion.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ðə slik ˈbrɪtɪʃ tɔp hæt wɑz fərst ˌɪntrəˈdust ɪn ðə ˈərli 19th* ˈsɛnʧəri ɛz ˈpaʊdərd wɪgz fɛl aʊt əv ˈfæʃən.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Originally crafted from the fur of animals like beavers,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ərˈɪʤənəli ˈkræftɪd frəm ðə fər əv ˈænəməlz laɪk ˈbivərz,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: hat makers would soak the material in vats filled with toxic mercuric nitrate
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: hæt ˈmeɪkərz wʊd soʊk ðə məˈtɪriəl ɪn væts fɪld wɪθ ˈtɑksɪk mərˈkjʊrɪk ˈnaɪˌtreɪt

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: to improve the felts quality and density.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: tɪ ˌɪmˈpruv ðə fɛlts kˈwɑləti ənd ˈdɛnsɪti.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: But prolonged exposure to the chemical in poorly ventilated spaces led many hatters to develop mercury poisoning,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: bət prəˈlɔŋd ɪkˈspoʊʒər tɪ ðə ˈkɛmɪkəl ɪn ˈpurli ˈvɛntɪˌleɪtɪd ˈspeɪsɪz lɛd ˈmɛni hatters* tɪ dɪˈvɛləp ˈmərkjəri ˈpɔɪzənɪŋ,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: suffering symptoms like irritability, slurred speech, memory problems, and tremors.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ˈsəfərɪŋ ˈsɪmptəmz laɪk ˌɪrɪtəˈbɪləti, slərd spiʧ, ˈmɛməri ˈprɑbləmz, ənd ˈtrɛmərz.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: The figure of the Mad Hatter, immortalized in Lewis Carroll’s “Alice in Wonderland,”
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ðə ˈfɪgjər əv ðə mæd ˈhætər, ˌɪˈmɔrtəlaɪzd ɪn luɪs carroll’s* ““alice* ɪn wonderland,”*,”

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: is a popular reference to this devastating plight.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ɪz ə ˈpɑpjələr ˈrɛfərəns tɪ ðɪs ˈdɛvəˌsteɪtɪŋ plaɪt.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Thankfully, in the late 1800s, as more people became aware of the dangers of mercury, many countries began to ban its use.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ˈθæŋkfəli, ɪn ðə leɪt 1800s*, ɛz mɔr ˈpipəl bɪˈkeɪm əˈwɛr əv ðə ˈdeɪnʤərz əv ˈmərkjəri, ˈmɛni ˈkəntriz bɪˈgæn tɪ bæn ɪts juz.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: Over the following century, top hats, bowlers, and bonnets largely fell out of fashion as everyday wear,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ˈoʊvər ðə ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ˈsɛnʧəri, tɔp hæts, ˈboʊlərz, ənd bonnets* ˈlɑrʤli fɛl aʊt əv ˈfæʃən ɛz ˈɛvriˈdeɪ wɛr,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: but one cap proves that hats are still big business: the baseball cap.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: bət wən kæp pruvz ðət hæts ər stɪl bɪg ˈbɪznɪs: ðə ˈbeɪsˈbɔl kæp.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: What began as a practical part of a baseball uniform stepped off the field and onto the streets in the 1960s.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: wət bɪˈgæn ɛz ə ˈpræktɪkəl pɑrt əv ə ˈbeɪsˈbɔl ˈjunəˌfɔrm stɛpt ɔf ðə fild ənd ˈɔntu ðə strits ɪn ðə 1960s*.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: The cap’s cost-effective, adjustable design, along with its easy integration of logos, badges, and trademarks,
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ðə cap’s* cost-effective*, əˈʤəstəbəl dɪˈzaɪn, əˈlɔŋ wɪθ ɪts ˈizi ˌɪnəˈgreɪʃən əv ˈloʊˌgoʊz, ˈbæʤɪz, ənd ˈtreɪdˌmɑrks,

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: has driven its mass commercialization.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: həz ˈdrɪvən ɪts mæs kəˌmərʃəlɪˈzeɪʃən.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: In the United States, some estimate that baseball caps alone make up around 80% of the entire hat market.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: ɪn ðə juˈnaɪtɪd steɪts, səm ˈɛstəˌmeɪt ðət ˈbeɪsˈbɔl kæps əˈloʊn meɪk əp əraʊnd 80 əv ðə ɪnˈtaɪər hæt ˈmɑrkɪt.

-Tw81EQYV_Y.text: But as for the future, who knows what type of hat will capture our hearts— and our heads— next.
-Tw81EQYV_Y.ipa: bət ɛz fər ðə fˈjuʧər, hu noʊz wət taɪp əv hæt wɪl ˈkæpʧər ɑr hearts—*— ənd ɑr heads—*— nɛkst.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: When ultraviolet sunlight hits our skin, it affects each of us a little differently.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: wɪn ˌəltrəˈvaɪəlɪt ˈsənˌlaɪt hɪts ɑr skɪn, ɪt əˈfɛkts iʧ əv ˈjuˈɛs ə ˈlɪtəl ˈdɪfərˈɛntli.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: Depending on skin color, it will take only minutes of exposure to turn one person beetroot-pink,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: dɪˈpɛndɪŋ ɔn skɪn ˈkələr, ɪt wɪl teɪk ˈoʊnli ˈmɪnəts əv ɪkˈspoʊʒər tɪ tərn wən ˈpərsən beetroot-pink*,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: while another requires hours to experience the slightest change. So what's to account for that difference
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: waɪl əˈnəðər rikˈwaɪərz aʊərz tɪ ɪkˈspɪriəns ðə sˈlaɪtəst ʧeɪnʤ. soʊ wəts tɪ əˈkaʊnt fər ðət ˈdɪfərəns

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: and how did our skin come to take on so many different hues to begin with? Whatever the color,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ənd haʊ dɪd ɑr skɪn kəm tɪ teɪk ɔn soʊ ˈmɛni ˈdɪfərənt hjuz tɪ bɪˈgɪn wɪθ? ˌwəˈtɛvər ðə ˈkələr,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: our skin tells an epic tale of human intrepidness and adaptability,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ɑr skɪn tɛlz ən ˈɛpɪk teɪl əv ˈjumən intrepidness* ənd əˌdæptəˈbɪləti,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: revealing its variance to be a function of biology. It all centers around melanin,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: rɪˈvilɪŋ ɪts ˈvɛriəns tɪ bi ə ˈfəŋkʃən əv baɪˈɑləʤi. ɪt ɔl ˈsɛnərz əraʊnd ˈmɛlənɪn,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: the pigment that gives skin and hair its color. This ingredient comes from skin cells called melanocytes
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ðə ˈpɪgmɛnt ðət gɪvz skɪn ənd hɛr ɪts ˈkələr. ðɪs ˌɪnˈgridiənt kəmz frəm skɪn sɛlz kɔld melanocytes*

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: and takes two basic forms. There's eumelanin, which gives rise to a range of brown skin tones,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ənd teɪks tu ˈbeɪsɪk fɔrmz. ðɛrz eumelanin*, wɪʧ gɪvz raɪz tɪ ə reɪnʤ əv braʊn skɪn toʊnz,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: as well as black, brown, and blond hair,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ɛz wɛl ɛz blæk, braʊn, ənd blɑnd hɛr,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: and pheomelanin, which causes the reddish browns of freckles and red hair. But humans weren't always like this.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ənd pheomelanin*, wɪʧ ˈkɔzɪz ðə ˈrɛdɪʃ braʊnz əv ˈfrɛkəlz ənd rɛd hɛr. bət ˈjumənz wərənt ˈɔlˌweɪz laɪk ðɪs.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: Our varying skin tones were formed by an evolutionary process driven by the Sun.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ɑr ˈvɛriɪŋ skɪn toʊnz wər fɔrmd baɪ ən ˌɛvəˈluʃəˌnɛri ˈprɔˌsɛs ˈdrɪvən baɪ ðə sən.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: In began some 50,000 years ago when our ancestors migrated north from Africa and into Europe and Asia.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ɪn bɪˈgæn səm 50,000* jɪrz əˈgoʊ wɪn ɑr ˈænˌsɛstərz ˈmaɪˌgreɪtɪd nɔrθ frəm ˈæfrɪkɑ ənd ˈɪntu ˈjʊrəp ənd ˈeɪʒə.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: These ancient humans lived between the Equator and the Tropic of Capricorn,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ðiz ˈeɪnʧənt ˈjumənz lɪvd bɪtˈwin ðə ɪkˈweɪtər ənd ðə ˈtrɑpɪk əv ˈkæprəkɔrn,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: a region saturated by the Sun's UV-carrying rays. When skin is exposed to UV for long periods of time,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ə ˈriʤən ˈsæʧərˌeɪtɪd baɪ ðə sənz uv-carrying* reɪz. wɪn skɪn ɪz ɪkˈspoʊzd tɪ uv* fər lɔŋ ˈpɪriədz əv taɪm,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: the UV light damages the DNA within our cells, and skin starts to burn. If that damage is severe enough,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ðə uv* laɪt ˈdæmɪʤɪz ðə ˈdiˌɛˈneɪ wɪˈθɪn ɑr sɛlz, ənd skɪn stɑrts tɪ bərn. ɪf ðət ˈdæmɪʤ ɪz səˈvɪr ɪˈnəf,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: the cells mutations can lead to melanoma, a deadly cancer that forms in the skin's melanocytes.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ðə sɛlz mjuˈteɪʃənz kən lɛd tɪ ˌmɛləˈnoʊmə, ə ˈdɛdli ˈkænsər ðət fɔrmz ɪn ðə skɪnz melanocytes*.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: Sunscreen as we know it today didn't exist 50,000 years ago.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ˈsənskrin ɛz wi noʊ ɪt təˈdeɪ ˈdɪdənt ɪgˈzɪst 50,000* jɪrz əˈgoʊ.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: So how did our ancestors cope with this onslaught of UV? The key to survival lay in their own personal sunscreen
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: soʊ haʊ dɪd ɑr ˈænˌsɛstərz koʊp wɪθ ðɪs ˈɔnsˌlɔt əv uv*? ðə ki tɪ sərˈvaɪvəl leɪ ɪn ðɛr oʊn ˈpərsɪnəl ˈsənskrin

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: manufactured beneath the skin: melanin. The type and amount of melanin in your skin
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ˌmænjəˈfækʧərd bɪˈniθ ðə skɪn: ˈmɛlənɪn. ðə taɪp ənd əˈmaʊnt əv ˈmɛlənɪn ɪn jʊr skɪn

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: determines whether you'll be more or less protected from the sun. This comes down to the skin's response as sunlight strikes it.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: dɪˈtərmənz ˈwɛðər jul bi mɔr ər lɛs prəˈtɛktɪd frəm ðə sən. ðɪs kəmz daʊn tɪ ðə skɪnz rɪˈspɑns ɛz ˈsənˌlaɪt straɪks ɪt.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: When it's exposed to UV light, that triggers special light-sensitive receptors called rhodopsin,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: wɪn ɪts ɪkˈspoʊzd tɪ uv* laɪt, ðət ˈtrɪgərz ˈspɛʃəl light-sensitive* rəˈsɛptərz kɔld roʊˈdɑpsən,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: which stimulate the production of melanin to shield cells from damage.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: wɪʧ ˈstɪmjəˌleɪt ðə pərˈdəkʃən əv ˈmɛlənɪn tɪ ʃild sɛlz frəm ˈdæmɪʤ.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: For light-skin people, that extra melanin darkens their skin and produces a tan. Over the course of generations,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: fər light-skin* ˈpipəl, ðət ˈɛkstrə ˈmɛlənɪn darkens* ðɛr skɪn ənd prəˈdusɪz ə tæn. ˈoʊvər ðə kɔrs əv ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃənz,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: humans living at the Sun-saturated latitudes in Africa adapted to have a higher melanin production threshold
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ˈjumənz ˈlɪvɪŋ æt ðə sun-saturated* ˈlætəˌtudz ɪn ˈæfrɪkɑ əˈdæptɪd tɪ hæv ə haɪər ˈmɛlənɪn pərˈdəkʃən θˈrɛˌʃoʊld

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: and more eumelanin, giving skin a darker tone.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ənd mɔr eumelanin*, ˈgɪvɪŋ skɪn ə ˈdɑrkər toʊn.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: This built-in sun shield helped protect them from melanoma, likely making them evolutionarily fitter
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ðɪs ˈbɪlˌtɪn sən ʃild hɛlpt prəˈtɛkt ðɛm frəm ˌmɛləˈnoʊmə, ˈlaɪkli ˈmeɪkɪŋ ðɛm evolutionarily* ˈfɪtər

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: and capable of passing this useful trait on to new generations.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ənd ˈkeɪpəbəl əv ˈpæsɪŋ ðɪs ˈjusfəl treɪt ɔn tɪ nu ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃənz.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: But soon, some of our Sun-adapted ancestors migrated northward out of the tropical zone,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: bət sun, səm əv ɑr sun-adapted* ˈænˌsɛstərz ˈmaɪˌgreɪtɪd ˈnɔrθwərd aʊt əv ðə ˈtrɑpɪkəl zoʊn,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: spreading far and wide across the Earth. The further north they traveled, the less direct sunshine they saw.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ˈsprɛdɪŋ fɑr ənd waɪd əˈkrɔs ðə ərθ. ðə ˈfərðər nɔrθ ðeɪ ˈtrævəld, ðə lɛs dɪˈrɛkt ˈsənˌʃaɪn ðeɪ sɔ.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: This was a problem because although UV light can damage skin, it also has an important parallel benefit.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ðɪs wɑz ə ˈprɑbləm bɪˈkəz ˌɔlˈðoʊ uv* laɪt kən ˈdæmɪʤ skɪn, ɪt ˈɔlsoʊ həz ən ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ˈpɛrəˌlɛl ˈbɛnəfɪt.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: UV helps our bodies produce vitamin D, an ingredient that strengthens bones and lets us absorb vital minerals,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: uv* hɛlps ɑr ˈbɑdiz ˈproʊdus ˈvaɪtəmən di, ən ˌɪnˈgridiənt ðət ˈstrɛŋθənz boʊnz ənd lɛts ˈjuˈɛs əbˈzɔrb ˈvaɪtəl ˈmɪnərəlz,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: like calcium, iron, magnesium, phosphate, and zinc.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: laɪk ˈkælsiəm, aɪərn, mægˈniziəm, ˈfɑsfeɪt, ənd zɪŋk.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: Without it, humans experience serious fatigue and weakened bones that can cause a condition known as rickets.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: wɪˈθaʊt ɪt, ˈjumənz ɪkˈspɪriəns ˈsɪriəs fəˈtig ənd ˈwikənd boʊnz ðət kən kɔz ə kənˈdɪʃən noʊn ɛz ˈrɪkɪts.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: For humans whose dark skin effectively blocked whatever sunlight there was,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: fər ˈjumənz huz dɑrk skɪn ˈifɛktɪvli blɑkt ˌwəˈtɛvər ˈsənˌlaɪt ðɛr wɑz,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: vitamin D deficiency would have posed a serious threat in the north. But some of them happened to produce less melanin.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ˈvaɪtəmən di dɪˈfɪʃənsi wʊd hæv poʊzd ə ˈsɪriəs θrɛt ɪn ðə nɔrθ. bət səm əv ðɛm ˈhæpənd tɪ ˈproʊdus lɛs ˈmɛlənɪn.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: They were exposed to small enough amounts of light that melanoma was less likely,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ðeɪ wər ɪkˈspoʊzd tɪ smɔl ɪˈnəf əˈmaʊnts əv laɪt ðət ˌmɛləˈnoʊmə wɑz lɛs ˈlaɪkli,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: and their lighter skin better absorbed the UV light. So they benefited from vitamin D, developed strong bones,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ənd ðɛr ˈlaɪtər skɪn ˈbɛtər əbˈzɔrbd ðə uv* laɪt. soʊ ðeɪ ˈbɛnəˌfɪtɪd frəm ˈvaɪtəmən di, dɪˈvɛləpt strɔŋ boʊnz,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: and survived well enough to produce healthy offspring. Over many generations of selection,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ənd sərˈvaɪvd wɛl ɪˈnəf tɪ ˈproʊdus ˈhɛlθi ˈɔfˌsprɪŋ. ˈoʊvər ˈmɛni ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃənz əv səˈlɛkʃən,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: skin color in those regions gradually lightened. As a result of our ancestor's adaptability,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: skɪn ˈkələr ɪn ðoʊz ˈriʤənz ˈgræʤuəli ˈlaɪtənd. ɛz ə rɪˈzəlt əv ɑr ˈænˌsɛstərz əˌdæptəˈbɪləti,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: today the planet is full of people with a vast palette of skin colors,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: təˈdeɪ ðə ˈplænət ɪz fʊl əv ˈpipəl wɪθ ə væst ˈpælət əv skɪn ˈkələrz,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: typically, darker eumelanin-rich skin in the hot, sunny band around the Equator,
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ˈtɪpɪkəli, ˈdɑrkər eumelanin-rich* skɪn ɪn ðə hɑt, ˈsəni bænd əraʊnd ðə ɪkˈweɪtər,

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: and increasingly lighter pheomelanin-rich skin shades fanning outwards as the sunshine dwindles.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ənd ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋgli ˈlaɪtər pheomelanin-rich* skɪn ʃeɪdz ˈfænɪŋ ˈaʊtwərdz ɛz ðə ˈsənˌʃaɪn dˈwɪndəlz.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: Therefore, skin color is little more than an adaptive trait for living on a rock that orbits the Sun.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ˈðɛrˌfɔr, skɪn ˈkələr ɪz ˈlɪtəl mɔr ðən ən əˈdæptɪv treɪt fər ˈlɪvɪŋ ɔn ə rɑk ðət ˈɔrbəts ðə sən.

_r4c2NT4naQ.text: It may absorb light, but it certainly does not reflect character.
_r4c2NT4naQ.ipa: ɪt meɪ əbˈzɔrb laɪt, bət ɪt ˈsərtənli dɪz nɑt rɪˈflɛkt ˈkɛrɪktər.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Order, order! Who do we have on the stand today? Alexander... the Great, eh? Well, what’s so great about him?
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˈɔrdər, ˈɔrdər! hu du wi hæv ɔn ðə stænd təˈdeɪ? ˌælɪgˈzændər... ðə greɪt, ɛ? wɛl, what’s* soʊ greɪt əˈbaʊt ɪm?

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Your honor, the better question is, what's not great about him? He was the King of Macedon
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: jʊr ˈɑnər, ðə ˈbɛtər kˈwɛʃən ɪz, wəts nɑt greɪt əˈbaʊt ɪm? hi wɑz ðə kɪŋ əv macedon*

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: and considered a living demigod by Egyptians and Greeks alike.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ənd kənˈsɪdərd ə ˈlɪvɪŋ demigod* baɪ ɪˈʤɪpʃənz ənd griks əˈlaɪk.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: He conquered Persia when it was one of Earth's largest empires. And he was a thoughtful student of Aristotle,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hi ˈkɑŋkərd ˈpərʒə wɪn ɪt wɑz wən əv ərθs ˈlɑrʤəst ˈɛmpaɪərz. ənd hi wɑz ə ˈθɔtfəl ˈstudənt əv ˈɛrəˌstɑtəl,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: creative enough to untie the impossibly tight Gordian knot by cutting it in half.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: kriˈeɪtɪv ɪˈnəf tɪ ənˈtaɪ ðə ˌɪmˈpɑsəbli taɪt ˈgɔrdiən nɑt baɪ ˈkətɪŋ ɪt ɪn hæf.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: The Gordian knot is a myth— just like the countless other stories invented to enhance Alexander’s legend!
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ðə ˈgɔrdiən nɑt ɪz ə myth—*— ʤɪst laɪk ðə ˈkaʊntləs ˈəðər ˈstɔriz ˌɪnˈvɛntɪd tɪ ɛnˈhæns alexander’s* ˈlɛʤənd!

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Calling him “great” just plays into this propaganda and glorifies his ruthless military ambitions,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˈkɔlɪŋ ɪm ““great”*” ʤɪst pleɪz ˈɪntu ðɪs ˌprɑpəˈgændə ənd ˈglɔrəˌfaɪz hɪz ˈruθləs ˈmɪlɪˌtɛri æmˈbɪʃənz,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: such as his unprovoked invasion of Persia.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: səʧ ɛz hɪz ˌənprəˈvoʊkt ˌɪnˈveɪʒən əv ˈpərʒə.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Alexander invaded Persia to free Greeks! A century before his time,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˌælɪgˈzændər ˌɪnˈveɪdɪd ˈpərʒə tɪ fri griks! ə ˈsɛnʧəri ˌbiˈfɔr hɪz taɪm,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Persia had sacked Athens and conquered countless Greek lands.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˈpərʒə hæd sækt ˈæθənz ənd ˈkɑŋkərd ˈkaʊntləs grik lændz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: So after Alexander’s father Philip expanded Macedonian control over much of Greece,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: soʊ ˈæftər alexander’s* ˈfɑðər ˈfɪlɪp ɪkˈspændɪd ˌmæsəˈdoʊnjən kənˈtroʊl ˈoʊvər məʧ əv gris,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: he planned a final campaign to liberate those still under Persian rule.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hi plænd ə ˈfaɪnəl kæmˈpeɪn tɪ ˈlɪˌbərˌeɪt ðoʊz stɪl ˈəndər ˈpərʒən rul.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: “Liberate” is a generous word— those Greeks were just going from one despot to another!
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ““liberate”*” ɪz ə ˈʤɛnərəs word—*— ðoʊz griks wər ʤɪst goʊɪŋ frəm wən ˈdɛspət tɪ əˈnəðər!

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Not that they ever even experienced Philip's leadership, since Alexander had his father assassinated to take power for himself.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: nɑt ðət ðeɪ ˈɛvər ˈivɪn ɪkˈspɪriənst ˈfɪlɪps ˈlidərˌʃɪp, sɪns ˌælɪgˈzændər hæd hɪz ˈfɑðər əˈsæsəˌneɪtəd tɪ teɪk paʊər fər hɪmˈsɛlf.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Objection, Your Honor. We don’t know for sure who killed Philip. Some evidence suggests he was murdered by a humiliated ex-lover.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: əˈbʤɛkʃən, jʊr ˈɑnər. wi don’t* noʊ fər ʃʊr hu kɪld ˈfɪlɪp. səm ˈɛvədəns səˈʤɛsts hi wɑz ˈmərdərd baɪ ə hjuˈmɪliˌeɪtɪd ex-lover*.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Oh, please— that “disgruntled lover” story is ridiculous.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: oʊ, please—*— ðət ““disgruntled* lover”*” ˈstɔri ɪz rɪˈdɪkjələs.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Before his death, Philip had taken a new Macedonian wife to father a new heir.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˌbiˈfɔr hɪz dɛθ, ˈfɪlɪp hæd ˈteɪkən ə nu ˌmæsəˈdoʊnjən waɪf tɪ ˈfɑðər ə nu ɛr.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: So it’s far more likely Alexander had his father murdered to take the throne
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: soʊ it’s* fɑr mɔr ˈlaɪkli ˌælɪgˈzændər hæd hɪz ˈfɑðər ˈmərdərd tɪ teɪk ðə θroʊn

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: and reap the glory from Philip's planned campaign.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ənd rip ðə ˈglɔri frəm ˈfɪlɪps plænd kæmˈpeɪn.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: That's one messy family.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ðæts wən ˈmɛsi ˈfæməli.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Regardless of Philip's fate, Alexander was hardly riding his father's coattails.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: rəˈgɑrdləs əv ˈfɪlɪps feɪt, ˌælɪgˈzændər wɑz ˈhɑrdli ˈraɪdɪŋ hɪz ˈfɑðərz ˈkoʊtˌteɪlz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: His tactical brilliance was essential for defeating Persia.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hɪz ˈtæktɪkəl ˈbrɪljəns wɑz ɛˈsɛnʃəl fər dɪˈfitɪŋ ˈpərʒə.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: For example, at the Battle of Issus, he strategically positioned his troops along the riverbank,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, æt ðə ˈbætəl əv issus*, hi strəˈtiʤɪkli pəˈzɪʃənd hɪz trups əˈlɔŋ ðə ˈrɪvərˌbæŋk,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: forcing his enemies onto a narrow plain that disrupted their formation,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˈfɔrsɪŋ hɪz ˈɛnəmiz ˈɔntu ə ˈnɛroʊ pleɪn ðət dɪsˈrəptɪd ðɛr fɔrˈmeɪʃən,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: limited their maneuverability, and neutralized their numerical advantage.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˈlɪmɪtɪd ðɛr məˌnuvərəˈbɪlɪti, ənd ˈnutrəˌlaɪzd ðɛr nuˈmɛrɪkəl ædˈvæntɪʤ.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: So what if he was a military genius?
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: soʊ wət ɪf hi wɑz ə ˈmɪlɪˌtɛri ˈʤinjəs?

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: His power-hungry conquering spree left a wake of widespread death and ruin.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hɪz power-hungry* ˈkɑŋkərɪŋ spri lɛft ə weɪk əv ˈwaɪdˈsprɛd dɛθ ənd ruɪn.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Actually, his conquests were neither bloody nor destructive!
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˈæˌkʧuəli, hɪz ˈkɑnkˌwɛsts wər ˈniðər ˈblədi nɔr dɪˈstrəktɪv!

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: The Persian governor of Egypt surrendered without a fight.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ðə ˈpərʒən ˈgəvərnər əv ˈiʤɪpt sərˈɛndərd wɪˈθaʊt ə faɪt.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: And after most victories, he usually expanded existing infrastructure.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ənd ˈæftər moʊst ˈvɪktəriz, hi ˈjuʒəwəli ɪkˈspændɪd ɪgˈzɪstɪŋ ˌɪnfrəˈstrəkʧər.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Not in Persepolis! He raised the Persian capital to the ground!
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: nɑt ɪn pərˈsɛpələs! hi reɪzd ðə ˈpərʒən ˈkæpɪtəl tɪ ðə graʊnd!

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: He only did that to maintain Greek support—
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hi ˈoʊnli dɪd ðət tɪ meɪnˈteɪn grik support—*—

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: razing Persepolis was considered payback for what Persia did to Athens.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˈreɪzɪŋ pərˈsɛpələs wɑz kənˈsɪdərd ˈpeɪˌbæk fər wət ˈpərʒə dɪd tɪ ˈæθənz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Besides, Persepolis was the only city he destroyed in over a decade of campaigning. Typically, Alexander founded cities.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˌbiˈsaɪdz, pərˈsɛpələs wɑz ðə ˈoʊnli ˈsɪti hi dɪˈstrɔɪd ɪn ˈoʊvər ə ˈdɛkeɪd əv kæmˈpeɪnɪŋ. ˈtɪpɪkəli, ˌælɪgˈzændər ˈfaʊndɪd ˈsɪtiz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: And befitting his massive ego, he named over 20 of them after himself.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ənd bɪˈfɪtɪŋ hɪz ˈmæsɪv ˈigoʊ, hi neɪmd ˈoʊvər 20 əv ðɛm ˈæftər hɪmˈsɛlf.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: That’s a lot of Alexandrias.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: that’s* ə lɔt əv alexandrias*.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: And it’s a huge part of why his legacy seems so impressive when, in reality, he only conquered one empire.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ənd it’s* ə juʤ pɑrt əv waɪ hɪz ˈlɛgəsi simz soʊ ˌɪmˈprɛsɪv wɪn, ɪn ˌriˈæləˌti, hi ˈoʊnli ˈkɑŋkərd wən ˈɛmpaɪər.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: He just thought he'd conquered the world because he had such a narrow conception of it.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hi ʤɪst θɔt hid ˈkɑŋkərd ðə wərld bɪˈkəz hi hæd səʧ ə ˈnɛroʊ kənˈsɛpʃən əv ɪt.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: He didn’t even know India existed until he stumbled upon it.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hi didn’t* ˈivɪn noʊ ˈɪndiə ɪgˈzɪstəd ənˈtɪl hi ˈstəmbəld əˈpɑn ɪt.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: And when he tried his hand at conquest there,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ənd wɪn hi traɪd hɪz hænd æt ˈkɑŋkwɛst ðɛr,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: his soldiers rebelled and forced him to turn back.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hɪz ˈsoʊlʤərz rɪˈbɛld ənd fɔrst ɪm tɪ tərn bæk.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: It’s true his men mutinied after being pushed too far, but Alexander and his forces had a deep respect for each other.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: it’s* tru hɪz mɛn mˈjutənid ˈæftər biɪŋ pʊʃt tu fɑr, bət ˌælɪgˈzændər ənd hɪz ˈfɔrsɪz hæd ə dip rɪˈspɛkt fər iʧ ˈəðər.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: He fought alongside them in battle, led cavalry charges, and never asked them to do anything he wouldn't do himself.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hi fɔt əˈlɔŋˈsaɪd ðɛm ɪn ˈbætəl, lɛd ˈkævəlri ˈʧɑrʤɪz, ənd ˈnɛvər æst ðɛm tɪ du ˈɛniˌθɪŋ hi ˈwʊdənt du hɪmˈsɛlf.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Part of his greatness was his ability to cultivate talented individuals.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: pɑrt əv hɪz ˈgreɪtnəs wɑz hɪz əˈbɪləˌti tɪ ˈkəltəˌveɪt ˈtæləntɪd ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəlz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: If only he paid similar attention to all his subjects. His attempts at cultural assimilation led to resentment and rebellion
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ɪf ˈoʊnli hi peɪd ˈsɪmələr əˈtɛnʃən tɪ ɔl hɪz ˈsəbʤɪkts. hɪz əˈtɛmpts æt ˈkəlʧərəl əˌsɪməˈleɪʃən lɛd tɪ rɪˈzɛntmənt ənd rɪˈbɛljən

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: from Greeks, Persians, and Macedonians.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: frəm griks, ˈpərʒənz, ənd ˌmæsəˈdoʊniənz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: And he left Macedon in the hands of a regent for almost his entire reign.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ənd hi lɛft macedon* ɪn ðə hænz əv ə ˈriʤənt fər ˈɔlˌmoʊst hɪz ɪnˈtaɪər reɪn.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: That regent was well regarded. And in his defense, Alexander’s diverse subjects all expected their king
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ðət ˈriʤənt wɑz wɛl rɪˈgɑrdɪd. ənd ɪn hɪz dɪˈfɛns, alexander’s* dɪˈvərs ˈsəbʤɪkts ɔl ɪkˈspɛktɪd ðɛr kɪŋ

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: to behave in very different ways. The Macedonians saw him as a hereditary king
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: tɪ bɪˈheɪv ɪn ˈvɛri ˈdɪfərənt weɪz. ðə ˌmæsəˈdoʊniənz sɔ ɪm ɛz ə hərˈɛdəˌtɛri kɪŋ

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: who needed public support to remain in power, so they expected him to respect their wishes.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hu ˈnidɪd ˈpəblɪk səˈpɔrt tɪ rɪˈmeɪn ɪn paʊər, soʊ ðeɪ ɪkˈspɛktɪd ɪm tɪ rɪˈspɛkt ðɛr ˈwɪʃɪz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Since the Greeks refused to be ruled by a king, they thought he should behave as a first among equals.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: sɪns ðə griks rɪfˈjuzd tɪ bi ruld baɪ ə kɪŋ, ðeɪ θɔt hi ʃʊd bɪˈheɪv ɛz ə fərst əˈməŋ ˈikwəlz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: And the Egyptians and Persians believed he should act as a semi-divine absolute monarch!
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ənd ðə ɪˈʤɪpʃənz ənd ˈpərʒənz bɪˈlivd hi ʃʊd ækt ɛz ə semi-divine* ˈæbsəˌlut ˈmɑˌnɑrk!

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: It was an impossible balancing act. And since he died suddenly of an unidentified illness at just 32 years old,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ɪt wɑz ən ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl ˈbælənsɪŋ ækt. ənd sɪns hi daɪd ˈsədənli əv ən ˌənaɪˈdɛntəˌfaɪd ˈɪlnəs æt ʤɪst 32 jɪrz oʊld,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: he simply didn't live long enough for us to judge his governing abilities.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hi ˈsɪmpli ˈdɪdənt lɪv lɔŋ ɪˈnəf fər ˈjuˈɛs tɪ ʤəʤ hɪz ˈgəvərnɪŋ əˈbɪləˌtiz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: I'll be the judge of what we can judge.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: aɪl bi ðə ʤəʤ əv wət wi kən ʤəʤ.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Of course, Your Honor. I just think failing to plan for his own death tells how uninvested he was in ruling.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: əv kɔrs, jʊr ˈɑnər. aɪ ʤɪst θɪŋk ˈfeɪlɪŋ tɪ plæn fər hɪz oʊn dɛθ tɛlz haʊ uninvested* hi wɑz ɪn ˈrulɪŋ.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: On his deathbed, he declared his empire should go “to the strongest,”
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ɔn hɪz ˈdɛθˌbɛd, hi dɪˈklɛrd hɪz ˈɛmpaɪər ʃʊd goʊ ““to* ðə strongest,”*,”

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: setting the stage for decades of destructive power struggles.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˈsɛtɪŋ ðə steɪʤ fər ˈdɛkeɪdz əv dɪˈstrəktɪv paʊər ˈstrəgəlz.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Now you're buying into the legends. Those final words were likely fabricated to suggest Alexander's approval
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: naʊ jʊr baɪɪŋ ˈɪntu ðə ˈlɛʤəndz. ðoʊz ˈfaɪnəl wərdz wər ˈlaɪkli ˈfæbrɪˌkeɪtɪd tɪ səˈʤɛst ˌælɪgˈzændərz əˈpruvəl

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: for the conflicts that followed. But you can hardly judge a man by those
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: fər ðə ˈkɑnflɪkts ðət ˈfɑloʊd. bət ju kən ˈhɑrdli ʤəʤ ə mæn baɪ ðoʊz

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: who invoke his name to justify their own ambitions. If we want to talk about legacy,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: hu ˌɪnˈvoʊk hɪz neɪm tɪ ˈʤəstəˌfaɪ ðɛr oʊn æmˈbɪʃənz. ɪf wi wɔnt tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt ˈlɛgəsi,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: let’s give him credit for ushering in the Hellenistic Age,
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: let’s* gɪv ɪm ˈkrɛdɪt fər ˈəʃərɪŋ ɪn ðə ˌhɛləˈnɪstɪk eɪʤ,

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: where Greek cultural influence spread across Europe and Asia.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: wɛr grik ˈkəlʧərəl ˈɪnfluəns sprɛd əˈkrɔs ˈjʊrəp ənd ˈeɪʒə.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Like it or not, Alexander fundamentally transformed the world. Fair enough.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: laɪk ɪt ər nɑt, ˌælɪgˈzændər ˌfəndəˈmɛnəli trænsˈfɔrmd ðə wərld. fɛr ɪˈnəf.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: But it’s hard to judge anyone clearly when they cast a shadow this, well, great.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: bət it’s* hɑrd tɪ ʤəʤ ˈɛniˌwən ˈklɪrli wɪn ðeɪ kæst ə ˈʃæˌdoʊ ðɪs, wɛl, greɪt.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: Whether he was a ruthless conqueror or an embattled leader, Alexander the Great is undeniably a legend.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: ˈwɛðər hi wɑz ə ˈruθləs ˈkɑŋkərər ər ən ɛmˈbætəld ˈlidər, ˌælɪgˈzændər ðə greɪt ɪz ˌəndɪˈnaɪəbli ə ˈlɛʤənd.

bVjKL1zd-vQ.text: But trying to separate fact from fiction is all part of putting history on trial.
bVjKL1zd-vQ.ipa: bət traɪɪŋ tɪ ˈsɛpərˌeɪt fækt frəm ˈfɪkʃən ɪz ɔl pɑrt əv ˈpʊtɪŋ ˈhɪstəri ɔn traɪəl.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: You probably already know everything is made up of little tiny things called atoms
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ju ˈprɑbəˌbli ɔˈrɛdi noʊ ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ɪz meɪd əp əv ˈlɪtəl ˈtaɪni θɪŋz kɔld ˈætəmz

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: or even that each atom is made up of even smaller particles called protons, neutrons and electrons.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ər ˈivɪn ðət iʧ ˈætəm ɪz meɪd əp əv ˈivɪn sˈmɔlər ˈpɑrtɪkəlz kɔld ˈproʊˌtɑnz, ˈnuˌtrɑnz ənd ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: And you've probably heard that atoms are small. But I bet you haven't ever thought about how small atoms really are.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ənd juv ˈprɑbəˌbli hərd ðət ˈætəmz ər smɔl. bət aɪ bɛt ju ˈhævənt ˈɛvər θɔt əˈbaʊt haʊ smɔl ˈætəmz ˈrɪli ər.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Well, the answer is that they are really, really small.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: wɛl, ðə ˈænsər ɪz ðət ðeɪ ər ˈrɪli, ˈrɪli smɔl.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: So you ask, just how small are atoms? To understand this, let's ask this question:
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: soʊ ju æsk, ʤɪst haʊ smɔl ər ˈætəmz? tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd ðɪs, lɛts æsk ðɪs kˈwɛʃən:

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: How many atoms are in a grapefruit?
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: haʊ ˈmɛni ˈætəmz ər ɪn ə ˈgreɪpˌfrut?

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Well, let's assume that the grapefruit is made up of only nitrogen atoms,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: wɛl, lɛts əˈsum ðət ðə ˈgreɪpˌfrut ɪz meɪd əp əv ˈoʊnli ˈnaɪtrəʤən ˈætəmz,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: which isn't at all true, but there are nitrogen atoms in a grapefruit.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: wɪʧ ˈɪzənt æt ɔl tru, bət ðɛr ər ˈnaɪtrəʤən ˈætəmz ɪn ə ˈgreɪpˌfrut.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: To help you visualize this, let's blow up each of the atoms to the size of a blueberry.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: tɪ hɛlp ju ˈvɪʒwəˌlaɪz ðɪs, lɛts bloʊ əp iʧ əv ðə ˈætəmz tɪ ðə saɪz əv ə ˈbluˌbɛri.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: And then how big would the grapefruit have to be?
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ənd ðɛn haʊ bɪg wʊd ðə ˈgreɪpˌfrut hæv tɪ bi?

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: It would have to be the same size of -- well, actually, the Earth. That's crazy!
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ɪt wʊd hæv tɪ bi ðə seɪm saɪz əv  wɛl, ˈæˌkʧuəli, ðə ərθ. ðæts ˈkreɪzi!

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: You mean to say that if I filled the Earth with blueberries,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ju min tɪ seɪ ðət ɪf aɪ fɪld ðə ərθ wɪθ ˈbluˌbɛriz,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: I would have the same number of nitrogen atoms as a grapefruit? That's right!
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: aɪ wʊd hæv ðə seɪm ˈnəmbər əv ˈnaɪtrəʤən ˈætəmz ɛz ə ˈgreɪpˌfrut? ðæts raɪt!

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: So how big is the atom?
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: soʊ haʊ bɪg ɪz ðə ˈætəm?

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Well, it's really, really small!
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: wɛl, ɪts ˈrɪli, ˈrɪli smɔl!

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: And you know what? It gets even more crazy.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ənd ju noʊ wət? ɪt gɪts ˈivɪn mɔr ˈkreɪzi.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Let's now look inside of each atom -- and thus the blueberry, right? -- What do you see there?
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: lɛts naʊ lʊk ˌɪnˈsaɪd əv iʧ ˈætəm  ənd ðəs ðə ˈbluˌbɛri, raɪt?  wət du ju si ðɛr?

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: In the center of the atom is something called the nucleus, which contains protons and neutrons,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ɪn ðə ˈsɛnər əv ðə ˈætəm ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ kɔld ðə ˈnukliəs, wɪʧ kənˈteɪnz ˈproʊˌtɑnz ənd ˈnuˌtrɑnz,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: and on the outside, you'd see electrons. So how big is the nucleus?
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ənd ɔn ðə ˈaʊtˈsaɪd, jʊd si ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz. soʊ haʊ bɪg ɪz ðə ˈnukliəs?

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: If atoms are like blueberries in the Earth, how big would the nucleus be?
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ɪf ˈætəmz ər laɪk ˈbluˌbɛriz ɪn ðə ərθ, haʊ bɪg wʊd ðə ˈnukliəs bi?

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: You might remember the old pictures of the atom from science class,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ju maɪt rɪˈmɛmbər ðə oʊld ˈpɪkʧərz əv ðə ˈætəm frəm saɪəns klæs,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: where you saw this tiny dot on the page with an arrow pointing to the nucleus.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: wɛr ju sɔ ðɪs ˈtaɪni dɑt ɔn ðə peɪʤ wɪθ ən ˈɛroʊ ˈpɔɪntɪŋ tɪ ðə ˈnukliəs.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Well, those pictures, they're not drawn to scale, so they're kind of wrong. So how big is the nucleus?
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: wɛl, ðoʊz ˈpɪkʧərz, ðɛr nɑt drɔn tɪ skeɪl, soʊ ðɛr kaɪnd əv rɔŋ. soʊ haʊ bɪg ɪz ðə ˈnukliəs?

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: So if you popped open the blueberry and were searching for the nucleus ...
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: soʊ ɪf ju pɑpt ˈoʊpən ðə ˈbluˌbɛri ənd wər ˈsərʧɪŋ fər ðə ˈnukliəs

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: You know what? It would be invisible. It's too small to see!
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ju noʊ wət? ɪt wʊd bi ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl. ɪts tu smɔl tɪ si!

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: OK. Let's blow up the atom -- the blueberry -- to the size of a house.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ. lɛts bloʊ əp ðə ˈætəm  ðə ˈbluˌbɛri  tɪ ðə saɪz əv ə haʊs.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: So imagine a ball that is as tall as a two-story house.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: soʊ ˌɪˈmæʤən ə bɔl ðət ɪz ɛz tɔl ɛz ə two-story* haʊs.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Let's look for the nucleus in the center of the atom. And do you know what? It would just barely be visible.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: lɛts lʊk fər ðə ˈnukliəs ɪn ðə ˈsɛnər əv ðə ˈætəm. ənd du ju noʊ wət? ɪt wʊd ʤɪst ˈbɛrli bi ˈvɪzəbəl.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: So to get our minds wrapped around how big the nucleus is,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: soʊ tɪ gɪt ɑr maɪndz ræpt əraʊnd haʊ bɪg ðə ˈnukliəs ɪz,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: we need to blow up the blueberry, up to the size of a football stadium.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: wi nid tɪ bloʊ əp ðə ˈbluˌbɛri, əp tɪ ðə saɪz əv ə ˈfʊtˌbɔl ˈsteɪdiəm.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: So imagine a ball the size of a football stadium, and right smack dab in the center of the atom,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: soʊ ˌɪˈmæʤən ə bɔl ðə saɪz əv ə ˈfʊtˌbɔl ˈsteɪdiəm, ənd raɪt smæk dæb ɪn ðə ˈsɛnər əv ðə ˈætəm,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: you would find the nucleus, and you could see it!
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ju wʊd faɪnd ðə ˈnukliəs, ənd ju kʊd si ɪt!

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: And it would be the size of a small marble.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ənd ɪt wʊd bi ðə saɪz əv ə smɔl ˈmɑrbəl.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: And there's more, if I haven't blown your mind by now. Let's consider the atom some more.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ənd ðɛrz mɔr, ɪf aɪ ˈhævənt bloʊn jʊr maɪnd baɪ naʊ. lɛts kənˈsɪdər ðə ˈætəm səm mɔr.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: It contains protons, neutrons and electrons. The protons and neutrons live inside of the nucleus,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ɪt kənˈteɪnz ˈproʊˌtɑnz, ˈnuˌtrɑnz ənd ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz. ðə ˈproʊˌtɑnz ənd ˈnuˌtrɑnz lɪv ˌɪnˈsaɪd əv ðə ˈnukliəs,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: and contain almost all of the mass of the atom. Way on the edge are the electrons.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ənd kənˈteɪn ˈɔlˌmoʊst ɔl əv ðə mæs əv ðə ˈætəm. weɪ ɔn ðə ɛʤ ər ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: So if an atom is like a ball the size of a football stadium,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: soʊ ɪf ən ˈætəm ɪz laɪk ə bɔl ðə saɪz əv ə ˈfʊtˌbɔl ˈsteɪdiəm,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: with the nucleus in the center, and the electrons on the edge, what is in between the nucleus and the electrons?
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: wɪθ ðə ˈnukliəs ɪn ðə ˈsɛnər, ənd ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz ɔn ðə ɛʤ, wət ɪz ɪn bɪtˈwin ðə ˈnukliəs ənd ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz?

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Surprisingly, the answer is empty space.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: səˈpraɪzɪŋli, ðə ˈænsər ɪz ˈɛmti speɪs.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: (Wind noise) That's right. Empty!
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: (wɪnd nɔɪz) ðæts raɪt. ˈɛmti!

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Between the nucleus and the electrons, there are vast regions of empty space. Now, technically there are some electromagnetic fields,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: bɪtˈwin ðə ˈnukliəs ənd ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz, ðɛr ər væst ˈriʤənz əv ˈɛmti speɪs. naʊ, ˈtɛknɪkəli ðɛr ər səm ˌɪˌlɛktroʊmægˈnɛtɪk fildz,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: but in terms of stuff, matter, it is empty. Remember this vast region of empty space is inside the blueberry,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: bət ɪn tərmz əv stəf, ˈmætər, ɪt ɪz ˈɛmti. rɪˈmɛmbər ðɪs væst ˈriʤən əv ˈɛmti speɪs ɪz ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə ˈbluˌbɛri,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: which is inside the Earth, which really are the atoms in the grapefruit.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: wɪʧ ɪz ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə ərθ, wɪʧ ˈrɪli ər ðə ˈætəmz ɪn ðə ˈgreɪpˌfrut.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: OK, one more thing, if I can even get more bizarre.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ, wən mɔr θɪŋ, ɪf aɪ kən ˈivɪn gɪt mɔr bɪˈzɑr.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Since virtually all the mass of an atom is in the nucleus --
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: sɪns ˈvərʧuəli ɔl ðə mæs əv ən ˈætəm ɪz ɪn ðə ˈnukliəs

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: now, there is some amount of mass in the electrons, but most of it is in the nucleus --
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: naʊ, ðɛr ɪz səm əˈmaʊnt əv mæs ɪn ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz, bət moʊst əv ɪt ɪz ɪn ðə ˈnukliəs

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: how dense is the nucleus? Well, the answer is crazy. The density of a typical nucleus
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: haʊ dɛns ɪz ðə ˈnukliəs? wɛl, ðə ˈænsər ɪz ˈkreɪzi. ðə ˈdɛnsɪti əv ə ˈtɪpɪkəl ˈnukliəs

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: is four times 10 to the 17th kilograms per meter cubed.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ɪz fɔr taɪmz 10 tɪ ðə 17th* ˈkɪləˌgræmz pər ˈmitər kjubd.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: But that's hard to visualize. OK, I'll put it in English units. 2.5 times 10 to the 16th pounds per cubic feet.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: bət ðæts hɑrd tɪ ˈvɪʒwəˌlaɪz. ˈoʊˈkeɪ, aɪl pʊt ɪt ɪn ˈɪŋlɪʃ ˈjunɪts. 2.5* taɪmz 10 tɪ ðə 16th* paʊnz pər ˈkjubɪk fit.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: OK, that's still kind of hard to figure. OK, here's what I want you to do.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ˈoʊˈkeɪ, ðæts stɪl kaɪnd əv hɑrd tɪ ˈfɪgjər. ˈoʊˈkeɪ, hɪrz wət aɪ wɔnt ju tɪ du.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Make a box that is one foot by one foot by one foot.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: meɪk ə bɑks ðət ɪz wən fʊt baɪ wən fʊt baɪ wən fʊt.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Now let's go and grab all of the nuclei from a typical car. Now, cars on average weigh two tons.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: naʊ lɛts goʊ ənd græb ɔl əv ðə ˈnukliˌaɪ frəm ə ˈtɪpɪkəl kɑr. naʊ, kɑz ɔn ˈævərɪʤ weɪ tu tənz.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: How many cars' nuclei would you have to put into the box
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: haʊ ˈmɛni kɑz' ˈnukliˌaɪ wʊd ju hæv tɪ pʊt ˈɪntu ðə bɑks

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: to have your one-foot-box have the same density of the nucleus? Is it one car? Two? How about 100?
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: tɪ hæv jʊr one-foot-box* hæv ðə seɪm ˈdɛnsɪti əv ðə ˈnukliəs? ɪz ɪt wən kɑr? tu? haʊ əˈbaʊt 100

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Nope, nope and nope.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: noʊp, noʊp ənd noʊp.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: The answer is much bigger.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ðə ˈænsər ɪz məʧ ˈbɪgər.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: It is 6.2 billion.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ɪt ɪz 6.2* ˈbɪljən.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: That is almost equal to the number of people in the Earth.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ðət ɪz ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˈikwəl tɪ ðə ˈnəmbər əv ˈpipəl ɪn ðə ərθ.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: So if everyone in the Earth owned their own car -- and they don't -- (Cars honking)
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: soʊ ɪf ˈɛvriˌwən ɪn ðə ərθ oʊnd ðɛr oʊn kɑr  ənd ðeɪ doʊnt  (kɑz ˈhɔŋkɪŋ)

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: and we put all of those cars into your box ...
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ənd wi pʊt ɔl əv ðoʊz kɑz ˈɪntu jʊr bɑks

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: That would be about the density of a nucleus.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ðət wʊd bi əˈbaʊt ðə ˈdɛnsɪti əv ə ˈnukliəs.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: So I'm saying that if you took every car in the world and put it into your one-foot box,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: soʊ əm seɪɪŋ ðət ɪf ju tʊk ˈɛvəri kɑr ɪn ðə wərld ənd pʊt ɪt ˈɪntu jʊr one-foot* bɑks,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: you would have the density of one nucleus. OK, let's review. The atom is really, really, really small.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ju wʊd hæv ðə ˈdɛnsɪti əv wən ˈnukliəs. ˈoʊˈkeɪ, lɛts ˌrivˈju. ðə ˈætəm ɪz ˈrɪli, ˈrɪli, ˈrɪli smɔl.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: Think atoms in a grapefruit like blueberries in the Earth.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: θɪŋk ˈætəmz ɪn ə ˈgreɪpˌfrut laɪk ˈbluˌbɛriz ɪn ðə ərθ.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: The nucleus is crazy small. Now look inside the blueberry, and blow it up to the size of a football stadium,
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ðə ˈnukliəs ɪz ˈkreɪzi smɔl. naʊ lʊk ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə ˈbluˌbɛri, ənd bloʊ ɪt əp tɪ ðə saɪz əv ə ˈfʊtˌbɔl ˈsteɪdiəm,

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: and now the nucleus is a marble in the middle.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ənd naʊ ðə ˈnukliəs ɪz ə ˈmɑrbəl ɪn ðə ˈmɪdəl.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: The atom is made up of vast regions of empty space.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ðə ˈætəm ɪz meɪd əp əv væst ˈriʤənz əv ˈɛmti speɪs.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: That's weird. The nucleus has a crazy-high density. Think of putting all those cars in your one-foot box.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: ðæts wɪrd. ðə ˈnukliəs həz ə crazy-high* ˈdɛnsɪti. θɪŋk əv ˈpʊtɪŋ ɔl ðoʊz kɑz ɪn jʊr one-foot* bɑks.

yQP4UJhNn0I.text: I think I'm tired.
yQP4UJhNn0I.ipa: aɪ θɪŋk əm taɪərd.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: In the summer of 1997, NASA's Pathfinder spacecraft landed on the surface of Mars,
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ɪn ðə ˈsəmər əv 1997 ˈnæsəz ˈpæθˌfaɪndər ˈspeɪˌskræft ˈlændɪd ɔn ðə ˈsərfəs əv mɑrz,

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: and began transmitting incredible, iconic images back to Earth. But several days in, something went terribly wrong.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ənd bɪˈgæn trænsˈmɪtɪŋ ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbəl, ˌaɪˈkɑnɪk ˈɪmɪʤɪz bæk tɪ ərθ. bət ˈsɛvərəl deɪz ɪn, ˈsəmθɪŋ wɛnt ˈtɛrəbli rɔŋ.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: The transmissions stopped. Pathfinder was, in effect, procrastinating:
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðə trænzˈmɪʃənz stɑpt. ˈpæθˌfaɪndər wɑz, ɪn ˈifɛkt, prəˈkræstəˌneɪtɪŋ:
iDbdXTMnOmE.vietnameseText: Các đường truyền đã dừng lại. Pathfinder, trên thực tế, đang trì hoãn:

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: keeping itself fully occupied but failing to do its most important work. What was going on?
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ˈkipɪŋ ˌɪtˈsɛlf ˈfʊli ˈɑkjəˌpaɪd bət ˈfeɪlɪŋ tɪ du ɪts moʊst ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt wərk. wət wɑz goʊɪŋ ɔn?

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: There was a bug, it turned out, in its scheduler. Every operating system has something called the scheduler
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðɛr wɑz ə bəg, ɪt tərnd aʊt, ɪn ɪts ˈskɛʤʊlər. ˈɛvəri ˈɔpərˌeɪtɪŋ ˈsɪstəm həz ˈsəmθɪŋ kɔld ðə ˈskɛʤʊlər

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: that tells the CPU how long to work on each task before switching, and what to switch to.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðət tɛlz ðə cpu* haʊ lɔŋ tɪ wərk ɔn iʧ tæsk ˌbiˈfɔr sˈwɪʧɪŋ, ənd wət tɪ swɪʧ tɪ.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Done right, computers move so fluidly between their various responsibilities, they give the illusion of doing everything simultaneously.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: dən raɪt, kəmˈpjutərz muv soʊ fluidly* bɪtˈwin ðɛr ˈvɛriəs riˌspɑnsəˈbɪlətiz, ðeɪ gɪv ðə ˌɪˈluʒən əv duɪŋ ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ˌsaɪməlˈteɪniəsli.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: But we all know what happens when things go wrong.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: bət wi ɔl noʊ wət ˈhæpənz wɪn θɪŋz goʊ rɔŋ.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: This should give us, if nothing else, some measure of consolation. Even computers get overwhelmed sometimes.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðɪs ʃʊd gɪv ˈjuˈɛs, ɪf ˈnəθɪŋ ɛls, səm ˈmɛʒər əv ˌkɑnsəˈleɪʃən. ˈivɪn kəmˈpjutərz gɪt ˌoʊvərˈwɛlmd ˈsəmˌtaɪmz.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Maybe learning about the computer science of scheduling can give us some ideas about our own human struggles with time.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ˈmeɪbi ˈlərnɪŋ əˈbaʊt ðə kəmˈpjutər saɪəns əv ˈskɛʤʊlɪŋ kən gɪv ˈjuˈɛs səm aɪˈdiəz əˈbaʊt ɑr oʊn ˈjumən ˈstrəgəlz wɪθ taɪm.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: One of the first insights is that all the time you spend prioritizing your work is time you aren't spending doing it.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: wən əv ðə fərst ˈɪnˌsaɪts ɪz ðət ɔl ðə taɪm ju spɛnd praɪˈɔrəˌtaɪzɪŋ jʊr wərk ɪz taɪm ju ˈɑrənt ˈspɛndɪŋ duɪŋ ɪt.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: For instance, let's say when you check your inbox, you scan all the messages, choosing which is the most important.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: fər ˈɪnstəns, lɛts seɪ wɪn ju ʧɛk jʊr inbox*, ju skæn ɔl ðə ˈmɛsɪʤɪz, ˈʧuzɪŋ wɪʧ ɪz ðə moʊst ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Once you've dealt with that one, you repeat. Seems sensible, but there's a problem here.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: wəns juv dɛlt wɪθ ðət wən, ju rɪˈpit. simz ˈsɛnsəbəl, bət ðɛrz ə ˈprɑbləm hir.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: This is what's known as a quadratic-time algorithm.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðɪs ɪz wəts noʊn ɛz ə quadratic-time* ˈælgərˌɪðəm.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: With an inbox that's twice as full, these passes will take twice as long
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: wɪθ ən inbox* ðæts twaɪs ɛz fʊl, ðiz ˈpæsɪz wɪl teɪk twaɪs ɛz lɔŋ

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: and you'll need to do twice as many of them! This means four times the work.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ənd jul nid tɪ du twaɪs ɛz ˈmɛni əv ðɛm! ðɪs minz fɔr taɪmz ðə wərk.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: The programmers of the operating system Linux encountered a similar problem in 2003.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðə ˈproʊˌgræmərz əv ðə ˈɔpərˌeɪtɪŋ ˈsɪstəm ˈlɪnəks ɪnˈkaʊnərd ə ˈsɪmələr ˈprɑbləm ɪn 2003

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Linux would rank every single one of its tasks in order of importance,
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ˈlɪnəks wʊd ræŋk ˈɛvəri ˈsɪŋgəl wən əv ɪts tæsks ɪn ˈɔrdər əv ˌɪmˈpɔrtəns,

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: and sometimes spent more time ranking tasks than doing them.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ənd ˈsəmˌtaɪmz spɛnt mɔr taɪm ˈræŋkɪŋ tæsks ðən duɪŋ ðɛm.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: The programmers’ counterintuitive solution was to replace this full ranking with a limited number of priority “buckets.”
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðə programmers’*’ counterintuitive* səˈluʃən wɑz tɪ ˌriˈpleɪs ðɪs fʊl ˈræŋkɪŋ wɪθ ə ˈlɪmɪtɪd ˈnəmbər əv praɪˈɔrəti ““buckets.”*.”

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: The system was less precise about what to do next
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðə ˈsɪstəm wɑz lɛs prɪˈsaɪs əˈbaʊt wət tɪ du nɛkst

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: but more than made up for it by spending more time making progress.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: bət mɔr ðən meɪd əp fər ɪt baɪ ˈspɛndɪŋ mɔr taɪm ˈmeɪkɪŋ ˈprɑˌgrɛs.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: So with your emails, insisting on always doing the very most important thing first could lead to a meltdown.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: soʊ wɪθ jʊr iˈmeɪlz, ˌɪnˈsɪstɪŋ ɔn ˈɔlˌweɪz duɪŋ ðə ˈvɛri moʊst ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt θɪŋ fərst kʊd lɛd tɪ ə ˈmɛltˌdaʊn.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Waking up to an inbox three times fuller than normal could take nine times longer to clear.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ˈweɪkɪŋ əp tɪ ən inbox* θri taɪmz ˈfʊlər ðən ˈnɔrməl kʊd teɪk naɪn taɪmz ˈlɔŋgər tɪ klɪr.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: You’d be better off replying in chronological order, or even at random!
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: you’d* bi ˈbɛtər ɔf rɪˈplaɪɪŋ ɪn ˌkrɑnəˈlɑʤɪkəl ˈɔrdər, ər ˈivɪn æt ˈrændəm!

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Surprisingly, sometimes giving up on doing things in the perfect order may be the key to getting them done.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: səˈpraɪzɪŋli, ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ˈgɪvɪŋ əp ɔn duɪŋ θɪŋz ɪn ðə ˈpərˌfɪkt ˈɔrdər meɪ bi ðə ki tɪ ˈgɪtɪŋ ðɛm dən.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Another insight that emerges from computer scheduling has to do with one of the most prevalent features of modern life: interruptions.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: əˈnəðər ˈɪnˌsaɪt ðət ˈimərʤɪz frəm kəmˈpjutər ˈskɛʤʊlɪŋ həz tɪ du wɪθ wən əv ðə moʊst ˈprɛvələnt ˈfiʧərz əv ˈmɑdərn laɪf: ˌɪntərˈəpʃənz.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: When a computer goes from one task to another, it has to do what's called a context switch,
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: wɪn ə kəmˈpjutər goʊz frəm wən tæsk tɪ əˈnəðər, ɪt həz tɪ du wəts kɔld ə ˈkɑntɛkst swɪʧ,

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: bookmarking its place in one task, moving old data out of its memory and new data in.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: bookmarking* ɪts pleɪs ɪn wən tæsk, ˈmuvɪŋ oʊld ˈdætə aʊt əv ɪts ˈmɛməri ənd nu ˈdætə ɪn.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Each of these actions comes at a cost. The insight here is that there’s a fundamental tradeoff
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: iʧ əv ðiz ˈækʃənz kəmz æt ə kɔst. ðə ˈɪnˌsaɪt hir ɪz ðət there’s* ə ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl ˈtreɪˌdɔf

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: between productivity and responsiveness. Getting serious work done means minimizing context switches.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: bɪtˈwin ˌproʊdəkˈtɪvɪti ənd rɪˈspɑnsɪvnəs. ˈgɪtɪŋ ˈsɪriəs wərk dən minz ˈmɪnəˌmaɪzɪŋ ˈkɑntɛkst sˈwɪʧɪz.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: But being responsive means reacting anytime something comes up.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: bət biɪŋ rɪˈspɑnsɪv minz riˈæktɪŋ ˈɛniˌtaɪm ˈsəmθɪŋ kəmz əp.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: These two principles are fundamentally in tension. Recognizing this tension allows us
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðiz tu ˈprɪnsəpəlz ər ˌfəndəˈmɛnəli ɪn ˈtɛnʃən. ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪzɪŋ ðɪs ˈtɛnʃən əˈlaʊz ˈjuˈɛs

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: to decide where we want to strike that balance. The obvious solution is to minimize interruptions.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: tɪ ˌdɪˈsaɪd wɛr wi wɔnt tɪ straɪk ðət ˈbæləns. ðə ˈɑbviəs səˈluʃən ɪz tɪ ˈmɪnəˌmaɪz ˌɪntərˈəpʃənz.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: The less obvious one is to group them. If no notification or email requires a response
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðə lɛs ˈɑbviəs wən ɪz tɪ grup ðɛm. ɪf noʊ ˌnoʊtəfəˈkeɪʃən ər iˈmeɪl rikˈwaɪərz ə rɪˈspɑns

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: more urgently than once an hour, say, then that’s exactly how often you should check them. No more.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: mɔr ˈərʤəntli ðən wəns ən aʊər, seɪ, ðɛn that’s* ɪgˈzæktli haʊ ˈɔfən ju ʃʊd ʧɛk ðɛm. noʊ mɔr.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: In computer science, this idea goes by the name of interrupt coalescing.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ɪn kəmˈpjutər saɪəns, ðɪs aɪˈdiə goʊz baɪ ðə neɪm əv ˌɪntərˈəpt ˌkoʊəˈlɛsɪŋ.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Rather than dealing with things as they come up – Oh, the mouse was moved? A key was pressed?
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ˈrəðər ðən ˈdilɪŋ wɪθ θɪŋz ɛz ðeɪ kəm əp –* oʊ, ðə maʊs wɑz muvd? ə ki wɑz prɛst?

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: More of that file downloaded? – the system groups these interruptions together based on how long they can afford to wait.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: mɔr əv ðət faɪl ˈdaʊnˌloʊdɪd? –* ðə ˈsɪstəm grups ðiz ˌɪntərˈəpʃənz təˈgɛðər beɪst ɔn haʊ lɔŋ ðeɪ kən əˈfɔrd tɪ weɪt.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: In 2013, interrupt coalescing triggered a massive improvement in laptop battery life.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ɪn 2013 ˌɪntərˈəpt ˌkoʊəˈlɛsɪŋ ˈtrɪgərd ə ˈmæsɪv ˌɪmˈpruvmənt ɪn ˈlæpˌtɑp ˈbætəri laɪf.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: This is because deferring interruptions lets a system check everything at once,
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðɪs ɪz bɪˈkəz dɪˈfərɪŋ ˌɪntərˈəpʃənz lɛts ə ˈsɪstəm ʧɛk ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ æt wəns,

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: then quickly re-enter a low-power state. As with computers, so it is with us.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ðɛn kˈwɪkli re-enter* ə low-power* steɪt. ɛz wɪθ kəmˈpjutərz, soʊ ɪt ɪz wɪθ ˈjuˈɛs.

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: Perhaps adopting a similar approach might allow us users to reclaim our own attention,
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: pərˈhæps əˈdɑptɪŋ ə ˈsɪmələr əˈproʊʧ maɪt əˈlaʊ ˈjuˈɛs ˈjuzərz tɪ riˈkleɪm ɑr oʊn əˈtɛnʃən,

iDbdXTMnOmE.text: and give us back one of the things that feels so rare in modern life: rest.
iDbdXTMnOmE.ipa: ənd gɪv ˈjuˈɛs bæk wən əv ðə θɪŋz ðət filz soʊ rɛr ɪn ˈmɑdərn laɪf: rɛst.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: When I was in fourth grade, my teacher said to us one day:
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: wɪn aɪ wɑz ɪn fɔrθ greɪd, maɪ ˈtiʧər sɛd tɪ ˈjuˈɛs wən deɪ:

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: "There are as many even numbers as there are numbers."
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: "ðɛr ər ɛz ˈmɛni ˈivɪn ˈnəmbərz ɛz ðɛr ər ˈnəmbərz."

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: "Really?", I thought. Well, yeah, there are infinitely many of both, so I suppose there are the same number of them.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: "ˈrɪli?", aɪ θɔt. wɛl, jæ, ðɛr ər ˈɪnfənətli ˈmɛni əv boʊθ, soʊ aɪ səˈpoʊz ðɛr ər ðə seɪm ˈnəmbər əv ðɛm.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: But even numbers are only part of the whole numbers, all the odd numbers are left over,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: bət ˈivɪn ˈnəmbərz ər ˈoʊnli pɑrt əv ðə hoʊl ˈnəmbərz, ɔl ðə ɑd ˈnəmbərz ər lɛft ˈoʊvər,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: so there's got to be more whole numbers than even numbers, right? To see what my teacher was getting at,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: soʊ ðɛrz gɑt tɪ bi mɔr hoʊl ˈnəmbərz ðən ˈivɪn ˈnəmbərz, raɪt? tɪ si wət maɪ ˈtiʧər wɑz ˈgɪtɪŋ æt,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: let's first think about what it means for two sets to be the same size.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: lɛts fərst θɪŋk əˈbaʊt wət ɪt minz fər tu sɛts tɪ bi ðə seɪm saɪz.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: What do I mean when I say I have the same number of fingers
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: wət du aɪ min wɪn aɪ seɪ aɪ hæv ðə seɪm ˈnəmbər əv ˈfɪŋgərz

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: on my right hand as I do on left hand?
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɔn maɪ raɪt hænd ɛz aɪ du ɔn lɛft hænd?

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Of course, I have five fingers on each, but it's actually simpler than that.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: əv kɔrs, aɪ hæv faɪv ˈfɪŋgərz ɔn iʧ, bət ɪts ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈsɪmpələr ðən ðət.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: I don't have to count, I only need to see that I can match them up, one to one.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: aɪ doʊnt hæv tɪ kaʊnt, aɪ ˈoʊnli nid tɪ si ðət aɪ kən mæʧ ðɛm əp, wən tɪ wən.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: In fact, we think that some ancient people who spoke languages that didn't have words for numbers greater than three
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɪn fækt, wi θɪŋk ðət səm ˈeɪnʧənt ˈpipəl hu spoʊk ˈlæŋgwɪʤɪz ðət ˈdɪdənt hæv wərdz fər ˈnəmbərz ˈgreɪtər ðən θri

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: used this sort of magic. For instance, if you let your sheep out of a pen to graze,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: juzd ðɪs sɔrt əv ˈmæʤɪk. fər ˈɪnstəns, ɪf ju lɛt jʊr ʃip aʊt əv ə pɛn tɪ greɪz,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: you can keep track of how many went out by setting aside a stone for each one,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ju kən kip træk əv haʊ ˈmɛni wɛnt aʊt baɪ ˈsɛtɪŋ əˈsaɪd ə stoʊn fər iʧ wən,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: and putting those stones back one by one when the sheep return, so you know if any are missing without really counting.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ənd ˈpʊtɪŋ ðoʊz stoʊnz bæk wən baɪ wən wɪn ðə ʃip rɪˈtərn, soʊ ju noʊ ɪf ˈɛni ər ˈmɪsɪŋ wɪˈθaʊt ˈrɪli ˈkaʊntɪŋ.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: As another example of matching being more fundamental than counting, if I'm speaking to a packed auditorium,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɛz əˈnəðər ɪgˈzæmpəl əv ˈmæʧɪŋ biɪŋ mɔr ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl ðən ˈkaʊntɪŋ, ɪf əm ˈspikɪŋ tɪ ə pækt ˌɔdəˈtɔriəm,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: where every seat is taken and no one is standing,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: wɛr ˈɛvəri sit ɪz ˈteɪkən ənd noʊ wən ɪz ˈstændɪŋ,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: I know that there are the same number of chairs as people in the audience,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: aɪ noʊ ðət ðɛr ər ðə seɪm ˈnəmbər əv ʧɛrz ɛz ˈpipəl ɪn ðə ˈɔdiəns,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: even though I don't know how many there are of either.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ˈivɪn ðoʊ aɪ doʊnt noʊ haʊ ˈmɛni ðɛr ər əv ˈiðər.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: So, what we really mean when we say that two sets are the same size is that the elements in those sets
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: soʊ, wət wi ˈrɪli min wɪn wi seɪ ðət tu sɛts ər ðə seɪm saɪz ɪz ðət ðə ˈɛləmənts ɪn ðoʊz sɛts

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: can be matched up one by one in some way. My fourth grade teacher showed us
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: kən bi mæʧt əp wən baɪ wən ɪn səm weɪ. maɪ fɔrθ greɪd ˈtiʧər ʃoʊd ˈjuˈɛs

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: the whole numbers laid out in a row, and below each we have its double.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðə hoʊl ˈnəmbərz leɪd aʊt ɪn ə roʊ, ənd bɪˈloʊ iʧ wi hæv ɪts ˈdəbəl.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: As you can see, the bottom row contains all the even numbers, and we have a one-to-one match.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɛz ju kən si, ðə ˈbɑtəm roʊ kənˈteɪnz ɔl ðə ˈivɪn ˈnəmbərz, ənd wi hæv ə one-to-one* mæʧ.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: That is, there are as many even numbers as there are numbers. But what still bothers us is our distress
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðət ɪz, ðɛr ər ɛz ˈmɛni ˈivɪn ˈnəmbərz ɛz ðɛr ər ˈnəmbərz. bət wət stɪl ˈbɑðərz ˈjuˈɛs ɪz ɑr dɪˈstrɛs

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: over the fact that even numbers seem to be only part of the whole numbers. But does this convince you
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ˈoʊvər ðə fækt ðət ˈivɪn ˈnəmbərz sim tɪ bi ˈoʊnli pɑrt əv ðə hoʊl ˈnəmbərz. bət dɪz ðɪs kənˈvɪns ju

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: that I don't have the same number of fingers on my right hand as I do on my left? Of course not.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðət aɪ doʊnt hæv ðə seɪm ˈnəmbər əv ˈfɪŋgərz ɔn maɪ raɪt hænd ɛz aɪ du ɔn maɪ lɛft? əv kɔrs nɑt.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: It doesn't matter if you try to match the elements in some way and it doesn't work,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɪt ˈdəzənt ˈmætər ɪf ju traɪ tɪ mæʧ ðə ˈɛləmənts ɪn səm weɪ ənd ɪt ˈdəzənt wərk,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: that doesn't convince us of anything. If you can find one way in which the elements of two sets do match up,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðət ˈdəzənt kənˈvɪns ˈjuˈɛs əv ˈɛniˌθɪŋ. ɪf ju kən faɪnd wən weɪ ɪn wɪʧ ðə ˈɛləmənts əv tu sɛts du mæʧ əp,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: then we say those two sets have the same number of elements.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðɛn wi seɪ ðoʊz tu sɛts hæv ðə seɪm ˈnəmbər əv ˈɛləmənts.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Can you make a list of all the fractions? This might be hard, there are a lot of fractions!
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: kən ju meɪk ə lɪst əv ɔl ðə ˈfrækʃənz? ðɪs maɪt bi hɑrd, ðɛr ər ə lɔt əv ˈfrækʃənz!

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: And it's not obvious what to put first, or how to be sure all of them are on the list.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ənd ɪts nɑt ˈɑbviəs wət tɪ pʊt fərst, ər haʊ tɪ bi ʃʊr ɔl əv ðɛm ər ɔn ðə lɪst.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Nevertheless, there is a very clever way that we can make a list of all the fractions.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ˌnɛvərðəˈlɛs, ðɛr ɪz ə ˈvɛri ˈklɛvər weɪ ðət wi kən meɪk ə lɪst əv ɔl ðə ˈfrækʃənz.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: This was first done by Georg Cantor, in the late eighteen hundreds. First, we put all the fractions into a grid.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðɪs wɑz fərst dən baɪ geɪɔrg ˈkæntər, ɪn ðə leɪt ˈeɪˈtin ˈhənərdz. fərst, wi pʊt ɔl ðə ˈfrækʃənz ˈɪntu ə grɪd.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: They're all there. For instance, you can find, say, 117/243, in the 117th row and 243rd column.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðɛr ɔl ðɛr. fər ˈɪnstəns, ju kən faɪnd, seɪ, 117/243* ɪn ðə 117th* roʊ ənd 243rd* ˈkɑləm.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Now we make a list out of this by starting at the upper left and sweeping back and forth diagonally,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: naʊ wi meɪk ə lɪst aʊt əv ðɪs baɪ ˈstɑrtɪŋ æt ðə ˈəpər lɛft ənd sˈwipɪŋ bæk ənd fɔrθ daɪˈægənəli,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: skipping over any fraction, like 2/2, that represents the same number as one the we've already picked.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ˈskɪpɪŋ ˈoʊvər ˈɛni ˈfrækʃən, laɪk 2/2* ðət ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnts ðə seɪm ˈnəmbər ɛz wən ðə wiv ɔˈrɛdi pɪkt.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: We get a list of all the fractions, which means we've created a one-to-one match between the whole numbers and the fractions,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: wi gɪt ə lɪst əv ɔl ðə ˈfrækʃənz, wɪʧ minz wiv kriˈeɪtɪd ə one-to-one* mæʧ bɪtˈwin ðə hoʊl ˈnəmbərz ənd ðə ˈfrækʃənz,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: despite the fact that we thought maybe there ought to be more fractions. OK, here's where it gets really interesting.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: dɪˈspaɪt ðə fækt ðət wi θɔt ˈmeɪbi ðɛr ɔt tɪ bi mɔr ˈfrækʃənz. ˈoʊˈkeɪ, hɪrz wɛr ɪt gɪts ˈrɪli ˈɪntəˌrɛstɪŋ.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: You may know that not all real numbers -- that is, not all the numbers on a number line -- are fractions.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ju meɪ noʊ ðət nɑt ɔl ril ˈnəmbərz  ðət ɪz, nɑt ɔl ðə ˈnəmbərz ɔn ə ˈnəmbər laɪn  ər ˈfrækʃənz.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: The square root of two and pi, for instance. Any number like this is called irrational. Not because it's crazy, or anything,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðə skwɛr rut əv tu ənd paɪ, fər ˈɪnstəns. ˈɛni ˈnəmbər laɪk ðɪs ɪz kɔld ˌɪˈræʃənəl. nɑt bɪˈkəz ɪts ˈkreɪzi, ər ˈɛniˌθɪŋ,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: but because the fractions are ratios of whole numbers, and so are called rationals; meaning the rest are non-rational, that is, irrational.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: bət bɪˈkəz ðə ˈfrækʃənz ər ˈreɪʃiˌoʊz əv hoʊl ˈnəmbərz, ənd soʊ ər kɔld rationals*; ˈminɪŋ ðə rɛst ər non-rational*, ðət ɪz, ˌɪˈræʃənəl.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Irrationals are represented by infinite, non-repeating decimals. So, can we make a one-to-one match
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: irrationals* ər ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnɪd baɪ ˈɪnfənət, non-repeating* ˈdɛsəməlz. soʊ, kən wi meɪk ə one-to-one* mæʧ

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: between the whole numbers and the set of all the decimals, both the rationals and the irrationals?
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: bɪtˈwin ðə hoʊl ˈnəmbərz ənd ðə sɛt əv ɔl ðə ˈdɛsəməlz, boʊθ ðə rationals* ənd ðə irrationals*?

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: That is, can we make a list of all the decimal numbers? Cantor showed that you can't.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðət ɪz, kən wi meɪk ə lɪst əv ɔl ðə ˈdɛsəməl ˈnəmbərz? ˈkæntər ʃoʊd ðət ju kænt.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Not merely that we don't know how, but that it can't be done.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: nɑt ˈmɪrli ðət wi doʊnt noʊ haʊ, bət ðət ɪt kænt bi dən.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Look, suppose you claim you have made a list of all the decimals. I'm going to show you that you didn't succeed,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: lʊk, səˈpoʊz ju kleɪm ju hæv meɪd ə lɪst əv ɔl ðə ˈdɛsəməlz. əm goʊɪŋ tɪ ʃoʊ ju ðət ju ˈdɪdənt səkˈsid,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: by producing a decimal that is not on your list. I'll construct my decimal one place at a time.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: baɪ prəˈdusɪŋ ə ˈdɛsəməl ðət ɪz nɑt ɔn jʊr lɪst. aɪl ˈkɑnstrəkt maɪ ˈdɛsəməl wən pleɪs æt ə taɪm.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: For the first decimal place of my number, I'll look at the first decimal place of your first number.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: fər ðə fərst ˈdɛsəməl pleɪs əv maɪ ˈnəmbər, aɪl lʊk æt ðə fərst ˈdɛsəməl pleɪs əv jʊr fərst ˈnəmbər.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: If it's a one, I'll make mine a two; otherwise I'll make mine a one. For the second place of my number,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɪf ɪts ə wən, aɪl meɪk maɪn ə tu; ˈəðərˌwaɪz aɪl meɪk maɪn ə wən. fər ðə ˈsɛkənd pleɪs əv maɪ ˈnəmbər,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: I'll look at the second place of your second number. Again, if yours is a one, I'll make mine a two,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: aɪl lʊk æt ðə ˈsɛkənd pleɪs əv jʊr ˈsɛkənd ˈnəmbər. əˈgɛn, ɪf jʊrz ɪz ə wən, aɪl meɪk maɪn ə tu,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: and otherwise I'll make mine a one. See how this is going? The decimal I've produced can't be on your list.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ənd ˈəðərˌwaɪz aɪl meɪk maɪn ə wən. si haʊ ðɪs ɪz goʊɪŋ? ðə ˈdɛsəməl aɪv prəˈdust kænt bi ɔn jʊr lɪst.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Why? Could it be, say, your 143rd number? No, because the 143rd place of my decimal
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: waɪ? kʊd ɪt bi, seɪ, jʊr 143rd* ˈnəmbər? noʊ, bɪˈkəz ðə 143rd* pleɪs əv maɪ ˈdɛsəməl

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: is different from the 143rd place of your 143rd number. I made it that way. Your list is incomplete.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɪz ˈdɪfərənt frəm ðə 143rd* pleɪs əv jʊr 143rd* ˈnəmbər. aɪ meɪd ɪt ðət weɪ. jʊr lɪst ɪz ˌɪnkəmˈplit.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: It doesn't contain my decimal number. And, no matter what list you give me, I can do the same thing,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɪt ˈdəzənt kənˈteɪn maɪ ˈdɛsəməl ˈnəmbər. ənd, noʊ ˈmætər wət lɪst ju gɪv mi, aɪ kən du ðə seɪm θɪŋ,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: and produce a decimal that's not on that list. So we're faced with this astounding conclusion:
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ənd ˈproʊdus ə ˈdɛsəməl ðæts nɑt ɔn ðət lɪst. soʊ wɪr feɪst wɪθ ðɪs əˈstaʊndɪŋ kənˈkluʒən:

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: The decimal numbers cannot be put on a list. They represent a bigger infinity that the infinity of whole numbers.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðə ˈdɛsəməl ˈnəmbərz ˈkænɑt bi pʊt ɔn ə lɪst. ðeɪ ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnt ə ˈbɪgər ˌɪnˈfɪnɪti ðət ðə ˌɪnˈfɪnɪti əv hoʊl ˈnəmbərz.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: So, even though we're familiar with only a few irrationals, like square root of two and pi, the infinity of irrationals
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: soʊ, ˈivɪn ðoʊ wɪr fəˈmɪljər wɪθ ˈoʊnli ə fju irrationals*, laɪk skwɛr rut əv tu ənd paɪ, ðə ˌɪnˈfɪnɪti əv irrationals*

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: is actually greater than the infinity of fractions. Someone once said that the rationals
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɪz ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈgreɪtər ðən ðə ˌɪnˈfɪnɪti əv ˈfrækʃənz. ˈsəmˌwən wəns sɛd ðət ðə rationals*

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: -- the fractions -- are like the stars in the night sky.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðə ˈfrækʃənz  ər laɪk ðə stɑrz ɪn ðə naɪt skaɪ.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: The irrationals are like the blackness. Cantor also showed that, for any infinite set,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðə irrationals* ər laɪk ðə ˈblæknəs. ˈkæntər ˈɔlsoʊ ʃoʊd ðət, fər ˈɛni ˈɪnfənət sɛt,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: forming a new set made of all the subsets of the original set represents a bigger infinity than that original set.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ˈfɔrmɪŋ ə nu sɛt meɪd əv ɔl ðə subsets* əv ðə ərˈɪʤənəl sɛt ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnts ə ˈbɪgər ˌɪnˈfɪnɪti ðən ðət ərˈɪʤənəl sɛt.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: This means that, once you have one infinity, you can always make a bigger one
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðɪs minz ðət, wəns ju hæv wən ˌɪnˈfɪnɪti, ju kən ˈɔlˌweɪz meɪk ə ˈbɪgər wən

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: by making the set of all subsets of that first set. And then an even bigger one
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: baɪ ˈmeɪkɪŋ ðə sɛt əv ɔl subsets* əv ðət fərst sɛt. ənd ðɛn ən ˈivɪn ˈbɪgər wən

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: by making the set of all the subsets of that one. And so on.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: baɪ ˈmeɪkɪŋ ðə sɛt əv ɔl ðə subsets* əv ðət wən. ənd soʊ ɔn.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: And so, there are an infinite number of infinities of different sizes. If these ideas make you uncomfortable, you are not alone.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ənd soʊ, ðɛr ər ən ˈɪnfənət ˈnəmbər əv infinities* əv ˈdɪfərənt ˈsaɪzɪz. ɪf ðiz aɪˈdiəz meɪk ju ənˈkəmfərtəbəl, ju ər nɑt əˈloʊn.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Some of the greatest mathematicians of Cantor's day were very upset with this stuff. They tried to make these different infinities irrelevant,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: səm əv ðə ˈgreɪtəst ˌmæθəməˈtɪʃənz əv ˈkæntərz deɪ wər ˈvɛri ˈəpˌsɛt wɪθ ðɪs stəf. ðeɪ traɪd tɪ meɪk ðiz ˈdɪfərənt infinities* ˌɪˈrɛləvənt,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: to make mathematics work without them somehow. Cantor was even vilified personally,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: tɪ meɪk ˌmæθəˈmætɪks wərk wɪˈθaʊt ðɛm ˈsəmˌhaʊ. ˈkæntər wɑz ˈivɪn ˈvɪləfaɪd ˈpərsənəli,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: and it got so bad for him that he suffered severe depression,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ənd ɪt gɑt soʊ bæd fər ɪm ðət hi ˈsəfərd səˈvɪr dɪˈprɛʃən,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: and spent the last half of his life in and out of mental institutions. But eventually, his ideas won out.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ənd spɛnt ðə læst hæf əv hɪz laɪf ɪn ənd aʊt əv ˈmɛntəl ˌɪnstɪˈtuʃənz. bət ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli, hɪz aɪˈdiəz wən aʊt.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Today, they're considered fundamental and magnificent. All research mathematicians accept these ideas, every college math major learns them,
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: təˈdeɪ, ðɛr kənˈsɪdərd ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl ənd mægˈnɪfɪsənt. ɔl ˈrisərʧ ˌmæθəməˈtɪʃənz əkˈsɛpt ðiz aɪˈdiəz, ˈɛvəri ˈkɑlɪʤ mæθ ˈmeɪʤər lərnz ðɛm,

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: and I've explained them to you in a few minutes. Some day, perhaps, they'll be common knowledge. There's more.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ənd aɪv ɪkˈspleɪnd ðɛm tɪ ju ɪn ə fju ˈmɪnəts. səm deɪ, pərˈhæps, ðɛl bi ˈkɑmən ˈnɑlɪʤ. ðɛrz mɔr.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: We just pointed out that the set of decimal numbers -- that is, the real numbers -- is a bigger infinity
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: wi ʤɪst ˈpɔɪntɪd aʊt ðət ðə sɛt əv ˈdɛsəməl ˈnəmbərz  ðət ɪz, ðə ril ˈnəmbərz  ɪz ə ˈbɪgər ˌɪnˈfɪnɪti

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: than the set of whole numbers. Cantor wondered whether there are infinities of different sizes between these two infinities.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðən ðə sɛt əv hoʊl ˈnəmbərz. ˈkæntər ˈwəndərd ˈwɛðər ðɛr ər infinities* əv ˈdɪfərənt ˈsaɪzɪz bɪtˈwin ðiz tu infinities*.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: He didn't believe there were, but couldn't prove it. Cantor's conjecture became known as the continuum hypothesis.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: hi ˈdɪdənt bɪˈliv ðɛr wər, bət ˈkʊdənt pruv ɪt. ˈkæntərz kənˈʤɛkʧər bɪˈkeɪm noʊn ɛz ðə kənˈtɪnjuəm haɪˈpɑθəsəs.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: In 1900, the great mathematician David Hilbert listed the continuum hypothesis as the most important unsolved problem in mathematics.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɪn 1900 ðə greɪt ˌmæθəməˈtɪʃən ˈdeɪvɪd ˈhɪlbərt ˈlɪstɪd ðə kənˈtɪnjuəm haɪˈpɑθəsəs ɛz ðə moʊst ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ənˈsɑlvd ˈprɑbləm ɪn ˌmæθəˈmætɪks.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: The 20th century saw a resolution of this problem, but in a completely unexpected, paradigm-shattering way.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðə 20th* ˈsɛnʧəri sɔ ə ˌrɛzəˈluʃən əv ðɪs ˈprɑbləm, bət ɪn ə kəmˈplitli ˌənɪkˈspɛktɪd, paradigm-shattering* weɪ.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: In the 1920s, Kurt Gödel showed that you can never prove that the continuum hypothesis is false.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ɪn ðə 1920s*, kərt gödel* ʃoʊd ðət ju kən ˈnɛvər pruv ðət ðə kənˈtɪnjuəm haɪˈpɑθəsəs ɪz fɔls.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Then, in the 1960s, Paul J. Cohen showed that you can never prove that the continuum hypothesis is true.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ðɛn, ɪn ðə 1960s*, pɔl ʤeɪ. koʊən ʃoʊd ðət ju kən ˈnɛvər pruv ðət ðə kənˈtɪnjuəm haɪˈpɑθəsəs ɪz tru.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Taken together, these results mean that there are unanswerable questions in mathematics. A very stunning conclusion.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ˈteɪkən təˈgɛðər, ðiz rɪˈzəlts min ðət ðɛr ər ˌəˈnænsərəbəl kˈwɛsʧənz ɪn ˌmæθəˈmætɪks. ə ˈvɛri ˈstənɪŋ kənˈkluʒən.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Mathematics is rightly considered the pinnacle of human reasoning, but we now know that even mathematics has its limitations.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: ˌmæθəˈmætɪks ɪz ˈraɪtli kənˈsɪdərd ðə ˈpɪnəkəl əv ˈjumən ˈrizənɪŋ, bət wi naʊ noʊ ðət ˈivɪn ˌmæθəˈmætɪks həz ɪts ˌlɪmɪˈteɪʃənz.

UPA3bwVVzGI.text: Still, mathematics has some truly amazing things for us to think about.
UPA3bwVVzGI.ipa: stɪl, ˌmæθəˈmætɪks həz səm ˈtruli əˈmeɪzɪŋ θɪŋz fər ˈjuˈɛs tɪ θɪŋk əˈbaʊt.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: hɛˈloʊ ənd ˈwɛlkəm tɪ ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ, ðə ˈpɔdˌkæst wɛr wi hæv ril conversations

u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: and* maɪ məm ˈɔlsoʊ juzd tɪ lɪv ðɛr fər ə while.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: So it's a dream of hers to go with the whole family. Um, and she can show us around a bit.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: so* ɪts ə drim əv hərz tɪ goʊ wɪθ ðə hoʊl ˈfæməli. əm, ənd ʃi kən ʃoʊ ˈjuˈɛs əraʊnd ə bɪt.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: um*, ənd soʊ wɪn ʃi ˈfɪnɪʃɪz ðət, hər skul jɪr ənd hər exams,

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: and it made me feel very bad about myself. So this year, I'm just going to keep it simple.
