u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: but* fər ˈrənɪŋ, aɪ θɪŋk ðɪs taɪm aɪ nid ə bɪg goal.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: So I'm a bit scared about it, but I think I'm going to book a half marathon.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: so* əm ə bɪt skɛrd əˈbaʊt ɪt, bət aɪ θɪŋk əm goʊɪŋ tɪ bʊk ə hæf marathon.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Um, because then I can make a training programme and feel really motivated.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: um*, bɪˈkəz ðɛn aɪ kən meɪk ə ˈtreɪnɪŋ ˈproʊˌgræm ənd fil ˈrɪli motivated.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Um, I think that's what I need. I think that's a great idea. Um, what about you, Neil? Any New Year's resolutions?
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: um*, aɪ θɪŋk ðæts wət aɪ nid. aɪ θɪŋk ðæts ə greɪt aɪˈdiə. əm, wət əˈbaʊt ju, nil? ˈɛni nu jɪrz resolutions?

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Well, usually once Christmas and New Year are over,
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: well*, ˈjuʒəwəli wəns ˈkrɪsməs ənd nu jɪr ər over,

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: we have the two darkest, saddest months of the year.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: we* hæv ðə tu ˈdɑrkəst, ˈsædəst mənθs əv ðə jɪr.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: January and February. And normally I really just wish that those months go away
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: ˈʤænjuˌɛri ənd ˈfɛbruˌɛri. ənd ˈnɔrməli aɪ ˈrɪli ʤɪst wɪʃ ðət ðoʊz mənθs goʊ away

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: and that we get into spring.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: and* ðət wi gɪt ˈɪntu spring.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: But this year I think I'm going to enjoy every day, regardless,
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: but* ðɪs jɪr aɪ θɪŋk əm goʊɪŋ tɪ ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈɛvəri deɪ, regardless,

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: you know, even if the weather is awful and it's dark and cold, I still want
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: you* noʊ, ˈivɪn ɪf ðə ˈwɛðər ɪz ˈɔfəl ənd ɪts dɑrk ənd koʊld, aɪ stɪl want

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: to get out there and appreciate it rather than wish my life away.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: to* gɪt aʊt ðɛr ənd əˈpriʃiˌeɪt ɪt ˈrəðər ðən wɪʃ maɪ laɪf away.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: So, Neil, do you have any goals for this year? So something you want to achieve?
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: so*, nil, du ju hæv ˈɛni goʊlz fər ðɪs jɪr? soʊ ˈsəmθɪŋ ju wɔnt tɪ achieve?

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Yeah, I think I don't really want to take up something new.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: yeah*, aɪ θɪŋk aɪ doʊnt ˈrɪli wɔnt tɪ teɪk əp ˈsəmθɪŋ new.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: 'Take up' means 'start a new activity'.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: 'take* əp' minz 'stɑrt ə nu activity'.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: But I think my goal is to become good at something I like doing.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: but* aɪ θɪŋk maɪ goʊl ɪz tɪ bɪˈkəm gʊd æt ˈsəmθɪŋ aɪ laɪk doing.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Right. And what could that be?
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: right*. ənd wət kʊd ðət be?

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: So I like learning Spanish, but I'm not very good. I'm not very dedicated,
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: so* aɪ laɪk ˈlərnɪŋ ˈspænɪʃ, bət əm nɑt ˈvɛri gʊd. əm nɑt ˈvɛri dedicated,

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: and I'm always jealous of people who can speak really well.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: and* əm ˈɔlˌweɪz ˈʤɛləs əv ˈpipəl hu kən spik ˈrɪli well.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Mmm. Um, and the same with my guitar. Yeah. I always want to get better.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: mmm*. əm, ənd ðə seɪm wɪθ maɪ gɪˈtɑr. jæ. aɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz wɔnt tɪ gɪt better.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Yeah. A goal could be to write a song and produce it.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: yeah*. ə goʊl kʊd bi tɪ raɪt ə sɔŋ ənd ˈproʊdus it.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Yeah. And play it on this podcast. If you're all really unlucky.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: yeah*. ənd pleɪ ɪt ɔn ðɪs ˈpɔdˌkæst. ɪf jʊr ɔl ˈrɪli ənˈləki.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: I don't think anyone wants that. Thank you for your honesty.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: aɪ doʊnt θɪŋk ˈɛniˌwən wɔnts ðət. θæŋk ju fər jʊr honesty.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Right. Let's recap the language we heard during the conversation.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: right*. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə ˈlæŋgwɪʤ wi hərd ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə conversation.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: We heard 'New Year's resolution', which is a promise you make to yourself
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: we* hərd 'nu jɪrz ˌrɛzəˈluʃən', wɪʧ ɪz ə ˈprɑməs ju meɪk tɪ yourself

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: at the start of a year to start doing something good or stop doing something bad.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: at* ðə stɑrt əv ə jɪr tɪ stɑrt duɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ gʊd ər stɑp duɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ bad.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: We heard 'goals'. Now, goals are things that we say that we want to do and try to achieve them.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: we* hərd 'goʊlz'. naʊ, goʊlz ər θɪŋz ðət wi seɪ ðət wi wɔnt tɪ du ənd traɪ tɪ əˈʧiv them.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: We also heard 'motivated', which is a feeling of really wanting to do something and doing it.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: we* ˈɔlsoʊ hərd 'ˈmoʊtəˌveɪtəd', wɪʧ ɪz ə ˈfilɪŋ əv ˈrɪli ˈwɑnɪŋ tɪ du ˈsəmθɪŋ ənd duɪŋ it.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: For example, I feel motivated to run a half marathon this year.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: for* ɪgˈzæmpəl, aɪ fil ˈmoʊtəˌveɪtəd tɪ rən ə hæf ˈmɛrəˌθɑn ðɪs year.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: And we heard the expression 'to wish your life away'.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: and* wi hərd ðə ɪkˈsprɛʃən 'tɪ wɪʃ jʊr laɪf away'.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: If you wish your life away, you don't appreciate what's happening now. You look far into the future.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: if* ju wɪʃ jʊr laɪf əˈweɪ, ju doʊnt əˈpriʃiˌeɪt wəts ˈhæpənɪŋ naʊ. ju lʊk fɑr ˈɪntu ðə future.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: That's it for this episode of Real Easy English. Do you have a New Year's resolution? We'd love to hear about it.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: that's* ɪt fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd əv ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ. du ju hæv ə nu jɪrz ˌrɛzəˈluʃən? wid ləv tɪ hir əˈbaʊt it.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Email us at. learningenglish@bbc.co.uk
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: email* ˈjuˈɛs æt. learningenglish@bbc.co.uk

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Is one of your New Year's resolutions to improve your English? If so, go to our website,
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: is* wən əv jʊr nu jɪrz ˌrɛzəˈluʃənz tɪ ˌɪmˈpruv jʊr ˈɪŋlɪʃ? ɪf soʊ, goʊ tɪ ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt,

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: bbclearningenglish.com, where you can find all kinds of activities and quizzes to help you improve.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: bbclearningenglish.com*, wɛr ju kən faɪnd ɔl kaɪnz əv ækˈtɪvɪtiz ənd kˈwɪzɪz tɪ hɛlp ju improve.

u5AHBqh2Ujs.text: Goodbye for now. Goodbye.
u5AHBqh2Ujs.ipa: goodbye* fər naʊ. ˌgʊdˈbaɪ.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Now’s the perfect time to grab the 12,026 calendar – and get it in time for the holidays.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: now’s* ðə ˈpərˌfɪkt taɪm tɪ græb ðə 12,026* ˈkæləndər –* ənd gɪt ɪt ɪn taɪm fər ðə holidays.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Our best theory of the universe could be wrong – finally. We’re going through an incredibly exciting moment.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: our* bɛst ˈθɪri əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs kʊd bi rɔŋ –* ˈfaɪnəli. we’re* goʊɪŋ θru ən ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli ɪkˈsaɪtɪŋ moment.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Thanks to amazing new technology, our understanding of the universe is moving faster than it has in years.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: thanks* tɪ əˈmeɪzɪŋ nu tɛkˈnɑləʤi, ɑr ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ɪz ˈmuvɪŋ ˈfæstər ðən ɪt həz ɪn years.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: And if we are fortunate, we could be at the edge of the next revolution in the way we see
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: and* ɪf wi ər ˈfɔrʧənət, wi kʊd bi æt ðə ɛʤ əv ðə nɛkst ˌrɛvəˈluʃən ɪn ðə weɪ wi see

zozEm4f_dlw.text: the cosmos – a moment as exciting as when we first realized that the Earth revolves around the Sun,
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: the* ˈkɑzmoʊs –* ə ˈmoʊmənt ɛz ɪkˈsaɪtɪŋ ɛz wɪn wi fərst ˈriəˌlaɪzd ðət ðə ərθ riˈvɑlvz əraʊnd ðə sun,

zozEm4f_dlw.text: that the stars are other suns, or that our galaxy is just one tiny island in an ocean of trillions.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: that* ðə stɑrz ər ˈəðər sənz, ər ðət ɑr ˈgæləksi ɪz ʤɪst wən ˈtaɪni ˈaɪlənd ɪn ən ˈoʊʃən əv trillions.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Why do we think this? Because for the first time in ages, the universe is misbehaving. Badly.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: why* du wi θɪŋk ðɪs? bɪˈkəz fər ðə fərst taɪm ɪn ˈeɪʤɪz, ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ɪz ˌmɪsbəˈheɪvɪŋ. badly.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Nasty Universe
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: nasty* universe

zozEm4f_dlw.text: For decades, we’ve had a beautiful theory of the cosmos.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: for* ˈdɛkeɪdz, we’ve* hæd ə ˈbjutəfəl ˈθɪri əv ðə cosmos.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: One that explained how the universe began, what it’s made of, and how it’s supposed to behave.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: one* ðət ɪkˈspleɪnd haʊ ðə ˈjunəˌvərs bɪˈgæn, wət it’s* meɪd əv, ənd haʊ it’s* səˈpoʊzd tɪ behave.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: It matched our observations astonishingly well and made us feel like we’d almost deciphered the cosmic code.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: it* mæʧt ɑr ˌɑbzərˈveɪʃənz əˈstɑnɪʃɪŋli wɛl ənd meɪd ˈjuˈɛs fil laɪk we’d* ˈɔlˌmoʊst dɪˈsaɪfərd ðə ˈkɑzmɪk code.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: But in the last few years, as our telescopes got better and our data sharper, cracks started to appear.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* ɪn ðə læst fju jɪrz, ɛz ɑr ˈtɛləˌskoʊps gɑt ˈbɛtər ənd ɑr ˈdætə ˈʃɑrpər, kræks ˈstɑrtɪd tɪ appear.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Strange mismatches between what the theory predicted and what we actually saw.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: strange* mɪsˈmæʧɪz bɪtˈwin wət ðə ˈθɪri prɪˈdɪktɪd ənd wət wi ˈæˌkʧuəli saw.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: At first they looked like silly mistakes, noise that would go away with more data.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: at* fərst ðeɪ lʊkt laɪk ˈsɪli mɪˈsteɪks, nɔɪz ðət wʊd goʊ əˈweɪ wɪθ mɔr data.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: But as new data came in, the opposite happened. Some cracks got larger, new ones emerged,
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* ɛz nu ˈdætə keɪm ɪn, ðə ˈɑpəzɪt ˈhæpənd. səm kræks gɑt ˈlɑrʤər, nu wənz ˈimərʤd,

zozEm4f_dlw.text: and our once perfect picture of the cosmos began to look less and less … perfect. Of course, this wasn’t new.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: ənd ɑr wəns ˈpərˌfɪkt ˈpɪkʧər əv ðə ˈkɑzmoʊs bɪˈgæn tɪ lʊk lɛs ənd lɛs …* ˈpərˌfɪkt. əv kɔrs, ðɪs wasn’t* new.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Two centuries ago, astronomers noticed that Uranus’ orbit didn’t quite follow the laws of gravity.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: two* ˈsɛnʧəriz əˈgoʊ, əˈstrɑnəmərz ˈnoʊtɪst ðət uranus’*’ ˈɔrbət didn’t* kwaɪt ˈfɑloʊ ðə lɔz əv gravity.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: But instead of throwing those laws away, they proposed that a “dark planet” was tugging on Uranus from afar.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* ˌɪnˈstɛd əv θroʊɪŋ ðoʊz lɔz əˈweɪ, ðeɪ prəˈpoʊzd ðət ə ““dark* planet”*” wɑz ˈtəgɪŋ ɔn ˈjərənəs frəm afar.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Shortly after, Neptune was discovered – exactly where the math said it would be. But then came Mercury.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: shortly* ˈæftər, ˈnɛptun wɑz dɪˈskəvərd –* ɪgˈzæktli wɛr ðə mæθ sɛd ɪt wʊd bi. bət ðɛn keɪm mercury.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Its orbit also didn’t make sense, so scientists tried the same trick. But this time, no new planet showed up.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: its* ˈɔrbət ˈɔlsoʊ didn’t* meɪk sɛns, soʊ ˈsaɪəntɪsts traɪd ðə seɪm trɪk. bət ðɪs taɪm, noʊ nu ˈplænət ʃoʊd up.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: The answer wasn’t more stuff, but a completely new idea.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: the* ˈænsər wasn’t* mɔr stəf, bət ə kəmˈplitli nu idea.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Gravity had to be reimagined, and we invented general relativity – opening a whole new dimension in our understanding of the universe.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: gravity* hæd tɪ bi reimagined*, ənd wi ˌɪnˈvɛntɪd ˈʤɛnərəl ˌrɛləˈtɪvəti –* ˈoʊpənɪŋ ə hoʊl nu dɪˈmɛnʃən ɪn ɑr ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ əv ðə universe.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: So are we going through a Uranus moment or a Mercury one?
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: so* ər wi goʊɪŋ θru ə ˈjərənəs ˈmoʊmənt ər ə ˈmərkjəri one?

zozEm4f_dlw.text: First Crack – Cosmic Monsters
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: first* kræk –* ˈkɑzmɪk monsters

zozEm4f_dlw.text: The first signs that something deep could be off began piling up around
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: the* fərst saɪnz ðət ˈsəmθɪŋ dip kʊd bi ɔf bɪˈgæn ˈpaɪlɪŋ əp around

zozEm4f_dlw.text: 15 years ago – in the form of a few seemingly impossible cosmic monsters:
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: 15* jɪrz əˈgoʊ –* ɪn ðə fɔrm əv ə fju ˈsimɪŋli ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl ˈkɑzmɪk monsters:

zozEm4f_dlw.text: A “giant arc” of galaxies over 3 billion light-years wide.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: a* ““giant* arc”*” əv ˈgæləksiz ˈoʊvər 3 ˈbɪljən ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz wide.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: A massive group of quasars spanning 4 billion light-years. A ring of galaxies 5 billion light-years across.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: a* ˈmæsɪv grup əv quasars* ˈspænɪŋ 4 ˈbɪljən ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz. ə rɪŋ əv ˈgæləksiz 5 ˈbɪljən ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz across.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: An unfathomable wall of galaxies stretching ten billion light-years
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: an* ənˈfæðəməbəl wɔl əv ˈgæləksiz ˈstrɛʧɪŋ tɛn ˈbɪljən light-years

zozEm4f_dlw.text: from end to end – a whopping 10% of the entire observable universe. The list goes on. That’s not all.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: from* ɛnd tɪ ɛnd –* ə ˈwɑpɪŋ 10 əv ðə ɪnˈtaɪər əbˈzərvəbəl ˈjunəˌvərs. ðə lɪst goʊz ɔn. that’s* nɑt ɔl.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: There are also monstrous voids: vast cosmic deserts with far fewer galaxies than normal.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: ðɛr ər ˈɔlsoʊ ˈmɑnstrəs vɔɪdz: væst ˈkɑzmɪk ˈdɛzərts wɪθ fɑr fjuər ˈgæləksiz ðən normal.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: And according to some surveys, we happen to be living deep inside
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: and* əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ səm ˈsərˌveɪz, wi ˈhæpən tɪ bi ˈlɪvɪŋ dip inside

zozEm4f_dlw.text: one of them – a gargantuan “local hole” 2 billion light-years across. Where’s the problem?
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: one* əv ðɛm –* ə gɑrˈgænʧuən ““local* hole”*” 2 ˈbɪljən ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz əˈkrɔs. where’s* ðə problem?

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Well, the universe is organized in ever larger structures: galaxies, galaxy clusters, superclusters and eventually
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: well*, ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ɪz ˈɔrgəˌnaɪzd ɪn ˈɛvər ˈlɑrʤər ˈstrəkʧərz: ˈgæləksiz, ˈgæləksi ˈkləstərz, superclusters* ənd eventually

zozEm4f_dlw.text: filaments – truly gargantuan structures separated by equally enormous voids.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: filaments* –* ˈtruli gɑrˈgænʧuən ˈstrəkʧərz ˈsɛpərˌeɪtɪd baɪ ˈikwəli ɪˈnɔrmɪs voids.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: But our cosmic theory says that these things can’t get arbitrarily large. At distances beyond one billion light-years or so,
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* ɑr ˈkɑzmɪk ˈθɪri sɪz ðət ðiz θɪŋz can’t* gɪt ˈɑrbɪˌtrɛrəli lɑrʤ. æt ˈdɪstənsɪz bɪɔnd wən ˈbɪljən ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz ər so,

zozEm4f_dlw.text: the filaments and voids should blur into a uniform soup. And this is more than a technical detail – it’s
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: the* ˈfɪləmənts ənd vɔɪdz ʃʊd blər ˈɪntu ə ˈjunəˌfɔrm sup. ənd ðɪs ɪz mɔr ðən ə ˈtɛknɪkəl ˈditeɪl –* it’s

zozEm4f_dlw.text: a basic pillar of all our attempts to make sense of the cosmos itself.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: a* ˈbeɪsɪk ˈpɪlər əv ɔl ɑr əˈtɛmpts tɪ meɪk sɛns əv ðə ˈkɑzmoʊs itself.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Our understanding of the universe rests on one key assumption: the cosmological principle.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: our* ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs rɛsts ɔn wən ki əˈsəmpʃən: ðə cosmological* principle.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: This is the idea that, if you zoom out far enough, the universe should be uniform, looking the same everywhere.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: this* ɪz ðə aɪˈdiə ðət, ɪf ju zum aʊt fɑr ɪˈnəf, ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ʃʊd bi ˈjunəˌfɔrm, ˈlʊkɪŋ ðə seɪm everywhere.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: This is crucial because it means that our limited view of the cosmos is a fair sample of the whole.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: this* ɪz ˈkruʃəl bɪˈkəz ɪt minz ðət ɑr ˈlɪmɪtɪd vju əv ðə ˈkɑzmoʊs ɪz ə fɛr ˈsæmpəl əv ðə whole.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: That, even if we are tiny creatures living in a speck of dust, we can learn things about the entire universe.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: that*, ˈivɪn ɪf wi ər ˈtaɪni ˈkriʧərz ˈlɪvɪŋ ɪn ə spɛk əv dəst, wi kən lərn θɪŋz əˈbaʊt ðə ɪnˈtaɪər universe.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Both the cosmos itself and our place in it might be more unruly and chaotic than they should be.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: both* ðə ˈkɑzmoʊs ˌɪtˈsɛlf ənd ɑr pleɪs ɪn ɪt maɪt bi mɔr ənˈruli ənd keɪˈɑtɪk ðən ðeɪ ʃʊd be.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: But if the cosmological principle turns out to be wrong, we have a huge problem.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* ɪf ðə cosmological* ˈprɪnsəpəl tərnz aʊt tɪ bi rɔŋ, wi hæv ə juʤ problem.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Because if the universe isn’t the same everywhere,
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: because* ɪf ðə ˈjunəˌvərs isn’t* ðə seɪm ˈɛvriˌwɛr,

zozEm4f_dlw.text: we could be like ants trying to guess the flavor of a cake while sitting on its only cherry.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: wi kʊd bi laɪk ænts traɪɪŋ tɪ gɛs ðə ˈfleɪvər əv ə keɪk waɪl ˈsɪtɪŋ ɔn ɪts ˈoʊnli cherry.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Everything we see might just be local weirdness – a cosmic quirk that doesn’t tell us the actual story of the universe.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: everything* wi si maɪt ʤɪst bi ˈloʊkəl ˈwɪrdnəs –* ə ˈkɑzmɪk kwərk ðət doesn’t* tɛl ˈjuˈɛs ðə ˈækʧəwəl ˈstɔri əv ðə universe.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Second Crack – A Universe at Two Speeds
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: second* kræk –* ə ˈjunəˌvərs æt tu speeds

zozEm4f_dlw.text: The next crack appeared about 10 years ago. It tore straight at the fabric of space – challenging how fast it grows.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: the* nɛkst kræk əˈpɪrd əˈbaʊt 10 jɪrz əˈgoʊ. ɪt tɔr streɪt æt ðə ˈfæbrɪk əv speɪs –* ˈʧælənʤɪŋ haʊ fæst ɪt grows.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Every second the universe gets a little bigger. We know this because we have different ways to measure it
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: every* ˈsɛkənd ðə ˈjunəˌvərs gɪts ə ˈlɪtəl ˈbɪgər. wi noʊ ðɪs bɪˈkəz wi hæv ˈdɪfərənt weɪz tɪ ˈmɛʒər it

zozEm4f_dlw.text: and all confirm that space is expanding. The problem? They can’t agree on how fast.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: and* ɔl kənˈfərm ðət speɪs ɪz ɪkˈspændɪŋ. ðə ˈprɑbləm? ðeɪ can’t* əˈgri ɔn haʊ fast.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: It’s like measuring the speed of a car using two devices and getting different results.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: it’s* laɪk ˈmɛʒərɪŋ ðə spid əv ə kɑr ˈjuzɪŋ tu dɪˈvaɪsɪz ənd ˈgɪtɪŋ ˈdɪfərənt results.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: You read 67 on the speedometer, but 73 on the GPS. One of the instruments must be broken, right?
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: you* rɛd 67 ɔn ðə spiˈdɑmətər, bət 73 ɔn ðə ˈgipiˈɛs. wən əv ðə ˈɪnstrəmənts məst bi ˈbroʊkən, right?

zozEm4f_dlw.text: But then you check them again and again and again… and both work flawlessly.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* ðɛn ju ʧɛk ðɛm əˈgɛn ənd əˈgɛn ənd again…*… ənd boʊθ wərk flawlessly.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: This is very much what happens with the universe. The details are messy and complicated, but they don’t matter for this story.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: this* ɪz ˈvɛri məʧ wət ˈhæpənz wɪθ ðə ˈjunəˌvərs. ðə ˈditeɪlz ər ˈmɛsi ənd ˈkɑmpləˌkeɪtəd, bət ðeɪ don’t* ˈmætər fər ðɪs story.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: The important part is that, as measurements and calculations have become more and more precise, the disagreement has only become worse.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: the* ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt pɑrt ɪz ðət, ɛz ˈmɛʒərmənts ənd ˌkælkjəˈleɪʃənz hæv bɪˈkəm mɔr ənd mɔr prɪˈsaɪs, ðə dɪsəˈgrimənt həz ˈoʊnli bɪˈkəm worse.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: By now, the chance that this mismatch is just an accidental fluke is less than one in a million.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: by* naʊ, ðə ʧæns ðət ðɪs ˈmɪsˌmæʧ ɪz ʤɪst ən ˌæksəˈdɛnəl fluk ɪz lɛs ðən wən ɪn ə million.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: The universe is literally giving us two different answers to the same question.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: the* ˈjunəˌvərs ɪz ˈlɪtərəli ˈgɪvɪŋ ˈjuˈɛs tu ˈdɪfərənt ˈænsərz tɪ ðə seɪm question.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: So something fundamental must be broken – either our measurements of the universe, or our basic understanding of it.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: so* ˈsəmθɪŋ ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl məst bi ˈbroʊkən –* ˈiðər ɑr ˈmɛʒərmənts əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs, ər ɑr ˈbeɪsɪk ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ əv it.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Third Crack – Old Galaxies in a Baby Universe
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: third* kræk –* oʊld ˈgæləksiz ɪn ə ˈbeɪbi ˈjunəˌvərs

zozEm4f_dlw.text: The latest surprise is only about 3 years old.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: ðə ˈleɪtəst səˈpraɪz ɪz ˈoʊnli əˈbaʊt 3 jɪrz old.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: It shattered a key part of our cosmic timeline – how and when the first galaxies formed.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: it* ˈʃætərd ə ki pɑrt əv ɑr ˈkɑzmɪk ˈtaɪmlaɪn –* haʊ ənd wɪn ðə fərst ˈgæləksiz formed.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Telescopes act like time machines. Light from distant galaxies takes so long to reach us
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: telescopes* ækt laɪk taɪm məˈʃinz. laɪt frəm ˈdɪstənt ˈgæləksiz teɪks soʊ lɔŋ tɪ riʧ us

zozEm4f_dlw.text: that we don’t see them as they are now, but as they were in the past.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: that* wi don’t* si ðɛm ɛz ðeɪ ər naʊ, bət ɛz ðeɪ wər ɪn ðə past.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: In 2021 we launched the James Webb, the most powerful space telescope ever built.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: in* 2021 wi lɔnʧt ðə ʤeɪmz wɛb, ðə moʊst ˈpaʊərfəl speɪs ˈtɛləˌskoʊp ˈɛvər built.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: And almost immediately, it began finding bright,
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: and* ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˌɪˈmiˌdiətli, ɪt bɪˈgæn ˈfaɪndɪŋ bright,

zozEm4f_dlw.text: massive galaxies so distant that they belong to a time when the universe was extremely young. The problem?
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: massive* ˈgæləksiz soʊ ˈdɪstənt ðət ðeɪ bɪˈlɔŋ tɪ ə taɪm wɪn ðə ˈjunəˌvərs wɑz ɪkˈstrimli jəŋ. ðə problem?

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Some are so premature that they date back to 280 million years after the Big Bang – far earlier than anyone expected.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: some* ər soʊ ˌpriməˈʧʊr ðət ðeɪ deɪt bæk tɪ 280 ˈmɪljən jɪrz ˈæftər ðə bɪg bæŋ –* fɑr ˈərliər ðən ˈɛniˌwən expected.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Our theory says that the amorphous soup of matter that emerged from the Big Bang
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: our* ˈθɪri sɪz ðət ðə əˈmɔrfəs sup əv ˈmætər ðət ˈimərʤd frəm ðə bɪg bang

zozEm4f_dlw.text: gave rise to the first galaxies through a long chain of mergers. Tiny lumps of dark and normal matter gathered under gravity,
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: gave* raɪz tɪ ðə fərst ˈgæləksiz θru ə lɔŋ ʧeɪn əv ˈmərʤərz. ˈtaɪni ləmps əv dɑrk ənd ˈnɔrməl ˈmætər ˈgæðərd ˈəndər gravity,

zozEm4f_dlw.text: building larger chunks that then fused into even bigger ones and so on. But this process is lengthy.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: building* ˈlɑrʤər ʧəŋks ðət ðɛn fjuzd ˈɪntu ˈivɪn ˈbɪgər wənz ənd soʊ ɔn. bət ðɪs ˈprɔˌsɛs ɪz lengthy.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: By our best estimates, the first large galaxies should
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: by* ɑr bɛst ˈɛstəˌmeɪts, ðə fərst lɑrʤ ˈgæləksiz should

zozEm4f_dlw.text: have emerged 500 million years after the Big Bang or so, not much before.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: have* ˈimərʤd 500 ˈmɪljən jɪrz ˈæftər ðə bɪg bæŋ ər soʊ, nɑt məʧ before.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: But it isn’t only that we’ve found large galaxies existing way before that. The new galaxies also seem to be too mature.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* ɪt isn’t* ˈoʊnli ðət we’ve* faʊnd lɑrʤ ˈgæləksiz ɪgˈzɪstɪŋ weɪ ˌbiˈfɔr ðət. ðə nu ˈgæləksiz ˈɔlsoʊ sim tɪ bi tu mature.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Matter in the baby universe was made up almost entirely of hydrogen and helium.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: matter* ɪn ðə ˈbeɪbi ˈjunəˌvərs wɑz meɪd əp ˈɔlˌmoʊst ɪnˈtaɪərli əv ˈhaɪdrəʤən ənd ˈhiliəm.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Heavy elements like carbon or nitrogen were only forged later in the cores of stars, which had to explode to release them.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: ˈhɛvi ˈɛləmənts laɪk ˈkɑrbən ər ˈnaɪtrəʤən wər ˈoʊnli fɔrʤd ˈleɪtər ɪn ðə kɔrz əv stɑrz, wɪʧ hæd tɪ ɪkˈsploʊd tɪ riˈlis them.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: But some of these super early galaxies contain a lot of heavy elements – meaning
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* səm əv ðiz ˈsupər ˈərli ˈgæləksiz kənˈteɪn ə lɔt əv ˈhɛvi ˈɛləmənts –* meaning

zozEm4f_dlw.text: that entire generations of stars must have lived and died even before them.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: that* ɪnˈtaɪər ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃənz əv stɑrz məst hæv lɪvd ənd daɪd ˈivɪn ˌbiˈfɔr them.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: So this is like finding grown-up kids in a kindergarten. Either the first galaxies sprouted in fast forwards,
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: so* ðɪs ɪz laɪk ˈfaɪndɪŋ ˈgroʊˌnəp kɪdz ɪn ə ˈkɪndərˌgɑrtən. ˈiðər ðə fərst ˈgæləksiz ˈspraʊtɪd ɪn fæst forwards,

zozEm4f_dlw.text: or we are missing something huge about the infancy of the universe.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: or* wi ər ˈmɪsɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ juʤ əˈbaʊt ðə ˈɪnfənsi əv ðə universe.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: From Cracks to Crisis
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: from* kræks tɪ crisis

zozEm4f_dlw.text: These problems aren’t the only ones. Our theory also says that the Big Bang should have created 3 times more lithium
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: these* ˈprɑbləmz aren’t* ðə ˈoʊnli wənz. ɑr ˈθɪri ˈɔlsoʊ sɪz ðət ðə bɪg bæŋ ʃʊd hæv kriˈeɪtɪd 3 taɪmz mɔr lithium

zozEm4f_dlw.text: than we see out there – a decades-old itch that astronomers just can’t scratch.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: than* wi si aʊt ðɛr –* ə decades-old* ɪʧ ðət əˈstrɑnəmərz ʤɪst can’t* scratch.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: It predicts that dark matter should pile up sharply at galaxy centers, but instead we find gentle hills.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: it* prɪˈdɪkts ðət dɑrk ˈmætər ʃʊd paɪl əp ˈʃɑrpli æt ˈgæləksi ˈsɛnərz, bət ˌɪnˈstɛd wi faɪnd ˈʤɛnəl hills.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: It says that dark energy, the mysterious force pushing the universe apart, has stayed constant since the Big Bang.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: it* sɪz ðət dɑrk ˈɛnərʤi, ðə mɪˈstɪriəs fɔrs ˈpʊʃɪŋ ðə ˈjunəˌvərs əˈpɑrt, həz steɪd ˈkɑnstənt sɪns ðə bɪg bang.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: But last year, one of the biggest galaxy surveys ever conducted dropped the bombshell that it may have been changing over time.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* læst jɪr, wən əv ðə ˈbɪgəst ˈgæləksi ˈsərˌveɪz ˈɛvər kənˈdəktəd drɑpt ðə ˈbɑmˌʃɛl ðət ɪt meɪ hæv bɪn ˈʧeɪnʤɪŋ ˈoʊvər time.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: If true, this would overturn our current picture of the universe, its past and its future.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: if* tru, ðɪs wʊd ˈoʊvərˌtərn ɑr ˈkɑrənt ˈpɪkʧər əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs, ɪts pæst ənd ɪts future.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Even things that we considered settled beyond any doubt, like the interpretation of the cosmic microwave background, are suddenly up
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: even* θɪŋz ðət wi kənˈsɪdərd ˈsɛtəld bɪɔnd ˈɛni daʊt, laɪk ðə ˌɪnˌtərprɪˈteɪʃən əv ðə ˈkɑzmɪk ˈmaɪkrəˌweɪv ˈbækˌgraʊnd, ər ˈsədənli up

zozEm4f_dlw.text: for debate – those early galaxies might have been bright enough to contaminate the signal.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: for* dəˈbeɪt –* ðoʊz ˈərli ˈgæləksiz maɪt hæv bɪn braɪt ɪˈnəf tɪ kənˈtæməˌneɪt ðə signal.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: These are bold claims that require much more evidence. But the mere fact that such fundamental pillars are being discussed is staggering.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: these* ər boʊld kleɪmz ðət ˌrikˈwaɪər məʧ mɔr ˈɛvədəns. bət ðə mɪr fækt ðət səʧ ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl ˈpɪlərz ər biɪŋ dɪˈskəst ɪz ˈstægərˌɪŋ.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: So ok – what does all this mean? Right now there are furious battles going on.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: soʊ ˈoʊˈkeɪ –* wət dɪz ɔl ðɪs min? raɪt naʊ ðɛr ər fˈjʊriəs ˈbætəlz goʊɪŋ on.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Some scientists argue that these aren’t real cracks,
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: some* ˈsaɪəntɪsts ˈɑrgju ðət ðiz aren’t* ril cracks,

zozEm4f_dlw.text: but mirages that will disappear with time, or raw gems that will end up refining our theories.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: but* mərˈɑʒəz ðət wɪl ˌdɪsəˈpɪr wɪθ taɪm, ər rɑ ʤɛmz ðət wɪl ɛnd əp rɪˈfaɪnɪŋ ɑr theories.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Others are more radical and say we need completely new ideas. But whatever the case, the big picture is
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: others* ər mɔr ˈrædɪkəl ənd seɪ wi nid kəmˈplitli nu aɪˈdiəz. bət ˌwəˈtɛvər ðə keɪs, ðə bɪg ˈpɪkʧər is

zozEm4f_dlw.text: difficult to ignore – the sense of crisis is growing. And for the first time in ages,
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: difficult* tɪ ˌɪgˈnɔr –* ðə sɛns əv ˈkraɪsəs ɪz groʊɪŋ. ənd fər ðə fərst taɪm ɪn ages,

zozEm4f_dlw.text: we don’t really know what cosmology will look like when the dust settles. Which is amazing.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: we* don’t* ˈrɪli noʊ wət kɔzˈmɔləʤi wɪl lʊk laɪk wɪn ðə dəst ˈsɛtəlz. wɪʧ ɪz amazing.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Because in science, “crisis” doesn’t mean failure. It means that the machine is healthy and working.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: because* ɪn saɪəns, ““crisis”*” doesn’t* min ˈfeɪljər. ɪt minz ðət ðə məˈʃin ɪz ˈhɛlθi ənd working.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: Science doesn’t move in a straight line, but in cycles – periods of calm followed by sudden crises.
zozEm4f_dlw.ipa: science* doesn’t* muv ɪn ə streɪt laɪn, bət ɪn ˈsaɪkəlz –* ˈpɪriədz əv kɑm ˈfɑloʊd baɪ ˈsədən crises.

zozEm4f_dlw.text: When a crisis hits, experiments start giving results that don’t fit existing theories,

lrTRNbC3BDQ.text: Yes, exactly, exactly. So, Georgie, you said you are looking forward to being in the countryside.
I did. And 'looking forward to' means that you're 'excited about something you're going to do in the future'.
Exactly, yes. Like I'm looking forward to seeing my dog. Is your dog looking forward to seeing you?
Yes, I'm sure he is. So, Neil, I have quite clear plans. I know what I'm going to do this weekend.
And to talk about plans like that, we usually use 'be going to' or the present continuous.
So I said, "I'm going to visit my parents at the weekend".
And sometimes we shorten 'going to' to 'gonna' when we're speaking quickly.
Yeah, now, your plans are certain. Mine are not so certain, so I used 'might' and 'may'.

OK, let's look at the vocabulary we learned during the conversation.
We learned 'might', which we use to talk about possible plans. I might meet my friend.
'Going to' which we use to talk about future plans that are a little bit more certain.
So I'm going to visit my parents this weekend. And 'looking forward to'
which means 'to be pleased or excited that something is going to happen'.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: I usually have porridge. And what about lunch? What time do you have lunch, usually?
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: i* ˈjuʒəwəli hæv ˈpɔrəʤ. ənd wət əˈbaʊt lənʧ? wət taɪm du ju hæv lənʧ, usually?

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Mm, I normally have lunch at about one o'clock. What about you?
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: mm*, aɪ ˈnɔrməli hæv lənʧ æt əˈbaʊt wən əˈklɑk. wət əˈbaʊt you?

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Yeah, the same. I don't like it at 12. That's too early.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: yeah*, ðə seɪm. aɪ doʊnt laɪk ɪt æt 12 ðæts tu early.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: But by two, I'm getting a little bit hungry and angry at the same time!
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: but* baɪ tu, əm ˈgɪtɪŋ ə ˈlɪtəl bɪt ˈhəŋgri ənd ˈæŋgri æt ðə seɪm time!

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: There's a word for that, Neil. So if you're hungry and angry, we can combine them and say 'hangry'.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: there's* ə wərd fər ðət, nil. soʊ ɪf jʊr ˈhəŋgri ənd ˈæŋgri, wi kən ˈkɑmbaɪn ðɛm ənd seɪ 'hangry'.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Yeah. So it's important to have regular meal times, so you don't get hangry.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: yeah*. soʊ ɪts ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt tɪ hæv ˈrɛgjələr mil taɪmz, soʊ ju doʊnt gɪt hangry.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: That's true. Now you've just reminded me of another word which is 'brunch'.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: that's* tru. naʊ juv ʤɪst riˈmaɪndɪd mi əv əˈnəðər wərd wɪʧ ɪz 'brunch'.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Now, that's a combination of the words 'breakfast' and 'lunch'. Do you ever have brunch, Neil?
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: now*, ðæts ə ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv ðə wərdz 'ˈbrɛkfəst' ənd 'lənʧ'. du ju ˈɛvər hæv brənʧ, neil?

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: I love brunch, especially at weekends.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: i* ləv brənʧ, əˈspɛʃəli æt weekends.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Yeah. When I was at university, I used to have brunch pretty much every day.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: yeah*. wɪn aɪ wɑz æt ˌjunəˈvərsəti, aɪ juzd tɪ hæv brənʧ ˈprɪti məʧ ˈɛvəri day.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: So I would meet up with my friends
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: so* aɪ wʊd mit əp wɪθ maɪ friends

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: and we would go to a café and we would have brunch.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: and* wi wʊd goʊ tɪ ə café*é ənd wi wʊd hæv brənʧ.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Beth, you said that you used to have brunch at university.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: bɛθ, ju sɛd ðət ju juzd tɪ hæv brənʧ æt university.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Used to — what's that mean? Well, 'used to' is used to talk about a habit in the past,
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: used* tɪ —* wəts ðət min? wɛl, 'juzd tɪ' ɪz juzd tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt ə ˈhæbət ɪn ðə pæst,

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: so something you did regularly in the past and don't do now.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: so* ˈsəmθɪŋ ju dɪd ˈrɛgjələrli ɪn ðə pæst ənd doʊnt du now.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Now, another way of talking about past habits is to use would.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: now*, əˈnəðər weɪ əv ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt pæst ˈhæbəts ɪz tɪ juz would.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: So if you remember, I said I would message my friends and we would have brunch.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: so* ɪf ju rɪˈmɛmbər, aɪ sɛd aɪ wʊd ˈmɛsɪʤ maɪ frɛndz ənd wi wʊd hæv brunch.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: So 'used to' and 'would' are both used to talk about past habits.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: so* 'juzd tɪ' ənd 'wʊd' ər boʊθ juzd tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt pæst habits.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Do you ever miss a meal? No. I think when I was at school, many years ago,
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: do* ju ˈɛvər mɪs ə mil? noʊ. aɪ θɪŋk wɪn aɪ wɑz æt skul, ˈmɛni jɪrz ago,

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: I used to miss breakfast, because I wanted to stay in bed. What about you?
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: i* juzd tɪ mɪs ˈbrɛkfəst, bɪˈkəz aɪ ˈwɔntɪd tɪ steɪ ɪn bɛd. wət əˈbaʊt you?

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: I don't like to miss meals, because, like I said, I get hangry.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: i* doʊnt laɪk tɪ mɪs milz, bɪˈkəz, laɪk aɪ sɛd, aɪ gɪt hangry.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: But I guess I also used to miss breakfast when I was a teenager
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: but* aɪ gɛs aɪ ˈɔlsoʊ juzd tɪ mɪs ˈbrɛkfəst wɪn aɪ wɑz ə teenager

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: and I needed to sleep for a huge amount of time every night and get up really late.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: and* aɪ ˈnidɪd tɪ slip fər ə juʤ əˈmaʊnt əv taɪm ˈɛvəri naɪt ənd gɪt əp ˈrɪli late.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Yeah, I think it's quite normal and quite interesting
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: yeah*, aɪ θɪŋk ɪts kwaɪt ˈnɔrməl ənd kwaɪt interesting

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: how your habits change as you get older.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: how* jʊr ˈhæbəts ʧeɪnʤ ɛz ju gɪt older.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: For example, missing meals and that sort of thing.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: for* ɪgˈzæmpəl, ˈmɪsɪŋ milz ənd ðət sɔrt əv thing.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Mm.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: mm*.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Right, let's recap the vocabulary we learned during the conversation.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: raɪt, lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wi ˈlərnɪd ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə conversation.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: We learnt 'routine', 'the time and way we usually do something'.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: we* lərnt 'ruˈtin', 'ðə taɪm ənd weɪ wi ˈjuʒəwəli du something'.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: We heard 'used to' which we use to talk about 'a habit in the past'
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: we* hərd 'juzd tɪ' wɪʧ wi juz tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt 'ə ˈhæbət ɪn ðə past'

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: and 'would', which we can also use to talk about past habits. And my favourite 'brunch',
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: and* 'wʊd', wɪʧ wi kən ˈɔlsoʊ juz tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt pæst ˈhæbəts. ənd maɪ ˈfeɪvərɪt 'brunch',

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: which is 'eating breakfast and lunch' at the same time'.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: which* ɪz 'ˈitɪŋ ˈbrɛkfəst ənd lənʧ' æt ðə seɪm time'.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: We also heard a really new word in English,
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: we* ˈɔlsoʊ hərd ə ˈrɪli nu wərd ɪn english,

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: which is a combination of two words, 'hungry' and 'angry'.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: which* ɪz ə ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv tu wərdz, 'ˈhəŋgri' ənd 'angry'.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: It's 'hangry'.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: it's* 'hangry'.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Thanks for joining us on Real Easy English. Visit our website
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: thanks* fər ˈʤɔɪnɪŋ ˈjuˈɛs ɔn ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ. ˈvɪzɪt ɑr website

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: for more activities and courses to help you with your English.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: for* mɔr ækˈtɪvɪtiz ənd ˈkɔrsɪz tɪ hɛlp ju wɪθ jʊr english.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: In the next episode we'll be talking more about our hobbies. See you then! Bye.
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: in* ðə nɛkst ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd wɪl bi ˈtɔkɪŋ mɔr əˈbaʊt ɑr ˈhɑbiz. si ju ðɛn! bye.

sJ7sRSK2in4.text: Goodbye!
sJ7sRSK2in4.ipa: goodbye*!

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Hello and welcome to Real Easy English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: hɛˈloʊ ənd ˈwɛlkəm tɪ ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm neil.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: And I'm Georgie. In this podcast we have real conversations in easy English about a different topic each week.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: and* əm ˈʤɔrʤi. ɪn ðɪs ˈpɔdˌkæst wi hæv ril ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz ɪn ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ əˈbaʊt ə ˈdɪfərənt ˈtɑpɪk iʧ week.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: And you can read along with the podcast using the script on our website:
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: and* ju kən rɛd əˈlɔŋ wɪθ ðə ˈpɔdˌkæst ˈjuzɪŋ ðə skrɪpt ɔn ɑr website:

0UIcwcYrPek.text: BBC Learning English dot com.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: bbc* ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ dɑt com.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Hi, Neil. How are things? Very good, thank you. How are you, Georgie? Yes, I'm pretty good, thank you, as well.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: hi*, nil. haʊ ər θɪŋz? ˈvɛri gʊd, θæŋk ju. haʊ ər ju, ˈʤɔrʤi? jɛs, əm ˈprɪti gʊd, θæŋk ju, ɛz well.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: So, Neil, what are we talking about today?
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: soʊ, nil, wət ər wi ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt today?

0UIcwcYrPek.text: We are talking about animals. We'll be talking about pets.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: we* ər ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt ˈænəməlz. wɪl bi ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt pets.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: OK, Neil. So do you have any pets? No, I don't have any pets, but I know you do, Georgie.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: ok*, nil. soʊ du ju hæv ˈɛni pɛts? noʊ, aɪ doʊnt hæv ˈɛni pɛts, bət aɪ noʊ ju du, georgie.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Yes, I do. I have a black dog called Rafi. — Rafi? — Yeah. Did you choose the name?
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: yes*, aɪ du. aɪ hæv ə blæk dɔg kɔld ˈrɑfi. —* ˈrɑfi? —* jæ. dɪd ju ʧuz ðə name?

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Partly, but it was a decision we made as a family. Mm. Did you have pets as a child?
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: partly*, bət ɪt wɑz ə dɪˈsɪʒən wi meɪd ɛz ə ˈfæməli. mm*. dɪd ju hæv pɛts ɛz ə child?

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Yes, I did. We used to have cats, actually, we started with cats
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: yes*, aɪ dɪd. wi juzd tɪ hæv kæts, ˈæˌkʧuəli, wi ˈstɑrtɪd wɪθ cats

0UIcwcYrPek.text: and then we got a dog and then we also had hamsters. What about you, Neil? Did you have pets?
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: and* ðɛn wi gɑt ə dɔg ənd ðɛn wi ˈɔlsoʊ hæd ˈhæmstərz. wət əˈbaʊt ju, nil? dɪd ju hæv pets?

0UIcwcYrPek.text: No, we didn't have pets when I was young. Oh, I did have a goldfish called Gilbert.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: no*, wi ˈdɪdənt hæv pɛts wɪn aɪ wɑz jəŋ. oʊ, aɪ dɪd hæv ə ˈgoʊldˌfɪʃ kɔld gilbert.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: But then, later on, when I was a teenager, my parents got a cat and a dog. — Wow. — Mm.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: but* ðɛn, ˈleɪtər ɔn, wɪn aɪ wɑz ə ˈtiˌneɪʤər, maɪ ˈpɛrənts gɑt ə kæt ənd ə dɔg. —* waʊ. —* mm.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Did you have a good relationship with Gilbert? I don't remember Gilbert, sadly.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: did* ju hæv ə gʊd riˈleɪʃənˌʃɪp wɪθ ˈgɪlbərt? aɪ doʊnt rɪˈmɛmbər ˈgɪlbərt, sadly.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: But we had a cat that I liked very much and I've never really liked dogs, I'm afraid.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: bət wi hæd ə kæt ðət aɪ laɪkt ˈvɛri məʧ ənd aɪv ˈnɛvər ˈrɪli laɪkt dɔgz, əm afraid.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: So, Neil, you don't have any pets now
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: so*, nil, ju doʊnt hæv ˈɛni pɛts now

0UIcwcYrPek.text: and you said you don't like dogs, but will you ever get one? No, Georgie, I don't think so.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: and* ju sɛd ju doʊnt laɪk dɔgz, bət wɪl ju ˈɛvər gɪt wən? noʊ, ˈʤɔrʤi, aɪ doʊnt θɪŋk so.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: I will never get a dog. That's OK. I mean, they're not for everyone. What was your cat like?
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: i* wɪl ˈnɛvər gɪt ə dɔg. ðæts ˈoʊˈkeɪ. aɪ min, ðɛr nɑt fər ˈɛvriˌwən. wət wɑz jʊr kæt like?

0UIcwcYrPek.text: The cat made me feel very calm, because she sat on my lap and purred.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: the* kæt meɪd mi fil ˈvɛri kɑm, bɪˈkəz ʃi sæt ɔn maɪ læp ənd purred.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: You just used the word purr. Can you explain what that is?
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: you* ʤɪst juzd ðə wərd pər. kən ju ɪkˈspleɪn wət ðət is?

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Yeah. 'Purring' is 'the noise that cats make'. Not miaow. Miaow is...
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: yeah*. 'pərɪŋ' ɪz 'ðə nɔɪz ðət kæts meɪk'. nɑt miaow*. miaow* is...

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Purring is... And the cat made that noise and made me feel calm.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: purring* ɪz... ənd ðə kæt meɪd ðət nɔɪz ənd meɪd mi fil calm.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Neil, that was a really good purr sound. I would say it was purr-fect!
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: neil*, ðət wɑz ə ˈrɪli gʊd pər saʊnd. aɪ wʊd seɪ ɪt wɑz purr-fect!

0UIcwcYrPek.text: So, Georgie, what was your first dog like? My first dog was really lovely.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: so*, ˈʤɔrʤi, wət wɑz jʊr fərst dɔg laɪk? maɪ fərst dɔg wɑz ˈrɪli lovely.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: I actually used to say that she was my best friend. And we actually have a common expression to describe dogs,
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: i* ˈæˌkʧuəli juzd tɪ seɪ ðət ʃi wɑz maɪ bɛst frɛnd. ənd wi ˈæˌkʧuəli hæv ə ˈkɑmən ɪkˈsprɛʃən tɪ dɪˈskraɪb dogs,

0UIcwcYrPek.text: which is man's best friend. 'Man' here means 'human', so human's best friend.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: which* ɪz mænz bɛst frɛnd. 'mæn' hir minz 'ˈjumən', soʊ ˈhjumənz bɛst friend.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: That's because dogs are usually quite loyal.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: that's* bɪˈkəz dɔgz ər ˈjuʒəwəli kwaɪt loyal.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: OK, so, Georgie, we've talked about dogs and cats, those kind of pets,
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: ok*, soʊ, ˈʤɔrʤi, wiv tɔkt əˈbaʊt dɔgz ənd kæts, ðoʊz kaɪnd əv pets,

0UIcwcYrPek.text: and hamsters and goldfish.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: and* ˈhæmstərz ənd ˈgoʊldˌfɪʃ.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: But have you ever thought about getting an exotic pet like a snake?
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: but* hæv ju ˈɛvər θɔt əˈbaʊt ˈgɪtɪŋ ən ɪgˈzɑtɪk pɛt laɪk ə snake?

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Erm, no, not really. When I think of pets, I think of fluffy animals with fur.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: erm*, noʊ, nɑt ˈrɪli. wɪn aɪ θɪŋk əv pɛts, aɪ θɪŋk əv ˈfləfi ˈænəməlz wɪθ fur.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: But I do have a friend that got a scorpion recently, which I really don't understand.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: but* aɪ du hæv ə frɛnd ðət gɑt ə ˈskɔrpiən ˈrisəntli, wɪʧ aɪ ˈrɪli doʊnt understand.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: Would you ever get an exotic pet?
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: would* ju ˈɛvər gɪt ən ɪgˈzɑtɪk pet?

0UIcwcYrPek.text: No, absolutely not. I think I would be scared of an exotic pet,
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: no*, ˌæbsəˈlutli nɑt. aɪ θɪŋk aɪ wʊd bi skɛrd əv ən ɪgˈzɑtɪk pet,

0UIcwcYrPek.text: — to be honest. — Yes. Yeah. — They just seem dangerous. — Mm.
Let's recap the vocabulary we learned during the conversation.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: — tɪ bi ˈɑnəst. —* jɛs. jæ. —* ðeɪ ʤɪst sim ˈdeɪnʤərəs. —* mm.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: We had 'ever', which means 'at any time'.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: let's* ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wi ˈlərnɪd ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə conversation.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: And you asked me Georgie, will I ever get a dog?
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: we* hæd 'ˈɛvər', wɪʧ minz 'æt ˈɛni time'.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: We've had 'never', which means 'not at any time'. So, Neil, you said you will never get a dog.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: we've* hæd 'ˈnɛvər', wɪʧ minz 'nɑt æt ˈɛni taɪm'. soʊ, nil, ju sɛd ju wɪl ˈnɛvər gɪt ə dɔg.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: We heard 'man's best friend', which is 'a common expression to describe dogs'.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: we've* hæd 'ˈnɛvər', wɪʧ minz 'nɑt æt ˈɛni taɪm'. soʊ, nil, ju sɛd ju wɪl ˈnɛvər gɪt ə dog.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: And 'purring', 'the noise a cat makes when it's happy'.
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: we* hərd 'mænz bɛst frɛnd', wɪʧ ɪz 'ə ˈkɑmən ɪkˈsprɛʃən tɪ dɪˈskraɪb dogs'.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: That's it for this episode of Real Easy English. You can find
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: and* 'pərɪŋ', 'ðə nɔɪz ə kæt meɪks wɪn ɪts happy'.

0UIcwcYrPek.text: See you then, goodbye. Bye!
0UIcwcYrPek.ipa: that's* ɪt fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd əv ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ. ju kən find

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: In the 1960s, the US planned to nuke Israel with hundreds of thermonuclear bombs. And Panama.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: in* ðə 1960s*, ðə ˈjuˈɛs plænd tɪ nuk ˈɪzriəl wɪθ ˈhənərdz əv ˌθərmoʊˈnukliər bɑmz. ənd panama.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: And Alaska. All in the name of progress, driven by a mad scientist,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: and* əˈlæskə. ɔl ɪn ðə neɪm əv ˈprɑˌgrɛs, ˈdrɪvən baɪ ə mæd scientist,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: to use the most destructive of weapons to… build things. Excavate canals. Carve out harbors.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: to* juz ðə moʊst dɪˈstrəktɪv əv ˈwɛpənz to…*… bɪld θɪŋz. ˈɛkskəˌveɪt kəˈnælz. kɑrv aʊt harbors.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Tap oil and gas. This was Project Plowshare.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: tap* ɔɪl ənd gæs. ðɪs wɑz ˈprɑʤɛkt plowshare.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: It kept the US busy for two decades, burned hundreds of millions of dollars and exploded
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: it* kɛpt ðə ˈjuˈɛs ˈbɪzi fər tu ˈdɛkeɪdz, bərnd ˈhənərdz əv ˈmɪljənz əv ˈdɔlərz ənd exploded

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: dozens of nukes for a bright future, in the most feverish years of the Cold War,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: dozens* əv nuks fər ə braɪt fˈjuʧər, ɪn ðə moʊst ˈfivərɪʃ jɪrz əv ðə koʊld wɔr,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: when the world got terrifyingly close to blowing itself up.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: wɪn ðə wərld gɑt terrifyingly* kloʊz tɪ bloʊɪŋ ˌɪtˈsɛlf up.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Nukes for Peace
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: nukes* fər peace

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Our story begins with the brilliant Hungarian physicist Edward Teller – one of the key
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: our* ˈstɔri bɪˈgɪnz wɪθ ðə ˈbrɪljənt həŋˈgɛriən ˈfɪzɪsɪst ˈɛdwərd ˈtɛlər –* wən əv ðə key

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: scientists of the Manhattan Project, the colossal effort that led to the first nuclear bomb.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: scientists* əv ðə mænˈhætən ˈprɑʤɛkt, ðə kəˈlɑsəl ˈɛfərt ðət lɛd tɪ ðə fərst ˈnukliər bomb.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But almost from the beginning, he dreamt of destroying the world much harder.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* ˈɔlˌmoʊst frəm ðə bɪˈgɪnɪŋ, hi drɛmt əv dɪˈstrɔɪɪŋ ðə wərld məʧ harder.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: “Traditional” nukes, like the Hiroshima and Nagasaki ones, release energy by splitting heavy atoms, like uranium.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: “traditional”*” nuks, laɪk ðə ˌhɪˈroʊʃɪmə ənd ˌnɑgɑˈsɑki wənz, riˈlis ˈɛnərʤi baɪ ˈsplɪtɪŋ ˈhɛvi ˈætəmz, laɪk uranium.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But these are rare, expensive, and the resulting bombs are difficult to scale up.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* ðiz ər rɛr, ɪkˈspɛnsɪv, ənd ðə rɪˈzəltɪŋ bɑmz ər ˈdɪfəkəlt tɪ skeɪl up.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: So Teller lobbied for a different approach – unleash the nuclear fire by smashing together hydrogen atoms.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: so* ˈtɛlər ˈlɑbid fər ə ˈdɪfərənt əˈproʊʧ –* ənˈliʃ ðə ˈnukliər faɪər baɪ sˈmæʃɪŋ təˈgɛðər ˈhaɪdrəʤən atoms.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: That would allow for bombs thousands of times more powerful. The problem was technically hard, but in the 1950s Teller solved it.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: that* wʊd əˈlaʊ fər bɑmz ˈθaʊzənz əv taɪmz mɔr ˈpaʊərfəl. ðə ˈprɑbləm wɑz ˈtɛknɪkəli hɑrd, bət ɪn ðə 1950s* ˈtɛlər sɑlvd it.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The terrifying new thermonuclear bomb made Hiroshima and Nagasaki look like firecrackers.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* ˈtɛrəˌfaɪɪŋ nu ˌθərmoʊˈnukliər bɔm meɪd ˌhɪˈroʊʃɪmə ənd ˌnɑgɑˈsɑki lʊk laɪk firecrackers.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Nuclear fire strong enough to end human civilization itself.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: ˈnukliər faɪər strɔŋ ɪˈnəf tɪ ɛnd ˈjumən ˌsɪvəlɪˈzeɪʃən itself.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The Soviet Union followed quickly and a new madness for destruction took over the world.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* ˈsoʊviˌɛt ˈjunjən ˈfɑloʊd kˈwɪkli ənd ə nu ˈmædnəs fər dɪˈstrəkʃən tʊk ˈoʊvər ðə world.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Teller was having the time of his life as he got to chase his nukiest dreams,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: teller* wɑz ˈhævɪŋ ðə taɪm əv hɪz laɪf ɛz hi gɑt tɪ ʧeɪs hɪz nukiest* dreams,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: like a bomb so powerful that it would wipe out civilization
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: like* ə bɔm soʊ ˈpaʊərfəl ðət ɪt wʊd waɪp aʊt civilization

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: in one single strike – a real thing the US actually started working on.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: in* wən ˈsɪŋgəl straɪk –* ə ril θɪŋ ðə ˈjuˈɛs ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈstɑrtɪd ˈwərkɪŋ on.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: We told this story before, if you want to watch it. And as the perfectly sane man he was,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: we* toʊld ðɪs ˈstɔri ˌbiˈfɔr, ɪf ju wɔnt tɪ wɔʧ ɪt. ənd ɛz ðə ˈpərfəktli seɪn mæn hi was,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Teller had other great ideas, like nuking the Moon to show the commies who’s boss.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: teller* hæd ˈəðər greɪt aɪˈdiəz, laɪk nuking* ðə mun tɪ ʃoʊ ðə ˈkɑmiz who’s* boss.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: And if H-bombs could destroy the planet… maybe they could also reshape it?
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: and* ɪf h-bombs* kʊd dɪˈstrɔɪ ðə planet…*… ˈmeɪbi ðeɪ kʊd ˈɔlsoʊ riˈʃeɪp it?

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The logic wasn’t entirely unhinged. Humans had been using explosives for mining and engineering for centuries.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* ˈlɑʤɪk wasn’t* ɪnˈtaɪərli ənˈhɪnʤd. ˈjumənz hæd bɪn ˈjuzɪŋ ɪkˈsploʊsɪvz fər ˈmaɪnɪŋ ənd ˈɛnʤəˈnɪrɪŋ fər centuries.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: And H-bombs offered the same service with much more enthusiasm at a decent cost.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: and* h-bombs* ˈɔfərd ðə seɪm ˈsərvɪs wɪθ məʧ mɔr ɪnˈθuziˌæzəm æt ə ˈdisənt cost.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Why not use them to dig ditches and make holes at a planetary scale?
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: why* nɑt juz ðɛm tɪ dɪg ˈdɪʧɪz ənd meɪk hoʊlz æt ə ˈplænəˌtɛri scale?

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The idea of "nukes for peace" was also pretty convenient.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* aɪˈdiə əv "nuks fər pis" wɑz ˈɔlsoʊ ˈprɪti convenient.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: With public opinion increasingly opposed to the arms race and ever more terrified by WW3,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: with* ˈpəblɪk əˈpɪnjən ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋgli əˈpoʊzd tɪ ðə ɑrmz reɪs ənd ˈɛvər mɔr ˈtɛrəˌfaɪd baɪ ww3,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: nuclear engineering was a way to keep on testing nuclear weapons without saying you were testing nuclear weapons.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: nuclear* ˈɛnʤəˈnɪrɪŋ wɑz ə weɪ tɪ kip ɔn ˈtɛstɪŋ ˈnukliər ˈwɛpənz wɪˈθaʊt seɪɪŋ ju wər ˈtɛstɪŋ ˈnukliər ˈwɛpənz.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: So in 1957, the US launched Project Plowshare, a bold plan to use nuclear explosions for peaceful engineering.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: soʊ ɪn 1957 ðə ˈjuˈɛs lɔnʧt ˈprɑʤɛkt ˈplaʊˌʃeɪr, ə boʊld plæn tɪ juz ˈnukliər ɪkˈsploʊʒənz fər ˈpisfəl engineering.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: And who would be put at the helm? Exactly.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: and* hu wʊd bi pʊt æt ðə hɛlm? exactly.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Cutting Through Continents
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: cutting* θru continents

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: In 1956 Egypt had just nationalized the Suez Canal, a vital artery for global trade and oil delivery to Europe.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: in* 1956 ˈiʤɪpt hæd ʤɪst ˈnæʃənəˌlaɪzd ðə ˈsuɛz kəˈnæl, ə ˈvaɪtəl ˈɑrtəri fər ˈgloʊbəl treɪd ənd ɔɪl dɪˈlɪvəri tɪ europe.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: So one of the first ideas of the new Build-With-Nukes doctrine was to blast open a new one through the Israeli desert.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: so* wən əv ðə fərst aɪˈdiəz əv ðə nu build-with-nukes* ˈdɔktərɪn wɑz tɪ blæst ˈoʊpən ə nu wən θru ðə ˌɪzˈreɪli desert.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: On the other side of the world, the Panama Canal had long been in need of an upgrade.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: on* ðə ˈəðər saɪd əv ðə wərld, ðə ˈpænəˌmɑ kəˈnæl hæd lɔŋ bɪn ɪn nid əv ən upgrade.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Scientists explored extension routes and found they would need to remove
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: scientists* ɪkˈsplɔrd ɪkˈstɛnʃən rʊts ənd faʊnd ðeɪ wʊd nid tɪ remove

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: 1.2 billion cubic meters of earth – 500 Great Pyramids of Giza.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: 1.2* ˈbɪljən ˈkjubɪk ˈmitərz əv ərθ –* 500 greɪt ˈpɪrəmɪdz əv giza.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: A monumental task that would cost 6 billion dollars if done with shovels, but only 3.1 billion with nukes.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: a* ˌmɑnjəˈmɛnəl tæsk ðət wʊd kɔst 6 ˈbɪljən ˈdɔlərz ɪf dən wɪθ ˈʃəvəlz, bət ˈoʊnli 3.1* ˈbɪljən wɪθ nukes.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Time to blast open a Pan-Atomic Canal! But even Teller understood that, before nuking through continents, you should start small.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: time* tɪ blæst ˈoʊpən ə pan-atomic* kəˈnæl! bət ˈivɪn ˈtɛlər ˌəndərˈstʊd ðət, ˌbiˈfɔr nuking* θru ˈkɑntənənts, ju ʃʊd stɑrt small.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: So the first miracle of Project Plowshare would be a new harbor in Alaska.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: so* ðə fərst ˈmɪrəkəl əv ˈprɑʤɛkt ˈplaʊˌʃeɪr wʊd bi ə nu ˈhɑrbər ɪn alaska.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: In just a few glorious milliseconds, five nukes with the combined punch of 170 Hiroshima bombs
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: in* ʤɪst ə fju ˈglɔriəs ˈmɪlɪˌsɛkəndz, faɪv nuks wɪθ ðə kəmˈbaɪnd pənʧ əv 170 ˌhɪˈroʊʃɪmə bombs

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: would carve out a 500 - by 200-meter trench, opening the region to commerce and prosperity.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: would* kɑrv aʊt ə 500  baɪ 200-meter* trɛnʧ, ˈoʊpənɪŋ ðə ˈriʤən tɪ ˈkɑmərs ənd prosperity.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The local communities weren’t thrilled at the idea, but Teller insisted that
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* ˈloʊkəl kəmˈjunɪtiz weren’t* θrɪld æt ðə aɪˈdiə, bət ˈtɛlər ˌɪnˈsɪstɪd that

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: the dangers of nuke engineering had been “greatly exaggerated”. The major concern was radioactive contamination.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* ˈdeɪnʤərz əv nuk ˈɛnʤəˈnɪrɪŋ hæd bɪn ““greatly* exaggerated”*”. ðə ˈmeɪʤər kənˈsərn wɑz ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv kənˌtæməˈneɪʃən.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Canals and harbors were great, but nobody wanted them glowing with radioactivity for thousands of years.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: kəˈnælz ənd ˈhɑrbərz wər greɪt, bət ˈnoʊˌbɑˌdi ˈwɔntɪd ðɛm gloʊɪŋ wɪθ ˌreɪdioʊˌækˈtɪvəˌti fər ˈθaʊzənz əv years.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Luckily, Teller had a plan: nuking the land from below. In a nutshell, nuclear excavation works like this.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: luckily*, ˈtɛlər hæd ə plæn: nuking* ðə lænd frəm bɪˈloʊ. ɪn ə ˈnətˌʃɛl, ˈnukliər ˌɛkskəˈveɪʃən wərks laɪk this.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: You drill a hole a few hundred meters deep and place the bomb at the bottom.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: you* drɪl ə hoʊl ə fju ˈhənərd ˈmitərz dip ənd pleɪs ðə bɔm æt ðə bottom.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: When it goes off, the blast hollows out a cavern in the rock and flash-melts its walls into magma.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: when* ɪt goʊz ɔf, ðə blæst ˈhɑloʊz aʊt ə ˈkævərn ɪn ðə rɑk ənd flash-melts* ɪts wɔlz ˈɪntu magma.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The molten rock pools at the bottom, the ceiling collapses,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* ˈmoʊltən rɑk pulz æt ðə ˈbɑtəm, ðə ˈsilɪŋ collapses,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: and the collapse ripples upwards until it reaches the floor, where it forms a crater.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: and* ðə kəˈlæps ˈrɪpəlz ˈəpwərdz ənˈtɪl ɪt ˈriʧɪz ðə flɔr, wɛr ɪt fɔrmz ə crater.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The result is a massive hole on top with the radioactive debris sealed under hundreds of thousands of tons of rock.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* rɪˈzəlt ɪz ə ˈmæsɪv hoʊl ɔn tɔp wɪθ ðə ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv dəˈbri sild ˈəndər ˈhənərdz əv ˈθaʊzənz əv tənz əv rock.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: With the theory looking flawless on paper, the time had come to actually test it.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: with* ðə ˈθɪri ˈlʊkɪŋ ˈflɔləs ɔn ˈpeɪpər, ðə taɪm hæd kəm tɪ ˈæˌkʧuəli tɛst it.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But before the government bombs anything, let’s get some propaganda protection – just in case.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* ˌbiˈfɔr ðə ˈgəvərnmənt bɑmz ˈɛniˌθɪŋ, let’s* gɪt səm ˌprɑpəˈgændə prəˈtɛkʃən –* ʤɪst ɪn case.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Our partner Ground News is a website and app built to help
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: our* ˈpɑrtnər graʊnd nuz ɪz ə ˈwɛbˌsaɪt ənd æp bɪlt tɪ help

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: you think critically about the information you consume – a mission we fully support.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: you* θɪŋk ˈkrɪtɪkəli əˈbaʊt ðə ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ju kənˈsum –* ə ˈmɪʃən wi ˈfʊli support.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: They curate news articles from across the globe, adding context on political bias,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: they* ˈkjʊrət nuz ˈɑrtɪkəlz frəm əˈkrɔs ðə gloʊb, ˈædɪŋ ˈkɑntɛkst ɔn pəˈlɪtɪkəl bias,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: reliability, ownership, and summaries that highlight what each side is leaving out.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: reliability*, ˈoʊnərˌʃɪp, ənd ˈsəməriz ðət ˈhaɪˌlaɪt wət iʧ saɪd ɪz ˈlivɪŋ out.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: You can even compare headlines to see how bias shapes the narrative.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: you* kən ˈivɪn kəmˈpɛr ˈhɛˌdlaɪnz tɪ si haʊ baɪəs ʃeɪps ðə narrative.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Take this story about Finland planning to lift its ban on nuclear weapons.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: take* ðɪs ˈstɔri əˈbaʊt ˈfɪnˌlænd ˈplænɪŋ tɪ lɪft ɪts bæn ɔn ˈnukliər ˈwɛpənz.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Some frame it as a prudent response to the state of the world, while others warn of escalation.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: səm freɪm ɪt ɛz ə ˈprudənt rɪˈspɑns tɪ ðə steɪt əv ðə wərld, waɪl ˈəðərz wɔrn əv escalation.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Ground News also reveals "blind spots" – stories that only one side is covering,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: ground* nuz ˈɔlsoʊ rɪˈvilz "blaɪnd spɑts" –* ˈstɔriz ðət ˈoʊnli wən saɪd ɪz covering,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: showing you what your usual news feed is hiding. As information bubbles are becoming the norm,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: showing* ju wət jʊr ˈjuʒəwəl nuz fid ɪz ˈhaɪdɪŋ. ɛz ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ˈbəbəlz ər bɪˈkəmɪŋ ðə norm,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: thinking critically about the news is no longer optional. And Ground News makes it easier to do just that.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: thinking* ˈkrɪtɪkəli əˈbaʊt ðə nuz ɪz noʊ ˈlɔŋgər ˈɔpʃənəl. ənd graʊnd nuz meɪks ɪt ˈiziər tɪ du ʤɪst that.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: If you’d like to give them a try, go to ground.news/nutshell, or scan the QR code on the screen.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: if* you’d* laɪk tɪ gɪv ðɛm ə traɪ, goʊ tɪ ground.news/nutshell*, ər skæn ðə qr* koʊd ɔn ðə screen.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Using this link gives you 40% off an unlimited access subscription, and directly supports our channel.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: using* ðɪs lɪŋk gɪvz ju 40 ɔf ən ənˈlɪmɪtɪd ˈækˌsɛs səbˈskrɪpʃən, ənd dɪˈrɛkli səˈpɔrts ɑr channel.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: And now back to nuking for peace.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: and* naʊ bæk tɪ nuking* fər peace.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The Fiasco Begins
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* fiˈæskoʊ begins

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Plowshare debuted in 1961 in the desert of New Mexico.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: plowshare* ˈdeɪbjutɪd ɪn 1961 ɪn ðə ˈdɛzərt əv nu mexico.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: A nuke with the power of 3,100 tonnes of TNT was buried 360 meters deep, in the heart of a salt deposit.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: a* nuk wɪθ ðə paʊər əv 3,100* tənz əv tnt* wɑz ˈbɛrid 360 ˈmitərz dip, ɪn ðə hɑrt əv ə sɔlt deposit.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The goal was to explore a new idea to generate electricity – the explosion would melt the deposit
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* goʊl wɑz tɪ ɪkˈsplɔr ə nu aɪˈdiə tɪ ˈʤɛnərˌeɪt ɪˌlɛkˈtrɪsəti –* ðə ɪkˈsploʊʒən wʊd mɛlt ðə deposit

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: into a pond of red-hot, liquid salt, whose heat could be pumped up to power turbines.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: into* ə pɑnd əv red-hot*, ˈlɪkwɪd sɔlt, huz hit kʊd bi pəmpt əp tɪ paʊər turbines.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: A hybrid of fusion and geothermal power, brought to you by H-bombs. Above ground, hundreds of officials,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: a* ˈhaɪbrɪd əv fˈjuʒən ənd ˌʤioʊˈθərməl paʊər, brɔt tɪ ju baɪ h-bombs*. əˈbəv graʊnd, ˈhənərdz əv officials,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: scientists and international guests had gathered to witness the birth of a new era.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: scientists* ənd ˌɪnərˈnæʃənɑl gɛsts hæd ˈgæðərd tɪ ˈwɪtnəs ðə bərθ əv ə nu era.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Everyone held their breath as the countdown ticked.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: everyone* hɛld ðɛr brɛθ ɛz ðə ˈkaʊntˌdaʊn tɪkt.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: And then, with a distant thud, the ground shuddered.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: ənd ðɛn, wɪθ ə ˈdɪstənt θəd, ðə graʊnd shuddered.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Deep below, the explosion hollowed a cavern the size of over ten Olympic swimming pools.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: deep* bɪˈloʊ, ðə ɪkˈsploʊʒən ˈhɑloʊd ə ˈkævərn ðə saɪz əv ˈoʊvər tɛn oʊˈlɪmpɪk sˈwɪmɪŋ pools.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But Teller’s team had missed a tiny detail – the salt contained far more water than expected.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* teller’s* tim hæd mɪst ə ˈtaɪni ˈditeɪl –* ðə sɔlt kənˈteɪnd fɑr mɔr ˈwɔtər ðən expected.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Which turned into superheated steam that massively amplified the blast.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: which* tərnd ˈɪntu ˌsupərˈhitɪd stim ðət ˈmæsɪvli ˈæmpləˌfaɪd ðə blast.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The hole used to lower the bomb had been designed to collapse and seal itself after
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* hoʊl juzd tɪ loʊər ðə bɔm hæd bɪn dɪˈzaɪnd tɪ kəˈlæps ənd sil ˌɪtˈsɛlf after

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: the explosion, but the unexpectedly monstrous pressure broke through.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* ɪkˈsploʊʒən, bət ðə ˌənɪkˈspɛktɪdli ˈmɑnstrəs ˈprɛʃər broʊk through.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: So a few minutes after the blast, a plume of radioactive steam erupted from the
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: so* ə fju ˈmɪnəts ˈæftər ðə blæst, ə plum əv ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv stim ˌɪˈrəptɪd frəm the

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: shaft – damaging cameras and instruments and bathing everyone in a strangely itchy sauna.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: shaft* –* ˈdæmɪʤɪŋ ˈkæmərəz ənd ˈɪnstrəmənts ənd ˈbeɪðɪŋ ˈɛvriˌwən ɪn ə ˈstreɪnʤli ˈɪʧi sauna.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The US government tried their best to convince everyone that things hadn't been that bad.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* ˈjuˈɛs ˈgəvərnmənt traɪd ðɛr bɛst tɪ kənˈvɪns ˈɛvriˌwən ðət θɪŋz ˈhædənt bɪn ðət bad.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But for some reason, the masses had a problem with clouds of radioactive fallout, even from peaceful nukes.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* fər səm ˈrizən, ðə ˈmæsɪz hæd ə ˈprɑbləm wɪθ klaʊdz əv ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv ˈfɔˌlaʊt, ˈivɪn frəm ˈpisfəl nukes.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But that wouldn’t discourage Teller. So to show the world how cool H-bombs could really be,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* ðət wouldn’t* dɪˈskərɪʤ ˈtɛlər. soʊ tɪ ʃoʊ ðə wərld haʊ kul h-bombs* kʊd ˈrɪli be,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: he scheduled another test – one 30 times more peaceful.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: he* ˈskɛʤʊld əˈnəðər tɛst –* wən 30 taɪmz mɔr peaceful.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: In July 1962, while Cuban officials were visiting Moscow to negotiate
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: in* ˌʤuˈlaɪ 1962 waɪl ˈkjubən əˈfɪʃəlz wər ˈvɪzɪtɪŋ ˈmɔˌskaʊ tɪ negotiate

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: a missile deal that would almost spark WW3 three months later, a thermonuclear
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: a* ˈmɪsəl dil ðət wʊd ˈɔlˌmoʊst spɑrk ww3* θri mənθs ˈleɪtər, ə thermonuclear

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: bomb worth 7 Hiroshima nukes exploded 200 meters underground in the Nevada desert.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: bomb* wərθ 7 ˌhɪˈroʊʃɪmə nuks ɪkˈsploʊdɪd 200 ˈmitərz ˈəndərˌgraʊnd ɪn ðə nəˈvɑdə ˈdɛzərt.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: This time the goal was to test nuclear excavation itself and check how big the crater could get.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: ðɪs taɪm ðə goʊl wɑz tɪ tɛst ˈnukliər ˌɛkskəˈveɪʃən ˌɪtˈsɛlf ənd ʧɛk haʊ bɪg ðə ˈkreɪtər kʊd get.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The blast displaced 12 million tonnes of soil and hollowed out a gargantuan
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* blæst dɪˈspleɪst 12 ˈmɪljən tənz əv sɔɪl ənd ˈhɑloʊd aʊt ə gargantuan

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: crater 100 meters deep and 400 meters across – the largest artificial crater in US history.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: crater* 100 ˈmitərz dip ənd 400 ˈmitərz əˈkrɔs –* ðə ˈlɑrʤəst ˌɑrtəˈfɪʃəl ˈkreɪtər ɪn ˈjuˈɛs history.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But digging with nukes is tricky. Bury the bomb too deep, and you’ll only get a disappointing dent.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* ˈdɪgɪŋ wɪθ nuks ɪz ˈtrɪki. ˈbɛri ðə bɔm tu dip, ənd you’ll* ˈoʊnli gɪt ə ˌdɪsəˈpɔɪnɪŋ dent.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Bury it too shallow, and you risk launching a radioactive cloud into the sky. Which was exactly what happened.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: bury* ɪt tu ˈʃæloʊ, ənd ju rɪsk ˈlɔnʧɪŋ ə ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv klaʊd ˈɪntu ðə skaɪ. wɪʧ wɑz ɪgˈzæktli wət happened.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Someone had miscalculated the depth of the explosion and a plume of radioactivity punched through.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: someone* hæd mɪˈskælkjəˌleɪtɪd ðə dɛpθ əv ðə ɪkˈsploʊʒən ənd ə plum əv ˌreɪdioʊˌækˈtɪvəˌti pənʧt through.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Fallout was detected as far as South Dakota and Illinois,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: fallout* wɑz dɪˈtɛktɪd ɛz fɑr ɛz saʊθ dəˈkoʊtə ənd illinois,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: and panic broke out in Utah when radioactive iodine was found in milk.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: and* ˈpænɪk broʊk aʊt ɪn ˈjuˌtɔ wɪn ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv ˈaɪəˌdaɪn wɑz faʊnd ɪn milk.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: It turned into one of the dirtiest tests on US soil ever.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: it* tərnd ˈɪntu wən əv ðə ˈdərtiəst tɛsts ɔn ˈjuˈɛs sɔɪl ever.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Just One More Nuke Bro
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: just* wən mɔr nuk bro

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The years that followed saw more peaceful explosions. And peaceful fallout. Meanwhile, Teller's proposals got weirder.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* jɪrz ðət ˈfɑloʊd sɔ mɔr ˈpisfəl ɪkˈsploʊʒənz. ənd ˈpisfəl ˈfɔˌlaʊt. ˈminˌwaɪl, ˈtɛlərz prəˈpoʊzəlz gɑt weirder.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Liquify tar sands in Canada, with nukes. Blast a highway through the Sierra Nevada, with nukes.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: liquify* tɑr sændz ɪn ˈkænədə, wɪθ nuks. blæst ə ˈhaɪˌweɪ θru ðə siˈɛrə nəˈvɑdə, wɪθ nukes.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Connect rivers in Mississippi, with nukes. But the crown jewel of nuclear dreams was still the Pan-Atomic Canal.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: connect* ˈrɪvərz ɪn ˌmɪsɪˈsɪpi, wɪθ nuks. bət ðə kraʊn ʤuəl əv ˈnukliər drimz wɑz stɪl ðə pan-atomic* canal.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: A legion of scientists worked on it and plans got disturbingly far.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: a* ˈliʤən əv ˈsaɪəntɪsts wərkt ɔn ɪt ənd plænz gɑt dɪˈstərbɪŋli far.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: One of the top contenders was Route 17, an 80-km trench across pristine
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: one* əv ðə tɔp kənˈtɛndərz wɑz rut 17 ən 80-km* trɛnʧ əˈkrɔs ˈprɪstin

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Panamanian jungle that was short, flat, pretty empty and perfectly nukable.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: ˌpænəˈmeɪniən ˈʤəŋgəl ðət wɑz ʃɔrt, flæt, ˈprɪti ˈɛmti ənd ˈpərfəktli nukable.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: 250 bombs with the power of 8,000 Hiroshimas would do the job.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: 250* bɑmz wɪθ ðə paʊər əv 8,000* hiroshimas* wʊd du ðə job.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: 43,000 people across 17,000 square kilometers would still have to be evacuated. You know, just in case.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: 43,000* ˈpipəl əˈkrɔs 17,000* skwɛr ˈkɪləˌmitərz wʊd stɪl hæv tɪ bi ɪˈvækjəˌweɪtɪd. ju noʊ, ʤɪst ɪn case.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: And shockwaves might shatter windows as far as Costa Rica and Colombia.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: and* ˈʃɑkˌweɪvz maɪt ˈʃætər ˈwɪndoʊz ɛz fɑr ɛz ˈkɔstɑ ˈrikə ənd colombia.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But what were such details in the grand scheme of progress? So let’s rewind history… and press the button to see what
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* wət wər səʧ ˈditeɪlz ɪn ðə grænd skim əv ˈprɑˌgrɛs? soʊ let’s* ˈriwaɪnd history…*… ənd prɛs ðə ˈbətən tɪ si what

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: might have happened if Teller had got his way. Ready to push the button? 3… 2… 1…
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: might* hæv ˈhæpənd ɪf ˈtɛlər hæd gɑt hɪz weɪ. ˈrɛdi tɪ pʊʃ ðə ˈbətən? 3…* 2…* 1…

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: boom
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: boom

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The first round of nuclear salvos, close to both coasts obliterates hundreds of square kilometers of pristine rainforest in seconds.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* fərst raʊnd əv ˈnukliər ˈsælvoʊz, kloʊz tɪ boʊθ koʊsts əˈblɪtərˌeɪts ˈhənərdz əv skwɛr ˈkɪləˌmitərz əv ˈprɪstin ˈraɪnˌfɔrəst ɪn seconds.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: A roaring success. Unfortunately, a cloud of fallout catches the Pacific winds.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: a* ˈrɔrɪŋ səkˈsɛs. ənˈfɔrʧənətli, ə klaʊd əv ˈfɔˌlaʊt ˈkæʧɪz ðə pəˈsɪfɪk winds.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Weeks later, spikes of radiation are found over the Caribbean and radioactive iodine,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: weeks* ˈleɪtər, spaɪks əv ˌreɪdiˈeɪʃən ər faʊnd ˈoʊvər ðə ˌkɛrɪˈbiən ənd ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv iodine,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: cesium and strontium start to enter the food chains of both oceans. Tuna and sharks become radioactive.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: cesium* ənd ˈstrɑntiəm stɑrt tɪ ˈɛnər ðə fud ʧeɪnz əv boʊθ ˈoʊʃənz. ˈtunə ənd ʃɑrks bɪˈkəm radioactive.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: One option would be to stop. But to have a radioactive scar across the jungle,
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: one* ˈɔpʃən wʊd bi tɪ stɑp. bət tɪ hæv ə ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv skɑr əˈkrɔs ðə jungle,

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: contamination of two oceans… and no canal? Better push through.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: contamination* əv tu oceans…*… ənd noʊ kəˈnæl? ˈbɛtər pʊʃ through.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: So a second round of blasts goes off in the central Chucunaque River valley – only
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: so* ə ˈsɛkənd raʊnd əv blæsts goʊz ɔf ɪn ðə ˈsɛntrəl chucunaque* ˈrɪvər ˈvæli –* only

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: to find that the central part of the isthmus had far more clay than expected.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: to* faɪnd ðət ðə ˈsɛntrəl pɑrt əv ðə ˈɪsməs hæd fɑr mɔr kleɪ ðən ɪkˈspɛktɪd.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: As soon as the rainy season hits, landslides clog the new craters like a self-healing wound.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: ɛz sun ɛz ðə ˈreɪni ˈsizən hɪts, ˈlænsˌlaɪdz klɑg ðə nu ˈkreɪtərz laɪk ə self-healing* wound.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: The choices? Leave a mud-choked, glowing blister through the rainforest – or begin
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: the* ˈʧɔɪsɪz? liv ə mud-choked*, gloʊɪŋ ˈblɪstər θru ðə ˈraɪnˌfɔrəst –* ər begin

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: a colossal engineering nightmare just to keep the jungle from landsliding onto the canal.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: a* kəˈlɑsəl ˈɛnʤəˈnɪrɪŋ ˈnaɪtˌmɛr ʤɪst tɪ kip ðə ˈʤəŋgəl frəm landsliding* ˈɔntu ðə canal.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Not to mention the ecological apocalypse – and the devastating PR victory that 120
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: not* tɪ ˈmɛnʃən ðə ɛkəˈlɑʤɪkəl əˈpɑkəˌlɪps –* ənd ðə ˈdɛvəˌsteɪtɪŋ pr* ˈvɪktəri ðət 120

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: megatons of Nuclear Peace over Latin America would give Cuba and the USSR.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: megatons* əv ˈnukliər pis ˈoʊvər ˈlætən əˈmɛrɪkə wʊd gɪv ˈkjubə ənd ðə ussr.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Luckily, in our timeline the US blinked and began to sober up.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: luckily*, ɪn ɑr ˈtaɪmlaɪn ðə ˈjuˈɛs blɪŋkt ənd bɪˈgæn tɪ ˈsoʊbər up.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: In 1970, a final report killed the Pan-Atomic Canal and, with it, all nuclear excavation projects.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: in* 1970 ə ˈfaɪnəl rɪˈpɔrt kɪld ðə pan-atomic* kəˈnæl ənd, wɪθ ɪt, ɔl ˈnukliər ˌɛkskəˈveɪʃən projects.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But Teller wasn’t ready to let go, so he turned to nuclear fracking.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* ˈtɛlər wasn’t* ˈrɛdi tɪ lɛt goʊ, soʊ hi tərnd tɪ ˈnukliər fracking.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: Not because it made sense, but to keep Plowshare alive. Just one more nuke and I will build the future, bro.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: not* bɪˈkəz ɪt meɪd sɛns, bət tɪ kip ˈplaʊˌʃeɪr əˈlaɪv. ʤɪst wən mɔr nuk ənd aɪ wɪl bɪld ðə fˈjuʧər, bro.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: But several explosions later, the result was ultra-expensive, radioactive gas impossible to sell.
h3DCdWyb0cc.ipa: but* ˈsɛvərəl ɪkˈsploʊʒənz ˈleɪtər, ðə rɪˈzəlt wɑz ultra-expensive*, ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv gæs ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl tɪ sell.

h3DCdWyb0cc.text: In 1977, after 20 years and dozens of nukes across the US,

yLu_M4yiCho.text: How are things, Beth? I'm very well, thank you, Neil. How are you?

yLu_M4yiCho.text: Oh, I'm a little tired. I didn't sleep very well last night.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: And today's episode is all about sleep.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: We'll be talking about our sleep habits, and also our dreams. Lovely.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: So, Neil, you said that you didn't sleep well last night.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: So that's not so lovely. Does that happen very often?
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: so* ðæts nɑt soʊ ˈləvli. dɪz ðət ˈhæpən ˈvɛri often?

yLu_M4yiCho.text: It happens all the time, Beth! I don't sleep very well. Oh, dear. How many hours do you sleep at night, Neil?
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: it* ˈhæpənz ɔl ðə taɪm, bɛθ! aɪ doʊnt slip ˈvɛri wɛl. oʊ, dɪr. haʊ ˈmɛni aʊərz du ju slip æt naɪt, neil?

yLu_M4yiCho.text: I think probably six and a half hours. — Really? — Mmm. How about you?
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: i* θɪŋk ˈprɑbəˌbli sɪks ənd ə hæf aʊərz. —* ˈrɪli? —* mmm*. haʊ əˈbaʊt you?

yLu_M4yiCho.text: Well, if I sleep less than eight hours, then I'm exhausted.
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: well*, ɪf aɪ slip lɛs ðən eɪt aʊərz, ðɛn əm exhausted.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: I really like to sleep quite a lot! Ah, you are exhausted. What does 'exhausted' mean, Beth?
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: i* ˈrɪli laɪk tɪ slip kwaɪt ə lɔt! ɑ, ju ər ɪgˈzɔstɪd. wət dɪz 'ɪgˈzɔstɪd' min, beth?

yLu_M4yiCho.text: — It means that you are 'really tired'. — Mm, yes.
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: —* ɪt minz ðət ju ər 'ˈrɪli taɪərd'. —* mm*, yes.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: Like you after last night because you didn't sleep very well. Are you exhausted?
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: like* ju ˈæftər læst naɪt bɪˈkəz ju ˈdɪdənt slip ˈvɛri wɛl. ər ju exhausted?

yLu_M4yiCho.text: I am, Beth. I am absolutely exhausted. What time do you usually go to bed, Neil?
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: i* æm, bɛθ. aɪ æm ˌæbsəˈlutli ɪgˈzɔstɪd. wət taɪm du ju ˈjuʒəwəli goʊ tɪ bɛd, neil?

yLu_M4yiCho.text: About 11. I go to bed about 11. I usually go earlier, but about ten o'clock.
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: about* 11 aɪ goʊ tɪ bɛd əˈbaʊt 11 aɪ ˈjuʒəwəli goʊ ˈərliər, bət əˈbaʊt tɛn o'clock.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: And do you have dreams? Do you dream often?

yLu_M4yiCho.text: Yeah, I do, but I find it quite hard to remember my dreams.
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: yeah*, aɪ du, bət aɪ faɪnd ɪt kwaɪt hɑrd tɪ rɪˈmɛmbər maɪ dreams.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: But sometimes I have nightmares, and then they are easier to remember.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: So I might wake up in the middle of the night and feel a bit scared or worried.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: Can you tell us about a strange dream that you've had?

yLu_M4yiCho.text: Yeah, I had a weird dream a couple of nights ago.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: I was at my auntie's house in the kitchen and then suddenly the floor was very wet
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: i* wɑz æt maɪ auntie's* haʊs ɪn ðə ˈkɪʧən ənd ðɛn ˈsədənli ðə flɔr wɑz ˈvɛri wet

yLu_M4yiCho.text: and there were three robbers that were going to come and open the front door.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: So I had to run across the wet kitchen and lock the front door. It was a bit scary and weird.
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: so* aɪ hæd tɪ rən əˈkrɔs ðə wɛt ˈkɪʧən ənd lɑk ðə frənt dɔr. ɪt wɑz ə bɪt ˈskɛri ənd weird.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: Yeah. That sounds like a really scary dream. Yeah. So, a scary dream we can call a 'nightmare'.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: But then, after a while of looking around the streets,
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: but* ðɛn, ˈæftər ə waɪl əv ˈlʊkɪŋ əraʊnd ðə streets,

yLu_M4yiCho.text: So it sounds like it started as a nightmare, but then it kind of went into a dream.
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: so* ɪt saʊnz laɪk ɪt ˈstɑrtɪd ɛz ə ˈnaɪtˌmɛr, bət ðɛn ɪt kaɪnd əv wɛnt ˈɪntu ə dream.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: Yeah. It was just a normal, happy dream by the end. Good! Well, I noticed there that you used the past simple
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: yeah*. ɪt wɑz ʤɪst ə ˈnɔrməl, ˈhæpi drim baɪ ðə ɛnd. gʊd! wɛl, aɪ ˈnoʊtɪst ðɛr ðət ju juzd ðə pæst simple

yLu_M4yiCho.text: and the past continuous to describe your dream.
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: and* ðə pæst kənˈtɪnjuəs tɪ dɪˈskraɪb jʊr dream.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: You said 'I was running around', which is the past continuous

yLu_M4yiCho.text: and then you said 'I found them', which was the past simple. And this is really common language that we use

yLu_M4yiCho.text: when we're describing things in the past like dreams. So, we've talked about how long we sleep for

yLu_M4yiCho.text: and we've talked about dreams and nightmares, but I have a big question for you, Neil.
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: and* wiv tɔkt əˈbaʊt drimz ənd ˈnaɪtˌmɛrz, bət aɪ hæv ə bɪg kˈwɛʃən fər ju, neil.

yLu_M4yiCho.text: but my wife says that I do. Oh, so you do snore!
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: but* maɪ waɪf sɪz ðət aɪ du. oʊ, soʊ ju du snɔr!

yLu_M4yiCho.text: No, I've never heard myself snore!
yLu_M4yiCho.ipa: noʊ, aɪv ˈnɛvər hərd ˌmaɪˈsɛlf snore!

yLu_M4yiCho.text: Let's have a look at the language we learned in this conversation.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Human existence is scary and confusing.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ˈjumən ɪgˈzɪstəns ɪz ˈskɛri ənd kənfˈjuzɪŋ.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: A few hundred thousand years ago, we became conscious and found ourselves in a strange place.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ə fju ˈhənərd ˈθaʊzənd jɪrz əˈgoʊ, wi bɪˈkeɪm ˈkɑnʃəs ənd faʊnd ɑrˈsɛlvz ɪn ə streɪnʤ pleɪs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: It was filled with other beings. We could eat some; some could eat us.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪt wɑz fɪld wɪθ ˈəðər biɪŋz. wi kʊd it səm; səm kʊd it ˈjuˈɛs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: There was liquid stuff we could drink; things we could use to make more things.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðɛr wɑz ˈlɪkwɪd stəf wi kʊd drɪŋk; θɪŋz wi kʊd juz tɪ meɪk mɔr θɪŋz.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: The daytime sky had a tiny yellow ball that warmed our skin. The night sky was filled with beautiful lights.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðə ˈdeɪˌtaɪm skaɪ hæd ə ˈtaɪni ˈjɛloʊ bɔl ðət wɔrmd ɑr skɪn. ðə naɪt skaɪ wɑz fɪld wɪθ ˈbjutəfəl laɪts.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: This place was obviously made for us. Something was watching over us.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðɪs pleɪs wɑz ˈɑbviəsli meɪd fər ˈjuˈɛs. ˈsəmθɪŋ wɑz ˈwɑʧɪŋ ˈoʊvər ˈjuˈɛs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We were home. This made everything much less scary and confusing.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi wər hoʊm. ðɪs meɪd ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ məʧ lɛs ˈskɛri ənd kənfˈjuzɪŋ.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: But the older we got, the more we learned about the world and ourselves.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: bət ðə ˈoʊldər wi gɑt, ðə mɔr wi ˈlərnɪd əˈbaʊt ðə wərld ənd ɑrˈsɛlvz.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We learned that the twinkling lights are not shining beautifully for us, they just are.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi ˈlərnɪd ðət ðə tˈwɪŋkəlɪŋ laɪts ər nɑt ˈʃaɪnɪŋ ˈbjutəfli fər ˈjuˈɛs, ðeɪ ʤɪst ər.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We learned that we're not at the center of what we now call the universe,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi ˈlərnɪd ðət wɪr nɑt æt ðə ˈsɛnər əv wət wi naʊ kɔl ðə ˈjunəˌvərs,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: and that it is much, much older than we thought. We learned that we're made of many little dead things,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ənd ðət ɪt ɪz məʧ, məʧ ˈoʊldər ðən wi θɔt. wi ˈlərnɪd ðət wɪr meɪd əv ˈmɛni ˈlɪtəl dɛd θɪŋz,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: which make up bigger things that are not dead, for some reason,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wɪʧ meɪk əp ˈbɪgər θɪŋz ðət ər nɑt dɛd, fər səm ˈrizən,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: and that we're just another temporary stage in a history going back over a billion years.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ənd ðət wɪr ʤɪst əˈnəðər ˈtɛmpərˌɛri steɪʤ ɪn ə ˈhɪstəri goʊɪŋ bæk ˈoʊvər ə ˈbɪljən jɪrz.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We learned, in awe, that we live on a moist speck of dust moving around a medium-sized star
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi ˈlərnɪd, ɪn ɔ, ðət wi lɪv ɔn ə mɔɪst spɛk əv dəst ˈmuvɪŋ əraʊnd ə medium-sized* stɑr

MBRqu0YOH14.text: in a quiet region of one arm of an average galaxy, which is part of a galaxy group that we will never leave.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪn ə kwaɪət ˈriʤən əv wən ɑrm əv ən ˈævərɪʤ ˈgæləksi, wɪʧ ɪz pɑrt əv ə ˈgæləksi grup ðət wi wɪl ˈnɛvər liv.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: And this group is only one of thousands that, together, make up a galaxy supercluster.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ənd ðɪs grup ɪz ˈoʊnli wən əv ˈθaʊzənz ðət, təˈgɛðər, meɪk əp ə ˈgæləksi supercluster*.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: But even our supercluster is only one in thousands that make up what we call the observable universe.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: bət ˈivɪn ɑr supercluster* ɪz ˈoʊnli wən ɪn ˈθaʊzənz ðət meɪk əp wət wi kɔl ðə əbˈzərvəbəl ˈjunəˌvərs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: The universe might be a million times bigger, but we will never know.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðə ˈjunəˌvərs maɪt bi ə ˈmɪljən taɪmz ˈbɪgər, bət wi wɪl ˈnɛvər noʊ.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We can throw words around like two hundred million galaxies or trillions of stars or bazillions of planets,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi kən θroʊ wərdz əraʊnd laɪk tu ˈhənərd ˈmɪljən ˈgæləksiz ər ˈtrɪljənz əv stɑrz ər bazillions* əv ˈplænəts,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: but all of these numbers mean nothing. Our brains can't comprehend these concepts.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: bət ɔl əv ðiz ˈnəmbərz min ˈnəθɪŋ. ɑr breɪnz kænt ˌkɑmpriˈhɛnd ðiz ˈkɑnsɛpts.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: The universe is too big. There is too much of it.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ɪz tu bɪg. ðɛr ɪz tu məʧ əv ɪt.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: But size is not the most troubling concept we have to deal with. It's time, or, more precisely, the time we have.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: bət saɪz ɪz nɑt ðə moʊst ˈtrəbəlɪŋ ˈkɑnsɛpt wi hæv tɪ dil wɪθ. ɪts taɪm, ər, mɔr prɪˈsaɪsli, ðə taɪm wi hæv.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: If you're lucky enough to live to one hundred, you have five thousand two hundred weeks at your disposal.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪf jʊr ˈləki ɪˈnəf tɪ lɪv tɪ wən ˈhənərd, ju hæv faɪv ˈθaʊzənd tu ˈhənərd wiks æt jʊr dɪˈspoʊzəl.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: If you're twenty-five now, then you have three thousand nine hundred weeks left.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪf jʊr tˈwɛntiˌfaɪv naʊ, ðɛn ju hæv θri ˈθaʊzənd naɪn ˈhənərd wiks lɛft.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: If you're going to die at seventy, then there are two thousand three hundred and forty weeks left -
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪf jʊr goʊɪŋ tɪ daɪ æt ˈsɛvənti, ðɛn ðɛr ər tu ˈθaʊzənd θri ˈhənərd ənd ˈfɔrti wiks lɛft

MBRqu0YOH14.text: a lot of time, but also ... not really.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ə lɔt əv taɪm, bət ˈɔlsoʊ  nɑt ˈrɪli.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: And then what?
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ənd ðɛn what?

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Your biological processes will break down, and the dynamic pattern that is you will stop beinge dynamic.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: jʊr ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl ˈprɑsɛsəz wɪl breɪk daʊn, ənd ðə daɪˈnæmɪk ˈpætərn ðət ɪz ju wɪl stɑp beinge* daɪˈnæmɪk.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: It will dissolve until there is no you left.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪt wɪl dɪˈzɑlv ənˈtɪl ðɛr ɪz noʊ ju lɛft.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Some believe that there is a part of us we can't see or measure,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: səm bɪˈliv ðət ðɛr ɪz ə pɑrt əv ˈjuˈɛs wi kænt si ər ˈmɛʒər,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: but we have no way to find out, so this life might be it and we might end up dead forever.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: bət wi hæv noʊ weɪ tɪ faɪnd aʊt, soʊ ðɪs laɪf maɪt bi ɪt ənd wi maɪt ɛnd əp dɛd fərˈɛvər.
