MBRqu0YOH14.text: This is less scary than it sounds, though. If you don't remember the 13.75 billion years that went by before you existed,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðɪs ɪz lɛs ˈskɛri ðən ɪt saʊnz, ðoʊ. ɪf ju doʊnt rɪˈmɛmbər ðə 13.75* ˈbɪljən jɪrz ðət wɛnt baɪ ˌbiˈfɔr ju ɪgˈzɪstəd,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Then the trillions and trillions and trillions of years that come after will pass in no time once you're gone.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðɛn ðə ˈtrɪljənz ənd ˈtrɪljənz ənd ˈtrɪljənz əv jɪrz ðət kəm ˈæftər wɪl pæs ɪn noʊ taɪm wəns jʊr gɔn.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Close your eyes. Count to 1.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: kloʊz jʊr aɪz. kaʊnt tɪ 1

MBRqu0YOH14.text: That's how long forever feels.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðæts haʊ lɔŋ fərˈɛvər filz.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: And as far as we know, in the end, the universe itself will die and nothing will ever change again.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ənd ɛz fɑr ɛz wi noʊ, ɪn ðə ɛnd, ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ˌɪtˈsɛlf wɪl daɪ ənd ˈnəθɪŋ wɪl ˈɛvər ʧeɪnʤ əˈgɛn.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Our videos induce existential dread in many people, and the last few minutes probably haven't helped.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɑr ˈvɪdioʊz ˌɪnˈdus ˌɛgˌzɪˈstɛnʃəl drɛd ɪn ˈmɛni ˈpipəl, ənd ðə læst fju ˈmɪnəts ˈprɑbəˌbli ˈhævənt hɛlpt.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: So, for once, we want to offer a different way of looking at these things:
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: soʊ, fər wəns, wi wɔnt tɪ ˈɔfər ə ˈdɪfərənt weɪ əv ˈlʊkɪŋ æt ðiz θɪŋz:

MBRqu0YOH14.text: an unscientific, subjective point of view, the philosophy of Kurzgesagt, if you want.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ən ənˌsaɪənˈtɪfɪk, səˈbʤɛktɪv pɔɪnt əv vju, ðə fəˈlɑsəfi əv kurzgesagt*, ɪf ju wɔnt.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Please take it with a grain of salt; we don't know any more about human existence than you do.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: pliz teɪk ɪt wɪθ ə greɪn əv sɔlt; wi doʊnt noʊ ˈɛni mɔr əˈbaʊt ˈjumən ɪgˈzɪstəns ðən ju du.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We counter existential dread with optimistic nihilism. What do we mean by that?
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: we* ˈkaʊntər ˌɛgˌzɪˈstɛnʃəl drɛd wɪθ ˌɑptɪˈmɪstɪk ˈnaɪəˌlɪzəm. wət du wi min baɪ that?

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Well, to summarize, it seems very unlikely that 200 trillion trillion stars have been made for us.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wɛl, tɪ ˈsəmərˌaɪz, ɪt simz ˈvɛri ənˈlaɪkli ðət 200 ˈtrɪljən ˈtrɪljən stɑrz hæv bɪn meɪd fər ˈjuˈɛs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: In a way, it feels like the cruelest joke in existence has been played on us.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪn ə weɪ, ɪt filz laɪk ðə ˈkruləst ʤoʊk ɪn ɪgˈzɪstəns həz bɪn pleɪd ɔn ˈjuˈɛs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We became self-aware only to realize this story is not about us.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi bɪˈkeɪm self-aware* ˈoʊnli tɪ ˈriəˌlaɪz ðɪs ˈstɔri ɪz nɑt əˈbaʊt ˈjuˈɛs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: While it is great to know about electrons and the powerhouse of the cell,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: waɪl ɪt ɪz greɪt tɪ noʊ əˈbaʊt ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz ənd ðə ˈpaʊərˌhaʊs əv ðə sɛl,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Science doesn't do a lot to make this less depressing.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: saɪəns ˈdəzənt du ə lɔt tɪ meɪk ðɪs lɛs dɪˈprɛsɪŋ.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Okay, but so what? You only get one shot at life, which is scary, but it also sets you free.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: okay*, bət soʊ wət? ju ˈoʊnli gɪt wən ʃɑt æt laɪf, wɪʧ ɪz ˈskɛri, bət ɪt ˈɔlsoʊ sɛts ju fri.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: If the universe ends in heat death, every humiliation you suffer in your life will be forgotten.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪf ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ɛndz ɪn hit dɛθ, ˈɛvəri hjuˌmɪliˈeɪʃən ju ˈsəfər ɪn jʊr laɪf wɪl bi fərˈgɑtən.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Every mistake you made will not matter in the end. Every bad thing you did will be voided.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ˈɛvəri mɪˈsteɪk ju meɪd wɪl nɑt ˈmætər ɪn ðə ɛnd. ˈɛvəri bæd θɪŋ ju dɪd wɪl bi ˈvɔɪdɪd.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: If our life is all we get to experience, then it's the only thing that matters.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪf ɑr laɪf ɪz ɔl wi gɪt tɪ ɪkˈspɪriəns, ðɛn ɪts ðə ˈoʊnli θɪŋ ðət ˈmætərz.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: If the universe has no principles, the only principles relevant are the ones we decide on.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪf ðə ˈjunəˌvərs həz noʊ ˈprɪnsəpəlz, ðə ˈoʊnli ˈprɪnsəpəlz ˈrɛləvənt ər ðə wənz wi ˌdɪˈsaɪd ɔn.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: If the universe has no purpose, then we get to dictate what its purpose is.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪf ðə ˈjunəˌvərs həz noʊ ˈpərpəs, ðɛn wi gɪt tɪ ˈdɪkˌteɪt wət ɪts ˈpərpəs ɪz.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Humans will most certainly cease to exist at some point,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ˈjumənz wɪl moʊst ˈsərtənli sis tɪ ɪgˈzɪst æt səm pɔɪnt,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: but before we do, we get to explore ourselves and the world around us.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: bət ˌbiˈfɔr wi du, wi gɪt tɪ ɪkˈsplɔr ɑrˈsɛlvz ənd ðə wərld əraʊnd ˈjuˈɛs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We get to experience feelings. We get to experience food, books, sunrises, and being with each other.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi gɪt tɪ ɪkˈspɪriəns ˈfilɪŋz. wi gɪt tɪ ɪkˈspɪriəns fud, bʊks, sunrises*, ənd biɪŋ wɪθ iʧ ˈəðər.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: The fact that we're even able to think about these things is already kind of incredible.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðə fækt ðət wɪr ˈivɪn ˈeɪbəl tɪ θɪŋk əˈbaʊt ðiz θɪŋz ɪz ɔˈrɛdi kaɪnd əv ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbəl.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: It's easy to think of ourselves as separated from everything, but this is not true.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪts ˈizi tɪ θɪŋk əv ɑrˈsɛlvz ɛz ˈsɛpərˌeɪtɪd frəm ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ, bət ðɪs ɪz nɑt tru.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We are as much the universe as a neutron star or a black hole or a nebula.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi ər ɛz məʧ ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ɛz ə ˈnuˌtrɑn stɑr ər ə blæk hoʊl ər ə ˈnɛbjələ.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Even better, actually, we are its thinking and feeling part: the centre organs of the universe.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ˈivɪn ˈbɛtər, ˈæˌkʧuəli, wi ər ɪts ˈθɪŋkɪŋ ənd ˈfilɪŋ pɑrt: ðə ˈsɛntər ˈɔrgənz əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We are truly free in a universe-sized playground,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi ər ˈtruli fri ɪn ə universe-sized* ˈpleɪˌgraʊnd,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: so we might as well aim to be happy and to build some kind of utopia in the stars.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: soʊ wi maɪt ɛz wɛl eɪm tɪ bi ˈhæpi ənd tɪ bɪld səm kaɪnd əv juˈtoʊpiə ɪn ðə stɑrz.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: It's not as if we've found out everything there is to know
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪts nɑt ɛz ɪf wiv faʊnd aʊt ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ðɛr ɪz tɪ noʊ

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We don't know why the rules of the universe are as they are, how life came into existence, what life is.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi doʊnt noʊ waɪ ðə rulz əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ər ɛz ðeɪ ər, haʊ laɪf keɪm ˈɪntu ɪgˈzɪstəns, wət laɪf ɪz.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: We have no idea what consciousness is or if we are alone in the universe.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: wi hæv noʊ aɪˈdiə wət ˈkɑnʃəsnəs ɪz ər ɪf wi ər əˈloʊn ɪn ðə ˈjunəˌvərs.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: But we can try to find some answers.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: bət wi kən traɪ tɪ faɪnd səm ˈænsərz.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: There are billions of stars to visit, diseases to cure, people to help, happy feelings to be experienced,
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ðɛr ər ˈbɪljənz əv stɑrz tɪ ˈvɪzɪt, dɪˈzizɪz tɪ kjʊr, ˈpipəl tɪ hɛlp, ˈhæpi ˈfilɪŋz tɪ bi ɪkˈspɪriənst,

MBRqu0YOH14.text: and video games to finish. There is so much to do.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ənd ˈvɪdioʊ geɪmz tɪ ˈfɪnɪʃ. ðɛr ɪz soʊ məʧ tɪ du.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: So, wrapping up, you've probably used up a good chunk of the time available to you.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: soʊ, ˈræpɪŋ əp, juv ˈprɑbəˌbli juzd əp ə gʊd ʧəŋk əv ðə taɪm əˈveɪləbəl tɪ ju.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: If this is our one shot at life, there is no reason not to have fun and live as happy as possible.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ɪf ðɪs ɪz ɑr wən ʃɑt æt laɪf, ðɛr ɪz noʊ ˈrizən nɑt tɪ hæv fən ənd lɪv ɛz ˈhæpi ɛz ˈpɑsəbəl.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Bonus points if you made the life of other people better. More bonus points if you help build a galactic human empire.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: ˈboʊnəs pɔɪnts ɪf ju meɪd ðə laɪf əv ˈəðər ˈpipəl ˈbɛtər. mɔr ˈboʊnəs pɔɪnts ɪf ju hɛlp bɪld ə gəˈlæktɪk ˈjumən ˈɛmpaɪər.

MBRqu0YOH14.text: Do the things that make you feel good. You get to decide whatever this means for you.
MBRqu0YOH14.ipa: du ðə θɪŋz ðət meɪk ju fil gʊd. ju gɪt tɪ ˌdɪˈsaɪd ˌwəˈtɛvər ðɪs minz fər ju.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Trees are the heaviest and largest living things on Earth,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: triz ər ðə ˈhɛviəst ənd ˈlɑrʤəst ˈlɪvɪŋ θɪŋz ɔn earth,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: with the most massive tree weighing almost 2,000 tons – as much as ten blue whales.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: with* ðə moʊst ˈmæsɪv tri weɪɪŋ ˈɔlˌmoʊst 2,000* tənz –* ɛz məʧ ɛz tɛn blu whales.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: But instead of floating weightlessly in the ocean it reaches 25 stories into the sky,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: but* ˌɪnˈstɛd əv ˈfloʊtɪŋ weightlessly* ɪn ðə ˈoʊʃən ɪt ˈriʧɪz 25 ˈstɔriz ˈɪntu ðə sky,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: held in place by surprisingly shallow roots. You’d think trees grow from the ground because,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: held* ɪn pleɪs baɪ səˈpraɪzɪŋli ˈʃæloʊ ruts. you’d* θɪŋk triz groʊ frəm ðə graʊnd because,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: well, they are made of stuff and there is stuff in the ground – but if something so
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: well*, ðeɪ ər meɪd əv stəf ənd ðɛr ɪz stəf ɪn ðə graʊnd –* bət ɪf ˈsəmθɪŋ so

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: massive and huge ate something down below, it would have to leave holes.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: massive* ənd juʤ eɪt ˈsəmθɪŋ daʊn bɪˈloʊ, ɪt wʊd hæv tɪ liv holes.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Instead trees are growing by literally eating thin air! But this is only half of the story because down in the ground,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: instead* triz ər groʊɪŋ baɪ ˈlɪtərəli ˈitɪŋ θɪn ɛr! bət ðɪs ɪz ˈoʊnli hæf əv ðə ˈstɔri bɪˈkəz daʊn ɪn ðə ground,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: roots are mining rocks in ways weirder than you can imagine. How does this work?
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: roots* ər ˈmaɪnɪŋ rɑks ɪn weɪz ˈwɪrdər ðən ju kən ˌɪˈmæʤən. haʊ dɪz ðɪs work?

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: How to Eat Air to Grow Huge
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: how* tɪ it ɛr tɪ groʊ huge

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Carbon is the most valuable material for living things. A chemical multi tool you can make almost everything from – and
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: carbon* ɪz ðə moʊst ˈvæljəbəl məˈtɪriəl fər ˈlɪvɪŋ θɪŋz. ə ˈkɛmɪkəl ˈməlti tul ju kən meɪk ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ frəm –* and

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: a good amount of it just floats around in the air and the oceans.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: a* gʊd əˈmaʊnt əv ɪt ʤɪst floʊts əraʊnd ɪn ðə ɛr ənd ðə oceans.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: What makes plants so incredibly successful is that over a billion
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: what* meɪks plænts soʊ ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli səkˈsɛsfəl ɪz ðət ˈoʊvər ə billion

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: years ago their ancestors became better than any other living thing at harvesting carbon.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: years* əˈgoʊ ðɛr ˈænˌsɛstərz bɪˈkeɪm ˈbɛtər ðən ˈɛni ˈəðər ˈlɪvɪŋ θɪŋ æt ˈhɑrvəstɪŋ carbon.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: They used it to grow and grow and grow.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: they* juzd ɪt tɪ groʊ ənd groʊ ənd grow.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Today plants make up 80% of the biomass on earth and are the basis for all complex
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: today* plænts meɪk əp 80 əv ðə ˈbaɪəmæs ɔn ərθ ənd ər ðə ˈbeɪsɪs fər ɔl ˈkɑmplɛks

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: life – all animals eat either plants or animals that eat plants, to get the carbon they need.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: laɪf –* ɔl ˈænəməlz it ˈiðər plænts ər ˈænəməlz ðət it plænts, tɪ gɪt ðə ˈkɑrbən ðeɪ need.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Trees are an especially ingenious way plants found to harvest massive amounts of carbon.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: trees* ər ən əˈspɛʃəli ˌɪnˈʤinjəs weɪ plænts faʊnd tɪ ˈhɑrvəst ˈmæsɪv əˈmaʊnts əv carbon.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Trees are big and heavy and so they need a lot of material.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: trees* ər bɪg ənd ˈhɛvi ənd soʊ ðeɪ nid ə lɔt əv material.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: But the atmosphere is only about 0.04% CO₂.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: but* ðə ˈætməsˌfɪr ɪz ˈoʊnli əˈbaʊt 0.04* co₂.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: 425 CO₂ molecules per million molecules in the air.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: 425* co₂*₂ ˈmɑləˌkjulz pər ˈmɪljən ˈmɑləˌkjulz ɪn ðə air.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: To get a single tonne of carbon a tree has to process 6000 tons,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: to* gɪt ə ˈsɪŋgəl tən əv ˈkɑrbən ə tri həz tɪ ˈprɔˌsɛs 6000 tons,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: or 5 million cubic meters of air! This is a lot.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: or* 5 ˈmɪljən ˈkjubɪk ˈmitərz əv ɛr! ðɪs ɪz ə lot.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: So trees developed sophisticated biological industrial megalopolises: Their crowns.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: so* triz dɪˈvɛləpt səˈfɪstɪˌkeɪtəd ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl ˌɪnˈdəstriəl megalopolises*: ðɛr crowns.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: A huge industrial park network, made from dozens of branches, subbranches and hundreds
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: a* juʤ ˌɪnˈdəstriəl pɑrk ˈnɛtˌwərk, meɪd frəm ˈdəzənz əv ˈbrænʧɪz, subbranches* ənd hundreds

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: of thousands of twigs that can sense the sun and shape the tree to grow towards it in slow motion.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: of* ˈθaʊzənz əv twɪgz ðət kən sɛns ðə sən ənd ʃeɪp ðə tri tɪ groʊ təˈwɔrdz ɪt ɪn sloʊ motion.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: They are carrying up to a million leaves, the industrial parks where a tree eats and builds,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: they* ər ˈkɛriɪŋ əp tɪ ə ˈmɪljən livz, ðə ˌɪnˈdəstriəl pɑrks wɛr ə tri its ənd builds,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: consuming extreme amounts of resources from the air and fed by the roots
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: consuming* ɪkˈstrim əˈmaʊnts əv ˈrisɔrsɪz frəm ðə ɛr ənd fɛd baɪ ðə roots

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: down below while vomiting waste and changing the climate around them. Let’s zoom into a single leaf.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: down* bɪˈloʊ waɪl ˈvɑmətɪŋ weɪst ənd ˈʧeɪnʤɪŋ ðə ˈklaɪmɪt əraʊnd ðɛm. let’s* zum ˈɪntu ə ˈsɪŋgəl leaf.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: It is made from hundreds of millions of factory cells and optimized to have as
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: it* ɪz meɪd frəm ˈhənərdz əv ˈmɪljənz əv ˈfæktəri sɛlz ənd optimized* tɪ hæv as

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: much surface area and be as thin as possible to harvest sunlight.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: much* ˈsərfəs ˈɛriə ənd bi ɛz θɪn ɛz ˈpɑsəbəl tɪ ˈhɑrvəst ˈsənˌlaɪt.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: While your skin is hundreds of cells thick, a leaf can be just ten cells top to bottom.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: waɪl jʊr skɪn ɪz ˈhənərdz əv sɛlz θɪk, ə lif kən bi ʤɪst tɛn sɛlz tɔp tɪ bottom.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: On their top leaves have only a single,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: on* ðɛr tɔp livz hæv ˈoʊnli ə single,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: ultra thin layer of protective transparent “skin” cells that let light through and keep water in.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: ultra* θɪn leɪər əv prəˈtɛktɪv trænˈspɛrənt ““skin”*” sɛlz ðət lɛt laɪt θru ənd kip ˈwɔtər in.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Below them are layers of factory cells, filled to the brink with chloroplasts that do the actual work.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: below* ðɛm ər leɪərz əv ˈfæktəri sɛlz, fɪld tɪ ðə brɪŋk wɪθ ˈklɔrəˌplæsts ðət du ðə ˈækʧəwəl work.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Beneath them a spongy layer of loose cells enables gases to travel around.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: beneath* ðɛm ə ˈspənʤi leɪər əv lus sɛlz ɪˈneɪbəlz ˈgæsɪz tɪ ˈtrævəl around.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: The whole leaf is traversed by a network of vein-like superhighways,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: the* hoʊl lif ɪz ˈtrævərst baɪ ə ˈnɛtˌwərk əv vein-like* superhighways,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: that carry sugars back down and bring water and minerals up from the roots.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: that* ˈkɛri ˈʃʊgərz bæk daʊn ənd brɪŋ ˈwɔtər ənd ˈmɪnərəlz əp frəm ðə roots.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: At the bottom is another protective layer of cells interrupted by hundreds of thousands
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: at* ðə ˈbɑtəm ɪz əˈnəðər prəˈtɛktɪv leɪər əv sɛlz ˌɪntərˈəptɪd baɪ ˈhənərdz əv thousands

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: of stomata – tiny mouths opened and closed by two guard cells that look a bit like lips.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: of* ˈstoʊmətə –* ˈtaɪni maʊðz ˈoʊpənd ənd kloʊzd baɪ tu gɑrd sɛlz ðət lʊk ə bɪt laɪk lips.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Each day an adult tree pulls up dozens of liters of water all the
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: each* deɪ ən ˈædəlt tri pʊlz əp ˈdəzənz əv ˈlitərz əv ˈwɔtər ɔl the

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: way from its roots in the ground to these veins,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: way* frəm ɪts ruts ɪn ðə graʊnd tɪ ðiz veins,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: where about 95% of it is sweated out through hundreds of billions of these tiny mouths.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: where* əˈbaʊt 95 əv ɪt ɪz sˈwɛtɪd aʊt θru ˈhənərdz əv ˈbɪljənz əv ðiz ˈtaɪni mouths.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: This cools the leaf factories, which need to stay in direct sunlight as long as possible, and the air around the tree.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: this* kulz ðə lif ˈfæktəriz, wɪʧ nid tɪ steɪ ɪn dɪˈrɛkt ˈsənˌlaɪt ɛz lɔŋ ɛz ˈpɑsəbəl, ənd ðə ɛr əraʊnd ðə tree.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: And it surrounds the tree with an invisible mist.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: and* ɪt səraʊndz ðə tri wɪθ ən ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl mist.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: The vapor from a forest of billions of trees can seed clouds and create rain.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: the* ˈveɪpər frəm ə ˈfɔrɪst əv ˈbɪljənz əv triz kən sid klaʊdz ənd kriˈeɪt reɪn.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Rainforest is literal – without the trees the amazon would be a sad dry shrubland or desert.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: ˈraɪnˌfɔrəst ɪz ˈlɪtərəl –* wɪˈθaʊt ðə triz ðə ˈæməˌzɑn wʊd bi ə sæd draɪ shrubland* ər desert.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: The other 5% of the water is used to keep the cells alive and to power the
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: the* ˈəðər 5 əv ðə ˈwɔtər ɪz juzd tɪ kip ðə sɛlz əˈlaɪv ənd tɪ paʊər the

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: factories where the magic happens: Photosynthesis. We are not going to explain the details here, but in a nutshell,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: factories* wɛr ðə ˈmæʤɪk ˈhæpənz: ˌfoʊtoʊˈsɪnθəsɪs. wi ər nɑt goʊɪŋ tɪ ɪkˈspleɪn ðə ˈditeɪlz hir, bət ɪn ə nutshell,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: with the energy from the sun, water molecules are split into hydrogen and oxygen.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: with* ðə ˈɛnərʤi frəm ðə sən, ˈwɔtər ˈmɑləˌkjulz ər splɪt ˈɪntu ˈhaɪdrəʤən ənd oxygen.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: The oxygen is ejected, while the leftover hydrogen and CO2 are forged
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: the* ˈɑksɪʤən ɪz ɪˈʤɛktɪd, waɪl ðə ˈlɛfˌtoʊvər ˈhaɪdrəʤən ənd co2* ər forged

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: and reduced into glucose – a simple sugar that’s both battery and building block.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: and* rɪˈdust ˈɪntu ˈgluˌkoʊs –* ə ˈsɪmpəl ˈʃʊgər that’s* boʊθ ˈbætəri ənd ˈbɪldɪŋ block.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: And the source of most carbon in the world for most animals. Oxygen is not just garbage to the tree though.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: and* ðə sɔrs əv moʊst ˈkɑrbən ɪn ðə wərld fər moʊst ˈænəməlz. ˈɑksɪʤən ɪz nɑt ʤɪst ˈgɑrbɪʤ tɪ ðə tri though.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: To actually use the energy stored in the glucose, the tree has to burn the sugar,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: to* ˈæˌkʧuəli juz ðə ˈɛnərʤi stɔrd ɪn ðə ˈgluˌkoʊs, ðə tri həz tɪ bərn ðə sugar,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: just like we humans do, with cellular respiration.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: just* laɪk wi ˈjumənz du, wɪθ ˈsɛljələr respiration.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: So all living cells in the tree suck in oxygen – through the tiny leaf mouths,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: so* ɔl ˈlɪvɪŋ sɛlz ɪn ðə tri sək ɪn ˈɑksɪʤən –* θru ðə ˈtaɪni lif mouths,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: cracks in the bark, and even root tips tapping into tiny air pockets hidden in the soil.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: cracks* ɪn ðə bɑrk, ənd ˈivɪn rut tɪps ˈtæpɪŋ ˈɪntu ˈtaɪni ɛr ˈpɑkəts ˈhɪdən ɪn ðə soil.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: This respiration runs nonstop, and especially at night when the leaf factories stop production.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: this* ˌrɛspərˈeɪʃən rənz ˌnɑnˈstɑp, ənd əˈspɛʃəli æt naɪt wɪn ðə lif ˈfæktəriz stɑp production.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Trees actually reabsorb some of the oxygen they produce and almost all
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: trees* ˈæˌkʧuəli ˌriəbˈzɔrb səm əv ðə ˈɑksɪʤən ðeɪ ˈproʊdus ənd ˈɔlˌmoʊst all

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: of the rest gets used up by microbes and everything else breathing nearby. Most of the world's free oxygen doesn't come
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: of* ðə rɛst gɪts juzd əp baɪ ˈmaɪˌkroʊbz ənd ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ɛls ˈbriðɪŋ ˈnɪrˈbaɪ. moʊst əv ðə wərldz fri ˈɑksɪʤən ˈdəzənt come

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: from trees but from algae and cyanobacteria in the oceans.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: from* triz bət frəm ˈælʤi ənd cyanobacteria* ɪn ðə ˈoʊʃənz.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: But this is only half of the story because the even more insane parts of
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: bət ðɪs ɪz ˈoʊnli hæf əv ðə ˈstɔri bɪˈkəz ðə ˈivɪn mɔr ˌɪnˈseɪn pɑrts of

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: trees are the second invisible crown: the underground empire of the roots.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: trees* ər ðə ˈsɛkənd ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl kraʊn: ðə ˈəndərˌgraʊnd ˈɛmpaɪər əv ðə roots.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Most of the water a tree needs comes from rainfall, which soaks mainly into the upper layers of soil.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: most* əv ðə ˈwɔtər ə tri nidz kəmz frəm ˈreɪnˌfɔl, wɪʧ soʊks ˈmeɪnli ˈɪntu ðə ˈəpər leɪərz əv soil.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Annoyingly for trees their crowns are big umbrellas, so their roots need to spread out far and wide towards the side.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: annoyingly* fər triz ðɛr kraʊnz ər bɪg əmˈbrɛləz, soʊ ðɛr ruts nid tɪ sprɛd aʊt fɑr ənd waɪd təˈwɔrdz ðə side.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: About 50% of their roots are packed into the top 25 centimeters of soil.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: about* 50 əv ðɛr ruts ər pækt ˈɪntu ðə tɔp 25 ˈsɛntəˌmitərz əv soil.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: They are not a mirror image of the crown, but a dense, tangled mat, deeply intermingled with their neighbours’.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: they* ər nɑt ə ˈmɪrər ˈɪmɪʤ əv ðə kraʊn, bət ə dɛns, ˈtæŋgəld mæt, ˈdipli ˌɪntərˈmɪŋgəld wɪθ ðɛr neighbours’.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Only if it’s very dry do roots grow straight down to tap hidden water reserves,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: only* ɪf it’s* ˈvɛri draɪ du ruts groʊ streɪt daʊn tɪ tæp ˈhɪdən ˈwɔtər reserves,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: in extreme cases more than 20 stories deep. But this is a rare exception. Most roots reach down 7m.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: in* ɪkˈstrim ˈkeɪsɪz mɔr ðən 20 ˈstɔriz dip. bət ðɪs ɪz ə rɛr ɪkˈsɛpʃən. moʊst ruts riʧ daʊn 7m.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: But roots have a far more complex job than just catching water.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: but* ruts hæv ə fɑr mɔr ˈkɑmplɛks ʤɑb ðən ʤɪst ˈkæʧɪŋ water.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Just like you can’t build a city from only bricks and steel, trees also need
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: just* laɪk ju can’t* bɪld ə ˈsɪti frəm ˈoʊnli brɪks ənd stil, triz ˈɔlsoʊ need

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: some rare materials: phosphorus to build DNA, nitrogen for proteins and many more.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: some* rɛr məˈtɪriəlz: ˈfɑsfərəs tɪ bɪld ˈdiˌɛˈneɪ, ˈnaɪtrəʤən fər ˈproʊˌtinz ənd ˈmɛni more.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: And all of these are stealthily buried underground.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: and* ɔl əv ðiz ər stealthily* ˈbɛrid underground.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Rocks, dry patches, nutrients, and rival roots are all scattered unpredictably and chaotically. To navigate this shifting maze,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: rocks*, draɪ ˈpæʧɪz, ˈnutriənts, ənd ˈraɪvəl ruts ər ɔl ˈskætərd ˌənprɪˈdɪktəbli ənd chaotically*. tɪ ˈnævəˌgeɪt ðɪs ˈʃɪftɪŋ maze,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: roots evolved a specialized sensor at their very tip: the root cap. Each cap is filled with gravity-sensing cells,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: roots* ɪˈvɑlvd ə ˈspɛʃəˌlaɪzd ˈsɛnsər æt ðɛr ˈvɛri tɪp: ðə rut kæp. iʧ kæp ɪz fɪld wɪθ gravity-sensing* cells,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: in which tiny dense particles sink like pebbles settling in a jar of water.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: in* wɪʧ ˈtaɪni dɛns ˈpɑrtɪkəlz sɪŋk laɪk ˈpɛbəlz ˈsɛtəlɪŋ ɪn ə ʤɑr əv ˈwɔtər.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: So the root always knows which way is down. As it pushes forward, specialized cells detect moisture, temperature,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: soʊ ðə rut ˈɔlˌweɪz noʊz wɪʧ weɪ ɪz daʊn. ɛz ɪt ˈpʊʃɪz ˈfɔrwərd, ˈspɛʃəˌlaɪzd sɛlz dɪˈtɛkt ˈmɔɪsʧər, temperature,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: chemical gradients and the smallest vibrations from water. This raw data flows into the root’s command center just behind the tip,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: chemical* ˈgreɪdiənts ənd ðə sˈmɔləst vaɪˈbreɪʃənz frəm ˈwɔtər. ðɪs rɑ ˈdætə floʊz ˈɪntu ðə root’s* kəˈmænd ˈsɛnər ʤɪst bɪˈhaɪnd ðə tip,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: where cells produce electrical pulses and move transmitter chemicals around.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: where* sɛlz ˈproʊdus ɪˈlɛktrɪkəl ˈpəlsɪz ənd muv trænsˈmɪtər ˈkɛmɪkəlz around.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Signals from the soil are processed, interpreted and turned into decisions about where to grow.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: signals* frəm ðə sɔɪl ər ˈprɑsɛst, ˌɪnˈtərprətəd ənd tərnd ˈɪntu dɪˈsɪʒənz əˈbaʊt wɛr tɪ grow.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: A single tree has hundreds of thousands of these command centers and they seem to share information with each other.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: a* ˈsɪŋgəl tri həz ˈhənərdz əv ˈθaʊzənz əv ðiz kəˈmænd ˈsɛnərz ənd ðeɪ sim tɪ ʃɛr ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən wɪθ iʧ other.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Once a root has chosen a path, fuzzy little drinking straws called root hairs,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: once* ə rut həz ˈʧoʊzən ə pæθ, ˈfəzi ˈlɪtəl ˈdrɪŋkɪŋ strɔz kɔld rut hairs,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: loaded with enzymes and transport proteins, begin soaking up water and dissolved minerals.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: loaded* wɪθ ˈɛnˌzaɪmz ənd ˈtrænspɔrt ˈproʊˌtinz, bɪˈgɪn ˈsoʊkɪŋ əp ˈwɔtər ənd dɪˈzɑlvd minerals.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: But many essential nutrients are locked away in solid rock. So roots evolved to move into the finest cracks.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: but* ˈmɛni ɛˈsɛnʃəl ˈnutriənts ər lɑkt əˈweɪ ɪn ˈsɑləd rɑk. soʊ ruts ɪˈvɑlvd tɪ muv ˈɪntu ðə ˈfaɪnəst cracks.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Once in, they fill with water and swell like tiny hydraulic jacks, creating enough pressure to break even the hardest rock.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: once* ɪn, ðeɪ fɪl wɪθ ˈwɔtər ənd swɛl laɪk ˈtaɪni haɪˈdrɔlɪk ʤæks, kriˈeɪtɪŋ ɪˈnəf ˈprɛʃər tɪ breɪk ˈivɪn ðə ˈhɑrdəst rock.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Next they release a mix of acids that seep into the fractures and dissolve the bonds that hold nutrients in place.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: next* ðeɪ riˈlis ə mɪks əv ˈæsədz ðət sip ˈɪntu ðə ˈfrækʧərz ənd dɪˈzɑlv ðə bɑndz ðət hoʊld ˈnutriənts ɪn place.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Claw-like molecules grab them and pull them in before they can slip away.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: claw-like* ˈmɑləˌkjulz græb ðɛm ənd pʊl ðɛm ɪn ˌbiˈfɔr ðeɪ kən slɪp away.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: This sophistication really is stunning but it gets even wilder.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: this* səˌfɪstəˈkeɪʃən ˈrɪli ɪz ˈstənɪŋ bət ɪt gɪts ˈivɪn wilder.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Even with all these tools, to really thrive, the tree needs allies.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: even* wɪθ ɔl ðiz tulz, tɪ ˈrɪli θraɪv, ðə tri nidz allies.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: And it found them: fungi. The underground networks of fungi can stretch for kilometers.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: and* ɪt faʊnd ðɛm: ˈfənʤaɪ. ðə ˈəndərˌgraʊnd ˈnɛtˌwərks əv ˈfənʤaɪ kən strɛʧ fər kilometers.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: They are so small that they can go where roots can’t, slipping between grains of soil to reach distant pockets of nutrients.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: they* ər soʊ smɔl ðət ðeɪ kən goʊ wɛr ruts can’t*, sˈlɪpɪŋ bɪtˈwin greɪnz əv sɔɪl tɪ riʧ ˈdɪstənt ˈpɑkəts əv ˈnutriənts.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: But they need food. So hundreds of millions of years ago roots and fungi formed a trade alliance.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: bət ðeɪ nid fud. soʊ ˈhənərdz əv ˈmɪljənz əv jɪrz əˈgoʊ ruts ənd ˈfənʤaɪ fɔrmd ə treɪd alliance.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: The trees provide a cut of the sugars they produce far up in the sky and fungi collect
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: the* triz prəˈvaɪd ə kət əv ðə ˈʃʊgərz ðeɪ ˈproʊdus fɑr əp ɪn ðə skaɪ ənd ˈfənʤaɪ collect

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: and give them nutrients and water in return. Some fungi grow directly into the root’s cells,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: and* gɪv ðɛm ˈnutriənts ənd ˈwɔtər ɪn rɪˈtərn. səm ˈfənʤaɪ groʊ dɪˈrɛkli ˈɪntu ðə root’s* cells,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: building tiny trade posts, where sugars and minerals change hands. Others wrap themselves around root tips, weaving between their outer
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: building* ˈtaɪni treɪd poʊsts, wɛr ˈʃʊgərz ənd ˈmɪnərəlz ʧeɪnʤ hænz. ˈəðərz ræp ðɛmˈsɛlvz əraʊnd rut tɪps, ˈwivɪŋ bɪtˈwin ðɛr outer

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: layers, insulating delicate tissues and protecting them against microorganisms.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: layers*, ˈɪnsəˌleɪtɪŋ ˈdɛləkət ˈtɪʃuz ənd prəˈtɛktɪŋ ðɛm əˈgɛnst microorganisms.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Today there are thousands of fungal tree ally species, each with its own specialties.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: today* ðɛr ər ˈθaʊzənz əv ˈfəŋgəl tri ˈælaɪ ˈspiʃiz, iʧ wɪθ ɪts oʊn specialties.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Some only partner with specific tree species, while others are happy to work with almost anyone.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: some* ˈoʊnli ˈpɑrtnər wɪθ spɪˈsɪfɪk tri ˈspiʃiz, waɪl ˈəðərz ər ˈhæpi tɪ wərk wɪθ ˈɔlˌmoʊst anyone.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: These connections often knit the roots of many trees together into vast underground networks.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: these* kəˈnɛkʃənz ˈɔfən nɪt ðə ruts əv ˈmɛni triz təˈgɛðər ˈɪntu væst ˈəndərˌgraʊnd networks.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Their scale is gigantic. In just one cubic meter of healthy forest floor, fine tree roots can stretch for several kilometers
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: their* skeɪl ɪz ʤaɪˈgænɪk. ɪn ʤɪst wən ˈkjubɪk ˈmitər əv ˈhɛlθi ˈfɔrɪst flɔr, faɪn tri ruts kən strɛʧ fər ˈsɛvərəl kilometers

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: and for every kilometer of root, there can be hundreds of kilometers of fungal networks.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: and* fər ˈɛvəri ˈkɪləˌmitər əv rut, ðɛr kən bi ˈhənərdz əv ˈkɪləˌmitərz əv ˈfəŋgəl networks.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: It’s one of the largest and most intricate living structures on Earth, and may even connect whole forests.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: it’s* wən əv ðə ˈlɑrʤəst ənd moʊst ˈɪntrəkət ˈlɪvɪŋ ˈstrəkʧərz ɔn ərθ, ənd meɪ ˈivɪn kəˈnɛkt hoʊl forests.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: We are only beginning to understand how complex and intricate the relationships between trees,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: we* ər ˈoʊnli bɪˈgɪnɪŋ tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd haʊ ˈkɑmplɛks ənd ˈɪntrəkət ðə riˈleɪʃənˌʃɪps bɪtˈwin trees,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: their offspring, relatives and rivals, microbes and fungal networks are.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: their* ˈɔfˌsprɪŋ, ˈrɛlətɪvz ənd ˈraɪvəlz, ˈmaɪˌkroʊbz ənd ˈfəŋgəl ˈnɛtˌwərks are.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: But the more we’ve learned over the last few decades,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: but* ðə mɔr we’ve* ˈlərnɪd ˈoʊvər ðə læst fju decades,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: the clearer one thing has become: Trees are just so incredibly wild.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: the* ˈklɪrər wən θɪŋ həz bɪˈkəm: triz ər ʤɪst soʊ ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli waɪld.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: And we have so much more to learn.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: ənd wi hæv soʊ məʧ mɔr tɪ learn.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: People once believed that all fungi — even those allied with trees — were just strange plants.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: people* wəns bɪˈlivd ðət ɔl ˈfənʤaɪ —* ˈivɪn ðoʊz ˈæˌlaɪd wɪθ triz —* wər ʤɪst streɪnʤ plants.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: But then, some incredible minds discovered that they were actually an entirely different life form!
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: but* ðɛn, səm ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbəl maɪndz dɪˈskəvərd ðət ðeɪ wər ˈæˌkʧuəli ən ɪnˈtaɪərli ˈdɪfərənt laɪf form!

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: This breakthrough in understanding was powered by a combination of technical knowledge and problem-solving skills.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: this* ˈbreɪkθˌru ɪn ˌəndərˈstændɪŋ wɑz paʊərd baɪ ə ˌkɑmbəˈneɪʃən əv ˈtɛknɪkəl ˈnɑlɪʤ ənd problem-solving* skills.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: And you can start building both right now, for free, with Brilliant.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: and* ju kən stɑrt ˈbɪldɪŋ boʊθ raɪt naʊ, fər fri, wɪθ brilliant.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Brilliant helps you excel in math, science, and computer science through visual, interactive learning that’s personalized for you.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: brilliant* hɛlps ju ɪkˈsɛl ɪn mæθ, saɪəns, ənd kəmˈpjutər saɪəns θru ˈvɪʒəwəl, ˌɪnərˈæktɪv ˈlərnɪŋ that’s* ˈpərsənəˌlaɪzd fər you.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: And as you master new concepts, you’ll also become
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: and* ɛz ju ˈmæstər nu ˈkɑnsɛpts, you’ll* ˈɔlsoʊ become

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: a better thinker and problem solver — and start seeing the world in a whole new way.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: a* ˈbɛtər ˈθɪŋkər ənd ˈprɑbləm ˈsɑlvər —* ənd stɑrt siɪŋ ðə wərld ɪn ə hoʊl nu way.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Brilliant is designed to be highly effective.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: brilliant* ɪz dɪˈzaɪnd tɪ bi ˈhaɪli effective.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: You learn by doing — a method shown to be far more powerful than passive learning.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: you* lərn baɪ duɪŋ —* ə ˈmɛθəd ʃoʊn tɪ bi fɑr mɔr ˈpaʊərfəl ðən ˈpæsɪv learning.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: It also tailors your experience: starting you at the right level,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: it* ˈɔlsoʊ ˈteɪlərz jʊr ɪkˈspɪriəns: ˈstɑrtɪŋ ju æt ðə raɪt level,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: customizing your practice sets and reviews, and helping you advance at your ideal pace.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: customizing* jʊr ˈpræktɪs sɛts ənd rəvˈjuz, ənd ˈhɛlpɪŋ ju ədˈvæns æt jʊr aɪˈdil pace.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Whether you’re mastering fundamental math, algebra, or calculus, diving into algorithms or exploring everyday physics, Brilliant will help
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: whether* you’re* ˈmæstərɪŋ ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl mæθ, ˈælʤəbrə, ər ˈkælkjələs, ˈdaɪvɪŋ ˈɪntu ˈælgərˌɪðəmz ər ɪkˈsplɔrɪŋ ˈɛvriˈdeɪ ˈfɪzɪks, ˈbrɪljənt wɪl help

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: you reach big learning goals – and give you the skills to make breakthroughs of your own.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: you* riʧ bɪg ˈlərnɪŋ goʊlz –* ənd gɪv ju ðə skɪlz tɪ meɪk ˈbreɪkθˌruz əv jʊr own.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: To explore everything Brilliant has to offer for free for a full 30 days, go to
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: to* ɪkˈsplɔr ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ˈbrɪljənt həz tɪ ˈɔfər fər fri fər ə fʊl 30 deɪz, goʊ tɪ

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: brilliant.org/nutshell, scan the QR code on the screen, or click on the link in the description.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: brilliant.org/nutshell*, skæn ðə qr* koʊd ɔn ðə skrin, ər klɪk ɔn ðə lɪŋk ɪn ðə description.

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: Brilliant’s also given our viewers 20% off an annual Premium subscription,
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: brilliant’s* ˈɔlsoʊ ˈgɪvɪn ɑr vjuərz 20 ɔf ən ˈænjuəl ˈprimiəm subscription,

pHJIhxZEoxg.text: which gives you unlimited daily access to everything on Brilliant.
pHJIhxZEoxg.ipa: which* gɪvz ju ənˈlɪmɪtɪd ˈdeɪli ˈækˌsɛs tɪ ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ɔn ˈbrɪljənt.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: You exist in a truly secret place nobody but you can enter: Your mind.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ju ɪgˈzɪst ɪn ə ˈtruli ˈsikrɪt pleɪs ˈnoʊˌbɑˌdi bət ju kən ˈɛnər: jʊr mind.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: When we say mind we mean the place where you, whatever you are, exist. It's the totality of your inner world,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: when* wi seɪ maɪnd wi min ðə pleɪs wɛr ju, ˌwəˈtɛvər ju ər, ɪgˈzɪst. ɪts ðə toʊˈtæləti əv jʊr ˈɪnər world,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: the location of your consciousness and memories, the toolset of your intelligence.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: the* loʊˈkeɪʃən əv jʊr ˈkɑnʃəsnəs ənd ˈmɛməriz, ðə toolset* əv jʊr intelligence.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: It’s the place where all of your wants, thoughts and emotions happen,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: it’s* ðə pleɪs wɛr ɔl əv jʊr wɔnts, θɔts ənd ˈɪˌmoʊʃənz happen,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: where you think, learn, dream and imagine things – consciously and unconsciously.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: where* ju θɪŋk, lərn, drim ənd ˌɪˈmæʤən θɪŋz –* ˈkɑnʃəsli ənd unconsciously.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Your mind is truly your own, a universe only directly accessible and inhabited
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: your* maɪnd ɪz ˈtruli jʊr oʊn, ə ˈjunəˌvərs ˈoʊnli dɪˈrɛkli ækˈsɛsəbəl ənd inhabited

dl0-TveDDGA.text: by you – and inaccessible to anyone else in the universe.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: by* ju –* ənd ˌɪnəkˈsɛsəbəl tɪ ˈɛniˌwən ɛls ɪn ðə universe.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: And your mind is probably unique to you.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: and* jʊr maɪnd ɪz ˈprɑbəˌbli juˈnik tɪ you.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Probably, because how would we know if two minds are the same? What do you see when you imagine an apple?
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: probably*, bɪˈkəz haʊ wʊd wi noʊ ɪf tu maɪndz ər ðə seɪm? wət du ju si wɪn ju ˌɪˈmæʤən ən apple?

dl0-TveDDGA.text: A detailed scenery, a crisp image with clear details and colour? Is the apple hanging in a void? Can you rotate it?
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: a* dɪˈteɪld ˈsinəri, ə krɪsp ˈɪmɪʤ wɪθ klɪr ˈditeɪlz ənd ˈkələr? ɪz ðə ˈæpəl ˈhæŋɪŋ ɪn ə vɔɪd? kən ju ˈroʊˌteɪt it?

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Is it a 2D shape? Is it colorless or transparent or very vague?
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: is* ɪt ə 2d* ʃeɪp? ɪz ɪt ˈkələrləs ər trænˈspɛrənt ər ˈvɛri vague?

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Or don’t you see anything at all but feel what an apple feels like to you? Maybe a weird mix?
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ər don’t* ju si ˈɛniˌθɪŋ æt ɔl bət fil wət ən ˈæpəl filz laɪk tɪ ju? ˈmeɪbi ə wɪrd mix?

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Do you have an internal voice that narrates your life? Do you have an inner monologue? Is there silence inside your mind?
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: do* ju hæv ən ˌɪnˈtərnəl vɔɪs ðət ˈnɛreɪts jʊr laɪf? du ju hæv ən ˈɪnər ˈmɑnəˌlɔg? ɪz ðɛr ˈsaɪləns ˌɪnˈsaɪd jʊr mind?

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Do you only hear voices when you read? Or do you process thoughts nonverbally or even just feel the world around you?
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: do* ju ˈoʊnli hir ˈvɔɪsɪz wɪn ju rɛd? ər du ju ˈprɔˌsɛs θɔts nonverbally* ər ˈivɪn ʤɪst fil ðə wərld əraʊnd you?

dl0-TveDDGA.text: It is a bit stunning how many variations of inner universes humans have and how different our minds must be.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: it* ɪz ə bɪt ˈstənɪŋ haʊ ˈmɛni ˌvɛriˈeɪʃənz əv ˈɪnər ˈjunəˌvərsɪz ˈjumənz hæv ənd haʊ ˈdɪfərənt ɑr maɪndz məst be.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Humans have arguably the most complex minds on earth but today it is widely
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: humans* hæv ˈɑrgjuəbli ðə moʊst ˈkɑmplɛks maɪndz ɔn ərθ bət təˈdeɪ ɪt ɪz widely

dl0-TveDDGA.text: accepted that minds are not exclusive to us. There are trillions of animal minds out in the
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: accepted* ðət maɪndz ər nɑt ɪkˈsklusɪv tɪ ˈjuˈɛs. ðɛr ər ˈtrɪljənz əv ˈænəməl maɪndz aʊt ɪn the

dl0-TveDDGA.text: world, uncountable little secret universes, forever locked to us.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: world*, uncountable* ˈlɪtəl ˈsikrɪt ˈjunəˌvərsɪz, fərˈɛvər lɑkt tɪ us.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: And by looking at them and their evolution we may be able to learn
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: and* baɪ ˈlʊkɪŋ æt ðɛm ənd ðɛr ˌɛvəˈluʃən wi meɪ bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ learn

dl0-TveDDGA.text: why our minds are as stunning as they are today.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: why* ɑr maɪndz ər ɛz ˈstənɪŋ ɛz ðeɪ ər təˈdeɪ.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: A pretty wild idea is that minds might have originally evolved to control your movements.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ə ˈprɪti waɪld aɪˈdiə ɪz ðət maɪndz maɪt hæv ərˈɪʤənəli ɪˈvɑlvd tɪ kənˈtroʊl jʊr movements.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: To create a gap between all of your sensory input,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: to* kriˈeɪt ə gæp bɪtˈwin ɔl əv jʊr ˈsɛnsəri input,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: and your motor output, how you react to information by moving your body.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: and* jʊr ˈmoʊtər ˈaʊtˌpʊt, haʊ ju riækt tɪ ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən baɪ ˈmuvɪŋ jʊr body.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: A Tool To Deal with the World
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: a* tul tɪ dil wɪθ ðə world

dl0-TveDDGA.text: When life emerged it was without any minds but just cells, able to eat, poop and reproduce.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: when* laɪf ˈimərʤd ɪt wɑz wɪˈθaʊt ˈɛni maɪndz bət ʤɪst sɛlz, ˈeɪbəl tɪ it, pup ənd reproduce.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: They already had to navigate a complex and dangerous world and to process sensory input – but they were extremely inflexible.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: they* ɔˈrɛdi hæd tɪ ˈnævəˌgeɪt ə ˈkɑmplɛks ənd ˈdeɪnʤərəs wərld ənd tɪ ˈprɔˌsɛs ˈsɛnsəri ˈɪnˌpʊt –* bət ðeɪ wər ɪkˈstrimli inflexible.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: If a cell had the inner state “hungry” and sensed “food” it automatically moved in that
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: if* ə sɛl hæd ðə ˈɪnər steɪt ““hungry”*” ənd sɛnst ““food”*” ɪt ˌɔtəˈmætɪkli muvd ɪn that

dl0-TveDDGA.text: direction – or it flapped around randomly until it ran into a meal by accident.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: direction* –* ər ɪt flæpt əraʊnd ˈrændəmli ənˈtɪl ɪt ræn ˈɪntu ə mil baɪ accident.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: This worked well enough for billions of years and still does. But as life exploded in complexity and became
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: this* wərkt wɛl ɪˈnəf fər ˈbɪljənz əv jɪrz ənd stɪl dɪz. bət ɛz laɪf ɪkˈsploʊdɪd ɪn kəmˈplɛksɪti ənd became

dl0-TveDDGA.text: multicellular, cells began to dedicate themselves to processing information.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: multicellular*, sɛlz bɪˈgæn tɪ ˈdɛdəˌkeɪt ðɛmˈsɛlvz tɪ ˈprɑsɛsɪŋ ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: The first very simple “minds” emerged: little more than tiny gaps.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ðə fərst ˈvɛri ˈsɪmpəl ““minds”*” ˈimərʤd: ˈlɪtəl mɔr ðən ˈtaɪni gaps.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Virtual spaces in which sensory information could be processed for a short moment before these early animals had to react.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: virtual* ˈspeɪsɪz ɪn wɪʧ ˈsɛnsəri ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən kʊd bi ˈprɑsɛst fər ə ʃɔrt ˈmoʊmənt ˌbiˈfɔr ðiz ˈərli ˈænəməlz hæd tɪ react.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Roundworms only have 302 neurons in total, yet they are already able to learn things like “this thing
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: roundworms* ˈoʊnli hæv 302 ˈnʊrɑnz ɪn ˈtoʊtəl, jɛt ðeɪ ər ɔˈrɛdi ˈeɪbəl tɪ lərn θɪŋz laɪk ““this* thing

dl0-TveDDGA.text: is bad” and retain a memory that changes behavior for a few hours before they forget again.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: is* bad”*” ənd rɪˈteɪn ə ˈmɛməri ðət ˈʧeɪnʤɪz bɪˈheɪvjər fər ə fju aʊərz ˌbiˈfɔr ðeɪ fərˈgɛt again.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Minds on this level most likely don’t reason or think, but use simple rules that work well in their environments.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: minds* ɔn ðɪs ˈlɛvəl moʊst ˈlaɪkli don’t* ˈrizən ər θɪŋk, bət juz ˈsɪmpəl rulz ðət wərk wɛl ɪn ðɛr environments.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Scientists still debate whether this counts as mind or are still just preprogrammed automated reflexes.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: scientists* stɪl dəˈbeɪt ˈwɛðər ðɪs kaʊnts ɛz maɪnd ər ər stɪl ʤɪst preprogrammed* ˈɔtəˌmeɪtɪd reflexes.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: With more neurons, animals can “freeze” before they act. Process and interpret information and make
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: with* mɔr ˈnʊrɑnz, ˈænəməlz kən ““freeze”*” ˌbiˈfɔr ðeɪ ækt. ˈprɔˌsɛs ənd ˌɪnˈtərprət ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ənd make

dl0-TveDDGA.text: decisions for what is the best option at that moment.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: decisions* fər wət ɪz ðə bɛst ˈɔpʃən æt ðət moment.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: This is arguably where a true inner space, a mind begins to emerge.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: this* ɪz ˈɑrgjuəbli wɛr ə tru ˈɪnər speɪs, ə maɪnd bɪˈgɪnz tɪ emerge.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: And some insects may have something like it, like our buddy the bee.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: and* səm ˈɪnˌsɛkts meɪ hæv ˈsəmθɪŋ laɪk ɪt, laɪk ɑr ˈbədi ðə bi.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: With a brain smaller than a sesame seed and about one million neurons, a bee has a much larger mind gap.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: wɪθ ə breɪn sˈmɔlər ðən ə ˈsɛsəmi sid ənd əˈbaʊt wən ˈmɪljən ˈnʊrɑnz, ə bi həz ə məʧ ˈlɑrʤər maɪnd gap.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: And it is filled with information about the world.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: and* ɪt ɪz fɪld wɪθ ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən əˈbaʊt ðə world.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: It builds and remembers a huge mental map of flowers over square kilometers of terrain,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: it* bɪldz ənd rɪˈmɛmbərz ə juʤ ˈmɛntəl mæp əv flaʊərz ˈoʊvər skwɛr ˈkɪləˌmitərz əv terrain,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: cross referencing it with the position of the sun to find its way.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: cross* ˈrɛfərənsɪŋ ɪt wɪθ ðə pəˈzɪʃən əv ðə sən tɪ faɪnd ɪts way.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Bees can take shortcuts, so they can’t be simply following reflexes but are actually navigating their internal maps.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: bees* kən teɪk ˈʃɔrtˌkəts, soʊ ðeɪ can’t* bi ˈsɪmpli ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ˈriflɛksəz bət ər ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈnævəˌgeɪtɪŋ ðɛr ˌɪnˈtərnəl maps.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: In extreme cases when resources are scarce, bees can move up to 10km from
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: in* ɪkˈstrim ˈkeɪsɪz wɪn ˈrisɔrsɪz ər skɛrs, biz kən muv əp tɪ 10km* from

dl0-TveDDGA.text: their hives to collect food – the equivalent of going from Paris to Rome for groceries.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: their* haɪvz tɪ kəˈlɛkt fud –* ðə ɪkˈwɪvələnt əv goʊɪŋ frəm ˈpɛrɪs tɪ roʊm fər groceries.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: And they teach the location of the food by dancing.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: and* ðeɪ tiʧ ðə loʊˈkeɪʃən əv ðə fud baɪ dancing.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Minds have emerged a few times in evolutionary history and evolved in different directions,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: minds* hæv ˈimərʤd ə fju taɪmz ɪn ˌɛvəˈluʃəˌnɛri ˈhɪstəri ənd ɪˈvɑlvd ɪn ˈdɪfərənt directions,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: so while we are telling this story by increasing complexity, in reality minds are
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: so* waɪl wi ər ˈtɛlɪŋ ðɪs ˈstɔri baɪ ˌɪnˈkrisɪŋ kəmˈplɛksɪti, ɪn ˌriˈæləˌti maɪndz ər

dl0-TveDDGA.text: more like a weird mix of different experiments, depending on what niches the animals fill.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: mɔr laɪk ə wɪrd mɪks əv ˈdɪfərənt ɪkˈspɛrəmənts, dɪˈpɛndɪŋ ɔn wət ˈnɪʧɪz ðə ˈænəməlz fill.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: If your mind wants to experiment with a business idea, our partner Odoo has got you covered.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: if* jʊr maɪnd wɔnts tɪ ɪkˈspɛrəmənt wɪθ ə ˈbɪznɪs aɪˈdiə, ɑr ˈpɑrtnər odoo* həz gɑt ju covered.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Odoo is an all-in-one management software that helps you run your business every day.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: odoo* ɪz ən all-in-one* ˈmænɪʤmənt ˈsɔfˌwɛr ðət hɛlps ju rən jʊr ˈbɪznɪs ˈɛvəri day.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Every business idea starts simple, but things can get complex fast.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: every* ˈbɪznɪs aɪˈdiə stɑrts ˈsɪmpəl, bət θɪŋz kən gɪt ˈkɑmplɛks fast.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: That’s where Odoo comes in: with more than 45 user-friendly apps, you can handle everything
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: that’s* wɛr odoo* kəmz ɪn: wɪθ mɔr ðən 45 user-friendly* æps, ju kən ˈhændəl everything

dl0-TveDDGA.text: from accounting and inventory to CRM and much more – all centralized on a single platform.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: from* əˈkaʊnɪŋ ənd ˌɪnvənˈtɔri tɪ crm* ənd məʧ mɔr –* ɔl ˈsɛntrəˌlaɪzd ɔn ə ˈsɪŋgəl platform.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: For example, you can create and send invoices in seconds: just select your customer,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: for* ɪgˈzæmpəl, ju kən kriˈeɪt ənd sɛnd ˈɪnvɔɪsɪz ɪn ˈsɛkəndz: ʤɪst səˈlɛkt jʊr customer,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: add your products and quantities, define the billing terms, and send everything via email.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: add* jʊr ˈprɑdəkts ənd kˈwɑntətiz, dɪˈfaɪn ðə ˈbɪlɪŋ tərmz, ənd sɛnd ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ˈviə email.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Plus, the built-in chatter gives you an overview of your emails, notes and activities – all in one place.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: plus*, ðə ˈbɪlˌtɪn ˈʧætər gɪvz ju ən ˈoʊvərvˌju əv jʊr iˈmeɪlz, noʊts ənd ækˈtɪvɪtiz –* ɔl ɪn wən place.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Tracking your payments, with clear statuses and deadlines, is just as simple.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: tracking* jʊr ˈpeɪmənts, wɪθ klɪr statuses* ənd ˈdɛˌdlaɪnz, ɪz ʤɪst ɛz ˈsɪmpəl.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: You can try all these features for free – because your first Odoo app is free forever,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ju kən traɪ ɔl ðiz ˈfiʧərz fər fri –* bɪˈkəz jʊr fərst odoo* æp ɪz fri forever,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: with unlimited support and hosting included.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: with* ənˈlɪmɪtɪd səˈpɔrt ənd ˈhoʊstɪŋ included.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: When you’re ready to scale up, Odoo adapts to your needs – you can simply add more apps.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: when* you’re* ˈrɛdi tɪ skeɪl əp, odoo* əˈdæpts tɪ jʊr nidz –* ju kən ˈsɪmpli æd mɔr apps.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: And you get access to all applications starting at just $24.90 a month.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: and* ju gɪt ˈækˌsɛs tɪ ɔl ˌæpləˈkeɪʃənz ˈstɑrtɪŋ æt ʤɪst 24.90* ə month.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: With Odoo, your business mind knows no limits. And now back to the video!
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: with* odoo*, jʊr ˈbɪznɪs maɪnd noʊz noʊ ˈlɪmɪts. ənd naʊ bæk tɪ ðə video!

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Mind complexity probably increases dramatically as we add more neurons, like the octopus that has about 500 million of them.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: mind* kəmˈplɛksɪti ˈprɑbəˌbli ˌɪnˈkrisɪz drəˈmætɪkəli ɛz wi æd mɔr ˈnʊrɑnz, laɪk ðə ˈɑktəˌpʊs ðət həz əˈbaʊt 500 ˈmɪljən əv them.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Only 40% of their neurons are in their central brain, while each of their arms has their own
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: only* 40 əv ðɛr ˈnʊrɑnz ər ɪn ðɛr ˈsɛntrəl breɪn, waɪl iʧ əv ðɛr ɑrmz həz ðɛr own

dl0-TveDDGA.text: mini nerve centers that taste, process information locally at the same time and act on their own.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: mini* nərv ˈsɛnərz ðət teɪst, ˈprɔˌsɛs ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ˈloʊkəli æt ðə seɪm taɪm ənd ækt ɔn ðɛr own.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: The central brain does still coordinate them for complex behavior, like catching prey,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: the* ˈsɛntrəl breɪn dɪz stɪl koʊˈɔrdəˌneɪt ðɛm fər ˈkɑmplɛks bɪˈheɪvjər, laɪk ˈkæʧɪŋ prey,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: because imagine 8 arms doing their own thing all the time. But different arms are specialized and used primarily for different tasks,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: because* ˌɪˈmæʤən 8 ɑrmz duɪŋ ðɛr oʊn θɪŋ ɔl ðə taɪm. bət ˈdɪfərənt ɑrmz ər ˈspɛʃəˌlaɪzd ənd juzd praɪˈmɛrəli fər ˈdɪfərənt tæsks,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: like exploring or movement. And we know their arms can make decisions independently to some degree and have some autonomy.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: laɪk ɪkˈsplɔrɪŋ ər ˈmuvmənt. ənd wi noʊ ðɛr ɑrmz kən meɪk dɪˈsɪʒənz ˌɪndɪˈpɛndəntli tɪ səm dɪˈgri ənd hæv səm autonomy.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Octopuses are very intelligent animals, able to do loads of complex things, so their minds should be somewhat complex.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: octopuses* ər ˈvɛri ˌɪnˈtɛləʤənt ˈænəməlz, ˈeɪbəl tɪ du loʊdz əv ˈkɑmplɛks θɪŋz, soʊ ðɛr maɪndz ʃʊd bi ˈsəmˈwət complex.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: But how does their mind experience things? Is it distributed over their whole body?
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: but* haʊ dɪz ðɛr maɪnd ɪkˈspɪriəns θɪŋz? ɪz ɪt dɪˈstrɪbjətəd ˈoʊvər ðɛr hoʊl ˈbɑdi?

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Does an Octopus feel like multiple smaller minds interacting as one thing?
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: does* ən ˈɑktəˌpʊs fil laɪk ˈməltəpəl sˈmɔlər maɪndz ˌɪnərˈæktɪŋ ɛz wən thing?

dl0-TveDDGA.text: If octopuses show how strange minds can be,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: if* octopuses* ʃoʊ haʊ streɪnʤ maɪndz kən be,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: birds show how sophisticated they can become if it develops in a different direction.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: birds* ʃoʊ haʊ səˈfɪstɪˌkeɪtəd ðeɪ kən bɪˈkəm ɪf ɪt dɪˈvɛləps ɪn ə ˈdɪfərənt direction.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Some bird species with billions of neurons probably have surprisingly sophisticated minds,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: some* bərd ˈspiʃiz wɪθ ˈbɪljənz əv ˈnʊrɑnz ˈprɑbəˌbli hæv səˈpraɪzɪŋli səˈfɪstɪˌkeɪtəd minds,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: where they can go one step further: While minds may have started as a gap to delay action,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: where* ðeɪ kən goʊ wən stɛp ˈfərðər: waɪl maɪndz meɪ hæv ˈstɑrtɪd ɛz ə gæp tɪ dɪˈleɪ action,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: bird minds are able to simulate a map of reality, including other players. Scrub Jays are hoarders that catch and hide all
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: bird* maɪndz ər ˈeɪbəl tɪ ˈsɪmjəˌleɪt ə mæp əv ˌriˈæləˌti, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ˈəðər pleɪərz. skrəb ʤeɪz ər hoarders* ðət kæʧ ənd haɪd all

dl0-TveDDGA.text: sorts of food in different places, from worms to nuts.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: sorts* əv fud ɪn ˈdɪfərənt ˈpleɪsɪz, frəm wərmz tɪ nəts.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Inside their mind they keep track of time and are aware that fresh food goes bad.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðɛr maɪnd ðeɪ kip træk əv taɪm ənd ər əˈwɛr ðət frɛʃ fud goʊz bad.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: So after a few days have passed they will ignore the hiding places of
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: so* ˈæftər ə fju deɪz hæv pæst ðeɪ wɪl ˌɪgˈnɔr ðə ˈhaɪdɪŋ ˈpleɪsɪz of

dl0-TveDDGA.text: dead worms and instead go for the nuts that are still good.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: dead* wərmz ənd ˌɪnˈstɛd goʊ fər ðə nəts ðət ər stɪl good.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Probably more impressive, they might be able to simulate the minds of
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: probably* mɔr ˌɪmˈprɛsɪv, ðeɪ maɪt bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ ˈsɪmjəˌleɪt ðə maɪndz of

dl0-TveDDGA.text: other birds – If a scrub jay is hiding food and notices another jay watching,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: other* bərdz –* ɪf ə skrəb ʤeɪ ɪz ˈhaɪdɪŋ fud ənd ˈnoʊtɪsɪz əˈnəðər ʤeɪ watching,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: they will return later and re-hide their stash so it doesn’t get stolen.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: they* wɪl rɪˈtərn ˈleɪtər ənd re-hide* ðɛr stæʃ soʊ ɪt doesn’t* gɪt stolen.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Which is a perfect transition to what makes the minds of humans so special:
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: which* ɪz ə ˈpərˌfɪkt trænˈzɪʃən tɪ wət meɪks ðə maɪndz əv ˈjumənz soʊ special:

dl0-TveDDGA.text: While we are probably not the only animals that can simulate other minds inside our
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: while* wi ər ˈprɑbəˌbli nɑt ðə ˈoʊnli ˈænəməlz ðət kən ˈsɪmjəˌleɪt ˈəðər maɪndz ˌɪnˈsaɪd our

dl0-TveDDGA.text: mind – humans with our 86 billion neurons have taken it to extraordinary depths.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: mind* –* ˈjumənz wɪθ ɑr 86 ˈbɪljən ˈnʊrɑnz hæv ˈteɪkən ɪt tɪ ˌɛkstrəˈɔrdəˌnɛri depths.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: The Internal Universe
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: the* ˌɪnˈtərnəl ˈjunəˌvərs

dl0-TveDDGA.text: When humans are born they are cute but selfish blobs of meat, solely focused on their own needs.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: wɪn ˈjumənz ər bɔrn ðeɪ ər kjut bət ˈsɛlfɪʃ blɑbz əv mit, ˈsoʊəli ˈfoʊkɪst ɔn ðɛr oʊn needs.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: But around 18–24 months a baby becomes able to recognise itself in a mirror.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: but* əraʊnd 18–24* mənθs ə ˈbeɪbi bɪˈkəmz ˈeɪbəl tɪ ˈrɛkəgˌnaɪz ˌɪtˈsɛlf ɪn ə mirror.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: It realizes that something that seems to be “other” is actually
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: it* ˈriəˌlaɪzɪz ðət ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət simz tɪ bi ““other”*” ɪz actually

dl0-TveDDGA.text: itself – which is usually a delightful experience for the human larvae.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: itself* –* wɪʧ ɪz ˈjuʒəwəli ə dɪˈlaɪtfəl ɪkˈspɪriəns fər ðə ˈjumən larvae.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: But it is also the first step of a powerful realization:
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: but* ɪt ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ ðə fərst stɛp əv ə ˈpaʊərfəl realization:

dl0-TveDDGA.text: You are not just an observer in the world – there are also others that can observe you.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: you* ər nɑt ʤɪst ən əbˈzərvər ɪn ðə wərld –* ðɛr ər ˈɔlsoʊ ˈəðərz ðət kən əbˈzərv you.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: You see and you are seen. And if you are seen… well then you can’t just behave egoistically all
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: you* si ənd ju ər sin. ənd ɪf ju ər seen…*… wɛl ðɛn ju can’t* ʤɪst bɪˈheɪv egoistically* all

dl0-TveDDGA.text: the time – because you need the goodwill of others to stay alive.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: the* taɪm –* bɪˈkəz ju nid ðə ˈgʊdˈwɪl əv ˈəðərz tɪ steɪ alive.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: We humans are not just simulating other minds in our minds. We simulate minds, simulating minds.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: we* ˈjumənz ər nɑt ʤɪst ˈsɪmjəˌleɪtɪŋ ˈəðər maɪndz ɪn ɑr maɪndz. wi ˈsɪmjəˌleɪt maɪndz, ˈsɪmjəˌleɪtɪŋ minds.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: We think about what others think. We think about what they think about us thinking about what they think. And so on.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: we* θɪŋk əˈbaʊt wət ˈəðərz θɪŋk. wi θɪŋk əˈbaʊt wət ðeɪ θɪŋk əˈbaʊt ˈjuˈɛs ˈθɪŋkɪŋ əˈbaʊt wət ðeɪ θɪŋk. ənd soʊ ɔn.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: There are a lot of layers.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ðɛr ər ə lɔt əv layers.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: This awareness of the minds of others may be one of the origins of our moral conscience and a huge
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: this* əˈwɛrnəs əv ðə maɪndz əv ˈəðərz meɪ bi wən əv ðə ˈɔrəʤɪnz əv ɑr ˈmɔrəl ˈkɑnʃəns ənd ə huge

dl0-TveDDGA.text: reason why we are able to live in large societies, with other humans we are not related to.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: reason* waɪ wi ər ˈeɪbəl tɪ lɪv ɪn lɑrʤ səˈsaɪɪtiz, wɪθ ˈəðər ˈjumənz wi ər nɑt rɪˈleɪtɪd to.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: And may have spawned one of our most charming obsessions: storytelling.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: and* meɪ hæv spɔnd wən əv ɑr moʊst ˈʧɑrmɪŋ əbˈsɛʃənz: storytelling.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: We don’t just remember that an event happened, we imagine what the people
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: we* don’t* ʤɪst rɪˈmɛmbər ðət ən ɪˈvɛnt ˈhæpənd, wi ˌɪˈmæʤən wət ðə people

dl0-TveDDGA.text: that were part of it were thinking, what we think they should have been thinking,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: that* wər pɑrt əv ɪt wər ˈθɪŋkɪŋ, wət wi θɪŋk ðeɪ ʃʊd hæv bɪn thinking,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: what might have happened if they acted and thought differently.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: what* maɪt hæv ˈhæpənd ɪf ðeɪ ˈæktɪd ənd θɔt differently.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Combined with our ability to create fictional universes to plan ahead we have created
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: combined* wɪθ ɑr əˈbɪləˌti tɪ kriˈeɪt ˈfɪkʃənəl ˈjunəˌvərsɪz tɪ plæn əˈhɛd wi hæv created

dl0-TveDDGA.text: countless fictional worlds, with people that don’t exist but that we deeply relate to. That are real to us.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: countless* ˈfɪkʃənəl wərldz, wɪθ ˈpipəl ðət don’t* ɪgˈzɪst bət ðət wi ˈdipli rɪˈleɪt tɪ. ðət ər ril tɪ us.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Every movie, novel or comic is humans sharing their internal simulations with each other.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: every* ˈmuvi, ˈnɑvəl ər ˈkɑmɪk ɪz ˈjumənz ˈʃɛrɪŋ ðɛr ˌɪnˈtərnəl ˌsɪmjəˈleɪʃənz wɪθ iʧ ˈəðər.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Whether you see a photorealistic apple in your mind or feel the concept of an apple,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ˈwɛðər ju si ə photorealistic* ˈæpəl ɪn jʊr maɪnd ər fil ðə ˈkɑnsɛpt əv ən apple,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: in the storytelling space we are all able to come together and understand each other.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: in* ðə ˈstɔriˌtɛlɪŋ speɪs wi ər ɔl ˈeɪbəl tɪ kəm təˈgɛðər ənd ˌəndərˈstænd iʧ other.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: This is how we transport information about morality and our values, about what we think is good or bad.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: this* ɪz haʊ wi ˈtrænspɔrt ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən əˈbaʊt ˌmɔˈræləˌti ənd ɑr ˈvæljuz, əˈbaʊt wət wi θɪŋk ɪz gʊd ər bad.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: So maybe the most amazing thing about human minds is that they're fundamentally
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: so* ˈmeɪbi ðə moʊst əˈmeɪzɪŋ θɪŋ əˈbaʊt ˈjumən maɪndz ɪz ðət ðɛr fundamentally

dl0-TveDDGA.text: social - filled with voices, ideas, and perspectives from countless others.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: social*  fɪld wɪθ ˈvɔɪsɪz, aɪˈdiəz, ənd pərˈspɛktɪvz frəm ˈkaʊntləs others.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Your thoughts and feelings have been shaped by stories that never happened
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: your* θɔts ənd ˈfilɪŋz hæv bɪn ʃeɪpt baɪ ˈstɔriz ðət ˈnɛvər happened

dl0-TveDDGA.text: but that you've absorbed and simulated in your mind in a way that is unique to you.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: but* ðət juv əbˈzɔrbd ənd ˈsɪmjəˌleɪtɪd ɪn jʊr maɪnd ɪn ə weɪ ðət ɪz juˈnik tɪ you.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: In a very real sense, your secret mind isn't just yours,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: in* ə ˈvɛri ril sɛns, jʊr ˈsikrɪt maɪnd ˈɪzənt ʤɪst yours,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: but a collaborative creation between you and all the human minds that came before.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: but* ə kəˈlæbərˌeɪtɪv kriˈeɪʃən bɪtˈwin ju ənd ɔl ðə ˈjumən maɪndz ðət keɪm before.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: And if you want you can add your stories as well. For the ones that come after.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: and* ɪf ju wɔnt ju kən æd jʊr ˈstɔriz ɛz wɛl. fər ðə wənz ðət kəm ˈæftər.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Our latest Limited Edition Pin is here – kicking off the new Cosmic Time Collection. It’s an homage to Halley’s Comet,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ɑr ˈleɪtəst ˈlɪmɪtɪd ɪˈdɪʃən pɪn ɪz hir –* ˈkɪkɪŋ ɔf ðə nu ˈkɑzmɪk taɪm kəˈlɛkʃən. it’s* ən ˈɑməʤ tɪ halley’s* comet,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: the cosmic traveler that has been visiting Earth for millennia.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: the* ˈkɑzmɪk ˈtrævələr ðət həz bɪn ˈvɪzɪtɪŋ ərθ fər millennia.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: It only returns around every 75 years but we captured it in a super sparkly, rotating pin.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: it* ˈoʊnli rɪˈtərnz əraʊnd ˈɛvəri 75 jɪrz bət wi ˈkæpʧərd ɪt ɪn ə ˈsupər ˈspɑrkli, ˈroʊˌteɪtɪŋ pin.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Only 10,000 exist, and each one is individually numbered. Wear it to complete your outfit, add it to your collection,
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: only* 10,000* ɪgˈzɪst, ənd iʧ wən ɪz ˌɪndɪˈvɪʤəli ˈnəmbərd. wɛr ɪt tɪ kəmˈplit jʊr ˈaʊtˌfɪt, æd ɪt tɪ jʊr collection,

dl0-TveDDGA.text: or make it the first piece of a brand new one. Over the years we’ve created quite a tradition
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: or* meɪk ɪt ðə fərst pis əv ə brænd nu wən. ˈoʊvər ðə jɪrz we’ve* kriˈeɪtɪd kwaɪt ə tradition

dl0-TveDDGA.text: of these tiny sculptures – and you’ve loved collecting them. So we decided to take it one step further.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: of* ðiz ˈtaɪni ˈskəlpʧərz –* ənd you’ve* ləvd kəˈlɛktɪŋ ðɛm. soʊ wi ˌdɪˈsaɪdɪd tɪ teɪk ɪt wən stɛp further.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Meet the first ever kurzgesagt figurines – small collectible scenes from the kurzgesagt universe.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: meet* ðə fərst ˈɛvər kurzgesagt* ˌfɪgjərˈinz –* smɔl kəˈlɛktəbəl sinz frəm ðə kurzgesagt* universe.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: If you’ve been around the channel for a while, these might look a little familiar.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: if* you’ve* bɪn əraʊnd ðə ˈʧænəl fər ə waɪl, ðiz maɪt lʊk ə ˈlɪtəl familiar.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Mission Control, meet Duck – always ready for another trip through space.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: mission* kənˈtroʊl, mit dək –* ˈɔlˌweɪz ˈrɛdi fər əˈnəðər trɪp θru space.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Never stare directly into a deadly ray from space – this birb learned the hard way.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: never* stɛr dɪˈrɛkli ˈɪntu ə ˈdɛdli reɪ frəm speɪs –* ðɪs birb* ˈlərnɪd ðə hɑrd weɪ.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: And whatever you do… never press the big red button.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: ənd ˌwəˈtɛvər ju do…*… ˈnɛvər prɛs ðə bɪg rɛd button.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Get one – or all of them – for your desk, bookshelf, or honestly … your life in general.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: get* wən –* ər ɔl əv ðɛm –* fər jʊr dɛsk, ˈbʊkˌʃɛlf, ər ˈɑnəstli …* jʊr laɪf ɪn general.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: We had a blast designing them, packing each one with love and tiny details.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: we* hæd ə blæst dɪˈzaɪnɪŋ ðɛm, ˈpækɪŋ iʧ wən wɪθ ləv ənd ˈtaɪni details.

dl0-TveDDGA.text: Head over to our shop, we’ve put together a few deals for you. Get your special piece of kurzgesagt now.
dl0-TveDDGA.ipa: head* ˈoʊvər tɪ ɑr ʃɑp, we’ve* pʊt təˈgɛðər ə fju dilz fər ju. gɪt jʊr ˈspɛʃəl pis əv kurzgesagt* naʊ.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I have a name, you have a name, everyone has a 'name'.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: aɪ hæv ə neɪm, ju hæv ə neɪm, ˈɛvriˌwən həz ə 'name'.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: My name is Elizabeth, but Beth is what I go by.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: my* neɪm ɪz ɪˈlɪzəbɪθ, bət bɛθ ɪz wət aɪ goʊ by.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If you go by a name or nickname, people know to call you that name.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: if* ju goʊ baɪ ə neɪm ər ˈnɪkˌneɪm, ˈpipəl noʊ tɪ kɔl ju ðət name.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: His name is Philip, but he goes by Phil. You can also take a name.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: his* neɪm ɪz ˈfɪlɪp, bət hi goʊz baɪ fɪl. ju kən ˈɔlsoʊ teɪk ə name.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: For example, when you get married, you might take your partner's name.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: for* ɪgˈzæmpəl, wɪn ju gɪt ˈmɛrid, ju maɪt teɪk jʊr ˈpɑrtnərz name.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: She was Amy Smith, but she took her husband's name. Now she's Amy Scott.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: she* wɑz ˈeɪmi smɪθ, bət ʃi tʊk hər ˈhəzbəndz neɪm. naʊ ʃiz ˈeɪmi scott.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Now when Amy Scott signs her name, she has to remember to write it correctly.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: now* wɪn ˈeɪmi skɑt saɪnz hər neɪm, ʃi həz tɪ rɪˈmɛmbər tɪ raɪt ɪt correctly.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Can you sign your name here, please?
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: kən ju saɪn jʊr neɪm hir, please?

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If a name rings a bell, you've heard it before, but you can't remember why.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: if* ə neɪm rɪŋz ə bɛl, juv hərd ɪt ˌbiˈfɔr, bət ju kænt rɪˈmɛmbər why.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Do you remember Mr Hill? Er, the name rings a bell, but I can't remember who he is!
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: do* ju rɪˈmɛmbər ˈmɪstər hɪl? ər, ðə neɪm rɪŋz ə bɛl, bət aɪ kænt rɪˈmɛmbər hu hi is!

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We are BBC Learning English.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we* ər ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ english.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Remember our name so you can always learn more with us.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: remember* ɑr neɪm soʊ ju kən ˈɔlˌweɪz lərn mɔr wɪθ us.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Get with the programme! Learn some verbs you can use with the noun 'programme'.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: get* wɪθ ðə ˈproʊˌgræm! lərn səm vərbz ju kən juz wɪθ ðə naʊn 'programme'.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: The first step to set up a programme is to plan it. Drawing up a programme means designing it.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: the* fərst stɛp tɪ sɛt əp ə ˈproʊˌgræm ɪz tɪ plæn ɪt. drɔɪŋ əp ə ˈproʊˌgræm minz dɪˈzaɪnɪŋ it.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: When you agree on the programme, everyone thinks the same way about it.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: when* ju əˈgri ɔn ðə ˈproʊˌgræm, ˈɛvriˌwən θɪŋks ðə seɪm weɪ əˈbaʊt ɪt.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We could set up a research programme to look at this question.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: wi kʊd sɛt əp ə ˈrisərʧ ˈproʊˌgræm tɪ lʊk æt ðɪs question.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: It took a long time to draw up the course programme, and even longer to agree on it.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: it* tʊk ə lɔŋ taɪm tɪ drɔ əp ðə kɔrs ˈproʊˌgræm, ənd ˈivɪn ˈlɔŋgər tɪ əˈgri ɔn it.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Next, you launch your programme. That means start it.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: next*, ju lɔnʧ jʊr ˈproʊˌgræm. ðət minz stɑrt it.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We run a programme when we manage it or make it happen. We offer a programme when we make it available.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we* rən ə ˈproʊˌgræm wɪn wi ˈmænɪʤ ɪt ər meɪk ɪt ˈhæpən. wi ˈɔfər ə ˈproʊˌgræm wɪn wi meɪk ɪt available.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We decided to run a support programme. We'll launch it next month. The university offers a part-time programme.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we* ˌdɪˈsaɪdɪd tɪ rən ə səˈpɔrt ˈproʊˌgræm. wɪl lɔnʧ ɪt nɛkst mənθ. ðə ˌjunəˈvərsəti ˈɔfərz ə ˈpɑrtˈtaɪm programme.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: You enrol on a programme when you join it. Then, you're following a programme.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* enrol* ɔn ə ˈproʊˌgræm wɪn ju ʤɔɪn ɪt. ðɛn, jʊr ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ə programme.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: You complete a programme when you get to the end of it. I've been following this programme for nearly a year.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* kəmˈplit ə ˈproʊˌgræm wɪn ju gɪt tɪ ðə ɛnd əv ɪt. aɪv bɪn ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ðɪs ˈproʊˌgræm fər ˈnɪrli ə year.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I enrolled on it in September. I'll get a job after I complete the programme. We've drawn up this programme of videos.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: i* ɛnˈroʊld ɔn ɪt ɪn sɛpˈtɛmbər. aɪl gɪt ə ʤɑb ˈæftər aɪ kəmˈplit ðə ˈproʊˌgræm. wiv drɔn əp ðɪs ˈproʊˌgræm əv videos.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We hope you enjoy following it, and that you find it helpful.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we* hoʊp ju ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ɪt, ənd ðət ju faɪnd ɪt helpful.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We hope you're ready to learn, because we mean 'business'.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we* hoʊp jʊr ˈrɛdi tɪ lərn, bɪˈkəz wi min 'business'.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If you mean business, you are serious and determined about what you're doing.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: if* ju min ˈbɪznɪs, ju ər ˈsɪriəs ənd dɪˈtərmənd əˈbaʊt wət jʊr doing.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Meet Barbara. She works in business. It's the area of work she does.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: meet* ˈbɑrbərə. ʃi wərks ɪn ˈbɪznɪs. ɪts ðə ˈɛriə əv wərk ʃi does.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I'm an operations analyst and my husband is an auditor. We work in business.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: i'm* ən ˌɑpərˈeɪʃənz ˈænəlɪst ənd maɪ ˈhəzbənd ɪz ən ˈɔdɪtər. wi wərk ɪn business.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Now meet Joshua. He's set up a business.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: now* mit ˈʤɑˌʃuə. hiz sɛt əp ə business.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: That means he's prepared, planned and now has a business.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: that* minz hiz priˈpɛrd, plænd ənd naʊ həz ə ˈbɪznɪs.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I've set up a bakery. I make and sell cakes and bread.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: aɪv sɛt əp ə ˈbeɪkəri. aɪ meɪk ənd sɛl keɪks ənd bread.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: He's the owner, but he's also running the business. That means he's managing it and working in it.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: he's* ðə ˈoʊnər, bət hiz ˈɔlsoʊ ˈrənɪŋ ðə ˈbɪznɪs. ðət minz hiz ˈmænəʤɪŋ ɪt ənd ˈwərkɪŋ ɪn it.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Unfortunately, his customers aren't very happy.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: unfortunately*, hɪz ˈkəstəmərz ˈɑrənt ˈvɛri happy.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: £10 for a loaf of bread? You're going to go out of business with those prices!
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: £10* fər ə loʊf əv brɛd? jʊr goʊɪŋ tɪ goʊ aʊt əv ˈbɪznɪs wɪθ ðoʊz prices!

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If you go out of business, you don't make enough money and you have to close down.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: if* ju goʊ aʊt əv ˈbɪznɪs, ju doʊnt meɪk ɪˈnəf ˈməni ənd ju hæv tɪ kloʊz down.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Poor Joshua.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: poor* joshua.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We're full of hope that you'll learn verbs that go with 'hope' in this lesson. If you are full of hope,
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we're* fʊl əv hoʊp ðət jul lərn vərbz ðət goʊ wɪθ 'hoʊp' ɪn ðɪs ˈlɛsən. ɪf ju ər fʊl əv hope,

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: you really want something to happen or believe that it will.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* ˈrɪli wɔnt ˈsəmθɪŋ tɪ ˈhæpən ər bɪˈliv ðət ɪt will.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Zoe didn't know if the interview had gone well or not, but she was full of hope.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: zoe* ˈdɪdənt noʊ ɪf ðə ˈɪntərvˌju hæd gɔn wɛl ər nɑt, bət ʃi wɑz fʊl əv hope.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If you express hope, it means you tell somebody the thing you want to happen.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: if* ju ɪkˈsprɛs hoʊp, ɪt minz ju tɛl ˈsəmˌbɑdi ðə θɪŋ ju wɔnt tɪ happen.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Zoe expressed hope to her mum that she would get the job.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: zoe* ɪkˈsprɛst hoʊp tɪ hər məm ðət ʃi wʊd gɪt ðə job.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: You can bring somebody hope or give someone hope. That means you encourage and share the hope of the person you're with.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* kən brɪŋ ˈsəmˌbɑdi hoʊp ər gɪv ˈsəmˌwən hoʊp. ðət minz ju ɪnˈkərəʤ ənd ʃɛr ðə hoʊp əv ðə ˈpərsən jʊr with.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Zoe's mum gave her hope. "You're brilliant, no matter what the outcome," she said.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: zoe's* məm geɪv hər hoʊp. "jʊr ˈbrɪljənt, noʊ ˈmætər wət ðə ˈaʊtˌkəm," ʃi said.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If you live in hope, you hope for something, even if you know it probably won't happen.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: if* ju lɪv ɪn hoʊp, ju hoʊp fər ˈsəmθɪŋ, ˈivɪn ɪf ju noʊ ɪt ˈprɑbəˌbli woʊnt happen.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Zoe didn't get the job, but she lives in hope.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: zoe* ˈdɪdənt gɪt ðə ʤɑb, bət ʃi lɪvz ɪn hoʊp.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I hope I've brought you some hope for your English today!
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: aɪ hoʊp aɪv brɔt ju səm hoʊp fər jʊr ˈɪŋlɪʃ today!

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Let me give you some examples of verbs to use with 'example'.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: let* mi gɪv ju səm ɪgˈzæmpəlz əv vərbz tɪ juz wɪθ 'example'.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We give an example or provide an example if we tell it to someone or put it in our writing.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we* gɪv ən ɪgˈzæmpəl ər prəˈvaɪd ən ɪgˈzæmpəl ɪf wi tɛl ɪt tɪ ˈsəmˌwən ər pʊt ɪt ɪn ɑr writing.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I'll give you an example of why I'm annoyed!
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: i'll* gɪv ju ən ɪgˈzæmpəl əv waɪ əm annoyed!

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I can provide you with some examples of my work, so you can see what I can do.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: i* kən prəˈvaɪd ju wɪθ səm ɪgˈzæmpəlz əv maɪ wərk, soʊ ju kən si wət aɪ kən do.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: You might be trying to find examples that show something's true when you're doing research.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* maɪt bi traɪɪŋ tɪ faɪnd ɪgˈzæmpəlz ðət ʃoʊ ˈsəmθɪŋz tru wɪn jʊr duɪŋ research.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: You can take something as an example when you think it shows something.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* kən teɪk ˈsəmθɪŋ ɛz ən ɪgˈzæmpəl wɪn ju θɪŋk ɪt ʃoʊz something.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I found many examples of this problem in the data. We can take this song as an example of traditional music.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: i* faʊnd ˈmɛni ɪgˈzæmpəlz əv ðɪs ˈprɑbləm ɪn ðə ˈdætə. wi kən teɪk ðɪs sɔŋ ɛz ən ɪgˈzæmpəl əv trəˈdɪʃənəl music.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We often include examples in an argument. When you cite examples, you say that they support your argument.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we* ˈɔfən ˌɪnˈklud ɪgˈzæmpəlz ɪn ən ˈɑrgjəmənt. wɪn ju saɪt ɪgˈzæmpəlz, ju seɪ ðət ðeɪ səˈpɔrt jʊr argument.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Drawing from examples means using examples to make an argument. I've included examples of waste.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: drawing* frəm ɪgˈzæmpəlz minz ˈjuzɪŋ ɪgˈzæmpəlz tɪ meɪk ən ˈɑrgjəmənt. aɪv ˌɪnˈkludɪd ɪgˈzæmpəlz əv waste.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I can cite all of them to show why we're losing money.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: i* kən saɪt ɔl əv ðɛm tɪ ʃoʊ waɪ wɪr ˈluzɪŋ money.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I've drawn from a range of examples to show why this is a problem.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: i've* drɔn frəm ə reɪnʤ əv ɪgˈzæmpəlz tɪ ʃoʊ waɪ ðɪs ɪz ə problem.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We've provided some examples of verbs to use with 'example'. Have you found any others?
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we've* prəˈvaɪdɪd səm ɪgˈzæmpəlz əv vərbz tɪ juz wɪθ 'ɪgˈzæmpəl'. hæv ju faʊnd ˈɛni others?

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: How much 'information' can you store in your brain? Let's test it. Storing information means you keep it in a place
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: how* məʧ 'ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən' kən ju stɔr ɪn jʊr breɪn? lɛts tɛst ɪt. ˈstɔrɪŋ ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən minz ju kip ɪt ɪn ə place

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: where you can find it and use it later. The hospital stores all the information in locked cupboards,
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: where* ju kən faɪnd ɪt ənd juz ɪt ˈleɪtər. ðə ˈhɑˌspɪtəl stɔrz ɔl ðə ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ɪn lɑkt ˈkəbərdz,

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: but they should really start storing it digitally for extra security. If you need information from somewhere,
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: bət ðeɪ ʃʊd ˈrɪli stɑrt ˈstɔrɪŋ ɪt ˈdɪʤətəli fər ˈɛkstrə sɪˈkjʊrəti. ɪf ju nid ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən frəm somewhere,

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: you can ask for or request the information. Then they can decide to withhold the information, to not give it to you,
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* kən æsk fər ər rɪkˈwɛst ðə ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən. ðɛn ðeɪ kən ˌdɪˈsaɪd tɪ wɪθˈhoʊld ðə ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən, tɪ nɑt gɪv ɪt tɪ you,

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: or disclose the information, to give it to you. The scientist requested information from her colleague,
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: or* dɪˈskloʊz ðə ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən, tɪ gɪv ɪt tɪ ju. ðə ˈsaɪəntɪst rɪkˈwɛstɪd ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən frəm hər colleague,

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: but he said it was too early to disclose the information, so he withheld the information.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: but* hi sɛd ɪt wɑz tu ˈərli tɪ dɪˈskloʊz ðə ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən, soʊ hi wɪθˈhɛld ðə information.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If you really need information, you can dig up the information — make an effort to go and find it yourself.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: if* ju ˈrɪli nid ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən, ju kən dɪg əp ðə ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən —* meɪk ən ˈɛfərt tɪ goʊ ənd faɪnd ɪt yourself.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Imagine digging to find gold.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: imagine* ˈdɪgɪŋ tɪ faɪnd gold.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Mike dug up some information about ancestors to draw his family tree.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: mike* dəg əp səm ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən əˈbaʊt ˈænˌsɛstərz tɪ drɔ hɪz ˈfæməli tree.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Well done for digging up this information today!
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: well* dən fər ˈdɪgɪŋ əp ðɪs ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən today!

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Wait! I've got a plan. Let's learn verbs to use with 'plan'.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: wait*! aɪv gɑt ə plæn. lɛts lərn vərbz tɪ juz wɪθ 'plan'.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Before you can have a plan, you need to make one or develop one.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: before* ju kən hæv ə plæn, ju nid tɪ meɪk wən ər dɪˈvɛləp one.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: You devise a plan or formulate a plan when you think about it a lot.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* dɪˈvaɪz ə plæn ər ˈfɔrmjəˌleɪt ə plæn wɪn ju θɪŋk əˈbaʊt ɪt ə lot.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I asked Maria to develop a plan for tomorrow's project. She spent all day devising one.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: i* æst mərˈiə tɪ dɪˈvɛləp ə plæn fər təˈmɑˌroʊz ˈprɑʤɛkt. ʃi spɛnt ɔl deɪ dɪˈvaɪzɪŋ one.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We've carefully formulated a plan to get more clients. Next, it's time to implement your plan.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we've* ˈkɛrfəli ˈfɔrmjəˌleɪtɪd ə plæn tɪ gɪt mɔr klaɪənts. nɛkst, ɪts taɪm tɪ ˈɪmpləmənt jʊr plan.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: That means start doing it. We could also say we go ahead with a plan.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: that* minz stɑrt duɪŋ ɪt. wi kʊd ˈɔlsoʊ seɪ wi goʊ əˈhɛd wɪθ ə plan.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We press ahead with a plan if we're starting or continuing a difficult plan.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we* prɛs əˈhɛd wɪθ ə plæn ɪf wɪr ˈstɑrtɪŋ ər kənˈtɪnjuɪŋ ə ˈdɪfəkəlt plæn.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We're going ahead with plan B. It's easier to implement. You need to press ahead with the recovery plan.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: wɪr goʊɪŋ əˈhɛd wɪθ plæn bi. ɪts ˈiziər tɪ ˈɪmpləmənt. ju nid tɪ prɛs əˈhɛd wɪθ ðə rɪˈkəvəri plan.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If it doesn't work, you can drop a plan. You can also scrap a plan. They just mean stop.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: if* ɪt ˈdəzənt wərk, ju kən drɔp ə plæn. ju kən ˈɔlsoʊ skræp ə plæn. ðeɪ ʤɪst min stop.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: You can shelve a plan if you might come back to it later. We need to drop the plan. Results are bad.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* kən ʃɛlv ə plæn ɪf ju maɪt kəm bæk tɪ ɪt ˈleɪtər. wi nid tɪ drɔp ðə plæn. rɪˈzəlts ər bad.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Do you want to scrap the plan, or just shelve it?
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: do* ju wɔnt tɪ skræp ðə plæn, ər ʤɪst ʃɛlv it?

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Jump in the back and let's talk about verbs that go with 'car'.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: jump* ɪn ðə bæk ənd lɛts tɔk əˈbaʊt vərbz ðət goʊ wɪθ 'car'.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If you jump in the car, you get in. If you jump out, you get out. This is informal.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: if* ju ʤəmp ɪn ðə kɑr, ju gɪt ɪn. ɪf ju ʤəmp aʊt, ju gɪt aʊt. ðɪs ɪz informal.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Hey! Jump in. Let's go! You can go or travel by car.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: hey*! ʤəmp ɪn. lɛts goʊ! ju kən goʊ ər ˈtrævəl baɪ car.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: You're travelling to Australia by car from London? Isn't that a bit far?
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you're* ˈtrævəlɪŋ tɪ ɔˈstreɪljə baɪ kɑr frəm ˈləndən? ˈɪzənt ðət ə bɪt far?

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: To go backwards, you reverse the car, or back the car up.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: to* goʊ ˈbækwərdz, ju rɪˈvərs ðə kɑr, ər bæk ðə kɑr up.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: We missed the turning! Can you back up? If someone else is driving a bit slowly, you might overtake them,
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: we* mɪst ðə ˈtərnɪŋ! kən ju bæk əp? ɪf ˈsəmˌwən ɛls ɪz ˈdraɪvɪŋ ə bɪt sˈloʊli, ju maɪt ˈoʊvərˌteɪk them,

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: in order to be in front of them. I'm going to overtake this car. He's driving so slowly.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: in* ˈɔrdər tɪ bi ɪn frənt əv ðɛm. əm goʊɪŋ tɪ ˈoʊvərˌteɪk ðɪs kɑr. hiz ˈdraɪvɪŋ soʊ slowly.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: But be careful! In wet conditions, a car can slip or skid on the road. Hopefully, it won't crash.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: but* bi ˈkɛrfəl! ɪn wɛt kənˈdɪʃənz, ə kɑr kən slɪp ər skɪd ɔn ðə roʊd. ˈhoʊpfəli, ɪt woʊnt crash.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: He crashed his car after skidding on the ice.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: he* kræʃt hɪz kɑr ˈæftər ˈskɪdɪŋ ɔn ðə ice.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Watch more English in a Minute later — but not while driving the car!
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: watch* mɔr ˈɪŋlɪʃ ɪn ə ˈmɪnət ˈleɪtər —* bət nɑt waɪl ˈdraɪvɪŋ ðə car!

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Let's learn verbs commonly used with the word 'market'.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: let's* lərn vərbz ˈkɑmənli juzd wɪθ ðə wərd 'ˈmɑrkɪt'.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: If we put something on the market, it means we make it available for sale.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: ɪf wi pʊt ˈsəmθɪŋ ɔn ðə ˈmɑrkɪt, ɪt minz wi meɪk ɪt əˈveɪləbəl fər sale.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: I want to sell my house, but I haven't put it on the market yet.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: i* wɔnt tɪ sɛl maɪ haʊs, bət aɪ ˈhævənt pʊt ɪt ɔn ðə ˈmɑrkɪt yet.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Next, if a company wants to expand their customer base, they might want to break into new markets,
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: next*, ɪf ə ˈkəmpəˌni wɔnts tɪ ɪkˈspænd ðɛr ˈkəstəmər beɪs, ðeɪ maɪt wɔnt tɪ breɪk ˈɪntu nu markets,

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: to enter new markets, often with difficulty.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: to* ˈɛnər nu ˈmɑrkɪts, ˈɔfən wɪθ difficulty.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: It took years of research for the brand to finally break into the European market.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: it* tʊk jɪrz əv ˈrisərʧ fər ðə brænd tɪ ˈfaɪnəli breɪk ˈɪntu ðə ˌjʊrəˈpiən market.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Companies must then supply the market with their products — provide the necessary amount of a product to meet demand.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: companies* məst ðɛn səˈplaɪ ðə ˈmɑrkɪt wɪθ ðɛr ˈprɑdəkts —* prəˈvaɪd ðə ˈnɛsəˌsɛri əˈmaʊnt əv ə ˈprɑdəkt tɪ mit demand.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: The farm supplies the local market with fresh vegetables every week.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: the* fɑrm səˈplaɪz ðə ˈloʊkəl ˈmɑrkɪt wɪθ frɛʃ ˈvɛʤtəbəlz ˈɛvəri week.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: But too much of a product can flood the market —
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: but* tu məʧ əv ə ˈprɑdəkt kən fləd ðə ˈmɑrkɪt —

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: when there is too much of a product than people can buy.
Cheap laptops have flooded the market, so no-one's making a profit.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: when* ðɛr ɪz tu məʧ əv ə ˈprɑdəkt ðən ˈpipəl kən buy.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: There's a big market for English learning materials. Why not choose ours?
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: cheap* ˈlæpˌtɑps hæv ˈflədɪd ðə ˈmɑrkɪt, soʊ ˈnoʊwənz ˈmeɪkɪŋ ə profit.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Do you have any idea how many verbs you can use with 'idea'? There are a lot.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: do* ju hæv ˈɛni aɪˈdiə haʊ ˈmɛni vərbz ju kən juz wɪθ 'aɪˈdiə'? ðɛr ər ə lɔt.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: You can have an idea, which we often say when you're suddenly inspired.
VX3iFFXBWdA.ipa: you* kən hæv ən aɪˈdiə, wɪʧ wi ˈɔfən seɪ wɪn jʊr ˈsədənli inspired.

VX3iFFXBWdA.text: Or you can have no idea, which means you don't know. What time does the show start? I have no idea.
