OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 6 Minute English. From BBC Learning English.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 6* ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ. frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ english.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Beth.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: hello*. ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm beth.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Nowadays, the word 'safari' is often used negatively.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: nowadays*, ðə wərd 'səˈfɑri' ɪz ˈɔfən juzd negatively.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: For many people, the idea of killing animals for sport is unacceptable. As the popularity of hunting declines,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: for* ˈmɛni ˈpipəl, ðə aɪˈdiə əv ˈkɪlɪŋ ˈænəməlz fər spɔrt ɪz ˌənækˈsɛptəbəl. ɛz ðə ˌpɑpjəˈlɛrəti əv ˈhəntɪŋ declines,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: safaris are swapping their guns for cameras, offering tourists the chance to photograph wild animals in their natural habitat.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: safaris* ər sˈwɑpɪŋ ðɛr gənz fər ˈkæmərəz, ˈɔfərɪŋ ˈtʊrɪsts ðə ʧæns tɪ ˈfoʊtəˌgræf waɪld ˈænəməlz ɪn ðɛr ˈnæʧərəl habitat.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: In recent years, nature and wildlife tourism, also called 'ecotourism', has grown massively.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: in* ˈrisənt jɪrz, ˈneɪʧər ənd ˈwaɪlˌdlaɪf ˈtʊˌrɪzəm, ˈɔlsoʊ kɔld 'ecotourism*', həz groʊn massively.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: But the story is complex. While money from ecotourism is supposed to support threatened wildlife
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: but* ðə ˈstɔri ɪz ˈkɑmplɛks. waɪl ˈməni frəm ecotourism* ɪz səˈpoʊzd tɪ səˈpɔrt θˈrɛtənd wildlife

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and traditional local cultures, the reality is sometimes different.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* trəˈdɪʃənəl ˈloʊkəl ˈkəlʧərz, ðə ˌriˈæləˌti ɪz ˈsəmˌtaɪmz different.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: In this programme, we'll be asking is ecotourism good or bad?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: in* ðɪs ˈproʊˌgræm, wɪl bi ˈæskɪŋ ɪz ecotourism* gʊd ər bad?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: And, as usual, we'll be learning some useful new vocabulary as well. But first, I have a question for you, Beth.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and*, ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl bi ˈlərnɪŋ səm ˈjusfəl nu voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri ɛz wɛl. bət fərst, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, beth.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Most tourists on safari are looking for 'the big five', the name given to Africa's most iconic large animals.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: most* ˈtʊrɪsts ɔn səˈfɑri ər ˈlʊkɪŋ fər 'ðə bɪg faɪv', ðə neɪm ˈgɪvɪn tɪ ˈæfrɪkɑz moʊst ˌaɪˈkɑnɪk lɑrʤ animals.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: But which animals are 'the big five'? a) The lion, leopard, giraffe, baboon and buffalo?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: but* wɪʧ ˈænəməlz ər 'ðə bɪg faɪv'? ə) ðə laɪən, ˈlɛpərd, ʤəræf, bəˈbun ənd ˈbəfəˌloʊ?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: b) The lion, leopard, tiger, elephant and buffalo?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: b*) ðə laɪən, ˈlɛpərd, ˈtaɪgər, ˈɛləfənt ənd buffalo?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Or c) The lion, leopard, rhinoceros, elephant and buffalo?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: ər si) ðə laɪən, ˈlɛpərd, raɪˈnɑsərəs, ˈɛləfənt ənd buffalo?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: I guess it's a) The lion, leopard, giraffe, baboon and buffalo.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: aɪ gɛs ɪts ə) ðə laɪən, ˈlɛpərd, ʤəræf, bəˈbun ənd buffalo.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: I'll reveal the answer at the end of the programme. The balance between the good and bad things ecotourism can bring
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: i'll* rɪˈvil ðə ˈænsər æt ðə ɛnd əv ðə ˈproʊˌgræm. ðə ˈbæləns bɪtˈwin ðə gʊd ənd bæd θɪŋz ecotourism* kən bring

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: is well understood by Vicky Smith, whose website, Earth Changers,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: is* wɛl ˌəndərˈstʊd baɪ ˈvɪki smɪθ, huz ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, ərθ changers,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: matches ecotourists with environmentally-friendly travel companies.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: matches* ecotourists* wɪθ environmentally-friendly* ˈtrævəl companies.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Here's Vicky talking with BBC Radio 4 programme Costing the Earth. Just because tourism is nature-based,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: here's* ˈvɪki ˈtɔkɪŋ wɪθ ˌbibiˈsi ˈreɪdiˌoʊ 4 ˈproʊˌgræm ˈkɔstɪŋ ðə ərθ. ʤɪst bɪˈkəz ˈtʊˌrɪzəm ɪz nature-based,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: it doesn't mean to say it's necessarily responsible or sustainable.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: it* ˈdəzənt min tɪ seɪ ɪts ˌnɛsəˈsɛrəli riˈspɑnsəbəl ər sustainable.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: So, there's a lot of animal activities in tourism that we know which are, you know,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: so*, ðɛrz ə lɔt əv ˈænəməl ækˈtɪvɪtiz ɪn ˈtʊˌrɪzəm ðət wi noʊ wɪʧ ər, ju know,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: highly irresponsible and unsustainable, like performing whale and dolphin shows,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: highly* ˌɪrəˈspɑnsəbəl ənd ˌənsəˈsteɪnəbəl, laɪk pərˈfɔrmɪŋ weɪl ənd ˈdɑlfən shows,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: or swimming with dolphins, elephant-riding, tiger selfies where the tigers are drugged.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: or* sˈwɪmɪŋ wɪθ ˈdɑlfənz, elephant-riding*, ˈtaɪgər ˈsɛlˌfiz wɛr ðə ˈtaɪgərz ər drugged.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Genuine ecotourism is 'sustainable' —
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: genuine* ecotourism* ɪz 'səˈsteɪnəbəl' —

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 'designed to continue at a steady level which does not damage the environment'.
Not every travel company which calls themselves eco-friendly acts sustainably,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 'designed* tɪ kənˈtɪnju æt ə ˈstɛdi ˈlɛvəl wɪʧ dɪz nɑt ˈdæmɪʤ ðə environment'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and may still advertise irresponsible tourist activities,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: not* ˈɛvəri ˈtrævəl ˈkəmpəˌni wɪʧ kɔlz ðɛmˈsɛlvz eco-friendly* ækts sustainably,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: including 'tiger selfies' — 'having your photo taken with a captive wild tiger'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* meɪ stɪl ˈædvərˌtaɪz ˌɪrəˈspɑnsəbəl ˈtʊrɪst activities,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: There are two requirements travel companies should meet to qualify as genuine ecotourism.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: including* 'ˈtaɪgər ˈsɛlˌfiz' —* 'ˈhævɪŋ jʊr ˈfoʊˌtoʊ ˈteɪkən wɪθ ə ˈkæptɪv waɪld tiger'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: First, tourists' main motivation should be to appreciate and observe the natural world without interfering,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: fərst, ˈtʊrɪsts' meɪn ˌmoʊtəˈveɪʃən ʃʊd bi tɪ əˈpriʃiˌeɪt ənd əbˈzərv ðə ˈnæʧərəl wərld wɪˈθaʊt interfering,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and second, the money they spend should support traditional communities.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* ˈsɛkənd, ðə ˈməni ðeɪ spɛnd ʃʊd səˈpɔrt trəˈdɪʃənəl communities.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Clearly, having your photograph taken with a chained and drugged tiger does not meet these requirements.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: clearly*, ˈhævɪŋ jʊr ˈfoʊtəˌgræf ˈteɪkən wɪθ ə ʧeɪnd ənd drəgd ˈtaɪgər dɪz nɑt mit ðiz requirements.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: But not all companies claiming to be ecotourism behave so irresponsibly.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: but* nɑt ɔl ˈkəmpəˌniz ˈkleɪmɪŋ tɪ bi ecotourism* bɪˈheɪv soʊ irresponsibly.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: According to Antonia Bolingbroke-Kent, who runs small-scale wildlife expeditions
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: according* tɪ ænˈtoʊniə bolingbroke-kent*, hu rənz small-scale* ˈwaɪlˌdlaɪf expeditions

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: to some of the most remote places on Earth,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: to* səm əv ðə moʊst rɪˈmoʊt ˈpleɪsɪz ɔn earth,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: it's possible to put travel companies on a sliding scale from good to bad.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: it's* ˈpɑsəbəl tɪ pʊt ˈtrævəl ˈkəmpəˌniz ɔn ə sˈlaɪdɪŋ skeɪl frəm gʊd tɪ bad.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: On BBC Radio 4's programme Costing the Earth,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: on* ˌbibiˈsi ˈreɪdiˌoʊ 4's* ˈproʊˌgræm ˈkɔstɪŋ ðə earth,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Antonia discussed her work in Tajikistan, a country where ecotourism
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: antonia* dɪˈskəst hər wərk ɪn ˌtɑˈʤikɪˌstæn, ə ˈkəntri wɛr ecotourism

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: is making a positive impact on both animal and human communities.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: is* ˈmeɪkɪŋ ə ˈpɑzətɪv ˌɪmˈpækt ɔn boʊθ ˈænəməl ənd ˈjumən communities.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: At the other end of the scale is Tajikistan, where I work a lot,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: at* ðə ˈəðər ɛnd əv ðə skeɪl ɪz ˌtɑˈʤikɪˌstæn, wɛr aɪ wərk ə lot,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: which gets less than two dozen wildlife tourists a year, and the money these visitors bring
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: which* gɪts lɛs ðən tu ˈdəzən ˈwaɪlˌdlaɪf ˈtʊrɪsts ə jɪr, ənd ðə ˈməni ðiz ˈvɪzɪtərz bring

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: is essential to the conservation work that grassroots NGOs are doing. So those few tourists, their money goes a very long way
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: is* ɛˈsɛnʃəl tɪ ðə ˌkɑnsərˈveɪʃən wərk ðət ˈgræsˈruts ɛŋˈgoʊz ər duɪŋ. soʊ ðoʊz fju ˈtʊrɪsts, ðɛr ˈməni goʊz ə ˈvɛri lɔŋ way

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and the animals people are looking at — snow leopards, rare mountain ungulates like Bukharan markhor —
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* ðə ˈænəməlz ˈpipəl ər ˈlʊkɪŋ æt —* snoʊ ˈlɛpərdz, rɛr ˈmaʊntən ungulates* laɪk bukharan* markhor* —

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: they are being observed from a distance, their behaviour is not being affected in any way,
and the local communities are genuinely benefiting. Antonia uses the phrase 'at the other end of the scale'
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* ðə ˈloʊkəl kəmˈjunɪtiz ər ˈʤɛnjuˌaɪnli ˈbɛnəfɪtɪŋ. ænˈtoʊniə ˈjuzɪz ðə freɪz 'æt ðə ˈəðər ɛnd əv ðə scale'

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: as a way of contrasting irresponsible tourist companies with what's happening in Tajikistan.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: as* ə weɪ əv kənˈtræstɪŋ ˌɪrəˈspɑnsəbəl ˈtʊrɪst ˈkəmpəˌniz wɪθ wəts ˈhæpənɪŋ ɪn tajikistan.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: There, animals including snow leopards and mountain ungulates, are being protected by ecotourist projects
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: there*, ˈænəməlz ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ snoʊ ˈlɛpərdz ənd ˈmaʊntən ungulates*, ər biɪŋ prəˈtɛktɪd baɪ ecotourist* projects

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: run by 'non-governmental organisations' or 'NGOs' —
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: run* baɪ 'nɑnˌgəvərnˈmɛntəl ˌɔrgənɪˈzeɪʃənz' ər 'ɛŋˈgoʊz' —

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 'organisations trying to achieve environmental or social aims, 'outside of government control'.
These NGOs are 'grassroots' organisations meaning that they are
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 'organisations* traɪɪŋ tɪ əˈʧiv ɪnˌvaɪrənˈmɛnəl ər ˈsoʊʃəl eɪmz, 'ˈaʊtˈsaɪd əv ˈgəvərnmənt control'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 'run from the bottom up, by ordinary people rather than leaders'. Despite getting very few ecotourists a year,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: these* ɛŋˈgoʊz ər 'ˈgræsˈruts' ˌɔrgənɪˈzeɪʃənz ˈminɪŋ ðət ðeɪ are

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: the money they spend in Tajikistan 'goes a long way'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 'run* frəm ðə ˈbɑtəm əp, baɪ ˈɔrdəˌnɛri ˈpipəl ˈrəðər ðən ˈlidərz'. dɪˈspaɪt ˈgɪtɪŋ ˈvɛri fju ecotourists* ə year,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: In other words, 'the money is an important factor in achieving their goals',
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: the* ˈməni ðeɪ spɛnd ɪn ˌtɑˈʤikɪˌstæn 'goʊz ə lɔŋ way'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: which in Tajikistan at least, means protecting rare wild animals. OK, it's time to reveal the answer to my question.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: in* ˈəðər wərdz, 'ðə ˈməni ɪz ən ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ˈfæktər ɪn əˈʧivɪŋ ðɛr goals',

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: You asked me about 'the big five', the name for Africa's iconic safari animals.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: which* ɪn ˌtɑˈʤikɪˌstæn æt list, minz prəˈtɛktɪŋ rɛr waɪld ˈænəməlz. ˈoʊˈkeɪ, ɪts taɪm tɪ rɪˈvil ðə ˈænsər tɪ maɪ question.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: I guessed they were the lion, leopard, giraffe, baboon and buffalo. You guessed right about the lion, leopard, and buffalo,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: you* æst mi əˈbaʊt 'ðə bɪg faɪv', ðə neɪm fər ˈæfrɪkɑz ˌaɪˈkɑnɪk səˈfɑri animals.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: but the others were the rhinoceros and the elephant. OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learned
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: i* gɛst ðeɪ wər ðə laɪən, ˈlɛpərd, ʤəræf, bəˈbun ənd ˈbəfəˌloʊ. ju gɛst raɪt əˈbaʊt ðə laɪən, ˈlɛpərd, ənd buffalo,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: from this programme about 'ecotourism' — 'travel to places of natural beauty where the tourists' motivation
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: but* ðə ˈəðərz wər ðə raɪˈnɑsərəs ənd ðə ˈɛləfənt. ˈoʊˈkeɪ, lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv learned

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 'is to appreciate nature and support the local culture'. The adjective 'sustainable'
describes 'actions designed to continue at a steady level, 'so as not to damage the environment'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: dɪˈskraɪbz 'ˈækʃənz dɪˈzaɪnd tɪ kənˈtɪnju æt ə ˈstɛdi ˈlɛvəl, 'soʊ ɛz nɑt tɪ ˈdæmɪʤ ðə environment'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: A 'tiger selfie' means 'having your photo taken with a captive wild tiger', not something to be advised!
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: a* 'ˈtaɪgər ˈsɛlˌfi' minz 'ˈhævɪŋ jʊr ˈfoʊˌtoʊ ˈteɪkən wɪθ ə ˈkæptɪv waɪld ˈtaɪgər', nɑt ˈsəmθɪŋ tɪ bi advised!

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: The phrase 'at the other end of the scale' is similar in meaning to the phrase, 'by contrast'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: the* freɪz 'æt ðə ˈəðər ɛnd əv ðə skeɪl' ɪz ˈsɪmələr ɪn ˈminɪŋ tɪ ðə freɪz, 'baɪ contrast'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: A 'grassroots NGO' is 'a non-governmental organisation 'which tries to achieve its aims
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: a* 'ˈgræsˈruts ɛŋˈgoʊ' ɪz 'ə nɑnˌgəvərnˈmɛntəl ˌɔrgənɪˈzeɪʃən 'wɪʧ traɪz tɪ əˈʧiv ɪts aims

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 'through the actions of local, ordinary people rather than leaders'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 'through* ðə ˈækʃənz əv ˈloʊkəl, ˈɔrdəˌnɛri ˈpipəl ˈrəðər ðən leaders'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: And finally, if something 'goes a long way' towards a certain goal, it's 'an important factor' in achieving that goal.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* ˈfaɪnəli, ɪf ˈsəmθɪŋ 'goʊz ə lɔŋ weɪ' təˈwɔrdz ə ˈsərtən goʊl, ɪts 'ən ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ˈfæktər' ɪn əˈʧivɪŋ ðət goal.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Once again, our six minutes are up. — Goodbye for now! — Bye!
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: once* əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp. —* ˌgʊdˈbaɪ fər naʊ! —* bye!

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 6 Minute English. From BBC Learning English.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 6* ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ. frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ english.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Alice. And I'm Neil. Did you have a good weekend, Alice?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: hello* ənd ˈwɛlkəm tɪ 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm ˈælɪs. ənd əm nil. dɪd ju hæv ə gʊd ˈwiˌkɪnd, alice?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Yes, but it 'flew by' — which means it 'went quickly' — and here we are again, back at work!
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: yes*, bət ɪt 'flu baɪ' —* wɪʧ minz ɪt 'wɛnt kˈwɪkli' —* ənd hir wi ər əˈgɛn, bæk æt work!

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Ah, I know what you mean. Though I must say, time really 'dragged' for me — and that means it 'went slowly'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: ah*, aɪ noʊ wət ju min. ðoʊ aɪ məst seɪ, taɪm ˈrɪli 'drægd' fər mi —* ənd ðət minz ɪt 'wɛnt slowly'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: — I was on a train, which broke down. — Oh, dear! And it felt like it took forever to arrive,
though actually it was only delayed by one hour. Well, today we're talking about our 'perception' of —
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: — aɪ wɑz ɔn ə treɪn, wɪʧ broʊk daʊn. —* oʊ, dɪr! ənd ɪt fɛlt laɪk ɪt tʊk fərˈɛvər tɪ arrive,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: or 'the way we see' — time. It's true that when we're busy doing lots of things, time flies by.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: though* ˈæˌkʧuəli ɪt wɑz ˈoʊnli dɪˈleɪd baɪ wən aʊər. wɛl, təˈdeɪ wɪr ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt ɑr 'pərˈsɛpʃən' əv —

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: And when we're bored or have nothing to do, it drags. And I didn't have anything to do on the train.
Do you think time flows at the same rate for everyone, even animals? My cat doesn't get bored doing nothing all day.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: do* ju θɪŋk taɪm floʊz æt ðə seɪm reɪt fər ˈɛvriˌwən, ˈivɪn ˈænəməlz? maɪ kæt ˈdəzənt gɪt bɔrd duɪŋ ˈnəθɪŋ ɔl day.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: I wonder if time drags for her sometimes? Good question! Did you know, Neil, that, according to a new study,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: i* ˈwəndər ɪf taɪm drægz fər hər ˈsəmˌtaɪmz? gʊd kˈwɛʃən! dɪd ju noʊ, nil, ðət, əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ə nu study,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: smaller animals perceive time as if it is passing in slow motion? That sounds weird.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: smaller* ˈænəməlz pərˈsiv taɪm ɛz ɪf ɪt ɪz ˈpæsɪŋ ɪn sloʊ ˈmoʊʃən? ðət saʊnz weird.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Do you think they hear us like this, taaalking slowwwly?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: do* ju θɪŋk ðeɪ hir ˈjuˈɛs laɪk ðɪs, taaalking* slowwwly?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Don't be silly, Neil! What I meant was that small animals such as insects and small birds
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: don't* bi ˈsɪli, nil! wət aɪ mɛnt wɑz ðət smɔl ˈænəməlz səʧ ɛz ˈɪnˌsɛkts ənd smɔl birds

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: can observe more detail in a certain period of time — for example, a second — than larger animals.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: can* əbˈzərv mɔr ˈditeɪl ɪn ə ˈsərtən ˈpɪriəd əv taɪm —* fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ə ˈsɛkənd —* ðən ˈlɑrʤər animals.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: And how does this help them, exactly? It sounds like the day would really drag
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* haʊ dɪz ðɪs hɛlp ðɛm, ɪgˈzæktli? ɪt saʊnz laɪk ðə deɪ wʊd ˈrɪli drag

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: if every second got stretched out like that! It helps them by giving them time to escape larger predators.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: if* ˈɛvəri ˈsɛkənd gɑt strɛʧt aʊt laɪk ðət! ɪt hɛlps ðɛm baɪ ˈgɪvɪŋ ðɛm taɪm tɪ ɪˈskeɪp ˈlɑrʤər predators.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Now, I have a question for you, Neil. Can you tell me roughly how much more quickly
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: now*, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, nil. kən ju tɛl mi ˈrəfli haʊ məʧ mɔr quickly

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: a fly's eye can react than a human eye?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: a* fly's* aɪ kən riækt ðən ə ˈjumən eye?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Is it a) Twice as quickly? b) Four times as quickly? Or c) Ten times as quickly?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: is* ɪt ə) twaɪs ɛz kˈwɪkli? bi) fɔr taɪmz ɛz kˈwɪkli? ər si) tɛn taɪmz ɛz quickly?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Well, I'll go for c) Ten times. Flies are pretty 'nippy' — and that's another word for 'quick'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: well*, aɪl goʊ fər si) tɛn taɪmz. flaɪz ər ˈprɪti 'ˈnɪpi' —* ənd ðæts əˈnəðər wərd fər 'quick'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Yes. Well, we'll find out later on if you got the answer right or not.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: yes*. wɛl, wɪl faɪnd aʊt ˈleɪtər ɔn ɪf ju gɑt ðə ˈænsər raɪt ər not.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Now, small animals can typically process more visual information than we can. But in a dangerous situation,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: now*, smɔl ˈænəməlz kən ˈtɪpɪkəli ˈprɔˌsɛs mɔr ˈvɪʒəwəl ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ðən wi kən. bət ɪn ə ˈdeɪnʤərəs situation,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: our brains can work in overdrive to process information more quickly. And 'overdrive' means 'a state of extreme activity'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: our* breɪnz kən wərk ɪn ˈoʊvərˌdraɪv tɪ ˈprɔˌsɛs ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən mɔr kˈwɪkli. ənd 'ˈoʊvərˌdraɪv' minz 'ə steɪt əv ɪkˈstrim ækˈtɪvɪti'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Let's listen to Raza Rumi, a writer and broadcaster in Pakistan, talking about the unusual way his brain worked
when gunmen opened fire on him in his car.
It lasted for a few minutes, but to me that particular incident feels like it was for hours.
I think my brain was working in a very strange way. Parallel and multiple thoughts
and streams of consciousness were sort of running along —
"I have to save my head, because if I get a bullet in my brain, I'm dead.'
And at the same time, "Was it all worth it?" And, "Alas, what a short life it was, it was lovely."
I was petrified that I am going to die.
Raza Rumi there. So, he was 'petrified' by the attack — which means 'extremely frightened'.
As a result, his brain started working in a strange way.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: ɛz ə rɪˈzəlt, hɪz breɪn ˈstɑrtɪd ˈwərkɪŋ ɪn ə streɪnʤ way.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: He was thinking and feeling lots of different things at the same time. That's right, he remembers thinking practical thoughts,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: he* wɑz ˈθɪŋkɪŋ ənd ˈfilɪŋ lɑts əv ˈdɪfərənt θɪŋz æt ðə seɪm taɪm. ðæts raɪt, hi rɪˈmɛmbərz ˈθɪŋkɪŋ ˈpræktɪkəl thoughts,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: like, "I have to save my head". But 'in parallel' — or 'at the same time' —
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: like*, "aɪ hæv tɪ seɪv maɪ hɛd". bət 'ɪn ˈpɛrəˌlɛl' —* ər 'æt ðə seɪm taɪm' —

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: he also remembers having philosophical thoughts, such as, "What a short life it was, it was lovely".
Mm. Have you ever been in a dangerous situation where your brain went into overdrive?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: he* ˈɔlsoʊ rɪˈmɛmbərz ˈhævɪŋ ˌfɪləˈsɑfɪkəl θɔts, səʧ ɛz, "wət ə ʃɔrt laɪf ɪt wɑz, ɪt wɑz lovely".

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Yeah, I was ten years old and I fell backwards out of a big tree in our garden.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: mm*. hæv ju ˈɛvər bɪn ɪn ə ˈdeɪnʤərəs ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən wɛr jʊr breɪn wɛnt ˈɪntu overdrive?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: — Oh, no! — Yeah. I have a vivid memory of the sun flashing above me,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: —* oʊ, noʊ! —* jæ. aɪ hæv ə ˈvɪvɪd ˈmɛməri əv ðə sən ˈflæʃɪŋ əˈbəv me,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and the clouds moving across the sky and the leaves rustling in the tree above me.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* ðə klaʊdz ˈmuvɪŋ əˈkrɔs ðə skaɪ ənd ðə livz ˈrəslɪŋ ɪn ðə tri əˈbəv me.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: My mum was screaming through the kitchen window as she saw me fall.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: my* məm wɑz ˈskrimɪŋ θru ðə ˈkɪʧən ˈwɪndoʊ ɛz ʃi sɔ mi fall.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: I experienced so much in the space of just a few seconds, just like Raza Rumi describes.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: i* ɪkˈspɪriənst soʊ məʧ ɪn ðə speɪs əv ʤɪst ə fju ˈsɛkəndz, ʤɪst laɪk ˈræzə rumi* describes.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Yes. A 'vivid' memory, by the way, is 'clear and detailed'. Oh, poor Neil! Did you hurt yourself?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: yes*. ə 'ˈvɪvɪd' ˈmɛməri, baɪ ðə weɪ, ɪz 'klɪr ənd dɪˈteɪld'. oʊ, pur nil! dɪd ju hərt yourself?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Some big bruises, but no broken bones. Glad to hear it!
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: some* bɪg ˈbruzɪz, bət noʊ ˈbroʊkən boʊnz. glæd tɪ hir it!

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Now, it's a strange trick of memory that, in a scary situation, your brain starts to record everything in great detail
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: now*, ɪts ə streɪnʤ trɪk əv ˈmɛməri ðət, ɪn ə ˈskɛri ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən, jʊr breɪn stɑrts tɪ ˈrɛkərd ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ɪn greɪt detail

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and the more memory you have of an event, the longer you believe it took.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* ðə mɔr ˈmɛməri ju hæv əv ən ɪˈvɛnt, ðə ˈlɔŋgər ju bɪˈliv ɪt took.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: This idea explains why children often feel that time is passing slowly,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: this* aɪˈdiə ɪkˈspleɪnz waɪ ˈʧɪldrən ˈɔfən fil ðət taɪm ɪz ˈpæsɪŋ slowly,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: because their experiences are new, and they are creating lots of new memories. Whereas boring grown-ups like us are following routines
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: because* ðɛr ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz ər nu, ənd ðeɪ ər kriˈeɪtɪŋ lɑts əv nu ˈmɛməriz. wɛˈræz ˈbɔrɪŋ ˈgroʊˌnəps laɪk ˈjuˈɛs ər ˈfɑloʊɪŋ routines

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: that don't require new memories, because they're so familiar. But let's listen to Claudia Hammond, author of Time Warped,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: that* doʊnt ˌrikˈwaɪər nu ˈmɛməriz, bɪˈkəz ðɛr soʊ fəˈmɪljər. bət lɛts ˈlɪsən tɪ ˈklɔdiə ˈhæmənd, ˈɔθər əv taɪm warped,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: talking about how we can stretch time and make our days feel longer — in a good way!
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: talking* əˈbaʊt haʊ wi kən strɛʧ taɪm ənd meɪk ɑr deɪz fil ˈlɔŋgər —* ɪn ə gʊd way!

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: If you can spend your weekend filling it with loads of new different activities,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: if* ju kən spɛnd jʊr ˈwiˌkɪnd ˈfɪlɪŋ ɪt wɪθ loʊdz əv nu ˈdɪfərənt activities,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: it'll go fast, at the time, because you're having fun. But when you look back, say, on Sunday night,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: it'll* goʊ fæst, æt ðə taɪm, bɪˈkəz jʊr ˈhævɪŋ fən. bət wɪn ju lʊk bæk, seɪ, ɔn ˈsənˌdi night,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and you've got to go to work next day,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* juv gɑt tɪ goʊ tɪ wərk nɛkst deɪ,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: it will feel as if your weekend was long, because you filled it with new memories.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: ɪt wɪl fil ɛz ɪf jʊr ˈwiˌkɪnd wɑz lɔŋ, bɪˈkəz ju fɪld ɪt wɪθ nu memories.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: We should do that this weekend, Neil. What do you think?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: yeah*, aɪ wɑz tɛn jɪrz oʊld ənd aɪ fɛl ˈbækwərdz aʊt əv ə bɪg tri ɪn ɑr garden.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Definitely. I'm going to buzz around like a fly, creating loads of new memories.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: definitely*. əm goʊɪŋ tɪ bəz əraʊnd laɪk ə flaɪ, kriˈeɪtɪŋ loʊdz əv nu memories.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Now, are you ready for the answer to today's quiz question?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: we* ʃʊd du ðət ðɪs ˈwiˌkɪnd, nil. wət du ju think?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: I asked roughly how much more quickly a fly's eye can react than a human eye?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: i* æst ˈrəfli haʊ məʧ mɔr kˈwɪkli ə fly's* aɪ kən riækt ðən ə ˈjumən eye?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Is it a) Twice as quickly? b) Four times as quickly? Or c) Ten times as quickly?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: now*, ər ju ˈrɛdi fər ðə ˈænsər tɪ ˈtudeɪz kwɪz question?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: And I said c) Ten times as quickly. The correct answer is b) Four times as quickly.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* aɪ sɛd si) tɛn taɪmz ɛz kˈwɪkli. ðə kərˈɛkt ˈænsər ɪz bi) fɔr taɪmz ɛz quickly.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Flies have eyes that send updates to the brain at much higher frequencies than our eyes,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: flies* hæv aɪz ðət sɛnd ˈəpˌdeɪts tɪ ðə breɪn æt məʧ haɪər ˈfrikwənsiz ðən ɑr eyes,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: because they can process the information more quickly. This speed illustrates the impressive capabilities of even the smallest animal brains.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: because* ðeɪ kən ˈprɔˌsɛs ðə ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən mɔr kˈwɪkli. ðɪs spid ˈɪləˌstreɪts ðə ˌɪmˈprɛsɪv ˌkeɪpəˈbɪlətiz əv ˈivɪn ðə sˈmɔləst ˈænəməl brains.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Well, before we buzz off, perhaps we should hear the words we learned today. They are flew by, dragged,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: well*, ˌbiˈfɔr wi bəz ɔf, pərˈhæps wi ʃʊd hir ðə wərdz wi ˈlərnɪd təˈdeɪ. ðeɪ ər flu baɪ, dragged,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: perception, nippy, overdrive, petrified, in parallel.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: perception*, ˈnɪpi, ˈoʊvərˌdraɪv, ˈpɛtrəˌfaɪd, ɪn parallel.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Well, that's the end of today's 6 Minute English. Remember to join us again soon! — Goodbye! — Bye! 6 Minute English.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: well*, ðæts ðə ɛnd əv ˈtudeɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ. rɪˈmɛmbər tɪ ʤɔɪn ˈjuˈɛs əˈgɛn sun! —* ˌgʊdˈbaɪ! —* baɪ! 6 ˈmɪnət english.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: From BBC Learning English.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: from* ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ english.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Georgie.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: hello*. ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm georgie.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Now, if I told you I'd been for a walk to see Big Ben and Buckingham Palace,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: now*, ɪf aɪ toʊld ju aɪd bɪn fər ə wɔk tɪ si bɪg bɛn ənd ˈbəkɪŋˌhæm ˈpæləs,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: you'd know straight away I was in London. But what if my walk went past cafes selling mozzarella and ricotta
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: jʊd noʊ streɪt əˈweɪ aɪ wɑz ɪn ˈləndən. bət wət ɪf maɪ wɔk wɛnt pæst ˌkæˈfeɪz ˈsɛlɪŋ mozzarella* ənd ricotta

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: where I smelled freshly made cannolis and focaccia. Where would I be then?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: is* ɪt ə) twaɪs ɛz kˈwɪkli? bi) fɔr taɪmz ɛz kˈwɪkli? ər si) tɛn taɪmz ɛz quickly?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Focaccia and mozzarella, well, you'd be in Italy, right?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: where* aɪ smɛld ˈfrɛʃli meɪd cannolis* ənd foʊˈkɑˌʧiə. wɛr wʊd aɪ bi then?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Yes, Italy, or 'Little Italy', to be exact — 'the neighbourhood in some cities
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: yes*, ˈɪtəli, ər 'ˈlɪtəl ˈɪtəli', tɪ bi ɪgˈzækt —* 'ðə ˈneɪbərˌhʊd ɪn səm cities

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 'where Italian communities settled and made their home'. These Italian arrivals opened shops and cafes,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 'where* ˌɪˈtæljən kəmˈjunɪtiz ˈsɛtəld ənd meɪd ðɛr hoʊm'. ðiz ˌɪˈtæljən ərˈaɪvəlz ˈoʊpənd ʃɑps ənd cafes,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: selling food to their own communities. Soon, dishes like spaghetti and meatballs attracted the attention of local people,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: selling* fud tɪ ðɛr oʊn kəmˈjunɪtiz. sun, ˈdɪʃɪz laɪk spəˈgɛti ənd ˈmitˌbɔlz əˈtræktəd ðə əˈtɛnʃən əv ˈloʊkəl people,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and gradually Italian food became famous around the world. In this programme, we'll be taking a walk through two Little Italys,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* ˈgræʤuəli ˌɪˈtæljən fud bɪˈkeɪm ˈfeɪməs əraʊnd ðə wərld. ɪn ðɪs ˈproʊˌgræm, wɪl bi ˈteɪkɪŋ ə wɔk θru tu ˈlɪtəl italys,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: one in Argentina, the other in New York, and, as usual, we'll be learning some useful new vocabulary as well.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: one* ɪn ˌɑrʤənˈtinə, ðə ˈəðər ɪn nu jɔrk, ənd, ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl bi ˈlərnɪŋ səm ˈjusfəl nu voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri ɛz well.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: But before that, I have a question for you, Georgie. According to a recent YouGov poll,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: but* ˌbiˈfɔr ðət, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, ˈʤɔrʤi. əˈkɔrdɪŋ tɪ ə ˈrisənt yougov* poll,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: which Italian food is most popular with British diners?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: which* ˌɪˈtæljən fud ɪz moʊst ˈpɑpjələr wɪθ ˈbrɪtɪʃ ˈdaɪnərz?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Is it a) Pizza? b) Lasagne? Or c) Garlic bread? I think it must be pizza.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: focaccia* ənd mozzarella*, wɛl, jʊd bi ɪn ˈɪtəli, right?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: OK, Georgie, I'll reveal the answer at the end of the programme. One country Italians moved to was Argentina.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: ok*, ˈʤɔrʤi, aɪl rɪˈvil ðə ˈænsər æt ðə ɛnd əv ðə ˈproʊˌgræm. wən ˈkəntri ˌɪˈtæljənz muvd tɪ wɑz argentina.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: In 1898, Giuseppe Banchero arrived in the neighbourhood of La Boca, the Little Italy of Buenos Aires,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: in* 1898 ʤɪˈsɛpi banchero* əraɪvd ɪn ðə ˈneɪbərˌhʊd əv lɑ ˈboʊkə, ðə ˈlɪtəl ˈɪtəli əv bˈweɪnəs aires,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: where many Italian immigrants started restaurants.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: where* ˈmɛni ˌɪˈtæljən ˈɪməgrənts ˈstɑrtɪd restaurants.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Here, Hugo Banchero, grandson of Giuseppe, tells his story to Veronica Smink,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: here*, ˈjugoʊ banchero*, ˈgrændˌsən əv ʤɪˈsɛpi, tɛlz hɪz ˈstɔri tɪ vərˈɑnɪkə smɪŋk,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: reporter for BBC World Service programme The Food Chain.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: rɪˈpɔrtər fər ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud chain.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Well, my grandfather came from Italy, from Genoa, from Liguria.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: well*, maɪ ˈgrænˌfɑðər keɪm frəm ˈɪtəli, frəm ˈʤɛnoʊə, frəm liguria.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: He was born in the centre of Genoa
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: he* wɑz bɔrn ɪn ðə ˈsɛntər əv genoa

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and arrived here in 1898, at the age of seven and a half,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* əraɪvd hir ɪn 1898 æt ðə eɪʤ əv ˈsɛvən ənd ə half,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and this pizzeria where we are was founded on March 28, 1972.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* ðɪs ˌpitsərˈiə wɛr wi ər wɑz ˈfaʊndɪd ɔn mɑrʧ 28 1972.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: We have been here for 91 years. So what culinary traditions did they bring with them?
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: is* ɪt ə) ˈpitsə? bi) lasagne*? ər si) ˈgɑrlɪk brɛd? aɪ θɪŋk ɪt məst bi pizza.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Well, our culinary tradition is pizzeria, and we incorporated the faina from Genoa,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: well*, ɑr ˈkjulɪˌnɛri trəˈdɪʃən ɪz ˌpitsərˈiə, ənd wi ˌɪnˈkɔrpərˌeɪtɪd ðə faina* frəm genoa,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: which is a pizza with chickpea flour.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: which* ɪz ə ˈpitsə wɪθ chickpea* flour.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: In 1898, Giuseppe founded his 'pizzeria' — 'a restaurant selling pizza'. When a business is 'founded',
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: in* 1898 ʤɪˈsɛpi ˈfaʊndɪd hɪz 'ˌpitsərˈiə' —* 'ə ˈrɛˌstrɑnt ˈsɛlɪŋ ˈpitsə'. wɪn ə ˈbɪznɪs ɪz 'founded',

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: it's 'established' — someone 'starts it up' or 'sets it up'. Giuseppe brought the culinary traditions
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: it's* 'ɪˈstæblɪʃt' —* ˈsəmˌwən 'stɑrts ɪt əp' ər 'sɛts ɪt əp'. ʤɪˈsɛpi brɔt ðə ˈkjulɪˌnɛri traditions

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: from his home in Liguria in northern Italy, including regional pizzas like faina and fugazzeta.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: from* hɪz hoʊm ɪn liguria* ɪn ˈnɔrðərn ˈɪtəli, ˌɪnˈkludɪŋ ˈriʤənəl ˈpitsəz laɪk faina* ənd fugazzeta.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: The adjective 'culinary' describes 'anything connected with cooking'. But probably the best-known Little Italy in the world
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: the* ˈæʤɪktɪv 'ˈkjulɪˌnɛri' dɪˈskraɪbz 'ˈɛniˌθɪŋ kəˈnɛktɪd wɪθ ˈkʊkɪŋ'. bət ˈprɑbəˌbli ðə best-known* ˈlɪtəl ˈɪtəli ɪn ðə world

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: is an area of Manhattan's Lower East side in New York.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: is* ən ˈɛriə əv mænˈhætənz loʊər ist saɪd ɪn nu york.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 90% of Italian immigrants who arrived in the US at the turn of the century came through this neighbourhood.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 90* əv ˌɪˈtæljən ˈɪməgrənts hu əraɪvd ɪn ðə ˈjuˈɛs æt ðə tərn əv ðə ˈsɛnʧəri keɪm θru ðɪs neighbourhood.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Di Palo's, one of the original shops selling Italian food in Little Italy,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: di* palo's*, wən əv ðə ərˈɪʤənəl ʃɑps ˈsɛlɪŋ ˌɪˈtæljən fud ɪn ˈlɪtəl ˈɪtəli,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: has been serving customers for 113 years. Here, Lou Di Palo, co-owner
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: həz bɪn ˈsərvɪŋ ˈkəstəmərz fər 113 jɪrz. hir, lu di ˈpæloʊ, co-owner

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and great-grandson of the original owner, Salvino, explains more about his family history
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* great-grandson* əv ðə ərˈɪʤənəl ˈoʊnər, sælˈvinoʊ, ɪkˈspleɪnz mɔr əˈbaʊt hɪz ˈfæməli history

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: to BBC World Service programme The Food Chain.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: to* ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud chain.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 1925, when my grandmother, Concetta, and my grandfather, Luigi, got married, they open their own shop.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 1925* wɪn maɪ ˈgrændˌməðər, concetta*, ənd maɪ ˈgrænˌfɑðər, luˈiʤi, gɑt ˈmɛrid, ðeɪ ˈoʊpən ðɛr oʊn shop.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: It's the shop we continue today, being the fourth generation working alongside my sister, Maria,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: it's* ðə ʃɑp wi kənˈtɪnju təˈdeɪ, biɪŋ ðə fɔrθ ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃən ˈwərkɪŋ əˈlɔŋˈsaɪd maɪ ˈsɪstər, maria,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and my brother, Sal, and our children, the fifth generation. Our business has expanded,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* maɪ ˈbrəðər, sæl, ənd ɑr ˈʧɪldrən, ðə fɪθ ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃən. ɑr ˈbɪznɪs həz expanded,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: expanded to represent the full food culture of the 20 regions of Italy.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: expanded* tɪ ˌrɛprɪˈzɛnt ðə fʊl fud ˈkəlʧər əv ðə 20 ˈriʤənz əv italy.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Little Italy is the stepping stone of the Italian immigrant.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: little* ˈɪtəli ɪz ðə ˈstɛpɪŋ stoʊn əv ðə ˌɪˈtæljən immigrant.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: This is where many of the Italians first came through Ellis Island,
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: this* ɪz wɛr ˈmɛni əv ðə ˌɪˈtæljənz fərst keɪm θru ˈɛlɪs island,

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: and then settled here, and then eventually moved into mainstream America, throughout the rest of the country.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: and* ðɛn ˈsɛtəld hir, ənd ðɛn ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli muvd ˈɪntu ˈmeɪnˌstrim əˈmɛrɪkə, θruaʊt ðə rɛst əv ðə country.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Lou Di Palo is the fourth generation of his family to run the shop, and his children will be the fifth.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: lou* di ˈpæloʊ ɪz ðə fɔrθ ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃən əv hɪz ˈfæməli tɪ rən ðə ʃɑp, ənd hɪz ˈʧɪldrən wɪl bi ðə fifth.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Phrases like 'fourth or fifth generation' describe 'the children of people 'whose parents immigrated to a particular country'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: phrases* laɪk 'fɔrθ ər fɪθ ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃən' dɪˈskraɪb 'ðə ˈʧɪldrən əv ˈpipəl 'huz ˈpɛrənts ˈɪməˌgreɪtɪd tɪ ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr country'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: After arriving in New York, many Italian immigrants moved on to start successful new lives elsewhere.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: after* ərˈaɪvɪŋ ɪn nu jɔrk, ˈmɛni ˌɪˈtæljən ˈɪməgrənts muvd ɔn tɪ stɑrt səkˈsɛsfəl nu lɪvz elsewhere.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: That's why Lou calls Little Italy 'a stepping stone', 'an experience that helps you achieve something else',
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: that's* waɪ lu kɔlz ˈlɪtəl ˈɪtəli 'ə ˈstɛpɪŋ stoʊn', 'ən ɪkˈspɪriəns ðət hɛlps ju əˈʧiv ˈsəmθɪŋ else',

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: like a real stepping stone helps you cross a river.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: like* ə ril ˈstɛpɪŋ stoʊn hɛlps ju krɔs ə ˈrɪvər.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: As a result, Italian newcomers became accepted in mainstream America, 'mainstream' meaning 'the culture and customs
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: ɛz ə rɪˈzəlt, ˌɪˈtæljən ˈnuˌkəmərz bɪˈkeɪm ækˈsɛptɪd ɪn ˈmeɪnˌstrim əˈmɛrɪkə, 'ˈmeɪnˌstrim' ˈminɪŋ 'ðə ˈkəlʧər ənd customs

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 'viewed as normal by most Americans'. It seems we owe a lot to Little Italy.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 'viewed* ɛz ˈnɔrməl baɪ moʊst əˈmɛrɪkənz'. ɪt simz wi oʊ ə lɔt tɪ ˈlɪtəl italy.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: But we still don't know which food is most popular here in the UK.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: but* wi stɪl doʊnt noʊ wɪʧ fud ɪz moʊst ˈpɑpjələr hir ɪn ðə uk.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Isn't it time to reveal the answer to your question, Neil? Right. I asked you which Italian food
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: we* hæv bɪn hir fər 91 jɪrz. soʊ wət ˈkjulɪˌnɛri trəˈdɪʃənz dɪd ðeɪ brɪŋ wɪθ them?

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: is most popular in Britain and you said pizza, which would be my guess too, but is the wrong answer, I'm afraid!
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: is* moʊst ˈpɑpjələr ɪn ˈbrɪtən ənd ju sɛd ˈpitsə, wɪʧ wʊd bi maɪ gɛs tu, bət ɪz ðə rɔŋ ˈænsər, əm afraid!

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: In fact, the top choice for Italian food fans here in the UK is garlic bread. Right, let's recap the vocabulary
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: in* fækt, ðə tɔp ʧɔɪs fər ˌɪˈtæljən fud fænz hir ɪn ðə uk* ɪz ˈgɑrlɪk brɛd. raɪt, lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə vocabulary

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: we've learned from this programme on Little Italy, starting with 'pizzeria' — 'a restaurant that sells pizza'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: we've* ˈlərnɪd frəm ðɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ɔn ˈlɪtəl ˈɪtəli, ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ 'ˌpitsərˈiə' —* 'ə ˈrɛˌstrɑnt ðət sɛlz pizza'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: If something is 'founded', it's 'started or established'. The adjective 'culinary' describes 'anything connected with cooking'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: if* ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪz 'ˈfaʊndɪd', ɪts 'ˈstɑrtɪd ər ɪˈstæblɪʃt'. ðə ˈæʤɪktɪv 'ˈkjulɪˌnɛri' dɪˈskraɪbz 'ˈɛniˌθɪŋ kəˈnɛktɪd wɪθ cooking'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Phrases like 'fourth or fifth generation' describe 'the children of people 'whose parents immigrated to a particular country'. A 'stepping stone'
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: phrases* laɪk 'fɔrθ ər fɪθ ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃən' dɪˈskraɪb 'ðə ˈʧɪldrən əv ˈpipəl 'huz ˈpɛrənts ˈɪməˌgreɪtɪd tɪ ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr ˈkəntri'. ə 'ˈstɛpɪŋ stone'

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: is 'an event or experience that helps you advance or achieve something new'. And finally, the adjective 'mainstream'
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: is* 'ən ɪˈvɛnt ər ɪkˈspɪriəns ðət hɛlps ju ədˈvæns ər əˈʧiv ˈsəmθɪŋ nu'. ənd ˈfaɪnəli, ðə ˈæʤɪktɪv 'mainstream'

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: describes 'the customs and culture 'which are accepted as normal by most people in a society'.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: describes* 'ðə ˈkəstəmz ənd ˈkəlʧər 'wɪʧ ər ækˈsɛptɪd ɛz ˈnɔrməl baɪ moʊst ˈpipəl ɪn ə society'.

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: Once again our six minutes are up. Until the next time, here at 6 Minute English, it's ciao! Ciao!
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: once* əˈgɛn ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp. ənˈtɪl ðə nɛkst taɪm, hir æt 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ, ɪts ʧaʊ! ciao!

OA1iXnZ0lNw.text: 6 Minute English. From BBC Learning English.
OA1iXnZ0lNw.ipa: 6* ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ. frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ.

DjA3naFOTvc.text: Hello. This is Real Easy English, the podcast

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Until recently, the vast majority of the world population worked on farms and the total production of the world's economy
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ənˈtɪl ˈrisəntli, ðə væst məˈʤɔrəti əv ðə wərld ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən wərkt ɔn fɑrmz ənd ðə ˈtoʊtəl pərˈdəkʃən əv ðə wərldz ɪˈkɑnəmi

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: was mostly the total agricultural output.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: wɑz ˈmoʊstli ðə ˈtoʊtəl ˌægrɪˈkəlʧərəl ˈaʊtˌpʊt.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: And this output was limited by the fixed size of the land.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ənd ðɪs ˈaʊtˌpʊt wɑz ˈlɪmɪtɪd baɪ ðə fɪkst saɪz əv ðə lænd.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The total output of the economy did not change a lot year by year.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə ˈtoʊtəl ˈaʊtˌpʊt əv ðə ɪˈkɑnəmi dɪd nɑt ʧeɪnʤ ə lɔt jɪr baɪ jɪr.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The size of the pie was fixed; the world was a zero-sum game.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə saɪz əv ðə paɪ wɑz fɪkst; ðə wərld wɑz ə ˈziroʊˌsəm geɪm.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: In such a stagnating world, the only way to get better off is if someone else gets worse off.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪn səʧ ə ˈstægˌneɪtɪŋ wərld, ðə ˈoʊnli weɪ tɪ gɪt ˈbɛtər ɔf ɪz ɪf ˈsəmˌwən ɛls gɪts wərs ɔf.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: if you take a bigger piece of the pie, someone else's gets smaller.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪf ju teɪk ə ˈbɪgər pis əv ðə paɪ, ˈsəmˌwən ˈɛlsɪz gɪts sˈmɔlər.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: If you want more food, then conquering, plundering, and stealing are great strategies.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪf ju wɔnt mɔr fud, ðɛn ˈkɑŋkərɪŋ, ˈpləndərɪŋ, ənd ˈstilɪŋ ər greɪt ˈstrætəʤiz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Your neighbors loss is your gain.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: jʊr ˈneɪbərz lɔs ɪz jʊr geɪn.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: This was the state of things for thousands of years.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðɪs wɑz ðə steɪt əv θɪŋz fər ˈθaʊzənz əv jɪrz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Societies invaded each other constantly to get more pie.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: səˈsaɪɪtiz ˌɪnˈveɪdɪd iʧ ˈəðər ˈkɑnstəntli tɪ gɪt mɔr paɪ.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Economic inequality was extreme. Some had all the pie they wanted, while others had to live with the crumbs.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɛkəˈnɑmɪk ˌɪnɪkˈwɑləti wɑz ɪkˈstrim. səm hæd ɔl ðə paɪ ðeɪ ˈwɔntɪd, waɪl ˈəðərz hæd tɪ lɪv wɪθ ðə krəmz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Then, the Industrial Revolution happened and everything changed.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðɛn, ðə ˌɪnˈdəstriəl ˌrɛvəˈluʃən ˈhæpənd ənd ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ʧeɪnʤd.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: We developed machinery, better crops, better fertilizers.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: wi dɪˈvɛləpt məˈʃinəri, ˈbɛtər krɑps, ˈbɛtər ˈfərtəˌlaɪzərz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Agricultural output skyrocketed, but we didn't just produce more food - every industrial sector exploded in terms of productivity.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌægrɪˈkəlʧərəl ˈaʊtˌpʊt ˈskaɪˌrɑkətɪd, bət wi ˈdɪdənt ʤɪst ˈproʊdus mɔr fud  ˈɛvəri ˌɪnˈdəstriəl ˈsɛktər ɪkˈsploʊdɪd ɪn tərmz əv ˌproʊdəkˈtɪvɪti.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: From 1700 to 1870, the production of iron in Britain increased 137 fold.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: frəm 1700 tɪ 1870 ðə pərˈdəkʃən əv aɪərn ɪn ˈbrɪtən ˌɪnˈkrist 137 foʊld.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The Industrial Revolution led to a previously unimaginable increase in economic output.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə ˌɪnˈdəstriəl ˌrɛvəˈluʃən lɛd tɪ ə ˈpriviəsli ˌənɪˈmæʤɪnəbəl ˌɪnˈkris ɪn ˌɛkəˈnɑmɪk ˈaʊtˌpʊt.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: This altered the nature of our societies.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðɪs ˈɔltərd ðə ˈneɪʧər əv ɑr səˈsaɪɪtiz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Economic growth changed the world from a zero-sum game to a positive-sum game.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɛkəˈnɑmɪk groʊθ ʧeɪnʤd ðə wərld frəm ə ˈziroʊˌsəm geɪm tɪ ə positive-sum* geɪm.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: We had found a way to create a bigger pie - but not only a bigger pie, but a pie that was growing bigger each year.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: wi hæd faʊnd ə weɪ tɪ kriˈeɪt ə ˈbɪgər paɪ  bət nɑt ˈoʊnli ə ˈbɪgər paɪ, bət ə paɪ ðət wɑz groʊɪŋ ˈbɪgər iʧ jɪr.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: More people could have more at the same time.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: mɔr ˈpipəl kʊd hæv mɔr æt ðə seɪm taɪm.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: This development is spreading and continuing today.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðɪs dɪˈvɛləpmənt ɪz ˈsprɛdɪŋ ənd kənˈtɪnjuɪŋ təˈdeɪ.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Antibiotics kill bacteria. Power plants deliver energy. Cell phones connect us. Planes let us travel cheaply.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌæntibaɪˈɑtɪks kɪl bækˈtɪriə. paʊər plænts dɪˈlɪvər ˈɛnərʤi. sɛl foʊnz kəˈnɛkt ˈjuˈɛs. pleɪnz lɛt ˈjuˈɛs ˈtrævəl ˈʧipli.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Fridges store food.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˈfrɪʤəz stɔr fud.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Continuous progress in all sectors of the economy seems normal to us today,
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: kənˈtɪnjuəs ˈprɑˌgrɛs ɪn ɔl ˈsɛktərz əv ðə ɪˈkɑnəmi simz ˈnɔrməl tɪ ˈjuˈɛs təˈdeɪ,

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: but the change from stagnation to economic growth really was the most drastic shift in human history.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: bət ðə ʧeɪnʤ frəm stægˈneɪʃən tɪ ˌɛkəˈnɑmɪk groʊθ ˈrɪli wɑz ðə moʊst ˈdræstɪk ʃɪft ɪn ˈjumən ˈhɪstəri.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: How was this possible?
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: haʊ wɑz ðɪs possible?

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: At the very core of this massive transformation stand new ideas that lead to innovation.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: æt ðə ˈvɛri kɔr əv ðɪs ˈmæsɪv ˌtrænsfərˈmeɪʃən stænd nu aɪˈdiəz ðət lɛd tɪ ˌɪnəˈveɪʃən.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Innovation has many different definitions, but in the context of this video, we mean better solutions to existing problems
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɪnəˈveɪʃən həz ˈmɛni ˈdɪfərənt ˌdɛfəˈnɪʃənz, bət ɪn ðə ˈkɑntɛkst əv ðɪs ˈvɪdioʊ, wi min ˈbɛtər səˈluʃənz tɪ ɪgˈzɪstɪŋ ˈprɑbləmz

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: and solutions to problems we didn't know we had.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ənd səˈluʃənz tɪ ˈprɑbləmz wi ˈdɪdənt noʊ wi hæd.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The more you innovate, the more complex and interesting problems you discover as your wishes and needs evolve.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə mɔr ju ˈɪnəˌveɪt, ðə mɔr ˈkɑmplɛks ənd ˈɪntəˌrɛstɪŋ ˈprɑbləmz ju dɪˈskəvər ɛz jʊr ˈwɪʃɪz ənd nidz ɪˈvɑlv.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The average citizen in Norway 250 years ago might have wanted some really good shoes.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə ˈævərɪʤ ˈsɪtɪzən ɪn ˈnɔrˌweɪ 250 jɪrz əˈgoʊ maɪt hæv ˈwɔntɪd səm ˈrɪli gʊd ʃuz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: 150 years ago, maybe a bicycle. 80 years ago, a car.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: 150 jɪrz əˈgoʊ, ˈmeɪbi ə ˈbaɪsɪkəl. 80 jɪrz əˈgoʊ, ə kɑr.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: 30 years ago, cheap air travel. And so on.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: 30 jɪrz əˈgoʊ, ʧip ɛr ˈtrævəl. ənd soʊ ɔn.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Once we get what we want, we don't stop;
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: wəns wi gɪt wət wi wɔnt, wi doʊnt stɑp;

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: we can see how we can improve things even more, and how to make things even better.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: wi kən si haʊ wi kən ˌɪmˈpruv θɪŋz ˈivɪn mɔr, ənd haʊ tɪ meɪk θɪŋz ˈivɪn ˈbɛtər.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The new positive-sum world has existed for 0.1% of human history and we have yet to get used to it.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə nu positive-sum* wərld həz ɪgˈzɪstəd fər 0.1* əv ˈjumən ˈhɪstəri ənd wi hæv jɛt tɪ gɪt juzd tɪ ɪt.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: It has a consequence that feels really unintuitive. In a positive-sum world,
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪt həz ə ˈkɑnsəkwəns ðət filz ˈrɪli unintuitive*. ɪn ə positive-sum* wərld,

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: it's in your personal selfish best interest that every human on planet earth is well off.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪts ɪn jʊr ˈpərsɪnəl ˈsɛlfɪʃ bɛst ˈɪntəˌrɛst ðət ˈɛvəri ˈjumən ɔn ˈplænət ərθ ɪz wɛl ɔf.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: It's good for you if people in obscure parts of countries you've never heard of are prospering.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪts gʊd fər ju ɪf ˈpipəl ɪn əbˈskjʊr pɑrts əv ˈkəntriz juv ˈnɛvər hərd əv ər ˈprɑspərɪŋ.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: There is a genuine selfish argument for making the world a better place.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðɛr ɪz ə ˈʤɛnjuˌaɪn ˈsɛlfɪʃ ˈɑrgjəmənt fər ˈmeɪkɪŋ ðə wərld ə ˈbɛtər pleɪs.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: In a positive-sum world, the more people are well-off, the better your own life is.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪn ə positive-sum* wərld, ðə mɔr ˈpipəl ər well-off*, ðə ˈbɛtər jʊr oʊn laɪf ɪz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: This is because of the nature of innovation; it is fundamentally driven by supply and demand.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðɪs ɪz bɪˈkəz əv ðə ˈneɪʧər əv ˌɪnəˈveɪʃən; ɪt ɪz ˌfəndəˈmɛnəli ˈdrɪvən baɪ səˈplaɪ ənd dɪˈmænd.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The supply increases when more people have the freedom and education to contribute.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə səˈplaɪ ˌɪnˈkrisɪz wɪn mɔr ˈpipəl hæv ðə ˈfridəm ənd ˌɛʤəˈkeɪʃən tɪ kənˈtrɪbjut.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: They become inventors, researchers, engineers or thinkers that come up with new ideas.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðeɪ bɪˈkəm ˌɪnˈvɛntərz, ˈrisərʧərz, ˈɛnʤəˈnɪrz ər ˈθɪŋkərz ðət kəm əp wɪθ nu aɪˈdiəz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The demand for ideas increases as people get richer, and can pay for new solutions.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə dɪˈmænd fər aɪˈdiəz ˌɪnˈkrisɪz ɛz ˈpipəl gɪt ˈrɪʧər, ənd kən peɪ fər nu səˈluʃənz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: They increase the size of the market for innovations.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðeɪ ˌɪnˈkris ðə saɪz əv ðə ˈmɑrkɪt fər ˌɪnəˈveɪʃənz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Innovation follows incentives.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɪnəˈveɪʃən ˈfɑloʊz ˌɪnˈsɪnɪvz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: So naturally, if many people want and can pay for something, it will get the innovators attention and energy.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: soʊ ˈnæʧərəli, ɪf ˈmɛni ˈpipəl wɔnt ənd kən peɪ fər ˈsəmθɪŋ, ɪt wɪl gɪt ðə ˈɪnəˌveɪtərz əˈtɛnʃən ənd ˈɛnərʤi.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Improving the lives of those who are worst off has a multiplying effect.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɪmˈpruvɪŋ ðə lɪvz əv ðoʊz hu ər wərst ɔf həz ə ˈməltəˌplaɪɪŋ ˈifɛkt.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: It increases demand for ideas while at the same time, making it easier for ideas to be produced.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪt ˌɪnˈkrisɪz dɪˈmænd fər aɪˈdiəz waɪl æt ðə seɪm taɪm, ˈmeɪkɪŋ ɪt ˈiziər fər aɪˈdiəz tɪ bi prəˈdust.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Let's take an example that interests all of us - a cure for cancer.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: lɛts teɪk ən ɪgˈzæmpəl ðət ˈɪntərɪsts ɔl əv ˈjuˈɛs  ə kjʊr fər ˈkænsər.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: If there are 1 billion people in the world that have the wealth to pay for cancer treatments,
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪf ðɛr ər 1 ˈbɪljən ˈpipəl ɪn ðə wərld ðət hæv ðə wɛlθ tɪ peɪ fər ˈkænsər ˈtritmənts,

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: innovation will follow this demand. So hundreds of billions of dollars have been invested in medical research.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɪnəˈveɪʃən wɪl ˈfɑloʊ ðɪs dɪˈmænd. soʊ ˈhənərdz əv ˈbɪljənz əv ˈdɔlərz hæv bɪn ˌɪnˈvɛstɪd ɪn ˈmɛdɪkəl ˈrisərʧ.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: This had a huge effect, but we're still nowhere near to curing all forms of cancer.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðɪs hæd ə juʤ ˈifɛkt, bət wɪr stɪl ˈnoʊˌwɛr nɪr tɪ ˈkjʊrɪŋ ɔl fɔrmz əv ˈkænsər.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Today, every sixth person in the world dies of cancer, and you might be one of them.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: təˈdeɪ, ˈɛvəri sɪksθ ˈpərsən ɪn ðə wərld daɪz əv ˈkænsər, ənd ju maɪt bi wən əv ðɛm.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Now, imagine if demand were higher.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: naʊ, ˌɪˈmæʤən ɪf dɪˈmænd wər haɪər.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Imagine instead of 1 billion people being able to pay for a cure for cancer, there were 4 billion or 7 billion.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɪˈmæʤən ˌɪnˈstɛd əv 1 ˈbɪljən ˈpipəl biɪŋ ˈeɪbəl tɪ peɪ fər ə kjʊr fər ˈkænsər, ðɛr wər 4 ˈbɪljən ər 7 ˈbɪljən.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Imagine how far medicine could have developed if we'd invested 7 times as much in curing cancer.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɪˈmæʤən haʊ fɑr ˈmɛdəsən kʊd hæv dɪˈvɛləpt ɪf wid ˌɪnˈvɛstɪd 7 taɪmz ɛz məʧ ɪn ˈkjʊrɪŋ ˈkænsər.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: On top of that, there's so much human potential being wasted right now.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɔn tɔp əv ðət, ðɛrz soʊ məʧ ˈjumən pəˈtɛnʃəl biɪŋ ˈweɪstɪd raɪt naʊ.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The work of a poor farmer in a developing nation is not useful to you.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə wərk əv ə pur ˈfɑrmər ɪn ə dɪˈvɛləpɪŋ ˈneɪʃən ɪz nɑt ˈjusfəl tɪ ju.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: But if he becomes better off, his children might spend their time in university developing things that are useful to you.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: bət ɪf hi bɪˈkəmz ˈbɛtər ɔf, hɪz ˈʧɪldrən maɪt spɛnd ðɛr taɪm ɪn ˌjunəˈvərsəti dɪˈvɛləpɪŋ θɪŋz ðət ər ˈjusfəl tɪ ju.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Instead of having some hotspots of innovation in the developed world, we would have many hotspots all over the world.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɪnˈstɛd əv ˈhævɪŋ səm hotspots* əv ˌɪnəˈveɪʃən ɪn ðə dɪˈvɛləpt wərld, wi wʊd hæv ˈmɛni hotspots* ɔl ˈoʊvər ðə wərld.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The research output of humanity would be many times what it is right now.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə ˈrisərʧ ˈaʊtˌpʊt əv juˈmænɪti wʊd bi ˈmɛni taɪmz wət ɪt ɪz raɪt naʊ.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Could we have cured cancer by now if that were the case? Well, maybe.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: could* wi hæv kjʊrd ˈkænsər baɪ naʊ ɪf ðət wər ðə keɪs? wɛl, maybe.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: If we spent 7 times as much on research, had 7 times as many people working on it,
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪf wi spɛnt 7 taɪmz ɛz məʧ ɔn ˈrisərʧ, hæd 7 taɪmz ɛz ˈmɛni ˈpipəl ˈwərkɪŋ ɔn ɪt,

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: and a global network of medical research, things would certainly be further ahead than they are now.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ənd ə ˈgloʊbəl ˈnɛtˌwərk əv ˈmɛdɪkəl ˈrisərʧ, θɪŋz wʊd ˈsərtənli bi ˈfərðər əˈhɛd ðən ðeɪ ər naʊ.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: And this is the core of the argument: the more people want the same thing that you want,
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ənd ðɪs ɪz ðə kɔr əv ðə ˈɑrgjəmənt: ðə mɔr ˈpipəl wɔnt ðə seɪm θɪŋ ðət ju wɔnt,

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: the more likely you are to get it.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə mɔr ˈlaɪkli ju ər tɪ gɪt ɪt.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: That is what it means to live in a positive-sum world.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðət ɪz wət ɪt minz tɪ lɪv ɪn ə positive-sum* wərld.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: You don't gain more pie if poor places stay poor.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ju doʊnt geɪn mɔr paɪ ɪf pur ˈpleɪsɪz steɪ pur.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Instead, you get more pie if poor places get richer, contribute ideas, and grow the global pie.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ˌɪnˈstɛd, ju gɪt mɔr paɪ ɪf pur ˈpleɪsɪz gɪt ˈrɪʧər, kənˈtrɪbjut aɪˈdiəz, ənd groʊ ðə ˈgloʊbəl paɪ.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Do you like space travel? Imagine there were billions of people in Africa and Asia with their own space programs,
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: do* ju laɪk speɪs ˈtrævəl? ˌɪˈmæʤən ðɛr wər ˈbɪljənz əv ˈpipəl ɪn ˈæfrɪkɑ ənd ˈeɪʒə wɪθ ðɛr oʊn speɪs programs,

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: and demand for satellites and moon bases and cities on Mars.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ənd dɪˈmænd fər ˈsætəˌlaɪts ənd mun ˈbeɪsɪz ənd ˈsɪtiz ɔn mɑrz.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: Do you like being alive? A few billion people paying for medical research could literally save your life.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: do* ju laɪk biɪŋ əˈlaɪv? ə fju ˈbɪljən ˈpipəl peɪɪŋ fər ˈmɛdɪkəl ˈrisərʧ kʊd ˈlɪtərəli seɪv jʊr laɪf.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: It's in your interest for people around the world to become better off.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪts ɪn jʊr ˈɪntəˌrɛst fər ˈpipəl əraʊnd ðə wərld tɪ bɪˈkəm ˈbɛtər ɔf.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: The faster we get to this version of the world, the better for you personally.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðə ˈfæstər wi gɪt tɪ ðɪs ˈvərʒən əv ðə wərld, ðə ˈbɛtər fər ju ˈpərsənəli.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: No matter what your motivation is, working on a better world is a very good thing to do -
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: noʊ ˈmætər wət jʊr ˌmoʊtəˈveɪʃən ɪz, ˈwərkɪŋ ɔn ə ˈbɛtər wərld ɪz ə ˈvɛri gʊd θɪŋ tɪ du

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: for others, and for you.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: fər ˈəðərz, ənd fər ju.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: This video was a collaboration with Max Roser and Our World in Data, and supported by the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ðɪs ˈvɪdioʊ wɑz ə kəˌlæbərˈeɪʃən wɪθ mæks ˈroʊzər ənd ɑr wərld ɪn ˈdætə, ənd səˈpɔrtɪd baɪ ðə bɪl ənd məˈlɪndə geɪts faʊnˈdeɪʃən.

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: If you want to help us stay afloat and make more videos,
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ɪf ju wɔnt tɪ hɛlp ˈjuˈɛs steɪ əˈfloʊt ənd meɪk mɔr ˈvɪdioʊz,

rvskMHn0sqQ.text: you can support us on Patreon or get some of our fancy posters.
rvskMHn0sqQ.ipa: ju kən səˈpɔrt ˈjuˈɛs ɔn patreon* ər gɪt səm əv ɑr ˈfænsi ˈpoʊstərz.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Weed is a serious drug that can have devastating consequences for your life.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: wid ɪz ə ˈsɪriəs drəg ðət kən hæv ˈdɛvəˌsteɪtɪŋ ˈkɑnsəkˌwɛnsəz fər jʊr life.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: This is an unpopular thing to say but it is true.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: this* ɪz ən ˌənˈpɑpjələr θɪŋ tɪ seɪ bət ɪt ɪz true.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: We don’t think it should be illegal because for most people using weed is not problematic or has only mild negative consequences.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: we* don’t* θɪŋk ɪt ʃʊd bi ˌɪˈligəl bɪˈkəz fər moʊst ˈpipəl ˈjuzɪŋ wid ɪz nɑt ˌprɑbləˈmætɪk ər həz ˈoʊnli maɪld ˈnɛgətɪv consequences.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: But about 20% of people who have ever tried it develop cannabis use disorder, and some of them become severely addicted.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: but* əˈbaʊt 20 əv ˈpipəl hu hæv ˈɛvər traɪd ɪt dɪˈvɛləp ˈkænəbəs juz dɪˈsɔrdər, ənd səm əv ðɛm bɪˈkəm səˈvɪrli addicted.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: That’s actually a stunning amount of people and many who start using weed don’t know what they are in for.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: that’s* ˈæˌkʧuəli ə ˈstənɪŋ əˈmaʊnt əv ˈpipəl ənd ˈmɛni hu stɑrt ˈjuzɪŋ wid don’t* noʊ wət ðeɪ ər ɪn for.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: In the US, daily or near daily weed use has skyrocketed since way before
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: in* ðə ˈjuˈɛs, ˈdeɪli ər nɪr ˈdeɪli wid juz həz ˈskaɪˌrɑkətɪd sɪns weɪ before

Brm71uCWr-I.text: legalization and there are now more daily users of weed than drinkers of alcohol. And most daily users consume very high amounts.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: legalization* ənd ðɛr ər naʊ mɔr ˈdeɪli ˈjuzərz əv wid ðən ˈdrɪŋkərz əv ˈælkəˌhɑl. ənd moʊst ˈdeɪli ˈjuzərz kənˈsum ˈvɛri haɪ amounts.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: In 2023, 1 in 15 adult Americans were suffering from some form of cannabis addiction.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: in* 2023 1 ɪn 15 ˈædəlt əˈmɛrɪkənz wər ˈsəfərɪŋ frəm səm fɔrm əv ˈkænəbəs addiction.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: In England, cannabis is by far the top drug teenagers seek help for.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: in* ˈɪŋglənd, ˈkænəbəs ɪz baɪ fɑr ðə tɔp drəg ˈtiˌneɪʤərz sik hɛlp for.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Today we’ll ignore medical use, casual users and people with mild
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: today* we’ll* ˌɪgˈnɔr ˈmɛdɪkəl juz, ˈkæʒəwəl ˈjuzərz ənd ˈpipəl wɪθ mild

Brm71uCWr-I.text: problems and instead we focus on the people who have a serious addiction
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: problems* ənd ˌɪnˈstɛd wi ˈfoʊkɪs ɔn ðə ˈpipəl hu hæv ə ˈsɪriəs addiction

Brm71uCWr-I.text: or are in danger of developing one, even if they are not aware of it.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: or* ər ɪn ˈdeɪnʤər əv dɪˈvɛləpɪŋ wən, ˈivɪn ɪf ðeɪ ər nɑt əˈwɛr əv it.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Weed is usually discussed in the context of teenagers because using
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: weed* ɪz ˈjuʒəwəli dɪˈskəst ɪn ðə ˈkɑntɛkst əv ˈtiˌneɪʤərz bɪˈkəz using

Brm71uCWr-I.text: any drug while your brain is melting and rebuilding itself is a bad idea.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: any* drəg waɪl jʊr breɪn ɪz ˈmɛltɪŋ ənd riˈbɪldɪŋ ˌɪtˈsɛlf ɪz ə bæd idea.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Instead we’ll have a frank conversation about how it affects you in your 20s and 30s.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: instead* we’ll* hæv ə fræŋk ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən əˈbaʊt haʊ ɪt əˈfɛkts ju ɪn jʊr 20s* ənd 30s*.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: We will combine research with our own personal experience of weed addiction – unfortunately we do have plenty of both.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: wi wɪl ˈkɑmbaɪn ˈrisərʧ wɪθ ɑr oʊn ˈpərsɪnəl ɪkˈspɪriəns əv wid əˈdɪkʃən –* ənˈfɔrʧənətli wi du hæv ˈplɛnti əv both.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Please check out our sources but also know this script is written
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: please* ʧɛk aʊt ɑr ˈsɔrsəz bət ˈɔlsoʊ noʊ ðɪs skrɪpt ɪz written

Brm71uCWr-I.text: by someone who used weed daily for 15 years before quitting. Ok, so let’s get into it.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: by* ˈsəmˌwən hu juzd wid ˈdeɪli fər 15 jɪrz ˌbiˈfɔr kˈwɪtɪŋ. ˈoʊˈkeɪ, soʊ let’s* gɪt ˈɪntu it.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Becoming Addicted By Accident
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: becoming* əˈdɪktɪd baɪ accident

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Most people slip into addiction gradually and slowly. They start using weed for a variety of reasons,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: most* ˈpipəl slɪp ˈɪntu əˈdɪkʃən ˈgræʤuəli ənd sˈloʊli. ðeɪ stɑrt ˈjuzɪŋ wid fər ə vərˈaɪəti əv reasons,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: to experiment, to cope or unwind from stress, because it is fun and easily available.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: to* ɪkˈspɛrəmənt, tɪ koʊp ər ənˈwaɪnd frəm strɛs, bɪˈkəz ɪt ɪz fən ənd ˈizəli available.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Weed can make you feel more alive and things more exciting. Music sounds better, movies are funnier, food is a delight.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: weed* kən meɪk ju fil mɔr əˈlaɪv ənd θɪŋz mɔr ɪkˈsaɪtɪŋ. mˈjuzɪk saʊnz ˈbɛtər, ˈmuviz ər ˈfəniər, fud ɪz ə delight.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: It can be great fun with friends and a major source of comfort.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: it* kən bi greɪt fən wɪθ frɛndz ənd ə ˈmeɪʤər sɔrs əv comfort.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: The frequency with which you are consuming weed is the most obvious thing to look out for.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* ˈfrikwənsi wɪθ wɪʧ ju ər kənˈsumɪŋ wid ɪz ðə moʊst ˈɑbviəs θɪŋ tɪ lʊk aʊt for.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: If you are using daily or almost daily this is a clear sign you are developing a problem.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: if* ju ər ˈjuzɪŋ ˈdeɪli ər ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˈdeɪli ðɪs ɪz ə klɪr saɪn ju ər dɪˈvɛləpɪŋ ə problem.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: As a daily user your risk of cannabis use disorder is up to 30% and certainly a pretty relevant risk,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: as* ə ˈdeɪli ˈjuzər jʊr rɪsk əv ˈkænəbəs juz dɪˈsɔrdər ɪz əp tɪ 30 ənd ˈsərtənli ə ˈprɪti ˈrɛləvənt risk,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: since it might take years before you realize the full weight of it. So if you are not careful about it,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: since* ɪt maɪt teɪk jɪrz ˌbiˈfɔr ju ˈriəˌlaɪz ðə fʊl weɪt əv ɪt. soʊ ɪf ju ər nɑt ˈkɛrfəl əˈbaʊt it,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: what started out as an occasional high becomes more and more normalized.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: what* ˈstɑrtɪd aʊt ɛz ən ɔˈkeɪʒənəl haɪ bɪˈkəmz mɔr ənd mɔr normalized.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Bit by bit your weekends revolve around weed or shift to activities that you can do stoned.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: bit* baɪ bɪt jʊr ˈwiˌkɛndz riˈvɑlv əraʊnd wid ər ʃɪft tɪ ækˈtɪvɪtiz ðət ju kən du stoned.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Often other hobbies or obligations become less of a priority.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: often* ˈəðər ˈhɑbiz ər ˌɑbləˈgeɪʃənz bɪˈkəm lɛs əv ə praɪˈɔrəti.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Then it creeps into most weekdays and finally it is an established ritual every day. Over time, weed’s nature changes.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: ðɛn ɪt krips ˈɪntu moʊst ˈwikˌdeɪz ənd ˈfaɪnəli ɪt ɪz ən ɪˈstæblɪʃt ˈrɪʧuəl ˈɛvəri deɪ. ˈoʊvər taɪm, weed’s* ˈneɪʧər changes.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: The joy enhancing effects dissipate, while a comfortable numbness takes over.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* ʤɔɪ ɛnˈhænsɪŋ ˈifɛkts ˈdɪsəˌpeɪt, waɪl ə ˈkəmfərtəbəl ˈnəmnəs teɪks over.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Weed can become your primary method of coping with life but it doesn’t make you stronger, better or more resilient.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: weed* kən bɪˈkəm jʊr ˈpraɪˌmɛri ˈmɛθəd əv ˈkoʊpɪŋ wɪθ laɪf bət ɪt doesn’t* meɪk ju ˈstrɔŋgər, ˈbɛtər ər mɔr resilient.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Instead it covers up negative emotions by building a snail shell that you can retreat into.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: instead* ɪt ˈkəvərz əp ˈnɛgətɪv ˈɪˌmoʊʃənz baɪ ˈbɪldɪŋ ə sneɪl ʃɛl ðət ju kən riˈtrit into.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Do you have a problem with weed? Super easy test: Stop doing it for four weeks, starting today. Not tomorrow, today.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: do* ju hæv ə ˈprɑbləm wɪθ wid? ˈsupər ˈizi tɛst: stɑp duɪŋ ɪt fər fɔr wiks, ˈstɑrtɪŋ təˈdeɪ. nɑt təˈmɑˌroʊ, today.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: No matter what you have coming up.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: no* ˈmætər wət ju hæv ˈkəmɪŋ up.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: If that feels challenging or you can’t make it, this should give you food for thought.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: if* ðət filz ˈʧælənʤɪŋ ər ju can’t* meɪk ɪt, ðɪs ʃʊd gɪv ju fud fər thought.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Once weed has become a daily or near daily habit the cumulative damage to your life can be profound.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: once* wid həz bɪˈkəm ə ˈdeɪli ər nɪr ˈdeɪli ˈhæbət ðə ˈkjumjələtɪv ˈdæmɪʤ tɪ jʊr laɪf kən bi profound.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: But first, let's talk about another thing that can become harmful without you noticing it – no data security.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: but* fərst, lɛts tɔk əˈbaʊt əˈnəðər θɪŋ ðət kən bɪˈkəm ˈhɑrmfəl wɪˈθaʊt ju ˈnoʊtɪsɪŋ ɪt –* noʊ ˈdætə security.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Today’s sponsor Incogni helps you reduce your risk of scam, harassment and more.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: today’s* ˈspɑnsər incogni* hɛlps ju rɪˈdus jʊr rɪsk əv skæm, hərˈæsmənt ənd more.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Data brokers constantly collect your valuable personal data, which can quickly fall into the wrong hands.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: data* ˈbroʊkərz ˈkɑnstəntli kəˈlɛkt jʊr ˈvæljəbəl ˈpərsɪnəl ˈdætə, wɪʧ kən kˈwɪkli fɔl ˈɪntu ðə rɔŋ hands.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: The result is lots of spam at best - or a stolen identity at worst.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* rɪˈzəlt ɪz lɑts əv spæm æt bɛst  ər ə ˈstoʊlən aɪˈdɛntəˌti æt worst.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: But you often don't even know that your data has been sold and it’s really hard to get it removed.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: but* ju ˈɔfən doʊnt ˈivɪn noʊ ðət jʊr ˈdætə həz bɪn soʊld ənd it’s* ˈrɪli hɑrd tɪ gɪt ɪt removed.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Besides, other brokers will continue right away so it starts all over again. Incogni helps you break the loop.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: besides*, ˈəðər ˈbroʊkərz wɪl kənˈtɪnju raɪt əˈweɪ soʊ ɪt stɑrts ɔl ˈoʊvər əˈgɛn. incogni* hɛlps ju breɪk ðə loop.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Simply give Incogni permission to work on your behalf, and your
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: simply* gɪv incogni* pərˈmɪʃən tɪ wərk ɔn jʊr bɪˈhæf, ənd jʊr

Brm71uCWr-I.text: data will be deleted automatically and is no longer offered for sale. You can also use the custom removal service to delete
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: ˈdætə wɪl bi dɪˈlitəd ˌɔtəˈmætɪkli ənd ɪz noʊ ˈlɔŋgər ˈɔfərd fər seɪl. ju kən ˈɔlsoʊ juz ðə ˈkəstəm rɪˈmuvəl ˈsərvɪs tɪ delete

Brm71uCWr-I.text: your data from specific sites that have exposed it without permission.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: your* ˈdætə frəm spɪˈsɪfɪk saɪts ðət hæv ɪkˈspoʊzd ɪt wɪˈθaʊt permission.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Incogni is the first and only data removal service that has been verified by Deloitte.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: incogni* ɪz ðə fərst ənd ˈoʊnli ˈdætə rɪˈmuvəl ˈsərvɪs ðət həz bɪn ˈvɛrəˌfaɪd baɪ deloitte.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Use the code KURZGESAGT to protect your peace of mind and get 60% off annual plans –
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: use* ðə koʊd kurzgesagt* tɪ prəˈtɛkt jʊr pis əv maɪnd ənd gɪt 60 ɔf ˈænjuəl plænz –

Brm71uCWr-I.text: and now back to weed.
Forever Tomorrow
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: and* naʊ bæk tɪ weed.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: For problematic weed users, life can sometimes progress in slow motion –
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: forever* tomorrow

Brm71uCWr-I.text: the addiction keeps you where you are, while your body ages and your friends move forward. But youth masks stagnation.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: for* ˌprɑbləˈmætɪk wid ˈjuzərz, laɪf kən ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ˈprɑˌgrɛs ɪn sloʊ ˈmoʊʃən –

Brm71uCWr-I.text: For years you don’t notice how much weed might be holding you back,
because if you are young, life changes by itself –
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* əˈdɪkʃən kips ju wɛr ju ər, waɪl jʊr ˈbɑdi ˈeɪʤɪz ənd jʊr frɛndz muv ˈfɔrwərd. bət juθ mæsks stagnation.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: the outside world takes on the initiative and provides a lot of support for you.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: for* jɪrz ju don’t* ˈnoʊtɪs haʊ məʧ wid maɪt bi ˈhoʊldɪŋ ju back,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: You are surrounded by peers, may still have childhood friends, progress through school,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: because* ɪf ju ər jəŋ, laɪf ˈʧeɪnʤɪz baɪ ˌɪtˈsɛlf –

Brm71uCWr-I.text: start work or university and find new places and friendships. Moving slowly is kind of ok in your twenties as life naturally
throws experiences and challenges at you. But it is still not great for you.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* ˈaʊtˈsaɪd wərld teɪks ɔn ðə ˌɪˈnɪʃətɪv ənd prəˈvaɪdz ə lɔt əv səˈpɔrt fər you.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: This is the time where your body is probably at its peak and you can use
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: you* ər sərˈaʊndɪd baɪ pɪrz, meɪ stɪl hæv ˈʧaɪlˌdhʊd frɛndz, ˈprɑˌgrɛs θru school,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: it for things you will not be able to do later.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: ɪt fər θɪŋz ju wɪl nɑt bi ˈeɪbəl tɪ du later.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: From sports to dating and staying out late, traveling and experiencing the world.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: from* spɔrts tɪ ˈdeɪtɪŋ ənd steɪɪŋ aʊt leɪt, ˈtrævəlɪŋ ənd ɪkˈspɪriənsɪŋ ðə world.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: It is the easiest time to build a healthy social life,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: it* ɪz ðə ˈiziəst taɪm tɪ bɪld ə ˈhɛlθi ˈsoʊʃəl life,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: the greatest predictor of how happy you will be over the next few decades.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* ˈgreɪtəst prɪˈdɪktər əv haʊ ˈhæpi ju wɪl bi ˈoʊvər ðə nɛkst fju decades.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: You will likely never have fewer responsibilities again, so you can explore and take risks.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: you* wɪl ˈlaɪkli ˈnɛvər hæv fjuər riˌspɑnsəˈbɪlətiz əˈgɛn, soʊ ju kən ɪkˈsplɔr ənd teɪk risks.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Spending this time stoned with passive activities like scrolling over reposts on reddit is something you might regret later.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: spending* ðɪs taɪm stoʊnd wɪθ ˈpæsɪv ækˈtɪvɪtiz laɪk scrolling* ˈoʊvər reposts* ɔn reddit* ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ ju maɪt rɪˈgrɛt later.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: You’ll never get this period of your life back. As you age out of your twenties things change massively.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: you’ll* ˈnɛvər gɪt ðɪs ˈpɪriəd əv jʊr laɪf bæk. ɛz ju eɪʤ aʊt əv jʊr tˈwɛntiz θɪŋz ʧeɪnʤ massively.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Around this time most people naturally tone down their weed consumption or quit entirely.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: around* ðɪs taɪm moʊst ˈpipəl ˈnæʧərəli toʊn daʊn ðɛr wid kənˈsəmʃən ər kwɪt entirely.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: You are entering one of life's great inflection points. And you risk getting left behind.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: you* ər ˈɛnərɪŋ wən əv laɪfs greɪt ˌɪnˈflɛkʃən pɔɪnts. ənd ju rɪsk ˈgɪtɪŋ lɛft behind.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Your Social Life is Different After 30
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: your* ˈsoʊʃəl laɪf ɪz ˈdɪfərənt ˈæftər 30

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Friendships need care, attention and active time investment to survive and weed addiction works against you on multiple levels.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: friendships* nid kɛr, əˈtɛnʃən ənd ˈæktɪv taɪm ˌɪnˈvɛstmənt tɪ sərˈvaɪv ənd wid əˈdɪkʃən wərks əˈgɛnst ju ɔn ˈməltəpəl levels.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Studies show that for some people weed can worsen social anxiety especially if you start using more and more.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: studies* ʃoʊ ðət fər səm ˈpipəl wid kən ˈwərsən ˈsoʊʃəl æŋˈzaɪəti əˈspɛʃəli ɪf ju stɑrt ˈjuzɪŋ mɔr ənd more.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: And once you feel anxious about socializing, it becomes pretty tempting to just stay home and avoid events or new experiences.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: and* wəns ju fil ˈæŋʃəs əˈbaʊt ˈsoʊʃəˌlaɪzɪŋ, ɪt bɪˈkəmz ˈprɪti ˈtɛmptɪŋ tɪ ʤɪst steɪ hoʊm ənd əˈvɔɪd ɪˈvɛnts ər nu experiences.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Since weed numbs feelings it’s easy to push away FOMO or guilt about canceling last minute.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: since* wid numbs* ˈfilɪŋz it’s* ˈizi tɪ pʊʃ əˈweɪ fomo* ər gɪlt əˈbaʊt ˈkænsəlɪŋ læst minute.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: You’ll go next time!! But will you? Speaking from personal experience – you may turn into a friend that is unreliable,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: you’ll* goʊ nɛkst taɪm!! bət wɪl ju? ˈspikɪŋ frəm ˈpərsɪnəl ɪkˈspɪriəns –* ju meɪ tərn ˈɪntu ə frɛnd ðət ɪz ˌənrɪˈlaɪəbəl,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: flaky or rarely shows up anymore.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: ˈfleɪki ər ˈrɛrli ʃoʊz əp anymore.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: But the more opportunities to hang with friends you skip, the fewer will be there in the future.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: but* ðə mɔr ˌɑpərˈtunətiz tɪ hæŋ wɪθ frɛndz ju skɪp, ðə fjuər wɪl bi ðɛr ɪn ðə future.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Especially if you do weed alone it can become the central focal point in your life.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: especially* ɪf ju du wid əˈloʊn ɪt kən bɪˈkəm ðə ˈsɛntrəl ˈfoʊkəl pɔɪnt ɪn jʊr life.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Reports show that many people with addictions gradually reduce the activities or hobbies they once enjoyed in favor of consuming.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: reports* ʃoʊ ðət ˈmɛni ˈpipəl wɪθ əˈdɪkʃənz ˈgræʤuəli rɪˈdus ðə ækˈtɪvɪtiz ər ˈhɑbiz ðeɪ wəns ˌɛnˈʤɔɪd ɪn ˈfeɪvər əv consuming.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Bit by bit this can make you withdraw from the people in your life.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: bit* baɪ bɪt ðɪs kən meɪk ju wɪθˈdrɔ frəm ðə ˈpipəl ɪn jʊr life.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: While this can be pretty bad for your social life in your 20s it
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: while* ðɪs kən bi ˈprɪti bæd fər jʊr ˈsoʊʃəl laɪf ɪn jʊr 20s* it

Brm71uCWr-I.text: may be catastrophic by the time you turn 30. Because in their 30s most people are plenty busy.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: may* bi ˌkætəˈstrɑfɪk baɪ ðə taɪm ju tərn 30 bɪˈkəz ɪn ðɛr 30s* moʊst ˈpipəl ər ˈplɛnti busy.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Careers, partners, travel, children and their closest friends are now priorities.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: careers*, ˈpɑrtnərz, ˈtrævəl, ˈʧɪldrən ənd ðɛr ˈkloʊsəst frɛndz ər naʊ priorities.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Free time becomes super valuable and casual hang outs turn into two adults comparing their calendars.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: free* taɪm bɪˈkəmz ˈsupər ˈvæljəbəl ənd ˈkæʒəwəl hæŋ aʊts tərn ˈɪntu tu ˈædəlts kəmˈpɛrɪŋ ðɛr calendars.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: So people in their 30s are way more selective about what and who they spend their time with.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: so* ˈpipəl ɪn ðɛr 30s* ər weɪ mɔr səˈlɛktɪv əˈbaʊt wət ənd hu ðeɪ spɛnd ðɛr taɪm with.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Smoking a joint drops way down their priority list. Even without falling out, you may simply become incompatible if your
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: smoking* ə ʤɔɪnt drɑps weɪ daʊn ðɛr praɪˈɔrəti lɪst. ˈivɪn wɪˈθaʊt ˈfɑlɪŋ aʊt, ju meɪ ˈsɪmpli bɪˈkəm ˌɪnkəmˈpætəbəl ɪf your

Brm71uCWr-I.text: identity becomes tied up with weed, or when your friends have a lot going on and you don’t.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: identity* bɪˈkəmz taɪd əp wɪθ wid, ər wɪn jʊr frɛndz hæv ə lɔt goʊɪŋ ɔn ənd ju don’t.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: This happens a lot without weed, but weed can supercharge it. So many problematic weed users focus on a small circle of
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: this* ˈhæpənz ə lɔt wɪˈθaʊt wid, bət wid kən ˌsupərˈʧɑrʤ ɪt. soʊ ˈmɛni ˌprɑbləˈmætɪk wid ˈjuzərz ˈfoʊkɪs ɔn ə smɔl ˈsərkəl of

Brm71uCWr-I.text: friends and acquaintances who are also using weed. This can create an isolation trap:
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: friends* ənd əkˈweɪntənsɪz hu ər ˈɔlsoʊ ˈjuzɪŋ wid. ðɪs kən kriˈeɪt ən ˌaɪsəˈleɪʃən trap:

Brm71uCWr-I.text: If your friends quit or they withdraw to use it alone, you can face sudden isolation and loneliness.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: if* jʊr frɛndz kwɪt ər ðeɪ wɪθˈdrɔ tɪ juz ɪt əˈloʊn, ju kən feɪs ˈsədən ˌaɪsəˈleɪʃən ənd ˈloʊnlinəs.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Or you may feel pressure to keep using it because you are scared of losing your friends.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: ər ju meɪ fil ˈprɛʃər tɪ kip ˈjuzɪŋ ɪt bɪˈkəz ju ər skɛrd əv ˈluzɪŋ jʊr friends.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: It is harder and takes longer to make new friends in your 30s than in your 20s,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: it* ɪz ˈhɑrdər ənd teɪks ˈlɔŋgər tɪ meɪk nu frɛndz ɪn jʊr 30s* ðən ɪn jʊr 20s,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: so to change your situation you need courage, energy and motivation – things weed addiction makes much harder to muster.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: so* tɪ ʧeɪnʤ jʊr ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən ju nid kərɪʤ, ˈɛnərʤi ənd ˌmoʊtəˈveɪʃən –* θɪŋz wid əˈdɪkʃən meɪks məʧ ˈhɑrdər tɪ muster.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Worse still, people who feel lonely tend to use more weed to cope – which is linked to even more social isolation.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: worse* stɪl, ˈpipəl hu fil ˈloʊnli tɛnd tɪ juz mɔr wid tɪ koʊp –* wɪʧ ɪz lɪŋkt tɪ ˈivɪn mɔr ˈsoʊʃəl isolation.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Weed can fill the role of your best friend. It is there when you are lonely,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: weed* kən fɪl ðə roʊl əv jʊr bɛst frɛnd. ɪt ɪz ðɛr wɪn ju ər lonely,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: it makes you feel ok in the moment. Your love life can also suffer.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: it* meɪks ju fil ˈoʊˈkeɪ ɪn ðə ˈmoʊmənt. jʊr ləv laɪf kən ˈɔlsoʊ suffer.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Social anxiety and complacency is a great motivator to put off dating and
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: social* æŋˈzaɪəti ənd kəmˈpleɪsənsi ɪz ə greɪt ˈmoʊtəveɪtər tɪ pʊt ɔf ˈdeɪtɪŋ and

Brm71uCWr-I.text: having sexual experiences in your 20s and it doesn’t get much easier as time passes.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: having* ˈsɛkʃuəl ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz ɪn jʊr 20s* ənd ɪt doesn’t* gɪt məʧ ˈiziər ɛz taɪm passes.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: In relationships weed addiction can be extremely disruptive. Reports show that partners of daily users can feel neglected,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: in* riˈleɪʃənˌʃɪps wid əˈdɪkʃən kən bi ɪkˈstrimli dɪsˈrəptɪv. rɪˈpɔrts ʃoʊ ðət ˈpɑrtnərz əv ˈdeɪli ˈjuzərz kən fil neglected,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: that couple communication can break down and that trust evaporates. The addicted partner usually doesn’t realize how poorly they
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: that* ˈkəpəl kəmˌjunəˈkeɪʃən kən breɪk daʊn ənd ðət trəst ɪˈvæpərˌeɪts. ðə əˈdɪktɪd ˈpɑrtnər ˈjuʒəwəli doesn’t* ˈriəˌlaɪz haʊ ˈpurli they

Brm71uCWr-I.text: are handling relationship issues and can be blindsided when their love leaves them.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: are* ˈhændəlɪŋ riˈleɪʃənˌʃɪp ˈɪʃuz ənd kən bi ˈblaɪndˌsaɪdɪd wɪn ðɛr ləv livz them.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: And to be frank, in their 30s people want a partner who is ready to share the growing
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: and* tɪ bi fræŋk, ɪn ðɛr 30s* ˈpipəl wɔnt ə ˈpɑrtnər hu ɪz ˈrɛdi tɪ ʃɛr ðə growing

Brm71uCWr-I.text: responsibilities of adulthood and wants to build a life together – the older you get,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: responsibilities* əv əˈdəlˌthʊd ənd wɔnts tɪ bɪld ə laɪf təˈgɛðər –* ðə ˈoʊldər ju get,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: the more unattractive your weed addiction makes you. Ok but who needs friends or love if you have a career, right?
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* mɔr ˌənəˈtræktɪv jʊr wid əˈdɪkʃən meɪks ju. ˈoʊˈkeɪ bət hu nidz frɛndz ər ləv ɪf ju hæv ə kərɪr, right?

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Well…
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: well…*…

Brm71uCWr-I.text: The Achievement Delay Machine
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: ðə əˈʧivmənt dɪˈleɪ machine

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Long term studies show that heavy weed users are more likely to end up with reduced academic
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: long* tərm ˈstədiz ʃoʊ ðət ˈhɛvi wid ˈjuzərz ər mɔr ˈlaɪkli tɪ ɛnd əp wɪθ rɪˈdust academic

Brm71uCWr-I.text: performance, a worse education, lower income, less savings, and less stable employment.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: performance*, ə wərs ˌɛʤəˈkeɪʃən, loʊər ˈɪnˌkəm, lɛs ˈseɪvɪŋz, ənd lɛs ˈsteɪbəl employment.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Even when compared to people from similar family backgrounds and social class.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: even* wɪn kəmˈpɛrd tɪ ˈpipəl frəm ˈsɪmələr ˈfæməli ˈbækˌgraʊndz ənd ˈsoʊʃəl class.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Many people with weed addiction struggle to show up for work, they’re not productive,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: many* ˈpipəl wɪθ wid əˈdɪkʃən ˈstrəgəl tɪ ʃoʊ əp fər wərk, they’re* nɑt productive,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: they procrastinate and they fail to meet their responsibilities.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: they* prəˈkræstəˌneɪt ənd ðeɪ feɪl tɪ mit ðɛr responsibilities.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: The consequences of missed opportunities and bad decisions slowly accumulate.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* ˈkɑnsəkˌwɛnsəz əv mɪst ˌɑpərˈtunətiz ənd bæd dɪˈsɪʒənz sˈloʊli accumulate.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Maybe you skipped networking events, had no ambition and didn’t get promoted, or hopped between entry-level jobs.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: maybe* ju skɪpt ˈnɛtˌwərkɪŋ ɪˈvɛnts, hæd noʊ æmˈbɪʃən ənd didn’t* gɪt prəˈmoʊtəd, ər hɑpt bɪtˈwin entry-level* jobs.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: It is also much harder to build up savings since the addiction can cost you thousands each year.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: it* ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ məʧ ˈhɑrdər tɪ bɪld əp ˈseɪvɪŋz sɪns ðə əˈdɪkʃən kən kɔst ju ˈθaʊzənz iʧ year.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: In your 20s you transition from being useless to being good at something.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: in* jʊr 20s* ju trænˈzɪʃən frəm biɪŋ ˈjusləs tɪ biɪŋ gʊd æt something.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: And so in your 30s expectations from others and yourself change sharply.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: and* soʊ ɪn jʊr 30s* ˌɛkspɛkˈteɪʃənz frəm ˈəðərz ənd ˈjɔrsɛlf ʧeɪnʤ sharply.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: People tend to cut you a lot of slack when you are young, but this has an expiration date.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: people* tɛnd tɪ kət ju ə lɔt əv slæk wɪn ju ər jəŋ, bət ðɪs həz ən ˌɛkspərˈeɪʃən date.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: The older you get, the worse it feels to compare
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* ˈoʊldər ju gɪt, ðə wərs ɪt filz tɪ compare

Brm71uCWr-I.text: yourself to peers that are moving on in life and start achieving things. And it is not just career goals,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: yourself* tɪ pɪrz ðət ər ˈmuvɪŋ ɔn ɪn laɪf ənd stɑrt əˈʧivɪŋ θɪŋz. ənd ɪt ɪz nɑt ʤɪst kərɪr goals,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: if you spend a decade or two using weed all the time you grow less as a person.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: if* ju spɛnd ə ˈdɛkeɪd ər tu ˈjuzɪŋ wid ɔl ðə taɪm ju groʊ lɛs ɛz ə ˈpərsən.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: You probably traveled less, went out less, met fewer people,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: ju ˈprɑbəˌbli ˈtrævəld lɛs, wɛnt aʊt lɛs, mɛt fjuər people,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: had fewer interesting experiences and in a way, lived less than your peers. Which also really becomes noticeable in your 30s.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: had* fjuər ˈɪntəˌrɛstɪŋ ɪkˈspɪriənsɪz ənd ɪn ə weɪ, lɪvd lɛs ðən jʊr pɪrz. wɪʧ ˈɔlsoʊ ˈrɪli bɪˈkəmz ˈnoʊtəsəbəl ɪn jʊr 30s.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: In some studies, long term chronic weed users had significantly lower life satisfaction, from
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: in* səm ˈstədiz, lɔŋ tərm ˈkrɑnɪk wid ˈjuzərz hæd sɪgˈnɪfɪkəntli loʊər laɪf ˌsætɪsˈfækʃən, from

Brm71uCWr-I.text: their motivation and pursuit of personal goals, social and love life to careers and options.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: their* ˌmoʊtəˈveɪʃən ənd pərˈsut əv ˈpərsɪnəl goʊlz, ˈsoʊʃəl ənd ləv laɪf tɪ kərɪrz ənd options.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Your life may just overall be much less fun and fulfilling that it could have been.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: your* laɪf meɪ ʤɪst ˈoʊvərˌɔl bi məʧ lɛs fən ənd fʊlˈfɪlɪŋ ðət ɪt kʊd hæv been.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: On top of all of this your mental health is the final thing that you may erode over time.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: on* tɔp əv ɔl əv ðɪs jʊr ˈmɛntəl hɛlθ ɪz ðə ˈfaɪnəl θɪŋ ðət ju meɪ ˈiroʊd ˈoʊvər time.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: The Prison of Weed
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: the* ˈprɪzən əv weed
