Brm71uCWr-I.text: What makes weed addiction so devious is how at first it can seem to improve your mental health.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: what* meɪks wid əˈdɪkʃən soʊ ˈdiviəs ɪz haʊ æt fərst ɪt kən sim tɪ ˌɪmˈpruv jʊr ˈmɛntəl health.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: It can calm anxiety or depressive feelings, make you more relaxed and feel less lonely.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: it* kən kɑm æŋˈzaɪəti ər dɪˈprɛsɪv ˈfilɪŋz, meɪk ju mɔr rɪˈlækst ənd fil lɛs lonely.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: But weed acts on your brain’s reward system and the effects can flip without you noticing.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: but* wid ækts ɔn jʊr brain’s* rɪˈwɔrd ˈsɪstəm ənd ðə ˈifɛkts kən flɪp wɪˈθaʊt ju noticing.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Weed can damage your ability to regulate your emotions and can
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: weed* kən ˈdæmɪʤ jʊr əˈbɪləˌti tɪ ˈrɛgjəˌleɪt jʊr ˈɪˌmoʊʃənz ənd can

Brm71uCWr-I.text: worsen anxiety or depressive feelings and can escalate into serious mental disorders.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: worsen* æŋˈzaɪəti ər dɪˈprɛsɪv ˈfilɪŋz ənd kən ˈɛskəˌleɪt ˈɪntu ˈsɪriəs ˈmɛntəl disorders.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Which you might think that you can self medicate against by doing even more weed.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: which* ju maɪt θɪŋk ðət ju kən sɛlf ˈmɛdɪˌkeɪt əˈgɛnst baɪ duɪŋ ˈivɪn mɔr weed.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: This can impair your coping skills, making you fragile and easily overwhelmed,
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: this* kən ˌɪmˈpɛr jʊr ˈkoʊpɪŋ skɪlz, ˈmeɪkɪŋ ju ˈfræʤəl ənd ˈizəli overwhelmed,

Brm71uCWr-I.text: unable to deal with stress, increase anxiety or depressive symptoms, worsen your mood, cause mood swings and irritability.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: unable* tɪ dil wɪθ strɛs, ˌɪnˈkris æŋˈzaɪəti ər dɪˈprɛsɪv ˈsɪmptəmz, ˈwərsən jʊr mud, kɔz mud swɪŋz ənd ˌɪrɪtəˈbɪləti.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: The addiction can hold back a dam of bottled up negative feelings that is
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: ðə əˈdɪkʃən kən hoʊld bæk ə dæm əv ˈbɑtəld əp ˈnɛgətɪv ˈfilɪŋz ðət is

Brm71uCWr-I.text: ready to crash into you and the people around you, at any time. Until eventually it doesn’t work anymore.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: ready* tɪ kræʃ ˈɪntu ju ənd ðə ˈpipəl əraʊnd ju, æt ˈɛni taɪm. ənˈtɪl ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli ɪt doesn’t* wərk anymore.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: Especially during a mental health crisis, weed can make things much worse for you.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: especially* ˈdʊrɪŋ ə ˈmɛntəl hɛlθ ˈkraɪsəs, wid kən meɪk θɪŋz məʧ wərs fər you.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: On the flipside there is research that shows that addicted people who
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: on* ðə flipside* ðɛr ɪz ˈrisərʧ ðət ʃoʊz ðət əˈdɪktɪd ˈpipəl who

Brm71uCWr-I.text: give up weed experience a noticeable improvement in their mental health. Here is the thing.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: give* əp wid ɪkˈspɪriəns ə ˈnoʊtəsəbəl ˌɪmˈpruvmənt ɪn ðɛr ˈmɛntəl hɛlθ. hir ɪz ðə thing.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: All the negatives we talked about, missing your life as it flies by, social isolation and not even
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: all* ðə ˈnɛgətɪvz wi tɔkt əˈbaʊt, ˈmɪsɪŋ jʊr laɪf ɛz ɪt flaɪz baɪ, ˈsoʊʃəl ˌaɪsəˈleɪʃən ənd nɑt even

Brm71uCWr-I.text: getting close to living up to your potential are not happening some time in the future but today. No matter your age.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: getting* kloʊz tɪ ˈlɪvɪŋ əp tɪ jʊr pəˈtɛnʃəl ər nɑt ˈhæpənɪŋ səm taɪm ɪn ðə fˈjuʧər bət təˈdeɪ. noʊ ˈmætər jʊr age.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: If you find yourself addicted or in danger of becoming addicted, there is a way out.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: if* ju faɪnd ˈjɔrsɛlf əˈdɪktɪd ər ɪn ˈdeɪnʤər əv bɪˈkəmɪŋ əˈdɪktɪd, ðɛr ɪz ə weɪ out.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: It is not fun and it will be hard. You need to quit.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: it* ɪz nɑt fən ənd ɪt wɪl bi hɑrd. ju nid tɪ quit.

Brm71uCWr-I.text: And the earlier you do, the better your life will be.
Brm71uCWr-I.ipa: and* ðə ˈərliər ju du, ðə ˈbɛtər jʊr laɪf wɪl bi.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: If you saw a wormhole in reality, it would appear round, spherical, a bit like a black hole.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪf ju sɔ ə ˈwərmˌhɔl ɪn ˌriˈæləˌti, ɪt wʊd əˈpɪr raʊnd, sˈfɛrɪkəl, ə bɪt laɪk ə blæk hoʊl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Light from the other side passes through and gives you a window to a faraway place.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: laɪt frəm ðə ˈəðər saɪd ˈpæsɪz θru ənd gɪvz ju ə ˈwɪndoʊ tɪ ə ˈfɑrəˈweɪ pleɪs.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Once crossed, the other side comes fully into view with your old home now receding into that shimmering spherical window.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: wəns krɔst, ðə ˈəðər saɪd kəmz ˈfʊli ˈɪntu vju wɪθ jʊr oʊld hoʊm naʊ rɪˈsidɪŋ ˈɪntu ðət ˈʃɪmərɪŋ sˈfɛrɪkəl ˈwɪndoʊ.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: But are wormholes real, or are they just magic disguised as physics and maths?
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: bət ər ˈwərmˌhɔlz ril, ər ər ðeɪ ʤɪst ˈmæʤɪk dɪsˈgaɪzd ɛz ˈfɪzɪks ənd maths?

9P6rdqiybaw.text: If they are real, how do they work and where can we find them?
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: if* ðeɪ ər ril, haʊ du ðeɪ wərk ənd wɛr kən wi faɪnd them?

9P6rdqiybaw.text: [Kurzgesagt Intro]
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: [kurzgesagt* ˈɪntroʊ]

9P6rdqiybaw.text: For most of human history, we thought space was pretty simple; a big flat stage where the events of the universe unfold.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: fər moʊst əv ˈjumən ˈhɪstəri, wi θɔt speɪs wɑz ˈprɪti ˈsɪmpəl; ə bɪg flæt steɪʤ wɛr ðə ɪˈvɛnts əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ənˈfoʊld.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Even if you take down the set of planets and stars, there's still something left.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈivɪn ɪf ju teɪk daʊn ðə sɛt əv ˈplænəts ənd stɑrz, ðɛrz stɪl ˈsəmθɪŋ lɛft.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: That empty stage is space and it exists,
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðət ˈɛmti steɪʤ ɪz speɪs ənd ɪt ɪgˈzɪsts,

9P6rdqiybaw.text: unchanging and eternal.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ənˈʧeɪnʤɪŋ ənd ɪˈtərnəl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Einstein's theory of relativity changed that.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈaɪnstaɪnz ˈθɪri əv ˌrɛləˈtɪvəti ʧeɪnʤd ðət.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: It says that space and time make up that stage together, and they aren't the same everywhere.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪt sɪz ðət speɪs ənd taɪm meɪk əp ðət steɪʤ təˈgɛðər, ənd ðeɪ ˈɑrənt ðə seɪm ˈɛvriˌwɛr.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: The things on the stage can affect the stage itself, stretching and warping it.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðə θɪŋz ɔn ðə steɪʤ kən əˈfɛkt ðə steɪʤ ˌɪtˈsɛlf, ˈstrɛʧɪŋ ənd ˈwɔrpɪŋ ɪt.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: If the old stage was like unmoving hardwood, Einstein's stage is more like a waterbed.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪf ðə oʊld steɪʤ wɑz laɪk unmoving* ˈhɑrdˌwʊd, ˈaɪnstaɪnz steɪʤ ɪz mɔr laɪk ə ˈwɔtərˌbɛd.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: This kind of elastic space can be bent and maybe even torn and patched together, which could make wormholes possible.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðɪs kaɪnd əv ɪˈlæstɪk speɪs kən bi bɛnt ənd ˈmeɪbi ˈivɪn tɔrn ənd pæʧt təˈgɛðər, wɪʧ kʊd meɪk ˈwərmˌhɔlz ˈpɑsəbəl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Let's see what that would look like in 2D.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: lɛts si wət ðət wʊd lʊk laɪk ɪn 2d*.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Our universe is like a big flat sheet, bent in just the right way,
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɑr ˈjunəˌvərs ɪz laɪk ə bɪg flæt ʃit, bɛnt ɪn ʤɪst ðə raɪt weɪ,

9P6rdqiybaw.text: wormholes could connect two very, very distant spots with a short bridge that you could cross almost instantaneously.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈwərmˌhɔlz kʊd kəˈnɛkt tu ˈvɛri, ˈvɛri ˈdɪstənt spɑts wɪθ ə ʃɔrt brɪʤ ðət ju kʊd krɔs ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˌɪnstənˈtæniəsli.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Enabling you to travel the universe even faster than the speed of light.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪˈneɪbəlɪŋ ju tɪ ˈtrævəl ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ˈivɪn ˈfæstər ðən ðə spid əv laɪt.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: So, where can we find a wormhole?
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: so*, wɛr kən wi faɪnd ə ˈwərmˌhɔl?

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Presently, only on paper.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈprɛzəntli, ˈoʊnli ɔn ˈpeɪpər.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: General relativity says they might be possible, but that doesn't mean they have to exist.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈʤɛnərəl ˌrɛləˈtɪvəti sɪz ðeɪ maɪt bi ˈpɑsəbəl, bət ðət ˈdəzənt min ðeɪ hæv tɪ ɪgˈzɪst.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: General relativity is a mathematical theory.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈʤɛnərəl ˌrɛləˈtɪvəti ɪz ə ˌmæθəˈmætɪkəl ˈθɪri.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: It's a set of equations that have many possible answers, but not all maths describes reality.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪts ə sɛt əv ɪkˈweɪʒənz ðət hæv ˈmɛni ˈpɑsəbəl ˈænsərz, bət nɑt ɔl maths* dɪˈskraɪbz ˌriˈæləˌti.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: But they are theoretically possible and there are different kinds.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: bət ðeɪ ər ˌθiərˈɛtɪkəli ˈpɑsəbəl ənd ðɛr ər ˈdɪfərənt kaɪnz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: EINSTEIN ROSEN BRIDGES
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈaɪnstaɪn ˈroʊzən ˈbrɪʤɪz

9P6rdqiybaw.text: The first kind of wormholes to be theorized were Einstein Rosen Bridges.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðə fərst kaɪnd əv ˈwərmˌhɔlz tɪ bi θiəraɪzd wər ˈaɪnstaɪn ˈroʊzən ˈbrɪʤɪz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: They describe every black hole as a sort of portal to an infinite parallel universe.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðeɪ dɪˈskraɪb ˈɛvəri blæk hoʊl ɛz ə sɔrt əv ˈpɔrtəl tɪ ən ˈɪnfənət ˈpɛrəˌlɛl ˈjunəˌvərs.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Let's try to picture them in 2D again. Empty space time is flat, but curved by objects on it.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: lɛts traɪ tɪ ˈpɪkʧər ðɛm ɪn 2d* əˈgɛn. ˈɛmti speɪs taɪm ɪz flæt, bət kərvd baɪ ˈɑbʤɛkts ɔn ɪt.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: If we compress that object, space-time gets more curved around it.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪf wi ˈkɑmprɛs ðət ˈɑbʤɛkt, ˈspeɪˌstaɪm gɪts mɔr kərvd əraʊnd ɪt.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Eventually, space-time becomes so warped that it has no choice but to collapse into a black hole.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli, ˈspeɪˌstaɪm bɪˈkəmz soʊ wɔrpt ðət ɪt həz noʊ ʧɔɪs bət tɪ kəˈlæps ˈɪntu ə blæk hoʊl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: A one-way barrier forms: the event horizon, which anything can enter but nothing can escape;
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ə ˈwənˈweɪ ˈbɛriər fɔrmz: ðə ɪˈvɛnt hərˈaɪzən, wɪʧ ˈɛniˌθɪŋ kən ˈɛnər bət ˈnəθɪŋ kən ɪˈskeɪp;

9P6rdqiybaw.text: trapped forever at the singularity at its core.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: træpt fərˈɛvər æt ðə singularity* æt ɪts kɔr.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: But maybe there is no singularity here.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: bət ˈmeɪbi ðɛr ɪz noʊ singularity* hir.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: One possibility is that the other side of the event horizon looks a bit like our universe again
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: wən ˌpɑsəˈbɪləˌti ɪz ðət ðə ˈəðər saɪd əv ðə ɪˈvɛnt hərˈaɪzən lʊks ə bɪt laɪk ɑr ˈjunəˌvərs əˈgɛn

9P6rdqiybaw.text: but mirrored upside down, where time runs backwards. In our universe things fall into the black hole.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: bət ˈmɪrərd ˈəpˈsaɪd daʊn, wɛr taɪm rənz ˈbækwərdz. ɪn ɑr ˈjunəˌvərs θɪŋz fɔl ˈɪntu ðə blæk hoʊl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: In the parallel universe, with backwards time, the mirror black hole is spewing things out a bit like a big bang.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪn ðə ˈpɛrəˌlɛl ˈjunəˌvərs, wɪθ ˈbækwərdz taɪm, ðə ˈmɪrər blæk hoʊl ɪz spjuɪŋ θɪŋz aʊt ə bɪt laɪk ə bɪg bæŋ.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: This is called a white hole.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðɪs ɪz kɔld ə waɪt hoʊl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Unfortunately, Einstein-rosen bridges can't actually be crossed.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ənˈfɔrʧənətli, einstein-rosen* ˈbrɪʤɪz kænt ˈæˌkʧuəli bi krɔst.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: It takes an infinite amount of time to cross over to the opposite universe and they crimp shut in the middle.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪt teɪks ən ˈɪnfənət əˈmaʊnt əv taɪm tɪ krɔs ˈoʊvər tɪ ðə ˈɑpəzɪt ˈjunəˌvərs ənd ðeɪ krɪmp ʃət ɪn ðə ˈmɪdəl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: If you go into a black hole, you won't become the stuff coming out of the white hole.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪf ju goʊ ˈɪntu ə blæk hoʊl, ju woʊnt bɪˈkəm ðə stəf ˈkəmɪŋ aʊt əv ðə waɪt hoʊl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: You'll only become dead.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: jul ˈoʊnli bɪˈkəm dɛd.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: So, to travel the cosmos in the blink of an eye, humans need a different kind of wormhole; a Traversable Wormhole.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: soʊ, tɪ ˈtrævəl ðə ˈkɑzmoʊs ɪn ðə blɪŋk əv ən aɪ, ˈjumənz nid ə ˈdɪfərənt kaɪnd əv ˈwərmˌhɔl; ə traversable* ˈwərmˌhɔl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: VERY OLD STRING THEORY WORMHOLES
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈvɛri oʊld strɪŋ ˈθɪri ˈwərmˌhɔlz

9P6rdqiybaw.text: If string theory or one of its variations is the correct description of our universe,
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪf strɪŋ ˈθɪri ər wən əv ɪts ˌvɛriˈeɪʃənz ɪz ðə kərˈɛkt dɪˈskrɪpʃən əv ɑr ˈjunəˌvərs,

9P6rdqiybaw.text: then we could be lucky and our universe might even have a tangled web of countless wormholes already.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðɛn wi kʊd bi ˈləki ənd ɑr ˈjunəˌvərs maɪt ˈivɪn hæv ə ˈtæŋgəld wɛb əv ˈkaʊntləs ˈwərmˌhɔlz ɔˈrɛdi.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Shortly after the Big Bang, Quantum fluctuations in the universe at the smallest scales
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈʃɔrtli ˈæftər ðə bɪg bæŋ, kˈwɑntəm ˌfləkʧuˈeɪʃənz ɪn ðə ˈjunəˌvərs æt ðə sˈmɔləst skeɪlz

9P6rdqiybaw.text: far far smaller than an atom may have created many, many traversable wormholes.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: fɑr fɑr sˈmɔlər ðən ən ˈætəm meɪ hæv kriˈeɪtɪd ˈmɛni, ˈmɛni traversable* ˈwərmˌhɔlz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Threaded through them are strings, called cosmic strings.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: θˈrɛdɪd θru ðɛm ər strɪŋz, kɔld ˈkɑzmɪk strɪŋz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: In the first billionth of a trillionth of a second after the Big Bang, the ends of these tiny, tiny wormholes were pulled light-years apart;
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪn ðə fərst ˈbɪljənθ əv ə trillionth* əv ə ˈsɛkənd ˈæftər ðə bɪg bæŋ, ðə ɛndz əv ðiz ˈtaɪni, ˈtaɪni ˈwərmˌhɔlz wər pʊld ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz əˈpɑrt;

9P6rdqiybaw.text: scattering them through the universe.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈskætərɪŋ ðɛm θru ðə ˈjunəˌvərs.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: If wormholes were made in the early universe,
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪf ˈwərmˌhɔlz wər meɪd ɪn ðə ˈərli ˈjunəˌvərs,

9P6rdqiybaw.text: whether with cosmic strings or some other way, they could be all over; just waiting to be discovered.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈwɛðər wɪθ ˈkɑzmɪk strɪŋz ər səm ˈəðər weɪ, ðeɪ kʊd bi ɔl ˈoʊvər; ʤɪst ˈweɪtɪŋ tɪ bi dɪˈskəvərd.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: One might even be closer than we realize.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: wən maɪt ˈivɪn bi ˈkloʊzər ðən wi ˈriəˌlaɪz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: From the outside, black holes and wormholes can look very similar;
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: frəm ðə ˈaʊtˈsaɪd, blæk hoʊlz ənd ˈwərmˌhɔlz kən lʊk ˈvɛri ˈsɪmələr;

9P6rdqiybaw.text: leading some physicists to suggest the supermassive black holes in the center of galaxies are actually wormholes.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈlidɪŋ səm ˈfɪzɪsɪsts tɪ səˈʤɛst ðə ˌsupərˈmæsɪv blæk hoʊlz ɪn ðə ˈsɛnər əv ˈgæləksiz ər ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈwərmˌhɔlz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: It will be very hard to go all the way to the center of the Milky Way to find out though, but that's okay.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪt wɪl bi ˈvɛri hɑrd tɪ goʊ ɔl ðə weɪ tɪ ðə ˈsɛnər əv ðə ˈmɪlki weɪ tɪ faɪnd aʊt ðoʊ, bət ðæts ˌoʊˈkeɪ.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: There might be an equally extremely hard way to get our hands on a wormhole, we could try to make one.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðɛr maɪt bi ən ˈikwəli ɪkˈstrimli hɑrd weɪ tɪ gɪt ɑr hænz ɔn ə ˈwərmˌhɔl, wi kʊd traɪ tɪ meɪk wən.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: MANMADE WORMHOLES
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈmænˈmeɪd ˈwərmˌhɔlz

9P6rdqiybaw.text: To be traversable and useful, there are a few properties we want a wormhole to have.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: tɪ bi traversable* ənd ˈjusfəl, ðɛr ər ə fju ˈprɑpərtiz wi wɔnt ə ˈwərmˌhɔl tɪ hæv.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: First, it must obviously connect to distant parts of space-time.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: fərst, ɪt məst ˈɑbviəsli kəˈnɛkt tɪ ˈdɪstənt pɑrts əv ˈspeɪˌstaɪm.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Like your bedroom and the bathroom,
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: laɪk jʊr ˈbɛˌdrum ənd ðə ˈbæθˌrum,

9P6rdqiybaw.text: or Earth and Jupiter.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ər ərθ ənd ˈʤupɪtər.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Second, it should not contain any event horizons, which would block two-way travel.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈsɛkənd, ɪt ʃʊd nɑt kənˈteɪn ˈɛni ɪˈvɛnt hərˈaɪzənz, wɪʧ wʊd blɑk ˈtuˌweɪ ˈtrævəl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Third, it should be sufficiently sized so that the gravitational forces don't kill human travelers.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: θərd, ɪt ʃʊd bi səˈfɪʃəntli saɪzd soʊ ðət ðə ˌgrævɪˈteɪʃənəl ˈfɔrsɪz doʊnt kɪl ˈjumən ˈtrævələrz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: The biggest problem we have to solve, is keeping our wormholes open.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðə ˈbɪgəst ˈprɑbləm wi hæv tɪ sɑlv, ɪz ˈkipɪŋ ɑr ˈwərmˌhɔlz ˈoʊpən.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: No matter how we make wormholes, gravity tries to close them.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: noʊ ˈmætər haʊ wi meɪk ˈwərmˌhɔlz, ˈgrævɪti traɪz tɪ kloʊz ðɛm.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Gravity wants to pinch it closed and cut the bridge; leaving only black holes at the ends.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈgrævɪti wɔnts tɪ pɪnʧ ɪt kloʊzd ənd kət ðə brɪʤ; ˈlivɪŋ ˈoʊnli blæk hoʊlz æt ðə ɛndz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Whether it's a traversable wormhole with both ends in ours, or a wormhole to another universe,
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈwɛðər ɪts ə traversable* ˈwərmˌhɔl wɪθ boʊθ ɛndz ɪn ɑrz, ər ə ˈwərmˌhɔl tɪ əˈnəðər ˈjunəˌvərs,

9P6rdqiybaw.text: it will try to close unless we have something propping it open.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪt wɪl traɪ tɪ kloʊz ənˈlɛs wi hæv ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈprɑpɪŋ ɪt ˈoʊpən.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: For very old string theory wormholes, that's the cosmic strings job.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: fər ˈvɛri oʊld strɪŋ ˈθɪri ˈwərmˌhɔlz, ðæts ðə ˈkɑzmɪk strɪŋz ʤɑb.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: For man-made wormholes, We need a new ingredient.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: fər ˈmænˌmeɪd ˈwərmˌhɔlz, wi nid ə nu ˌɪnˈgridiənt.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Exotic matter.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪgˈzɑtɪk ˈmætər.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: This isn't anything like we find on earth, or even antimatter.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðɪs ˈɪzənt ˈɛniˌθɪŋ laɪk wi faɪnd ɔn ərθ, ər ˈivɪn ætaɪˈmætər.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: It's something totally new and different and exciting, with crazy properties like nothing that's ever been seen before.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪts ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈtoʊtəli nu ənd ˈdɪfərənt ənd ɪkˈsaɪtɪŋ, wɪθ ˈkreɪzi ˈprɑpərtiz laɪk ˈnəθɪŋ ðæts ˈɛvər bɪn sin ˌbiˈfɔr.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Exotic matter is stuff that has a negative mass.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ɪgˈzɑtɪk ˈmætər ɪz stəf ðət həz ə ˈnɛgətɪv mæs.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Positive mass like people and planets and everything else in the universe, is attractive because of gravity.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈpɑzətɪv mæs laɪk ˈpipəl ənd ˈplænəts ənd ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ɛls ɪn ðə ˈjunəˌvərs, ɪz əˈtræktɪv bɪˈkəz əv ˈgrævɪti.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: But negative mass would be repulsive; it would push you away.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: bət ˈnɛgətɪv mæs wʊd bi riˈpəlsɪv; ɪt wʊd pʊʃ ju əˈweɪ.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: This makes a kind of anti-gravity the props open our wormholes.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðɪs meɪks ə kaɪnd əv anti-gravity* ðə prɑps ˈoʊpən ɑr ˈwərmˌhɔlz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: And exotic matter must exert enormous pressure to push space-time open, greater even than the pressure of the centers of neutron stars.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ənd ɪgˈzɑtɪk ˈmætər məst ɪgˈzərt ɪˈnɔrmɪs ˈprɛʃər tɪ pʊʃ ˈspeɪˌstaɪm ˈoʊpən, ˈgreɪtər ˈivɪn ðən ðə ˈprɛʃər əv ðə ˈsɛnərz əv ˈnuˌtrɑn stɑrz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: With exotic matter, we could weave space-time however we see fit.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: wɪθ ɪgˈzɑtɪk ˈmætər, wi kʊd wiv ˈspeɪˌstaɪm ˌhaʊˈɛvər wi si fɪt.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: We may even have a candidate for this exotic matter, the vacuum of space itself.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: wi meɪ ˈivɪn hæv ə ˈkænədɪt fər ðɪs ɪgˈzɑtɪk ˈmætər, ðə ˈvækjum əv speɪs ˌɪtˈsɛlf.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Quantum fluctuations in empty space are constantly creating pairs of particles and antiparticles, only for them to be annihilated an instant later.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: kˈwɑntəm ˌfləkʧuˈeɪʃənz ɪn ˈɛmti speɪs ər ˈkɑnstəntli kriˈeɪtɪŋ pɛrz əv ˈpɑrtɪkəlz ənd antiparticles*, ˈoʊnli fər ðɛm tɪ bi əˈnaɪəˌleɪtɪd ən ˈɪnstənt ˈleɪtər.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: The vacuum of space is boiling with them, and we can already manipulate them to produce an effect similar to the negative mass we're looking for.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðə ˈvækjum əv speɪs ɪz ˈbɔɪlɪŋ wɪθ ðɛm, ənd wi kən ɔˈrɛdi məˈnɪpjəˌleɪt ðɛm tɪ ˈproʊdus ən ˈifɛkt ˈsɪmələr tɪ ðə ˈnɛgətɪv mæs wɪr ˈlʊkɪŋ fər.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: We could use this to stabilize our wormholes.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: wi kʊd juz ðɪs tɪ ˈsteɪbəˌlaɪz ɑr ˈwərmˌhɔlz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Once we're keeping it open, the ends would start together. So, we'd have to move them around to interesting places.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: wəns wɪr ˈkipɪŋ ɪt ˈoʊpən, ðə ɛndz wʊd stɑrt təˈgɛðər. soʊ, wid hæv tɪ muv ðɛm əraʊnd tɪ ˈɪntəˌrɛstɪŋ ˈpleɪsɪz.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: We could start by wiring the solar system; leaving one end of each wormhole in orbit around the earth.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: wi kʊd stɑrt baɪ ˈwaɪrɪŋ ðə ˈsoʊlər ˈsɪstəm; ˈlivɪŋ wən ɛnd əv iʧ ˈwərmˌhɔl ɪn ˈɔrbət əraʊnd ðə ərθ.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: We could flick others into deep space.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: wi kʊd flɪk ˈəðərz ˈɪntu dip speɪs.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: The earth could be a wormhole hub for a vast interstellar human civilization spread over light-years,
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ðə ərθ kʊd bi ə ˈwərmˌhɔl həb fər ə væst ˌɪntərˈstɛlər ˈjumən ˌsɪvəlɪˈzeɪʃən sprɛd ˈoʊvər ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz,

9P6rdqiybaw.text: but only a wormhole away.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: bət ˈoʊnli ə ˈwərmˌhɔl əˈweɪ.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: However, wormholes have a dark side.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ˈwərmˌhɔlz hæv ə dɑrk saɪd.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Even opening a single wormhole, kind-of breaks the universe in fundamental ways, potentially creating time travel paradoxes,
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈivɪn ˈoʊpənɪŋ ə ˈsɪŋgəl ˈwərmˌhɔl, kind-of* breɪks ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ɪn ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl weɪz, pəˈtɛnʃəli kriˈeɪtɪŋ taɪm ˈtrævəl ˈpɛrəˌdɑksɪz,

9P6rdqiybaw.text: and violating the causal structure of the universe.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ənd ˈvaɪəleɪtɪŋ ðə ˈkɔzəl ˈstrəkʧər əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: Many scientists think that this not only means they should be impossible to make, but that it's impossible for them to exist at all.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: ˈmɛni ˈsaɪəntɪsts θɪŋk ðət ðɪs nɑt ˈoʊnli minz ðeɪ ʃʊd bi ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl tɪ meɪk, bət ðət ɪts ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl fər ðɛm tɪ ɪgˈzɪst æt ɔl.

9P6rdqiybaw.text: So, for now, we only know that wormholes exist in our hearts, and on paper in the form of equations.
9P6rdqiybaw.ipa: soʊ, fər naʊ, wi ˈoʊnli noʊ ðət ˈwərmˌhɔlz ɪgˈzɪst ɪn ɑr hɑrts, ənd ɔn ˈpeɪpər ɪn ðə fɔrm əv ɪkˈweɪʒənz.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: The vast majority of the cosmos is voids.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: the* væst məˈʤɔrəti əv ðə ˈkɑzmoʊs ɪz voids.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Gigantic, unfathomably large spaces of empty nothingness.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: gigantic*, unfathomably* lɑrʤ ˈspeɪsɪz əv ˈɛmti nothingness.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Bubbles of eternal night, stretching hundreds of millions of light-years,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: bubbles* əv ɪˈtərnəl naɪt, ˈstrɛʧɪŋ ˈhənərdz əv ˈmɪljənz əv light-years,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: almost entirely devoid of galaxies, stars, or light.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: almost* ɪnˈtaɪərli dɪˈvɔɪd əv ˈgæləksiz, stɑrz, ər light.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: The loneliest places in existence.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: the* loneliest* ˈpleɪsɪz ɪn existence.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Voids are not just the absence of stuff but weird worlds of darkness that are growing,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: voids* ər nɑt ʤɪst ðə ˈæbsəns əv stəf bət wɪrd wərldz əv ˈdɑrknəs ðət ər growing,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: drifting, colliding and merging with each other – Inside them space itself
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: drifting*, kəˈlaɪdɪŋ ənd ˈmərʤɪŋ wɪθ iʧ ˈəðər –* ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðɛm speɪs itself

yDAAlojz8NU.text: is stretched violently and it's almost impossible to enter them.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: is* strɛʧt ˈvaɪələntli ənd ɪts ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl tɪ ˈɛnər them.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Simply put: voids are weird and scary.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: simply* pʊt: vɔɪdz ər wɪrd ənd scary.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: But they sculpt the entire universe and may ultimately decide its fate.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: but* ðeɪ skəlpt ðə ɪnˈtaɪər ˈjunəˌvərs ənd meɪ ˈəltəmətli ˌdɪˈsaɪd ɪts fate.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Today we know of over 8,000 voids and supervoids, and we keep discovering more.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: today* wi noʊ əv ˈoʊvər 8,000* vɔɪdz ənd supervoids*, ənd wi kip dɪˈskəvərɪŋ more.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: No matter how large a cluster or supercluster of galaxies gets,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: no* ˈmætər haʊ lɑrʤ ə ˈkləstər ər supercluster* əv ˈgæləksiz gets,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: there always seems to be an even larger void nearby.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: there* ˈɔlˌweɪz simz tɪ bi ən ˈivɪn ˈlɑrʤər vɔɪd nearby.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Let’s jump off the cosmic cliff and drop into the heart of cosmic nothingness.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: let’s* ʤəmp ɔf ðə ˈkɑzmɪk klɪf ənd drɔp ˈɪntu ðə hɑrt əv ˈkɑzmɪk ˈnəθɪŋnəs.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: The Loneliest Place in the Universe You are zooming away from Earth, at thousands of times the speed of light,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: ðə loneliest* pleɪs ɪn ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ju ər ˈzumɪŋ əˈweɪ frəm ərθ, æt ˈθaʊzənz əv taɪmz ðə spid əv light,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: leaving our solar system and our solar neighborhood behind. Now we see the entire Milky Way with its 200 billion stars and
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: leaving* ɑr ˈsoʊlər ˈsɪstəm ənd ɑr ˈsoʊlər ˈneɪbərˌhʊd bɪˈhaɪnd. naʊ wi si ðə ɪnˈtaɪər ˈmɪlki weɪ wɪθ ɪts 200 ˈbɪljən stɑrz and

yDAAlojz8NU.text: dozens of dwarf galaxies zipping around it. 2.5 million light-years away on a collision course
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: dozens* əv dwɔrf ˈgæləksiz ˈzɪpɪŋ əraʊnd ɪt. 2.5* ˈmɪljən ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz əˈweɪ ɔn ə kəˈlɪʒən course

yDAAlojz8NU.text: is giant Andromeda and its own swarm of satellite galaxies.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: is* ʤaɪənt ænˈdrɑmədə ənd ɪts oʊn swɔrm əv ˈsætəˌlaɪt galaxies.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: We are now moving a million times faster than the speed of light, seeing the local group of over 50
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: we* ər naʊ ˈmuvɪŋ ə ˈmɪljən taɪmz ˈfæstər ðən ðə spid əv laɪt, siɪŋ ðə ˈloʊkəl grup əv ˈoʊvər 50

yDAAlojz8NU.text: galaxies woven together by gravity, rivers of gas, and invisible scaffolds of dark matter.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: galaxies* ˈwoʊvən təˈgɛðər baɪ ˈgrævɪti, ˈrɪvərz əv gæs, ənd ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl ˈskæfəldz əv dɑrk matter.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: This is our pocket of the universe, 10 million light years across, no human will ever leave it.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: this* ɪz ɑr ˈpɑkət əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs, 10 ˈmɪljən laɪt jɪrz əˈkrɔs, noʊ ˈjumən wɪl ˈɛvər liv it.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Except for you apparently.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: except* fər ju apparently.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: As we zoom away even faster we see the Virgo Supercluster,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: as* wi zum əˈweɪ ˈivɪn ˈfæstər wi si ðə ˈvərgoʊ supercluster,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: a colossal wall of more than 2,000 galaxies spread over roughly 100 million light-years.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: a* kəˈlɑsəl wɔl əv mɔr ðən 2,000* ˈgæləksiz sprɛd ˈoʊvər ˈrəfli 100 ˈmɪljən light-years.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Careful now, you are right on the edge of the cosmic cliff where the true,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: careful* naʊ, ju ər raɪt ɔn ðə ɛʤ əv ðə ˈkɑzmɪk klɪf wɛr ðə true,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: deep darkness begins: the Local Void – a gigantic, empty bubble 200 million light-years across.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: deep* ˈdɑrknəs bɪˈgɪnz: ðə ˈloʊkəl vɔɪd –* ə ʤaɪˈgænɪk, ˈɛmti ˈbəbəl 200 ˈmɪljən ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz across.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: If it was a bright thing and not absolute darkness, it would fill 40% of the night sky we see from earth.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: if* ɪt wɑz ə braɪt θɪŋ ənd nɑt ˈæbsəˌlut ˈdɑrknəs, ɪt wʊd fɪl 40 əv ðə naɪt skaɪ wi si frəm earth.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: All around us are dozens of other superclusters
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: all* əraʊnd ˈjuˈɛs ər ˈdəzənz əv ˈəðər superclusters*

yDAAlojz8NU.text: and gigantic voids filled with suffocating emptiness.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: ənd ʤaɪˈgænɪk vɔɪdz fɪld wɪθ ˈsəfəˌkeɪtɪŋ emptiness.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: You are now traveling towards the greatest and
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: you* ər naʊ ˈtrævəlɪŋ təˈwɔrdz ðə ˈgreɪtəst and

yDAAlojz8NU.text: emptiest nothing in existence – right into the center of the Boötes Supervoid.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: emptiest* ˈnəθɪŋ ɪn ɪgˈzɪstəns –* raɪt ˈɪntu ðə ˈsɛnər əv ðə boötes* supervoid.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: A cosmic desert around 300 million light-years wide.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: a* ˈkɑzmɪk ˈdɛzərt əraʊnd 300 ˈmɪljən ˈlaɪˌtjɪrz wide.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: So gigantic it should contain thousands of galaxies. But instead, what do you see?
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: so* ʤaɪˈgænɪk ɪt ʃʊd kənˈteɪn ˈθaʊzənz əv ˈgæləksiz. bət ˌɪnˈstɛd, wət du ju see?

yDAAlojz8NU.text: You are surrounded by perfect darkness, the most absolute blackness the human mind can conceive.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: you* ər sərˈaʊndɪd baɪ ˈpərˌfɪkt ˈdɑrknəs, ðə moʊst ˈæbsəˌlut ˈblæknəs ðə ˈjumən maɪnd kən conceive.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: There is no up or down. No motion. Nothing to orient yourself.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: there* ɪz noʊ əp ər daʊn. noʊ ˈmoʊʃən. ˈnəθɪŋ tɪ ˈɔriˌɛnt yourself.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: There is not a single sign that the outside universe even exists. It’s an inescapable prison.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: there* ɪz nɑt ə ˈsɪŋgəl saɪn ðət ðə ˈaʊtˈsaɪd ˈjunəˌvərs ˈivɪn ɪgˈzɪsts. it’s* ən ˌɪnɪˈskeɪpəbəl prison.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: And this isn’t some exotic corner of the cosmos. This is how the vast majority of the universe feels to human eyes.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: and* ðɪs isn’t* səm ɪgˈzɑtɪk ˈkɔrnər əv ðə ˈkɑzmoʊs. ðɪs ɪz haʊ ðə væst məˈʤɔrəti əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs filz tɪ ˈjumən eyes.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Just silent blackness without any movement. Everywhere. Forever.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: just* ˈsaɪlənt ˈblæknəs wɪˈθaʊt ˈɛni ˈmuvmənt. ˈɛvriˌwɛr. forever.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Although there is something mysterious hiding in the dark – faint tendrils of dark matter,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: although* ðɛr ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ mɪˈstɪriəs ˈhaɪdɪŋ ɪn ðə dɑrk –* feɪnt tendrils* əv dɑrk matter,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: penetrating into the void like cosmic lichen. A miniature echo of the much larger forest of dark matter filaments that
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: penetrating* ˈɪntu ðə vɔɪd laɪk ˈkɑzmɪk ˈlaɪkən. ə ˈmɪnɪˌʧʊr ˈɛkoʊ əv ðə məʧ ˈlɑrʤər ˈfɔrɪst əv dɑrk ˈmætər ˈfɪləmənts that

yDAAlojz8NU.text: forms the scaffold of galaxies and galaxy clusters outside the void.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: forms* ðə ˈskæfəld əv ˈgæləksiz ənd ˈgæləksi ˈkləstərz ˈaʊtˈsaɪd ðə void.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: And at their tips we find faint blueish specs in the ocean of darkness:
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: and* æt ðɛr tɪps wi faɪnd feɪnt bluɪʃ spɛks ɪn ðə ˈoʊʃən əv darkness:

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Void galaxies, lonely fireflies unable to light up the night.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: void* ˈgæləksiz, ˈloʊnli ˈfaɪərˌflaɪz əˈneɪbəl tɪ laɪt əp ðə naɪt.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: The rarest galaxies we know, very isolated, very lonely.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: ðə ˈrɛrəst ˈgæləksiz wi noʊ, ˈvɛri ˈaɪsəˌleɪtɪd, ˈvɛri lonely.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: A Universe of Bubbles Before we could look deep into space,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: a* ˈjunəˌvərs əv ˈbəbəlz ˌbiˈfɔr wi kʊd lʊk dip ˈɪntu space,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: astronomers thought we lived in a uniform cosmos with galaxies spread out evenly. But instead, we found that galaxies,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: astronomers* θɔt wi lɪvd ɪn ə ˈjunəˌfɔrm ˈkɑzmoʊs wɪθ ˈgæləksiz sprɛd aʊt ˈivənli. bət ˌɪnˈstɛd, wi faʊnd ðət galaxies,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: cosmic gas and dark matter were arranged into a vast cosmic web.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: cosmic* gæs ənd dɑrk ˈmætər wər əreɪnʤd ˈɪntu ə væst ˈkɑzmɪk web.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: A recurring pattern of sheets and filaments, organized around enormous empty gaps,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: a* rɪˈkərɪŋ ˈpætərn əv ʃits ənd ˈfɪləmənts, ˈɔrgəˌnaɪzd əraʊnd ɪˈnɔrmɪs ˈɛmti gaps,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: meeting at dense knots with galaxy clusters and super clusters. But this structure is not static,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: meeting* æt dɛns nɑts wɪθ ˈgæləksi ˈkləstərz ənd ˈsupər ˈkləstərz. bət ðɪs ˈstrəkʧər ɪz nɑt static,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: it just seems to be because the distances between galaxies are so incredibly vast.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: it* ʤɪst simz tɪ bi bɪˈkəz ðə ˈdɪstənsɪz bɪtˈwin ˈgæləksiz ər soʊ ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli vast.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: In reality galaxies shoot through space at speeds of millions of kilometers per hour.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: in* ˌriˈæləˌti ˈgæləksiz ʃut θru speɪs æt spidz əv ˈmɪljənz əv ˈkɪləˌmitərz pər hour.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: They are on collision courses, orbiting each other, moving towards the center of larger galaxy clusters millions of light years away.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: they* ər ɔn kəˈlɪʒən ˈkɔrsɪz, ˈɔrbətɪŋ iʧ ˈəðər, ˈmuvɪŋ təˈwɔrdz ðə ˈsɛnər əv ˈlɑrʤər ˈgæləksi ˈkləstərz ˈmɪljənz əv laɪt jɪrz away.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: But they always seem to stick to the rims of voids
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: but* ðeɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz sim tɪ stɪk tɪ ðə rɪmz əv voids

yDAAlojz8NU.text: like reflections of light on soap bubbles – which is kind of weird.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: like* rɪˈflɛkʃənz əv laɪt ɔn soʊp ˈbəbəlz –* wɪʧ ɪz kaɪnd əv weird.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: If they are this dynamic, shouldn’t a galaxy shoot into a void occasionally?
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: if* ðeɪ ər ðɪs daɪˈnæmɪk, shouldn’t* ə ˈgæləksi ʃut ˈɪntu ə vɔɪd occasionally?

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Well voids are actually extremely hard to enter for galaxies from the outside – at
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: well* vɔɪdz ər ˈæˌkʧuəli ɪkˈstrimli hɑrd tɪ ˈɛnər fər ˈgæləksiz frəm ðə ˈaʊtˈsaɪd –* at

yDAAlojz8NU.text: least naturally – because gravity becomes weird at their edges and even weirder deeper inside them.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: least* ˈnæʧərəli –* bɪˈkəz ˈgrævɪti bɪˈkəmz wɪrd æt ðɛr ˈɛʤɪz ənd ˈivɪn ˈwɪrdər ˈdipər ˌɪnˈsaɪd them.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: If you didn't know better, you might think voids spit out anything trying to get inside, pushing galaxies to the edge.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: if* ju ˈdɪdənt noʊ ˈbɛtər, ju maɪt θɪŋk vɔɪdz spɪt aʊt ˈɛniˌθɪŋ traɪɪŋ tɪ gɪt ˌɪnˈsaɪd, ˈpʊʃɪŋ ˈgæləksiz tɪ ðə ɛʤ.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: The way gravity works is that everything with mass in the universe attracts every other thing with mass.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: ðə weɪ ˈgrævɪti wərks ɪz ðət ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ wɪθ mæs ɪn ðə ˈjunəˌvərs əˈtrækts ˈɛvəri ˈəðər θɪŋ wɪθ mass.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: And since there is almost no mass inside,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: and* sɪns ðɛr ɪz ˈɔlˌmoʊst noʊ mæs inside,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: the cosmic web of galaxy super clusters on their edges are pulling things out of voids.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: the* ˈkɑzmɪk wɛb əv ˈgæləksi ˈsupər ˈkləstərz ɔn ðɛr ˈɛʤɪz ər ˈpʊlɪŋ θɪŋz aʊt əv voids.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: The emptier a void is, the harder gravity is pulling on what remains.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: the* ˈɛmtiər ə vɔɪd ɪz, ðə ˈhɑrdər ˈgrævɪti ɪz ˈpʊlɪŋ ɔn wət remains.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: It's really like a tug of war where one side isn’t even trying.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: it's* ˈrɪli laɪk ə təg əv wɔr wɛr wən saɪd isn’t* ˈivɪn trying.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Over time voids are really only getting even emptier and the walls and knots around them denser and brighter.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: over* taɪm vɔɪdz ər ˈrɪli ˈoʊnli ˈgɪtɪŋ ˈivɪn ˈɛmtiər ənd ðə wɔlz ənd nɑts əraʊnd ðɛm ˈdɛnsər ənd brighter.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Would it be hard to fly into a cosmic void with a spaceship? Well not technically,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: would* ɪt bi hɑrd tɪ flaɪ ˈɪntu ə ˈkɑzmɪk vɔɪd wɪθ ə ˈspeɪsˌʃɪp? wɛl nɑt technically,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: the hardest part is escaping the gravity of your home galaxy.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: ðə ˈhɑrdəst pɑrt ɪz ɪˈskeɪpɪŋ ðə ˈgrævɪti əv jʊr hoʊm galaxy.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: It also doesn’t make much sense because… what exactly do you want
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: it* ˈɔlsoʊ doesn’t* meɪk məʧ sɛns because…*… wət ɪgˈzæktli du ju want

yDAAlojz8NU.text: to visit inside a void? It makes sense to fly into a void if you want to hide. Like really, really hide.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: to* ˈvɪzɪt ˌɪnˈsaɪd ə vɔɪd? ɪt meɪks sɛns tɪ flaɪ ˈɪntu ə vɔɪd ɪf ju wɔnt tɪ haɪd. laɪk ˈrɪli, ˈrɪli hide.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: But what cosmic horror would be scary enough for a civilization to try to escape into a cosmic void?
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: but* wət ˈkɑzmɪk ˈhɔrər wʊd bi ˈskɛri ɪˈnəf fər ə ˌsɪvəlɪˈzeɪʃən tɪ traɪ tɪ ɪˈskeɪp ˈɪntu ə ˈkɑzmɪk void?

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Galaxies in the crowded cluster regions like our milky way are very active,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: galaxies* ɪn ðə ˈkraʊdɪd ˈkləstər ˈriʤənz laɪk ɑr ˈmɪlki weɪ ər ˈvɛri active,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: since its neighbours’ gravity tugs and pulls at it, they collide and merge.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: since* ɪts neighbours’*’ ˈgrævɪti təgz ənd pʊlz æt ɪt, ðeɪ kəˈlaɪd ənd merge.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Void galaxies are so isolated that they are aging in slow motion.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: void* ˈgæləksiz ər soʊ ˈaɪsəˌleɪtɪd ðət ðeɪ ər ˈeɪʤɪŋ ɪn sloʊ motion.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: They tend to be smaller, bluer and full of gas, birthing new stars slower and calmer.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: they* tɛnd tɪ bi sˈmɔlər, bluər ənd fʊl əv gæs, ˈbərθɪŋ nu stɑrz sloʊər ənd calmer.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: So void galaxies could be the last places that will stay habitable in our dying universe.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: so* vɔɪd ˈgæləksiz kʊd bi ðə læst ˈpleɪsɪz ðət wɪl steɪ ˈhæbətəbəl ɪn ɑr daɪɪŋ universe.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: The last star in the universe will likely be born here.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: the* læst stɑr ɪn ðə ˈjunəˌvərs wɪl ˈlaɪkli bi bɔrn hir.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: So maybe in a 100 trillion years or so a desperate alien race will embark on an
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: soʊ ˈmeɪbi ɪn ə 100 ˈtrɪljən jɪrz ər soʊ ə ˈdɛspərɪt ˈeɪliən reɪs wɪl ɪmˈbɑrk ɔn an

yDAAlojz8NU.text: impossible journey to stretch their existence just a bit longer inside a void galaxy.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: impossible* ˈʤərni tɪ strɛʧ ðɛr ɪgˈzɪstəns ʤɪst ə bɪt ˈlɔŋgər ˌɪnˈsaɪd ə vɔɪd galaxy.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: There is one more thing that makes voids unique places: Dark Energy. The mysterious force that most scientists think is accelerating
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: there* ɪz wən mɔr θɪŋ ðət meɪks vɔɪdz juˈnik ˈpleɪsɪz: dɑrk ˈɛnərʤi. ðə mɪˈstɪriəs fɔrs ðət moʊst ˈsaɪəntɪsts θɪŋk ɪz accelerating

yDAAlojz8NU.text: the expansion of the universe and will ultimately cause its demise.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: the* ɪkˈspænʧən əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ənd wɪl ˈəltəmətli kɔz ɪts demise.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: We can’t see dark energy do anything inside our galaxy or inside clusters because there is too
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: we* can’t* si dɑrk ˈɛnərʤi du ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ˌɪnˈsaɪd ɑr ˈgæləksi ər ˌɪnˈsaɪd ˈkləstərz bɪˈkəz ðɛr ɪz too

yDAAlojz8NU.text: much stuff pulling things together via gravity – but we can see its effects inside voids.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: much* stəf ˈpʊlɪŋ θɪŋz təˈgɛðər ˈviə ˈgrævɪti –* bət wi kən si ɪts ˈifɛkts ˌɪnˈsaɪd voids.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Here dark energy blows up the bubbles of nothing.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: here* dɑrk ˈɛnərʤi bloʊz əp ðə ˈbəbəlz əv nothing.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: This is where the acceleration of cosmic expansion becomes visible.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: this* ɪz wɛr ðə ˌækˌsɛlərˈeɪʃən əv ˈkɑzmɪk ɪkˈspænʧən bɪˈkəmz visible.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: As voids are getting larger and larger, they are breaking the structure of the universe,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: as* vɔɪdz ər ˈgɪtɪŋ ˈlɑrʤər ənd ˈlɑrʤər, ðeɪ ər ˈbreɪkɪŋ ðə ˈstrəkʧər əv ðə universe,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: the beautiful galaxy filaments are slowly being ripped apart.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: the* ˈbjutəfəl ˈgæləksi ˈfɪləmənts ər sˈloʊli biɪŋ rɪpt apart.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: As the emptiness encroaches, walls of thousands of galaxies are thinned out
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: as* ðə ˈɛmtinəs ɪnˈkroʊʧɪz, wɔlz əv ˈθaʊzənz əv ˈgæləksiz ər θɪnd out

yDAAlojz8NU.text: and pulled towards the edges – attracted by much denser regions at the margins,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: and* pʊld təˈwɔrdz ðə ˈɛʤɪz –* əˈtræktəd baɪ məʧ ˈdɛnsər ˈriʤənz æt ðə margins,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: giving space to the emptiness of two void bubbles becoming one.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: giving* speɪs tɪ ðə ˈɛmtinəs əv tu vɔɪd ˈbəbəlz bɪˈkəmɪŋ one.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: In the far, far future supervoids will take over the observable universe.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: in* ðə fɑr, fɑr fˈjuʧər supervoids* wɪl teɪk ˈoʊvər ðə əbˈzərvəbəl universe.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Crushing clusters, and expanding further and further, until the entire observable
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: crushing* ˈkləstərz, ənd ɪkˈspændɪŋ ˈfərðər ənd ˈfərðər, ənˈtɪl ðə ɪnˈtaɪər əbˈzərvəbəl

yDAAlojz8NU.text: universe is nothing more than a gigantic void of nothingness.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: ˈjunəˌvərs ɪz ˈnəθɪŋ mɔr ðən ə ʤaɪˈgænɪk vɔɪd əv nothingness.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: The loneliest place in existence. Let’s head back to Earth.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: the* loneliest* pleɪs ɪn ɪgˈzɪstəns. let’s* hɛd bæk tɪ earth.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: When we land, please exit through the gift shop and pick up a few souvenirs.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: when* wi lænd, pliz ˈɛksət θru ðə gɪft ʃɑp ənd pɪk əp ə fju souvenirs.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: We created this latest Space Souvenir Drop for all of you who have been
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: we* kriˈeɪtɪd ðɪs ˈleɪtəst speɪs ˌsuvəˈnɪr drɔp fər ɔl əv ju hu hæv been

yDAAlojz8NU.text: travelling with us for so many years now. While you’re there, take a look around,
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: travelling* wɪθ ˈjuˈɛs fər soʊ ˈmɛni jɪrz naʊ. waɪl you’re* ðɛr, teɪk ə lʊk around,

yDAAlojz8NU.text: you might discover a unique product dedicated to your favorite science topic.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: you* maɪt dɪˈskəvər ə juˈnik ˈprɑdəkt ˈdɛdəkeɪtəd tɪ jʊr ˈfeɪvərɪt saɪəns topic.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Everything is created with lots of care by our team of scientists and designers to inspire you and spark your curiosity.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: everything* ɪz kriˈeɪtɪd wɪθ lɑts əv kɛr baɪ ɑr tim əv ˈsaɪəntɪsts ənd dɪˈzaɪnərz tɪ ˌɪnˈspaɪr ju ənd spɑrk jʊr curiosity.

yDAAlojz8NU.text: Thank you for traveling with kurzgesagt space tours.
yDAAlojz8NU.ipa: thank* ju fər ˈtrævəlɪŋ wɪθ kurzgesagt* speɪs tʊrz.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Every tree you have ever seen is dead.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: ˈɛvəri tri ju hæv ˈɛvər sin ɪz dead.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: It turns out the alive part of a tree is just a tiny, paper thin strip of cells,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: it* tərnz aʊt ðə əˈlaɪv pɑrt əv ə tri ɪz ʤɪst ə ˈtaɪni, ˈpeɪpər θɪn strɪp əv cells,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: trapped between a dead skeleton and dead outsides.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: trapped* bɪtˈwin ə dɛd ˈskɛlətən ənd dɛd outsides.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Trees are some of the most extreme beings on earth with one of the most unique strategies in
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: trees* ər səm əv ðə moʊst ɪkˈstrim biɪŋz ɔn ərθ wɪθ wən əv ðə moʊst juˈnik ˈstrætəʤiz in

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: nature – on top of being mostly dead stuff, they forge their body out of thin air, they mercilessly
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: nature* –* ɔn tɔp əv biɪŋ ˈmoʊstli dɛd stəf, ðeɪ fɔrʤ ðɛr ˈbɑdi aʊt əv θɪn ɛr, ðeɪ mercilessly

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: crush rocks with acid, and they have an internal negative pressure that would kill you instantly.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: crush* rɑks wɪθ ˈæsəd, ənd ðeɪ hæv ən ˌɪnˈtərnəl ˈnɛgətɪv ˈprɛʃər ðət wʊd kɪl ju instantly.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: So, how do trees actually work? Let’s go back…to the beginning.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: so*, haʊ du triz ˈæˌkʧuəli wərk? let’s* goʊ back…to* ðə beginning.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: The Ancient Battle for the Sky
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: the* ˈeɪnʧənt ˈbætəl fər ðə sky

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: For over a billion years the ancestors of plants only inhabited the sun drenched surfaces of the oceans.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: for* ˈoʊvər ə ˈbɪljən jɪrz ðə ˈænˌsɛstərz əv plænts ˈoʊnli ˌɪnˈhæbətəd ðə sən drɛnʧt ˈsərfəsɪz əv ðə oceans.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Their bodies were thin and delicate and absorbed water straight through their surfaces, getting their energy through photosynthesis.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: their* ˈbɑdiz wər θɪn ənd ˈdɛləkət ənd əbˈzɔrbd ˈwɔtər streɪt θru ðɛr ˈsərfəsɪz, ˈgɪtɪŋ ðɛr ˈɛnərʤi θru photosynthesis.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Forging sunlight, carbon dioxide and water into sugar.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: forging* ˈsənˌlaɪt, ˈkɑrbən daɪˈɑkˌsaɪd ənd ˈwɔtər ˈɪntu sugar.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: But about 470 million years ago they decided to conquer a hostile alien planet: the land.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: but* əˈbaʊt 470 ˈmɪljən jɪrz əˈgoʊ ðeɪ ˌdɪˈsaɪdɪd tɪ ˈkɑŋkər ə ˈhɑstəl ˈeɪliən ˈplænət: ðə land.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Like green rugs with ambition these plant ancestors began clinging to the ground wherever it was wet and damp.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: like* grin rəgz wɪθ æmˈbɪʃən ðiz plænt ˈænˌsɛstərz bɪˈgæn ˈklɪŋɪŋ tɪ ðə graʊnd wɛˈrɛvər ɪt wɑz wɛt ənd damp.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: But now, with solid ground beneath and no ability to float around a new
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: but* naʊ, wɪθ ˈsɑləd graʊnd bɪˈniθ ənd noʊ əˈbɪləˌti tɪ floʊt əraʊnd ə new

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: dimension became a place of intense warfare: up.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: dimension* bɪˈkeɪm ə pleɪs əv ˌɪnˈtɛns ˈwɔrˌfɛr: əp.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: The higher they grew, the more sun they would get, while starving their competition below.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: ðə haɪər ðeɪ gru, ðə mɔr sən ðeɪ wʊd gɪt, waɪl ˈstɑrvɪŋ ðɛr ˌkɑmpəˈtɪʃən below.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Height became a deadly weapon. The battle for the sky had begun.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: height* bɪˈkeɪm ə ˈdɛdli ˈwɛpən. ðə ˈbætəl fər ðə skaɪ hæd begun.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Until now, plants were built mostly from cellulose, which was great for shape,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: until* naʊ, plænts wər bɪlt ˈmoʊstli frəm ˈsɛljəˌloʊs, wɪʧ wɑz greɪt fər shape,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: but not for strength, which limited how tall they could grow. Over dozens of millions of years of evolutionary warfare,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: but* nɑt fər strɛŋθ, wɪʧ ˈlɪmɪtɪd haʊ tɔl ðeɪ kʊd groʊ. ˈoʊvər ˈdəzənz əv ˈmɪljənz əv jɪrz əv ˌɛvəˈluʃəˌnɛri warfare,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: a group of plants developed one of life's greatest breakthroughs: lignin. Lignin is a macromolecule made of ring-shaped structures.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: a* grup əv plænts dɪˈvɛləpt wən əv laɪfs ˈgreɪtəst ˈbreɪkθˌruz: ˈlɪgnɪn. ˈlɪgnɪn ɪz ə macromolecule* meɪd əv ring-shaped* structures.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: It is rigid, tough, waterproof, and incredibly hard to break down. Concrete in a world made of jelly.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: it* ɪz ˈrɪʤɪd, təf, ˈwɔtərˌpruf, ənd ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbli hɑrd tɪ breɪk daʊn. ˈkɑnkrit ɪn ə wərld meɪd əv jelly.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: It filled gaps between cellulose threads, stiffening and locking everything into place.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: it* fɪld gæps bɪtˈwin ˈsɛljəˌloʊs θrɛdz, ˈstɪfənɪŋ ənd ˈlɑkɪŋ ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ˈɪntu place.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Lignin gave plants the strength to grow taller and claim the sun for themselves.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: lignin* geɪv plænts ðə strɛŋθ tɪ groʊ ˈtɔlər ənd kleɪm ðə sən fər themselves.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: But of course now they were competing with each other, which must have been really annoying for them.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: but* əv kɔrs naʊ ðeɪ wər kəmˈpitɪŋ wɪθ iʧ ˈəðər, wɪʧ məst hæv bɪn ˈrɪli əˈnɔɪɪŋ fər them.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: So as more millions of years passed, some plants just went all in on lignin
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: so* ɛz mɔr ˈmɪljənz əv jɪrz pæst, səm plænts ʤɪst wɛnt ɔl ɪn ɔn lignin

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: and produced more and more of it, becoming stiffer, harder and stronger.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: and* prəˈdust mɔr ənd mɔr əv ɪt, bɪˈkəmɪŋ ˈstɪfər, ˈhɑrdər ənd stronger.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Until one day, around 385 million years ago they
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: until* wən deɪ, əraʊnd 385 ˈmɪljən jɪrz əˈgoʊ they

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: got the biological equivalent of steel reinforced concrete: wood.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: got* ðə ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl ɪkˈwɪvələnt əv stil ˌriɪnˈfɔrst ˈkɑnkrit: wood.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: More on it later, but with this magic material the first trees emerged.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: more* ɔn ɪt ˈleɪtər, bət wɪθ ðɪs ˈmæʤɪk məˈtɪriəl ðə fərst triz emerged.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Almost immediately they became the largest living beings alive,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: almost* ˌɪˈmiˌdiətli ðeɪ bɪˈkeɪm ðə ˈlɑrʤəst ˈlɪvɪŋ biɪŋz əˈlaɪv,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: shooting up to 20 meters high into the sky – and they only got bigger from here.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: ˈʃutɪŋ əp tɪ 20 ˈmitərz haɪ ˈɪntu ðə skaɪ –* ənd ðeɪ ˈoʊnli gɑt ˈbɪgər frəm here.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: But this enormous size created extremely hard problems:
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: but* ðɪs ɪˈnɔrmɪs saɪz kriˈeɪtɪd ɪkˈstrimli hɑrd problems:

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: How do you get water from the ground up to the green parts that do the actual photosynthesis?
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: how* du ju gɪt ˈwɔtər frəm ðə graʊnd əp tɪ ðə grin pɑrts ðət du ðə ˈækʧəwəl photosynthesis?

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: And how do you get the sugar they produce down to the cells that keep you up there?
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: and* haʊ du ju gɪt ðə ˈʃʊgər ðeɪ ˈproʊdus daʊn tɪ ðə sɛlz ðət kip ju əp there?

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: On the scale of a cell a distance of a few meters is like you’re working
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: on* ðə skeɪl əv ə sɛl ə ˈdɪstəns əv ə fju ˈmitərz ɪz laɪk you’re* working

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: in Britain while your lunch box is in Egypt and your drink in New York. How do you not die?
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: in* ˈbrɪtən waɪl jʊr lənʧ bɑks ɪz ɪn ˈiʤɪpt ənd jʊr drɪŋk ɪn nu jɔrk. haʊ du ju nɑt die?

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: So trees came up with one of the most amazing ways any organism grows, and became nearly immortal by accident.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: so* triz keɪm əp wɪθ wən əv ðə moʊst əˈmeɪzɪŋ weɪz ˈɛni ˈɔrgəˌnɪzəm groʊz, ənd bɪˈkeɪm ˈnɪrli ˌɪˈmɔrtəl baɪ accident.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: A Conveyor Belt of Death
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: a* kənˈveɪər bɛlt əv death

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Let us slice an ancient tree in half and get to the heart running it all:
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: let* ˈjuˈɛs slaɪs ən ˈeɪnʧənt tri ɪn hæf ənd gɪt tɪ ðə hɑrt ˈrənɪŋ ɪt all:

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: the cambium, a razor-thin, circular zone, just a few stem cells wide.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: the* cambium*, ə razor-thin*, ˈsərkjələr zoʊn, ʤɪst ə fju stɛm sɛlz wide.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: These stem cells grow inward and outwards, turning into two groups of specialists.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: these* stɛm sɛlz groʊ ˈɪnwərd ənd ˈaʊtwərdz, ˈtərnɪŋ ˈɪntu tu grups əv specialists.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: The inward specialists are on a conveyor belt of death, the xylem. With each new division it is pushing the cambium outwards,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: the* ˈɪnwərd ˈspɛʃəlɪsts ər ɔn ə kənˈveɪər bɛlt əv dɛθ, ðə xylem*. wɪθ iʧ nu dɪˈvɪʒən ɪt ɪz ˈpʊʃɪŋ ðə cambium* outwards,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: making the tree thicker the older it gets. As the xylem cells mature their lignin production goes into overdrive,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: making* ðə tri ˈθɪkər ðə ˈoʊldər ɪt gɪts. ɛz ðə xylem* sɛlz məˈʧʊr ðɛr ˈlɪgnɪn pərˈdəkʃən goʊz ˈɪntu overdrive,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: and they become hard and stuffy, like a muscle slowly transforming into bone.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: and* ðeɪ bɪˈkəm hɑrd ənd ˈstəfi, laɪk ə ˈməsəl sˈloʊli trænsˈfɔrmɪŋ ˈɪntu bone.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: They begin to hollow themselves out, shedding everything that once made them alive. And then they die.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: they* bɪˈgɪn tɪ ˈhɑloʊ ðɛmˈsɛlvz aʊt, ˈʃɛdɪŋ ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ðət wəns meɪd ðɛm əˈlaɪv. ənd ðɛn ðeɪ daɪ.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: What’s left is a corpse, a hard, empty tube.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: what’s* lɛft ɪz ə kɔrps, ə hɑrd, ˈɛmti tube.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: As the tree grows year after year, new corpses are layered on old corpses, forming rings of hardened, dead tissue.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: as* ðə tri groʊz jɪr ˈæftər jɪr, nu ˈkɔrpsɪz ər leɪərd ɔn oʊld ˈkɔrpsɪz, ˈfɔrmɪŋ rɪŋz əv ˈhɑrdənd, dɛd tissue.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: A graveyard of trillions of plant bones. This is what we call wood.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: a* ˈgreɪvˌjɑrd əv ˈtrɪljənz əv plænt boʊnz. ðɪs ɪz wət wi kɔl wood.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Stacked together they form a giant network of pipes that stretches the whole length of the tree.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: stacked* təˈgɛðər ðeɪ fɔrm ə ʤaɪənt ˈnɛtˌwərk əv paɪps ðət ˈstrɛʧɪz ðə hoʊl lɛŋθ əv ðə tree.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: This network uses the chemical properties of water and a few other tricks to move it with incredible efficiency.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: this* ˈnɛtˌwərk ˈjuzɪz ðə ˈkɛmɪkəl ˈprɑpərtiz əv ˈwɔtər ənd ə fju ˈəðər trɪks tɪ muv ɪt wɪθ ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbəl efficiency.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Water molecules are, for a lack of a better word, sticky, like tiny magnets, and naturally cling tightly to each other.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: water* ˈmɑləˌkjulz ər, fər ə læk əv ə ˈbɛtər wərd, ˈstɪki, laɪk ˈtaɪni ˈmægnəts, ənd ˈnæʧərəli klɪŋ ˈtaɪtli tɪ iʧ other.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: When one moves, it pulls the next along with it, like pulling on a rope.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: when* wən muvz, ɪt pʊlz ðə nɛkst əˈlɔŋ wɪθ ɪt, laɪk ˈpʊlɪŋ ɔn ə rope.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: In trees this rope starts in the roots and ends in the leaves that bathe in the warmth from the sun.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: in* triz ðɪs roʊp stɑrts ɪn ðə ruts ənd ɛndz ɪn ðə livz ðət beɪð ɪn ðə wɔrmθ frəm ðə sun.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Here the heat from the sun evaporates 95% of the water that got sucked into the roots, from billions of tiny pores,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: here* ðə hit frəm ðə sən ɪˈvæpərˌeɪts 95 əv ðə ˈwɔtər ðət gɑt səkt ˈɪntu ðə ruts, frəm ˈbɪljənz əv ˈtaɪni pores,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: releasing a constant invisible mist of water molecules around the crown of the tree.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: releasing* ə ˈkɑnstənt ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl mɪst əv ˈwɔtər ˈmɑləˌkjulz əraʊnd ðə kraʊn əv ðə tree.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: This process, called transpiration in plants, creates tension on the rope of water molecules,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: this* ˈprɔˌsɛs, kɔld transpiration* ɪn plænts, kriˈeɪts ˈtɛnʃən ɔn ðə roʊp əv ˈwɔtər molecules,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: stretching and lifting the entire column upwards, all the way from the roots to the leaves.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: stretching* ənd ˈlɪftɪŋ ðə ɪnˈtaɪər ˈkɑləm ˈəpwərdz, ɔl ðə weɪ frəm ðə ruts tɪ ðə leaves.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: This pull is so insanely strong that it can lift water over 100 meters,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: this* pʊl ɪz soʊ insanely* strɔŋ ðət ɪt kən lɪft ˈwɔtər ˈoʊvər 100 meters,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: which requires sucking forces equivalent to the pressure of
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: which* rikˈwaɪərz ˈsəkɪŋ ˈfɔrsɪz ɪkˈwɪvələnt tɪ ðə ˈprɛʃər of

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: dozens of atmospheres – as much as the crushing pressure hundreds of meters deep in the ocean.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: dozens* əv atmospheres* –* ɛz məʧ ɛz ðə ˈkrəʃɪŋ ˈprɛʃər ˈhənərdz əv ˈmitərz dip ɪn ðə ˈoʊʃən.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Nothing humans have ever built comes even close to this power.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: ˈnəθɪŋ ˈjumənz hæv ˈɛvər bɪlt kəmz ˈivɪn kloʊz tɪ ðɪs power.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Even our best machines can’t pull water higher than about 10 meters,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: even* ɑr bɛst məˈʃinz can’t* pʊl ˈwɔtər haɪər ðən əˈbaʊt 10 meters,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: because the negative pressure required to pull hard enough makes it boil.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: because* ðə ˈnɛgətɪv ˈprɛʃər rikˈwaɪərd tɪ pʊl hɑrd ɪˈnəf meɪks ɪt boil.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: But the water pipes of trees are so tiny and narrow, almost perfectly airtight that
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: but* ðə ˈwɔtər paɪps əv triz ər soʊ ˈtaɪni ənd ˈnɛroʊ, ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˈpərfəktli ˈɛrˌtaɪt that

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: despite the insane suction pressure inside a tree, water stays liquid and reaches the top.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: despite* ðə ˌɪnˈseɪn ˈsəkʃən ˈprɛʃər ˌɪnˈsaɪd ə tri, ˈwɔtər steɪz ˈlɪkwɪd ənd ˈriʧɪz ðə top.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: As the tree ages old xylem cells eventually stop working and fill up with resins and other protective substances.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: as* ðə tri ˈeɪʤɪz oʊld xylem* sɛlz ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli stɑp ˈwərkɪŋ ənd fɪl əp wɪθ ˈrɛzɪnz ənd ˈəðər prəˈtɛktɪv substances.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Slowly they turn into heartwood, a dense, chemically fortified core that is extremely resistant to decay.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: slowly* ðeɪ tərn ˈɪntu ˈhɑrtˌwʊd, ə dɛns, ˈkɛmɪkli ˈfɔrtəˌfaɪd kɔr ðət ɪz ɪkˈstrimli rɪˈzɪstənt tɪ decay.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: The core of a mighty tree. But water is only one half of the story.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: the* kɔr əv ə ˈmaɪti tri. bət ˈwɔtər ɪz ˈoʊnli wən hæf əv ðə story.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: The sugar produced in the sky needs to be transported to nourish cells down below.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: the* ˈʃʊgər prəˈdust ɪn ðə skaɪ nidz tɪ bi trænˈspɔrtəd tɪ nərɪʃ sɛlz daʊn below.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: And cells from the roots to the leaves need to coordinate and exchange information about damage and growth.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: and* sɛlz frəm ðə ruts tɪ ðə livz nid tɪ koʊˈɔrdəˌneɪt ənd ɪksˈʧeɪnʤ ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən əˈbaʊt ˈdæmɪʤ ənd growth.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: This is the job of the cambium stem cells that grow outwards: the phloem.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: this* ɪz ðə ʤɑb əv ðə cambium* stɛm sɛlz ðət groʊ ˈaʊtwərdz: ðə phloem.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: The Tiny Living Part of the Tree
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: the* ˈtaɪni ˈlɪvɪŋ pɑrt əv ðə tree

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: As the Cells of the phloem grow outwards they separate into three teams and make a brutal compromise.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: as* ðə sɛlz əv ðə phloem* groʊ ˈaʊtwərdz ðeɪ ˈsɛpərˌeɪt ˈɪntu θri timz ənd meɪk ə ˈbrutəl compromise.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: The first group are Sieve cells and their grim fate is to become a living transport system.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: the* fərst grup ər sɪv sɛlz ənd ðɛr grɪm feɪt ɪz tɪ bɪˈkəm ə ˈlɪvɪŋ ˈtrænspɔrt system.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Although “living” is not exactly right. As they mature they start to destroy themselves,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: although* ““living”*” ɪz nɑt ɪgˈzæktli raɪt. ɛz ðeɪ məˈʧʊr ðeɪ stɑrt tɪ dɪˈstrɔɪ ðɛmˈsɛlvz,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: digest their organelles and even their nuclei that houses their genetic code. At the same time they hollow themselves out,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: ˈdaɪʤɛst ðɛr ˌɔrgəˈnɛlz ənd ˈivɪn ðɛr ˈnukliˌaɪ ðət ˈhaʊsɪz ðɛr ʤəˈnɛtɪk koʊd. æt ðə seɪm taɪm ðeɪ ˈhɑloʊ ðɛmˈsɛlvz out,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: connecting to the sieve cells above and below them. This goes on until they are a sad shadow of living beings.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: connecting* tɪ ðə sɪv sɛlz əˈbəv ənd bɪˈloʊ ðɛm. ðɪs goʊz ɔn ənˈtɪl ðeɪ ər ə sæd ˈʃæˌdoʊ əv ˈlɪvɪŋ beings.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: A drooling, living tool without a brain or arms, unable to support themselves.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: a* ˈdrulɪŋ, ˈlɪvɪŋ tul wɪˈθaʊt ə breɪn ər ɑrmz, əˈneɪbəl tɪ səˈpɔrt themselves.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Only the second group, their companion cells, keeps them alive.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: only* ðə ˈsɛkənd grup, ðɛr kəmˈpænjən sɛlz, kips ðɛm alive.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: They connect with the crippled Sieve cells via tiny channels and start maintaining them.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: they* kəˈnɛkt wɪθ ðə ˈkrɪpəld sɪv sɛlz ˈviə ˈtaɪni ˈʧænəlz ənd stɑrt meɪnˈteɪnɪŋ them.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Sending over energy, instructions or repairing them if needed.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: sending* ˈoʊvər ˈɛnərʤi, ˌɪnˈstrəkʃənz ər rɪˈpɛrɪŋ ðɛm ɪf needed.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Running along the entire tree, these two teams form a tiny and very thin
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: running* əˈlɔŋ ðə ɪnˈtaɪər tri, ðiz tu timz fɔrm ə ˈtaɪni ənd ˈvɛri thin

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: layer of living sugar pipelines and signal cables that stretch throughout the entire body.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: layer* əv ˈlɪvɪŋ ˈʃʊgər ˈpaɪˌplaɪnz ənd ˈsɪgnəl ˈkeɪbəlz ðət strɛʧ θruaʊt ðə ɪnˈtaɪər body.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Providing food and information wherever it is needed in the tree. The third team are Parenchyma cells, the silent
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: providing* fud ənd ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən wɛˈrɛvər ɪt ɪz ˈnidɪd ɪn ðə tri. ðə θərd tim ər parenchyma* sɛlz, ðə silent

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: workers of the tree that are carrying out essential labor in the background. Some are like mini pantries that store nutrients,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: workers* əv ðə tri ðət ər ˈkɛriɪŋ aʊt ɛˈsɛnʃəl ˈleɪbər ɪn ðə ˈbækˌgraʊnd. səm ər laɪk ˈmɪni pantries* ðət stɔr nutrients,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: sugars or water that the tree uses to survive the winter, when it is unable to produce food.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: sugars* ər ˈwɔtər ðət ðə tri ˈjuzɪz tɪ sərˈvaɪv ðə ˈwɪntər, wɪn ɪt ɪz əˈneɪbəl tɪ ˈproʊdus food.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Others are like mini healers that can repair damage, while some go onto the
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: others* ər laɪk ˈmɪni ˈhilərz ðət kən rɪˈpɛr ˈdæmɪʤ, waɪl səm goʊ ˈɔntu the

ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: offensive* ənd kriˈeɪt ˈtɑksənz ənd ˈænˌti ˈfəŋgəl baɪoʊ ˈwɛpənz tɪ kɪl intruders.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: On top of the phloem sits another layer of stem cells. They are producing a second conveyor belt of death, moving outside.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: on* tɔp əv ðə phloem* sɪts əˈnəðər leɪər əv stɛm sɛlz. ðeɪ ər prəˈdusɪŋ ə ˈsɛkənd kənˈveɪər bɛlt əv dɛθ, ˈmuvɪŋ outside.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Special cells grow from this layer and as they mature, just like the xylem cells in the center,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: special* sɛlz groʊ frəm ðɪs leɪər ənd ɛz ðeɪ məˈʧʊr, ʤɪst laɪk ðə xylem* sɛlz ɪn ðə ˈsɛnər,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: they kill themselves for the team, turning into a hard guard wall – the bark.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: ðeɪ kɪl ðɛmˈsɛlvz fər ðə tim, ˈtərnɪŋ ˈɪntu ə hɑrd gɑrd wɔl –* ðə bark.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Just like your skin, it protects the tiny living layer from damage, parasites and invaders.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: just* laɪk jʊr skɪn, ɪt prəˈtɛkts ðə ˈtaɪni ˈlɪvɪŋ leɪər frəm ˈdæmɪʤ, ˈpɛrəˌsaɪts ənd invaders.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: So what is a tree? The living part of the stem is really just this extremely thin and tiny layer,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: so* wət ɪz ə tri? ðə ˈlɪvɪŋ pɑrt əv ðə stɛm ɪz ˈrɪli ʤɪst ðɪs ɪkˈstrimli θɪn ənd ˈtaɪni layer,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: a few millimeters thick, sitting on a thick mountain of cell corpses, surrounded by another layer of cell corpses.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: a* fju ˈmɪləˌmitərz θɪk, ˈsɪtɪŋ ɔn ə θɪk ˈmaʊntən əv sɛl ˈkɔrpsɪz, sərˈaʊndɪd baɪ əˈnəðər leɪər əv sɛl corpses.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: The vast majority of the biomass of a tree is dead.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: the* væst məˈʤɔrəti əv ðə ˈbaɪəmæs əv ə tri ɪz dead.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: This is also why you really should not damage the bark of trees because while
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: this* ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ waɪ ju ˈrɪli ʃʊd nɑt ˈdæmɪʤ ðə bɑrk əv triz bɪˈkəz while

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: it seems you are only doing a little damage, you are actually killing the living part of the tree.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: it* simz ju ər ˈoʊnli duɪŋ ə ˈlɪtəl ˈdæmɪʤ, ju ər ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈkɪlɪŋ ðə ˈlɪvɪŋ pɑrt əv ðə tree.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: But unless a tree is stopped by droughts, diseases, storms or a human axe,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: but* ənˈlɛs ə tri ɪz stɑpt baɪ draʊts, dɪˈzizɪz, stɔrmz ər ə ˈjumən axe,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: this system of being mostly dead kind of makes trees potentially immortal.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: this* ˈsɪstəm əv biɪŋ ˈmoʊstli dɛd kaɪnd əv meɪks triz pəˈtɛnʃəli immortal.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: They don’t age like we do, in principle they could grow this way nearly forever.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: they* don’t* eɪʤ laɪk wi du, ɪn ˈprɪnsəpəl ðeɪ kʊd groʊ ðɪs weɪ ˈnɪrli forever.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Which is why we still have trees around that were born when the Egyptians started building their first pyramid, 5000 years ago.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: which* ɪz waɪ wi stɪl hæv triz əraʊnd ðət wər bɔrn wɪn ðə ɪˈʤɪpʃənz ˈstɑrtɪd ˈbɪldɪŋ ðɛr fərst ˈpɪrəmɪd, 5000 jɪrz ago.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Really what kills a tree is the world around it.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: really* wət kɪlz ə tri ɪz ðə wərld əraʊnd it.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Trees are not a real biological category but one of the most successful ideas life has ever had,
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: trees* ər nɑt ə ril ˌbaɪəˈlɑʤɪkəl ˈkætəˌgɔri bət wən əv ðə moʊst səkˈsɛsfəl aɪˈdiəz laɪf həz ˈɛvər had,

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: and many different species developed on their own. These plants won the battle for the sky, solved every challenge that had kept
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: and* ˈmɛni ˈdɪfərənt ˈspiʃiz dɪˈvɛləpt ɔn ðɛr oʊn. ðiz plænts wən ðə ˈbætəl fər ðə skaɪ, sɑlvd ˈɛvəri ˈʧælənʤ ðət hæd kept

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: plants small and fragile and they took over the planet in a few million years.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: plants* smɔl ənd ˈfræʤəl ənd ðeɪ tʊk ˈoʊvər ðə ˈplænət ɪn ə fju ˈmɪljən jɪrz.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Even today three trillion of them cast their majestic shadows.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: ˈivɪn təˈdeɪ θri ˈtrɪljən əv ðɛm kæst ðɛr məˈʤɛstɪk shadows.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: But we’ve barely covered half of it: on top and below there are the crown
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: but* we’ve* ˈbɛrli ˈkəvərd hæf əv ɪt: ɔn tɔp ənd bɪˈloʊ ðɛr ər ðə crown

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: and the root system – one building the tree from air, another mining
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: and* ðə rut ˈsɪstəm –* wən ˈbɪldɪŋ ðə tri frəm ɛr, əˈnəðər mining

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: minerals with acid and involved in complex communications with fungi and other trees.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: minerals* wɪθ ˈæsəd ənd ˌɪnˈvɑlvd ɪn ˈkɑmplɛks kəmˌjunəˈkeɪʃənz wɪθ ˈfənʤaɪ ənd ˈəðər trees.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: There’s so much more to talk about, so we’re already working on the next part - stay tuned!
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: there’s* soʊ məʧ mɔr tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt, soʊ we’re* ɔˈrɛdi ˈwərkɪŋ ɔn ðə nɛkst pɑrt  steɪ tuned!

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Trees are truly incredible – but many of our most beautiful forests are
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: trees* ər ˈtruli ˌɪnˈkrɛdəbəl –* bət ˈmɛni əv ɑr moʊst ˈbjutəfəl ˈfɔrəsts are

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: disappearing fast and we’re running out of time to protect what’s left. That’s why we have partnered up with Planet Wild.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: disappearing* fæst ənd we’re* ˈrənɪŋ aʊt əv taɪm tɪ prəˈtɛkt what’s* lɛft. that’s* waɪ wi hæv partnered* əp wɪθ ˈplænət wild.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Planet Wild is a community-based nature protection organization that supports conservation projects around the globe.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: planet* waɪld ɪz ə community-based* ˈneɪʧər prəˈtɛkʃən ˌɔrgənəˈzeɪʃən ðət səˈpɔrts ˌkɑnsərˈveɪʃən ˈprɑʤɛkts əraʊnd ðə globe.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Every month, their community of over 15,000 members funds a new mission to restore nature.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: every* mənθ, ðɛr kəmˈjunɪti əv ˈoʊvər 15,000* ˈmɛmbərz fəndz ə nu ˈmɪʃən tɪ rɪˈstɔr nature.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: We at kurzgesagt really value transparency. That’s why we love that Planet Wild documents all their missions in video
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: we* æt kurzgesagt* ˈrɪli ˈvælju trænˈspɛrənsi. that’s* waɪ wi ləv ðət ˈplænət waɪld ˈdɑkjəmənts ɔl ðɛr ˈmɪʃənz ɪn video

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: reports right here on YouTube so you can see what your contribution helped achieve.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: reports* raɪt hir ɔn ˈjuˌtub soʊ ju kən si wət jʊr ˌkɑntrəˈbjuʃən hɛlpt achieve.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Take their underground forest mission in Tanzania: While you think this desert is dead, there’s actually life still here.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: take* ðɛr ˈəndərˌgraʊnd ˈfɔrɪst ˈmɪʃən ɪn ˌtænˈzeɪnjə: waɪl ju θɪŋk ðɪs ˈdɛzərt ɪz dɛd, there’s* ˈæˌkʧuəli laɪf stɪl here.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Beneath the surface, underground forests are just waiting to grow. And with an ancient technique, Planet Wild helped make that happen.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: beneath* ðə ˈsərfəs, ˈəndərˌgraʊnd ˈfɔrəsts ər ʤɪst ˈweɪtɪŋ tɪ groʊ. ənd wɪθ ən ˈeɪnʧənt tɛkˈnik, ˈplænət waɪld hɛlpt meɪk ðət happen.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: If you would like to help our planet’s ecosystems, consider joining Planet Wild.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: if* ju wʊd laɪk tɪ hɛlp ɑr planet’s* ˈikoʊˌsɪstəmz, kənˈsɪdər ˈʤɔɪnɪŋ ˈplænət wild.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: You can give any amount that feels right to you – every dollar counts.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: you* kən gɪv ˈɛni əˈmaʊnt ðət filz raɪt tɪ ju –* ˈɛvəri ˈdɔlər kaʊnts.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: The first 100 people to sign up using our code KURZGESAGT11 will get their first month paid for by us!
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: ðə fərst 100 ˈpipəl tɪ saɪn əp ˈjuzɪŋ ɑr koʊd kurzgesagt11* wɪl gɪt ðɛr fərst mənθ peɪd fər baɪ us!

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Just scan the QR code or click the link in the description.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: just* skæn ðə qr* koʊd ər klɪk ðə lɪŋk ɪn ðə description.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: Make a tree friend, and join the Planet Wild community – today.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: make* ə tri frɛnd, ənd ʤɔɪn ðə ˈplænət waɪld kəmˈjunɪti –* today.

ZSch_NgZpQs.text: If you want to see them in action first, check out their underground forest mission here.
ZSch_NgZpQs.ipa: if* ju wɔnt tɪ si ðɛm ɪn ˈækʃən fərst, ʧɛk aʊt ðɛr ˈəndərˌgraʊnd ˈfɔrɪst ˈmɪʃən hir.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Very tasteful art!
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: ˈvɛri ˈteɪstfəl art!

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Oh, you hate it? You are not alone!
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: oh*, ju heɪt ɪt? ju ər nɑt alone!

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: One in four people with a tattoo regret it, 25 million people in the US.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: one* ɪn fɔr ˈpipəl wɪθ ə ˌtæˈtu rɪˈgrɛt ɪt, 25 ˈmɪljən ˈpipəl ɪn ðə us.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Getting ink carved into your body permanently has exploded in popularity in the last few decades, especially among Millennials.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: getting* ɪŋk kɑrvd ˈɪntu jʊr ˈbɑdi ˈpərmɑˌnɛnˌtli həz ɪkˈsploʊdɪd ɪn ˌpɑpjəˈlɛrəti ɪn ðə læst fju ˈdɛkeɪdz, əˈspɛʃəli əˈməŋ millennials.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Often their tattoos were about a current cultural thing, a moment in their life, following a trend,
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: often* ðɛr ˌtæˈtuz wər əˈbaʊt ə ˈkɑrənt ˈkəlʧərəl θɪŋ, ə ˈmoʊmənt ɪn ðɛr laɪf, ˈfɑloʊɪŋ ə trend,

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: expressing individuality or about feeling like being part of something greater.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: expressing* ˌɪnˌdɪvɪʤuˈælɪti ər əˈbaʊt ˈfilɪŋ laɪk biɪŋ pɑrt əv ˈsəmθɪŋ greater.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: This is all fine and good but as people age they often stop
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: this* ɪz ɔl faɪn ənd gʊd bət ɛz ˈpipəl eɪʤ ðeɪ ˈɔfən stop

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: identifying with what their younger versions wanted on their skin forever.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: identifying* wɪθ wət ðɛr ˈjəŋgər ˈvərʒənz ˈwɔntɪd ɔn ðɛr skɪn forever.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Or sometimes their tattoos simply just look ugly or have aged badly.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: or* ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ðɛr ˌtæˈtuz ˈsɪmpli ʤɪst lʊk ˈəgli ər hæv ˈeɪʤɪd badly.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: And so today laser tattoo removal is booming, being as normalized as dentist appointments.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: and* soʊ təˈdeɪ ˈleɪzər ˌtæˈtu rɪˈmuvəl ɪz ˈbumɪŋ, biɪŋ ɛz ˈnɔrməˌlaɪzd ɛz ˈdɛntɪst appointments.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Which is wild since lasering a tattoo away is one of the most metal things you can do to your body.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: which* ɪz waɪld sɪns lasering* ə ˌtæˈtu əˈweɪ ɪz wən əv ðə moʊst ˈmɛtəl θɪŋz ju kən du tɪ jʊr body.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: A high tech procedure that is extremely painful and makes your skin explode from the inside. How does it work?
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: a* haɪ tɛk prəˈsiʤər ðət ɪz ɪkˈstrimli ˈpeɪnfəl ənd meɪks jʊr skɪn ɪkˈsploʊd frəm ðə ˌɪnˈsaɪd. haʊ dɪz ɪt work?

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: We explained what a tattoo is in detail before, but in a nutshell:
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: we* ɪkˈspleɪnd wət ə ˌtæˈtu ɪz ɪn ˈditeɪl ˌbiˈfɔr, bət ɪn ə nutshell:

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: From the perspective of your cells your tattoo is a mountain range of colourful boulders, from small to building-sized.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: from* ðə pərˈspɛktɪv əv jʊr sɛlz jʊr ˌtæˈtu ɪz ə ˈmaʊntən reɪnʤ əv colourful* ˈboʊldərz, frəm smɔl tɪ building-sized.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Injected deep into your dermis by violently ripping huge wounds with gigantic needles.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: injected* dip ˈɪntu jʊr ˈdərməs baɪ ˈvaɪələntli ˈrɪpɪŋ juʤ wundz wɪθ ʤaɪˈgænɪk ˈnidəlz.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Your body is trying hard to destroy the tattoo, but the ink particles are
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: jʊr ˈbɑdi ɪz traɪɪŋ hɑrd tɪ dɪˈstrɔɪ ðə ˌtæˈtu, bət ðə ɪŋk ˈpɑrtɪkəlz are

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: way too large to be transported away and your immune cells can’t eat them. So they do the next best thing:
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: way* tu lɑrʤ tɪ bi trænˈspɔrtəd əˈweɪ ənd jʊr ˌɪmˈjun sɛlz can’t* it ðɛm. soʊ ðeɪ du ðə nɛkst bɛst thing:

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Keeping the ink trapped in a prison of flesh. This is why tattoos remain forever, millions of your cells give their
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: keeping* ðə ɪŋk træpt ɪn ə ˈprɪzən əv flɛʃ. ðɪs ɪz waɪ ˌtæˈtuz rɪˈmeɪn fərˈɛvər, ˈmɪljənz əv jʊr sɛlz gɪv their

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: lives to keep the ink in place – when they die new ones take their place.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: lives* tɪ kip ðə ɪŋk ɪn pleɪs –* wɪn ðeɪ daɪ nu wənz teɪk ðɛr place.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: In principle removing a tattoo is very easy: Just destroy the ink particles.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: in* ˈprɪnsəpəl riˈmuvɪŋ ə ˌtæˈtu ɪz ˈvɛri ˈizi: ʤɪst dɪˈstrɔɪ ðə ɪŋk particles.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: The hard part is to do that without destroying you.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: the* hɑrd pɑrt ɪz tɪ du ðət wɪˈθaʊt dɪˈstrɔɪɪŋ you.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Amazingly the best way is still to blast you with a deadly laser.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: amazingly* ðə bɛst weɪ ɪz stɪl tɪ blæst ju wɪθ ə ˈdɛdli laser.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Lasers, UV light, wifi or x-rays are all the same thing: Electromagnetic radiation,
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: lasers*, uv* laɪt, ˈwiˌfi ər ˈɛksˌreɪz ər ɔl ðə seɪm θɪŋ: ˌɪˌlɛktroʊmægˈnɛtɪk radiation,

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: photons that travel through space at the speed of light in the form of a wave.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: photons* ðət ˈtrævəl θru speɪs æt ðə spid əv laɪt ɪn ðə fɔrm əv ə wave.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: The more energetic they are, the higher their frequency and the more punch they carry.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: the* mɔr ˌɛnərˈʤɛtɪk ðeɪ ər, ðə haɪər ðɛr ˈfrikwənsi ənd ðə mɔr pənʧ ðeɪ carry.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: Imagine your skin as jelly and your tattoo as crumbs of toast.
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: imagine* jʊr skɪn ɛz ˈʤɛli ənd jʊr ˌtæˈtu ɛz krəmz əv toast.

H4KMQuyxH_Y.text: If you shine a flashlight against it, most light passes right through
H4KMQuyxH_Y.ipa: if* ju ʃaɪn ə ˈflæʃˌlaɪt əˈgɛnst ɪt, moʊst laɪt ˈpæsɪz raɪt through

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Fat is your most dangerous organ. Yes, organ.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: fæt ɪz jʊr moʊst ˈdeɪnʤərəs ˈɔrgən. jɛs, organ.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: While it is often falsely described as a mere expression of laziness or gluttony,
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: while* ɪt ɪz ˈɔfən ˈfɔlsli dɪˈskraɪbd ɛz ə mɪr ɪkˈsprɛʃən əv ˈleɪzinəs ər gluttony,

sOsqXKr4l30.text: fat is essential for health, controlling and guiding crucial processes in your body.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: fat* ɪz ɛˈsɛnʃəl fər hɛlθ, kənˈtroʊlɪŋ ənd ˈgaɪdɪŋ ˈkruʃəl ˈprɑsɛsəz ɪn jʊr body.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: But if you have too much fat, it starts to disrupt your metabolism becoming
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: but* ɪf ju hæv tu məʧ fæt, ɪt stɑrts tɪ dɪsˈrəpt jʊr məˈtæbəˌlɪzəm becoming

sOsqXKr4l30.text: one of the most deadly things that can happen to your body.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: one* əv ðə moʊst ˈdɛdli θɪŋz ðət kən ˈhæpən tɪ jʊr body.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Today more people are obese than starving, which is a huge victory. There are all sorts of ideas about the cause
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: today* mɔr ˈpipəl ər əˈbis ðən ˈstɑrvɪŋ, wɪʧ ɪz ə juʤ ˈvɪktəri. ðɛr ər ɔl sɔrts əv aɪˈdiəz əˈbaʊt ðə cause

sOsqXKr4l30.text: of the obesity epidemic but it really comes down to one thing:
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: of* ðə əˈbisəti ˌɛpɪˈdɛmɪk bət ɪt ˈrɪli kəmz daʊn tɪ wən thing:

sOsqXKr4l30.text: For millions of years, humans had to expend a lot of effort to get food and often faced hunger.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: for* ˈmɪljənz əv jɪrz, ˈjumənz hæd tɪ ɪkˈspɛnd ə lɔt əv ˈɛfərt tɪ gɪt fud ənd ˈɔfən feɪst hunger.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Our bodies evolved to hold on to every bit of energy.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: our* ˈbɑdiz ɪˈvɑlvd tɪ hoʊld ɔn tɪ ˈɛvəri bɪt əv energy.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: And then suddenly we had an overabundance of food we didn't need to move much for.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: and* ðɛn ˈsədənli wi hæd ən ˈoʊvərəˈbəndəns əv fud wi ˈdɪdənt nid tɪ muv məʧ for.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: We made food hyperpalatable and ultra processed, with loads of unhealthy fats, salt and sugar.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: we* meɪd fud hyperpalatable* ənd ˈəltrə ˈprɑsɛst, wɪθ loʊdz əv ənˈhɛlθi fæts, sɔlt ənd sugar.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Our brains love this, so our food is extremely hard to resist, to the point of being addictive.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: our* breɪnz ləv ðɪs, soʊ ɑr fud ɪz ɪkˈstrimli hɑrd tɪ rɪˈzɪst, tɪ ðə pɔɪnt əv biɪŋ addictive.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: It is also extremely convenient, cheap and you get a lot of energy per volume – so it’s not very satiating.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: it* ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ ɪkˈstrimli kənˈvinjənt, ʧip ənd ju gɪt ə lɔt əv ˈɛnərʤi pər ˈvɑljum –* soʊ it’s* nɑt ˈvɛri satiating.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: This leads to most of us regularly overeating without realising it.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: this* lidz tɪ moʊst əv ˈjuˈɛs ˈrɛgjələrli ˈoʊvərˈitɪŋ wɪˈθaʊt realising* it.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: And food is aggressively marketed, especially to children.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: and* fud ɪz əˈgrɛsɪvli ˈmɑrkətɪd, əˈspɛʃəli tɪ ˈʧɪldrən.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Fat comes with a lot of shame and blame which is pretty unfair,
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: fæt kəmz wɪθ ə lɔt əv ʃeɪm ənd bleɪm wɪʧ ɪz ˈprɪti unfair,

sOsqXKr4l30.text: in a world full of things that make you feel nice and are available within minutes.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: in* ə wərld fʊl əv θɪŋz ðət meɪk ju fil nis ənd ər əˈveɪləbəl wɪˈθɪn minutes.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Body fat is also often unfairly villainized – but it is crucially important for your health
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: body* fæt ɪz ˈɔlsoʊ ˈɔfən ənˈfɛrli villainized* –* bət ɪt ɪz ˈkruʃəli ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt fər jʊr health

sOsqXKr4l30.text: and if you don't have enough, you can suffer problems from infertility to a
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: and* ɪf ju doʊnt hæv ɪˈnəf, ju kən ˈsəfər ˈprɑbləmz frəm ˌɪnfərˈtɪlɪˌti tɪ a

sOsqXKr4l30.text: weak immune system, fatigue, mental health issues and osteoporosis.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: weak* ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm, fəˈtig, ˈmɛntəl hɛlθ ˈɪʃuz ənd osteoporosis.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Still none of this changes how bad excess fat is for us.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: still* nən əv ðɪs ˈʧeɪnʤɪz haʊ bæd ˈɛkˌsɛs fæt ɪz fər us.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: So how does our fat organ become so destructive and can we help ourselves?
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: so* haʊ dɪz ɑr fæt ˈɔrgən bɪˈkəm soʊ dɪˈstrəktɪv ənd kən wi hɛlp ourselves?

sOsqXKr4l30.text: What Is Fat?
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: what* ɪz fat?

sOsqXKr4l30.text: If you consume more energy than you burn you store it as triglycerides, an organic battery bustling with energy.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: if* ju kənˈsum mɔr ˈɛnərʤi ðən ju bərn ju stɔr ɪt ɛz traɪˈglɪsərˌaɪdz, ən ɔrˈgænɪk ˈbætəri ˈbəsəlɪŋ wɪθ energy.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Collected in a large drop of fat inside a white fat cell.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: collected* ɪn ə lɑrʤ drɔp əv fæt ˌɪnˈsaɪd ə waɪt fæt cell.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Gain weight and white fat cells expand with fat, lose weight and they shrink.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: gain* weɪt ənd waɪt fæt sɛlz ɪkˈspænd wɪθ fæt, luz weɪt ənd ðeɪ shrink.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Only in the last few decades have we learned fats' most important job:
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: only* ɪn ðə læst fju ˈdɛkeɪdz hæv wi ˈlərnɪd fæts' moʊst ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt job:

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Fat is an endocrine organ, part of the system that makes and regulates your hormones:
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: fat* ɪz ən ˈɛndoʊˌkraɪn ˈɔrgən, pɑrt əv ðə ˈsɪstəm ðət meɪks ənd ˈrɛgjəˌleɪts jʊr hormones:

sOsqXKr4l30.text: chemical signals for your brain, liver, muscles,
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: chemical* ˈsɪgnəlz fər jʊr breɪn, ˈlɪvər, muscles,

sOsqXKr4l30.text: digestive tract and immune system, making them work correctly together.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: digestive* trækt ənd ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm, ˈmeɪkɪŋ ðɛm wərk kərˈɛktli təˈgɛðər.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Unfortunately if you become overweight and obese,
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: ənˈfɔrʧənətli ɪf ju bɪˈkəm ˌoʊvərˈweɪt ənd obese,

sOsqXKr4l30.text: your fat organ and the many hormones it releases turn insane.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: your* fæt ˈɔrgən ənd ðə ˈmɛni ˈhɔrˌmoʊnz ɪt rɪˈlisɪz tərn insane.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: In adults fat comes in two types of white fat depots – most of it is subcutaneous fat, the
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: in* ˈædəlts fæt kəmz ɪn tu taɪps əv waɪt fæt ˈdipoʊz –* moʊst əv ɪt ɪz səbˌkjuˈteɪniəs fæt, the

sOsqXKr4l30.text: gooey soft stuff under your skin that insulates against the cold and serves as energy storage.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: gooey* sɔft stəf ˈəndər jʊr skɪn ðət insulates* əˈgɛnst ðə koʊld ənd sərvz ɛz ˈɛnərʤi storage.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: The other one is visceral fat nestled between your organs providing a soft cushion for your sensitive insides.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: the* ˈəðər wən ɪz ˈvɪsərəl fæt ˈnɛsəld bɪtˈwin jʊr ˈɔrgənz prəˈvaɪdɪŋ ə sɔft ˈkʊʃən fər jʊr ˈsɛnsɪtɪv insides.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: But it also happens to be more dangerous.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: but* ɪt ˈɔlsoʊ ˈhæpənz tɪ bi mɔr dangerous.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: These fat cells are super sensitive to stress hormones like cortisol or adrenaline.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: these* fæt sɛlz ər ˈsupər ˈsɛnsɪtɪv tɪ strɛs ˈhɔrˌmoʊnz laɪk ˈkɔrtɪˌsɔl ər adrenaline.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: When they pick up a surge of stress, they release fatty acids directly into
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: when* ðeɪ pɪk əp ə sərʤ əv strɛs, ðeɪ riˈlis ˈfæti ˈæsədz dɪˈrɛkli into

sOsqXKr4l30.text: your blood and are picked up by your liver, as a rapid source of energy.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: your* bləd ənd ər pɪkt əp baɪ jʊr ˈlɪvər, ɛz ə ˈræpɪd sɔrs əv energy.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: On top of that your visceral fat is very metabolically active, in a constant hormonal dialog with the rest of your body.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: on* tɔp əv ðət jʊr ˈvɪsərəl fæt ɪz ˈvɛri metabolically* ˈæktɪv, ɪn ə ˈkɑnstənt ˈhɔrˌmoʊnəl ˈdaɪəlɔg wɪθ ðə rɛst əv jʊr body.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: This is also why the health of two people with the same weight and amount of fat can be totally different.

sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: if* ju ər pɛr ʃeɪpt ənd jʊr fæt ɪz ˈmoʊstli ɪn jʊr hɪps ər lɪmz you

sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: bite* baɪ bite

sOsqXKr4l30.text: As you gain unhealthy weight, excess visceral fat triggers a cascade of negative changes all at once.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Fat cells bloat up to their limit until they outgrow their blood supply and get starved of oxygen.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: They become critically stressed or even die. This is bad. If you are overweight or obese your fat is basically a wounded
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: they* bɪˈkəm ˈkrɪtɪkəli strɛst ər ˈivɪn daɪ. ðɪs ɪz bæd. ɪf ju ər ˌoʊvərˈweɪt ər əˈbis jʊr fæt ɪz ˈbeɪsɪkli ə wounded
