sOsqXKr4l30.text: even more triggered by the extra stress hormones, makes your blood more fatty.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: even* mɔr ˈtrɪgərd baɪ ðə ˈɛkstrə strɛs ˈhɔrˌmoʊnz, meɪks jʊr bləd mɔr fatty.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: This overfeeds your organs like your liver or your muscles, who can’t keep up anymore and begin to take damage.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: So they stay and call for more help.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: so* ðeɪ steɪ ənd kɔl fər mɔr help.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: In a lean person’s fat, immune cells make up about 5% of cells – in an obese person's fat, up to 40%!
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: in* ə lin person’s* fæt, ˌɪmˈjun sɛlz meɪk əp əˈbaʊt 5 əv sɛlz –* ɪn ən əˈbis ˈpərsənz fæt, əp tɪ 40%!

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Active immune cells cause inflammation, bloating up your tissue and releasing even more alarm signals.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: But if it becomes chronic, it’s like your entire body is under friendly fire.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: The inflammation molecules and fatty acids rip countless tiny wounds into
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: the* ˌɪnfləˈmeɪʃən ˈmɑləˌkjulz ənd ˈfæti ˈæsədz rɪp ˈkaʊntləs ˈtaɪni wundz into

sOsqXKr4l30.text: the insides of your blood vessels, which leads to plaques that desperately try to close them.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: to work harder and your risk of heart attacks or strokes increases massively.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: to* wərk ˈhɑrdər ənd jʊr rɪsk əv hɑrt əˈtæks ər stroʊks ˌɪnˈkrisɪz massively.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: To make things worse your fat organ’s hormone production gets out of whack. Like leptin, the satiety hormone.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: to* meɪk θɪŋz wərs jʊr fæt organ’s* ˈhɔrˌmoʊn pərˈdəkʃən gɪts aʊt əv wæk. laɪk ˈlɛptɪn, ðə satiety* hormone.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: With a healthy amount of fat, leptin tells your brain when you
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: with* ə ˈhɛlθi əˈmaʊnt əv fæt, ˈlɛptɪn tɛlz jʊr breɪn wɪn you

sOsqXKr4l30.text: have enough energy storage, can eat less and spend more energy.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: This breaks your internal food thermostat.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: this* breɪks jʊr ˌɪnˈtərnəl fud thermostat.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Which is one of the reasons why many people who are overweight feel intense hunger.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: which* ɪz wən əv ðə ˈrizənz waɪ ˈmɛni ˈpipəl hu ər ˌoʊvərˈweɪt fil ˌɪnˈtɛns hunger.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Their fat is screaming at their brain that they have enough but the brain is not hearing the message anymore.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Your sex hormones also get out of whack, testosterone is lowered while estrogen is over produced.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: In women this increases the risk of breast cancer significantly.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Most people are not aware how much the risk of cancer rises with excess fat.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: And to top this off, obese cancer patients have much worse outcomes, succumbing to the disease more often and sooner.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: and* tɪ tɔp ðɪs ɔf, əˈbis ˈkænsər ˈpeɪʃənz hæv məʧ wərs ˈaʊtˌkəmz, səˈkəmɪŋ tɪ ðə dɪˈziz mɔr ˈɔfən ənd sooner.

sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: wɪn ðə ˈhɔrˌmoʊn wərld explodes

sOsqXKr4l30.text: It is your body’s way of screaming “dinner time”. Disastrously bombarded by the stress from your excess fat,

sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: cells* ɔl ˈoʊvər jʊr ˈbɑdi bɪˈkəm ˈɪnsələn rɪˈzɪstənt –* wərs æt ˈitɪŋ ˈgluˌkoʊs ənd ˈteɪkɪŋ əp energy.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Your body tries to compensate by pumping out more insulin, screaming louder.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: your* ˈbɑdi traɪz tɪ ˈkɑmpənˌseɪt baɪ ˈpəmpɪŋ aʊt mɔr ˈɪnsələn, ˈskrimɪŋ louder.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: This can go on silently for many years and progress to prediabetes – with no
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: this* kən goʊ ɔn ˈsaɪləntli fər ˈmɛni jɪrz ənd ˈprɑˌgrɛs tɪ prediabetes* –* wɪθ no

sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: stop* ˈfəŋkʃənɪŋ ˈprɑpərli ənd ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli gɪv in.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: now saturated with glucose – yet you are starving and feel exhausted and unwell.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: now* ˈsæʧərˌeɪtɪd wɪθ ˈgluˌkoʊs –* jɛt ju ər ˈstɑrvɪŋ ənd fil ɪgˈzɔstɪd ənd unwell.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Imagine this as trillions of tiny, sharp shards floating through you,
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: imagine* ðɪs ɛz ˈtrɪljənz əv ˈtaɪni, ʃɑrp ʃɑrdz ˈfloʊtɪŋ θru you,

sOsqXKr4l30.text: At this point your body is so chronically inflamed that almost all organ systems are affected.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: at* ðɪs pɔɪnt jʊr ˈbɑdi ɪz soʊ ˈkrɑnɪkəli ɪnˈfleɪmd ðət ˈɔlˌmoʊst ɔl ˈɔrgən ˈsɪstəmz ər əˈfɛktɪd.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: The kidneys are overwhelmed, making you pee way more, your vision gets blurry, your
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: ðə ˈkɪdniz ər ˌoʊvərˈwɛlmd, ˈmeɪkɪŋ ju pi weɪ mɔr, jʊr ˈvɪʒən gɪts ˈbləri, your

sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: you* meɪ ɪkˈspɪriəns ˈʃɔrtnəs əv brɛθ, ʧɛst dɪˈskəmfərt, erectile* dɪsˈfəŋkʃən and

sOsqXKr4l30.text: high blood pressure, problems with your memory, focus, mood and even depression.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: high* bləd ˈprɛʃər, ˈprɑbləmz wɪθ jʊr ˈmɛməri, ˈfoʊkɪs, mud ənd ˈivɪn depression.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Your risk of developing just about every possible deadly disease goes through the roof.

sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: on* ˈævərɪʤ taɪp 2 ˌdaɪəˈbitiz ʃeɪvz 10 jɪrz ɔf jʊr laɪf ənd reduces

sOsqXKr4l30.text: There is no nice way to put this: Excessive fat strains nearly every organ system in your body,
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: there* ɪz noʊ nis weɪ tɪ pʊt ðɪs: ɪkˈsɛsɪv fæt streɪnz ˈnɪrli ˈɛvəri ˈɔrgən ˈsɪstəm ɪn jʊr body,

sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: ages* ju məʧ kˈwɪkər ənd ˈɔfən lidz tɪ multisystem* ˈdæmɪʤ ənd dysfunction.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: And yet, fat is still mostly discussed through the lens of aesthetics first and health second.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: Once your fat cells contract again they stop being stressed and your immune system calms down.
sOsqXKr4l30.ipa: once* jʊr fæt sɛlz ˈkɑnˌtrækt əˈgɛn ðeɪ stɑp biɪŋ strɛst ənd jʊr ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm kɑmz down.

sOsqXKr4l30.text: The excess blood fat and sugar drops to normal levels and your body recovers.

Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: so*, aɪv gɑt ə ˈteɪbəl, fɔr chairs,

Te-UyphkKRk.text: and some drawers in my dining room. This is the kitchen.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: and* səm drɔrz ɪn maɪ ˈdaɪnɪŋ rum. ðɪs ɪz ðə ˈkɪʧən.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: What do I need here? You need a fridge. A fridge.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: what* du aɪ nid hir? ju nid ə frɪʤ. ə fridge.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: You need some cupboards. Cupboards. You need an oven. An oven.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: you* nid səm ˈkəbərdz. ˈkəbərdz. ju nid ən ˈəvən. ən oven.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: And you need a microwave. A microwave.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: and* ju nid ə ˈmaɪkrəˌweɪv. ə microwave.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: So, I've got a fridge,
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: so*, aɪv gɑt ə fridge,

Te-UyphkKRk.text: some cupboards, an oven, and a microwave in my kitchen.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: some* ˈkəbərdz, ən ˈəvən, ənd ə ˈmaɪkrəˌweɪv ɪn maɪ kitchen.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: You can see all the episodes of Easy English Conversations in this playlist.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: you* kən si ɔl ðə ˈɛpɪˌsoʊdz əv ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz ɪn ðɪs playlist.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: Now back to the episode.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: naʊ bæk tɪ ðə episode.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: Can you remember the rooms in Georgie's house? What's this room?
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: can* ju rɪˈmɛmbər ðə rumz ɪn georgie's* haʊs? wəts ðɪs room?

Te-UyphkKRk.text: This is the living room. That's right! How about this room?
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: this* ɪz ðə ˈlɪvɪŋ rum. ðæts raɪt! haʊ əˈbaʊt ðɪs room?

Te-UyphkKRk.text: This is the bedroom. Correct! What's this room?
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: this* ɪz ðə ˈbɛˌdrum. kərˈɛkt! wəts ðɪs room?

Te-UyphkKRk.text: This is the dining room. That's right. And what's this room?
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: this* ɪz ðə ˈdaɪnɪŋ rum. ðæts raɪt. ənd wəts ðɪs room?

Te-UyphkKRk.text: This is the kitchen. What has Georgie got in her living room?
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: this* ɪz ðə ˈkɪʧən. wət həz ˈʤɔrʤi gɑt ɪn hər ˈlɪvɪŋ rum?

Te-UyphkKRk.text: She's got a sofa, a bookshelf,
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: she's* gɑt ə ˈsoʊfə, ə bookshelf,

Te-UyphkKRk.text: a TV and a rug. That's right.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: a* ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən ənd ə rəg. ðæts right.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: What's Georgie got in her bedroom?
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: what's* ˈʤɔrʤi gɑt ɪn hər bedroom?

Te-UyphkKRk.text: She's got a bed, a lamp,
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: she's* gɑt ə bɛd, ə læmp,

Te-UyphkKRk.text: a bedside table and a wardrobe. Correct!
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: ə ˈbɛdˌsaɪd ˈteɪbəl ənd ə ˈwɔrˌdroʊb. correct!

Te-UyphkKRk.text: What's Georgie got in her dining room?
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: what's* ˈʤɔrʤi gɑt ɪn hər ˈdaɪnɪŋ room?

Te-UyphkKRk.text: She's got a table, four chairs,
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: she's* gɑt ə ˈteɪbəl, fɔr chairs,

Te-UyphkKRk.text: and some drawers. That's right!
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: and* səm drɔrz. ðæts right!

Te-UyphkKRk.text: What have I got in my kitchen?
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: what* hæv aɪ gɑt ɪn maɪ kitchen?

Te-UyphkKRk.text: You've got a fridge, some cupboards,
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: you've* gɑt ə frɪʤ, səm cupboards,

Te-UyphkKRk.text: an oven, and a microwave.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: an* ˈəvən, ənd ə microwave.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: Correct! What rooms have you got in your house? And what have you got in those rooms?
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: kərˈɛkt! wət rumz hæv ju gɑt ɪn jʊr haʊs? ənd wət hæv ju gɑt ɪn ðoʊz rumz?

Te-UyphkKRk.text: Tell us in the comments. Click here for ten easy furniture words.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: tell* ˈjuˈɛs ɪn ðə ˈkɑmɛnts. klɪk hir fər tɛn ˈizi ˈfərnɪʧər words.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: And click here to subscribe to our channel.
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: and* klɪk hir tɪ səbˈskraɪb tɪ ɑr channel.

Te-UyphkKRk.text: Bye!
Te-UyphkKRk.ipa: bye*!

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Easy English conversations.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: easy* ˈɪŋlɪʃ conversations.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: How often do you go swimming? Hello, Buli. Hi Sian! What is your daily routine?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: how* ˈɔfən du ju goʊ sˈwɪmɪŋ? hɛˈloʊ, buli*. haɪ siɑn! wət ɪz jʊr ˈdeɪli routine?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Well, I always get up at seven o'clock.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: well*, aɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz gɪt əp æt ˈsɛvən o'clock.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I usually have fruit and yogurt for breakfast.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈjuʒəwəli hæv frut ənd ˈjoʊgərt fər breakfast.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I often go jogging.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈɔfən goʊ jogging.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I sometimes go swimming after work.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈsəmˌtaɪmz goʊ sˈwɪmɪŋ ˈæftər work.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: And I occasionally watch a film.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: and* aɪ ɔˈkeɪʒənəˌli wɔʧ ə film.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Do you go clothes shopping? No. I hardly ever go clothes shopping.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: do* ju goʊ kloʊðz ˈʃɑpɪŋ? noʊ. aɪ ˈhɑrdli ˈɛvər goʊ kloʊðz shopping.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: How about skateboarding? Do you go skateboarding?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: haʊ əˈbaʊt ˈskeɪtˌbɔrdɪŋ? du ju goʊ skateboarding?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: No, I never go skateboarding. How about you, Buli?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: no*, aɪ ˈnɛvər goʊ ˈskeɪtˌbɔrdɪŋ. haʊ əˈbaʊt ju, buli?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: What is your daily routine? I usually get up at quarter past six.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: what* ɪz jʊr ˈdeɪli ruˈtin? aɪ ˈjuʒəwəli gɪt əp æt kˈwɔrtər pæst six.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I often have porridge for breakfast.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈɔfən hæv ˈpɔrəʤ fər ˈbrɛkfəst.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I sometimes listen to a podcast. Welcome to Real Easy English.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: aɪ ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ˈlɪsən tɪ ə ˈpɔdˌkæst. ˈwɛlkəm tɪ ril ˈizi english.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I always cook dinner after work.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈɔlˌweɪz kʊk ˈdɪnər ˈæftər work.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: And I occasionally play video games.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: and* aɪ ɔˈkeɪʒənəˌli pleɪ ˈvɪdioʊ games.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Do you go swimming in the sea?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: do* ju goʊ sˈwɪmɪŋ ɪn ðə sea?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: No. I hardly ever go swimming in the sea.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: no*. aɪ ˈhɑrdli ˈɛvər goʊ sˈwɪmɪŋ ɪn ðə sea.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Do you drink tea? No, I never drink tea.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: do* ju drɪŋk ti? noʊ, aɪ ˈnɛvər drɪŋk tea.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Find out what Buli did at the weekend in the next episode.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: find* aʊt wət buli* dɪd æt ðə ˈwiˌkɪnd ɪn ðə nɛkst episode.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Now back to the lesson.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: now* bæk tɪ ðə lesson.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Hi Georgie. Hello. What's your daily routine?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: hi* ˈʤɔrʤi. hɛˈloʊ. wəts jʊr ˈdeɪli routine?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I usually get up at quarter to eight.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈjuʒəwəli gɪt əp æt kˈwɔrtər tɪ eight.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I often eat cereal for breakfast.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈɔfən it ˈsɪriəl fər breakfast.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I always read the news on the train.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: aɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz rɛd ðə nuz ɔn ðə train.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I sometimes exercise after work.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ˈɛksərˌsaɪz ˈæftər work.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: And I occasionally watch TV.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: and* aɪ ɔˈkeɪʒənəˌli wɔʧ tv.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Do you sometimes go to the gym? No. I hardly ever go to the gym.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: do* ju ˈsəmˌtaɪmz goʊ tɪ ðə ʤɪm? noʊ. aɪ ˈhɑrdli ˈɛvər goʊ tɪ ðə ʤɪm.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Do you sometimes play video games?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: do* ju ˈsəmˌtaɪmz pleɪ ˈvɪdioʊ games?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: No, I never play video games. How about you, Tim?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: no*, aɪ ˈnɛvər pleɪ ˈvɪdioʊ geɪmz. haʊ əˈbaʊt ju, tim?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: What is your daily routine?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: wət ɪz jʊr ˈdeɪli routine?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I sometimes get up at half past seven.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: aɪ ˈsəmˌtaɪmz gɪt əp æt hæf pæst seven.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: But sometimes, I get up at half past eight.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: but* ˈsəmˌtaɪmz, aɪ gɪt əp æt hæf pæst eight.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I occasionally eat toast for breakfast. I always brush my teeth.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ɔˈkeɪʒənəˌli it toʊst fər ˈbrɛkfəst. aɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz brəʃ maɪ teeth.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I usually walk to work. I often read a book.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈjuʒəwəli wɔk tɪ wərk. aɪ ˈɔfən rɛd ə book.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Do you sometimes go camping? No. I hardly ever go camping.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: do* ju ˈsəmˌtaɪmz goʊ ˈkæmpɪŋ? noʊ. aɪ ˈhɑrdli ˈɛvər goʊ camping.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Do you sometimes watch cartoons? No, I never watch cartoons.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: do* ju ˈsəmˌtaɪmz wɔʧ kɑrˈtunz? noʊ, aɪ ˈnɛvər wɔʧ cartoons.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Click on the link to download a free worksheet for this episode. Now back to the lesson.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: click* ɔn ðə lɪŋk tɪ ˈdaʊnˌloʊd ə fri ˈwərkˌʃit fər ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd. naʊ bæk tɪ ðə lesson.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Can you remember our routines? What does Sian occasionally do?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: can* ju rɪˈmɛmbər ɑr ruˈtinz? wət dɪz siɑn ɔˈkeɪʒənəˌli do?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Sian occasionally watches a film. That's right. I occasionally watch a film.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: sian* ɔˈkeɪʒənəˌli ˈwɑʧɪz ə fɪlm. ðæts raɪt. aɪ ɔˈkeɪʒənəˌli wɔʧ ə film.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: What does Buli sometimes do?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: what* dɪz buli* ˈsəmˌtaɪmz do?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Buli sometimes listens to a podcast. Correct!
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: buli* ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ˈlɪsənz tɪ ə ˈpɔdˌkæst. correct!

gCs4knrlnD4.text: I sometimes listen to a podcast.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: i* ˈsəmˌtaɪmz ˈlɪsən tɪ ə podcast.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: What does Georgie always do?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: what* dɪz ˈʤɔrʤi ˈɔlˌweɪz du?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Georgie always reads the news on the train. That's right! I always read the news on the train.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: georgie* ˈɔlˌweɪz ridz ðə nuz ɔn ðə treɪn. ðæts raɪt! aɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz rɛd ðə nuz ɔn ðə train.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: What does Tim often do?
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: what* dɪz tɪm ˈɔfən do?

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Tim often reads a book. Correct! I often read a book.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: tɪm ˈɔfən ridz ə bʊk. kərˈɛkt! aɪ ˈɔfən rɛd ə book.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: What do you sometimes do? What do you usually do? Tell us about your daily routine
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: what* du ju ˈsəmˌtaɪmz du? wət du ju ˈjuʒəwəli du? tɛl ˈjuˈɛs əˈbaʊt jʊr ˈdeɪli routine

gCs4knrlnD4.text: in the comments.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: in* ðə comments.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Click here for more practice. And click here to subscribe to our channel.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: click* hir fər mɔr ˈpræktɪs. ənd klɪk hir tɪ səbˈskraɪb tɪ ɑr channel.

gCs4knrlnD4.text: Bye.
gCs4knrlnD4.ipa: bye*.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: Easy English Conversations.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ conversations.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: What's she like? Hi, Sian. Hello, Georgie.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: wəts ʃi laɪk? haɪ, siɑn. hɛˈloʊ, georgie.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: Oh! Who's this? This is my friend, Shannon. What's your friend like?
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: oh*! huz ðɪs? ðɪs ɪz maɪ frɛnd, ˈʃænən. wəts jʊr frɛnd like?

osDr7To4Ml4.text: She's very busy. She's always doing something. I'm also hardworking.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: she's* ˈvɛri ˈbɪzi. ʃiz ˈɔlˌweɪz duɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ. əm ˈɔlsoʊ hardworking.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: I work all the time. Who's this? This is my friend, Gina.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: i* wərk ɔl ðə taɪm. huz ðɪs? ðɪs ɪz maɪ frɛnd, gina.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: What's Gina like? She is very talkative. She talks a lot.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: what's* ˈʤinə laɪk? ʃi ɪz ˈvɛri ˈtɔkətɪv. ʃi tɔks ə lot.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: And I'm sporty. I enjoy doing lots of sports.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: and* əm ˈspɔrti. aɪ ˌɛnˈʤɔɪ duɪŋ lɑts əv sports.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: Hello. Hi. Hello, Buli! Who's this?
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: hello*. haɪ. hɛˈloʊ, buli*! huz this?

osDr7To4Ml4.text: This is Billy. What's Billy like? He is very funny.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: this* ɪz ˈbɪli. wəts ˈbɪli laɪk? hi ɪz ˈvɛri funny.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: He makes us all laugh. And that's how you make a lemonade.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: he* meɪks ˈjuˈɛs ɔl læf. ənd ðæts haʊ ju meɪk ə lemonade.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: I'm also friendly. I'm nice to everyone.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: i'm* ˈɔlsoʊ ˈfrɛndli. əm nis tɪ everyone.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: Click on the link to download a free worksheet for this episode.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: click* ɔn ðə lɪŋk tɪ ˈdaʊnˌloʊd ə fri ˈwərkˌʃit fər ðɪs episode.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: Now, back to the lesson. Hi, Tim. Hello. Who's this?
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: now*, bæk tɪ ðə ˈlɛsən. haɪ, tɪm. hɛˈloʊ. huz this?

osDr7To4Ml4.text: This is my friend, Tom. What's your friend like? He's not friendly.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: this* ɪz maɪ frɛnd, tɑm. wəts jʊr frɛnd laɪk? hiz nɑt friendly.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: He's quite grumpy. He's usually angry or annoyed about something!
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: he's* kwaɪt ˈgrəmpi. hiz ˈjuʒəwəli ˈæŋgri ər əˈnɔɪd əˈbaʊt ˈsəmθɪŋ!

osDr7To4Ml4.text: But I'm also brave. I like extreme sports like bungee jumping,
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: bət əm ˈɔlsoʊ breɪv. aɪ laɪk ɪkˈstrim spɔrts laɪk ˈbənʤi jumping,

osDr7To4Ml4.text: skydiving, and rock climbing.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: skydiving*, ənd rɑk climbing.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: Wow, that is brave. Tom sounds like my friend, Gina!
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: wow*, ðət ɪz breɪv. tɑm saʊnz laɪk maɪ frɛnd, gina!

osDr7To4Ml4.text: What is Gina like? She is sporty, and she is talkative.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: what* ɪz ˈʤinə laɪk? ʃi ɪz ˈspɔrti, ənd ʃi ɪz talkative.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: Talkative. Tom isn't talkative. He's the opposite of talkative.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: talkative*. tɑm ˈɪzənt ˈtɔkətɪv. hiz ðə ˈɑpəzɪt əv talkative.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: He's quiet. He doesn't talk very much. Sounds like Shannon!
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: he's* kwaɪət. hi ˈdəzənt tɔk ˈvɛri məʧ. saʊnz laɪk shannon!

osDr7To4Ml4.text: She's quiet. She's also hardworking. Hardworking?
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: she's* kwaɪət. ʃiz ˈɔlsoʊ ˈhɑrdˌwərkɪŋ. hardworking?

osDr7To4Ml4.text: Tom isn't hardworking. He's the opposite of hardworking. He's lazy.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: tom* ˈɪzənt ˈhɑrdˌwərkɪŋ. hiz ðə ˈɑpəzɪt əv ˈhɑrdˌwərkɪŋ. hiz lazy.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: He sits on the sofa a lot and watches TV.
osDr7To4Ml4.ipa: he* sɪts ɔn ðə ˈsoʊfə ə lɔt ənd ˈwɑʧɪz tv.

osDr7To4Ml4.text: Learn how to describe someone's appearance in the next episode. He had brown eyes.

81MmK177K_c.text: It's twenty-five to three.

81MmK177K_c.text: It's twenty to three. It's quarter to three.

81MmK177K_c.text: It's ten to three. What time is it now?

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Pippa. And I'm Beth.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: hɛˈloʊ ənd ˈwɛlkəm tɪ 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm pippa*. ənd əm beth.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Have you ever been scared of speaking in English? In this episode, we're talking about why
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: have* ju ˈɛvər bɪn skɛrd əv ˈspikɪŋ ɪn ˈɪŋlɪʃ? ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, wɪr ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt why

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: speaking in a foreign language can be so stressful.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: speaking* ɪn ə ˈfɔrən ˈlæŋgwɪʤ kən bi soʊ stressful.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: So, Pippa, do you ever get nervous speaking in a different language?
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: soʊ, pippa*, du ju ˈɛvər gɪt ˈnərvəs ˈspikɪŋ ɪn ə ˈdɪfərənt language?

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Yes, I learnt French when I was younger and I was actually quite good at it, I think,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: yes*, aɪ lərnt frɛnʧ wɪn aɪ wɑz ˈjəŋgər ənd aɪ wɑz ˈæˌkʧuəli kwaɪt gʊd æt ɪt, aɪ think,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: but I was really nervous when I was speaking French. How about you? Yeah, I've always felt like that with Spanish.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: but* aɪ wɑz ˈrɪli ˈnərvəs wɪn aɪ wɑz ˈspikɪŋ frɛnʧ. haʊ əˈbaʊt ju? jæ, aɪv ˈɔlˌweɪz fɛlt laɪk ðət wɪθ spanish.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: I am scared to speak because I don't want to make mistakes, even though I know it's OK to make mistakes.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: i* æm skɛrd tɪ spik bɪˈkəz aɪ doʊnt wɔnt tɪ meɪk mɪˈsteɪks, ˈivɪn ðoʊ aɪ noʊ ɪts ˈoʊˈkeɪ tɪ meɪk mistakes.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Well, in this episode of 6 Minute English, we'll be hearing about how to feel less nervous when you speak English.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: well*, ɪn ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd əv 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ, wɪl bi ˈhirɪŋ əˈbaʊt haʊ tɪ fil lɛs ˈnərvəs wɪn ju spik english.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: And as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases. You can practise these with the quiz and worksheet
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: and* ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl bi ˈlərnɪŋ səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd ˈfreɪzɪz. ju kən ˈpræktɪs ðiz wɪθ ðə kwɪz ənd worksheet

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: on our website, bbclearningenglish.com. But first I have a question for you, Pippa.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: on* ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*. bət fərst aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, pippa.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: So, English has around one million words, but on average,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: so*, ˈɪŋlɪʃ həz əraʊnd wən ˈmɪljən wərdz, bət ɔn average,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: how many words does a typical English speaker use in daily conversation?
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: how* ˈmɛni wərdz dɪz ə ˈtɪpɪkəl ˈɪŋlɪʃ ˈspikər juz ɪn ˈdeɪli ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən?

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Is it a) between 2000 and 3000?
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: is* ɪt ə) bɪtˈwin 2000 ənd 3000?

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: b) between 20000 and 30000.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: b*) bɪtˈwin 20000 ənd 30000.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Or c) between 200000 and 300000.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: or* si) bɪtˈwin 200000 ənd 300000

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: I'm going to say a) 2000 to 3000. OK. Well we will find out later in the programme.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: əm goʊɪŋ tɪ seɪ ə) 2000 tɪ 3000 ˈoʊˈkeɪ. wɛl wi wɪl faɪnd aʊt ˈleɪtər ɪn ðə programme.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Now, Pippa, you said you found it stressful speaking in French, even though you are quite good at it.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: now*, pippa*, ju sɛd ju faʊnd ɪt ˈstrɛsfəl ˈspikɪŋ ɪn frɛnʧ, ˈivɪn ðoʊ ju ər kwaɪt gʊd æt it.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Now, someone else who has had this experience is Hanan Razek. Hanan is a journalist at BBC Arabic
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: now*, ˈsəmˌwən ɛls hu həz hæd ðɪs ɪkˈspɪriəns ɪz ˈheɪnən razek*. ˈheɪnən ɪz ə ˈʤərnəlɪst æt ˌbibiˈsi arabic

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: and also host of the podcast Beating Speaking Anxiety.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: and* ˈɔlsoʊ hoʊst əv ðə ˈpɔdˌkæst ˈbitɪŋ ˈspikɪŋ anxiety.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Here she is telling BBC Learning English presenter Georgie about speaking English.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: here* ʃi ɪz ˈtɛlɪŋ ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ ˈprɛzəntər ˈʤɔrʤi əˈbaʊt ˈspikɪŋ english.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: My English was actually pretty good but having conversations with people, I found it really difficult.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: my* ˈɪŋlɪʃ wɑz ˈæˌkʧuəli ˈprɪti gʊd bət ˈhævɪŋ ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃənz wɪθ ˈpipəl, aɪ faʊnd ɪt ˈrɪli difficult.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: So when I first joined the BBC, the Learning English team made an assessment
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: so* wɪn aɪ fərst ʤɔɪnd ðə ˌbibiˈsi, ðə ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ tim meɪd ən assessment

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: of my English level, which they used to do for all new joiners to see if they need any help or courses.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: of* maɪ ˈɪŋlɪʃ ˈlɛvəl, wɪʧ ðeɪ juzd tɪ du fər ɔl nu joiners* tɪ si ɪf ðeɪ nid ˈɛni hɛlp ər courses.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: My results were pretty good and I was fluent. But on that very same day,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: my* rɪˈzəlts wər ˈprɪti gʊd ənd aɪ wɑz fluənt. bət ɔn ðət ˈvɛri seɪm day,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: leaving the building and going to get some coffee, I couldn't really understand what the barista was saying,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: leaving* ðə ˈbɪldɪŋ ənd goʊɪŋ tɪ gɪt səm ˈkɔfi, aɪ ˈkʊdənt ˈrɪli ˌəndərˈstænd wət ðə barista* wɑz saying,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: and I felt pretty nervous to order coffee and was trying to stress every single word,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: and* aɪ fɛlt ˈprɪti ˈnərvəs tɪ ˈɔrdər ˈkɔfi ənd wɑz traɪɪŋ tɪ strɛs ˈɛvəri ˈsɪŋgəl word,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: hoping that my grammar is correct and I am pronouncing the words right.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: hoping* ðət maɪ ˈgræmər ɪz kərˈɛkt ənd aɪ æm prəˈnaʊnsɪŋ ðə wərdz right.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Yeah, I'm sure that's a situation lots of people can relate to. Hanan's results on her English assessment were pretty good,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: yeah*, əm ʃʊr ðæts ə ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən lɑts əv ˈpipəl kən rɪˈleɪt tɪ. hanan's* rɪˈzəlts ɔn hər ˈɪŋlɪʃ əˈsɛsmənt wər ˈprɪti good,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: but she was still pretty nervous to speak in English, for example when ordering a coffee in English.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: but* ʃi wɑz stɪl ˈprɪti ˈnərvəs tɪ spik ɪn ˈɪŋlɪʃ, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl wɪn ˈɔrdərɪŋ ə ˈkɔfi ɪn english.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: The modifier pretty means quite or a bit. Georgie says that Hanan's experience is something
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: the* modifier* ˈprɪti minz kwaɪt ər ə bɪt. ˈʤɔrʤi sɪz ðət hanan's* ɪkˈspɪriəns ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: that lots of people can relate to. If you relate to a situation, you know what it feels like,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: ðət lɑts əv ˈpipəl kən rɪˈleɪt tɪ. ɪf ju rɪˈleɪt tɪ ə ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən, ju noʊ wət ɪt filz like,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: usually because a similar thing has happened to you. So lots of people feel nervous speaking another language,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: usually* bɪˈkəz ə ˈsɪmələr θɪŋ həz ˈhæpənd tɪ ju. soʊ lɑts əv ˈpipəl fil ˈnərvəs ˈspikɪŋ əˈnəðər language,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: even if they're good at it, like Hanan We can call this speaking anxiety.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: even* ɪf ðɛr gʊd æt ɪt, laɪk ˈheɪnən wi kən kɔl ðɪs ˈspikɪŋ anxiety.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Anxiety means an uncomfortable feeling or worry about something that is happening or could happen in the future.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: anxiety* minz ən ənˈkəmfərtəbəl ˈfilɪŋ ər ˈwəri əˈbaʊt ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ɪz ˈhæpənɪŋ ər kʊd ˈhæpən ɪn ðə future.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: So why do so many people have anxiety about speaking a foreign language?
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: so* waɪ du soʊ ˈmɛni ˈpipəl hæv æŋˈzaɪəti əˈbaʊt ˈspikɪŋ ə ˈfɔrən language?

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Experts think it's connected to the beliefs we have about ourselves.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: experts* θɪŋk ɪts kəˈnɛktɪd tɪ ðə bɪˈlifs wi hæv əˈbaʊt ourselves.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Han Luo, professor of Chinese at Lafayette College in the United States,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: han* lwoʊ, prəˈfɛsər əv ʧaɪˈniz æt ˌlɑfiˈɛt ˈkɑlɪʤ ɪn ðə juˈnaɪtɪd states,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: explains more to BBC Learning English presenter Georgie.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: explains* mɔr tɪ ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ ˈprɛzəntər georgie.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: You know, usually the beliefs that cause anxiety, especially severe anxiety,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: you* noʊ, ˈjuʒəwəli ðə bɪˈlifs ðət kɔz æŋˈzaɪəti, əˈspɛʃəli səˈvɪr anxiety,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: are, we call it irrational beliefs.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: are*, wi kɔl ɪt ˌɪˈræʃənəl beliefs.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: And also like, uh, some low self-perceptions, um, fear
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: and* ˈɔlsoʊ laɪk, ə, səm loʊ self-perceptions*, əm, fear

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: of negative evaluation, all those learner internal, you know, factors.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: of* ˈnɛgətɪv ɪˌvæljuˈeɪʃən, ɔl ðoʊz ˈlərnər ˌɪnˈtərnəl, ju noʊ, factors.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: And Han says that learners worry about mistakes because they're scared of negative evaluation. In other words,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: and* hɑn sɪz ðət ˈlərnərz ˈwəri əˈbaʊt mɪˈsteɪks bɪˈkəz ðɛr skɛrd əv ˈnɛgətɪv ɪˌvæljuˈeɪʃən. ɪn ˈəðər words,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: that people will judge them for their mistakes and think badly of them. Han argues that irrational beliefs can make us anxious.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: that* ˈpipəl wɪl ʤəʤ ðɛm fər ðɛr mɪˈsteɪks ənd θɪŋk ˈbædli əv ðɛm. hɑn ˈɑrgjuz ðət ˌɪˈræʃənəl bɪˈlifs kən meɪk ˈjuˈɛs anxious.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Irrational beliefs are beliefs that aren't based on things that are true. They're not logical.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: irrational* bɪˈlifs ər bɪˈlifs ðət ˈɑrənt beɪst ɔn θɪŋz ðət ər tru. ðɛr nɑt ˈlɑʤɪkəl.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Han says some people who have speaking anxiety have low self-perceptions.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: hɑn sɪz səm ˈpipəl hu hæv ˈspikɪŋ æŋˈzaɪəti hæv loʊ self-perceptions.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: Your self-perception is what you think about yourself. Self-perception and irrational beliefs are both internal factors.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: your* self-perception* ɪz wət ju θɪŋk əˈbaʊt ˈjɔrsɛlf. self-perception* ənd ˌɪˈræʃənəl bɪˈlifs ər boʊθ ˌɪnˈtərnəl factors.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: They come from inside your head, not from the people around you. So, for example,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: they* kəm frəm ˌɪnˈsaɪd jʊr hɛd, nɑt frəm ðə ˈpipəl əraʊnd ju. soʊ, fər example,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: when you speak your first language, you might feel confident and intelligent,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: when* ju spik jʊr fərst ˈlæŋgwɪʤ, ju maɪt fil ˈkɑnfədənt ənd intelligent,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: but when you speak in a new language, you make mistakes and feel less confident.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: but* wɪn ju spik ɪn ə nu ˈlæŋgwɪʤ, ju meɪk mɪˈsteɪks ənd fil lɛs confident.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: You can't show people what you're really like, and this is frustrating.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: you* kænt ʃoʊ ˈpipəl wət jʊr ˈrɪli laɪk, ənd ðɪs ɪz frustrating.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: But as we tell people all the time at BBC Learning English, mistakes are normal. You have to try to speak,
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: but* ɛz wi tɛl ˈpipəl ɔl ðə taɪm æt ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ, mɪˈsteɪks ər ˈnɔrməl. ju hæv tɪ traɪ tɪ speak,

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: even though you make mistakes, and mistakes can be a good thing. They can help us learn.
YAsDeXcYyTg.ipa: even* ðoʊ ju meɪk mɪˈsteɪks, ənd mɪˈsteɪks kən bi ə gʊd θɪŋ. ðeɪ kən hɛlp ˈjuˈɛs learn.

YAsDeXcYyTg.text: And if you want more tips to improve your speaking, listen to the Beating Speaking Anxiety podcast.

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: Yes, I asked: What percentage of the British population believes society is divided,
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: yes*, aɪ æst: wət pərˈsɛnɪʤ əv ðə ˈbrɪtɪʃ ˌpɑpjəˈleɪʃən bɪˈlivz soʊˈsaɪɪti ɪz divided,

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: according to a recent poll by the organisation Ipsos? I said 50%. Was that correct?
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: according* tɪ ə ˈrisənt poʊl baɪ ðə ˌɔrgənɪˈzeɪʃən ipsos*? aɪ sɛd 50 wɑz ðət correct?

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: It was not correct. It is in fact 80%. Wow, that's a lot!
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: it* wɑz nɑt kərˈɛkt. ɪt ɪz ɪn fækt 80 waʊ, ðæts ə lot!

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: OK. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned in this programme, starting with divided, which means in disagreement – not together.
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: ok*. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv ˈlərnɪd ɪn ðɪs ˈproʊˌgræm, ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ dɪˈvaɪdɪd, wɪʧ minz ɪn dɪsəˈgrimənt –* nɑt together.

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: Aroused means feel a strong emotion like anger.
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: aroused* minz fil ə strɔŋ ˈiˌmoʊʃən laɪk anger.

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: If something is motivational, it makes us determined and enthusiastic.
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: if* ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪz ˌmoʊtəˈveɪʃənəl, ɪt meɪks ˈjuˈɛs dɪˈtərmənd ənd enthusiastic.

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: Heat means an intense feeling, like anger. If you are open-minded, you are willing to listen to and consider new ideas.
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: heat* minz ən ˌɪnˈtɛns ˈfilɪŋ, laɪk ˈæŋgər. ɪf ju ər open-minded*, ju ər ˈwɪlɪŋ tɪ ˈlɪsən tɪ ənd kənˈsɪdər nu ideas.

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: And finally, a silver bullet means a simple solution to a complicated problem.
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: and* ˈfaɪnəli, ə ˈsɪlvər ˈbʊlət minz ə ˈsɪmpəl səˈluʃən tɪ ə ˈkɑmpləˌkeɪtəd problem.

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: Once again, our six minutes are up. If you've enjoyed this programme, why not try practising the new vocabulary
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: once* əˈgɛn, ɑr sɪks ˈmɪnəts ər əp. ɪf juv ˌɛnˈʤɔɪd ðɪs ˈproʊˌgræm, waɪ nɑt traɪ practising* ðə nu vocabulary

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: you've learnt with our worksheet? It's available to download now from our website, bbclearningenglish.com,
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: you've* lərnt wɪθ ɑr ˈwərkˌʃit? ɪts əˈveɪləbəl tɪ ˈdaʊnˌloʊd naʊ frəm ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*,

oeoQU26Lx0A.text: and you could also try out the quiz while you're there. Goodbye for now. Goodbye!
oeoQU26Lx0A.ipa: ənd ju kʊd ˈɔlsoʊ traɪ aʊt ðə kwɪz waɪl jʊr ðɛr. ˌgʊdˈbaɪ fər naʊ. ˌgʊdˈbaɪ!

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Beth.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: hɛˈloʊ. ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm beth.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: In Britain we call them crisps and you might know them as potato chips, but whatever you call them,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: in* ˈbrɪtən wi kɔl ðɛm crisps* ənd ju maɪt noʊ ðɛm ɛz pəˈteɪˌtoʊ ʧɪps, bət ˌwəˈtɛvər ju kɔl them,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: these fried potato slices are probably the world's favourite snack.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: these* frid pəˈteɪˌtoʊ sˈlaɪsɪz ər ˈprɑbəˌbli ðə wərldz ˈfeɪvərɪt snack.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Neil, are you a big crisp eater and what's your favourite flavour? I love crisps Beth. They are my favourite snack.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: neil*, ər ju ə bɪg krɪsp ˈitər ənd wəts jʊr ˈfeɪvərɪt flavour*? aɪ ləv crisps* bɛθ. ðeɪ ər maɪ ˈfeɪvərɪt snæk.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: My favourite flavour is prawn cocktail, which is controversial because a lot of people don't like that flavour.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: my* ˈfeɪvərɪt flavour* ɪz prɔn ˈkɑkˌteɪl, wɪʧ ɪz ˌkɑntrəˈvərʃəl bɪˈkəz ə lɔt əv ˈpipəl doʊnt laɪk ðət flavour.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: I think my favourite is salt and vinegar. Yummy! You're making me hungry. Well, crisps might be popular but
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: i* θɪŋk maɪ ˈfeɪvərɪt ɪz sɔlt ənd ˈvɪnəgər. ˈjəmi! jʊr ˈmeɪkɪŋ mi ˈhəŋgri. wɛl, crisps* maɪt bi ˈpɑpjələr but

EAMoWvtjvag.text: with lots of oil and salt they're not the healthiest food. So, why do we love them so much?
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: wɪθ lɑts əv ɔɪl ənd sɔlt ðɛr nɑt ðə ˈhɛlθiɪst fud. soʊ, waɪ du wi ləv ðɛm soʊ much?

EAMoWvtjvag.text: That's what BBC World Service programme The Food Chain wanted to find out. Here, presenter Ruth Alexander talks to Mitchell Dingwall,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: that's* wət ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud ʧeɪn ˈwɔntɪd tɪ faɪnd aʊt. hir, ˈprɛzəntər ruθ ˌælɪgˈzændər tɔks tɪ ˈmɪʧəl dingwall,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: a food executive responsible for developing new crisp flavours: Why do we love crisps so much?
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: a* fud ɪgˈzɛkjətɪv riˈspɑnsəbəl fər dɪˈvɛləpɪŋ nu krɪsp flavours*: waɪ du wi ləv crisps* soʊ much?

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Hmm. I think it's just that crunch, right? You can't beat the crunch.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: hmm*. aɪ θɪŋk ɪts ʤɪst ðət krənʧ, raɪt? ju kænt bit ðə crunch.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: And then that delicious, salty feeling in your mouth and it just becomes a snowball effect. You just want more of it!
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: and* ðɛn ðət dɪˈlɪʃəs, ˈsɔlti ˈfilɪŋ ɪn jʊr maʊθ ənd ɪt ʤɪst bɪˈkəmz ə sˈnoʊˌbɔl ˈifɛkt. ju ʤɪst wɔnt mɔr əv it!

EAMoWvtjvag.text: For Mitchell, the best thing about crisps is the loud crunch. He says, You can't beat it!
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: for* ˈmɪʧəl, ðə bɛst θɪŋ əˈbaʊt crisps* ɪz ðə laʊd krənʧ. hi sɪz, ju kænt bit it!

EAMoWvtjvag.text: An idiom meaning something like: That's the best thing and you won't find anything better!
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: an* ˈɪdiəm ˈminɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ laɪk: ðæts ðə bɛst θɪŋ ənd ju woʊnt faɪnd ˈɛniˌθɪŋ better!

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Mitchell says once you started eating crisps, it's hard to stop and you just want to eat more and more.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: mitchell* sɪz wəns ju ˈstɑrtɪd ˈitɪŋ crisps*, ɪts hɑrd tɪ stɑp ənd ju ʤɪst wɔnt tɪ it mɔr ənd more.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: He calls this a snowball effect – a situation where doing something causes other similar things to happen.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: he* kɔlz ðɪs ə sˈnoʊˌbɔl ˈifɛkt –* ə ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən wɛr duɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈkɔzɪz ˈəðər ˈsɪmələr θɪŋz tɪ ˈhæpən.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: You eat one crisp and before you know it, the whole packet's gone.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: ju it wən krɪsp ənd ˌbiˈfɔr ju noʊ ɪt, ðə hoʊl packet's* gone.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: In this episode, we'll be visiting two countries in love with crisps and,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: in* ðɪs ˈɛpɪˌsoʊd, wɪl bi ˈvɪzɪtɪŋ tu ˈkəntriz ɪn ləv wɪθ crisps* and,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: as* ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl bi ˈlərnɪŋ səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd phrases,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: all of which you can find on our website, bbclearningenglish.com. But first, I have a question for you, Beth.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: all* əv wɪʧ ju kən faɪnd ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*. bət fərst, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, beth.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: The record for the world's largest packet of crisps was recently broken in Saudi Arabia, but how much did the packet weigh?
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: the* ˈrɛkərd fər ðə wərldz ˈlɑrʤəst ˈpækɪt əv crisps* wɑz ˈrisəntli ˈbroʊkən ɪn ˈsɔdi əˈreɪbiə, bət haʊ məʧ dɪd ðə ˈpækɪt weigh?

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Was it: a) about 1.5 tonnes, b) about 2.5 tonnes, or c) about 3.5 tonnes?
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: was* ɪt: ə) əˈbaʊt 1.5* tənz, bi) əˈbaʊt 2.5* tənz, ər si) əˈbaʊt 3.5* tonnes?

EAMoWvtjvag.text: OK. Wow! They are all huge! I'm going to guess about 3.5 tonnes.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: ok*. waʊ! ðeɪ ər ɔl juʤ! əm goʊɪŋ tɪ gɛs əˈbaʊt 3.5* tonnes.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: OK. We'll find out the answer later. Let's travel first to Singapore, home of Jolene Ng,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: ok*. wɪl faɪnd aʊt ðə ˈænsər ˈleɪtər. lɛts ˈtrævəl fərst tɪ ˈsɪŋəˌpɔr, hoʊm əv ʤoʊˈlin ng,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: a market research analyst who specialises in salty snacks. Here's Jolene talking with Ruth Alexander
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: a* ˈmɑrkɪt ˈrisərʧ ˈænəlɪst hu specialises* ɪn ˈsɔlti snæks. hɪrz ʤoʊˈlin ˈtɔkɪŋ wɪθ ruθ alexander

EAMoWvtjvag.text: for BBC programme The Food Chain. Is the potato chip the most popular of all salty snacks?
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: for* ˌbibiˈsi ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud ʧeɪn. ɪz ðə pəˈteɪˌtoʊ ʧɪp ðə moʊst ˈpɑpjələr əv ɔl ˈsɔlti snacks?

EAMoWvtjvag.text: It's very, very much dependent on personal preference but
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: it's* ˈvɛri, ˈvɛri məʧ dɪˈpɛndənt ɔn ˈpərsɪnəl ˈprɛfərəns but

EAMoWvtjvag.text: if I were to look at certain countries, you could see, like in the UK,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: if* aɪ wər tɪ lʊk æt ˈsərtən ˈkəntriz, ju kʊd si, laɪk ɪn ðə uk,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: it's very much part and parcel of life. You don't have it only during snack times.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: it's* ˈvɛri məʧ pɑrt ənd ˈpɑrsəl əv laɪf. ju doʊnt hæv ɪt ˈoʊnli ˈdʊrɪŋ snæk times.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: You have it with lunch as well. You put it...
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: you* hæv ɪt wɪθ lənʧ ɛz wɛl. ju pʊt it...

EAMoWvtjvag.text: My husband's from the UK and he, kind of, puts it in his sandwich as well.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: my* ˈhəzbəndz frəm ðə uk* ənd hi, kaɪnd əv, pʊts ɪt ɪn hɪz ˈsænwɪʧ ɛz wɛl.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Oh yeah, a cheese sandwich with a crisp inside. Oh yeah, that's great! Exactly!
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: oʊ jæ, ə ʧiz ˈsænwɪʧ wɪθ ə krɪsp ˌɪnˈsaɪd. oʊ jæ, ðæts greɪt! exactly!

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Jolene says the popularity of different snacks depends on personal preference – someone's subjective liking
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: jolene* sɪz ðə ˌpɑpjəˈlɛrəti əv ˈdɪfərənt snæks dɪˈpɛndz ɔn ˈpərsɪnəl ˈprɛfərəns –* ˈsəmˌwənz səˈbʤɛktɪv liking

EAMoWvtjvag.text: or disliking for something based on their personal opinion.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: or* dɪsˈlaɪkɪŋ fər ˈsəmθɪŋ beɪst ɔn ðɛr ˈpərsɪnəl opinion.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: In some places, crisps are part and parcel of everyday life.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: in* səm ˈpleɪsɪz, crisps* ər pɑrt ənd ˈpɑrsəl əv ˈɛvriˈdeɪ life.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Part and parcel means to be an essential element or feature of something.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: part* ənd ˈpɑrsəl minz tɪ bi ən ɛˈsɛnʃəl ˈɛləmənt ər ˈfiʧər əv something.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: In Britain, crisps are so essential that people even eat crisp sandwiches!
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: in* ˈbrɪtən, crisps* ər soʊ ɛˈsɛnʃəl ðət ˈpipəl ˈivɪn it krɪsp sandwiches!

EAMoWvtjvag.text: But not everywhere is so in love with potato chips.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: but* nɑt ˈɛvriˌwɛr ɪz soʊ ɪn ləv wɪθ pəˈteɪˌtoʊ chips.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Let's meet Jenny Martinez, a food vlogger from Mexico.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: let's* mit ˈʤɛni mɑrˈtinɛz, ə fud vlogger* frəm mexico.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: In Mexico, tortilla corn chips are eaten instead of potato chips,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: in* ˈmɛksəˌkoʊ, tɔrˈtiə kɔrn ʧɪps ər ˈitən ˌɪnˈstɛd əv pəˈteɪˌtoʊ chips,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: and it's common to see street vendors selling bags of corn tortillas filled with different sauces.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: and* ɪts ˈkɑmən tɪ si strit ˈvɛndərz ˈsɛlɪŋ bægz əv kɔrn ˌtɔrˈtiəz fɪld wɪθ ˈdɪfərənt sauces.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Here, Jenny tells Ruth Alexander more for BBC World Service programme The Food Chain:
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: here*, ˈʤɛni tɛlz ruθ ˌælɪgˈzændər mɔr fər ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud chain:

EAMoWvtjvag.text: For you, which is your go-to snack – the tortilla chip or the potato chip? Definitely the tortilla chip. Why?
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: for* ju, wɪʧ ɪz jʊr go-to* snæk –* ðə tɔrˈtiə ʧɪp ər ðə pəˈteɪˌtoʊ ʧɪp? ˈdɛfənətli ðə tɔrˈtiə ʧɪp. why?

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Because you could do so many things – dip it in almost everything,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: because* ju kʊd du soʊ ˈmɛni θɪŋz –* dɪp ɪt ɪn ˈɔlˌmoʊst everything,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: adding some guacamole, spicy salsa or just sour cream
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: adding* səm gˌwɑkəˈmoʊli, ˈspaɪsi ˈsɑlsə ər ʤɪst saʊər cream

EAMoWvtjvag.text: with a mixture of chipotle sauce. Ruth asks if tortillas are Jenny's go-to snack.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: with* ə ˈmɪksʧər əv ʧɪˈpoʊˌtli sɔs. ruθ æsks ɪf ˌtɔrˈtiəz ər ˈʤɛniz go-to* snæk.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: You can use the phrase go-to to describe something which is relied
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: ju kən juz ðə freɪz go-to* tɪ dɪˈskraɪb ˈsəmθɪŋ wɪʧ ɪz relied

EAMoWvtjvag.text: upon to be the best thing for a particular purpose. For example,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: upon* tɪ bi ðə bɛst θɪŋ fər ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr ˈpərpəs. fər example,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: for a lot of people, Wikipedia is the go-to website for online information.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: for* ə lɔt əv ˈpipəl, ˈwɪkiˌpiˌdiə ɪz ðə go-to* ˈwɛbˌsaɪt fər ˈɔnˌlaɪn information.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: And Jenny thinks corn tortillas are especially good with guacamole,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: and* ˈʤɛni θɪŋks kɔrn ˌtɔrˈtiəz ər əˈspɛʃəli gʊd wɪθ guacamole,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: a paste made of mashed avocado... which sounds delicious and maybe a little healthier
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: a* peɪst meɪd əv mæʃt ˌævəˈkɑdoʊ... wɪʧ saʊnz dɪˈlɪʃəs ənd ˈmeɪbi ə ˈlɪtəl healthier

EAMoWvtjvag.text: than a British crisp sandwich! Speaking of which, what was the answer to your question, Neil?
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: than* ə ˈbrɪtɪʃ krɪsp ˈsænwɪʧ! ˈspikɪŋ əv wɪʧ, wət wɑz ðə ˈænsər tɪ jʊr kˈwɛʃən, neil?

EAMoWvtjvag.text: I asked you how heavy the world's largest bag of crisps was.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: i* æst ju haʊ ˈhɛvi ðə wərldz ˈlɑrʤəst bæg əv crisps* was.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: And I said very heavy – over three and a half tonnes. Well, it's not that big
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: and* aɪ sɛd ˈvɛri ˈhɛvi –* ˈoʊvər θri ənd ə hæf tənz. wɛl, ɪts nɑt ðət big

EAMoWvtjvag.text: but the real answer is still enormous – about 1.5 tonnes. The bag, made in Riyadh, was nine metres tall.
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: but* ðə ril ˈænsər ɪz stɪl ɪˈnɔrmɪs –* əˈbaʊt 1.5* tənz. ðə bæg, meɪd ɪn riɑd, wɑz naɪn ˈmitərz tall.

EAMoWvtjvag.text: Yeah. OK. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with the phrase, you can't beat that,
EAMoWvtjvag.ipa: yeah*. ˈoʊˈkeɪ. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv ˈlərnɪd, ˈstɑrtɪŋ wɪθ ðə freɪz, ju kænt bit that,

EAMoWvtjvag.text: meaning that something is the best and you won't find anything better. A snowball effect is a situation where one action causes

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Pippa. Whatever you think of social media,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: hɛˈloʊ, ðɪs ɪz 6 ˈmɪnət ˈɪŋlɪʃ frəm ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ. əm nil. ənd əm pippa*. ˌwəˈtɛvər ju θɪŋk əv ˈsoʊʃəl media,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: in less than a generation it has changed the internet. Facebook, Twitter, now known as X,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: in* lɛs ðən ə ˌʤɛnərˈeɪʃən ɪt həz ʧeɪnʤd ðə ˈɪntərˌnɛt. ˈfeɪsˌbʊk, tˈwɪtər, naʊ noʊn ɛz x,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: and, in the early days, MySpace used to be places to post messages to friends or share photos of your breakfast.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: and*, ɪn ðə ˈərli deɪz, ˈmaɪˌspeɪs juzd tɪ bi ˈpleɪsɪz tɪ poʊst ˈmɛsɪʤɪz tɪ frɛndz ər ʃɛr ˈfoʊˌtoʊz əv jʊr breakfast.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: But with the rise of AI, the fun of connecting with friends changed into something much stranger,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: but* wɪθ ðə raɪz əv eɪaɪ, ðə fən əv kəˈnɛktɪŋ wɪθ frɛndz ʧeɪnʤd ˈɪntu ˈsəmθɪŋ məʧ stranger,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: as Tristan and Asma, presenters of BBC World Service programme The Global Story, found out. Oh... What is this?
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: ɛz ˈtrɪˌstæn ənd asma*, ˈprɛzəntərz əv ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə ˈgloʊbəl ˈstɔri, faʊnd aʊt. oʊ... wət ɪz this?

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: I think it's a man who's supposed to look like Jesus...
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: i* θɪŋk ɪts ə mæn huz səˈpoʊzd tɪ lʊk laɪk jesus...

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: It's a man who looks a bit like... ...who also looks like his dog. He's been AI-ed, right? Has to be AI-ed.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: it's* ə mæn hu lʊks ə bɪt laɪk... ...hu ˈɔlsoʊ lʊks laɪk hɪz dɔg. hiz bɪn ai-ed*, raɪt? həz tɪ bi ai-ed.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: He's holding his dog and his face looks like his dog, so he's half Jesus, half dog.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: he's* ˈhoʊldɪŋ hɪz dɔg ənd hɪz feɪs lʊks laɪk hɪz dɔg, soʊ hiz hæf ˈʤizəs, hæf dog.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: A half-dog, half-Jesus photo is generated by AI and posted online.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: a* half-dog*, half-jesus* ˈfoʊˌtoʊ ɪz ˈʤɛnərˌeɪtɪd baɪ eɪaɪ ənd ˈpoʊstɪd online.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: It's an example of how the use of AI in social media has evolved so much
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: it's* ən ɪgˈzæmpəl əv haʊ ðə juz əv eɪaɪ ɪn ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə həz ɪˈvɑlvd soʊ much

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: that today it's hard to know what's created by humans and what's not. In this episode,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: that* təˈdeɪ ɪts hɑrd tɪ noʊ wəts kriˈeɪtɪd baɪ ˈjumənz ənd wəts nɑt. ɪn ðɪs episode,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: and, as usual, we'll learn some useful new words and phrases. And remember, you'll find all the vocabulary
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: and*, ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl lərn səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd ˈfreɪzɪz. ənd rɪˈmɛmbər, jul faɪnd ɔl ðə vocabulary

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: along with a transcript on our website, bbclearningenglish.com. But first, I have a question for you, Pippa.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: along* wɪθ ə ˈtrænˌskrɪpt ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com*. bət fərst, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, pippa.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: You mentioned MySpace as one of the earliest social media platforms,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: you* ˈmɛnʃənd ˈmaɪˌspeɪs ɛz wən əv ðə ˈərliəst ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə ˈplætˌfɔrmz,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: but the first electronic message was actually sent much earlier, in 1844,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: bət ðə fərst ˌɪˌlɛkˈtrɑnɪk ˈmɛsɪʤ wɑz ˈæˌkʧuəli sɛnt məʧ ˈərliər, ɪn 1844,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: using a series of dots and dashes tapped out by hand on a telegraph wire.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: using* ə ˈsɪriz əv dɑts ənd ˈdæʃɪz tæpt aʊt baɪ hænd ɔn ə ˈtɛləˌgræf wire.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: So, what was this new form of communication called?
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: so*, wət wɑz ðɪs nu fɔrm əv kəmˌjunəˈkeɪʃən called?

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: Was it: a) semaphore, b) dial-up, or c) Morse code?
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: was* ɪt: ə) semaphore*, bi) dial-up*, ər si) mɔrs code?

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: I think Morse code. OK. We'll find out the answer later.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: i* θɪŋk mɔrs koʊd. ˈoʊˈkeɪ. wɪl faɪnd aʊt ðə ˈænsər later.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: The introduction of AI has turned some users away from social media.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: the* ˌɪntrəˈdəkʃən əv eɪaɪ həz tərnd səm ˈjuzərz əˈweɪ frəm ˈsoʊʃəl media.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: Indeed, a recent report found that social media activity peaked in 2022
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: indeed*, ə ˈrisənt rɪˈpɔrt faʊnd ðət ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə ækˈtɪvɪti pikt ɪn 2022

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: and has been going down ever since. Millennial and digital native Kyle Chayka is a writer specialising
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: and* həz bɪn goʊɪŋ daʊn ˈɛvər sɪns. mɪˈlɛniəl ənd ˈdɪʤɪtəl ˈneɪtɪv kaɪl chayka* ɪz ə ˈraɪtər specialising

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: in social media. He spoke about the problem to Tristan Redmond for BBC World Service programme The Global Story.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: in* ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə. hi spoʊk əˈbaʊt ðə ˈprɑbləm tɪ ˈtrɪˌstæn ˈrɛdmənd fər ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə ˈgloʊbəl story.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: OK. Kyle, was there a moment for you when you felt like social media changed?
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: ok*. kaɪl, wɑz ðɛr ə ˈmoʊmənt fər ju wɪn ju fɛlt laɪk ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə ʧeɪnʤd?

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: Oh man! I mean... In 2015/2016, I think, in the United States,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: oh* mæn! aɪ min... ɪn 2015/2016* aɪ θɪŋk, ɪn ðə juˈnaɪtɪd states,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: when Twitter became the hub of election discourse, it kind of shattered
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: when* tˈwɪtər bɪˈkeɪm ðə həb əv ɪˈlɛkʃən ˈdɪskɔrs, ɪt kaɪnd əv shattered

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: the illusion that the internet was a space you could go to, just like, have fun and express yourself
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: the* ˌɪˈluʒən ðət ðə ˈɪntərˌnɛt wɑz ə speɪs ju kʊd goʊ tɪ, ʤɪst laɪk, hæv fən ənd ɪkˈsprɛs yourself

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: and find cool friends and discuss the latest cultural happenings.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: and* faɪnd kul frɛndz ənd dɪˈskəs ðə ˈleɪtəst ˈkəlʧərəl happenings.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: Kyle uses the informal expression, "Oh man!" an exclamation showing excitement or enthusiasm.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: kyle* ˈjuzɪz ðə ˌɪnˈfɔrməl ɪkˈsprɛʃən, "oʊ mæn!" ən ˌɛkskləˈmeɪʃən ʃoʊɪŋ ɪkˈsaɪtmənt ər ɪnˈθuziˌæzəm.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: He remembers the internet in 2016, when Twitter was full of angry political debate about the US elections.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: hi rɪˈmɛmbərz ðə ˈɪntərˌnɛt ɪn 2016 wɪn tˈwɪtər wɑz fʊl əv ˈæŋgri pəˈlɪtɪkəl dəˈbeɪt əˈbaʊt ðə ˈjuˈɛs elections.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: For Kyle, this shattered the illusion that the internet was fun.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: for* kaɪl, ðɪs ˈʃætərd ðə ˌɪˈluʒən ðət ðə ˈɪntərˌnɛt wɑz fun.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: If something shatters your illusions, it destroys a false belief you hold, making you realise an unpleasant truth.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: if* ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈʃætərz jʊr ˌɪˈluʒənz, ɪt dɪˈstrɔɪz ə fɔls bɪˈlif ju hoʊld, ˈmeɪkɪŋ ju ˈriəˌlaɪz ən ənˈplɛzənt truth.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: Kyle thinks social media was friendlier before – an online space
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: kyle* θɪŋks ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə wɑz ˈfrɛndliər ˌbiˈfɔr –* ən ˈɔnˌlaɪn space

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: to make friends, have fun and discuss the latest cultural happenings.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: to* meɪk frɛndz, hæv fən ənd dɪˈskəs ðə ˈleɪtəst ˈkəlʧərəl happenings.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: A cultural happening is an event that embodies the values and interests of a particular group at a particular time in history.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: a* ˈkəlʧərəl ˈhæpənɪŋ ɪz ən ɪˈvɛnt ðət ɛmˈbɑdiz ðə ˈvæljuz ənd ˈɪntərɪsts əv ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr grup æt ə ˌpɑˈtɪkjələr taɪm ɪn history.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: For example, the hit television show Game of Thrones was a cultural happening for millennials in the 2010s.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: for* ɪgˈzæmpəl, ðə hɪt ˈtɛləˌvɪʒən ʃoʊ geɪm əv thrones* wɑz ə ˈkəlʧərəl ˈhæpənɪŋ fər millennials* ɪn ðə 2010s.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: So, what's the future of social media? Will the amount of AI-generated content continue to grow?
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: so*, wəts ðə fˈjuʧər əv ˈsoʊʃəl ˈmidiə? wɪl ðə əˈmaʊnt əv ai-generated* ˈkɑntɛnt kənˈtɪnju tɪ grow?

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: Here's Kyle Chayka again, sharing his thoughts with BBC World Service's The Global Story: We see the large social networks
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: here's* kaɪl chayka* əˈgɛn, ˈʃɛrɪŋ hɪz θɔts wɪθ ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪsɪz ðə ˈgloʊbəl ˈstɔri: wi si ðə lɑrʤ ˈsoʊʃəl networks

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: and the tech behemoths leaning into generated content, and I think users don't actually want that.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: and* ðə tɛk bɪˈhiməθs ˈlinɪŋ ˈɪntu ˈʤɛnərˌeɪtɪd ˈkɑntɛnt, ənd aɪ θɪŋk ˈjuzərz doʊnt ˈæˌkʧuəli wɔnt that.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: I think it's a novelty for now and people will drift away from it.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: i* θɪŋk ɪts ə ˈnɑvəlti fər naʊ ənd ˈpipəl wɪl drɪft əˈweɪ frəm it.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: So instead, I see people gravitating towards smaller online spaces,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: so* ˌɪnˈstɛd, aɪ si ˈpipəl ˈgrævɪˌteɪtɪŋ təˈwɔrdz sˈmɔlər ˈɔnˌlaɪn spaces,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: connecting more closely with individual voices, like creator types,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: connecting* mɔr ˈkloʊsli wɪθ ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəl ˈvɔɪsɪz, laɪk kriˈeɪtər types,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: and just posting less in general. Kyle thinks the tech behemoths,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: and* ʤɪst ˈpoʊstɪŋ lɛs ɪn ˈʤɛnərəl. kaɪl θɪŋks ðə tɛk behemoths,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: meaning large and powerful companies, will continue pushing AI.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: meaning* lɑrʤ ənd ˈpaʊərfəl ˈkəmpəˌniz, wɪl kənˈtɪnju ˈpʊʃɪŋ eɪaɪ.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: However, many people want content which is more personal and meaningful.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: ˌhaʊˈɛvər, ˈmɛni ˈpipəl wɔnt ˈkɑntɛnt wɪʧ ɪz mɔr ˈpərsɪnəl ənd meaningful.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: They think AI is a novelty – something which is new and unusual and therefore exciting, but which probably won't last.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: they* θɪŋk eɪaɪ ɪz ə ˈnɑvəlti –* ˈsəmθɪŋ wɪʧ ɪz nu ənd ənˈjuˌʒuəl ənd ˈðɛrˌfɔr ɪkˈsaɪtɪŋ, bət wɪʧ ˈprɑbəˌbli woʊnt last.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: Instead, Kyle thinks people will gravitate towards smaller online communities.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: instead*, kaɪl θɪŋks ˈpipəl wɪl ˈgrævɪˌteɪt təˈwɔrdz sˈmɔlər ˈɔnˌlaɪn communities.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: If you gravitate towards something, you are naturally drawn or attracted to it.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: if* ju ˈgrævɪˌteɪt təˈwɔrdz ˈsəmθɪŋ, ju ər ˈnæʧərəli drɔn ər əˈtræktəd tɪ it.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: Social media has changed fast and is still in motion. It's come a long way from the first electronic messages
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: social* ˈmidiə həz ʧeɪnʤd fæst ənd ɪz stɪl ɪn ˈmoʊʃən. ɪts kəm ə lɔŋ weɪ frəm ðə fərst ˌɪˌlɛkˈtrɑnɪk messages

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: of 1844 which you mentioned in your question, Neil. So, did I get the correct answer?
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: of* 1844 wɪʧ ju ˈmɛnʃənd ɪn jʊr kˈwɛʃən, nil. soʊ, dɪd aɪ gɪt ðə kərˈɛkt answer?

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: I asked what this new form of communication was, and you said that it was c) Morse code.
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: i* æst wət ðɪs nu fɔrm əv kəmˌjunəˈkeɪʃən wɑz, ənd ju sɛd ðət ɪt wɑz si) mɔrs code.

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: And that, Pippa, is the correct answer. OK. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned,
iMcP-ZydB6s.ipa: and* ðət, pippa*, ɪz ðə kərˈɛkt ˈænsər. ˈoʊˈkeɪ. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv learned,

iMcP-ZydB6s.text: starting with the exclamation, "Oh man!" used to show excitement or enthusiasm.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: So, we'll be hearing more about mealtime manners from some college students and an expert on British etiquette.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: and* aɪ ˈnɛvər ˈrɪli ˌəndərˈstʊd waɪ, bət aɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz rɪˈmɛmbər ðət one.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: And, as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases, all of which you can find on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: so*, wɪl bi ˈhirɪŋ mɔr əˈbaʊt ˈmilˌtaɪm ˈmænərz frəm səm ˈkɑlɪʤ ˈstudənts ənd ən ˈɛkspərt ɔn ˈbrɪtɪʃ etiquette.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: But first, I have a question for you, Pippa. Ideas about polite behaviour change a lot from country to country,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: and*, ɛz ˈjuʒəwəl, wɪl bi ˈlərnɪŋ səm ˈjusfəl nu wərdz ənd ˈfreɪzɪz, ɔl əv wɪʧ ju kən faɪnd ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: so when BBC programme The Food Chain asked listeners from around the world about eating manners in their country,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: but* fərst, aɪ hæv ə kˈwɛʃən fər ju, pippa*. aɪˈdiəz əˈbaʊt pəˈlaɪt bɪˈheɪvjər ʧeɪnʤ ə lɔt frəm ˈkəntri tɪ country,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: what do you think was the most common complaint?
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: so* wɪn ˌbibiˈsi ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud ʧeɪn æst ˈlɪsənərz frəm əraʊnd ðə wərld əˈbaʊt ˈitɪŋ ˈmænərz ɪn ðɛr country,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Was it: a) people eating with their hands, b) people eating noisily,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: wɑz ɪt: ə) ˈpipəl ˈitɪŋ wɪθ ðɛr hænz, bi) ˈpipəl ˈitɪŋ noisily,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: or c) people burping at the table? Hmm. I don't think it's people eating with their hands,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: was* ɪt: ə) ˈpipəl ˈitɪŋ wɪθ ðɛr hænz, bi) ˈpipəl ˈitɪŋ noisily,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: because lots of people around the world do that as, kind of, their normal way of eating,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: bɪˈkəz lɑts əv ˈpipəl əraʊnd ðə wərld du ðət ɛz, kaɪnd əv, ðɛr ˈnɔrməl weɪ əv eating,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: but maybe it's b) people eating noisily, I think. Like you mentioned, Phil, that's quite annoying.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: but* ˈmeɪbi ɪts bi) ˈpipəl ˈitɪŋ ˈnɔɪzəli, aɪ θɪŋk. laɪk ju ˈmɛnʃənd, fɪl, ðæts kwaɪt annoying.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Well, we'll find out the answer later in the programme. The Food Chain also interviewed students at London's City of Westminster College
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: well*, wɪl faɪnd aʊt ðə ˈænsər ˈleɪtər ɪn ðə ˈproʊˌgræm. ðə fud ʧeɪn ˈɔlsoʊ ˈɪntərvˌjud ˈstudənts æt ˈləndənz ˈsɪti əv ˌwɛstˈmɪnstər college

cGJCcidYYaY.text: about family dinner times. Samuel is 19 and from Sierra Leone,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: about* ˈfæməli ˈdɪnər taɪmz. ˈsæmjul ɪz 19 ənd frəm siˈɛrə leone,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: and Aya is 18 and British but her family are from Iraq.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: and* aya* ɪz 18 ənd ˈbrɪtɪʃ bət hər ˈfæməli ər frəm iraq.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Let's listen as they chat with BBC World Service's The Food Chain: Yeah, I would say first impressions really do matter
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: let's* ˈlɪsən ɛz ðeɪ ʧæt wɪθ ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪsɪz ðə fud ʧeɪn: jæ, aɪ wʊd seɪ fərst ˌɪmˈprɛʃənz ˈrɪli du matter

cGJCcidYYaY.text: because it also is really important how you portray yourself – how you present yourself to the person.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: because* ɪt ˈɔlsoʊ ɪz ˈrɪli ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt haʊ ju pɔrˈtreɪ ˈjɔrsɛlf –* haʊ ju ˈprɛzənt ˈjɔrsɛlf tɪ ðə person.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: What I don't want to see is any loud chewing, or with your mouth open, please and thank you!
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: what* aɪ doʊnt wɔnt tɪ si ɪz ˈɛni laʊd ʧuɪŋ, ər wɪθ jʊr maʊθ ˈoʊpən, pliz ənd θæŋk you!

cGJCcidYYaY.text: I would like to see though eye contact, having a conversation, using your cutlery.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: i* wʊd laɪk tɪ si ðoʊ aɪ ˈkɑnˌtækt, ˈhævɪŋ ə ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən, ˈjuzɪŋ jʊr cutlery.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Sometimes – and I know this could be, like, picky for everyone else – but
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: sometimes* –* ənd aɪ noʊ ðɪs kʊd bi, laɪk, ˈpɪki fər ˈɛvriˌwən ɛls –* but

cGJCcidYYaY.text: having a tissue or a napkin on your lap just in case you spill anything. The students think etiquette is important.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: having* ə ˈtɪʃu ər ə ˈnæpkɪn ɔn jʊr læp ʤɪst ɪn keɪs ju spɪl ˈɛniˌθɪŋ. ðə ˈstudənts θɪŋk ˈɛtəkət ɪz important.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Samuel says it's about making a good first impression.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: samuel* sɪz ɪts əˈbaʊt ˈmeɪkɪŋ ə gʊd fərst impression.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Your first impression is the opinion you form about someone when you meet them for the first time.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: your* fərst ˌɪmˈprɛʃən ɪz ðə əˈpɪnjən ju fɔrm əˈbaʊt ˈsəmˌwən wɪn ju mit ðɛm fər ðə fərst time.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: For Aya, eating with your mouth open is definitely rude. She also thinks people should use a napkin,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: for* aya*, ˈitɪŋ wɪθ jʊr maʊθ ˈoʊpən ɪz ˈdɛfənətli rud. ʃi ˈɔlsoʊ θɪŋks ˈpipəl ʃʊd juz ə napkin,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: although that could be seen as picky. Picky here means being too strict about the rules. For this group of students,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: although* ðət kʊd bi sin ɛz ˈpɪki. ˈpɪki hir minz biɪŋ tu strɪkt əˈbaʊt ðə rulz. fər ðɪs grup əv ˈstudənts,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: showing respect and appreciation for the meal is very important, and this idea is common across most, if not all,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: ʃoʊɪŋ rɪˈspɛkt ənd əˌpriʃiˈeɪʃən fər ðə mil ɪz ˈvɛri ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt, ənd ðɪs aɪˈdiə ɪz ˈkɑmən əˈkrɔs moʊst, ɪf nɑt all,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: cultures throughout history. So, imagine a scene from a TV show like Game of Thrones:
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: cultures* θruaʊt ˈhɪstəri. soʊ, ˌɪˈmæʤən ə sin frəm ə ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən ʃoʊ laɪk geɪm əv thrones:

cGJCcidYYaY.text: two enemy forces have stopped fighting, made peace and now gather for a meal.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: two* ˈɛnəmi ˈfɔrsɪz hæv stɑpt ˈfaɪtɪŋ, meɪd pis ənd naʊ ˈgæðər fər ə meal.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: The leaders clink glasses, meaning they lift up and touch drinks,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: the* ˈlidərz klɪŋk ˈglæsɪz, ˈminɪŋ ðeɪ lɪft əp ənd təʧ drinks,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: and make a toast – they hold up a glass of alcohol and drink it to show respect.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: and* meɪk ə toʊst –* ðeɪ hoʊld əp ə glæs əv ˈælkəˌhɑl ənd drɪŋk ɪt tɪ ʃoʊ respect.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Now, keep that image in mind because we're going to hear
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: now*, kip ðət ˈɪmɪʤ ɪn maɪnd bɪˈkəz wɪr goʊɪŋ tɪ hear

cGJCcidYYaY.text: British etiquette coach Rupert Wesson explaining why people clink glasses.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: british* ˈɛtəkət koʊʧ ˈrupərt ˈwɛsən ɪkˈspleɪnɪŋ waɪ ˈpipəl klɪŋk glasses.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Here he is, talking to Ruth Alexander, presenter of BBC World Service programme The Food Chain:
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: here* hi ɪz, ˈtɔkɪŋ tɪ ruθ ˌælɪgˈzændər, ˈprɛzəntər əv ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈproʊˌgræm ðə fud chain:

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Perhaps the simplest example is: Why do we clink glasses when we have a toast?
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: perhaps* ðə ˈsɪmpləst ɪgˈzæmpəl ɪz: waɪ du wi klɪŋk ˈglæsɪz wɪn wi hæv ə toast?

cGJCcidYYaY.text: If you clink glasses, you're slopping a bit of your drink into his cup
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: if* ju klɪŋk ˈglæsɪz, jʊr slopping* ə bɪt əv jʊr drɪŋk ˈɪntu hɪz cup

cGJCcidYYaY.text: and he's slopping a bit of drink into your cup. And therefore, by having that toast you are showing:
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: and* hiz slopping* ə bɪt əv drɪŋk ˈɪntu jʊr kəp. ənd ˈðɛrˌfɔr, baɪ ˈhævɪŋ ðət toʊst ju ər showing:

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Look, I trust you. You trust me. And that's why we do it.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: look*, aɪ trəst ju. ju trəst mi. ənd ðæts waɪ wi du it.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: If you've poisoned my drink that's now slopped into your cup, you won't drink it. Correct.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: if* juv ˈpɔɪzənd maɪ drɪŋk ðæts naʊ slopped* ˈɪntu jʊr kəp, ju woʊnt drɪŋk ɪt. correct.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Hats at the table – we're hearing from BBC World Service listeners that that is a no-no. Is that right?
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: hats* æt ðə ˈteɪbəl –* wɪr ˈhirɪŋ frəm ˌbibiˈsi wərld ˈsərvɪs ˈlɪsənərz ðət ðət ɪz ə no-no*. ɪz ðət raɪt?

cGJCcidYYaY.text: It's not something I would do. In fact, I would not be wearing a hat inside anyway.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: it's* nɑt ˈsəmθɪŋ aɪ wʊd du. ɪn fækt, aɪ wʊd nɑt bi ˈwɛrɪŋ ə hæt ˌɪnˈsaɪd ˈɛniˌweɪ.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: So, Phil, when we clink glasses in a toast, some of my drink might spill into your glass
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: soʊ, fɪl, wɪn wi klɪŋk ˈglæsɪz ɪn ə toʊst, səm əv maɪ drɪŋk maɪt spɪl ˈɪntu jʊr glass

cGJCcidYYaY.text: and some of your drink might spill into mine. Historically, this mixing of drinks was a way of proving the drinks
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: and* səm əv jʊr drɪŋk maɪt spɪl ˈɪntu maɪn. hɪˈstɔrɪkəli, ðɪs ˈmɪksɪŋ əv drɪŋks wɑz ə weɪ əv ˈpruvɪŋ ðə drinks

cGJCcidYYaY.text: were not poisoned. Ruth also asks about wearing hats at the table
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: were* nɑt ˈpɔɪzənd. ruθ ˈɔlsoʊ æsks əˈbaʊt ˈwɛrɪŋ hæts æt ðə table

cGJCcidYYaY.text: and Rupert calls this a no-no. A no-no is an informal phrase
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: and* ˈrupərt kɔlz ðɪs ə no-no*. ə no-no* ɪz ən ˌɪnˈfɔrməl phrase

cGJCcidYYaY.text: for something that is considered socially unacceptable. Etiquette varies a lot over time and culture,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: for* ˈsəmθɪŋ ðət ɪz kənˈsɪdərd ˈsoʊʃəli ˌənækˈsɛptəbəl. ˈɛtəkət ˈvɛriz ə lɔt ˈoʊvər taɪm ənd culture,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: but the essential point is always to show appreciation by doing some things and avoiding others.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: but* ðə ɛˈsɛnʃəl pɔɪnt ɪz ˈɔlˌweɪz tɪ ʃoʊ əˌpriʃiˈeɪʃən baɪ duɪŋ səm θɪŋz ənd əˈvɔɪdɪŋ others.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: And speaking of things to avoid, what was the answer to your question, Phil?
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: and* ˈspikɪŋ əv θɪŋz tɪ əˈvɔɪd, wət wɑz ðə ˈænsər tɪ jʊr kˈwɛʃən, phil?

cGJCcidYYaY.text: OK. Well, I asked you what listeners of the food chain thought was the biggest etiquette problem when people are eating.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: ok*. wɛl, aɪ æst ju wət ˈlɪsənərz əv ðə fud ʧeɪn θɔt wɑz ðə ˈbɪgəst ˈɛtəkət ˈprɑbləm wɪn ˈpipəl ər eating.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: You said that it was people eating noisily and I can reveal... that's the right answer!
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: you* sɛd ðət ɪt wɑz ˈpipəl ˈitɪŋ ˈnɔɪzəli ənd aɪ kən rɪˈvil... ðæts ðə raɪt answer!

cGJCcidYYaY.text: It was people eating noisily. OK. Let's recap the vocabulary we've learned about mealtime dos and don'ts –
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: it* wɑz ˈpipəl ˈitɪŋ ˈnɔɪzəli. ˈoʊˈkeɪ. lɛts ˈriˌkæp ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wiv ˈlərnɪd əˈbaʊt ˈmilˌtaɪm dɔs ənd doʊnts –

cGJCcidYYaY.text: rules about how you should and shouldn't behave. Your first impression of someone is the opinion you form when meeting them
for the first time. The word picky is used to describe someone who is too strict about rules.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: rules* əˈbaʊt haʊ ju ʃʊd ənd ˈʃʊdənt bɪˈheɪv. jʊr fərst ˌɪmˈprɛʃən əv ˈsəmˌwən ɪz ðə əˈpɪnjən ju fɔrm wɪn ˈmitɪŋ them

cGJCcidYYaY.text: If you clink glasses, you touch two drinking glasses together as a gesture of friendship, probably because you're making a toast –
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: for* ðə fərst taɪm. ðə wərd ˈpɪki ɪz juzd tɪ dɪˈskraɪb ˈsəmˌwən hu ɪz tu strɪkt əˈbaʊt rules.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: a show of respect that involves drinking from a glass of alcohol. And finally, a no-no is an informal word
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: if* ju klɪŋk ˈglæsɪz, ju təʧ tu ˈdrɪŋkɪŋ ˈglæsɪz təˈgɛðər ɛz ə ˈʤɛsʧər əv ˈfrɛndʃɪp, ˈprɑbəˌbli bɪˈkəz jʊr ˈmeɪkɪŋ ə toʊst –

cGJCcidYYaY.text: for something considered socially unacceptable. Once again, our six minutes are up,
but if you'd like to find out more about dining etiquette then head over to our website, bbclearningenglish.com,
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: bət ɪf jʊd laɪk tɪ faɪnd aʊt mɔr əˈbaʊt ˈdaɪnɪŋ ˈɛtəkət ðɛn hɛd ˈoʊvər tɪ ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt, bbclearningenglish.com,

cGJCcidYYaY.text: to download a programme worksheet and try the quiz. See you again soon, but for now, it's goodbye.
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: to* ˈdaʊnˌloʊd ə ˈproʊˌgræm ˈwərkˌʃit ənd traɪ ðə kwɪz. si ju əˈgɛn sun, bət fər naʊ, ɪts goodbye.

cGJCcidYYaY.text: Bye!
cGJCcidYYaY.ipa: bye*!

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: My name is Grace Chloe. I am a singer and pianist and songwriter.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: maɪ neɪm ɪz greɪs kloʊi. aɪ æm ə ˈsɪŋər ənd ˈpiənɪst ənd songwriter.

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: I grew up living in Hertfordshire, which is just by London,
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: i* gru əp ˈlɪvɪŋ ɪn hertfordshire*, wɪʧ ɪz ʤɪst baɪ london,

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: but I moved up to Liverpool for university two years ago.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: but* aɪ muvd əp tɪ ˈlɪvərˌpul fər ˌjunəˈvərsəti tu jɪrz ago.

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: Walking around with my head in the clouds. Got my feet on the ground. And no doubts about me.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: walking* əraʊnd wɪθ maɪ hɛd ɪn ðə klaʊdz. gɑt maɪ fit ɔn ðə graʊnd. ənd noʊ daʊts əˈbaʊt me.

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: What we've been through. We can make it through anything. Cos I love you. But you say you're back.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: what* wiv bɪn θru. wi kən meɪk ɪt θru ˈɛniˌθɪŋ. kɔs aɪ ləv ju. bət ju seɪ jʊr back.

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: And I can't quite believe. Your lies in your eyes. Hidden behind the green.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: and* aɪ kænt kwaɪt bɪˈliv. jʊr laɪz ɪn jʊr aɪz. ˈhɪdən bɪˈhaɪnd ðə green.

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: I don't understand what's going on I'm feeling something is wrong. I'm lost with you, babe.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: i* doʊnt ˌəndərˈstænd wəts goʊɪŋ ɔn əm ˈfilɪŋ ˈsəmθɪŋ ɪz rɔŋ. əm lɔst wɪθ ju, babe.

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: Don't keep saying we're great. It's time we did some straight up talking. I need some understanding.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: don't* kip seɪɪŋ wɪr greɪt. ɪts taɪm wi dɪd səm streɪt əp ˈtɔkɪŋ. aɪ nid səm understanding.

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: Don't know where we're standing. Don't know where we're at. And I can't get no answers from you.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: don't* noʊ wɛr wɪr ˈstændɪŋ. doʊnt noʊ wɛr wɪr æt. ənd aɪ kænt gɪt noʊ ˈænsərz frəm you.

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: No, you know it's you and I don't know you.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: no*, ju noʊ ɪts ju ənd aɪ doʊnt noʊ you.

DVWWWrVFTrI.text: And you say two. I'm really missing the subtext. Got me perplexed.
DVWWWrVFTrI.ipa: and* ju seɪ tu. əm ˈrɪli ˈmɪsɪŋ ðə ˈsəbˌtɛkst. gɑt mi perplexed.

n9sAY_fnclI.text: This morning, I went to the shops and I bought some bread making flour, and this is yeast.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈɪs mˈɔɹnɪŋ ˈaɪ wˈɛnt tˈu ðə ʃˈɑps ənd ˈaɪ bˈɑt sˈʌm bɹˈɛd mˈeɪkɪŋ flˈaʊɚ ənd ðˈɪs ˈɪz jˈist/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Yeast is the stuff that makes bread rise.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /jˈist ˈɪz ðə stˈʌf ðˈæt mˈeɪks bɹˈɛd ɹˈaɪz/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: We're going to make Well, we're going to try and make a loaf of bread.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /wˈiɹ ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu mˈeɪk wˈɛl wˈiɹ ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu tɹˈaɪ ənd mˈeɪk ə lˈoʊf ˈʌv bɹˈɛd/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: That is the hobby.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈæt ˈɪz ðə hˈɑbi/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: The hobby is baking.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðə hˈɑbi ˈɪz bˈeɪkɪŋ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: So, we need to mix 500 g of strong white flour, 2 Tbsp Oh, tsp.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /sˈoʊ wˈi nˈid tˈu mˈɪks 500 dʒˈi ˈʌv stɹˈɔŋ wˈaɪt flˈaʊɚ 2 tbsp ˈoʊ tsp/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: 2 tsp of salt and a 7-g sachet of fast-action yeast in a large bowl.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /2 tsp ˈʌv sˈɔlt ənd ə 7g sæʃˈeɪ ˈʌv fastaction jˈist ɪn ə lˈɑɹdʒ bˈoʊl/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: It then says, "Make a well in the center."
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈɪt ðˈɛn sˈɛz mˈeɪk ə wˈɛl ɪn ðə sˈɛntɚ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Like this.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /lˈaɪk ðˈɪs/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Then, we need to add 3 Tbsp of olive oil and some water and mix again.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈɛn wˈi nˈid tˈu ˈæd 3 tbsp ˈʌv ˈɑləv ˈɔɪl ənd sˈʌm wˈɔtɚ ənd mˈɪks əɡˈɛn/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: What I'm going to do now is I'm going to knead the bread.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /wˈʌt ˈaɪm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu dˈu nˈaʊ ˈɪz ˈaɪm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu nˈid ðə bɹˈɛd/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Silent K.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /sˈaɪlənt kˈeɪ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: So, this action here, this is kneading.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /sˈoʊ ðˈɪs ˈækʃən hˈiɹ ðˈɪs ˈɪz nˈidɪŋ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Kneading bread.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /nˈidɪŋ bɹˈɛd/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: But, with a K.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /bˈʌt wˈɪð ə kˈeɪ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Silent K.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /sˈaɪlənt kˈeɪ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I need to do this for 10 minutes.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ nˈid tˈu dˈu ðˈɪs fˈɔɹ 10 mˈɪnəts/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: So, Alexa, put a timer on for 10 minutes.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /sˈoʊ əlˈɛksə pˈʊt ə tˈaɪmɚ ˈɑn fˈɔɹ 10 mˈɪnəts/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Alexa, stop.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /əlˈɛksə stˈɑp/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Okay.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˌoʊkˈeɪ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Why am I out of breath?
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /wˈaɪ ˈæm ˈaɪ ˈaʊt ˈʌv bɹˈɛθ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Get a grip.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ɡˈɛt ə ɡɹˈɪp/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Put it in a bowl and cover it with cling film and basically just leave it.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /pˈʊt ˈɪt ɪn ə bˈoʊl ənd kˈʌvɚ ˈɪt wˈɪð klˈɪŋ fˈɪlm ənd bˈeɪsɪkli dʒˈʌst lˈiv ˈɪt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Leave it for 1 hour.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /lˈiv ˈɪt fˈɔɹ 1 ˈaʊɚ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Literally, look.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /lˈɪtɚəli lˈʊk/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: It's massive.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈɪts mˈæsɪv/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: It's huge.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈɪts hjˈudʒ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: It's enormous.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈɪts ɪnˈɔɹməs/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: The bread has sat and proven.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðə bɹˈɛd hˈæz sˈæt ənd pɹˈuvən/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: That's a word you'll never use.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈæts ə wˈɝd jˈul nˈɛvɚ jˈus/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Unless you're a baker, maybe.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ənlˈɛs jˈʊɹ ə bˈeɪkɚ mˈeɪbi/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: What that means is it's now It's twice the size.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /wˈʌt ðˈæt mˈinz ˈɪz ˈɪts nˈaʊ ˈɪts twˈaɪs ðə sˈaɪz/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I'm now going to dust it with a little bit more flour.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪm nˈaʊ ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu dˈʌst ˈɪt wˈɪð ə lˈɪtəl bˈɪt mˈɔɹ flˈaʊɚ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: With a sharp knife, I'm going to score it.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /wˈɪð ə ʃˈɑɹp nˈaɪf ˈaɪm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu skˈɔɹ ˈɪt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: And now, we put this in the oven for 25 to 30 minutes.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ənd nˈaʊ wˈi pˈʊt ðˈɪs ɪn ðə ˈʌvən fˈɔɹ 25 tˈu 30 mˈɪnəts/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: If you tap on it and it sounds hollow,
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈɪf jˈu tˈæp ˈɑn ˈɪt ənd ˈɪt sˈaʊndz hˈɑloʊ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: then it's then it's done.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈɛn ˈɪts ðˈɛn ˈɪts dˈʌn/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: That's like the best loaf of bread ever.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈæts lˈaɪk ðə bˈɛst lˈoʊf ˈʌv bɹˈɛd ˈɛvɚ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I can't believe I did that.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ kˈænt bɪlˈiv ˈaɪ dˈɪd ðˈæt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Guys, I'm actually quite shocked at how good it looks.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ɡˈaɪz ˈaɪm ˈæktʃˌuəli kwˈaɪt ʃˈɑkt ˈæt hˈaʊ ɡˈʊd ˈɪt lˈʊks/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Look at this bread.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /lˈʊk ˈæt ðˈɪs bɹˈɛd/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: bread Okay, I'm going to chop
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /bɹˈɛd ˌoʊkˈeɪ ˈaɪm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu tʃˈɑp/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: a piece off the end and I'm going to have it with some butter.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ə pˈis ˈɔf ðə ˈɛnd ənd ˈaɪm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu hˈæv ˈɪt wˈɪð sˈʌm bˈʌtɚ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: It's still warm.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈɪts stˈɪl wˈɔɹm/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: This is the moment of truth.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈɪs ˈɪz ðə mˈoʊmənt ˈʌv tɹˈuθ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: That might be the best
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈæt mˈaɪt bˈi ðə bˈɛst/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I've ever eaten in my life.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪv ˈɛvɚ ˈitən ɪn mˈaɪ lˈaɪf/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Before I literally pig out
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /bɪfˈɔɹ ˈaɪ lˈɪtɚəli pˈɪɡ ˈaʊt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: pig out and eat this whole loaf of bread, do I think this is a good hobby?
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /pˈɪɡ ˈaʊt ənd ˈit ðˈɪs hˈoʊl lˈoʊf ˈʌv bɹˈɛd dˈu ˈaɪ θˈɪŋk ðˈɪs ˈɪz ə ɡˈʊd hˈɑbi/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Yes, I do.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /jˈɛs ˈaɪ dˈu/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Mostly because you get bread at the end
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /mˈoʊstli bɪkˈɔz jˈu ɡˈɛt bɹˈɛd ˈæt ðə ˈɛnd/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: of it.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈʌv ˈɪt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I don't know if I enjoyed the kneading very much, but honestly, this was so easy to make and it's delicious, like 10 out of 10 delicious, so
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ dˈoʊnt nˈoʊ ˈɪf ˈaɪ ˌɛndʒˈɔɪd ðə nˈidɪŋ vˈɛɹi mˈʌtʃ bˈʌt ˈɑnəstli ðˈɪs wˈɑz sˈoʊ ˈizi tˈu mˈeɪk ənd ˈɪts dɪlˈɪʃəs lˈaɪk 10 ˈaʊt ˈʌv 10 dɪlˈɪʃəs sˈoʊ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I would do this again, for sure.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ wˈʊd dˈu ðˈɪs əɡˈɛn fˈɔɹ ʃˈʊɹ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Hi guys.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /hˈaɪ ɡˈaɪz/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Today is Saturday.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /tədˈeɪ ˈɪz sˈætɚdi/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: It's Saturday.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈɪts sˈætɚdi/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: It's Saturday today.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈɪts sˈætɚdi tədˈeɪ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I try and be busy on my weekends, but
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ tɹˈaɪ ənd bˈi bˈɪzi ˈɑn mˈaɪ wˈikˌɛndz bˈʌt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I think often I still lose a lot of time to my phone.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ θˈɪŋk ˈɔfən ˈaɪ stˈɪl lˈuz ə lˈɑt ˈʌv tˈaɪm tˈu mˈaɪ fˈoʊn/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I don't think this will be a surprise to anyone that I really enjoy language learning.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ dˈoʊnt θˈɪŋk ðˈɪs wˈɪl bˈi ə sɚpɹˈaɪz tˈu ˈɛniwˌʌn ðˈæt ˈaɪ ɹˈɪli ˌɛndʒˈɔɪ lˈæŋɡwədʒ lˈɝnɪŋ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I love talking to people who are learning English.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ lˈʌv tˈɔkɪŋ tˈu pˈipəl hˈu ˈɑɹ lˈɝnɪŋ ˈɪŋɡlɪʃ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I love learning languages myself.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ lˈʌv lˈɝnɪŋ lˈæŋɡwədʒəz mˌaɪsˈɛlf/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: For my next hobby, I am going to try and learn a new language.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /fˈɔɹ mˈaɪ nˈɛkst hˈɑbi ˈaɪ ˈæm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu tɹˈaɪ ənd lˈɝn ə nˈu lˈæŋɡwədʒ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I didn't mention this, but a few weeks ago, I started learning Korean.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ dˈɪdənt mˈɛnʃən ðˈɪs bˈʌt ə fjˈu wˈiks əɡˈoʊ ˈaɪ stˈɑɹtɪd lˈɝnɪŋ kˌɔɹˈiən/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: And every every single Korean person I know that I have said, "Oh, I'm thinking about learning Korean."
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ənd ˈɛvɚi ˈɛvɚi sˈɪŋɡəl kˌɔɹˈiən pˈɝsən ˈaɪ nˈoʊ ðˈæt ˈaɪ hˈæv sˈɛd ˈoʊ ˈaɪm θˈɪŋkɪŋ əbˈaʊt lˈɝnɪŋ kˌɔɹˈiən/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: They have said to me, "Why?"
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈeɪ hˈæv sˈɛd tˈu mˈi wˈaɪ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Like, why?
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /lˈaɪk wˈaɪ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: They say why or don't bother is what they say.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈeɪ sˈeɪ wˈaɪ ˈɔɹ dˈoʊnt bˈɑðɚ ˈɪz wˈʌt ðˈeɪ sˈeɪ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Um The other day I basically I just learned the alphabet.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈʌm ðə ˈʌðɚ dˈeɪ ˈaɪ bˈeɪsɪkli ˈaɪ dʒˈʌst lˈɝnd ðə ˈælfəbˌɛt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: So I can show you.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /sˈoʊ ˈaɪ kˈæn ʃˈoʊ jˈu/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: This is me trying to learn the Korean alphabet.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈɪs ˈɪz mˈi tɹˈaɪɪŋ tˈu lˈɝn ðə kˌɔɹˈiən ˈælfəbˌɛt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: It's actually it's kind of hard.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈɪts ˈæktʃˌuəli ˈɪts kˈaɪnd ˈʌv hˈɑɹd/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I have found some videos of comprehensible input in Korean.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ hˈæv fˈaʊnd sˈʌm vˈɪdioʊz ˈʌv kˌɑmpɹihˈɛnsəbəl ˈɪnpˌʊt ɪn kˌɔɹˈiən/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: So, these are videos that even though I do not understand anything I can understand by looking at the pictures on the screen.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /sˈoʊ ðˈiz ˈɑɹ vˈɪdioʊz ðˈæt ˈivɪn ðˈoʊ ˈaɪ dˈu nˈɑt ˌʌndɚstˈænd ˈɛniθˌɪŋ ˈaɪ kˈæn ˌʌndɚstˈænd bˈaɪ lˈʊkɪŋ ˈæt ðə pˈɪktʃɚz ˈɑn ðə skɹˈin/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Um and I'm going to I'm going to watch that video today.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈʌm ənd ˈaɪm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu ˈaɪm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu wˈɑtʃ ðˈæt vˈɪdioʊ tədˈeɪ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: This video here by this lovely this lovely young man.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈɪs vˈɪdioʊ hˈiɹ bˈaɪ ðˈɪs lˈʌvli ðˈɪs lˈʌvli jˈʌŋ mˈæn/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: He plays Sudoku Sudoku.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /hˈi plˈeɪz sudoku sudoku/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: What?
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /wˈʌt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: While I'm here I'm drinking
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /wˈaɪl ˈaɪm hˈiɹ ˈaɪm dɹˈɪŋkɪŋ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: you already know an iced an iced latte with caramel.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /jˈu ɔlɹˈɛdi nˈoʊ ˈæn ˈaɪst ˈæn ˈaɪst lˈɑtˌeɪ wˈɪð kˈɛɹəməl/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: And I have I have a little muffin as well.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ənd ˈaɪ hˈæv ˈaɪ hˈæv ə lˈɪtəl mˈʌfən ˈæz wˈɛl/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I I just finished the video.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ ˈaɪ dʒˈʌst fˈɪnɪʃt ðə vˈɪdioʊ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I did I put a jumper on because it's kind of cold in here.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ dˈɪd ˈaɪ pˈʊt ə dʒˈʌmpɚ ˈɑn bɪkˈɔz ˈɪts kˈaɪnd ˈʌv kˈoʊld ɪn hˈiɹ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I think I learned I think I learned quite a bit.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪ θˈɪŋk ˈaɪ lˈɝnd ˈaɪ θˈɪŋk ˈaɪ lˈɝnd kwˈaɪt ə bˈɪt/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Now now I've learned these new words, I want to try and actually remember them.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /nˈaʊ nˈaʊ ˈaɪv lˈɝnd ðˈiz nˈu wˈɝdz ˈaɪ wˈɑnt tˈu tɹˈaɪ ənd ˈæktʃˌuəli ɹɪmˈɛmbɚ ðˈɛm/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: I'm going to write them down in my notebook, and I'm going to use Ling, who are the sponsor of this video.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ˈaɪm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu ɹˈaɪt ðˈɛm dˈaʊn ɪn mˈaɪ nˈoʊtbˌʊk ənd ˈaɪm ɡˈoʊɪŋ tˈu jˈus lˈɪŋ hˈu ˈɑɹ ðə spˈɑnsɚ ˈʌv ðˈɪs vˈɪdioʊ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Ling is a language learning platform that lets you learn from real content that you actually enjoy, not just like textbook exercises.

n9sAY_fnclI.text: So, for example, I can import the Korean video that I just watched, and Ling will create a full transcript for me.
