n9sAY_fnclI.text: Then, I can save the new vocabulary, the new words, and I can track them and go over them again with flashcards and other exercises on
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /ðˈɛn ˈaɪ kˈæn sˈeɪv ðə nˈu voʊkˈæbjəlˌɛɹi ðə nˈu wˈɝdz ənd ˈaɪ kˈæn tɹˈæk ðˈɛm ənd ɡˈoʊ ˈoʊvɚ ðˈɛm əɡˈɛn wˈɪð flˈæʃkˌɑɹdz ənd ˈʌðɚ ˈɛksɚsˌaɪzəz ˈɑn/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: Ling.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /lˈɪŋ/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: You can use Ling to study English from YouTube or Netflix or TikTok.
n9sAY_fnclI.ipa: /jˈu kˈæn jˈus lˈɪŋ tˈu stˈʌdi ˈɪŋɡlɪʃ fɹˈʌm jˈutjˌub ˈɔɹ nˈɛtflɪks ˈɔɹ tiktok/

n9sAY_fnclI.text: You can then view a transcript of the video.

BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: so*, fər ɪgˈzæmpəl, ju sɛd ðə fɪlm ɪz əˈbaʊt ə boy

BSABmr8hX7s.text: who discovers that he is a wizard and he goes to a school,
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: who* dɪˈskəvərz ðət hi ɪz ə ˈwɪzərd ənd hi goʊz tɪ ə school,

BSABmr8hX7s.text: a special school for wizards, and he makes friends with Ron and Hermione.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: a* ˈspɛʃəl skul fər ˈwɪzərdz, ənd hi meɪks frɛndz wɪθ rɑn ənd hermione.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: All present simple. Yes, that's right.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: all* ˈprɛzənt ˈsɪmpəl. jɛs, ðæts right.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: We use the present simple to describe the plot of a story like a book or a film.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: we* juz ðə ˈprɛzənt ˈsɪmpəl tɪ dɪˈskraɪb ðə plɑt əv ə ˈstɔri laɪk ə bʊk ər ə film.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: We also use the present simple to tell stories sometimes, things that happened to us in the past, which is interesting.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: we* ˈɔlsoʊ juz ðə ˈprɛzənt ˈsɪmpəl tɪ tɛl ˈstɔriz ˈsəmˌtaɪmz, θɪŋz ðət ˈhæpənd tɪ ˈjuˈɛs ɪn ðə pæst, wɪʧ ɪz ˈɪntəˌrɛstɪŋ.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: What about you? What's your favourite film?
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: what* əˈbaʊt ju? wəts jʊr ˈfeɪvərɪt film?

BSABmr8hX7s.text: Well, I think my favourite film is from when I was a kid,
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: well*, aɪ θɪŋk maɪ ˈfeɪvərɪt fɪlm ɪz frəm wɪn aɪ wɑz ə kid,

BSABmr8hX7s.text: and it's Raiders Of The Lost Ark. Wow. I haven't seen that.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: and* ɪts ˈreɪdərz əv ðə lɔst ɑrk. waʊ. aɪ ˈhævənt sin that.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: It's very old, but it's got Indiana Jones, and he was my hero when I was a kid. — Really? — Yeah.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: it's* ˈvɛri oʊld, bət ɪts gɑt ˌɪndiˈænə ʤoʊnz, ənd hi wɑz maɪ ˈhɪroʊ wɪn aɪ wɑz ə kɪd. —* ˈrɪli? —* yeah.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: Why was he your hero? Because it was, he's all about action and adventure and catching bad guys.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: why* wɑz hi jʊr ˈhɪroʊ? bɪˈkəz ɪt wɑz, hiz ɔl əˈbaʊt ˈækʃən ənd ədˈvɛnʧər ənd ˈkæʧɪŋ bæd guys.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: Oh, interesting! See, I'm not a big fan of action and adventure.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: oh*, ˈɪntəˌrɛstɪŋ! si, əm nɑt ə bɪg fæn əv ˈækʃən ənd adventure.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: So, Georgie, do you watch lots of movies?
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: so*, ˈʤɔrʤi, du ju wɔʧ lɑts əv movies?

BSABmr8hX7s.text: I actually don't, because I have quite a short attention span.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: i* ˈæˌkʧuəli doʊnt, bɪˈkəz aɪ hæv kwaɪt ə ʃɔrt əˈtɛnʃən span.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: I actually prefer to watch TV or series. What about you?
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: i* ˈæˌkʧuəli prɪˈfər tɪ wɔʧ ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən ər ˈsɪriz. wət əˈbaʊt you?

BSABmr8hX7s.text: Yeah, again, I think there's so much choice these days,
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: yeah*, əˈgɛn, aɪ θɪŋk ðɛrz soʊ məʧ ʧɔɪs ðiz days,

BSABmr8hX7s.text: sometimes actually I watch nothing, because I don't know what to choose.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: sometimes* ˈæˌkʧuəli aɪ wɔʧ ˈnəθɪŋ, bɪˈkəz aɪ doʊnt noʊ wət tɪ choose.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: But sometimes, I really get into a TV series
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: but* ˈsəmˌtaɪmz, aɪ ˈrɪli gɪt ˈɪntu ə ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən series

BSABmr8hX7s.text: and, yeah, watch it all very quickly and talk about it a lot.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: and*, jæ, wɔʧ ɪt ɔl ˈvɛri kˈwɪkli ənd tɔk əˈbaʊt ɪt ə lot.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: — Mm-hm. That's called binge watching. — Mm. How do you watch these series?
Erm, on TV. But, you know, I stream them, like everybody these days.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: erm*, ɔn ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən. bət, ju noʊ, aɪ strim ðɛm, laɪk ˈɛvriˌbɑdi ðiz deɪz.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: So, you just used the word stream. You 'stream' TV shows and movies
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: soʊ, ju ʤɪst juzd ðə wərd strim. ju 'strim' ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən ʃoʊz ənd movies

BSABmr8hX7s.text: and that means 'watching films or TV using the internet'.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: and* ðət minz 'ˈwɑʧɪŋ fɪlmz ər ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən ˈjuzɪŋ ðə internet'.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: You can watch things whenever you want when you stream things.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: you* kən wɔʧ θɪŋz wɛˈnɛvər ju wɔnt wɪn ju strim things.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: OK. Let's look again at the vocabulary we learned during the conversation. We learned 'plot',
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: ok*. lɛts lʊk əˈgɛn æt ðə voʊˈkæbjəˌlɛri wi ˈlərnɪd ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən. wi ˈlərnɪd 'plot',

BSABmr8hX7s.text: which means 'the story of a film, TV programme or book', so what happens.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: which* minz 'ðə ˈstɔri əv ə fɪlm, ˌtɛləˈvɪʒən ˈproʊˌgræm ər bʊk', soʊ wət happens.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: 'Character', that's 'a person in a story'. And 'stream' — 'watch a film or programme online'.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: 'character*', ðæts 'ə ˈpərsən ɪn ə ˈstɔri'. ənd 'strim' —* 'wɔʧ ə fɪlm ər ˈproʊˌgræm online'.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: And we learned how to use present simple to talk about what happens in a film.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: and* wi ˈlərnɪd haʊ tɪ juz ˈprɛzənt ˈsɪmpəl tɪ tɔk əˈbaʊt wət ˈhæpənz ɪn ə film.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: For example, Harry Potter goes to a magic school called Hogwarts and meets two best friends.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: for* ɪgˈzæmpəl, ˈhɛri ˈpɑtər goʊz tɪ ə ˈmæʤɪk skul kɔld ˈhɑgˌwɔrts ənd mits tu bɛst friends.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: Thanks for listening to Real Easy English. You
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: thanks* fər ˈlɪsənɪŋ tɪ ril ˈizi ˈɪŋlɪʃ. you

BSABmr8hX7s.text: can find more easy conversation on our website: BBC Learning English dot com.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: can* faɪnd mɔr ˈizi ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən ɔn ɑr ˈwɛbˌsaɪt: ˌbibiˈsi ˈlərnɪŋ ˈɪŋlɪʃ dɑt com.

BSABmr8hX7s.text: Next time, we'll be talking about pets. Bye for now. Bye.
BSABmr8hX7s.ipa: next* taɪm, wɪl bi ˈtɔkɪŋ əˈbaʊt pɛts. baɪ fər naʊ. baɪ.

7wK4peez9zE.text: You might be unkillable, impossible to delete out of existence – if one of the most exciting
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: you* maɪt bi unkillable*, ˌɪmˈpɑsəbəl tɪ dɪˈlit aʊt əv ɪgˈzɪstəns –* ɪf wən əv ðə moʊst exciting

7wK4peez9zE.text: ideas about physics, quantum immortality, is true. To test this idea, we will put a nuclear
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: ideas* əˈbaʊt ˈfɪzɪks, kˈwɑntəm ˌɪmɔrˈtælɪti, ɪz tru. tɪ tɛst ðɪs aɪˈdiə, wi wɪl pʊt ə nuclear

7wK4peez9zE.text: bomb in your living room. But more on that later. This is real science happening around
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: bomb* ɪn jʊr ˈlɪvɪŋ rum. bət mɔr ɔn ðət ˈleɪtər. ðɪs ɪz ril saɪəns ˈhæpənɪŋ around

7wK4peez9zE.text: one of the most successful theories of physics. To understand why, we need to tell you a story.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: one* əv ðə moʊst səkˈsɛsfəl ˈθɪriz əv ˈfɪzɪks. tɪ ˌəndərˈstænd waɪ, wi nid tɪ tɛl ju ə story.

7wK4peez9zE.text: The story of the smallest parts in the universe, atoms and fundamental particles.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: the* ˈstɔri əv ðə sˈmɔləst pɑrts ɪn ðə ˈjunəˌvərs, ˈætəmz ənd ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl particles.

7wK4peez9zE.text: With everyday objects things intuitively make sense – if you kick a marble ten
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: with* ˈɛvriˈdeɪ ˈɑbʤɛkts θɪŋz ˌɪnˈtuɪtɪvli meɪk sɛns –* ɪf ju kɪk ə ˈmɑrbəl ten

7wK4peez9zE.text: times with the same force and at the same angle, it always lands in the same spot.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: times* wɪθ ðə seɪm fɔrs ənd æt ðə seɪm ˈæŋgəl, ɪt ˈɔlˌweɪz lændz ɪn ðə seɪm spot.

7wK4peez9zE.text: But if you kick an electron ten times exactly the same way, it shows up in different places.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: but* ɪf ju kɪk ən ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn tɛn taɪmz ɪgˈzæktli ðə seɪm weɪ, ɪt ʃoʊz əp ɪn ˈdɪfərənt places.

7wK4peez9zE.text: On the most fundamental level of reality, randomness is the rule.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: on* ðə moʊst ˌfəndəˈmɛnəl ˈlɛvəl əv ˌriˈæləˌti, ˈrændəmnəs ɪz ðə rule.

7wK4peez9zE.text: The story that solves this is called quantum mechanics. Simplified it goes something like this:
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: the* ˈstɔri ðət sɑlvz ðɪs ɪz kɔld kˈwɑntəm məˈkænɪks. ˈsɪmpləˌfaɪd ɪt goʊz ˈsəmθɪŋ laɪk this:

7wK4peez9zE.text: A particle like an electron is not like a tiny marble but like a shapeshifter – a diffuse thing
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: a* ˈpɑrtɪkəl laɪk ən ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ɪz nɑt laɪk ə ˈtaɪni ˈmɑrbəl bət laɪk ə shapeshifter* –* ə dɪfˈjuz thing

7wK4peez9zE.text: we call a wave, that ebbs and flows. But to make it much weirder: It’s not a wave of matter or energy,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: we* kɔl ə weɪv, ðət ɛbz ənd floʊz. bət tɪ meɪk ɪt məʧ ˈwɪrdər: it’s* nɑt ə weɪv əv ˈmætər ər energy,

7wK4peez9zE.text: but a wave of probability – an immaterial essence that has values between 0% and 100%.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: but* ə weɪv əv ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti –* ən ˌɪməˈtɪriəl ˈɛsəns ðət həz ˈvæljuz bɪtˈwin 0 ənd 100%.

7wK4peez9zE.text: What does this even mean?
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: what* dɪz ðɪs ˈivɪn min?

7wK4peez9zE.text: Quantum mechanics doesn't really tell us much about the electron.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: kˈwɑntəm məˈkænɪks ˈdəzənt ˈrɪli tɛl ˈjuˈɛs məʧ əˈbaʊt ðə electron.

7wK4peez9zE.text: It only tells us how this entity that we call an electron behaves on average.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: it* ˈoʊnli tɛlz ˈjuˈɛs haʊ ðɪs ˈɛntɪti ðət wi kɔl ən ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn bɪˈheɪvz ɔn average.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Say you shoot an electron gun at your apartment. You shoot it 100 times –
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: say* ju ʃut ən ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn gən æt jʊr əˈpɑrtmənt. ju ʃut ɪt 100 taɪmz –

7wK4peez9zE.text: 80 times the electron shows up in your living room, and 20 times in your kitchen.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: 80* taɪmz ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ʃoʊz əp ɪn jʊr ˈlɪvɪŋ rum, ənd 20 taɪmz ɪn jʊr kitchen.

7wK4peez9zE.text: So the probability wave of our electron is 80% in the living room and 20% in the kitchen.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: so* ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti weɪv əv ɑr ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ɪz 80 ɪn ðə ˈlɪvɪŋ rum ənd 20 ɪn ðə kitchen.

7wK4peez9zE.text: What is deeply annoying is that this story of probability waves works insanely well in reality.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: what* ɪz ˈdipli əˈnɔɪɪŋ ɪz ðət ðɪs ˈstɔri əv ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti weɪvz wərks insanely* wɛl ɪn reality.

7wK4peez9zE.text: It's not just some brainchild that only works on paper.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: it's* nɑt ʤɪst səm ˈbreɪnˌʧaɪld ðət ˈoʊnli wərks ɔn paper.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Scientists have tested it in countless experiments and it works every single time.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: scientists* hæv ˈtɛstɪd ɪt ɪn ˈkaʊntləs ɪkˈspɛrəmənts ənd ɪt wərks ˈɛvəri ˈsɪŋgəl time.

7wK4peez9zE.text: It explains how a lot of real things work, from information passing through
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: it* ɪkˈspleɪnz haʊ ə lɔt əv ril θɪŋz wərk, frəm ˌɪnˌfɔrˈmeɪʃən ˈpæsɪŋ through

7wK4peez9zE.text: microchips to the atoms fusing in the center of stars. And yet how can this story make sense?
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: microchips* tɪ ðə ˈætəmz fˈjuzɪŋ ɪn ðə ˈsɛnər əv stɑrz. ənd jɛt haʊ kən ðɪs ˈstɔri meɪk sense?

7wK4peez9zE.text: All of our other scientific stories give us a mental picture of the things they are describing –
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: all* əv ɑr ˈəðər ˌsaɪənˈtɪfɪk ˈstɔriz gɪv ˈjuˈɛs ə ˈmɛntəl ˈpɪkʧər əv ðə θɪŋz ðeɪ ər dɪˈskraɪbɪŋ –

7wK4peez9zE.text: a sphere of iron at the center of earth, an elastic fabric of space.
But a wave of probability? What’s that even supposed to look like?
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: a* sfɪr əv aɪərn æt ðə ˈsɛnər əv ərθ, ən ɪˈlæstɪk ˈfæbrɪk əv space.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Quantum mechanics doesn’t tell us anything about the electron itself – what it looks like, how it moves, or how it tastes.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: but* ə weɪv əv ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti? what’s* ðət ˈivɪn səˈpoʊzd tɪ lʊk like?

7wK4peez9zE.text: But if our story doesn’t describe the electrons themselves, what does it describe and how does it potentially make you immortal?
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: quantum* məˈkænɪks doesn’t* tɛl ˈjuˈɛs ˈɛniˌθɪŋ əˈbaʊt ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ˌɪtˈsɛlf –* wət ɪt lʊks laɪk, haʊ ɪt muvz, ər haʊ ɪt tastes.

7wK4peez9zE.text: What Is the Quantum Story Really About?
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: wət ɪz ðə kˈwɑntəm ˈstɔri ˈrɪli about?

7wK4peez9zE.text: Scientists have been debating this for a century. They’ve come up with tons of ideas
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: scientists* hæv bɪn dəˈbeɪtɪŋ ðɪs fər ə ˈsɛnʧəri. they’ve* kəm əp wɪθ tənz əv ideas

7wK4peez9zE.text: we can’t cover here, but there are two popular schools of thought. We’ll call them linguists and literary critics.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: we* can’t* ˈkəvər hir, bət ðɛr ər tu ˈpɑpjələr skulz əv θɔt. we’ll* kɔl ðɛm ˈlɪŋgwɪsts ənd ˈlɪtərˌɛri critics.

7wK4peez9zE.text: They are screaming and arguing over what the quantum story really means.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: they* ər ˈskrimɪŋ ənd ˈɑrgjuɪŋ ˈoʊvər wət ðə kˈwɑntəm ˈstɔri ˈrɪli means.

7wK4peez9zE.text: The position of the linguists is “shut up and calculate”. They basically claim that quantum
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: the* pəˈzɪʃən əv ðə ˈlɪŋgwɪsts ɪz ““shut* əp ənd calculate”*”. ðeɪ ˈbeɪsɪkli kleɪm ðət quantum

7wK4peez9zE.text: mechanics is not a story about reality, but just a kind of grammar for the theoretical
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: mechanics* ɪz nɑt ə ˈstɔri əˈbaʊt ˌriˈæləˌti, bət ʤɪst ə kaɪnd əv ˈgræmər fər ðə theoretical

7wK4peez9zE.text: language of the universe. With this language we can predict experiments. Nothing else.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: language* əv ðə ˈjunəˌvərs. wɪθ ðɪs ˈlæŋgwɪʤ wi kən prɪˈdɪkt ɪkˈspɛrəmənts. ˈnəθɪŋ else.

7wK4peez9zE.text: If there is no story, there is no point in asking what the story says about the electron.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: if* ðɛr ɪz noʊ ˈstɔri, ðɛr ɪz noʊ pɔɪnt ɪn ˈæskɪŋ wət ðə ˈstɔri sɪz əˈbaʊt ðə electron.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Whether electrons are tiny marbles or clouds, waves of negative charge or Detective
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: whether* ˌɪˈlɛktrɑnz ər ˈtaɪni ˈmɑrbəlz ər klaʊdz, weɪvz əv ˈnɛgətɪv ʧɑrʤ ər detective

7wK4peez9zE.text: Pikachu is missing the point. Quantum mechanics doesn’t enable you to visualize any electrons.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: pikachu* ɪz ˈmɪsɪŋ ðə pɔɪnt. kˈwɑntəm məˈkænɪks doesn’t* ɪˈneɪbəl ju tɪ ˈvɪʒwəˌlaɪz ˈɛni electrons.

7wK4peez9zE.text: All you can do is to run experiments. And physics is all about experiments,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: all* ju kən du ɪz tɪ rən ɪkˈspɛrəmənts. ənd ˈfɪzɪks ɪz ɔl əˈbaʊt experiments,

7wK4peez9zE.text: not about creating mental pictures for us. So shut up, stop imagining fairy tales,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: not* əˈbaʊt kriˈeɪtɪŋ ˈmɛntəl ˈpɪkʧərz fər ˈjuˈɛs. soʊ ʃət əp, stɑp ˌɪˈmæʤənɪŋ ˈfɛri tales,

7wK4peez9zE.text: and just use the probability wave to calculate things. Follow the grammar, ignore the plot.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* ʤɪst juz ðə ˌprɑbəˈbɪləˌti weɪv tɪ ˈkælkjəˌleɪt θɪŋz. ˈfɑloʊ ðə ˈgræmər, ˌɪgˈnɔr ðə plot.

7wK4peez9zE.text: “Nooooo!” scream the literary critics: Read between the lines, there’s a hidden meaning here!
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: “nooooo!”*!” skrim ðə ˈlɪtərˌɛri ˈkrɪtɪks: rɛd bɪtˈwin ðə laɪnz, there’s* ə ˈhɪdən ˈminɪŋ here!

7wK4peez9zE.text: They are “many-worldians” and they are convinced that quantum mechanics
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: they* ər ““many-worldians”*” ənd ðeɪ ər kənˈvɪnst ðət kˈwɑntəm məˈkænɪks

7wK4peez9zE.text: is a story about reality. And they want to interpret the story.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: ɪz ə ˈstɔri əˈbaʊt ˌriˈæləˌti. ənd ðeɪ wɔnt tɪ ˌɪnˈtərprət ðə story.

7wK4peez9zE.text: The many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics is not the same as the multiverse by the way,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: the* many-worlds* ˌɪnˌtərprɪˈteɪʃən əv kˈwɑntəm məˈkænɪks ɪz nɑt ðə seɪm ɛz ðə multiverse* baɪ ðə way,

7wK4peez9zE.text: which is a whole different can of worms, but we’ll get to that another time.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: which* ɪz ə hoʊl ˈdɪfərənt kən əv wərmz, bət we’ll* gɪt tɪ ðət əˈnəðər time.

7wK4peez9zE.text: The many-worldians think that the universe is an infinitely complex quantum state,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: the* many-worldians* θɪŋk ðət ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ɪz ən ˈɪnfənətli ˈkɑmplɛks kˈwɑntəm state,

7wK4peez9zE.text: where all physically possible outcomes coexist simultaneously. If the probability
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: where* ɔl ˈfɪzɪkəli ˈpɑsəbəl ˈaʊtˌkəmz ˌkoʊəgˈzɪst ˌsaɪməlˈteɪniəsli. ɪf ðə probability

7wK4peez9zE.text: wave of your electron is split 80% to 20% between your living room and the kitchen,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: wave* əv jʊr ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ɪz splɪt 80 tɪ 20 bɪtˈwin jʊr ˈlɪvɪŋ rum ənd ðə kitchen,

7wK4peez9zE.text: that means that the electron is split in a similar way. That there are 5 versions
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: that* minz ðət ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ɪz splɪt ɪn ə ˈsɪmələr weɪ. ðət ðɛr ər 5 versions

7wK4peez9zE.text: of the electron – 4 of them are going to the living room, and 1 to the kitchen.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: of* ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn –* 4 əv ðɛm ər goʊɪŋ tɪ ðə ˈlɪvɪŋ rum, ənd 1 tɪ ðə kitchen.

7wK4peez9zE.text: But it isn’t just the electron that splits. You are observing the electron, so there are five versions of you, too!
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: but* ɪt isn’t* ʤɪst ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ðət splɪts. ju ər əbˈzərvɪŋ ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn, soʊ ðɛr ər faɪv ˈvərʒənz əv ju, too!

7wK4peez9zE.text: Four of them see the electron in the living room, and one in the kitchen.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: four* əv ðɛm si ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ɪn ðə ˈlɪvɪŋ rum, ənd wən ɪn ðə kitchen.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Each of these different versions of you and the electron are equally real, equally true.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: each* əv ðiz ˈdɪfərənt ˈvərʒənz əv ju ənd ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ər ˈikwəli ril, ˈikwəli true.

7wK4peez9zE.text: And all of them exist at this moment, in your house. But they can’t communicate
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* ɔl əv ðɛm ɪgˈzɪst æt ðɪs ˈmoʊmənt, ɪn jʊr haʊs. bət ðeɪ can’t* communicate

7wK4peez9zE.text: or interact in any way, so they are totally invisible to each other. Which means that,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: or* ˌɪnərˈækt ɪn ˈɛni weɪ, soʊ ðeɪ ər ˈtoʊtəli ˌɪnˈvɪzəbəl tɪ iʧ ˈəðər. wɪʧ minz that,

7wK4peez9zE.text: whoever “you” are right now, you are just one of your versions,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: whoever* ““you”*” ər raɪt naʊ, ju ər ʤɪst wən əv jʊr versions,

7wK4peez9zE.text: experiencing just one thing – either seeing the electron in the living room, or in the kitchen.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: experiencing* ʤɪst wən θɪŋ –* ˈiðər siɪŋ ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ɪn ðə ˈlɪvɪŋ rum, ər ɪn ðə ˈkɪʧən.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Many-worlds means that all physically possible worlds are coexisting right here, right now,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: many-worlds* minz ðət ɔl ˈfɪzɪkəli ˈpɑsəbəl wərldz ər ˌkoʊɪgˈzɪstɪŋ raɪt hir, raɪt now,

7wK4peez9zE.text: but independently of each other, like the branches of a tree. And there are a lot of them.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: but* ˌɪndɪˈpɛndəntli əv iʧ ˈəðər, laɪk ðə ˈbrænʧɪz əv ə tri. ənd ðɛr ər ə lɔt əv them.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Every possible quantum process means there are other possible worlds.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: every* ˈpɑsəbəl kˈwɑntəm ˈprɔˌsɛs minz ðɛr ər ˈəðər ˈpɑsəbəl worlds.

7wK4peez9zE.text: A radioactive atom decays? Another world exists where it didn’t. A cosmic ray hits one of your cells?
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: a* ˌreɪdioʊˈæktɪv ˈætəm dəˈkeɪz? əˈnəðər wərld ɪgˈzɪsts wɛr ɪt didn’t*. ə ˈkɑzmɪk reɪ hɪts wən əv jʊr cells?

7wK4peez9zE.text: Another world exists where the ray just passed through.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: another* wərld ɪgˈzɪsts wɛr ðə reɪ ʤɪst pæst through.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Each second, bazillions upon bazillions of new worlds exist on top of each other.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: each* ˈsɛkənd, bazillions* əˈpɑn bazillions* əv nu wərldz ɪgˈzɪst ɔn tɔp əv iʧ other.

7wK4peez9zE.text: “Stop it!”, shout the linguists. “If these worlds can’t interact with each other,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: “stop* it!”*!”, ʃaʊt ðə ˈlɪŋgwɪsts. ““if* ðiz wərldz can’t* ˌɪnərˈækt wɪθ iʧ other,

7wK4peez9zE.text: we can't check whether they exist! This is not science. So please shut up and calculate…”
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: we* kænt ʧɛk ˈwɛðər ðeɪ ɪgˈzɪst! ðɪs ɪz nɑt saɪəns. soʊ pliz ʃət əp ənd calculate…”

7wK4peez9zE.text: Wait! There is a way to find out.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: wait*! ðɛr ɪz ə weɪ tɪ faɪnd out.

7wK4peez9zE.text: But to do it, you’ll have to die a few hundred times. And maybe prove that you are immortal!
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: but* tɪ du ɪt, you’ll* hæv tɪ daɪ ə fju ˈhənərd taɪmz. ənd ˈmeɪbi pruv ðət ju ər immortal!

7wK4peez9zE.text: The Ultimate Experiment
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: the* ˈəltəmət experiment

7wK4peez9zE.text: All we need are two electron detectors connected to a nuclear bomb in your living room.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: all* wi nid ər tu ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn dɪˈtɛktərz kəˈnɛktɪd tɪ ə ˈnukliər bɔm ɪn jʊr ˈlɪvɪŋ room.

7wK4peez9zE.text: If the detector in your living room is activated the nuke explodes. If the one
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: if* ðə dɪˈtɛktər ɪn jʊr ˈlɪvɪŋ rum ɪz ˈæktɪˌveɪtɪd ðə nuk ɪkˈsploʊdz. ɪf ðə one

7wK4peez9zE.text: in the kitchen is activated you are safe. Now sit on the nuke and have your brave
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: in* ðə ˈkɪʧən ɪz ˈæktɪˌveɪtɪd ju ər seɪf. naʊ sɪt ɔn ðə nuk ənd hæv jʊr brave

7wK4peez9zE.text: assistant shoot the electron gun. There are two ways this can go. There is a 20% chance
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: assistant* ʃut ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn gən. ðɛr ər tu weɪz ðɪs kən goʊ. ðɛr ɪz ə 20 ʧæns

7wK4peez9zE.text: the electron lands in the kitchen and you survive, and an 80% chance you die.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn lændz ɪn ðə ˈkɪʧən ənd ju sərˈvaɪv, ənd ən 80 ʧæns ju die.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Ok, let’s shoot!
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: ok*, let’s* shoot!

7wK4peez9zE.text: Beep.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: beep.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Oh, lucky you! The electron landed in the kitchen. Let’s try once more:
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: oh*, ˈləki ju! ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn ˈlændɪd ɪn ðə ˈkɪʧən. let’s* traɪ wəns more:

7wK4peez9zE.text: Beep.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: beep.

7wK4peez9zE.text: And once again:
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* wəns again:

7wK4peez9zE.text: Beep.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: beep.

7wK4peez9zE.text: And another 100 times!
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* əˈnəðər 100 times!

7wK4peez9zE.text: Beep, Beep, Beep, Beep, Beep, Beep, Beep…
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: beep*, bip, bip, bip, bip, bip, beep…

7wK4peez9zE.text: If there is only one universe, you’ll die fairly soon.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: if* ðɛr ɪz ˈoʊnli wən ˈjunəˌvərs, you’ll* daɪ ˈfɛrli soon.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Sooner rather than later the electron will trigger the nuke and you’ll be instantly vaporized.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: sooner* ˈrəðər ðən ˈleɪtər ðə ˌɪˈlɛktrɑn wɪl ˈtrɪgər ðə nuk ənd you’ll* bi ˈɪnstəntli vaporized.

7wK4peez9zE.text: But if the many worlds interpretation is true, then every time your assistant shoots the gun,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: but* ɪf ðə ˈmɛni wərldz ˌɪnˌtərprɪˈteɪʃən ɪz tru, ðɛn ˈɛvəri taɪm jʊr əˈsɪstənt ʃuts ðə gun,

7wK4peez9zE.text: there are five versions of you and four instantly die. Only one of your versions
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: there* ər faɪv ˈvərʒənz əv ju ənd fɔr ˈɪnstəntli daɪ. ˈoʊnli wən əv jʊr versions

7wK4peez9zE.text: is alive and there’s only one thing you can experience – your survival.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: is* əˈlaɪv ənd there’s* ˈoʊnli wən θɪŋ ju kən ɪkˈspɪriəns –* jʊr survival.

7wK4peez9zE.text: It doesn’t matter how many times you try. From your perspective, you will survive every time.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: it* doesn’t* ˈmætər haʊ ˈmɛni taɪmz ju traɪ. frəm jʊr pərˈspɛktɪv, ju wɪl sərˈvaɪv ˈɛvəri taɪm.

7wK4peez9zE.text: At first it will seem like luck. But at some point your “luck” will become near-impossible.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: æt fərst ɪt wɪl sim laɪk lək. bət æt səm pɔɪnt jʊr ““luck”*” wɪl bɪˈkəm near-impossible.

7wK4peez9zE.text: In a universe with just one reality, your odds of surviving 100 times in a row
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: in* ə ˈjunəˌvərs wɪθ ʤɪst wən ˌriˈæləˌti, jʊr ɑdz əv sərˈvaɪvɪŋ 100 taɪmz ɪn ə row

7wK4peez9zE.text: are about 1 in ten duovigintillion – a 1 followed by 70 zeroes.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: are* əˈbaʊt 1 ɪn tɛn duovigintillion* –* ə 1 ˈfɑloʊd baɪ 70 zeroes.

7wK4peez9zE.text: So if you did the experiment 100 times and you are still here, the universe just whispered
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: so* ɪf ju dɪd ðə ɪkˈspɛrəmənt 100 taɪmz ənd ju ər stɪl hir, ðə ˈjunəˌvərs ʤɪst whispered

7wK4peez9zE.text: its deepest secret to you. Yes, you killed hundreds of versions of yourself. But you
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: its* ˈdipəst ˈsikrɪt tɪ ju. jɛs, ju kɪld ˈhənərdz əv ˈvərʒənz əv ˈjɔrsɛlf. bət you

7wK4peez9zE.text: now know for sure that the many worlds are true. Because you are still here experiencing things.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: now* noʊ fər ʃʊr ðət ðə ˈmɛni wərldz ər tru. bɪˈkəz ju ər stɪl hir ɪkˈspɪriənsɪŋ things.

7wK4peez9zE.text: And that you are kind of immortal! Okay, well, all that might be a bit overwhelming - let’s break it down again.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* ðət ju ər kaɪnd əv ˌɪˈmɔrtəl! ˌoʊˈkeɪ, wɛl, ɔl ðət maɪt bi ə bɪt ˌoʊvərˈwɛlmɪŋ  let’s* breɪk ɪt daʊn again.

7wK4peez9zE.text: The Cosmic Secret
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: the* ˈkɑzmɪk secret

7wK4peez9zE.text: If every possible quantum process always happens in some branch, then this doesn’t only mean that there are almost, but not quite,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: if* ˈɛvəri ˈpɑsəbəl kˈwɑntəm ˈprɔˌsɛs ˈɔlˌweɪz ˈhæpənz ɪn səm brænʧ, ðɛn ðɪs doesn’t* ˈoʊnli min ðət ðɛr ər ˈɔlˌmoʊst, bət nɑt quite,

7wK4peez9zE.text: infinite versions of you – but there is always a “you” that gets insanely lucky.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: infinite* ˈvərʒənz əv ju –* bət ðɛr ɪz ˈɔlˌweɪz ə ““you”*” ðət gɪts insanely* lucky.

7wK4peez9zE.text: A tumor starts, but a cosmic ray kills it before it spreads. A bolt of lightning strikes at you,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: a* ˈtumər stɑrts, bət ə ˈkɑzmɪk reɪ kɪlz ɪt ˌbiˈfɔr ɪt sprɛdz. ə boʊlt əv ˈlaɪtnɪŋ straɪks æt you,

7wK4peez9zE.text: but a quantum fluke makes it miss by a meter. A washing machine falls
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: but* ə kˈwɑntəm fluk meɪks ɪt mɪs baɪ ə ˈmitər. ə ˈwɑʃɪŋ məˈʃin falls

7wK4peez9zE.text: from a roof, but all its atoms quantum-tunnel through your body.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: from* ə rʊf, bət ɔl ɪts ˈætəmz quantum-tunnel* θru jʊr body.

7wK4peez9zE.text: No matter how extremely high the likelihood is that you will die, there may always be a branch in which you survive.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: no* ˈmætər haʊ ɪkˈstrimli haɪ ðə ˈlaɪkliˌhʊd ɪz ðət ju wɪl daɪ, ðɛr meɪ ˈɔlˌweɪz bi ə brænʧ ɪn wɪʧ ju survive.

7wK4peez9zE.text: So should you start wingsuit-flying today? Well, not so fast. For every version of you that
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: so* ʃʊd ju stɑrt wingsuit-flying* təˈdeɪ? wɛl, nɑt soʊ fæst. fər ˈɛvəri ˈvərʒən əv ju ðət

7wK4peez9zE.text: survives, bazillions don’t. And all those versions of you are just as real as you are right now,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: sərˈvaɪvz, bazillions* don’t*. ənd ɔl ðoʊz ˈvərʒənz əv ju ər ʤɪst ɛz ril ɛz ju ər raɪt now,

7wK4peez9zE.text: watching this video. So if you care about the current version of yourself, you should care
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: watching* ðɪs ˈvɪdioʊ. soʊ ɪf ju kɛr əˈbaʊt ðə ˈkɑrənt ˈvərʒən əv ˈjɔrsɛlf, ju ʃʊd care

7wK4peez9zE.text: just as much about all the versions still waiting to be and their loved ones. In other words – if
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: just* ɛz məʧ əˈbaʊt ɔl ðə ˈvərʒənz stɪl ˈweɪtɪŋ tɪ bi ənd ðɛr ləvd wənz. ɪn ˈəðər wərdz –* if

7wK4peez9zE.text: you see a washing machine falling, better move away and save as many of your versions as you can.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: you* si ə ˈwɑʃɪŋ məˈʃin ˈfɑlɪŋ, ˈbɛtər muv əˈweɪ ənd seɪv ɛz ˈmɛni əv jʊr ˈvərʒənz ɛz ju can.

7wK4peez9zE.text: The more universes exist with you in them the better, your existence makes the universe richer.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: the* mɔr ˈjunəˌvərsɪz ɪgˈzɪst wɪθ ju ɪn ðɛm ðə ˈbɛtər, jʊr ɪgˈzɪstəns meɪks ðə ˈjunəˌvərs richer.

7wK4peez9zE.text: And there is another good reason to move. While the many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics feels beautiful and elegant,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* ðɛr ɪz əˈnəðər gʊd ˈrizən tɪ muv. waɪl ðə many-worlds* ˌɪnˌtərprɪˈteɪʃən əv kˈwɑntəm məˈkænɪks filz ˈbjutəfəl ənd elegant,

7wK4peez9zE.text: that doesn’t make it true. We don’t know if it’s true and so far no literary critic sat

7wK4peez9zE.ipa: just* wən wərld. ʤɪst wən ˈvərʒən əv ju. ənd ɪf ðə ˈwɑʃɪŋ məˈʃin hɪts ju, ju ər dən for.

7wK4peez9zE.ipa: quantum* məˈkænɪks ɪz ən əˈmeɪzɪŋ tul tɪ ɪkˈsplɔr ˈdɪfərənt ˌriˈæləˌtiz. bət wət əˈbaʊt ðɪs reality?

7wK4peez9zE.ipa: ground* nuz, ðə ˈspɑnsər əv ðɪs ˈvɪdioʊ, kən hɛlp ju ˈnævəˌgeɪt ɑr ˈmidiə universe,

7wK4peez9zE.text: In a nutshell, Ground News is a website and app designed to make reading the news easier
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: ɪn ə ˈnətˌʃɛl, graʊnd nuz ɪz ə ˈwɛbˌsaɪt ənd æp dɪˈzaɪnd tɪ meɪk ˈrɛdɪŋ ðə nuz easier

7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* mɔr data-driven*. ˈɛvəri deɪ, ðeɪ kəˈlɛkt ˈɑrtɪkəlz frəm əraʊnd ðə world

7wK4peez9zE.text: and break each story down visually. You see reporting bias, reliability and ownership;
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* breɪk iʧ ˈstɔri daʊn ˈvɪʒwəli. ju si rɪˈpɔrtɪŋ baɪəs, riˌlaɪəˈbɪləti ənd ownership;

7wK4peez9zE.text: all backed by three independent media monitoring organizations.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: all* bækt baɪ θri ˌɪndɪˈpɛndənt ˈmidiə ˈmɑnətərɪŋ organizations.

7wK4peez9zE.text: Take this story about a new concept for a quantum computer. Right away you can see that over 90 news
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: take* ðɪs ˈstɔri əˈbaʊt ə nu ˈkɑnsɛpt fər ə kˈwɑntəm kəmˈpjutər. raɪt əˈweɪ ju kən si ðət ˈoʊvər 90 news

7wK4peez9zE.text: outlets reported on it. Then, you can see where these outlets lean politically and how reliable
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: outlets* ˌriˈpɔrtəd ɔn ɪt. ðɛn, ju kən si wɛr ðiz ˈaʊˌtlɛts lin ˈplɪtɪkli ənd haʊ reliable

7wK4peez9zE.text: they generally are - you can even compare headlines to see how these potential biases
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: they* ˈʤɛnərəli ər  ju kən ˈivɪn kəmˈpɛr ˈhɛˌdlaɪnz tɪ si haʊ ðiz pəˈtɛnʃəl biases

7wK4peez9zE.text: might affect framing. Some media outlets frame it as a major leap forward for IBM as a business,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: might* əˈfɛkt ˈfreɪmɪŋ. səm ˈmidiə ˈaʊˌtlɛts freɪm ɪt ɛz ə ˈmeɪʤər lip ˈfɔrwərd fər ibm* ɛz ə business,

7wK4peez9zE.text: and highlight the commercial potential for quantum computing. Others focus more on the
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* ˈhaɪˌlaɪt ðə kəˈmərʃəl pəˈtɛnʃəl fər kˈwɑntəm kəmˈpjutɪŋ. ˈəðərz ˈfoʊkɪs mɔr ɔn the

7wK4peez9zE.text: science itself, and use precise language to emphasize the technical challenges that
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: science* ˌɪtˈsɛlf, ənd juz prɪˈsaɪs ˈlæŋgwɪʤ tɪ ˈɛmfəˌsaɪz ðə ˈtɛknɪkəl ˈʧælənʤɪz that

7wK4peez9zE.text: were overcome. This way you get to see how headlines alone can affect
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: were* ˈoʊvərˌkəm. ðɪs weɪ ju gɪt tɪ si haʊ ˈhɛˌdlaɪnz əˈloʊn kən affect

7wK4peez9zE.text: your perception and decide for yourself if this finding is the future or just a fad.
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: your* pərˈsɛpʃən ənd ˌdɪˈsaɪd fər ˈjɔrsɛlf ɪf ðɪs ˈfaɪndɪŋ ɪz ðə fˈjuʧər ər ʤɪst ə fad.

7wK4peez9zE.ipa: if* you’d* laɪk tɪ gɪv ðɛm ə traɪ, goʊ tɪ ground.news/kin*, ər skæn the

7wK4peez9zE.ipa: qr* koʊd ɔn ðə skrin. ˈjuzɪŋ ðɪs lɪŋk seɪvz ju 40 ɔf ən ənˈlɪmɪtɪd ˈækˌsɛs subscription,

7wK4peez9zE.ipa: and* dɪˈrɛkli səˈpɔrts ɑr ˈʧænəl. wi θɪŋk ðə ˈpipəl æt graʊnd nuz du ə

7wK4peez9zE.text: really important job - if you can never know all quantum versions of yourself,
7wK4peez9zE.ipa: ˈrɪli ˌɪmˈpɔrtənt ʤɑb  ɪf ju kən ˈnɛvər noʊ ɔl kˈwɑntəm ˈvərʒənz əv ˈjɔrsɛlf,

7wK4peez9zE.ipa: ˈbɛtər meɪk ʃʊr ðɪs ˈvərʒən həz ðə fʊl vju əv ðə horizon.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Pregnancy is a hardcore sci-fi epic of war, peace and compromise.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: pregnancy* ɪz ə ˈhɑrdˈkɔr ˌsaɪˈfaɪ ˈɛpɪk əv wɔr, pis ənd compromise.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Babies are basically very cute tumors that grow a completely new organ inside a different human.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: babies* ər ˈbeɪsɪkli ˈvɛri kjut ˈtumərz ðət groʊ ə kəmˈplitli nu ˈɔrgən ˌɪnˈsaɪd ə ˈdɪfərənt human.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Their mom’s body graciously allows this, while the new life saps her
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: their* mom’s* ˈbɑdi ˈgreɪʃəsli əˈlaʊz ðɪs, waɪl ðə nu laɪf sæps her

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: resources and challenges her immune system to its limits for months.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: resources* ənd ˈʧælənʤɪz hər ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm tɪ ɪts ˈlɪmɪts fər months.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Biology is brutal and beautiful and metal.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: biology* ɪz ˈbrutəl ənd ˈbjutəfəl ənd metal.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Let us zoom in and observe this brutal struggle for survival – that in the
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: let* ˈjuˈɛs zum ɪn ənd əbˈzərv ðɪs ˈbrutəl ˈstrəgəl fər sərˈvaɪvəl –* ðət ɪn the

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: best case ends in one of the most magical things: A new life.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: best* keɪs ɛndz ɪn wən əv ðə moʊst ˈmæʤɪkəl θɪŋz: ə nu life.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Our story begins with an army of tens of millions of sperm cells that immediately face death.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: our* ˈstɔri bɪˈgɪnz wɪθ ən ˈɑrmi əv tɛnz əv ˈmɪljənz əv spərm sɛlz ðət ˌɪˈmiˌdiətli feɪs death.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: They are on a tight timer before they run out of energy and die.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: they* ər ɔn ə taɪt ˈtaɪmər ˌbiˈfɔr ðeɪ rən aʊt əv ˈɛnərʤi ənd die.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: But this is the least of their problems right now.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: but* ðɪs ɪz ðə list əv ðɛr ˈprɑbləmz raɪt now.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Like all bodily openings, the female reproductive tract is a fortress defending against invaders.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: like* ɔl ˈbɑdəli ˈoʊpənɪŋz, ðə ˈfiˌmeɪl ˌriprəˈdəktɪv trækt ɪz ə ˈfɔrtrəs dɪˈfɛndɪŋ əˈgɛnst invaders.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The future mom’s body has set up deadly barriers and puts the sperm through a
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* fˈjuʧər mom’s* ˈbɑdi həz sɛt əp ˈdɛdli ˈbɛriərz ənd pʊts ðə spərm θru ə

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: brutal process that kills almost all of them and ideally selects the strongest and fittest one.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: ˈbrutəl ˈprɔˌsɛs ðət kɪlz ˈɔlˌmoʊst ɔl əv ðɛm ənd aɪˈdili səˈlɛkts ðə ˈstrɔŋgəst ənd ˈfɪtəst one.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The first deadly obstacle is a highly acidic environment guarded by hundreds of thousands of guard cells.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* fərst ˈdɛdli ˈɑbstəkəl ɪz ə ˈhaɪli əˈsɪdɪk ɪnˈvaɪrənmənt ˈgɑrdɪd baɪ ˈhənərdz əv ˈθaʊzənz əv gɑrd cells.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The acidity kills millions, especially of the weaker sperm within the first half hour, although they have a bit of protection:
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* əˈsɪdəti kɪlz ˈmɪljənz, əˈspɛʃəli əv ðə ˈwikər spərm wɪˈθɪn ðə fərst hæf aʊər, ˌɔlˈðoʊ ðeɪ hæv ə bɪt əv protection:

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Seminal fluid is alkaline and so it makes this first obstacle a little less deadly.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: seminal* fluɪd ɪz ˈælkəˌlaɪn ənd soʊ ɪt meɪks ðɪs fərst ˈɑbstəkəl ə ˈlɪtəl lɛs deadly.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The survivors reach the next obstacle, a treacherous maze
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* sərˈvaɪvərz riʧ ðə nɛkst ˈɑbstəkəl, ə ˈtrɛʧərəs maze

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: full of protein nets where millions more find a brutal end getting stuck and lost.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: full* əv ˈproʊˌtin nɛts wɛr ˈmɪljənz mɔr faɪnd ə ˈbrutəl ɛnd ˈgɪtɪŋ stək ənd lost.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Now if a woman is in the part of her menstrual cycle where she is ovulating , her body helps
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: now* ɪf ə ˈwʊmən ɪz ɪn ðə pɑrt əv hər ˈmɛnstrəl ˈsaɪkəl wɛr ʃi ɪz ovulating*  hər ˈbɑdi helps

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: the sperm out a bit, by releasing chemicals that guide the way and by being a bit less hostile.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* spərm aʊt ə bɪt, baɪ riˈlisɪŋ ˈkɛmɪkəlz ðət gaɪd ðə weɪ ənd baɪ biɪŋ ə bɪt lɛs hostile.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Luckily for our brave sperm this is the case today.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: luckily* fər ɑr breɪv spərm ðɪs ɪz ðə keɪs today.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Only a couple hundred – less than 0.0001% of the original sperm – pass through into the
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: only* ə ˈkəpəl ˈhənərd –* lɛs ðən 0.0001* əv ðə ərˈɪʤənəl spərm –* pæs θru ˈɪntu the

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: uterine cavity, where two tunnels branch out.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: uterine* ˈkævəti, wɛr tu ˈtənəlz brænʧ out.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Here mucus helps transport the stronger sperm to move on, while weeding out the weaker ones.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: here* mˈjukəs hɛlps ˈtrænspɔrt ðə ˈstrɔŋgər spərm tɪ muv ɔn, waɪl ˈwidɪŋ aʊt ðə ˈwikər ones.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The mom’s immune cells pick off and devour every cell that trails behind or takes the wrong turn.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* mom’s* ˌɪmˈjun sɛlz pɪk ɔf ənd dɪˈvaʊər ˈɛvəri sɛl ðət treɪlz bɪˈhaɪnd ər teɪks ðə rɔŋ turn.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: And then finally, maybe just a few dozen sperm reach their goal, the mighty, giant egg.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: and* ðɛn ˈfaɪnəli, ˈmeɪbi ʤɪst ə fju ˈdəzən spərm riʧ ðɛr goʊl, ðə ˈmaɪti, ʤaɪənt egg.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: 10,000 times bigger than them, it is an industrial scale monster loaded with nutrients and nearly
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: 10,000* taɪmz ˈbɪgər ðən ðɛm, ɪt ɪz ən ˌɪnˈdəstriəl skeɪl ˈmɑnstər ˈloʊdɪd wɪθ ˈnutriənts ənd ˈnɪrli

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: 100,000 mitochondria, the powerhouse of the cell – 50 times more than an average cell.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: 100,000* ˌmaɪtəˈkɑndriə, ðə ˈpaʊərˌhaʊs əv ðə sɛl –* 50 taɪmz mɔr ðən ən ˈævərɪʤ cell.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: But the egg is not easily conquered – the survivors get closer and try
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: but* ðə ɛg ɪz nɑt ˈizəli ˈkɑŋkərd –* ðə sərˈvaɪvərz gɪt ˈkloʊzər ənd try

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: to get in until the egg decides to accept a single one of them. The genes of two individuals merge.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: to* gɪt ɪn ənˈtɪl ðə ɛg ˌdɪˈsaɪdz tɪ əkˈsɛpt ə ˈsɪŋgəl wən əv ðɛm. ðə ʤinz əv tu ˌɪndəˈvɪʤəwəlz merge.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The egg stops being part of its mother – it is something else now. A new being with its own agenda.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* ɛg stɑps biɪŋ pɑrt əv ɪts ˈməðər –* ɪt ɪz ˈsəmθɪŋ ɛls naʊ. ə nu biɪŋ wɪθ ɪts oʊn agenda.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Not a human yet, but the potential to become one. Like a thought that is not yet a word.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: not* ə ˈjumən jɛt, bət ðə pəˈtɛnʃəl tɪ bɪˈkəm wən. laɪk ə θɔt ðət ɪz nɑt jɛt ə word.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: But its mom’s body hasn’t decided yet if she wants to say it out loud. It will have to fight for that.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: but* ɪts mom’s* ˈbɑdi hasn’t* ˌdɪˈsaɪdɪd jɛt ɪf ʃi wɔnts tɪ seɪ ɪt aʊt laʊd. ɪt wɪl hæv tɪ faɪt fər that.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: For a few days it rapidly divides and grows to a couple of hundred cells, while traveling down to
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: for* ə fju deɪz ɪt ˈræpədli dɪˈvaɪdz ənd groʊz tɪ ə ˈkəpəl əv ˈhənərd sɛlz, waɪl ˈtrævəlɪŋ daʊn to

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: the uterus, the part of the female reproductive system where it will try to make its new home.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* ˈjutərəs, ðə pɑrt əv ðə ˈfiˌmeɪl ˌriprəˈdəktɪv ˈsɪstəm wɛr ɪt wɪl traɪ tɪ meɪk ɪts nu home.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Here it begins to divide into two specialised teams of cells - one will eventually become the baby.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: here* ɪt bɪˈgɪnz tɪ dɪˈvaɪd ˈɪntu tu specialised* timz əv sɛlz  wən wɪl ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli bɪˈkəm ðə baby.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The other cells are trophoblasts and their job is to turn into a temporary organ inside the mother:
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* ˈəðər sɛlz ər trophoblasts* ənd ðɛr ʤɑb ɪz tɪ tərn ˈɪntu ə ˈtɛmpərˌɛri ˈɔrgən ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə mother:

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: the placenta, that will make pregnancy possible and eventually die.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* pləˈsɛntə, ðət wɪl meɪk ˈprɛgnənsi ˈpɑsəbəl ənd ɪˈvɛnʧəwəli die.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: This means that when you were an embryo there was this whole other part of you,
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: this* minz ðət wɪn ju wər ən ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ðɛr wɑz ðɪs hoʊl ˈəðər pɑrt əv you,

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: not like a twin, even closer, maybe like a guardian angel clone. These cells never had the chance to become a human,
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: not* laɪk ə twɪn, ˈivɪn ˈkloʊzər, ˈmeɪbi laɪk ə ˈgɑrdiən ˈeɪnʤəl kloʊn. ðiz sɛlz ˈnɛvər hæd ðə ʧæns tɪ bɪˈkəm ə human,

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: their only purpose in life was to make you exist.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: ðɛr ˈoʊnli ˈpərpəs ɪn laɪf wɑz tɪ meɪk ju exist.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: In any case, the following days are the most dangerous for the potential new life. Like a tennis ball rolling through syrup
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: in* ˈɛni keɪs, ðə ˈfɑloʊɪŋ deɪz ər ðə moʊst ˈdeɪnʤərəs fər ðə pəˈtɛnʃəl nu laɪf. laɪk ə ˈtɛnɪs bɔl ˈroʊlɪŋ θru syrup

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: the young embryo moves along the uterus and tries to take a hold.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* jəŋ ˈɛmbriˌoʊ muvz əˈlɔŋ ðə ˈjutərəs ənd traɪz tɪ teɪk ə hoʊld.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: An intense chemical dialog between two living things begins, the embryo
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: ən ˌɪnˈtɛns ˈkɛmɪkəl ˈdaɪəlɔg bɪtˈwin tu ˈlɪvɪŋ θɪŋz bɪˈgɪnz, ðə embryo

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: releases dozens of chemicals that announce its presence and asks the guard cells of the
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: releases* ˈdəzənz əv ˈkɛmɪkəlz ðət əˈnaʊns ɪts ˈprɛzəns ənd æsks ðə gɑrd sɛlz əv the

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: uterine wall to allow it to please, please, please attach itself so it can survive.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: uterine* wɔl tɪ əˈlaʊ ɪt tɪ pliz, pliz, pliz əˈtæʧ ˌɪtˈsɛlf soʊ ɪt kən survive.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The uterus responds with dozens of hormones and immune signals itself.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* ˈjutərəs rɪˈspɑndz wɪθ ˈdəzənz əv ˈhɔrˌmoʊnz ənd ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪgnəlz itself.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: If it is satisfied with the quality of the chemical conversation its cells allow it.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: if* ɪt ɪz ˈsætɪsˌfaɪd wɪθ ðə kˈwɑləti əv ðə ˈkɛmɪkəl ˌkɑnvərˈseɪʃən ɪts sɛlz əˈlaʊ it.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: If it doesn’t the embryo will be lost and die. This is where we begin to see that the interests of both
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: if* ɪt doesn’t* ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ wɪl bi lɔst ənd daɪ. ðɪs ɪz wɛr wi bɪˈgɪn tɪ si ðət ðə ˈɪntərɪsts əv both

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: living beings no longer completely align anymore. Pregnancy is a huge energy investment so the mother’s
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: living* biɪŋz noʊ ˈlɔŋgər kəmˈplitli əˈlaɪn ˌɛniˈmɔr. ˈprɛgnənsi ɪz ə juʤ ˈɛnərʤi ˌɪnˈvɛstmənt soʊ ðə mother’s

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: body will eject the embryo if it doesn’t see it as viable. The embryo on the other hand faces a life and
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: body* wɪl ɪˈʤɛkt ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ɪf ɪt doesn’t* si ɪt ɛz ˈvaɪəbəl. ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ɔn ðə ˈəðər hænd ˈfeɪsɪz ə laɪf and

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: death situation and tries to stay alive at any price.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: death* ˌsɪʧuˈeɪʃən ənd traɪz tɪ steɪ əˈlaɪv æt ˈɛni price.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: So it’s not asking politely but deploying thousands of infiltration units:
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: so* it’s* nɑt ˈæskɪŋ pəˈlaɪtli bət dɪˈplɔɪɪŋ ˈθaʊzənz əv ˌɪnfɪlˈtreɪʃən units:

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Bubbles filled with genetic material, kind of like a human virus!
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: bubbles* fɪld wɪθ ʤəˈnɛtɪk məˈtɪriəl, kaɪnd əv laɪk ə ˈjumən virus!

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: They sort of invade the uterine cells and try to brainwash them, so they help it attach itself, rather than rejecting it.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: they* sɔrt əv ˌɪnˈveɪd ðə ˈjutərən sɛlz ənd traɪ tɪ ˈbreɪnˌwɑʃ ðɛm, soʊ ðeɪ hɛlp ɪt əˈtæʧ ˌɪtˈsɛlf, ˈrəðər ðən rɪˈʤɛktɪŋ it.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: During this time one of the weirdest features of pregnancy occurs:
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: during* ðɪs taɪm wən əv ðə ˈwɪrdəst ˈfiʧərz əv ˈprɛgnənsi occurs:

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: the uterus provides uterine milk, not real milk but a clear fluid filled with nutrients and
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* ˈjutərəs prəˈvaɪdz ˈjutərən mɪlk, nɑt ril mɪlk bət ə klɪr fluɪd fɪld wɪθ ˈnutriənts and

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: hormones that the young embryo sucks up hungrily to gain additional energy until it gets inside.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: hormones* ðət ðə jəŋ ˈɛmbriˌoʊ səks əp ˈhəŋgrəli tɪ geɪn əˈdɪʃənəl ˈɛnərʤi ənˈtɪl ɪt gɪts ˌɪnˈsaɪd.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: If the embryo is allowed in, the implantation was successful, the first critical hurdle.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: ɪf ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ɪz əˈlaʊd ɪn, ðə ˌɪmplænˈteɪʃən wɑz səkˈsɛsfəl, ðə fərst ˈkrɪtɪkəl hurdle.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The next step is to reach the blood vessels of its mother,
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* nɛkst stɛp ɪz tɪ riʧ ðə bləd ˈvɛsəlz əv ɪts mother,

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: the only way to survive if it doesn't want to starve.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* ˈoʊnli weɪ tɪ sərˈvaɪv ɪf ɪt ˈdəzənt wɔnt tɪ starve.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The trophoblasts begin to clone themselves on a massive scale and turn into different specialists.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* trophoblasts* bɪˈgɪn tɪ kloʊn ðɛmˈsɛlvz ɔn ə ˈmæsɪv skeɪl ənd tərn ˈɪntu ˈdɪfərənt specialists.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: One of them turns into a violent invader that starts drilling into the uterine
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: one* əv ðɛm tərnz ˈɪntu ə ˈvaɪələnt ˌɪnˈveɪdər ðət stɑrts ˈdrɪlɪŋ ˈɪntu ðə uterine

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: tissue like a tiny parasitic octopus with many arms, spreading and growing.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: tissue* laɪk ə ˈtaɪni ˌpɛrəˈsɪtɪk ˈɑktəˌpʊs wɪθ ˈmɛni ɑrmz, ˈsprɛdɪŋ ənd growing.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: This is a brutal process, ordering loads of it’s mom’s uterus cells to destroy themselves,
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: this* ɪz ə ˈbrutəl ˈprɔˌsɛs, ˈɔrdərɪŋ loʊdz əv it’s* mom’s* ˈjutərəs sɛlz tɪ dɪˈstrɔɪ themselves,

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: killing others directly or even devouring some cells whole.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: killing* ˈəðərz dɪˈrɛkli ər ˈivɪn dɪˈvaʊərɪŋ səm sɛlz whole.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: While this sounds brutal, and it is, this process is highly regulated and doesn’t hurt the mom. It is another test.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: while* ðɪs saʊnz ˈbrutəl, ənd ɪt ɪz, ðɪs ˈprɔˌsɛs ɪz ˈhaɪli ˈrɛgjəˌleɪtɪd ənd doesn’t* hərt ðə mɑm. ɪt ɪz əˈnəðər test.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The mom’s body monitors carefully how the embryo is doing – is it growing quickly or if it is more chill.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* mom’s* ˈbɑdi ˈmɑnətərz ˈkɛrfəli haʊ ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ɪz duɪŋ –* ɪz ɪt groʊɪŋ kˈwɪkli ər ɪf ɪt ɪz mɔr chill.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: If an embryo has genetic damage or chromosomal abnormalities it
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: if* ən ˈɛmbriˌoʊ həz ʤəˈnɛtɪk ˈdæmɪʤ ər chromosomal* ˌæbnɔrˈmælətiz it

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: will spend way more energy on repairing itself and maybe grow more erratically.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: will* spɛnd weɪ mɔr ˈɛnərʤi ɔn rɪˈpɛrɪŋ ˌɪtˈsɛlf ənd ˈmeɪbi groʊ mɔr erratically.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: This makes it metabolically noisy, releasing loads of chemicals the mom’s immune cells pick up.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: this* meɪks ɪt metabolically* ˈnɔɪzi, riˈlisɪŋ loʊdz əv ˈkɛmɪkəlz ðə mom’s* ˌɪmˈjun sɛlz pɪk up.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Which provokes them and makes it more likely that they will destroy it.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: which* prəˈvoʊks ðɛm ənd meɪks ɪt mɔr ˈlaɪkli ðət ðeɪ wɪl dɪˈstrɔɪ it.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: On the other hand, if the embryo is weak it is metabolically too quiet
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: on* ðə ˈəðər hænd, ɪf ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ɪz wik ɪt ɪz metabolically* tu kwaɪət

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: and the mom will stop talking to it – which also ends the pregnancy.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: ənd ðə mɑm wɪl stɑp ˈtɔkɪŋ tɪ ɪt –* wɪʧ ˈɔlsoʊ ɛndz ðə pregnancy.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: This embryo is just right, so the mom’s cells release a flood of different chemicals
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: this* ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ɪz ʤɪst raɪt, soʊ ðə mom’s* sɛlz riˈlis ə fləd əv ˈdɪfərənt chemicals

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: to help it out, support it growing and most importantly, activate her immune system.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: to* hɛlp ɪt aʊt, səˈpɔrt ɪt groʊɪŋ ənd moʊst ˌɪmˈpɔrtəntli, ˈæktəˌveɪt hər ˌɪmˈjun system.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Usually this would be very bad – the immune system kills everything
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: usually* ðɪs wʊd bi ˈvɛri bæd –* ðə ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm kɪlz everything

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: that is not part of the mom’s body, and this embryo is clearly not part of her.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: that* ɪz nɑt pɑrt əv ðə mom’s* ˈbɑdi, ənd ðɪs ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ɪz ˈklɪrli nɑt pɑrt əv her.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: But the immune cells of the uterus surround the embryo and start helping it, guiding the trophoblast to grow further.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: but* ðə ˌɪmˈjun sɛlz əv ðə ˈjutərəs səraʊnd ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ənd stɑrt ˈhɛlpɪŋ ɪt, ˈgaɪdɪŋ ðə trophoblast* tɪ groʊ further.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: They create a physical and chemical safe zone that tells dangerous immune cells like T Cells to stay away.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: they* kriˈeɪt ə ˈfɪzɪkəl ənd ˈkɛmɪkəl seɪf zoʊn ðət tɛlz ˈdeɪnʤərəs ˌɪmˈjun sɛlz laɪk ti sɛlz tɪ steɪ away.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The embryo has its own motives and doesn't want to rely on its mothers goodwill alone –
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* ˈɛmbriˌoʊ həz ɪts oʊn ˈmoʊtɪvz ənd ˈdəzənt wɔnt tɪ rɪˈlaɪ ɔn ɪts ˈməðərz ˈgʊdˈwɪl əˈloʊn –

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: so defensive trophoblasts send out signals that kill her immune cells if they get too close and could start attacking it.
Hundreds of cells go even further and leave the embryo behind.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: so* dɪˈfɛnsɪv trophoblasts* sɛnd aʊt ˈsɪgnəlz ðət kɪl hər ˌɪmˈjun sɛlz ɪf ðeɪ gɪt tu kloʊz ənd kʊd stɑrt əˈtækɪŋ it.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: They spread all across the mom’s body, entering organs, even the brain.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: hundreds* əv sɛlz goʊ ˈivɪn ˈfərðər ənd liv ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ behind.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: We don’t know what all of these cells are doing but we think that they are
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: they* sprɛd ɔl əˈkrɔs ðə mom’s* ˈbɑdi, ˈɛnərɪŋ ˈɔrgənz, ˈivɪn ðə brain.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: probably telling her immune system that the embryo is not to be attacked. That it should be left alone or even protected.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: we* don’t* noʊ wət ɔl əv ðiz sɛlz ər duɪŋ bət wi θɪŋk ðət ðeɪ are

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: These cells may stay inside the mother for years or even decades.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: these* sɛlz meɪ steɪ ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə ˈməðər fər jɪrz ər ˈivɪn decades.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: It is likely that parts of you are still in your mother.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: it* ɪz ˈlaɪkli ðət pɑrts əv ju ər stɪl ɪn jʊr mother.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Around 8 weeks after the egg was fertilized, the transition from the embryo to fetus begins –
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: probably* ˈtɛlɪŋ hər ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm ðət ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ ɪz nɑt tɪ bi əˈtækt. ðət ɪt ʃʊd bi lɛft əˈloʊn ər ˈivɪn protected.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: the size of an olive, its organs begin to form and it turns from a blob into something vaguely human-like.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: around* 8 wiks ˈæftər ðə ɛg wɑz ˈfərtəˌlaɪzd, ðə trænˈzɪʃən frəm ðə ˈɛmbriˌoʊ tɪ ˈfitəs bɪˈgɪnz –

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Is there a clear point where a clump of cells becomes a human? Not really.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: ɪz ðɛr ə klɪr pɔɪnt wɛr ə kləmp əv sɛlz bɪˈkəmz ə ˈjumən? nɑt really.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: It is a fluid transition and every society and person – be it morally or legally – marks this moment differently.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: it* ɪz ə fluɪd trænˈzɪʃən ənd ˈɛvəri soʊˈsaɪɪti ənd ˈpərsən –* bi ɪt ˈmɔrəli ər ˈligəli –* mɑrks ðɪs ˈmoʊmənt differently.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Meanwhile construction trophoblasts are busy building a spongy fingerlike
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: meanwhile* kənˈstrəkʃən trophoblasts* ər ˈbɪzi ˈbɪldɪŋ ə ˈspənʤi fingerlike

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: structure that expands further into the uterus, a completely new organ:
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: structure* ðət ɪkˈspændz ˈfərðər ˈɪntu ðə ˈjutərəs, ə kəmˈplitli nu organ:

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The placenta – an organ that you once grew inside your mother, that was ejected after your birth
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* pləˈsɛntə –* ən ˈɔrgən ðət ju wəns gru ˌɪnˈsaɪd jʊr ˈməðər, ðət wɑz ɪˈʤɛktɪd ˈæftər jʊr birth

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: and died a silent death while everybody was busy welcoming you into the world.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: and* daɪd ə ˈsaɪlənt dɛθ waɪl ˈɛvriˌbɑdi wɑz ˈbɪzi ˈwɛlkəmɪŋ ju ˈɪntu ðə world.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: In our story the placenta is now the new home of the fetus. An enormous fortress,
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: in* ɑr ˈstɔri ðə pləˈsɛntə ɪz naʊ ðə nu hoʊm əv ðə ˈfitəs. ən ɪˈnɔrmɪs fortress,

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: protecting it from microbes that could infect and kill it in its still pretty fragile state.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: protecting* ɪt frəm ˈmaɪˌkroʊbz ðət kʊd ˌɪnˈfɛkt ənd kɪl ɪt ɪn ɪts stɪl ˈprɪti ˈfræʤəl state.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: It even has its own mini immune system, placental immune cells that gobble up anything that poses a threat.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: it* ˈivɪn həz ɪts oʊn ˈmɪni ˌɪmˈjun ˈsɪstəm, placental* ˌɪmˈjun sɛlz ðət ˈgɑbəl əp ˈɛniˌθɪŋ ðət ˈpoʊzɪz ə threat.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: Other placental cells creep along the inside of the mom's blood vessels,
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: other* placental* sɛlz krip əˈlɔŋ ðə ˌɪnˈsaɪd əv ðə mɑmz bləd vessels,

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: stretching them and connecting the fetus through the umbilical cord on the other side.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: stretching* ðɛm ənd kəˈnɛktɪŋ ðə ˈfitəs θru ðə əmˈbɪlɪkəl kɔrd ɔn ðə ˈəðər side.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: With the blood flow secured, it’s time to load it with as much food as possible.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: with* ðə bləd floʊ sɪˈkjʊrd, it’s* taɪm tɪ loʊd ɪt wɪθ ɛz məʧ fud ɛz possible.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The placenta releases hormones that funnel glucose directly to the fetus, stealing energy from its mom.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: the* pləˈsɛntə rɪˈlisɪz ˈhɔrˌmoʊnz ðət ˈfənəl ˈgluˌkoʊs dɪˈrɛkli tɪ ðə ˈfitəs, ˈstilɪŋ ˈɛnərʤi frəm ɪts mom.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: If the fetus goes too far and asks too much, this can sometimes lead
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: if* ðə ˈfitəs goʊz tu fɑr ənd æsks tu məʧ, ðɪs kən ˈsəmˌtaɪmz lead

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: to gestational diabetes for the mom during the pregnancy, starving her body of energy.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: to* gestational* ˌdaɪəˈbitiz fər ðə mɑm ˈdʊrɪŋ ðə ˈprɛgnənsi, ˈstɑrvɪŋ hər ˈbɑdi əv ˈɛnərʤi.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: The mothers body is trying to support the new life, but not at the cost of her own survival.
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: ðə ˈməðərz ˈbɑdi ɪz traɪɪŋ tɪ səˈpɔrt ðə nu laɪf, bət nɑt æt ðə kɔst əv hər oʊn survival.

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: In a sense the mothers genes inside the fetus still have a stronger allegiance
Zpq1GbPqhy4.ipa: in* ə sɛns ðə ˈməðərz ʤinz ˌɪnˈsaɪd ðə ˈfitəs stɪl hæv ə ˈstrɔŋgər allegiance

Zpq1GbPqhy4.text: to her than to the new being – but it’s fathers genes don’t. They want the fetus to survive at all costs.

dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: they* ər mɔr laɪk bægz əv ˈlusli pækt ˈgrævəl –* hips əv ˈpɛbəlz, ˈprɛʃəs ˈmɪnərəlz ənd dəst, ˈbɛrli hɛld together.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: And the obvious choice is, of course, a nuke!
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: which* minz ðət ˌɪnˈstɛd əv ʤɪst ˈnəʤɪŋ ðɛm, wi kən du ˈsəmθɪŋ ˈbɛtər –* ˈpəlvərˌaɪz them.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: So, let’s go!
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: and* ðə ˈɑbviəs ʧɔɪs ɪz, əv kɔrs, ə nuke!

dKm7T13X7n4.text: We load up a nuclear warhead, launch, aim at our deadly asteroid and…
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: so*, let’s* go!

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Oh. The asteroid destroyed the nuke!
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: oʊ. ðə ˈæstərˌɔɪd dɪˈstrɔɪd ðə nuke!

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Asteroids can approach Earth at 70,000 km/h – enough to cross the Atlantic in 5 minutes.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: asteroids* kən əˈproʊʧ ərθ æt 70,000* km/h* –* ɪˈnəf tɪ krɔs ðə əˈtlæntɪk ɪn 5 minutes.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: No bomb we’ve ever made could survive such an impact.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: noʊ bɔm we’ve* ˈɛvər meɪd kʊd sərˈvaɪv səʧ ən impact.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: So if we go for a head-on collision, the asteroid will wreck the nuke before it even has a chance to explode.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: so* ɪf wi goʊ fər ə head-on* kəˈlɪʒən, ðə ˈæstərˌɔɪd wɪl rɛk ðə nuk ˌbiˈfɔr ɪt ˈivɪn həz ə ʧæns tɪ explode.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Ok easy, let’s explode the bomb before it touches the asteroid.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: ok* ˈizi, let’s* ɪkˈsploʊd ðə bɔm ˌbiˈfɔr ɪt ˈtəʧɪz ðə asteroid.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Scientists have estimated the optimal distance, which for our 100 m killer friend
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: scientists* hæv ˈɛstəˌmeɪtɪd ðə ˈɑptɪməl ˈdɪstəns, wɪʧ fər ɑr 100 ɛm ˈkɪlər friend

dKm7T13X7n4.text: would be a few tens of meters above the surface.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: would* bi ə fju tɛnz əv ˈmitərz əˈbəv ðə surface.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Launch, set the timer and…
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: launch*, sɛt ðə ˈtaɪmər and…

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Sad Boom! The explosion has made a dramatic crater and... nothing else.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: sad* bum! ðə ɪkˈsploʊʒən həz meɪd ə drəˈmætɪk ˈkreɪtər ənd... ˈnəθɪŋ else.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Unfortunately in space there is no air to carry a shockwave, so most of the explosion’s energy is lost.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: unfortunately* ɪn speɪs ðɛr ɪz noʊ ɛr tɪ ˈkɛri ə ˈʃɑkˌweɪv, soʊ moʊst əv ðə explosion’s* ˈɛnərʤi ɪz lost.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The rock will still hit us in two weeks, only a few kilometers to the left.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* rɑk wɪl stɪl hɪt ˈjuˈɛs ɪn tu wiks, ˈoʊnli ə fju ˈkɪləˌmitərz tɪ ðə left.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Striking such a behemoth with a nuke is like hitting our cargo ship with a washing machine instead of potatoes
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: striking* səʧ ə ˈbihəməθ wɪθ ə nuk ɪz laɪk ˈhɪtɪŋ ɑr ˈkɑrˌgoʊ ʃɪp wɪθ ə ˈwɑʃɪŋ məˈʃin ˌɪnˈstɛd əv potatoes

dKm7T13X7n4.text: – better, but still useless.
Ok, let’s go the movie route
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: – ˈbɛtər, bət stɪl useless.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: – land someone on the asteroid, drill a hole and bury a nuke inside to avoid all of these problems!
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: ok*, let’s* goʊ ðə ˈmuvi route

dKm7T13X7n4.text: And indeed this is possible in theory, albeit suicidal in practice.
Landing on any space body is a nightmare.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: ˈlændɪŋ ɔn ˈɛni speɪs ˈbɑdi ɪz ə nightmare.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Even on fairly big Mars, whose surface we know almost perfectly, roughly 70% of our attempts have failed.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: even* ɔn ˈfɛrli bɪg mɑrz, huz ˈsərfəs wi noʊ ˈɔlˌmoʊst ˈpərfəktli, ˈrəfli 70 əv ɑr əˈtɛmpts hæv failed.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: So imagine the chances of landing a crew and a nuclear bomb on a small and extremely fast asteroid
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: so* ˌɪˈmæʤən ðə ˈʧænsɪz əv ˈlændɪŋ ə kru ənd ə ˈnukliər bɔm ɔn ə smɔl ənd ɪkˈstrimli fæst asteroid

dKm7T13X7n4.text: discovered just two weeks in advance.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: discovered* ʤɪst tu wiks ɪn advance.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Even if we succeeded, drilling in microgravity is painfully slow, since there is no downward pull to help you.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: even* ɪf wi səkˈsidɪd, ˈdrɪlɪŋ ɪn microgravity* ɪz ˈpeɪnfəli sloʊ, sɪns ðɛr ɪz noʊ ˈdaʊnwərd pʊl tɪ hɛlp you.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: So we’d need an agonizing amount of time that we don’t have.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: so* we’d* nid ən ˈægənaɪzɪŋ əˈmaʊnt əv taɪm ðət wi don’t* have.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: So sadly, that's not the answer.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: so* ˈsædli, ðæts nɑt ðə answer.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: We have to think less like Hollywood and more like a lumberjack.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: we* hæv tɪ θɪŋk lɛs laɪk ˈhɑliˌwʊd ənd mɔr laɪk ə lumberjack.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The Smart Way
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* smɑrt way

dKm7T13X7n4.text: If you want to split a log, you don’t hit it with a rock.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: if* ju wɔnt tɪ splɪt ə lɔg, ju don’t* hɪt ɪt wɪθ ə rock.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: You use an axe – a dense and perfectly shaped tool designed to break things apart.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: you* juz ən æks –* ə dɛns ənd ˈpərfəktli ʃeɪpt tul dɪˈzaɪnd tɪ breɪk θɪŋz apart.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: And just in the same way, scientists have found a new tool to destroy asteroids: super-dense, ultra-fast, cosmic bullets.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: and* ʤɪst ɪn ðə seɪm weɪ, ˈsaɪəntɪsts hæv faʊnd ə nu tul tɪ dɪˈstrɔɪ ˈæstərˌɔɪdz: super-dense*, ultra-fast*, ˈkɑzmɪk bullets.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: We won’t even have to shoot them!
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: we* won’t* ˈivɪn hæv tɪ ʃut them!

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Our cosmic bullets are called “penetrators”.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: our* ˈkɑzmɪk ˈbʊləts ər kɔld ““penetrators”.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: A few meters long, slim and made of tungsten, a metal way denser and harder than rock.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: a* fju ˈmitərz lɔŋ, slɪm ənd meɪd əv ˈtəŋstən, ə ˈmɛtəl weɪ ˈdɛnsər ənd ˈhɑrdər ðən rɑk.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: They work in an extremely simple way:
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: ðeɪ wərk ɪn ən ɪkˈstrimli ˈsɪmpəl way:

dKm7T13X7n4.text: You just put the penetrators in the way of the asteroid, to float silently in space.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: you* ʤɪst pʊt ðə ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtərz ɪn ðə weɪ əv ðə ˈæstərˌɔɪd, tɪ floʊt ˈsaɪləntli ɪn space.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: From the perspective of the asteroid, you wouldn’t see a few tiny bullets sitting still.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: from* ðə pərˈspɛktɪv əv ðə ˈæstərˌɔɪd, ju wouldn’t* si ə fju ˈtaɪni ˈbʊləts ˈsɪtɪŋ still.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: You’d see them rushing at you at 70,000 km/h!
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: you’d* si ðɛm ˈrəʃɪŋ æt ju æt 70,000* km/h!

dKm7T13X7n4.text: It doesn’t matter who stands still and who is fast!
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: it* doesn’t* ˈmætər hu stændz stɪl ənd hu ɪz fast!

dKm7T13X7n4.text: But this speed means that by far the longest part of this mission is to get to the asteroid in time.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: but* ðɪs spid minz ðət baɪ fɑr ðə ˈlɔŋgɪst pɑrt əv ðɪs ˈmɪʃən ɪz tɪ gɪt tɪ ðə ˈæstərˌɔɪd ɪn time.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: We can’t destroy it too close to Earth because its fragments will slam into the atmosphere all at once
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: we* can’t* dɪˈstrɔɪ ɪt tu kloʊz tɪ ərθ bɪˈkəz ɪts ˈfrægmənts wɪl slæm ˈɪntu ðə ˈætməsˌfɪr ɔl æt once

dKm7T13X7n4.text: with the power of thousands of nuclear bombs.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: with* ðə paʊər əv ˈθaʊzənz əv ˈnukliər bombs.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Our atmosphere can absorb isolated chunks – but if thousands of them strike together, their shockwaves will add up and kill millions.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: our* ˈætməsˌfɪr kən əbˈzɔrb ˈaɪsəˌleɪtɪd ʧəŋks –* bət ɪf ˈθaʊzənz əv ðɛm straɪk təˈgɛðər, ðɛr ˈʃɑkˌweɪvz wɪl æd əp ənd kɪl millions.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: So instead we need to get it one day before impact, when the asteroid will be nearly 2 million kilometers away,
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: so* ˌɪnˈstɛd wi nid tɪ gɪt ɪt wən deɪ ˌbiˈfɔr ˌɪmˈpækt, wɪn ðə ˈæstərˌɔɪd wɪl bi ˈnɪrli 2 ˈmɪljən ˈkɪləˌmitərz away,

dKm7T13X7n4.text: more than 4 times further than the Moon.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: more* ðən 4 taɪmz ˈfərðər ðən ðə moon.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: A vast distance, but doable.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: a* væst ˈdɪstəns, bət doable.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Our current rockets can cover this in about a week.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: our* ˈkɑrənt ˈrɑkəts kən ˈkəvər ðɪs ɪn əˈbaʊt ə week.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: We’ll send just one penetrator, about 2 meters long and weighing 2.5 tonnes.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: we’ll* sɛnd ʤɪst wən ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtər, əˈbaʊt 2 ˈmitərz lɔŋ ənd weɪɪŋ 2.5* tonnes.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Once our rocket arrives, it puts the penetrator in place to cause maximum damage … and then we wait.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: once* ɑr ˈrɑkət əraɪvz, ɪt pʊts ðə ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtər ɪn pleɪs tɪ kɔz ˈmæksəməm ˈdæmɪʤ …* ənd ðɛn wi weɪt.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: A tiny speck of light appears in the vast distance, and then suddenly it's here,
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: ə ˈtaɪni spɛk əv laɪt əˈpɪrz ɪn ðə væst ˈdɪstəns, ənd ðɛn ˈsədənli ɪts here,

dKm7T13X7n4.text: shooting at us faster than the speed of sound.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: shooting* æt ˈjuˈɛs ˈfæstər ðən ðə spid əv sound.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: We’ll slow down time to see exactly what happens.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: we’ll* sloʊ daʊn taɪm tɪ si ɪgˈzæktli wət happens.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The asteroid crashes into the penetrator so fast and with so much violence
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* ˈæstərˌɔɪd ˈkræʃɪz ˈɪntu ðə ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtər soʊ fæst ənd wɪθ soʊ məʧ violence

dKm7T13X7n4.text: that the power of 120 metric tons of TNT is released into the asteroid.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: that* ðə paʊər əv 120 ˈmɛtrɪk tənz əv tnt* ɪz riˈlist ˈɪntu ðə asteroid.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The rock vaporizes and the tungsten melts away, carving a wound that tunnels through the asteroid.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* rɑk vaporizes* ənd ðə ˈtəŋstən mɛlts əˈweɪ, ˈkɑrvɪŋ ə wund ðət ˈtənəlz θru ðə asteroid.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The damage is too much, and with all of this energy looking for a place to go,
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* ˈdæmɪʤ ɪz tu məʧ, ənd wɪθ ɔl əv ðɪs ˈɛnərʤi ˈlʊkɪŋ fər ə pleɪs tɪ go,

dKm7T13X7n4.text: the asteroid is blasted into thousands of pieces.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* ˈæstərˌɔɪd ɪz ˈblæstɪd ˈɪntu ˈθaʊzənz əv pieces.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The debris spreads out into a diffuse cloud.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* dəˈbri sprɛdz aʊt ˈɪntu ə dɪfˈjuz cloud.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: A day later the fragments hit Earth, dispersed over hundreds of thousands of square kilometers
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: a* deɪ ˈleɪtər ðə ˈfrægmənts hɪt ərθ, dɪˈspərst ˈoʊvər ˈhənərdz əv ˈθaʊzənz əv skwɛr kilometers

dKm7T13X7n4.text: and turning an apocalypse into a mostly harmless show of cosmic fireworks.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: and* ˈtərnɪŋ ən əˈpɑkəˌlɪps ˈɪntu ə ˈmoʊstli ˈhɑrmləs ʃoʊ əv ˈkɑzmɪk fireworks.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: So if we prepare and get everything ready, spotting a killer asteroid with two-weeks notice would be enough.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: so* ɪf wi priˈpɛr ənd gɪt ˈɛvriˌθɪŋ ˈrɛdi, ˈspɑtɪŋ ə ˈkɪlər ˈæstərˌɔɪd wɪθ two-weeks* ˈnoʊtɪs wʊd bi enough.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: But this was just a small asteroid.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: but* ðɪs wɑz ʤɪst ə smɔl asteroid.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: What if we face a cosmic mountain, a planet killer, carrying the destructive power of tens of thousands of nuclear arsenals.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: what* ɪf wi feɪs ə ˈkɑzmɪk ˈmaʊntən, ə ˈplænət ˈkɪlər, ˈkɛriɪŋ ðə dɪˈstrəktɪv paʊər əv tɛnz əv ˈθaʊzənz əv ˈnukliər arsenals.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The non avian dinosaurs would tell you about it but they’re dead.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* nɑn ˈeɪviən ˈdaɪnəˌsɔrz wʊd tɛl ju əˈbaʊt ɪt bət they’re* dɛd.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: What If It Is a Planet Killer?
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: wət ɪf ɪt ɪz ə ˈplænət killer?

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Planet killers are objects so vast and powerful that they would end most life on earth in a single strike.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: planet* ˈkɪlərz ər ˈɑbʤɛkts soʊ væst ənd ˈpaʊərfəl ðət ðeɪ wʊd ɛnd moʊst laɪf ɔn ərθ ɪn ə ˈsɪŋgəl strike.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The most dangerous ones are comets from the outer fringes of the solar system,
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* moʊst ˈdeɪnʤərəs wənz ər ˈkɑməts frəm ðə ˈaʊtər ˈfrɪnʤɪz əv ðə ˈsoʊlər system,

dKm7T13X7n4.text: so distant and dark that tracking them is impossible.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: so* ˈdɪstənt ənd dɑrk ðət ˈtrækɪŋ ðɛm ɪz impossible.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Comets are dirty ice balls the size of mountains, more fragile than pure rock
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: comets* ər ˈdərti aɪs bɔlz ðə saɪz əv ˈmaʊntənz, mɔr ˈfræʤəl ðən pjʊr rock

dKm7T13X7n4.text: but also much faster and violent, traveling at around 140,000 km per hour.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: but* ˈɔlsoʊ məʧ ˈfæstər ənd ˈvaɪələnt, ˈtrævəlɪŋ æt əraʊnd 140,000* km* pər hour.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: In 2020 the comet NEOWISE, with the power of 6,000 times all the nuclear bombs on Earth,
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: in* 2020 ðə ˈkɑmət neowise*, wɪθ ðə paʊər əv 6,000* taɪmz ɔl ðə ˈnukliər bɑmz ɔn earth,

dKm7T13X7n4.text: was discovered just 4 months before its closest approach to Earth.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: was* dɪˈskəvərd ʤɪst 4 mənθs ˌbiˈfɔr ɪts ˈkloʊsəst əˈproʊʧ tɪ earth.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: What if we find such a beast six months before it’s going to crash into Earth?
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: what* ɪf wi faɪnd səʧ ə bist sɪks mənθs ˌbiˈfɔr it’s* goʊɪŋ tɪ kræʃ ˈɪntu earth?

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Do we have a chance of surviving this?
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: do* wi hæv ə ʧæns əv sərˈvaɪvɪŋ this?

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Unfortunately this would be extremely hard for a bunch of reasons.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: unfortunately* ðɪs wʊd bi ɪkˈstrimli hɑrd fər ə bənʧ əv reasons.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: First of all our planet killer comet has so much more mass than a tiny killer asteroid,
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: first* əv ɔl ɑr ˈplænət ˈkɪlər ˈkɑmət həz soʊ məʧ mɔr mæs ðən ə ˈtaɪni ˈkɪlər asteroid,

dKm7T13X7n4.text: that simply breaking it into millions of pieces would not help us that much.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: that* ˈsɪmpli ˈbreɪkɪŋ ɪt ˈɪntu ˈmɪljənz əv ˈpisɪz wʊd nɑt hɛlp ˈjuˈɛs ðət much.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The chunks that hit Earth would still be massive and numerous enough
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* ʧəŋks ðət hɪt ərθ wʊd stɪl bi ˈmæsɪv ənd ˈnumərəs enough

dKm7T13X7n4.text: to set the sky ablaze and kill most life on Earth.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: to* sɛt ðə skaɪ əˈbleɪz ənd kɪl moʊst laɪf ɔn ərθ.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: So we need to make most if not all of its fragments completely miss Earth
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: soʊ wi nid tɪ meɪk moʊst ɪf nɑt ɔl əv ɪts ˈfrægmənts kəmˈplitli mɪs earth

dKm7T13X7n4.text: – but to do that we need to destroy it much further away – as far away as Mars.
And to destroy a mountain, we need way more penetrators – hundreds of thousands. And this is... well, a huge problem.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: – bət tɪ du ðət wi nid tɪ dɪˈstrɔɪ ɪt məʧ ˈfərðər əˈweɪ –* ɛz fɑr əˈweɪ ɛz mars.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: To travel this far and to transport this much payload, we need at least 24,000 super heavy rockets.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: and* tɪ dɪˈstrɔɪ ə ˈmaʊntən, wi nid weɪ mɔr ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtərz –* ˈhənərdz əv ˈθaʊzənz. ənd ðɪs ɪz... wɛl, ə juʤ problem.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: As of today, humanity has…
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: to* ˈtrævəl ðɪs fɑr ənd tɪ ˈtrænspɔrt ðɪs məʧ ˈpeɪˌloʊd, wi nid æt list 24,000* ˈsupər ˈhɛvi rockets.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: two
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: as* əv təˈdeɪ, juˈmænɪti has…

dKm7T13X7n4.text: – and they’re not really finished yet.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: two

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Even if all the industries in the world do nothing but switch to building rockets, we would not finish in time.
If we actually discovered a planet killer today, there is literally nothing we could do about it.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: – ənd they’re* nɑt ˈrɪli ˈfɪnɪʃt yet.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Except…
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: even* ɪf ɔl ðə ˈɪndəstriz ɪn ðə wərld du ˈnəθɪŋ bət swɪʧ tɪ ˈbɪldɪŋ ˈrɑkəts, wi wʊd nɑt ˈfɪnɪʃ ɪn time.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Maybe if we combine penetrators with a bit of Hollywood and our old friend, the nuclear bomb, there is still a way.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: if* wi ˈæˌkʧuəli dɪˈskəvərd ə ˈplænət ˈkɪlər təˈdeɪ, ðɛr ɪz ˈlɪtərəli ˈnəθɪŋ wi kʊd du əˈbaʊt it.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: For this plan to work we basically need to have all the parts ready beforehand.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: except…

dKm7T13X7n4.text: A rocket like NASA’s SLS – the one planned to take astronauts to the Moon
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: maybe* ɪf wi ˈkɑmbaɪn ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtərz wɪθ ə bɪt əv ˈhɑliˌwʊd ənd ɑr oʊld frɛnd, ðə ˈnukliər bɔm, ðɛr ɪz stɪl ə way.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: – loaded up with everything we need, ready to launch.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: for* ðɪs plæn tɪ wərk wi ˈbeɪsɪkli nid tɪ hæv ɔl ðə pɑrts ˈrɛdi beforehand.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: As soon as the planet killer is spotted on its way to wipe us out we launch a single rocket to meet it.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: a* ˈrɑkət laɪk nasa’s* sls* –* ðə wən plænd tɪ teɪk ˈæstrəˌnɔts tɪ ðə moon

dKm7T13X7n4.text: For 5 long months it travels through the nothingness of space as life on Earth nervously continues.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: fər 5 lɔŋ mənθs ɪt ˈtrævəlz θru ðə ˈnəθɪŋnəs əv speɪs ɛz laɪf ɔn ərθ ˈnərvəsli continues.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Finally it reaches its destination a bit beyond the orbit of Mars.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: finally* ɪt ˈriʧɪz ɪts ˌdɛstɪˈneɪʃən ə bɪt bɪɔnd ðə ˈɔrbət əv mars.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Now we deploy 5 massive penetrators in sequence, one perfectly lined up two kilometers after the other.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: now* wi dɪˈplɔɪ 5 ˈmæsɪv ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtərz ɪn ˈsikwəns, wən ˈpərfəktli laɪnd əp tu ˈkɪləˌmitərz ˈæftər ðə other.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The engineering challenge of aligning and timing this correctly is horrendous and we only have one attempt.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* ˈɛnʤəˈnɪrɪŋ ˈʧælənʤ əv əˈlaɪnɪŋ ənd ˈtaɪmɪŋ ðɪs kərˈɛktli ɪz ˌhɔˈrɛndəs ənd wi ˈoʊnli hæv wən attempt.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: So a few very brave astronauts go on this one way trip to supervise the process, with no way home.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: so* ə fju ˈvɛri breɪv ˈæstrəˌnɔts goʊ ɔn ðɪs wən weɪ trɪp tɪ ˈsupərˌvaɪz ðə ˈprɔˌsɛs, wɪθ noʊ weɪ home.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Nervous hours pass as all of humanity watches the skies and screens.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: nervous* aʊərz pæs ɛz ɔl əv juˈmænɪti ˈwɑʧɪz ðə skaɪz ənd screens.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: And then the moment comes.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: and* ðɛn ðə ˈmoʊmənt comes.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The icy mountain of death appears in full and then it’s already here!
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* ˈaɪsi ˈmaʊntən əv dɛθ əˈpɪrz ɪn fʊl ənd ðɛn it’s* ɔˈrɛdi here!

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Slow down time again!
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: slow* daʊn taɪm again!

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The comet smashes into the first penetrator at 140,000 km/h,
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* ˈkɑmət sˈmæʃɪz ˈɪntu ðə fərst ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtər æt 140,000* km/h,

dKm7T13X7n4.text: unleashing the power of 2,000 tons of TNT. Ice, rock and tungsten liquify in an instant
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: unleashing* ðə paʊər əv 2,000* tənz əv tnt*. aɪs, rɑk ənd ˈtəŋstən liquify* ɪn ən instant

dKm7T13X7n4.text: as the energy of the impact eats itself dozens of meters deep into the mountain.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: as* ðə ˈɛnərʤi əv ðə ˌɪmˈpækt its ˌɪtˈsɛlf ˈdəzənz əv ˈmitərz dip ˈɪntu ðə mountain.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Here is the second one, perfectly hitting the same spot punching directly into the crater, smashing, melting, drilling.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: here* ɪz ðə ˈsɛkənd wən, ˈpərfəktli ˈhɪtɪŋ ðə seɪm spɑt ˈpənʧɪŋ dɪˈrɛkli ˈɪntu ðə ˈkreɪtər, sˈmæʃɪŋ, ˈmɛltɪŋ, drilling.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: The third and fourth penetrators repeat the process again, now smashing a tunnel about 100 meters deep.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: the* θərd ənd fɔrθ ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtərz rɪˈpit ðə ˈprɔˌsɛs əˈgɛn, naʊ sˈmæʃɪŋ ə ˈtənəl əˈbaʊt 100 ˈmitərz deep.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: But this time this is just a scratch on the surface of the monster, the comet is not really damaged yet.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: but* ðɪs taɪm ðɪs ɪz ʤɪst ə skræʧ ɔn ðə ˈsərfəs əv ðə ˈmɑnstər, ðə ˈkɑmət ɪz nɑt ˈrɪli ˈdæmɪʤd yet.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: And then comes the final penetrator and its toxic load.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: and* ðɛn kəmz ðə ˈfaɪnəl ˈpɛnəˌtreɪtər ənd ɪts ˈtɑksɪk loʊd.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: 300 megatons in nuclear warheads – 20,000 times more energy than the bomb that destroyed Hiroshima.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: 300 ˈmɛgəˌtənz ɪn ˈnukliər ˈwɔˌrhɛdz –* 20,000* taɪmz mɔr ˈɛnərʤi ðən ðə bɔm ðət dɪˈstrɔɪd hiroshima.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: It travels deep into the tunnel and just before it hits the end, it explodes.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: it* ˈtrævəlz dip ˈɪntu ðə ˈtənəl ənd ʤɪst ˌbiˈfɔr ɪt hɪts ðə ɛnd, ɪt explodes.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: This time the nuke works.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: this* taɪm ðə nuk works.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Instead of hitting nothing in the vacuum the energy of the explosion smashes into rock and gravel and ice.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: instead* əv ˈhɪtɪŋ ˈnəθɪŋ ɪn ðə ˈvækjum ðə ˈɛnərʤi əv ðə ɪkˈsploʊʒən sˈmæʃɪz ˈɪntu rɑk ənd ˈgrævəl ənd ice.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: On top of vaporizing it from the inside,

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Turning into a cloud of millions of fragments spreading in all directions.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Humanity is saved.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: humanity* ɪz saved.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: To make this happen in reality would take unprecedented planning and precision. And yet, it’s possible.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: Not in a century with sci-fi technology. Just with the rockets, engineering, and knowledge we already have today.
dKm7T13X7n4.ipa: not* ɪn ə ˈsɛnʧəri wɪθ ˌsaɪˈfaɪ tɛkˈnɑləʤi. ʤɪst wɪθ ðə ˈrɑkəts, ˈɛnʤəˈnɪrɪŋ, ənd ˈnɑlɪʤ wi ɔˈrɛdi hæv today.

dKm7T13X7n4.text: And now, we’re incredibly excited to finally share it with you.
